# Islamic Beliefs: Reclaiming the Narrative

### *An Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an*

#### طرح کلی اندیشه اسلامی در قرآن

*A series of twenty-eight lectures delivered during the holy month of Ramadan, 1353 (1974)*

---

*This English edition was produced by faithful, full translation of the Persian original. The text follows the original's four-part structure — Faith, Tawhid, Prophethood, and Wilayah — across twenty-eight sessions, preserving the lecturer's argument, examples, Qur'anic expositions, and footnotes in full.*

---

# Note

Forty years have passed — forty years…

A precious and cherished jewel was right there, all around us, yet we were heedless of it and deprived of its blessing. A radiant jewel from the limpid ocean of the Wise Qur'an, a luminous jewel of the very substance of the Book of Light — for forty years it gathered dust, and its absence pained a people thirsting for the teachings of the Qur'an and the Ahl al-Bayt. A jewel without peer or match, named *A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an*.

\* \* \*

In the closing days of the summer of 1353 (1974), the prayer leader of a small, half-finished mosque — the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan al-Mujtabā, at the far end of the Sarshur Bazaar in Mashhad — with the arrival of the month of Ramadan, draws up a new program for his mosque. Sayyid ʿAlī Khāmeneʾī, thirty-five years old, with his tall stature and his penetrating eyes, resolves that every day of the blessed month, after the noon and afternoon prayers, he will stand behind the lectern with the Qur'an in hand and deliver a lecture for one hour, on the subject of *A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an*.

With the start of these sessions, the city of Mashhad is thrown into a stir. In the final days of summer — at the hour of the noon prayer, when the city normally empties out and people give themselves over to a few hours of rest — in one corner of the city an extraordinary bustle is afoot. People, old and young, women and men, from near and far, in the searing heat of the noon hours, with the parched tongues of those who are fasting, are on the move toward the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan. All of them eagerly make their way to this mosque so that they may drink deeply from the blessing-laden table of teachings that the young sayyid has spread out for them in the best month of God, and quench their thirst.

Each day the number of listeners to this young cleric grows, and his gathering becomes more crowded. Soon there is no longer even any room to sit in the very large garage behind the mosque. When you cast your eyes about, you see that people of every class are seated there: seminary student, university student, schoolchild, tradesman, local resident, woman, man — and most of them young. The medical students have hired a bus to bring them each day from the university to the prayer and then take them back after the session.

In the session of Sayyid ʿAlī Āqā-yi Khāmeneʾī, everything is fresh and new. Before the session begins, each person is handed a mimeographed sheet on which a summary of the session's discussion has been written. The lecturer, contrary to custom, does not sit on the pulpit (*minbar*); he stands behind the lectern and each day, with a fasting tongue, for more than an hour, speaks with fervor and in a loud voice. After the lecture, a Qur'an reciter sits in a place that has been prepared for him atop the pulpit and recites — in a beautiful voice and tone — the verses that were taken up in that session's discourse. The mosque's session bears more resemblance to a classroom lesson than to an ordinary lecture.

These are years — years of suffocation, despotism, and the dictatorship of the Pahlavi regime; black years of torture and of killing under torture. In these dark years — during which Imam Khomeini was living in exile — it was impossible, in any religious gathering, to utter the slightest word about, or even allude to, the sensitive matters of the regime of the Ṭāghūt. SAVAK is at the height of its power and has informers everywhere. Reciting the verses pertaining to *jihād* and struggle is a crime, and if a lecturer mentions the name of the Children of Israel in a verse and makes any allusion to Israel, his gathering is shut down and he himself is imprisoned. For all that, the young cleric of the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan is a veteran of the struggle. He knows how to conduct the lecture-session so as not to be caught by SAVAK's agents. With the utmost shrewdness and discernment, he opens a passageway for conveying the light of God's word to a people kept far from the Qur'an. Mr. Khāmeneʾī, with the greatest precision and astuteness, sets forth the entire body of Islamic beliefs in the language of the Qur'an in such a way that the people — beyond rebuilding their own beliefs upon the foundation of the teachings of the Qur'an — also begin to think about bringing an Islamic society into being! The young sayyid begins his discussions with Faith, carries them on to Tawhid, and then, by taking up Prophethood and Wilayah — and with the ending of the month of Ramadan — brings this pure and matchless course on belief to its conclusion. In these sessions the young sayyid was able, by sowing the seed of conscious Faith in the hearts and souls of the youth of Mashhad, to reconcile them with the Qur'an — and to draw the cherished Qur'an out of the estrangement of the mantelpiece niches, the marriage-contract spreads, and the funeral gatherings, and to place it in the hands of the young.

For years on end, certain people who had no correct understanding of religion had, with their rigid notions, kept the people away from the Qur'an under various pretexts and excuses — for instance, that understanding the Qur'an is not the business of ordinary people, and that the Qur'an should be read only in hope of its reward, and other such talk; talk that the governments welcomed. But Sayyid ʿAlī Āqā-yi Khāmeneʾī regarded these things either as the enemy's plot or as the misunderstandings of the elite. He himself was enraptured by the Qur'an, a lover of the Qur'an, a devotee of the Qur'an, and he had girded himself to put an end to the Qur'an's neglected estrangement. Firm and steadfast, with warm and captivating speech, he would take the Qur'an in hand, stand behind the lectern, and devote his entire being to the words of God. He wanted to revive pure Islamic thought once more, from the heart of the purest of divine texts, and to bring the Qur'an back into the hearts and the conduct of a Muslim people.

In the end, a few months after the sessions of the *General Outline* came to an end at the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan, and following the sessions explicating the *Nahj al-Balāgha*, Sayyid ʿAlī Āqā-yi Khāmeneʾī is arrested and taken off to the capital of oppression; he is sent to one of the most terrifying torture-chambers of that era of the world: the "Joint Committee Against Sabotage."

\* \* \*

Now forty years have passed since that unforgettable month of Ramadan, and in these forty years that ocean of Qur'anic teachings has remained only in the breasts of those same few thousand Mashhadi listeners, and the rest of the thirsting were deprived of it. In these forty years, of those pure Qur'anic teachings there remained only a report and a trace — just enough that we might be certain they had once existed — and only a small booklet bearing the name *A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an*.

Yes! Forty years were lost and gone. But thanks and praise be to God all the same that at last, after the passing of all these years, the dust has been lifted from this treasure-hoard, and that luminous jewel has lifted its head and come into view. *Al-ḥamdu lillāh*…

A forty-year-old vintage has raised its dignity and its allure, while from every one of its words there rains down freshness and youth. Let us open our arms to this dear guest who has remained on the road for forty years. The treasure-hoard *A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an* has reached us out of the dark, black years of utter suffocation — for today, for tomorrow, and for the tomorrows to come — so that it may illuminate hearts. Alas for the years that have gone by…

## The Booklet *A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an*

*A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an* was the fifth written work of His Eminence Ayatollah Khāmeneʾī, published before the Revolution, in the year 1354 (1975).

This booklet was a collection of those very mimeographs that had been handed out to those present at the daily Ramadan sessions at the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan in 1353 (1974) — together with a preface that the author himself had written upon it before publication. A preface in which the necessity and the indispensability of presenting a general outline of Islamic thought grounded in the Qur'an are well expressed, and its aims and fruits are mentioned and its accomplishments explained to the reader.

What is striking is that the text of this very booklet, for all its brevity and concision, set the individual and the social dimensions of religion squarely side by side and offered a fresh and original reading of the Qur'an. A booklet outwardly brief, yet with a profound content.

In addition to this booklet, after the Revolution, the content of six of the twenty-eight sessions of this collection was published in the form of a pamphlet titled *Wilāyat* by the Islamic Republic Party in the year 1360 (1981); it was never reprinted and is not readily available.

## How Ṣahbā Came to Obtain the Tapes of the Sessions

For a long time, gaining access to the content of these sessions was one of our heart's wishes and prayers — until one day we saw an interview with Mr. Murtaḍā Sādāt-Fāṭimī about his Qur'anic activity before the Revolution, and about a tape-sales center that he had had on Sanābād Street in Mashhad. He is among the country's distinguished Qur'an reciters and masters who, in the period before the Revolution, were among the companions, intimates, and trusted associates of the Supreme Leader, and who after the Revolution likewise continue to enjoy his attention and favor.

At one point in the interview Mr. Sādāt-Fāṭimī had said that at that time he had been in charge of the audio recording of the *General Outline* sessions. We did not hesitate; we went to Mashhad and presented ourselves before him. We brought up his interview and asked about the audio of the *General Outline* sessions. He told us that the audio of all the sessions had been recorded, that the book *A General Outline of Islamic Thought* had also been published, and that one could obtain the content of the sessions from that book. When we put it to him that the book contained only the content of the mimeographs and was empty of the text of the sessions themselves, he was astonished and lamented. We asked him whether he still had the tapes. The answer was yes — but the reels, which were the original source-documents themselves, had gone to the central archive holding the documents pertaining to the author, and he had only a single copy on a cassette tape.

Mr. Sādāt-Fāṭimī, without any expectation in return, placed those tapes at our disposal, and we for our part were never able to thank him as we ought to have. He humbly did the very same thing that had been entrusted to him forty years before: distributing and reproducing the tapes for trusted associates. It was as though, after long years, once again someone had come from afar to the center for distributing religious tapes in order to procure Mr. Khāmeneʾī's tapes — and he did the very same thing he used to do in the years before the Revolution. May God preserve his luminous, Qur'anic being.

Because the tapes had been recorded over from the reels, they had certain defects: for example, they begin from the middle of the discussion rather than from the start of the session; or they generally end at the conclusion of the discussion and do not include the recitation of the Qur'an and the supplication of the author — for the sessions generally ran longer than an hour.

One option was to wait in the hope of gaining access to the reel tape — a thing all but remote and beyond possibility. After careful study of the content of the sessions, we reached the conclusion that nothing of the content had been lost, save to the extent of one or two percent. We therefore decided to publish the book, so that whenever, at any time, we found new audio bearing upon the content of the sessions, we might add it to the book.

## The Value and Standing of the Book

The value of this book's content will become ever more apparent to the reader through careful study, review, repeated reading, and supplementary reading. Here, by briefly setting out the book's distinguishing features, we make the esteemed reader more keenly aware of the importance of publishing and studying it, so that — being conscious of the value of the book's content — he may set aside suitable and sufficient time to study it, and apply himself to it with precision and keen attention.

The present book is a work unique in its correct and complete presentation of Islamic thought. This book differs from the numerous books that have been written under the title of *Islamic Thought* or *Principles of Belief*. Those books treat the doctrinal principles of religion largely as mental concepts, and set themselves to proving the correctness of these principles and answering the doubts that have been raised. In contrast to those books, *A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an* introduces the essential matters of religion in a manner that engenders commitment. Hence the first feature is precisely this: that this book is not of the kind of theological (*kalāmī*) works that take up the course of proof and refutation.

The second feature of the book is its presentation of a general picture of Islamic thought to the whole world — not only to Muslims, but to each and every human being, whether a monotheist or not. Anyone, with any idea or way of thinking, can be an addressee of this book, because the course of the discussion and the points presented in the book are so logical and so far removed from any kind of bigotry that they will meet with the approval of every sound mind.

The third feature of the book is that in it a fresh definition of religion is given to the reader. A definition that does not regard religion as merely a collection of separate intellectual or practical principles, but rather defines it as a unified whole whose parts have been set side by side in a particular order.

This book is an eloquent testament to the efforts of His Eminence Ayatollah Khāmeneʾī toward the revival of Islamic thought — in those years when the concepts of religion had been emptied of their true meanings and nothing remained of them but a husk. Although the movements and the words of certain Islamic thinkers had once again turned attention toward Islam and its profound concepts, what was lacking — or altogether absent — was a comprehensive outline of religion. An outline that could fill the intellectual void of Muslims in the face of the hollow, empty human schools of thought, and prepare the ground for the penetration of Islam among societies and free-thinking people.

His Eminence Imam Khomeini[^note-hr] was assuredly the reviver of Islam in this age. With the start of the Imam's uprising, the name of Islam was once again on everyone's lips, and the dormant hearts of the Muslims were awakened. After the Imam's exile, this fervor and ardor that had been kindled became, for the Imam's struggling disciples, a fertile ground for deep intellectual and faith-based work — for building a firm substructure of the pure thought of Muḥammadan Islam.

The presentation of such an outline was not feasible save from a thinker who was aware and responsible, familiar with the concepts of the Qur'an and conscious of the purpose and the destination of religion. His Eminence Ayatollah Khāmeneʾī — sensing this void and need in society, and with mastery over the works of Islamic thinkers and through association with the time-conscious scholars of the age such as the martyr Muṭahharī, and with command over and devotion to the Qur'an — set forth the discussions of *A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an* in the blessed month of Ramadan; discussions that, owing to their presentation of a comprehensive view of monotheistic thought and their consideration of the various dimensions of human life, are a gateway for entering the domain of Islamic thought. This is the fourth feature of this book.

The fifth feature of the book is its reflection of his fresh and original view of religious concepts. Although the words of the Qur'an are words that have reached the listener's ear time and again, in his speech they take on a fresh life; a view that grants new dimensions to religious concepts — and that, in those days, came in for criticism:

"A certain group in Mashhad raised an uproar against me, asking why so-and-so had said these things under the word 'Wilāyat' — whereas this was not a denial of the wilāyat that they used to speak of. They said wilāyat means love. Well, we accept the love of the Imams; wilāyat means belief in the Imamate of ʿAlī (peace be upon him) — that we did accept. There were certain things, over and above these, that we had discovered in the word 'wilāyat.' I gathered together the verses of wilāyat in the Qur'an — the verses in which the word 'wilāyat' is used; for instance, imagine,

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Kahf (18:44):** *hunālika l-walāyatu lillāh* — "There, authority [walāyah] belongs to God."[^note-1]

and many verses; from these verses I put forward, I set out, a new interpretation of the word 'wilāyat.' Well, this ought to have been deemed a thing of value. In the field of medicine, if someone comes along and — suppose — puts forward a new idea about typhoid that adds to the previous knowledge, how do they treat him? Well, they honor him, they show him respect, they thank him. In religious matters, if a new word was put forward, if a fresh interpretation was set out on some question — even though it did not impugn the words of the past, simply by virtue of being new — a certain group would set about opposing it; and this state of affairs still persists in a certain group even now."[^note-2]

The sixth feature of the book is the conformity of its discussions with the political-militant conduct (*sīrah*) of the Imams (peace be upon them) for the establishment of Islamic governance. His view of the life of the Imams — gathered in the collection of episodes *The 250-Year-Old Person* — had led him, in the arena of struggle, always to keep three points in mind: first, expounding Islam in its correct form; second, speaking of the Islamic society and the ʿAlawī government; and third, speaking of the ruler of the Islamic society and directing people to the actual exemplar. This book is an example of his shrewd manner of struggle in that dark era. "Under the former regime, at times when things became very hard for us and I was lecturing at the Mosque of Karāmat or the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan in Mashhad, a point that I thought it would be severely harmful for the system and the regime for people to know, I would put forward without making the slightest allusion to the apparatus. Nor did I make any taunt or innuendo whatsoever against the apparatus. For instance, suppose that at that time we would say 'the ʿAlawī government.' If we said 'Islamic government,' they would grow sensitive. We would say 'the ʿAlawī order' or 'the ʿAlawī government.' My discussions in those days were full of such expressions. It was as though we were slipping a crowbar under the root of the apparatus so as to wrench it out with a single heave. And the apparatus was not even aware of it, because it did not strike its trunk at all."[^note-3]

The seventh feature of the book is its special attention to the chief affliction of human and Muslim societies — namely, the weakness of faith. By plunging deep into various verses of the Qur'an, he introduces the reality of Faith to his audience and explains that this conscious, commitment-bearing belief, if it is correctly known, is like the spirit that sets the body in motion: it brings beliefs onto the stage of life and human gatherings, and then this Faith becomes a maker of human beings and a maker of society.

The eighth feature of the book is the unity-engendering character of its discussions for the Islamic Ummah. The author at that time, with the insight he possessed, clearly perceived the hidden hands of colonialism at work in sowing discord among the Islamic Ummah; therefore, by relying upon the fundamental doctrinal principles of Islam, such as Tawhid, and by attending carefully to their dimensions, he prepared the ground for the unity of the Islamic Ummah in the face of the Ṭāghūts of the age.

The author's effort and striving to teach his audience the method of pondering (*tadabbur*) and going deep into the Qur'an is the ninth feature of the book. He would generally select a group of verses for his discussions and, while translating the verses, would encourage his audience to find the relation of the verses with one another. As he translated and explained the verses, he advanced step by step alongside his audience and impelled them to reflect upon and discover the connection among the verses. And by the interpretation that he offered of the verses, he created in his audience a fresh view toward the Qur'an.

The tenth feature of the book is His Eminence Ayatollah Khāmeneʾī's particular attention to the young. What emerges from his words in this series of lessons is that the principal addressees of these sessions are generally the young. "When I look at you dear young university students, I am reminded of the Mosque of Karāmat and the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan al-Mujtabā; for there too these same people — these same people of you, of thirty-five years ago — would sit, and the exegesis of the Qur'an, the exegesis of the *Nahj al-Balāgha*, and the foundations of the Islamic uprising were debated and discussed, written down and spoken. We got beaten for it too — both you got beaten, and we got beaten. The tyrannical apparatus of the Ṭāghūt could not, in those days, tolerate a seminary student sitting with a gathering of university students and speaking of religion — especially since our student gathering in those days was also a warm gathering; it was a populous and densely packed gathering. To be sure, these crowds that you see today, after the Revolution, formed nowhere and on no occasion before the Revolution; but compared to the sessions and assemblies of those days, perhaps no assembly in the country — no student assembly — possessed the unity, the uniformity, and the density of the assembly at the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan or the Mosque of Karāmat, where I would give exegesis lessons for the students."[^note-4]

Beyond all the unique features that have been mentioned, the importance of this book among the author's works lies in two respects:

The first respect is that the content of these sessions, from beginning to end, is all arranged according to the author's own view. From the selection of the title *A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an* for the collection of sessions and for the booklet that was published, to the ordering of the sections of Faith, Tawhid, Prophethood, and Wilāyat, the selection of a title for each session, the manner of entering into and exiting from a discussion, the selection of verses, and so on — all of it was according to his own view.

The second respect is that one may say there is no essential and important topic that has not been touched upon in this collection. More important than this is that this outline is, in truth, an expression of the author's intellectual system. In the course of these sessions, he has set forth the foundations of his own intellectual system, drawn from the verses of the Qur'an and the conduct of the Imams. The truth of this claim becomes more evident when, with a careful eye, we examine the author's words and orientations across these years. No general and fundamental line is to be found that does not have its root in this delineated system. Even the verses taken up in this series of sessions are among the verses that he ordinarily employs in his addresses. Hence the reader, by studying the present book, has taken an important step toward becoming acquainted with the author's intellectual system.

"A few years ago I had gone to Mashhad; one of our old friends — who has been very much present at our private and public discussions, and whom I still see a great deal — gave me a tape and said, please, listen to this tape just as you are, sitting in the car. I said, very well. I put on the tape, and I saw that he had searched through my lectures of long-ago periods and had selected from each a piece in which there is the image of the future, the promise of the future, and the ʿAlawī government — and he wishes to ask me: do you still have that same image in mind even now? Of course, his objection did not hold. Afterward I saw him in person and told him this objection does not hold. Even now, if I wanted to speak in that station, I would say the very same things, and my word has not changed.

I once held continuous sessions of lectures, thirty in all, for a month of Ramadan at the Mosque of Imam Ḥasan in Mashhad. At that time little importance was attached to recording lectures; but, exceptionally, all thirty of these lectures were recorded. These lectures are a fine source for catching me out! In those lectures, Tawhid, Imamate, Wilāyat, Prophethood, and other fundamental topics are discussed — which even now I affirm. These were the intellectual foundations for bringing an Islamic system into being; although at that time we were not hopeful that an Islamic system would be realized in six or seven years' time. We would say that even if it is not brought about for another fifty years, in the end these are its intellectual foundations. That work was about giving direction to the thought of the young generation of those days."[^note-5]

## The Style of the Book's Arrangement

While preserving the order of the sessions in the book, the course of the presentation of the material within a single session, the tone of the author's speech, the material unrelated to the content within the sessions, and the inclusion of the mimeographs of that time — we have endeavored to make the content of the work itself convey the serial nature of the sessions, their style, the atmosphere of the sessions, the limited facilities, and the venue where the sessions were held.

\* \* \*

In arranging the content of this book, several things have been done:

1. The main body of the book has taken shape from the setting side by side of the content of the sessions. The book has four main sections, named Faith, Tawhid, Prophethood, and Wilāyat, beneath each of which the related sessions appear. To preserve the independent identity of the sessions, the session title and the date of the session's holding have been inserted at the beginning of each session. The end of each session, too, has been marked by the placement of that session's mimeograph.

2. To preserve the flow of each session's content, in the manner that the author had expressed it, we have refrained from breaking up his words and inserting subheadings. Thus the reader keenly senses the flow of a single session's content.

3. In most of the cassette tapes of the sessions, the opening sentences of the session were not recorded. In the audio of one or two sessions, the author began the session with the recitation of a few selected verses of that session. We too, in order that all the sessions might have a uniform opening, in light of the author's method of reciting verses in those two sessions, have begun the sessions with verses of the Qur'an; but since we did not wish the reader to imagine that the author had recited these verses, the verses have been set in a form distinct from the body text.

4. The tone of Ayatollah Khāmeneʾī's speech in the *General Outline* sessions of the year '53 differs in certain respects from the manner and tone of the author's speech during the period of his Leadership. We for our part resolved to arrange the book in such a way that his tone would be conveyed to the reader. His tone of speech in those years is the tone of a revolutionary orator who, with great fervor and ardor, stands behind the lectern and speaks to a gathering of the young. His speech is warm and captivating, and his words are fresh and engaging. We therefore made our every effort, by inserting precise editorial marks alongside smoothing out the text, to convey the author's tone of speech to the reader in the best possible way.

5. Another thing that helps in conveying the atmosphere of the session and the circumstances of that period is the incidental material that the author expressed in the midst of his talks. Preserving this material within the main discussion causes interruptions and, in some cases, the losing of the thread of speech. On the other hand, in some cases the author touched upon points that are pleasant for the reader to hear and that make him aware, to some degree, of the circumstances of that period. To include this material, we have set it in a different typeface, within parentheses. The reader of the book is free to read these portions or to pass over them.

6. Another of the book's components is its mimeographs, which were handed out to those present at the beginning of each session. We have placed images of them at the end of the relevant session. After writing the material pertaining to the mimeographs, the author would give it to a person so that the material would first be written out in fine calligraphy and then mimeographed from that sheet. The style and the script of the writing of the mimeographs indicate that more than one person wrote these mimeographs. It was by God's grace that, in the course of the project, we found seventeen of those mimeographs. Taking these mimeographs as a model — in terms of script and appearance — and using the text of the session summaries in the booklet *A General Outline of Islamic Thought*, we reconstructed ten more mimeographs and placed them at the end of the relevant sessions. The errors present in the mimeographs — especially in the verses — were also corrected.

7. The main body of the book and its flow of content took shape through the arranging of the sessions side by side; but we felt it a pity not to include in this book the preface that the author wrote for the publication of the collection of mimeographs. Anyone who has read that preface will, with a glance at this book, confirm that the author's preface was, more than it suited the case of that booklet, set down in writing for these sessions and the content expounded in them.

8. Since the verses of the text are constantly broken up and repeated, or another verse is introduced amid them as a corroborating example, the reader may run into difficulty in correctly identifying the verses. To solve the problem: first, the verses of the Qur'an have been set in a different typeface; second, when the author reads the first portion of a group of verses for translation and explanation, the citation for that group of verses appears in the footnote. In this case, a footnote has been inserted only for those verses that, in between, have been recited from somewhere else. So that the narrations may be distinguished by their Arabic phrasing and terminology, they have been set in «» and in an Arabic font. Because there is an index of narrations at the end of the book, we have refrained from inserting their citations on the pages, and have referred the reader to the index merely by the mention of a number.

\* \* \*

At the end of the book there are two indexes: the index of verses and the index of narrations. The index of verses comprises the collection of verses that are cited in the text of the book. The verses are arranged in order of surah and verse number. In these sessions, the author recited more than three hundred verses. Owing to the observance of brevity and the mention of the meaning of the verses in the text of the session and in the mimeographs, the repeated mention of the meaning of the verses in the index has been avoided. In this index, the portions of a verse that the author recited are in black, and the portions that were not recited are in gray. This manner of presentation provides a context and a ground for the reader's greater acquaintance and intimacy with the Qur'anic foundations of the topics taken up and the key verses in the thought of the Supreme Leader.

In the index of narrations, the order in which the hadiths are placed is, first, according to the order of the Infallibles, and, in the second stage, according to the page number of the book. The Arabic texts that are not a narration from an Infallible but describe a historical event are not in this index, and their citation has been inserted as a footnote in the main text of the book.

We also thank the esteemed masters, Messrs. Ghulām-Riḍā Khalaj and Masʿūd Najābatī, who undertook the labor of the calligraphy of the book's *Bismillāh* and the design of the book's emblem.

If someone takes a broad and deep look at the span of the luminous life of His Eminence Ayatollah Khāmeneʾī — and especially at the period of his Leadership — and reflects upon the root of his orientations and actions, and searches for this root in his own words, he will find one of the most prominent and most effective roots to be "the verses of the Holy Qur'an."

The bond of His Eminence with the Qur'an, although it goes back to childhood and to the foot of his mother's Qur'anic tales and his learning of the ʿamma-juzʾ [the thirtieth part of the Qur'an] in the maktab-khānah [traditional schoolhouse], very soon turns into a bond of affection, of knowledge, and of practical application, and day by day its depth and effect increase. The "movement of return to the Qur'an" first occurs within His Eminence the Master himself, and the Qur'an becomes the most important and most practically useful road-map and the most reliable guide, illuminating for the human being the great and the small of thoughts, beliefs, faiths, orientations, traits, deeds, relations, and so on — in the individual, the social, and the governmental domain. The examination and recognition of this bond, in its outward dimensions (special attention to powerful and beautiful recitation, memorization, calligraphy, and so on) and in the meaning of the verses, is a most sweet and instructive undertaking that calls for a separate and ample occasion. We hope this important matter may come about among Ṣahbā's products.

The adoption of the epithet "Qur'anic Leader" for His Eminence Ayatollah Khāmeneʾī is a claim that, on the one hand, goes back to the recognition of that bond and shows his deep and long-standing intimacy with the Qur'an; and, on the other hand, derives from attention to his addresses and to the original, precise, manifest, and disclosed Qur'anic uses within them, as well as from reflection upon his decisions and actions — which guides us to the Qur'anic wellsprings.

*A General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qur'an* is perhaps the most important intellectual cornerstone for recognizing the thought of this "Qur'anic Leader" and for benefiting from his Qur'anic teachings. Yet this line has many points, great and small, and we hope that by moving along the luminous path of the school of the "Qur'anic Leader" we may benefit from them, and become Qur'anic to the extent of our own capacity.

\* \* \*

Our Leader, dearer to us than our own souls, is a grace from the holy and merciful being of His Eminence al-Riḍā (upon him be thousands of greetings and praise); for whatever our nation possesses is from the blessings of the Sun of Ṭūs. "Ṣahbā," from the very beginning, has set out by recourse to His Eminence ʿAlī ibn Mūsā and has been a sipper at his threshold, so that it might take a single step in the weighty duty of conveying the teachings and lights of this Leader — this Riḍawī gift — to the longing and thirsting people. We hope that, through the attention and prayer of His Eminence al-Riḍā, this small step may meet with the satisfaction of his precious son, His Eminence the Master of the Age and the Time [the Imam of the Age].

Fāṭimiyyah 1434 — Farvardīn 1392 (March–April 2013)

\* \* \*

## The Discourse "The Spirit of Tawhid: The Negation of Servitude to Other than God"

The tenth discourse of the collection of Ṣahbā discourses is the discourse "The Spirit of Tawhid: The Negation of Servitude to Other than God." This discourse is an article that the author wrote at the request of a university student and in answer to the question "What role does belief in Tawhid actually play in a human being's life?" This article was published for the first time in the year 1356 (1977) in a book titled *The Monotheistic Worldview* (*Dīdgāh-i Tawḥīdī*). Although the title of this article bears a resemblance to the title of the twelfth session of this book, in terms of content it is deeper and is, in truth, a summary and a conclusion of all the discussions of the chapter on Tawhid.

\* \* \*

In response to the many requests of Ṣahbā's audience for listening to the audio of the book, an audio collection has been prepared. This collection comprises: the complete audio of the sessions, twenty-eight audio segments of the session summaries, ten thematic audio segments, and ten audio segments about the session and its margins. To obtain whichever items you wish, you may go to the address jahadi.ir/sahba/tarhekoli.

Urdībihisht-māh 1399 (May 2020)

[^note-hr]: *raḥmatu-llāhi ʿalayh* — "may God have mercy upon him" (an honorific invocation following the name of Imam Khomeini in the source).

[^note-1]: Sūrat al-Kahf (the Blessed Sūra), verse 44.

[^note-2]: Remarks of the author, dated 13 Mehr 1367 (5 October 1988).

[^note-3]: Remarks of the author, dated 8 Tīr 1371 (29 June 1992).

[^note-4]: Remarks of the author, dated 25 Urdībihisht 1386 (15 May 2007).

[^note-5]: Remarks at a meeting with the members of the founding board and the board of trustees of the Research-Cultural Institute of the Islamic Revolution, 4 Tīr 1381 (25 June 2002).

<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

# Author's Introduction

To present Islam as an *ideology*—a coherent and harmonious social school of thought, possessed of integrated principles and oriented toward the collective life of human beings—is one of the most urgent necessities of religious thought.

Islamic discussions and studies have, for the most part, until now lacked these two vital characteristics. For this reason, in comparing Islam with the social schools and ideologies of our age, they have not brought researchers and seekers to a fruitful conclusion or a decisive judgment to the degree that one might wish and ought to expect. That is to say, they have proven incapable of presenting—as a whole—the design and blueprint of a unified, cohesive creed whose every part holds together, and of defining its relationship to the other schools and creeds.

In addition, because the discussions have generally been carried out in a mental, abstract manner, far removed from the sphere of practical and concrete—and especially social—impact, they have yielded nothing more than an intellectual, mental sort of knowledge. With respect to the collective life of human beings—and in particular with respect to determining the shape and structure of society—they have offered no commitment, no obligation, and not even a clear and definite theory.

Another point is this: the Qur'an—Islam's decisive and indisputable document—has in most cases been given no share in illuminating the way and opening the path. In its place, quasi-rational subtleties and deep speculations, or conjectural narrations and transmitted reports—sometimes accorded even greater authority—have been treated as the standard-bearers and the responsible authorities. As a result, doctrinal thought has grown and taken shape apart from the Qur'an and in disregard of it. Perhaps it is precisely this disconnection and indifference—or a sense of needing nothing from it, or a despair of the possibility of making sound use of it (each of which, in turn, is the effect of particular causes)—that has caused contemplation of the verses of the Qur'an to give way to a superficial, careless, fruitless recitation and chanting, devoid of worldly benefit and aimed entirely at otherworldly reward and recompense, so that the noble Book of God has become the raw material of vulgar habits and of demagoguery that exploits the common people.

In view of this reality, one may, in brief, regard three important features as necessities in the intellectual discussions and accounts of Islam—necessities that it is unworthy for the aware and responsible thinkers of this age to neglect:

First, that the teachings and intellectual apparatus of Islam should come out of pure abstraction and mentalism and—like all social schools of thought—should be oriented toward practical duties and especially toward social life; and that each of its theoretical topics should be examined, reflected upon, and investigated from this standpoint: what plan it offers for the life of the human being, what purpose it offers for his existence, and what path it offers for reaching that purpose.

Second, that the intellectual questions of Islam should be studied in a connected manner, as the parts of a single whole—each one examined in light of the fact that it is a part of the totality of the religion, an element of this compound, and a pillar in this firm edifice, harmonious with and connected to the other parts and elements (and not separate and unrelated to them)—so that, from a knowledge of these principles taken as a whole, a comprehensive and all-encompassing general design of the religion may be derived, in the form of a complete, unambiguous ideology possessed of dimensions commensurate with the multidimensional life of the human being.

Third, that in deducing and understanding the principles of Islam, the basic documents and texts of the religion should be the source and origin—not personal tastes and opinions, nor the mental and intellectual stockpiles of this person or that—so that the result of the inquiry and investigation may truly be "Islamic," and nothing other than that. To fulfill this aim, the Qur'an is the most complete and most trustworthy document upon which one can rely, that "falsehood cannot reach it from any direction,"[^intro-1] and in which "there lies the means of clarifying every thing,"[^intro-2]—and this, of course, in the light of the deep, far-reaching contemplation that the Qur'an itself has commanded us to undertake.

What has been gathered in this treatise is a reflection of an effort to fulfill these aims, in the form of an account of Islam delivered through a series of lectures.

In these lectures, the effort was made to seek out the most important intellectual foundations of Islam, in their most constructive and living dimensions, within the eloquent and luminous verses of the Qur'an. Then, while offering an explanatory exposition that would teach the listeners the method of contemplating and pondering deeply over the Qur'an, those foundations were to be identified and shown within these verses; and, where necessary, recourse was to be had to the authentic narrations issued from the Prophet of God and the Infallible Imams (peace be upon them) for clarification and emphasis—so that, while verses of the Qur'an were reflected upon, pondered, and understood, one of the principles of Islam would be expounded—and this from a practical standpoint, generative of commitment and obligation, and as one of the points of Islam's mode of thought and ideology.

…And finally, so that a summary and a distillation of the whole of the material presented might remain in the minds and thoughts of the listeners, and might serve as a starting point for independent and settled thought and reflection, the substance of each lecture's content was recorded on a sheet of paper and placed, day by day, at the disposal of those present…

What is now offered is the collection of those "summaries" (with slight modification), the publication of which is a necessary response to the logical and acceptable request of many of those who heard the lectures, or heard of them… in the hope that it may prove useful and find acceptance.

The third of Ābān, 1353

[^intro-1]: The Holy Qur'an.

[^intro-2]: The Holy Qur'an.

<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

# Part One: Faith

### ایمان


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 1: Faith (1)

*ایمان (۱) — delivered Thursday, 28 Shahrīvar 1353 / 2 Ramaḍān al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:133–134):** "And hasten to a forgiveness from your Lord and to a Garden whose breadth is the heavens and the earth, prepared for the God-conscious — those who spend in prosperity and adversity, who restrain their anger and pardon people; and God loves those who do good."

The monks of Christianity, in order that the hem of their garment should not be stained by sins, took up monasticism[^s1-1]; they sought refuge in caves and mountains and desolate corners. The Qurʾān says:

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat al-Ḥadīd (57:27):** "And the monasticism which they invented — We did not prescribe it for them."[^s1-2]

That is: the monasticism which they themselves brought forth, which they established as an innovation — We had not prescribed monasticism for them. But the Muslim scholar has no monasticism, no seclusion, no flight. The Muslim scholar is precisely the one who strives to lay hold of the drowning man.[^s1-3] An aware, Muslim individual — every Muslim human being, since being a Muslim and being responsible are mutually entailing one another — is exactly like this: he strives to save the drowning man, the cholera-stricken, the sick. This cannot be accomplished by flight. He makes himself God-conscious (*muttaqī*), possessed of *taqwā* — that is, he puts on the necessary equipment, the necessary armor, against the harm of sin, and enters the zone of sin in order to take the sinners by the hand. In short, *taqwā* is this.

Once this is the meaning of *taqwā*, is *taqwā* a prerequisite and a means for victory, or not? You see that, quite easily, it is a means of victory. The one who wishes to overcome this disease, to be victorious in this zone — if he were perpetually afraid that this microbe might take effect in his own body, how could he rescue those struck by the microbe, those struck by cholera? He must have made his own mind easy; he must be confident about himself, and then enter the zone of the cholera-stricken and rescue the others. It is then that he will also attain victory. It is then that he will also accomplish this task with ease.

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:130–132):** "And be conscious of God"[^s1-4] — adopt the God-consciousness of God — "that perhaps you may be the victorious" — so that perhaps you may become triumphant, prosperous, and successful.

> "And be conscious of the Fire that has been prepared for the disbelievers" — guard yourselves and beware of that Fire which has been made ready for the disbelievers.

> "And obey God and the Messenger, that perhaps you may be shown mercy" — obey God and the Prophet, that perhaps you may be placed within the mercy of the Lord.

Now, obedience to God is no different from obedience to the Prophet — so on what account does He say: obey God and obey the Prophet? Is it not redundant to mention both of them together? No. If He were only to say "Obey God" — obey God — and not mention the Prophet as a concrete instance and exemplar, and not call to mind obedience to the Prophet, then those who stand at the opposite pole from the Prophet might also claim: we are obeying God. The scope of claims is wide; anyone can openly profess the cause of religion and faith and *taqwā*. Anyone can claim to be a servant of God and obedient to Him.

In the time of the Prophet, both the Prophet claimed servitude to God and obedience to Him, and likewise those who, on the front opposing him, fought against him and made trouble — the leaders and lords and monks of Christianity, and the rabbis[^s1-5] of the Jews — these too made the claim.

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:18):** "And the Jews and the Christians said, 'We are the sons of God.'"[^s1-6]

— even a notch above the Prophet: he said, "I am the servant of God," while these said, "We are the children and the sons of God." They imagined that obedience to God was entirely at their disposal. Or, to put it another way: these people do not even truly think it; some of those disobedient to God merely pretend in this manner that they are obedient to God. Is that right or not? There are some who, when they sit alone with themselves, find what a black record they have; they understand that what they say is, from head to foot, a lie. Yet before the people they pretend in this way that they are the good servants of God and the obedient ones and the worshippers of the Lord. Over against the truly truth-worshipping people, these must be set apart, they must be distinguished[^s1-7]. This is why, here, God the Exalted — in the station of explaining obedience and its necessity for the believers — speaks in this manner:

> "And obey God and the Messenger" — obey God and the Prophet. If He had not said "the Prophet," the enemies of the Prophet too would have said, "We are obedient to God." Therefore it must be made clear what obedience to God means. Those who consider themselves servants of God, but are not servants of the command of God's law, who do not act upon the law, who are not bound to the requirements of this servitude — how can these say, "We are servants of God"?

> "Obey God and the Messenger" — obey God and the Messenger — "that perhaps you may be shown mercy" — that perhaps you may be placed within the mercy of the Lord.

What does the mercy of God mean? What does it mean to be placed within the mercy of God? Here, make a comparison between the expression of the Qurʾān and our popular, vulgar notion. We say: if we are sinners, if we have disobeyed God, if we have not acted upon the obligatory duties and commitments and responsibilities, if we have not drawn our foot back from the forbidden zones of the Lord — we have only one hope. What is it? It is the mercy of God. That God should deal with us by His mercy, that God should have mercy upon us — this is our claim and our talk. That is to say, the customary talk of our society and our people is this. Where do we place the mercy of God? For there where we did not act. Whereas — when we have disobeyed, when we have trampled underfoot the forbidden zone of God, when we have not observed the divine responsibility and commitment — in just such cases we say: we have no deeds, unless God have mercy on us. We regard mercy — the mercy of God, the mercy of the Lord — as the rival of acting and the substitute for acting.

The verse of the Qurʾān is the reverse; it says: act, obey, that perhaps you may be placed within the mercy of the Lord. The mercy of God is for that time when a nation acts upon its responsibility. God has mercy upon a people at the time when they obey Him, when they carry out their duties. Should seven hundred million Muslims sit awaiting the cloud of the Lord's mercy to rain down upon their heads — and meanwhile open the roads so that the thieves of honor and the plunderers of religion may come and carry off everything they have — sitting in hope of the mercy of God? Then tell them to keep sitting, for now!

> "And obey God and the Messenger, that perhaps you may be shown mercy" — obey God and the Messenger, the Prophet, that perhaps you may be placed within His mercy. What does it mean, "Obey God"? In what does obedience to God consist? In this: that we bear upon our shoulders all the duties and divine proofs; that we carry out what has been placed in our charge. As the noble verse of the Qurʾān says: the believers are those who, when a dispute occurs among them, refer it to you, O Prophet — they have recourse to you — and when you have issued a ruling:

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:65):** "Then they find within themselves no discomfort from what you have judged"[^s1-8] — the command which you have issued leaves not the least speck of resentment upon their spirits and their hearts — "and they submit in full submission" — submitting to the command.

The true believer is like this. If a nation were like this, an *ummah* — if a body of people came under the command of God in this manner — then it is that the mercy of the Lord and His infinite grace would encompass them; then it is that an *ummah* attains lordship; then it is that a nation reaches human maturity; then it is that the captivities and chains are loosed from its hand and foot; then it is that divine mercy encompasses it.

> "And obey God and the Messenger, that perhaps you may be shown mercy" — obey God and the Prophet, that you may be placed within the mercy of the Lord.

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:133):** "And hasten to a forgiveness from your Lord"[^s1-9] — here is the field of contest, the field of taking the lead and of racing. Outstrip one another, take the lead — "to a forgiveness," toward a forgiveness — "and a Garden," and a Paradise "whose breadth is the heavens and the earth," from the side of your Lord, which is as wide as the heavens and the earth — "prepared for the God-conscious," made ready for the people of *taqwā*.

There, one sign of *taqwā* and one result of *taqwā* was mentioned; here, the signs of *taqwā* will be mentioned one after another. O human being who, for a handspan of earth and a measure of water and clay in such-and-such a region of the world, are ready to take the lead, ready to leave others behind, ready to put to work and employ all your strength and powers, so that you may — in the auction of such-and-such a piece of land, in acquiring such-and-such capital, in seizing such-and-such a shop, in taking such-and-such a corner of such-and-such a country, in setting up such-and-such a company in such-and-such a region of the world, in obtaining material privileges as much as possible — for these you are ready to compete, to gather speed, to leave others behind, even though you have trampled honors and virtues underfoot — O human being! They do not tell you: do not gather speed; they do not tell you: sleep at home, do not put your powers to work. The one who, in the name of religion, says this to you, lies and does not know. Religion does not say: leave your powers idle; religion says: gather speed, as much as possible; compete, as much as possible — but toward what? Toward that which is worthy of you, not toward a handspan of water and clay, not toward some trifling sum, not toward the material life of this world which, whatever it may be, is small and slight for you. O great human being! Toward something that befits your grandeur, that accords with your station — for the human being is the loftiest of the creatures of this world, the greatest of the grandeurs of existence after the Lord, lodged within this small and limited frame — O great human being, gather speed, compete, but toward what?

> "To a forgiveness from your Lord and a Garden." Let your speed be toward the forgiveness of the Lord, toward that supernal divine Paradise before which all the heavens are small, and all the earth is but little against it.

What does this mean? Pay close attention to the Qurʾānic expressions so that you may understand well. The Qurʾān says: if you wish to set your aspiration upon something — for you, the earth and the heaven are little and trifling; set your aspiration upon something higher than these. O great human being! Forgiveness is what matters for you; higher than everything is forgiveness, and after forgiveness, that thing whose worth and grandeur is higher than the heavens and the earth.

What does forgiveness (*maghfirah*) mean? We have seen: we said to so-and-so, "Sir, I beg your pardon; I gave you an insult; forgive me." And he too, whether with airs and pride, or with good nature and graciousness — in whatever manner — said, "Very well, we have overlooked it." Somewhere, some unintentional offense was committed; the person goes round the one who holds the right, with pleading and entreaty and "Sir, forgive me, sir, be gracious," and he too says, "Very well, I have forgiven you." In some government and state office, a certain amount of tax was levied upon you — on what reckoning, rightly or wrongly. You went, paid a courtesy, spoke a flattering word, said a couple of sentences, took someone along with you, a letter, a recommendation, a telephone call; they said, "Very well, we have forgiven you a certain amount." Having seen these acts of pardon, we imagined that the divine forgiveness, too, was of the same kind as these pardons. Such-and-such a person has wronged, has committed a crime, has sinned, has bred and stirred up corruption in the land, and deserves the chastisement of God; then, on the Day of Resurrection, on account of a drop of a tear he shed, or some attention and recourse he made, God should say, "Very well, now that it has come to this, We too have overlooked your faults; We have forgiven you." Is the forgiveness of God this?

> "Hasten to a forgiveness" — is this what it is saying? No.

Concerning the meaning of forgiveness (*ghufrān*), too, I have spoken much. The reason I say "I have spoken" is that you brothers, who were mostly present at our discussions, should return to your memory and bring forth, out of the hidden places and recesses of memory, what we have said. *Ghufrān* means the healing and the filling of a void. Your body sustains a wound; this thigh, this arm, sustains a deep wound; here the flesh is torn open. They give you a salve, you apply a medicine, you take a vitamin, you have an injection — until it knits together, until it seeps and breeds, until at last it brings into being, beside itself, its own like, its own kind: that flesh of the body, that muscle of the body. The structures of your body work, this wound is filled, it finds healing. Keep before your eyes this healing of a wound, of an injury, so that I may liken it and draw it as a parable for *ghufrān*; see how *ghufrān* is.

If your soul, in the station of parable, were like a body, then every sin you commit strikes a blow upon the soul and brings about a wound. Why, sir? Why do we say that sin is a blow upon the soul? Because the soul must rise higher, and sin is anything that holds the human soul back one step from its intended exaltation and perfection. In the station of likeness and parable, this is the very wounding, the very bringing-into-being of a gash upon the body of your soul. When that sin was committed, that deficiency took shape. You consumed people's property unlawfully, God forbid; the one who drank wine, the one who took usury, the one who committed fornication, the one who lied, the one who slandered — by each of these acts, a gash entered into his soul, into the body of his soul; this soul was wounded, was rendered deficient, fell far from the perfection which is the utmost of aspirations[^s1-10]. Now this sin must find forgiveness.

What does *ghufrān* mean? *Ghufrān* means that this void, this deficiency of the soul, this wound of the soul, this defect that has come into being within his self, should be removed — this is what they call *ghufrān*. How is it removed? How is that defect removed which sin has brought into being in your soul? By making amends. The one who, by some sin, has somewhat debased[^s1-11] and cast away his life from the summit of humanity and perfection and the point of human ascent — his falling-behind is made good at the time when he goes up a measure.

Let me give another example. You boarded a car; you are going to the fifty-*farsakh* mark. If this car halted along the road, what became of you? You fell behind. By what is this falling-behind made good? By going a little faster, by going a little more without interruption, by giving up a little more of your rest, so that at the appointed time you reach the intended point. Otherwise, by sitting there and saying, "O God, I did wrong; an hour ago I lounged here in the coffeehouse and set off late; O God, I erred; O God, I did wrong" — this gets nothing done. Now, after all, the wrong was done; set off sooner, go faster, so that you may make good this one-hour halt and delay. Divine forgiveness is like this.

God the Exalted does not say: because you sinned, now whatever good work you do, We will not at all wipe out that sin — God does not act out of spite.

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat Ṭāhā (20:82):** "And indeed I am Most Forgiving"[^s1-12] — We are not spiteful, We are the Forgiver. Those sins that were committed, those errors that came into being — We are ready to overlook those errors, provided that amends are made. We do not say: because you erred one day, now though you have striven a hundredfold, your striving counts for nothing because of your error. No.

> "And indeed I am Most Forgiving" — We are the Forgiver, We fill up the voids, We heal the wounds — but for whom?

> "For whoever repents and believes and does righteous deeds and then is guided"[^s1-13] — I am the Forgiver for the one who repents. What does "repents" mean? It means: he turns back. The path of your perfection was from here; you ought to have gone this way. We are ready to forgive you — when? When you go toward perfection, when you return once more to the right path.

> "For whoever repents" — repents, that is, turns back. *Tawbah* means turning back. Let him strengthen his faith, let him do righteous deeds. Deeds! One must not be heedless of deeds and content oneself with mere speech and self-satisfaction.

Therefore, forgiveness means the filling of those voids which are the wounds of the human soul; by filling them, the human being reaches perfection. This is something for which it is fitting that the human being should strive on its path, should compete, should gather speed. Forgiveness is very important. Forgiveness is not that God the Exalted, without reckoning, out of arbitrary whim, should single out some person for an unmerited grace, without that person himself having made any effort on the path of this divine grace.

> "And hasten to a forgiveness from your Lord" — hasten toward a forgiveness from your Lord — "and a Garden," and a Paradise — "whose breadth is the heavens and the earth," whose breadth and expanse is as much as the heavens and the earth — "prepared," it has been made ready — "for the God-conscious," for the people of *taqwā*.

That well-known saying they utter — "Sir, you are of the people of Paradise and will go to Paradise, God willing, if they let you in" — this is a very apt remark. We are all longing for Paradise; we all ask for Paradise in our supplications. Sometimes asking for Paradise alone does not even satisfy us — we bring its particular features, one by one, into our supplication too: its houris[^s1-14] and its good food and its bird-meat[^s1-15] and so forth, we line up in our supplications. But God Himself says: We have prepared this Paradise for the people of *taqwā*; We have spread this table for a certain special people; they must come and sit at this table and enter this zone. Who are they? The people of *taqwā*.

Who is the person of *taqwā*?

> "Prepared for the God-conscious." (Pay close attention to the translations, for now, when our dear reciter comes here to recite the verses, I have laid stress on every word I emphasized, so that it may be in your mind, so that during the recitation you may immediately return to the meanings of the words.) "Prepared for the God-conscious" — made ready for the people of *taqwā*. Who are the people of *taqwā*?

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:134):** "Those who spend in prosperity and adversity"[^s1-16] — those who give freely in ease and in hardship. This is one condition of being a person of *taqwā*: spending (*infāq*).

*Infāq*, too, I have explained several times by now. There is no harm in repeating these things, since these are words that, for the most part, reach your ears fresh — the more they are repeated, the more they remain in the heart, and so much the better. *Infāq* differs from spending. Spending means that a person spends some money. *Infāq* is spending, but not every kind of spending. *Infāq* is that spending by which a void is filled, a true need is met. Where are those who spend millions — and apparently spend it on good works too — and yet, in the language of the Qurʾān, we must tell them that they are the most wretched of people, because their work is not *infāq*?

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat al-Kahf (18:103–104):** "Shall We inform you of the greatest losers in deeds? Those whose effort in the life of this world has gone astray, while they reckon that they are doing good."[^s1-17] These monies that are spent, this gold and these ornaments hung upon the starving body of a group of people, these superfluous and false adornments — these are not *infāq*. Why? Because they fill no void.

Sir! If you give these five *rials*[^s1-18] to someone who has a hundred such five-*rial* coins and a one-*tūmān* coin in his pocket, and can provide a hundred more, this is not *infāq*; but if you gave it to that person who is awaiting a single five-*rial* coin to buy a loaf of *sangak* bread and fill his stomach, this is *infāq*. Of course, by this sentence I did not mean to say that the gentlemen should now go and keep handing out five-*rial* coins to the hungry and the beggars so that they may fill their stomachs — no. Sometimes filling the stomach of a hungry person is not even *infāq*; under certain conditions it is so. Under those conditions in which poverty and hunger, like a weed, are sprouting up over the lands without reckoning, there filling the stomach of one hungry person is like snipping off the top of a single blade of a weed. In the wilderness, how much value does a weed have? After all, one weed is removed from before the eye, but how fundamental is this work? How worthwhile is this work? Very little and slight. Therefore *infāq* is that which fills a void, which meets a need. That nation which today has need of a certain thing — like air and water — if you helped that nation with something other than that thing, here you did not perform *infāq*; you committed a squandering of money. So *infāq* is not the work of everyone; *infāq* is the work of intelligent people — those who understand the voids and the needs, and are ready to fill those voids and needs in their proper place. *Infāq* is very important.

> "Those who spend in prosperity and adversity" — one of the signs of the people of *taqwā* is this: they spend in ease and in hardship.

> "And those who restrain anger" — and those who swallow down their wrath. What does swallowing down wrath mean? It means: they do not act on the basis of emotion — everywhere, intellect. But sometimes intellect is also accompanied by righteous wrath. Do you not see that the Qurʾān says:

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat al-Fatḥ (48:29):** "Harsh against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves."[^s1-19] When they say "those who restrain anger," its meaning is not what you might imagine — that we mean to say the wrath of a nation, the wrath of a human being, the wrath of a society against those upon whom wrath ought to be vented should be done away with and brought to naught. No. The Qurʾān, too, does not say to swallow down these instances of wrath; it says: do not perform an action on the basis of wrath. "And those who restrain anger" — those who suppress, those who swallow it down — not those who forget, but those who swallow down rage and wrath; so that when the wrath has subsided, the human being may, with intellect, with understanding, do what is fitting.

> "And those who pardon people" — those who overlook from people, who forgive people's faults. From people's errors, from people's mistakes, from people's sins, from people's slips, one must overlook, one must let pass. One must not overlook that sin which was not a slip. God too would hardly overlook such a sin. One must not overlook that wrongful deed which was done out of deliberate intent and obstinacy — but the slips, the shortcomings, which abound in the conduct of the common run of people, these are pardonable and to be overlooked.

> "And those who pardon people; and God loves those who do good" — and God loves the people who do good.

> What else are the signs of the people of *taqwā*?

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:135):** "And those who, when they commit an indecency or wrong themselves, remember God and seek forgiveness for their sins" — those who, when they commit some great sin, or wrong their own selves — "or wrong themselves," they do injustice upon their own selves — "remember God," immediately fall to remembering God. In the valley of heedlessness, beneath the cover of heedlessness, they do not tarry long.

There is a very strange verse in the Qurʾān on the theme of remembrance and recollection — and, as it happens, this one too is for the people of *taqwā*:

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:201):** "Indeed, those who are God-conscious — when a touch of Satan afflicts them, they remember."[^s1-20] When a band of satans — with the wide meaning that "satan" has — when a band of satans and the satan-natured ones surround a man to lead him astray, to draw him off the path, to cast him into forgetfulness, "they remember God," the human being immediately falls to remembering God. The remembrance of God is just such a thing. The remembrance of God is a weapon in our hand against the satans; the remembrance of God is a rope at our disposal, for rescue from the chasm which the enemies have brought into being before us, against our awareness. The remembrance of God is a very precious and valuable thing.

> "They remember God" — when they do some wrongful deed or wrong their own selves, they fall to remembering God — "and seek forgiveness for their sins" — then they seek forgiveness and pardon for their sins, they come seeking that this sin, this void, may find healing; but even this is not possible without the help of the Lord.

> "And who forgives sins except God?" — and who grants forgiveness and pardon for sins except God? The striving is from you and the movement is from you, and the blessing is from God; the striving and the effort are from us, and the acceptance is from our God. So we have no right to delete the striving from our own record.

> "And they do not persist knowingly in what they have done" — they do not persist in what they know to be a sin.

> "Those — their reward is forgiveness from their Lord" — such people, who do not desist from striving and effort, who seek forgiveness for their sins, who do not persist on the way of error and wrong — the reward of such people is forgiveness from the side of their Lord —

> **Qurʾān, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:136):** "And gardens beneath which rivers flow" — and gardens beneath which rivers flow — "abiding therein eternally; and excellent is the reward of the workers" — they abide therein forever, and how excellent is the reward of those who work. Here again it says: the reward of the workers, "the reward of the workers." The matter of deeds is one of the very important matters.

(In this series of Islamic teachings, which God willing I intend to set forth and expound in order, in these Qurʾānic discussions and these recitations, through to the end of the month of Ramaḍān, one of them will be devoted to this very matter of deeds.)

---

*\* In the booklet "The General Outline of Islamic Thought in the Qurʾān" (Ṭarḥ-i kullī-yi andīsha-yi islāmī dar Qurʾān), which the author arranged at the conclusion of the sessions, this section was placed in place of the sessions "Faith 1" and "Faith 2." So that this content should not be overlooked, an image of the pages of that book has been included at the end of this session. The text of the other mimeographed copies corresponds with the text given in the booklet.*

---

[^s1-1]: (r-h-b) perpetual fear; *rahbāniyyah*: seclusion and renunciation of the world.
[^s1-2]: Sūrat al-Ḥadīd (al-Mubārakah) / verse 27.
[^s1-3]: A man of heart came to the school from the monastery / and broke the covenant of fellowship with the people of the Path. / I said, "Between scholar and ascetic, what was the difference, / that you chose this party over that?" / He said, "The one carries his own blanket out of the waves, / while this one strives to lay hold of the drowning man." (Saʿdī)
[^s1-4]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (al-Mubārakah) / verses 130 to 132.
[^s1-5]: Plural of *ḥabr*: the learned ones (rabbis).
[^s1-6]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah (al-Mubārakah) / verse 18.
[^s1-7]: (m-y-z) separated out, distinguished.
[^s1-8]: Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (al-Mubārakah) / verse 65.
[^s1-9]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (al-Mubārakah) / verse 133.
[^s1-10]: The utmost of desires (*ghāyat al-āmāl*).
[^s1-11]: (ḥ-ṭ-ṭ) one who becomes degraded, who is brought low.
[^s1-12]: Sūrat Ṭāhā (al-Mubārakah) / verse 82.
[^s1-13]: "Surely I am the Forgiver of one who repents and believes and does righteous work and is guided to the straight path."
[^s1-14]: The women of Paradise, drawn from the verses of the Qurʾān, among them verse 54 of Sūrat al-Dukhān (al-Mubārakah): "…and We shall wed them to fair maidens with large, lovely eyes" — and We give the maidens of Paradise to them in marriage.
[^s1-15]: Bird-meat, alluding to verse 21 of Sūrat al-Wāqiʿah (al-Mubārakah): "And the flesh of birds, of whatever they desire" — and any bird-meat they may desire.
[^s1-16]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (al-Mubārakah) / verses 134 to 136.
[^s1-17]: Sūrat al-Kahf (al-Mubārakah) / verses 103 and 104: "Say: Shall We inform you of the greatest losers in deeds? They are those whose effort in the life of this world has gone to waste, while they suppose that they are doing good work."
[^s1-18]: Five *rials*.
[^s1-19]: Sūrat al-Fatḥ (al-Mubārakah) / verse 29.
[^s1-20]: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (al-Mubārakah) / verse 201.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 2: Faith (2)

*ایمان (۲) — delivered Friday, 29 Shahrīvar 1353 / 3 Ramaḍān al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anfāl (8:2–4):** "The believers are only those who, when God is mentioned, their hearts tremble; and when His signs are recited to them, it increases them in faith; and upon their Lord they rely; those who keep up the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them. Those are the true believers; for them are ranks with their Lord, and forgiveness, and a generous provision."

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anfāl (8:1):** "They ask you — O Prophet — about the spoils (al-anfāl)…"[^s2-1]

They are asking you, briefly, about the anfāl — for whom are the anfāl, and to whom do they rightfully belong? The anfāl can be translated this way: the anfāl means the wealth that belongs to the generality of the Muslims. One example is the income that falls to the Muslims in war — not every income, but specific incomes. Another example of the anfāl is the mines, this underground wealth. Another example of the anfāl is the forests. Another example of the anfāl is these vast plains and grazing lands that lie here and there and upon the mountains. In short, the anfāl refers to those forms of wealth that do not belong to any one particular individual or to any one particular group, but are at the disposal of everyone — belonging to the whole nation. One example of these is, of course — as has been mentioned — the particular spoils that fall to the warriors of the Muslim army in war.

The first time this issue was raised for the Muslims was, it seems, in the Battle of Badr. The Muslims were debating among themselves: to whom do these spoils belong? A disagreement arose among them. They referred the matter to the Holy Prophet, they asked that noble one, and the verse of the Qur'an gave the answer. God inspires His Prophet: **"Say: the anfāl belong to God and the Messenger."**[^s2-1] The anfāl belong to God. What does "belonging to God" mean? It means that they are not for any specific, designated group of God's servants, and they belong to the Prophet. To belong to God means that whatever should bear God's name upon it — this is in reality that which must be expended for divine aims, for divine goals. It is clear that the "wealth of God" is not spent for God's own needs; God Almighty has no such interests, no need, no want that His wealth should be expended to meet that need. What belongs to God is in reality the wealth of God's servants. It must be expended and spent in the channels of welfare that God Almighty has appointed. So *māl-Allāh* — the anfāl belonging to God — means this.

**"And the Messenger"** — what does this mean? Does the Messenger stand as a pole opposite God? No. Anyone might think himself entitled to dispose of God's wealth. Everyone might say, "This is God's wealth, and we too are God's servants" — and under this pleasant-looking, fine-seeming name, under this attractive, popular slogan that "this wealth is God's wealth and we too are God's servants," they might expend the public wealth on their own personal expenses. Such a thing is possible. Therefore the wealth of God, although it must reach the welfare of the generality of Muslims and the true servants of God, yet its reaching the welfare of the generality does not mean a free-for-all[^s2-2] — that everyone spends whatever his heart desires. There must be a central authority, a powerful hand that is reckoned the representative of God and the guardian (qayyim) of the people's affairs. Who is he? He is the Messenger. The Messenger here is not raised in his capacity as messengership and prophethood, but in his capacity as divine governance. That is, when the Messenger departs from this world, the Imam has full charge of the anfāl. The Imam means the divine ruler. In the era when the Infallible Imam is not governing the people, the one who, on God's behalf, can and must govern the people — he has full charge of the anfāl and the public wealth.

In any case, even though this wealth is public — that is, the mines belong to everyone, the forests and reed-beds are for everyone, the grazing lands are for everyone, the spoils obtained from the enemy are for everyone, the royal treasures (ṣawāfī al-mulūk)[^s2-3] are for everyone, and all the like cases and instances cited for the anfāl — even though it is for everyone, in the end it must rest at the disposal of a powerful divine authority, who is none other than the Islamic ruler. Who is that? In the time of the Prophet, the Messenger of God; after him, the Infallible Imam; and if there is no Infallible Imam, the just divine leader — the one in whose hand the reins of the Islamic government must lie. He has authority over the matter of the anfāl. This is the first part of the verse, which of course was not the part under our discussion.

After it appoints the use of the anfāl, it says: **"So fear God, and set right the relations between you, and obey God and His Messenger, if you are believers."** If you are believers, do these three things. First, the fear of God — **"fear God"** — so be wary of God. In the necessarily approximate Persian translation we have given on this sheet[^s2-4]: **"fear God,"** so be wary of God — this is one. **"And set right the relations between you"** — set right what is between you. Remove the disputes from among yourselves.
