In favor of truth, let those who speak something other than the truth give up their words; remove the disputes from among yourselves. **"And set right the relations between you"** — set right what is between you; do not fall upon each other over trivial things; do not seek pretexts to fall upon one another. The characteristic of a people who delight in quarreling with friends and with enemies alike is precisely this: they go looking for small pretexts to fight, to dispute, and to create discord. The counsel of God and the Messenger to people of this sort is this: instead of seeking pretexts to fight with your friends, do not conceal the pretexts for fighting with your enemies. If you are a person of war, then fight the enemy — but why your brother? Why your friend? **"And set right the relations between you."**

These two are great counsels. The third counsel is a matter that is general and encompasses all good deeds and abstention from all evil deeds: **"and obey God and His Messenger"** — and obey, take the command of, God and His Messenger, **"if you are believers."**

The matter of faith — this inclination of the heart, this intellectual, doctrinal, and psychological attachment to a matter, to a person, to a pole, to a center, which is named "faith" — is not summed up merely in the heart's having this inclination, this leaning, and this attachment. Faith truly exists in a person only when he acts in accordance with that faith. A person can claim to be a true believer only when he is bound to the requirements of faith and the commitments of faith. A person can say "I believe in God and am convinced of Him" only when his life, and the very reality of his being, differs from that of one who denies God. The one who today denies God — how does his deed differ from the deeds of those who claim to believe in God? Both commit oppression. Both are drowned up to the throat in materiality. Both, for the sake of these few breaths of life, a few more days of breathing and a couple more morsels to eat and fouling the air, for a couple more days of comfortable living, are ready to trample every virtue underfoot. Except that this one openly says, "I am not convinced of God," while the other claims that he is convinced of God. What kind of faith is this? The verse of the Qur'an here is explicit. Here there is no longer the reckoning of rational arguments that admit of doubt; you must do these deeds, if you are believers. And one of these deeds is **"obey God and the Messenger."**[^s2-5] Obey God and the Prophet; set out in the path of following their command.

What is the command of God? Whatever it is, it is evident that God, concerning a person's wealth, concerning a person's life, concerning a person's lifespan, concerning the relations of a human with other humans, concerning the relations of a human with God, concerning the relations of a human with animals and even with plants, has appointed obligations, responsibilities, duties, and tasks. In this too, if you obeyed God, you can say "we are believers." Otherwise, faith — by the mere fact that a person's heart and soul are attached to some pole, while the radiance of this attachment is not reflected at all in action, in the hand, in the tongue, in the foot, and in the other limbs and members — is of no use; rather, the very name of faith, in the view of Islam, does not hold true for him. This is the logic of the Qur'an.

**"Fear God"** — so be wary of God; **"and set right the relations between you"** — set right what is between you; **"and obey God and His Messenger"** — obey God and His Messenger; **"if you are believers."**

Now, on the occasion of the name "believer" at the end of this verse, the next verse continues the discourse concerning the attributes of the believers and the conditions of faith:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anfāl (8:2–4):** "The believers are only those who, when God is mentioned, their hearts tremble; and when His signs are recited to them, it increases them in faith; and upon their Lord they rely; those who keep up the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them. Those are the true believers."[^s2-6]

For the true believer, five traits are appointed here. It may be that these five traits are not present in the speaker and the listener; but if a person makes some effort and exertion toward bringing these five traits into being within himself, he is still on the path of faith, on the path of the goal of faith, and worthy of the name "believer." The true believer is the one in whom these five traits are present.

First: **"The believers are only" (innamā al-muʾminūn)** — exclusively, the believers; **"those who" (alladhīna)** are the ones who, **"when God is mentioned" (idhā dhukira llāh)** — when God is remembered, **"their hearts tremble" (wa-jilat qulūbuhum)** — their hearts come into fear. What does "to come into fear" mean? What does fearing God mean? Is it like a sinner before a judge, or is it another and subtler kind? Someone might say, "I have committed no sin, so I do not fear God." Yes, the fearing of a sinner before the judge and the prosecutor — the one who must punish the man — that kind of fear is, of course, ruled out by the absence of sin; but who is the person who can be certain that he has no sin?

But there is another kind of fear, and it arises from knowledge (maʿrifah). In the face of great things, mighty essences, majestic realities, a human inevitably feels awe[^s2-7] and bewilderment. This is the property of the human being's existence; the spiritual and bodily composition of the human necessitates it. In the face of anything of grandeur, every human feels a state of awe within himself. This state of awe is not from the standpoint that he fears that being — it may be that he has no fear at all, in the sense of any aggression[^s2-8] — the fear here is not the fear that arises from sin; rather this awe and dread arises from the sense of His grandeur and the sense of one's own smallness before Him. Such a fear of God is fitting, is desirable, is necessary, is beneficial. The one who sees himself, before the Lord, as small and deficient and lowly, and beholds God as dominant, all-encompassing, and overpowering (muhaymin, musayṭir)[^s2-9] over every aspect of his affairs — such a person strives not to travel by any path other than that straight line of conduct which the God of the world has appointed for him, and not to move otherwise. This is the greatest guarantor of the carrying-out of action, movement, and effort in a Muslim person and in a Muslim society.

This that you see — the Commander of the Faithful, in the depths of the night, in the nights of the month of Ramadan, writhing upon himself and shedding tears; this that you see — Imam Sajjād at times crying out; this that you see — the Messenger of God, with all his majesty and grandeur, when the last ten days of the month of Ramadan come, saying, "Roll up my bedding," not spreading any bed for himself — that is, the nights are no longer the time for sleep; they are the time for worship, the time for supplication, the time for humility before the Lord — do not imagine that these are contrived acts being performed. How deficient and unaware is the one who says that the Imam, in the Duʿā of Abū Ḥamzah, wishes to teach the people something and is not speaking of himself. How untutored and uninformed of the spirit of supplication and of the manner of the intimate communion of God's righteous servants with God is the one who imagines that the weeping and lamentation and tears of the Imam, peace be upon him, are for the sake of instruction. That is, did they weep artificially so that you and I would learn? This is a mistake. They truly shed tears. Why did they shed tears? Because their knowledge of God is greater. In the sacred being of the Lord, the Commander of the Faithful sees a grandeur that these near-sighted eyes of yours and mine cannot see. "You are great, and do not show forth in a small mirror."[^s2-10] This small mirror of our soul cannot reflect within itself that wondrous grandeur; but what of the mirror of the soul of the Commander of the Faithful? He can; he comprehends this grandeur, he grasps it, he perceives it — and it is for this very reason that he writhes upon himself like that and sheds tears.

When the remembrance of God comes into the midst, that state of awe, that state of reverent fear (khashyah), that sense of fear and dread, that state of terror that arises from the sense of one's own smallness before the grandeur of God, takes hold of the believer's heart: **"when God is mentioned, their hearts tremble."** God ceases, for a person, to be a plaything; the name of God ceases to be a kind of habitual incantation. In a gathering a person sits down and says "yā Allāh" as a mere phrase of respect; he yawns and finishes it off with "lā ilāha illā Allāh," out of weariness; from these states that are the sign of indifference and lack of perception and the absence of sensitivity in a person's soul before the remembrance of God and the name of God and the grandeur of God — from these forms the heart of a God-knowing, grandeur-perceiving human departs, one who comprehends and feels the grandeur of the Lord. **"The believers are only those who, when God is mentioned, their hearts tremble."** The believers are those who, when God is remembered, their hearts come into trembling (wajal) — into fear and dread and terror, into the sense of grandeur in the Lord and of smallness in themselves; their hearts become filled with this sense. This is one.

**"And when His signs are recited to them, it increases them in faith."** Another sign of the believer is that when the signs (āyāt) of God are recited to them — whether they recite them themselves or someone else recites these signs to them — their faith is increased: **"it increases them in faith."** This faith, like a seed in his heart, in his soul, grows, becomes great, comes up like a plant, becomes — like a tree — sturdy[^s2-11] in stem and root, to the point that the possibility of uprooting it disappears. Faith in the form of stagnant water, within the being of a believer, has no meaning. Those who learned a single word in childhood, and through the doubt of the years of adolescence that one word fell from its firmness and from its grandeur in their souls — it grew small, became deficient, became insignificant; a half-faith, a half-truth, remained in their souls; and that too, through the coming of various currents and events, was continually diminished and diminished. Such a faith too will one day be easily taken from a person. A faith held in trust (mustawdaʿ), a borrowed (ʿāriyat) faith — the believer is not like this. The true believer is the believer who, if a single word of the realities and the religious and divine teachings is in his heart, takes that one word — through reflection, through precision of insight, through thought, through whatever is conducive[^s2-12] to that one word — and makes his faith greater, and rescues this faith from vanishing. This is the true believer.

From this verse and from this sentence we derive that, through the recitation of the Qur'an, the believing person's faith must increase. And from this sentence we adopt a proof — pay attention, from this word of God we adopt a proof — and to those who say "do not translate the Qur'an, do not give its meaning, do not interpret it, for our minds do not reach it," we say: if we could not understand the Qur'an, how is it that our faith increases by the reading of the Qur'an? So it is evident: the Qur'an is not a book of code. The Qur'an is a book that must be read with the intent of understanding, and understood with the intent of increasing and growing stronger in the faith of the believer. We adopt this proof; pay attention.

The continuation of the verse: **"and upon their Lord they rely."** Another sign of the believers is that they rely and depend upon the Lord. **"And when His signs are recited to them, it increases them in faith"** — this is the second condition, the second trait. **"And upon their Lord they rely"** — that is, what does it mean that they rely (tawakkul)? Does it mean they fold their hands together and say, "Let God set it right Himself"? No, the meaning of tawakkul is not this. The one who folds his hands together in the face of the obligations and commitments and responsibilities, and instead of holding his own power responsible, presumes a divine miracle to be responsible — such a man should know that the Qur'an has rejected this deed, in the form of a firm blow to the mouth of the Children of Israel, who said to Moses:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:24):** "Go, you and your Lord, and fight; we will sit here."[^s2-13]

You and your Lord go, busy yourselves with the war, do battle with the enemy; we will sit here — in my own expression, by the wall, in the shade — when you have conquered, then inform us too so that we may come. The Qur'an negates this method. This method is the method of the accursed, banished Children of Israel, far from the road of humanity, deprived of the delights of religion and faith; it is for them, not befitting a Muslim. So tawakkul does not mean this. This saying that has become current among the people — I am saying it so that all may hear and all may understand — this thing that is current among the people, when they say, "Sir, God Himself must set things right; nothing can come from God's servant" — this is wrong. If nothing could come from God's servant, if for the removal and uprooting of corruption the will of man and the power of man were of no avail, God would not have mobilized the prophets. He would not have invited the people to follow the prophets. He would not have sent the divine prophets, with their heavy missions, into the arena of battle in this world. This that you see — that God has sent persons for the uprooting of corruption, and those persons are of the human race — so know that human corruption must be uprooted by humans.

So what does tawakkul mean? Tawakkul means that, in every state, your reliance and your hope are upon God. If you examine carefully, you will see that with this expression which I am stating — that in every state your reliance and hope are upon God — with this expression, tawakkul ceases to be a kind of narcotic and manifests itself as a stirring agent, an agent of dynamism.

You have seen what some people do in the hardships of life, in crises, in the place where their hands fall short of all outward means — have you seen? Have you heard? They do one of several things: either he surrenders to the enemy, saying, "When nothing can come from me, what am I to do?" — surrender to the enemy, this is one. Or he surrenders to the ordinary course of life: he does not go and fall prostrate before the enemy, but in practice he takes up some path and routine, leaves behind some movement and effort, and forgets that he has, in reality, surrendered to the ordinary current of life. Such a man too, although outwardly he has not surrendered to the enemy, has inwardly surrendered to the enemy. This too is a kind — a state that the common run of people adopt in such crises. The other way is to put an end to their own life. For example, that gentleman who, when he comes to power, is harassed from all around by the gamesmanship[^s2-14] of the international powers — every time, every day, they raise some uproar against him from some corner of his country — when he is forced and compelled, when he is wearied, exhausted, made wretched, he puts an end to his own life, he commits suicide.[^s2-15]

These are the paths that present themselves to a Godless person at impasses. When you have reached an impasse, when you have reached a place where it seems to you that this is the end of the alley, that there is no path through, for ordinary people several doors open: surrendering to the enemy — that is, in surrender; surrendering to the natural course of life — that is, in surrender; surrendering to events — that is, in surrender; suicide and self-destruction — destroying oneself and being relieved and at times regret. But for the person with God, another door opens at impasses, a door whose opening shuts upon him the other doors, the doors of self-betrayal of honor. What is that door? It is the door of tawakkul upon God. He says, "Here is an impasse." He says, "The God I know breaks through the impasse too. What is an impasse? In God's view we have no impasses. All impasses, by the hand of God's power, are open at the end! There is a way."

Is there an impasse higher than the Battle of Uḥud? The small army of Islam, in the midst of being occupied with gathering the spoils, is suddenly attacked from two directions, because of the heedlessness of a few soldiers. One group attacks from the front, another group from behind. These men have laid down their swords, have dismounted from their horses, have set their weapons on the ground; suddenly they see two groups attacking them from two sides — armed, savage, enraged, furious. Well, it is evident what an army without weapons and without equipment[^s2-16] does at such times: flight. They fled. Satan, from the throat of the Satan-natured ones, proclaimed that the Prophet too had perished. It is very clear; Satan always does these things. One of Satan's very clear schemes and his exposed hands is this: to proclaim, sooner than its time, the defeat of the divine and merciful front — that, "Yes, you are defeated; the Prophet has passed away." In such a crisis, in such an impasse, what does a believer who relies on God do? Sir, is there an impasse higher than this? The paths of deliverance entirely closed, the weapons fallen on the ground, the enemy equipped, dominant, overpowering — what comes to the aid of a person here is that window which only the God-worshippers possess: reliance upon God. What impasse? Which is the impasse? Suppose the Prophet has passed away — the God of the Prophet has not perished. The duty has not perished. With tawakkul here, the man acts like whom? Like the Commander of the Faithful, ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib; like Abū Dujānah[^s2-17]; like two or three other soldiers — these were people of tawakkul. Who were the ones without tawakkul? Those who, from Uḥud to Medina, did not even look back, but ran all the way to the very gates of the city. Now you have seen what tawakkul means.

The one who pretends that tawakkul is the folding of hands together, sitting without effort in hope of an unknown future, stripping his faith of his own power — the one who pretends that tawakkul means a stroke of cancellation[^s2-18] upon man's will and his power — such a man either does not understand tawakkul (Islam does not get into his head; he does not perceive tawakkul), or else he does understand it, but honor does not get into his head; he wants to misinterpret it deliberately, so that people are raised without tawakkul, so that people are raised wrongly. The meaning of tawakkul is this: **"and upon their Lord they rely."** In my view there are two mighty wings for the flight of man in the efforts of life: one is patience, one is tawakkul. Any ummah that has these two wings will become wholly beyond the range of the earthbound enemies; the wing of patience and the wing of tawakkul: **"and upon their Lord they rely."** These are three signs for the believer. (The worth of faith; what kind of thing is faith? The characteristics concerning faith — these are very necessary discussions that, God willing, if God grants the success, I shall prepare tomorrow, so that they may again make a duplicate and bring it and place it at the disposal of the gentlemen, and they may also find its Qur'anic verses and recite them for you. For now, what is raised today concerning faith is these signs and commitments of faith.)

Third: **"and upon their Lord they rely."** Fourth: **"those who keep up the prayer"** — those who establish the prayer. Gentlemen, pay attention: there is a difference between the expression *alladhīna yuṣallūn* (those who pray) and **"those who keep up the prayer" (alladhīna yuqīmūn al-ṣalāh)**. *Yuṣallūn* means "they perform the prayer." If only the performing of prayer were at issue, if only this getting up and bending down were at issue, here there would have been no need to say **"those who keep up the prayer"** — to bring two words and give them length and interpretation; they could have said *yuṣallūn*, *alladhīna yuṣallūn*, those who pray. It is evident that **"those who keep up the prayer"** — those who establish the prayer — is something other than reciting prayer; it is something else, a higher and loftier reality. In your view, what is this reality? What does "establishing the prayer" mean?

Several possibilities can be supposed, and it may be that all of these possibilities are correct. One is that we say: the establishment of prayer, the keeping up of prayer, means performing the prayer in a complete form, in an all-around form, in a perfect form: **"those who keep up the prayer."** And "establishment" (iqāmah), in the Arabic language, in Arabic usage, is precisely in these very meanings — that is, carrying out the work in a complete form:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Rūm (30:30):** "So set your face toward the religion, devoted purely (to it)."[^s2-19]

Turn the whole face, the whole being, toward religion, in a complete form. This is one possibility. And the truth of it is precisely this: that if a person establishes the prayer in this sense — that is, performs it in a complete form, with correct pillars, with attention to the teachings and inspirations of the prayer (about which I conducted, in that mosque[^s2-20] over the course of several weeks, a relatively detailed discussion on prayer) — if a person prays in this manner, then success and salvation surely await him. A person who prays well — all his difficulties become easy for him. You have heard that some of the great ones of religion, at the time of facing hardships and calamities, would pray two rakʿahs? You have heard that the Messenger of God, at the time of crises and severities, would turn to Bilāl and say, "Comfort us, O Bilāl" (*ariḥnā yā Bilāl*) — Bilāl, ease us; go give the call to prayer. "Cool us, cool us, O Bilāl" (*abrid, abrid yā Bilāl*) — cool our hearts, O Bilāl; that is, give the call to prayer.[^s2-21] Truly, if a person establishes the prayer — with attention, with humility, with reverence, with presence of heart, with comprehension of what he is doing and what he is saying, and with the intent of taking a result from the prayer — whoever prays, if he establishes prayer in this sense, then certainly that which has been promised to the believers will exist for him.

This is one possibility. The other possibility is that **"those who keep up the prayer"** means: they establish prayer in society, they make society a praying society. Some people are content that they themselves pray — instead of seventeen rakʿahs in a day and night, they pray fifty-one rakʿahs, besides the other recommended prayers. If they are told, "Sir, the people are turning away from religion in droves," they feel no grief over it. As the saying goes: "He carries his own rug off the wave."[^s2-22] He says, "Sir, to carry our own rug off is already a great task, a great feat; we cannot get to others." Some are like this. That deed which is the sign of faith is not this deed. For anyone to pray well himself, but pray for himself and have no concern for others — this is not right. It is not right — that is, it is not complete; complete.

What is the sign of faith? The establishment of prayer — keeping up the prayer in society, making everyone a praying people — not in the sense that everyone performs a single act of worship. (I want you to take your mind out of the mold of the words, to think more broadly. I too think in just that way. Words are small and inadequate and deficient.) Not that so-and-so is not a prayer-performer, so let us make him a prayer-performer; rather, a praying society means a society that is always remembering God and on God's path. A society that says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Fātiḥah (1:5):** "You alone do we worship, and from You alone do we seek help."[^s2-23]

A society that worships and serves none but God, and relies upon none but God and seeks help from none but God. A praying society means that society which every day disavows[^s2-24] the ringleaders of corruption — that is, **"those upon whom is wrath (al-maghḍūbi ʿalayhim)"** — and the followers of corruption — that is, **"those who go astray (al-ḍāllīn)."** This is their prayer.

If a person strives to make other human beings into prayer-performers in this sense, he has in reality made an effort and exertion on the path of the absolute servitude of God, on the path of uprooting corruption, on the path of doing away with "me" and "us," on the path of bringing about social and human unity among the individuals of the Muslim ummah and the individuals of humanity — an effort on these paths; this is what the establishment of prayer means. That is, you do something so that all the people, every day, five times, and each time several times, say **"You alone do we worship, and from You alone do we seek help."** O God! Only before You do we bend the neck of servitude and the back of servitude — not before any other than You. Five times every day, and each time several times, from **"those upon whom is wrath"** and from **"those who go astray"** — that is, from the ringleaders of corruption and from their followers — we disavow, we declare our innocence; we make such a society. The establishment of prayer may also have this meaning.

**"And spend out of what We have provided them" (wa mimmā razaqnāhum yunfiqūn).** What is another sign of the believers? It is that they spend (infāq) out of what We have provided them. What does infāq mean? (Yesterday I mentioned it by way of allusion. Up to now I have explained the meaning of infāq perhaps ten times on the occasion of various discussions — this time too, let it be the eleventh.) Infāq means filling voids and needs. That spending which fills a void, which meets a need — this is called infāq. The wall of this mosque has been freshly painted; if you came and gave money and painted it over again, that is not infāq. Why? Because there was no need for this work; there was paint already here. Or if there was no paint, but it was a place that had no need of paint — if you came to the dirt floors of the Imam Ḥasan Mosque and sprinkled sums of money in paint, and said, "I spent for the Imam Ḥasan Mosque; we took several boxes and cans of paint to the mosque and used them up — what did you do, sir? Over the clods and the dust and the mud, we painted it all" — yes, this is spending, but it is not infāq. Infāq consists of there being a void and a need in the very fabric of society, and your filling that void and need; infāq means this.

**"And spend out of what We have provided them"** — what does it mean? It means that the believers are those who, out of what We have provided them — the word "wealth" is not in it; out of the moneys We gave them? No; it makes no difference — out of the money We gave, out of the lifespan We gave, out of the child We gave, out of the reputation We gave, out of the bodily strength We gave, out of the tongue We gave, out of the thought and brain We gave, out of all the resources We gave; He said **"what We have provided them"** — out of everything We have provided them, what do they do? **"They spend."** They make infāq. That is, they spend it in its place — not just any spending. This is one of the signs of the believer.

O brother named "believer," O believer-seeming brother, O brother who, if you are called "believer," are pleased — if someone shows the slightest wavering and doubt in your faith, you take offense, and it may even be that you say, "What benefit is there that you count us as irreligious and faithless in your talk?" — O brother named "believer," do you make infāq? I do not say, do you spend or not; yes, you spend a great deal. How much money do you give in these days of the month of Ramadan, you cook delicious food, you spread a colorful spread and invite the well-fed; how much you spend — but do you also make infāq?

O dear speaker, how much you talk, how much breath you spend, how much from your chest and your lungs and your being, from your body and your nerves, you draw upon; how much of the power of your speech you spend — but do you also make infāq out of this power? To speak much is no feat; to say what is needed, that is a feat. That is not infāq; this is infāq. O you who spend out of your reputations, out of your standing — you write a letter to someone, you go to see someone for some affair, you arrange that some deed be carried out, that some job be given to so-and-so, that some fitting respect be paid by such-and-such a person to so-and-so — you are spending your standing, but what of infāq? Do you also make infāq? O you who spend money in various places, in the name of religion and in the name of other than religion — yes, sometimes a person spends money in the name of religion while he has not made infāq — do not take fright at this saying, for it is a reality, and what a bitter reality! How much money is expended and spent in the name of religion, but it is not infāq, because it does not fill a void, because it does not bring a pain to its cure, because it does not remove a need from this needy society. That which is the substance of being a believer, that which is the condition and sign of faith, is infāq: **"and spend out of what We have provided them."** After this, if you wish to spend time, if you wish to lay down your reputation, if you wish to spend money, think well; see whether, by this expenditure of yourself, you are making infāq, or you are spending pointlessly.

**"Those are the true believers"** — these are the true believers. **"For them are ranks with their Lord."** For them are ranks — degrees, ranks, stations — **"with their Lord."** With their Lord. **"And forgiveness (maghfirah)"** — and forgiveness too is among these. The very forgiveness that I explained for you yesterday — I hope it has remained completely in your mind and memory and stays there. If God intends to give someone forgiveness, He intends to heal a wound that has arisen in someone's soul from sin; He will heal the wound of sin of such persons. **"And forgiveness and a generous provision (rizq karīm)"** — and an honorable provision, a generous provision, without abasement, without hardship, without disgrace. Such a provision God gives them.

Wherever you find a believing society — I am not speaking of an individual; do not say, "By God's grace, we obtain a morsel of bread, we are comfortable too, we have no grief, we are beholden to no one." If you look carefully through the inner workings of his affairs, you see that it is all wretchedness and obligation. All those who have the most disgraceful occupations do not feel that this deed they have is disgraceful and that this morsel of bread is a morsel of disgrace — I am speaking of a society. A society's morsel of bread is pure, honorable, joined with pride and dignity; it receives a noble and honorable provision only when it is believing and possessed of these attributes. If it were this way, all those slogans that the political parties of the world raise today, here and there, behind the loudspeakers, rightly or falsely, among the nations of the world — all those slogans would be realized in a society of faith. Peace, freedom, welfare, comfort, without war, without bloodshed, with brotherhood, with sincerity, a high level of culture, a high level of literacy, a high level of provision — and all these words and verbiage, some of which have meaning beneath them and some of which have no meaning beneath them — taken all together are realizable in a society of faith. **"And a generous provision"** — and a generous and magnanimous provision.

(These are the four verses that we have explained. Mr. Riḍāʾī, our dear reciter today, is present — let him now rise and come here and recite these few verses. However, since it is Friday and you gentlemen probably have no work, occupation, or program, and can sit, we may request that he add two or three more verses and recite them. Concerning faith, at the foot of this sheet I have written something — this duplicated sheet that is in the gentlemen's hands; please pay attention, I am still speaking — this mimeographed sheet that we gave to our friends, those who have it: here I have written below it, I have written that concerning faith attention should be paid to these matters, and I mentioned several matters that I will speak about again — from the Qur'an, I will conduct a Qur'anic discussion like today's discussion. Concerning these very matters that we wrote here — the consciousness of faith, its not being fanatical and imitative; let me say, the realities and effects of faith, for example, the practical commitments of faith of the sort that we read today — concerning these, God willing, on another day I think we shall speak about faith and these subjects and these discussions, until afterward, God willing, we reach the discussions that follow upon faith.)

[^s2-1]: Sūrat al-Anfāl, verse 1.
[^s2-2]: A state of chaos and disorder in which no one thinks of anyone else.
[^s2-3]: (ṣ-f-w) A kind of spoils comprising the precious and valuable objects of kings — movable property — that fall to the Muslims in war. This word is used in contrast to *qaṭāʾiʿ al-mulūk*, the lands and immovable property of kings.
[^s2-4]: Refer to the mimeograph at the end of the session.
[^s2-5]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, verses 32, 132.
[^s2-6]: Sūrat al-Anfāl, verses 2 to 4.
[^s2-7]: (d-h-sh) Bewilderment, fear.
[^s2-8]: (ʿ-r-ḍ) Stretching out the hand (i.e., transgressing).
[^s2-9]: (m-h-m) One who gives security, a guardian; (s-ṭ-r) a protector and guardian — this word, according to the rules of the Arabic language, is also written *muṣayṭir*.
[^s2-10]: "You are great, and do not show forth in a small mirror / I marvel at that head which, like your tresses, falls at your feet." (Shahriyār)
[^s2-11]: Thick, firm, and steadfast.
[^s2-12]: (s-ʿ-d) Helpful, agreeable.
[^s2-13]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah, verse 24.
[^s2-14]: A figure of speech for making flimsy excuses, fabricating pretexts, putting off from day to day.
[^s2-15]: (n-ḥ-r) Self-killing.
[^s2-16]: Equipment, gear and means.
[^s2-17]: Sammāk ibn Kharashah, known as Abū Dujānah, one of the great companions, who after the passing of the Prophet was martyred in the year 11 AH in the Battle of Yamāmah. His valor and self-sacrifice in the Battle of Uḥud were such that the fourth verse of Sūrat al-Ṣaff was revealed concerning his standing and that of great ones such as the Commander of the Faithful and Ḥamzah. In a narration, Imam Ṣādiq has mentioned him among the commanders of the army of Imam Mahdī who, after the appearance of that noble one, will return (in the rajʿah).
[^s2-18]: (n-s-kh) Annulling something.
[^s2-19]: Sūrat al-Rūm, verse 30.
[^s2-20]: The Karāmat Mosque in holy Mashhad.
[^s2-21]: Hadith no. 35.
[^s2-22]: Saʿdī. (The full line: "He carries his own rug off the wave" — i.e., he saves only himself.)
[^s2-23]: Sūrat al-Fātiḥah, verse 5.
[^s2-24]: (b-r-ʾ) To keep away from and seek disavowal.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 3: Faith Through Awareness

*ایمان از روی آگاهی — delivered Saturday, 30 Shahrivar 1353 / 4 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:190–191):** "Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth, and in the alternation of night and day, there are signs for those endowed with understanding — those who remember God standing and sitting and lying upon their sides, and who reflect upon the creation of the heavens and the earth, [saying:] 'Our Lord, You did not create this in vain. Glory be to You! Protect us, then, from the punishment of the Fire.'"

In light of the several verses we are reciting today — those written on that sheet — two or three further truths concerning faith come to hand.

Faith is one of the outstanding traits of God's prophets, of the believers, and of those who follow them: to have faith, to believe, to be convinced of one's own mission. Herein lies the difference between divine leaders and the political leaders of the world. The divine leader, like one who himself treads the road, is sincerely a believer with his whole being — in what he says, in every step he takes, in the path he walks. Whereas the politicians of the world may at times have beautiful words and charming, eloquent speech, yet they do not have the requisite faith — or even any faith at all — in what they say.

It has been related of the leaders of one of the greatest countries of the Eastern Bloc[^s3-1] — with all their atheistic pretensions, and with a culture and a school of thought that severely condemns every non-materialist idea — that after the independence of India, in order to win the hearts of the millions of the newly independent Indian masses, on a journey they made to the land of India, the Hindus and the Indians generally looked on in utter astonishment, for they saw upon the foreheads of the leaders of this communist country the mark of Tilak. Tilak — or Tilak — was one of the freedom-loving leaders of India who was a clergyman. He was one of the great Hindu holy men, who was reckoned, for a span of time, the chief and forerunner of the Indian revolution. It is not amiss for me to mention this to you in the margin of these remarks — though of course it has no bearing on the subject I am discussing, yet it is not bad to know — that the Indian movement, which from the day of its blossoming to the day of its fruition lasted a full ninety years, had as its principal leaders, from beginning to end, the clergy. Either Muslim clergy such as Mawlānā Shāh Maḥmūd Dihlawī, Mawlānā Maḥmūd al-Ḥasan,[^s3-2] Mawlānā Abū al-Kalām Āzād,[^s3-3] Mawlānā Muḥammad ʿAlī, Mawlānā Shawkat ʿAlī[^s3-4] — for *Mawlānā* means *Āyatullāh*; *Mawlānā*, in the usage of the Muslims of India, is something equivalent to the rank of "Ḥaḍrat Āyatullāh" that we have. Āyatullāh Muḥammad ʿAlī, Āyatullāh Shawkat ʿAlī, Āyatullāh Abū al-Kalām, Āyatullāh Maḥmūd al-Ḥasan — these "Āyatullāhs" were the great leaders of the Indian movement on the part of the Muslims. On the part of the Hindus, Mahatma Gandhi was a Hindu holy man, a Jain holy man, attached to the school of Jainism.[^s3-5]

Let me say that Tilak was a Hindu holy man of most astonishing greatness. He was a very remarkable man, this Tilak — who of course died in the midst of the movement, before its outcome was reached; he passed from this world and did not see the freedom of India. Yet thirty, forty years after his death, those whom he had nurtured tasted the sweet and pleasant drink of freedom, and a mark called the mark of Tilak remained behind, in memory of that great freedom-loving man who was a clergyman. This mark is the mark of a holy man; there is an aspect of spirituality and the spiritual in it, a metaphysical aspect to it — because Tilak himself was a person who possessed spiritual stations in the view of the Hindus. They saw that the materialist leaders of the Soviet Union, who had now come to India, in order to attract the attention of the masses of the people, drew the mark of Tilak upon their foreheads — meaning: yes, we too; meaning, we too are in agreement with you in this matter — even though their own pretensions belied this statement, and their own school of thought belies it.

Such is the standing of a number of leaders who are politicians remote from spiritual responsibilities, from faith, and from the divine. But the prophets of God — no; they have believed in what they have said with their whole being. To what they have summoned the people, they themselves, before all others, have come hastening, eager. It makes no sense for me to recline and sleep at the foot of the mountain, letting my whole being wilt and grow cold, and then say to you, "Gentlemen, up there at the top of the mountain there is delicious water; run, go, hurry, be swift, *sāriʿū* (vie with one another), *sābiqū* (outstrip one another)" — while I myself stir not from here. Everyone would have the right to say: if you were telling the truth, if you really knew of the existence of a refreshing spring, you yourself who are burning with thirst, you poor wretch, would have set out yourself! So you are lying; so you do not believe in what you are saying. The divine leaders, before all others — like the vanguards of a road, banner in hand, foot on the path, firm — were busy moving forward and went on; they themselves were ahead of everyone. Abraham, the Friend of the All-Merciful, says: the first who submitted [in Islam] and yielded before You, O Lord, was I myself; after me came the other people, the rest of God's creation following me. This is the characteristic of divine leaders. The divine leader must be like this, and is like this. The Most Noble Prophet, in the most important and most dangerous events of early Islam, was himself there. It is true that ʿAbdullāh ibn Masʿūd[^s3-6] was beaten, it is true that Khabbāb[^s3-7] was tortured, it is true that ʿAmmār Yāsir[^s3-8] fell under torture — but the torture of the Prophet himself was no less than theirs; it was greater. If you compare the situation of the Prophet — that man who staked his all — with the situation of these Muslims around him, you will see that the severity exercised against the Messenger of God himself was greater and more grueling than against them; first to step forward, first to advance.

Faith is one of the characteristics found in the prophets of God; and here is acceptance with one's whole being, belief in what one says. Faith means belief, acceptance, and reception with one's whole being — belief in what one says. And the sign of belief is precisely this: that one oneself, ahead of others, takes a step on that road and moves forward. Thus the verse of the Qur'an teaches us in this way; it says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:285):** "The Messenger has believed in what was sent down to him from his Lord, and so have the believers. Each one has believed in God, His angels, His scriptures, and His messengers. [They say:] 'We make no distinction between any of His messengers.' And they say: 'We hear and we obey. [Grant us] Your forgiveness, our Lord, and to You is the [final] return.'"

*Āmana al-Rasūl bimā unzila ilayhi min Rabbihi*[^s3-9] — the Prophet has believed in what was sent down to him from his Lord. *Wa al-muʾminūn* — and the believers, those who came to faith in him, those outstanding individuals who gathered around him and became the loudspeakers of his summons, are likewise so. *Kullun āmana billāh* — each one, or every one of them, believed in God, accepted God, came to believe in Him; *wa malāʾikatihi* — and the angels of God; *wa kutubihi* — and the heavenly scriptures from beginning to end, entirely; *wa rusulihi* — and all His messengers. The faith of the Prophet and his companions is in all of them, for the road is one road —

> In this path the prophets are like caravan-guides;
> they are the guides and signposts of the caravan.[^s3-10]

All are caravan-guides of a single caravan, the caravan-leaders of a single road, the guides toward a single goal; with the people everywhere, all have been fellow travelers, charging forward toward a single destination. *Lā nufarriqu bayna aḥadin min rusulih* — this is from the tongue of the believers — we make no distinction between any of His prophets. For us Jesus is honored, just as Moses, just as Abraham, just as Idrīs,[^s3-11] just as Jacob, just as Jirjīs (St. George),[^s3-12] just as Noah, just as all the prophets from beginning to end. All were envoys of God, envoys of a single goal, heralds of a single felicity and a single paradise, all upon a single line. *Lā nufarriqu bayna aḥadin min rusulih* — we make no distinction between any of His messengers. (In the discussion I will, God willing, give on other days within this very series concerning the belief in prophethood, in its own time and turn, I may also touch upon this question.)

*Wa qālū* — pay careful attention to these two or three sentences, for they are a point of evidence for a matter I shall speak of later. So the prophets have faith, our Prophet too has it, and the believers and adherents and those attached to him also — all have faith. *Wa qālū* — and they say: *samiʿnā* — we heard, we understood, we hearkened[^s3-13]; *samiʿnā* — not that it merely fell upon our ears. *Samʿ*, *samaʿ*, means hearing — the state of hearing, which is other than the ear; the ear, in the sense of that particular bodily organ, is expressed in Arabic by the word *udhun*. *Samʿ* means hearing. You see this in Persian too: they say of so-and-so that he is "obedient" (literally, "ear-heeding"), that so-and-so hears what is said. "Did you hear what I said, or not?" you say to your interlocutor — "Did you hear what I said?" Well, it is obvious; there was no more than half a meter between you, your voice would carry even ten meters; it is obvious that he heard. What you mean is: "Did you understand what I said? Did it sink into your brain? Did it enter your mind or not?" These [believers] say *samiʿnā* — we understood with our whole being what God had appointed and sent for us. *Wa aṭaʿnā* — and we obeyed. What does that mean? It means our obedience was not blind; our obedience was out of clarity and awareness and *samʿ* (true hearing). *Ghufrānaka Rabbanā* — Your forgiveness, Your pardon, O our Lord. The reward we ask of You, O God, is Your forgiveness and nothing else. *Wa ilayka al-maṣīr* — and to You is our return. (Here again, concerning the matter of the return to God, there are very interesting things which, God willing, I will explain within this very series in the section on the Resurrection [maʿād] from the Qur'an — if we are alive and there is life and opportunity.[^s3-14])

What have we understood thus far from these verses? This point: that faith and belief are the characteristic of those attached to the summons of Islam. People who have no faith, no belief, who merely go along because others went — going, as it were, just to be on the safe side — these are not within the domain and realm of Islamic thought; there need be no awkward reticence about it. Faith is necessary. Faith means belief, accepting and receiving the matter in full clarity; it means moving in pursuit of an attraction. If this attraction be not present in the school of religion and the Qur'an, and has not captured your heart — that is, if faith be not in this heart — then this heart is dead; it is not alive with the light of Islam; it cannot be called a *Muslim*. So faith is necessary. This is the first point.

The second point (which we marked with a "2" on these sheets[^s3-15]) is that faith is of two kinds. One kind of faith is imitative, prejudiced (fanatical): because our fathers and elders had believed, we too believed; because in our book, in the domain of our religion, they say so, we too say so — even if you bring proof, you have brought it in vain; we do not accept what you say. This too is a kind of faith, but it is a faith that is either out of imitation or out of prejudice — it is of two kinds. Either it is out of imitation, like the common run of people: if you ask one of the common folk, "Sir, on what basis did you say that the Prophet of Islam is true?" they know nothing at all — nothing at all! Because their fathers said so, because the teacher at school said so, because the people in the street and bazaar say the Prophet is true, this person too says the Prophet is true. He does have faith, mind you; that is, he truly does believe that the Prophet is true — but this belief was obtained from the tongue of this person and that, out of imitation, with eyes closed.

Like this imitative faith is prejudiced (fanatical) faith — by which some people are prepared, God forbid, to disrespect the other prophets, for the sake of pleasing our own Prophet! They imagine that even in the highest heavens there is contradiction among the prophets: "Moses! Who was Moses, man — our Prophet!" And unfortunately this is not peculiar to the ignorant class. Sometimes among classes from whom one has greater expectations — from whom one expects that they should be more understanding, should have grasped more — errors of this kind are seen, examples of which we have at times noticed here and there, and there is no need to recount them. This is what is called prejudice: because the religion of Islam says so, this is right; and because the other religions say it, it is wrong. Because we Muslims perform such-and-such an act, this is right; because others perform some other act, this is wrong. He has a faith, but this faith does not rest upon proof; it is only out of prejudice. And you know what prejudice means: partisanship without proof, partisanship out of feeling, not out of logic. This is called *prejudice*. This faith is imitative and prejudiced.

I must say, for your attention, my friends, that the faith which has worth in Islam is not imitative and prejudiced faith; that is, imitative and prejudiced faith has no value. You want proof? Of dozens of proofs let me state one, and that one proof is this: that when faith is out of imitation and prejudice, its loss too is easy. Just as the child, without any effort of its own, and the pupil, without any striving of its own, received a faith for free from its father and mother or from the authorities of its school, in the very same way the thieves of faith take this faith from him for free. And suddenly, when you look, there is a faithless generation, the faiths all plundered, gone, the faiths all become hollow; before the flame of materialism, the faiths have melted like snow in the dead of summer and seeped into the ground. Whom am I speaking of? It would be very ordinary and commonplace for me to say "the young generation," but I must say that I do not say that the faithless generation is the young generation; because if we are still observing here and there the blossoms of faith, as a glimmer, a flash, it is in the young generation that we are observing it — bravo once again to the young generation, who seek a faith that is non-imitative, non-prejudiced, and out of awareness. No indeed; I mean to speak of the generation *before* the young — this generation before our society's youth, these who hold to certain things in the form of blind faiths. Of course such a one goes to the *rawḍah*-recitation gathering, of course he goes to congregational prayer if the chance arises; but because it is not out of logic, because it is not out of awareness and understanding and perception, he is very readily prepared to set even the mosque on fire, to trample even Imam Husayn underfoot, and to sacrifice all of these for the thing that has real worth in his eyes — just as they do, just as we see them do. And one of our misfortunes today is the confrontation with the generation before today's young. Those who were deprived of the special awareness and clear-sightedness of today's young Muslim, and also deprived — alas — of the deep, firm faith that, two generations back, remained immune to harm. They have neither that understanding and perception and awareness whereby, in the shade of that understanding, they could bring into being within themselves a settled faith and cast off the bond of money, rank, fame, popularity, comfort, and ease; nor do they have a firm strongbox and a strong, sturdy enclosure — such as existed two centuries ago and one century ago around them — whereby at least to preserve their faith in a strongroom, even if it be that same imitative faith.

After all, a hundred years ago, there did not exist this multitude of highwaymen of thought, faith, and creed. In those days the hands of the enemy, those treacherous fingers, had not yet found their way so deeply into the body of our society. The schemes had not yet reached the conclusion that the people's authentic religious faith must be taken from them so that any calamity could be visited upon their heads — these things came later. This generation before the young, this generation that today is not young and was the youth of yesterday, is today exposed to a foundation-uprooting flood of faith and creed, and against this flood it does not have even a sturdy faith. Blessed are those who choose their faith out of awareness, out of understanding, out of perception, out of comprehension. The flood comes and carries off the towering trees; but that plant which, though slender and delicate and tender, has roots beneath the ground twice its own size — it remains sound; it survives. That which is rooted from the foundation is not to be effaced by these things.

In any case, this is a truth and a certain point in Islam; brothers! Worthy faith is aware faith. It is faith joined with understanding and perception; it is a faith that has come into being out of insight, with open eyes, without fear of difficulties. As for that faith which a certain Muslim man has — to preserve which we must say: let him not read the newspaper, let him not read such-and-such a book, let him not walk in the street and bazaar, let him not speak with such-and-such a person, let him not suffer cold or heat, let him not see sun or moon, so that it may last — this faith, alas, will not last. What is needed is a faith chosen so consciously that, in the harshest of conditions, that faith is not taken from him.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:106):** "…except one who is compelled while his heart is at rest in faith…"[^s3-16]

Concerning ʿAmmār Yāsir, the verse of the Qur'an says: if under torture, in order to turn the enemy away from you for a moment, you said a single word — say it; your faith is not a faith that is effaced from your heart by torture. That faith which Khabbāb ibn al-Aratt had — they would heat iron and press it to his neck — this is no joke; they would bring glowing iron near his body and press it to the skin of his body — and because of that deep, aware faith of his, he would not give up. Faith is this.

When faith out of clarity, out of understanding, out of thought, with correct reckonings, has been accomplished, then there is no need for us to place this faith in cloth and in rags and in a strongbox and in a strongroom, lest heat and cold and dust and grime harm it; no harm reaches it. It is these unaware faiths that keep a person constantly anxious, in dread: lest, lest, lest. If we want faiths to be firm, if we want faiths not to be effaced, if we want faith to be aware faith, we must continually give awareness to those whom we want to be believers. Let us not dread their becoming aware; let us not take pleasure in their remaining shut of eye and ear. The way is this: until a good substance of awareness comes into being in the minds and hearts and thoughts, and with that awareness a correct, sturdy faith — a reinforced concrete[^s3-17] — is built within the heart; then, even with a shrapnel cannon,[^s3-18] as the old young men used to say, it is not to be effaced. Islam says faith must be aware. These verses at the end of Sūrat Āl ʿImrān introduce aware faith to us.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:190–193):**[^s3-19] "Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth, and in the alternation of night and day, there are signs for those endowed with understanding…"

*Inna fī khalqi al-samāwāti wa al-arḍ* — these are an introduction — *wa ikhtilāfi al-layli wa al-nahār* — indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day. *Laʾāyāt* — indeed there are signs — for the dull-witted? For the senseless? For those who do not think? Never! *Li-ulī al-albāb* — for those of understanding (the wise). The wise one is the one who possesses intellect and the power to understand — and that is all people, provided they put their thought and intelligence to work. For the wise were not, from the moment of birth, set apart by a special emblem such that we might say "we were not among them and cannot become wise, and the wise are thus and so" — no; "wise" means all of us people, along with the other three billion human beings who are in the world, all are wise, on condition that they put to work, a little, this power of intellect that is within them. If you paid money and bought this machine, and set it down in the house, and did not open it up, did not put it to use, and after a while you go to check on it, you will find it has no efficiency, no functioning, it has rusted; and even if it has not rusted, it does not run smoothly. The fault was not the machine's; the fault was yours, for not putting it to work. *Ulū al-albāb* means those who put the power of thought and reflection and intellect to work until they become wise.

Who are *ulū al-albāb*? Look — here is one of the subtle points of the Qur'an. When it wishes to speak of *ulū al-albāb*, to speak of the wise and to introduce them — if ordinary people wished to introduce [the wise], they would say: the wise one is he who is foremost in all the affairs of his life, who is never duped in any matter, who in business dealings, in politics,[^s3-20] in political maneuverings, in disputes and confrontations with rivals — everywhere has the upper hand over his rivals. Since the Qur'an accepts none of these games, since it deems the real worth of a human being to be his connection and relation with God, it introduces the wise one in this way: the wise one, in the view of the Qur'an, is the one who holds these highest values in regard above everything and everyone else. *Alladhīna yadhkurūna Allāh* — they are the wise ones who remember God, *qiyāman* — while standing, *wa quʿūdan* — while sitting, *wa ʿalā junūbihim* — while fallen upon their sides. That is, in every state — in every state they are mindful of God. But this being mindful of God does not mean some ecstatic, mystical, dervish-like state in which some people are pleased to think, "Very well, we too are always mindful of God; we are always crying out 'Hū!'" — no; being mindful of God in an active way, a being-mindful-of-God that counts as a deed. How so?

*Wa yatafakkarūna fī khalqi al-samāwāti wa al-arḍ* — and they reflect upon the creation of the heavens and the earth; they are in a state of contemplation. You see, *ulū al-albāb*, the wise, are those who are in a state of reflection: *wa yatafakkarūna fī khalqi al-samāwāti wa al-arḍ*. Then, when they perform this reflection and contemplation, with the tongue of the heart and the tongue of the outward they say thus: *Rabbanā* — our Lord! *Mā khalaqta hādhā bāṭilan, subḥānak* — You did not create this in vain; transcendent are You above this work of creating in vain; that is, the most important and most fundamental point of an ideology.[^s3-21]

Of every life-shaping ideology, the fundamental point is this: that I am here for some purpose. If one believes in God, he says, "I was brought here for a purpose"; if one does not believe in God, he says, "In any case I have some task here." You see, the most fundamental points of an intellectual philosophy that is to inspire an individual and social life are precisely this: *Rabbanā mā khalaqta hādhā bāṭilan*. He is a believer in the divine; his Lord — therefore he says: our Lord, You did not create this heaven and earth, all this uproar, in vain and in futility. *Subḥānak* — You are too transcendent and pure to do a vain work; so I have a responsibility; so I must tread a road; so I, in the face of this strange and astonishing order, am a point — and for a purpose. In this strange order, I too have a place, and for a purpose; so that if I do not do it rightly and correctly — that act in the very manner You have willed — I have ruined this order. *Subḥānak, qinā ʿadhāba al-nār* — so guard us and keep us safe from the torment of the Fire; the fire of the Resurrection, which, while it is a reality, is also the symbol of the fire of God's wrath and anger and retribution, and is likewise [part of] the constitution of the world.

You must pay attention: all of these are an introduction — an introduction so that we may rightly grasp aware faith from within these verses. By now you friends yourselves must have noticed how awareness ascends and rises up from these verses; but now pay attention. *Rabbanā innaka man tudkhili al-nāra faqad akhzaytah* — our Lord, the one whom You make enter the Fire, You have disgraced and humiliated and abased; *wa mā lil-ẓālimīna min anṣār* — and for the wrongdoers whom You have made enter the Fire there is no helper and no supporter, neither from the constitution [of the world] nor any hand from the Unseen, in no way whatsoever. That is, those who move along the path of injustice and oppression, along the path of unbelief and hypocrisy, along the path of falsehood, are condemned to extinction and nonexistence; nothing in this world supports them.

Well, *Rabbanā* — these wise, intelligent ones, these *ulū al-albāb*, these thinkers upon heaven and earth, these who have understood that they did not come into this world in vain and that this world was not created in vain, continue their speech — we are arriving here at faith — they say: *Rabbanā* — O our Lord — *innanā samiʿnā* — we heard and understood, with the ear of the body and the ear of the heart; *munādiyan* — a caller, a crier — who *yunādī lil-īmān* — was calling out for faith, sounding the call[^s3-22] of faith; *an āminū bi-Rabbikum* — saying, "Believe in your Lord"; *fa-āmannā* — and then we believed. What sort of faith did these believe with? Someone said "Believe," and they believed [just like that]? No; these are the very *ulū al-albāb*, the very thinkers. This caller may outwardly be a prophet, but inwardly he is the prophet of their own intellect and insight, who summons them toward reflection and faith in God and calls them. So the caller said to them, "Believe," and they, out of insight, out of understanding, out of perception and complete awareness, believed. This kind of faith is what is desired in Islam: aware faith. Well, that is the second point.

There is also a third point, which I will state very briefly (since unfortunately a portion of the time was taken up by the beginning of our session). The third point is this: that since aware faith is what is desired in Islam, and since God Most High does not accept unaware faith and assigns it no honor or worth — therefore in several places in the Qur'an it severely rebukes the blind, imitative, prejudiced faiths — for often, too, such faiths slam a person to the ground head-first — it severely censures such faiths. We have written one of its verses here, though if I want to find it in the Qur'an it becomes a bit difficult.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:104):** "And when it is said to them, 'Come to what God has sent down and to the Messenger…'"[^s3-23]

— the talk is of the unbelievers and the reactionaries — and when it is said to them, "Come toward what God has sent down, and come toward the Prophet." (The way I translated it here: and when it is said to them, "Come, and test and understand what God has sent down and what the Prophet teaches." Well — come close, understand, then; come and listen, see what the Prophet says; does the Prophet have something to say?) What is their answer? Instead of coming, instead of thinking, instead of trying to understand and to choose their road — *qālū* — they say: *ḥasbunā mā wajadnā ʿalayhi ābāʾanā* — "the way and custom upon which we found our fathers is enough for us. We do not go after these new words." The unbeliever — in the words of one of the researchers — the unbelievers in the Qur'an are everywhere another name and expression for the reactionaries of the centuries and the ages. Everywhere the Prophet is the enlightened intellectual of his time; he has a new word, he has a new road, he summons toward the new; whereas the unbelievers and opponents are the prejudiced, the imitators, the rigid-minded, and the reactionaries who do not like and do not accept his new road.

Their words too are: "Tread the road just as the [former] travelers trod."[^s3-24] We did not see our fathers and mothers this way; we saw our fathers in another way; we want to understand it the same way, to act the same way. Then the Qur'an, in answer, what does it say? *A wa law kāna ābāʾuhum lā yaʿlamūna shayʾan wa lā yahtadūn* — these have carried the matter of this imitation of their fathers to such a point that: even though their fathers knew nothing and found no way [aright]. (Unfortunately the translation of this latter passage — this last sentence of the verse — has not been written here, although I had written it on that paper, and it has apparently been forgotten, so that it should be written.) In any case, the Qur'an says to these: even if your fathers understood nothing, found no road, could not rightly distinguish their own good from their own ill — even so, do you imitate them? See how it blames and reproaches imitation.

(Well, now these verses at the end of Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, our dear reciter Mr. Fāṭimī will recite today. After he has finished, I have two matters that I wish to present, and I request that you gentlemen pay full attention; after his recitation, do not rise, until I rise, and I wish to present two matters. Mr. Fāṭimī, if you please.)

[^s3-1]: A term applied to the communist country of the Soviet Union and its allied states.

[^s3-2]: Mawlānā Maḥmūd al-Ḥasan (1851–1920), known as "the Father of India" (Shaykh al-Hind), the first student of the famous Deoband seminary and a pupil of Mawlānā Aḥmad Rashīd Gangohī, who later took over the administration of the school. He was among the leaders of the Muslims of India in the struggle against British colonialism, and was repeatedly sentenced to prison and exile. He was the originator of the theory of the Non-Cooperation Movement with the British.

[^s3-3]: Abū al-Kalām Muḥyī al-Dīn Aḥmad (1888–1959), whose pen-name was "Āzād," was among the scholars and militant leaders of the Muslims of India. He began his struggle by publishing the weekly *al-Hilāl*. He went on to found the Dār al-Irshād school. The government later closed the school and exiled him to a mountainous region. After his exile, he stood alongside Gandhi in the ranks of the struggle against colonialism. After the independence of India, he assumed the responsibility of the Ministry of Education and Scientific Research of India.

[^s3-4]: Mawlānā Shawkat ʿAlī (1873–1938) and Mawlānā Muḥammad ʿAlī (1878–1931) were two brothers among the Shīʿī scholars of India who, during the First World War, in support of the king of the Ottoman Empire, launched a movement called the "Khilāfat Movement." Continuing their struggles, they rose up together with Gandhi against British colonialism, and made great efforts toward the union of the Muslims and the Hindus.

[^s3-5]: A mystical school older than Buddhism, whose followers are bound by certain pledges, including: the commitment not to kill or harm animals, abstention from speech and conduct that lead to anger and falsehood, not encroaching upon the property of others, forbidding sexual pleasures to oneself, and abandoning every attachment, small or great, to objects, whether animate or inanimate.

[^s3-6]: He was among the first persons to believe in the Prophet, and among the believers, the first to recite the verses of the Qur'an in Mecca to summon the Quraysh to Islam. After enduring much torture, by the Prophet's order he emigrated to Abyssinia. In the time of the second caliph, together with ʿAmmār Yāsir, he was sent to Kufa as a teacher of the Qur'an. Owing to his oppositions to the governor of Kufa in the time of ʿUthmān, he was brought to Medina. ʿAbdullāh ibn Masʿūd finally died in the year 32 AH from an illness arising from the beating he received from the caliph's agents.

[^s3-7]: Khabbāb ibn al-Aratt, a native of Nabaṭiyya, was among the slaves who answered the Prophet's call and remained at his side despite much torture. He was with the Commander of the Faithful (ʿAlī) in the battles of Ṣiffīn and Nahrawān. After his death, the Imam, in a saying — Wisdom 42 of *Nahj al-Balāgha* — described him thus: "May God have mercy on Khabbāb, for he embraced Islam willingly, emigrated in obedience to God's command, lived content with what sufficed him, was pleased with God, and spent his life in struggle."

[^s3-8]: He, together with his father Yāsir and his mother Sumayya, accepted the religion of Islam. ʿAmmār, while his father and mother had perished under torture, dissociated himself [verbally] from the Prophet in order to remain safe. Then this verse was revealed: "…except one who is compelled while his heart is at rest in faith." After the passing of the Prophet, ʿAmmār remained at the side of the Commander of the Faithful. In the time of the second caliph he was for a while the governor of Kufa, but through the slanders of a group the caliph deposed him. He played an effective role in mobilizing the Imam's army in the Battle of the Camel, and in answering the doubts of that Imam's soldiers in the Battle of Ṣiffīn. ʿAmmār was martyred at the age of ninety-one in the Battle of Ṣiffīn.

[^s3-9]: Sūrat al-Baqarah, verse 285.

[^s3-10]: From a poem by Shaykh Maḥmūd Shabistarī:
> There is one line from the first to the last; / upon it the people of the world have become travelers.
> In this path the prophets are like caravan-guides; / they are the guides and signposts of the caravan.
> And of them our master (the Prophet) has become the leader; / he is both the first and the last in this matter.

[^s3-11]: The Prophet Idrīs was raised to the rank of prophethood after Adam and Seth. According to a tradition from Imam al-Ṣādiq, he became renowned as "Idrīs" on account of his much teaching (*tadrīs*). Through this prophet, God taught writing and script to mankind.

[^s3-12]: A prophet (Jirjīs / St. George) raised to prophethood among the Children of Israel, some years after Jesus. In the course of summoning his people and warning the ruler of that time, he endured much torture.

[^s3-13]: To listen, to comprehend.

[^s3-14]: Unfortunately, the opportunity to present the discussion of "the Resurrection (maʿād)" did not arise in this series of sessions.

[^s3-15]: Please refer to the duplicated handout at the end of the session.

[^s3-16]: Sūrat al-Naḥl, verse 106.

[^s3-17]: Concrete reinforced by the insertion of steel rebar.

[^s3-18]: A type of old cannon inside whose main shell are placed numerous explosive projectiles. The main shell, after being fired, explodes along its trajectory near the target and releases the projectiles within it, which scatter over a large area and cause many casualties.

[^s3-19]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, verses 190 to 193.

[^s3-20]: "Politics" (rendering the colloquial term *pultīk*).

[^s3-21]: A set of beliefs; a worldview.

[^s3-22]: (root ṣ-l-w) To cry out and summon.

[^s3-23]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah, verse 104.

[^s3-24]: From a poem by Shāh Niʿmatullāh Walī:
> Accept this counsel from us, / that you may enter the Paradise of Refuge (Jannat al-Maʾwā).
> Tread the road just as the travelers trod; / they trod the road and traversed the way.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 4: Productive Faith Accompanied by Practical Commitments

*ایمان زاینده و همراه با تعهدات عملی — delivered Sunday, 31 Shahrīvar 1353 / 5 Ramaḍān al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḥajj (22:77–78):** "…And strive for Allah with the striving due to Him. He has chosen you and has not laid upon you in religion any hardship — the faith of your father Abraham. It is He who named you Muslims before and in this [Revelation], that the Messenger may be a witness over you and that you may be witnesses over mankind. So establish prayer and give zakat and hold fast to Allah. He is your Protector — and excellent is the Protector, and excellent is the Helper."[^s4-1]

The matter is simply this: faith, according to the undeniable culture of the Qur'an, is not merely an affair of the heart. It is true that faith means belief, and belief is connected to the heart; but the Qur'an does not recognize as valid every belief, every faith and acceptance and assent. Abstract faith, a faith confined to a dry and empty heart — a faith whose radiance is not visible in the limbs and organs of the believer — such faith, in the view of Islam, is of no worth.

The very first to have faith in God was Satan. Iblīs, for years before the boastful servants of the Lord and the proud, pampered children of Adam came to this dusty earth, used to worship God Almighty, and his heart was a center of the knowledge of God. But at that decisive juncture — at the very point where faith is supposed to come into play, that is, at the moment of choice, at the moment of determining one's final path — this faith was of no use to Iblīs; this faith remained right there in the heart. I say that a faith which only remains in the heart will rot and dry up. You may say, "No, it endures." Well, let us grant the objection its full strength: let us say faith remains in the depths of the heart. Yet a faith that stays in the heart and does not reach the hands and feet and eyes and ears and brain, the limbs and organs and life and faculties and energies of a person — that faith, from the standpoint of Qur'anic culture, is of no worth.

We have summarized this point in a single heading, and it is the very heading written at the top of this sheet[^s4-2] which is in your hands: *productive faith.* A faith that, like an overflowing spring, gives birth to action; a faith accompanied by commitment; a faith that places a burden upon the believer's shoulders; a faith that has action alongside it.

I set out to count up in the Qur'an the verses that read *«inna'lladhīna āmanū wa ʿamilu'ṣ-ṣāliḥāt»* — "those who believed and did righteous deeds." I found that with this very expression, faith joined with righteous action appears in dozens of instances. Faith without commitment — a faith unaccompanied by any sense of responsibility — is good neither for this world nor for the Hereafter. This is the logic of the Qur'an.

(Tomorrow's recitation, too, is roughly on this same theme; we will recite some verses tomorrow as well and reflect upon them, God willing, with deliberation — for they too declare this very meaning explicitly, with utmost clarity, leaving no room for argument.) Those who supposed that faith is something belonging to the region of a person's heart — even if the person is not bound to the requisites and commitments of faith — and that he can still belong to the circle of believers; those who imagined that by being a believer alone, that is, by holding the belief alone, without action, without effort, without striving, God's promises concerning the believers would fall to one's share; those who thought Paradise is granted in exchange for an affair of the heart, and that, without action, government over the earth is granted in exchange for an affair of the heart and not for action; those who imagine, in short, that if we remove action, something of faith still remains — these people ought to reflect carefully upon these verses, and upon the other verses in the coming recitation, and upon the dozens of other instances throughout the entire Qur'an, so that they may see that the faith which has value in the view of Islam is the faith accompanied by action, by responsibility, by duty, by commitment.

If you have felt no commitment, then doubt your own being a believer. A society that does not act upon the commitments of faith should not name itself a believing society.

Those who have heard from the Qur'an *«wa lā tahinū wa lā taḥzanū wa antum al-aʿlawna in kuntum muʾminīn»*[^s4-3] — "Do not weaken, do not grieve; if you are believers you are the highest of all" — those who heard this astonishing summons from the Qur'an and then look at the realities, see that the believers in the Qur'an are not above everyone; rather, they are debtors[^s4-4] to everyone, and they are amazed: where, then, is this promise of the Qur'an? And if, at some time, they do not find any fulfilling[^s4-5] of this promise, they sit waiting for the appearance of the Walī of the Age (may the blessings of God be upon him). To these people, too, it must be pointed out: yes, the divine promise is true — both at the time of the appearance of the Promised Mahdī (may the blessings of God be upon him) and wherever faith takes form. But it is the faith that the Qur'an regards as accompanied by action, the faith it does not consider separate from commitment. Faith is not merely an affair of the heart.

If mere assent and acceptance of the truth of the word were enough for faith, then I say the first to have faith in the Prophet was Abū Lahab[^s4-6] or al-Walīd ibn al-Mughīra al-Makhzūmī.[^s4-7] Those shrewd Arabs understood very well that the Messenger of God did not lie; they understood well that he spoke the truth. Do you want proof? The proof is that they would sit together with their drinking companions and decide to disgrace the Prophet. They would say, "Let us go and listen, let us see what he says, and find fault with him." Then they would go and sit; and the next night they would come back and say, "No, this is not the speech of a human being; this is the word of the God of mankind." So they accepted, they affirmed that he spoke from God. Yet you, after fourteen centuries from that time, do not regard him as a believer. I say: if he is not a believer — on the grounds that this faith of his, this acceptance of his, this assent of his, was not accompanied by the appropriate commitments — then are *we* believers? — while our own assent, too, is not accompanied by the appropriate commitments. What do you say?

If mere recognition and heart-felt faith and belief were enough, then ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ[^s4-8] would have to be the first Shīʿa in the world. ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ, who either saw the affair of Ghadīr Khumm with his own eyes or heard of it from those who saw it with their own eyes — whereas you and I, after thirteen-odd centuries, merely read of this affair in books. ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ, who composes poetry about Imam ʿAlī (peace be upon him);[^s4-9] ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ, who at the moment of death, in those most critical hours and moments that present themselves to a human mind, expresses remorse, expresses regret, saying, "I sold my religion for Muʿāwiya's world; I fought against ʿAlī, whom I knew to be the truth."[^s4-10] So ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ, it seems to me, grasped and affirmed the immediate succession (*wilāyat-i bilā-faṣl*) of the Commander of the Faithful more deeply and more knowledgeably than a Shīʿa of the fourteenth century after the Hijra. But is he a Shīʿa? You say no. Why is he not a Shīʿa? Because belief in the Imamate of the Commander of the Faithful brings certain commitments. Its very first commitment is not to pledge allegiance to Muʿāwiya ibn Abī Sufyān. ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ, who becomes complicit with Muʿāwiya and fights against this very ʿAlī — that is, he does not remain bound to the commitments of Shīʿism, to the responsibilities and duties that this belief directs upon a person — is not a Shīʿa.

This statement is correct, and because it is correct, I turn the discourse back upon ourselves once again, and I say: by this very reasoning, can you and I believe and be certain that we are Shīʿa? Are we acting upon the commitments of being a Shīʿa? Are we bound by them? And the word of the Qur'an on this matter is clear, undoubting, and unambiguous.
