These things that we have written here are ten or twelve subjects. See: is it truly the case that if even one of these subjects is lacking, a human being can feel happy? You will see that he cannot. Of course, there are other subjects too that play a part in a human being's feeling of happiness. And all of these subjects that are written here, and a great many other subjects besides, exist in the Qur'an in the form of a glad tiding and good news upon faith and to the believer. What conclusion do we draw? We draw the conclusion that faith — belief coupled with action, in the way that the Qur'anic culture defines it — is equivalent to all the conditions of happiness and felicity, as regards the needs and requirements that a human being might imagine for himself.

The matter is not a matter of bias for the sake of a creed or of religiosity. What we have written here as the conditions of becoming happy — for a materialist, too, these very same things are the conditions of becoming happy. Now we will read them one by one and see whether a materialist, if he were to possess these, would feel happy or not. And after it becomes clear that these are the conditions of becoming happy, then we will return to the Qur'an, we will lend our ear to the heart-soothing speech of the Qur'an, and we will see that it has promised all of these to the believer, given the believer glad tidings of all of these, brought all of these as a gift for the believer —

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aḥzāb (33:22):** "…and God has spoken the truth…"[^s6-5]

— God does not give a false promise; though, of course, this part is connected to our particular religious way of thinking, namely that we believe in God.

What does a human being need? **One:** he needs to know the goal and the destination of happiness. He needs to know where he wants to arrive, to know for what goal he wants to strive, to see the point of completion and the end of the road from the very beginning, and to know the road to it. In addition to knowing and recognizing and understanding the goal, he needs to know by which road one must travel toward this goal in order to arrive, and to arrive sooner, and to arrive with certainty. Is not the recognition of the goal, the recognition of the end, the recognition of the destination, and the recognition of the road that leads to the destination, the very first and principal element of a human being's happiness? Here there is no difference between the godly and the materialist; the materialist, too, accepts this, agrees to it, feels it. In parentheses we wrote **"guidance"** — the first condition. Of course, this "first," and the "second" that we have numbered here, are certainly not meant in the sense of a logical sequence such that you might say this "second" could be placed first and the "first" could be placed second. Well, let it be so; we have now brought them in succession, and it is possible that other things too could be inserted in between these.

**Two:** that the veils of ignorance, of conceit, of fancy, and of everything that wraps the jewel of his insight and reason in a dark covering and strips from him the power of seeing and understanding — that these be removed. Many things prevent a human being from understanding. A person's conceit prevents him from understanding; ignorances prevent a human being from seeing and understanding; fancies and superstitions prevent a human being or a nation from grasping and understanding the truth; tyrannical systems prevent human beings from understanding and knowing. Various veils and obstacles, from within and from without, prevent a human being from putting to work the God-given jewel of intellect and reason and from knowing and understanding; they keep him in darkness, they seat the human being in a prison of darknesses, they hold him far from light, from the radiance of correct perception and understanding. One of the pillars of a human being's happiness, and one of the elements of a human being's felicity, is this: that a human being be delivered from these darknesses, from everything that creates darkness for him, and find his way to the light and the radiance of truth, and that a beam of the light of truth shine upon his heart. In parentheses we wrote **"light."** So, first, guidance is necessary, in the sense that has been explained; second, light is necessary, in the sense that has, again, been explained — and these are two distinct things.

**Three:** that on his long road toward happiness — on this road that he is traversing toward that destination and the end of the road — he be delivered from the inner anxieties and temptations (pay attention) which are more exhausting than the external deterring factors. Sometimes they block your road and say, "Sir, we will not let you pass from here." Experience has shown, and history has told us explicitly, that when someone's road is blocked, the traveler becomes more eager in traversing this road, his enthusiasm increases, the fire of his ardor blazes more fiercely. If they say, "We will not let you go," the human being presses harder to go, to pass through. This is the external deterring factor. This deterring factor is outside the human being's existence. But sometimes it is from within that they rot the human being. They create doubt within him; they do not block the road — the road is open — but they strip from the human being the power to go, the will to go, the resolve to move, the possibility of striving; and this is worse. "Why are you going? What benefit does it have? Perhaps you will not arrive; perhaps a thief will reach you on the road; perhaps a wolf will come; on what warrant are you going? You need not go." Very coolly, sympathetically, in the manner of a wise old man, in the manner of a master — and the road is also open. This obstacle, this temptation, this anxiety, is by far more exhausting than that stick they place in the middle of the road, saying, "We will not let you go."

This anxiety has existed for most travelers, most wayfarers of the roads of happiness throughout history. How much they pleaded with Moses, saying, "We fear that you have said something false, that a false promise has been given to you." In the Qur'an it says that the pressure and poverty were so great that even the elite would waver, asking:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqara (2:214):** "…When will the help of God come?…"[^s6-6]

— So when? So where is it? What happened? You see, these doubts and waverings and inner anxieties make even the elite slip. If a human being wishes to be happy and to reach the destination and the end of the road of happiness, one of his conditions, too, is this: that he be delivered from this anxiety, from this agitation, from this spiritual insecurity, from this lack of calm and this absence of assurance — that he be able to overcome this anxiety and agitation and inner temptation. This, too, is one of the things that brings a human being to happiness: that on his long road toward happiness he be delivered from the anxieties, the temptations, and the inner temptations that are more exhausting than the external deterring factors. Inside the parentheses we wrote **"assurance and security."** You may put "safety" in place of "security"; it makes no difference. We chose "security" because it was the very Qur'anic expression. It would not be amiss for me to mention this sentence here — perhaps I have said it several other times as well: in the Duʿāʾ Kumayl we recite, *"Yā Rabbi, yā Rabbi, yā Rabbi"*[^s6-7] — O my Lord! O my Lord! O my Lord! *"Qawwi ʿalā khidmatika jawāriḥī"* — strengthen my limbs and members in Your service; *"wa ashdud ʿalā al-ʿazīmati jawāniḥī"* — make my heart firm upon resolve. May I be able to resolve, may I be able to overcome weaknesses, doubts, uncertainties, temptations, and anxieties — for this is what halts a human being on the road, seats him long upon the road, and bars him from traversing the road.

**Four:** that he reckon his striving to be fruitful, that he be hopeful that this striving will reach somewhere. Those who are not hopeful that their striving and movement will lead to some result will assuredly not reach the destination of happiness and salvation. He should be assured that his striving is fruitful, should know that every act he performs leaves behind a positive effect, should know that every step he takes brings him one step closer to the goal. In a wilderness, if you know that the lodging is in this direction, you know where you are going; even though it has grown late, even though you have been left alone, you have fallen behind the caravan — steadfastly, firmly, with striving, with fervor, you move, you walk, you go forward. But if you have lost the road, you do not know whether to go this way or that way; in whatever direction you set off, you take a step, you see that you are sluggish — why? Because you do not know whether this striving will be fruitful. You reckon it possible that this very step, this one step, takes you farther from the lodging; and so you turn back from this way, you go again that way, you go again that way. Thus one of the conditions for a human being to be able to reach happiness — that wayfaring person, that striving person, that exerting person — his condition is this: that he reckon his striving and exertion to be fruitful.

**Five:** that his slips and errors be reparable and subject to pardon. This, too, is very important. In the course of his life and movement a human being has mistakes, has errors. If every error he commits were to remain as an unhealable wound, were to remain as an irreparable act, the human being would always be in anxiety lest he commit yet another error, and this further error throw him still farther from the goal and cast him aside from the road. He is always despairing of the past and always pessimistic about the future. But if he knows that his errors — on the condition that he himself sets about repairing them — are reparable; that his mistakes, conditioned upon his being himself remorseful of those mistakes, are subject to being overlooked — if he knows these things, his desire, his hope, his vigor, and his fervor will multiply several-fold. Inside the parentheses we wrote **"forgiveness and mercy."**

**Six:** that in every state he enjoy a trustworthy support to hold onto. He should know that everywhere, in all circumstances, there is a helper of whom he can avail himself — exactly like a person who has placed the complete map of the road in his pocket, set out upon the road, and is going along these roadways; he does not make mistakes, of course, but he also has no anxiety. He knows that if, perchance, at some time, at some place, he loses the road by mistake and goes astray, this map is at his side; he takes it out, looks at it, and avails himself of this map. Everywhere there is a handhold and a refuge[^s6-8] that he can grasp and avail himself of.

**Seven:** that in confronting enemies and hostilities he enjoy the help and aid of God. This, too, is another condition of happiness, felicity, and success. Of course, the materialist does not believe in God; but with the materialist we do not bring up the name of God. We say, "Sir, in this material striving of yours, in this social striving of yours, in this jihad you are carrying out — if you knew that there were a power, suppose, beyond the power of matter and nature, and that this power is with you and accompanies you, how would that be? Were you to have such a thing, were you to suppose for yourself such a help and aid and support, how would that be?" You see the spark fly from his eyes; he says, "That would be very good." How interesting it is for a human being to have a power beyond the power of matter and material things as his backer, so that when he faces enemies and their hostilities, their plots, their intrigues, their executioners' cruelties, he believes and knows that that power beyond matter is his helper and protector. Except that the materialist does not know the name of God, has no belief in God — although he also has no certainty that God does not exist. But the godly person, who has certainty in the existence of such a dominant and overpowering[^s6-9] might, beyond all these phenomena and relying upon it — see how much more swiftly and admirably he moves along the road of happiness.

**Eight:** that he have superiority and preponderance[^s6-10] over the opposing fronts and ranks; that he know that, in the end, superiority and preponderance and preferability belong to him. This, too, in itself, has a remarkable effect on enabling a human being to traverse this road with greater ease.

**Nine:** that he be victorious over the enemies of his road and his goal, who are the obstructers and neutralizers of his striving. Should a person make all these efforts and then, in the end, be defeated?! That does not bring him to happiness. So one of the most important elements of a human being's happiness is this: that in the end he be victorious — is it otherwise? Do not the world's schools of thought strive for victory? So one of the elements and factors of human happiness — individual, social, and collective — is this: that in confronting enemies he be, in the end, victorious over them.

**Ten:** that in the end he be released from all the hardships and pressures and bonds and barriers, and attain his object and aim, reaching that destination. Inside the parentheses we wrote **"triumph and prosperity"** — these very Qur'anic expressions.

**Eleven:** that in every state, on the road and at the lodging — both on the road to the goal and at the destination of the goal itself — he benefit from and enjoy the stores that have been made ready for the human being in this world. That the blessings of heaven and earth rain down upon him: the wheat of the earth, the drop of rain from heaven, the stores of the seas, the stores of the forests and the mines of the mountains, and all the vital materials and the non-vital materials necessary for the human being — and, above all, the source and mine of intelligence and perception and reason and aptitude and ingenuity of the human being — that all of these be opened up to him, and that he avail himself of all of them. This, too, is one of the things that has a part in a human being's happiness. And other things of this sort, which a human being might reckon to play a part in his own becoming happy.

And finally, after all these — which come to pass during the span of a human being's life and striving and wakefulness — afterward, when he dies, when this lamp is extinguished, when he becomes, to all appearance, the equal of an inanimate thing: then, anew, may the thread of his benefit not come to an end. May his rest only then begin; may his receiving of reward and recompense only then begin; may it be reckoned the first point and the first step of his ease and enjoyment. A materialist, after he dies, holds that all his strivings have only a worldly result; beyond the world he has no hope of anywhere. To that very one we say, "Sir, after you have died and gone — granting the impossible, which here is not impossible — supposing that anew the moment of your death becomes the first moment of your ease, how would that be?" You see that this is the greatest pillar of happiness. And finally, that after the passing of the span of life and the ending of all strivings, he find himself met by a worthy reward, and repose in the Paradise of bounty and good pleasure.

These are the conditions of happiness. For a human being to be happy, for a human being or a society to become fortunate, these are necessary. Now lend your ear to the speech of the Qur'an, which promises all of this to the possessor of faith — a faith that is committed and coupled with action. How is it? The Qur'an gives the believing people glad tidings of all those things that are reckoned to be the elements and constructive factors of happiness and felicity — these, and dozens of things besides these. It says these are for you. It promises guidance, it promises light, it promises safety and assurance and tranquility and repose of the spirit. It promises the fruitfulness and non-waste of strivings. And all of this — if we look at history and the past with a clear eye — we will observe in historical and human realities. And the way of God is also always one and the same.

Now give attention and care to today's verses, with regard to this introduction that was read out and partly explained. The first verse pertains to Sūrat Yūnus. Today's verses are scattered; they are not from one place, they are from several places.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Yūnus (10:9):** "Indeed those who believe and do righteous deeds — their Lord guides them by their faith…"[^s6-11]

Those who have brought faith and have done righteous deed — we explained "righteous deed," in parentheses, with a particular expression: righteous deed means the commitment commensurate with that faith. A human being's faith places a commitment upon his shoulders; the fulfillment of that commitment, the taking of that commitment upon one's shoulder, is the righteous deed. Those who bring faith, who attain that belief, and then also act in accordance with its commitments — *"yahdīhim rabbuhum bi-īmānihim"* — their Lord will guide them on account of their faith. Faith itself causes them to find the road. To which road do they find it? Both to the goal and to the roads, to the means. Some people say, "Sir, how can we reach that desired destination?" When you probe this remark, you see that the faith of the first step is not in his heart; or if there is faith, action does not accompany it. If he acts, illumination and guidance will fall to his lot; he will also find the second step. "Set foot upon the road, and ask nothing at all; the road itself will tell you how one must go."[^s6-12] When a human being brings faith in the goal and the road, and moves in pursuit of this faith, the road, of itself, shows itself. *"Yahdīhim rabbuhum bi-īmānihim"* — by means of faith their Lord will guide them, will open the road before them.

At the first step, none of the leaders and great ones and wayfarers and followers knew what the tenth step was. I sometimes give an example. I say: in a wilderness which, suppose, is tens of kilometers or more in length and breadth, on a dark and pitch-black night — no moon, no star — your honored self is going along the road alone, and a single small flashlight, a small bulb of the fifth size, a small candle, is also in your hand. They say to you, "Sir, with this candle you must go to the very end of that wilderness." You take a look and say, "Sir, this candle of mine illuminates no more than a radius of one meter; am I to traverse all these ten kilometers with this single candle? This candle of mine illuminates only one meter — am I to go ten kilometers?" This is a logic that an uninformed, inexperienced, unfamiliar person might have. What is its answer? What, in your view, is the answer to this blind logic? Is not the answer to this remark this: "Respected sir, is one meter around you lit, or not? Take one step forward — one more meter will be lit. If it is not, do not go. Go this one meter that is lit; take this one step that you can take and where you know you are placing your foot. If one more step is not lit before you, do not go; if it is, then go again. You will see that, gradually, to the very end of the wilderness it will become lit, and you will traverse this road and reach the lodging." Is it otherwise? Is it otherwise? *"Yahdīhim rabbuhum bi-īmānihim"* — God, by their faith, will guide them; faith itself is the cause of a human being finding the roads.

In several other verses of the Qur'an, too, there is a reference to this matter. In one place, in a verse — which we have not written here — He says that when a sura or a verse is sent down, the unbelievers and opponents and hypocrites and the ill-willed and the diseased of heart say:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Tawba (9:124):** "…Which of you has this increased in faith?…"[^s6-13]

— Whose faith is increased by this verse? Then the Qur'an replies, saying: Say that those who are believers, those who have truly given their adherence, are increased in faith by this verse and this sign. That very faith of theirs, in their hearts, is what causes them to take guidance from this source of guidance. (These parentheses that we have opened — we wrote inside them, in the form of explanation, that thing — pay attention to these.)

The next verse:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:174–175):** *"Yā ayyuhā al-nāsu qad jāʾakum burhānun min rabbikum wa anzalnā ilaykum nūran mubīnan."*[^s6-14] — "O mankind, a proof has come to you from your Lord, and We have sent down to you a clear light."

Behold, O people, from your Lord a proof has come for you — a decisive and clear argument, a firm and establishing demonstration; *"wa anzalnā ilaykum nūran mubīnan"* — and We have sent down to you a manifest light. What is meant by this proof and light is the Qur'an and the Qur'anic truths. The evidence for our claim is in its following verse:

> *"Fa-ammā alladhīna āmanū bi-llāhi wa-ʿtaṣamū bihi fa-sa-yudkhiluhum fī raḥmatin minhu wa faḍlin wa yahdīhim ilayhi ṣirāṭan mustaqīman."* — "As for those who believe in God and hold fast to Him, He will admit them into mercy and bounty from Himself, and guide them to Himself along a straight path."

So those who bring faith in God and become attached to and reliant upon Him — for faith in the heart alone is also not enough; one must grasp God, the skirt of God, that is, the skirt of God's religion and the godly road, become attached to it. *"Fa-ammā alladhīna āmanū bi-llāhi wa-ʿtaṣamū bihi"* — they clung, they became attached; if it is thus, *"fa-sa-yudkhiluhum fī raḥmatin minhu wa faḍlin"* — God will admit them into His mercy and bounty. After it: *"wa yahdīhim ilayhi ṣirāṭan mustaqīman"* — He guides them toward Himself, leads them; and He guides by a straight and near road. This guidance is special to the believers. If you do not have faith — or if you have faith but do not have holding-fast, do not act upon your commitments before God — you will not find the road to God; the illumination of that guidance will not cast its radiance in your heart. This is special to the believers. Sūrat al-Nisāʾ, verse 175.

Another verse:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAnkabūt (29:69):** *"Wa-lladhīna jāhadū fīnā la-nahdiyannahum subulanā, wa inna Allāha la-maʿa al-muḥsinīn."* — "And those who strive for Us — We will surely guide them to Our roads; and indeed God is with the doers of good."

This is a well-known verse that is much upon people's tongues. *"Wa-lladhīna jāhadū fīnā…"* — those who, on Our road — God says it, and what is God's road? — and for the sake of the divine goals, the divine aims, any goal that God has in this world, strive… What are God's goals? It is justice, it is security, it is the servitude of the servants before Him, it is the servants of God reaching maturity and perfection, it is the flourishing of the face of the earth, it is the flourishing[^s6-15] of human hearts, it is the rendering-prosperous of this world and the next world of human beings, it is all beings falling onto the track and rail of perfection — these are God's wishes. It is the absence of conjecture, the absence of polytheism, the absence of unbelief, the absence of insecurity, the absence of bestiality and brutality,[^s6-16] the absence of rebellion and tyranny — these are God's wishes. *"Wa-lladhīna jāhadū fīnā"* — those who strive on the road of Our goals and wishes, the divine wishes; *"la-nahdiyannahum subulanā"* — without doubt and without question, We will show them Our roads; We do not leave them bewildered, We do not leave them astray. That poem I recited is fitting here:

> Set foot upon the road, and ask nothing at all;
> The road itself will tell you how one must go.

*"Wa-lladhīna jāhadū fīnā la-nahdiyannahum subulanā."* In every field it is thus. In the field of understanding religion, in the field of grasping religion, in the field of researching religious matters, in social matters, in world matters, in every field — whoever entered upon the divine goals, set foot, every step he advanced, the next step is lit for him. Those who strive on Our road and for the sake of the divine goals — to their own roads, which are the roads of the happiness and perfection of the human being, We lead them. *"Wa inna Allāha la-maʿa al-muḥsinīn"* — and without doubt God is with the doers of good. Sūrat al-ʿAnkabūt, verse 69.

These were the verses that exist in the Qur'an in connection with guidance, that first subject. And there are abundant other verses, such that if I had wished to gather all the verses, to write them here and read them, I would have had to speak about guidance alone for at least three or four days.

We said that light is one of the things necessary for a human being to become happy, with that explanation that we gave about light on that previous page. To the believers, light has been promised:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqara (2:257):** *"Allāhu waliyyu alladhīna āmanū, yukhrijuhum mina al-ẓulumāti ilā al-nūr…"*[^s6-17] — "God is the walī of those who believe; He brings them out of the darknesses into the light…"

God is the guardian and the comrade-in-the-same-front of the believers. I render *walī* as comrade-in-the-same-front, joined, on the same level, and I prefer it over "guardian" and "friend" and "helper" and these things that are customary. Because *wilāyah* means connectedness — two things that are bound and joined to each other: to this they say *wilāyah*. God is the *walī* of the believers, that is, He is joined with the believers. What does it mean that He is joined with the believers? It means that God and the believers are in one rank. The enemies of God are in another rank, facing the believers and facing God. But wherever it may be:

> *"Wa-llāhu waliyyu al-muʾminīn"*[^s6-18] — "And God is the walī of the believers,"

or:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:68):** *"…awliyāʾu…"*[^s6-19]

— and the expressions that exist in the Qur'an are in this sense; keep this in mind.

*"Yukhrijuhum mina al-ẓulumāti ilā al-nūr"* — He delivers them from the darknesses and brings them to the light. *"Allāhu waliyyu alladhīna āmanū"* — God is the guardian and comrade-in-the-same-front of the believers. Here we gave an explanation, and this explanation is also not amiss for understanding the meaning of "the darknesses." He delivers them from the darknesses — the darknesses of ignorance, superstition, conceit, domineering and anti-human systems, all those things that, for humankind and for the jewel of human insight, are reckoned a prison and a dungeon — He delivers them from these darknesses and brings them to light. What light? The light of knowledge, learning, and human values. God does this for the believer. He never brings the non-believer, the faithless one, the doubting and uncertain one, the ungrateful unbeliever to light. Therefore the polytheist is always agitated, the polytheist always has anxiety, the polytheist's life is always a life coupled with agitation; for him there is no luminosity, there is no true knowledge, there is no correct recognition, wherever he reaches. *"Wa-lladhīna kafarū…"* — but what of the unbelievers? "Unbelievers" means those who were ungrateful for religious belief and the school of religion, who rejected this gift and present of God with ingratitude — the ungrateful of blessing. Pay attention to these Persian expressions that I am presenting; these show where the root of the word is and how it came to be that this word was used in this meaning. The unbeliever (*kāfir*) is not the one who did not accept religion; *kāfir* means the coverer of blessing, the ingrate of blessing. Why is such a one called *kāfir*? Well, this person did not accept this religion — why "unbeliever"? For the reason that this religion was a gift of God, this was a present from God for the happiness of him and of all human beings; he rejected this — this ingrate, this thankless one, this ungrateful-of-blessing — and so they call him *kāfir*. *"Wa-lladhīna kafarū"* — those who practiced unbelief and denial; *"awliyāʾuhumu al-ṭāghūt"* — their guardians and comrades-in-the-same-front are the false idols and the transgressors. And those who practiced unbelief and denial — their guardians and masters are the false idols and the rebellious and the transgressors. *"Yukhrijūnahum mina al-nūri ilā al-ẓulumāt"* — they cast them out from the light of knowledge, drawing them into the prison of darknesses and obscurities. *"Ulāʾika aṣḥābu al-nāri hum fīhā khālidūn"* — they are all the companions[^s6-20] of the Fire, and in it they abide forever. Sūrat al-Baqara, verse 257.

(One more verse — that is, one more passage of the Qur'an — remains for me to read. I have both grown tired, and the time is passing. And afterward our dear reciter, the honorable Mr. Riḍāʾī, recites these very verses today; he reads them too from the handout. It had been arranged that he would collate them with the Qur'an; doubtless he has collated them and reads them from the handout. These verses, with these meanings and translations that have been written here and that I have explained, then, with his charming and affecting voice that you will hear — you will see that it becomes much more pleasing to the mind.)

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aḥzāb (33:41–43):** *"Yā ayyuhā alladhīna āmanū"* — O you who have believed — *"udhkurū Allāha dhikran kathīran"* — remember God with much remembrance; *"wa sabbiḥūhu bukratan wa aṣīlan"* — and morning and evening, extol Him with purity and sanctity. What has happened? *"Huwa alladhī yuṣallī ʿalaykum wa malāʾikatuh"*[^s6-21] — it is He who sends blessing upon you, and His angels also.

O you who have believed, remember God with much remembrance; and morning and evening, praise Him with cleanliness and purity. What has happened? It is He who sends blessing upon you, and His angels too. Why? *"Li-yukhrijakum mina al-ẓulumāti ilā al-nūr"* — that He may deliver you from the darknesses and lead you to light and illumination. This is the Qur'an, and this is the glad tiding of the Qur'an. Of these glad tidings there is also another number that, God willing, I will present tomorrow. And if I wished to state all of these Qur'anic glad tidings, and to enumerate one by one all that the Qur'an promises us, it would certainly take more than ten days and twelve days for me to be able to state the glad tidings and good news that are upon faith and for the believer. Therefore we will discuss only tomorrow; if it becomes necessary, if we are compelled, we will speak the day after tomorrow as well — and if not, we will conclude it tomorrow.

[^s6-1]: Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (the blessed Chapter of Women), verses 174 and 175. (This is the verse with which the session opens and which is treated in detail later in the lecture.)

[^s6-2]: *ghawr* (غور): to plunge in, to sink in — i.e., to delve deeply.

[^s6-3]: The blessed Sūrat al-Kahf, verse 31.

[^s6-4]: Refer to the photocopied handout at the end of the session.

[^s6-5]: The blessed Sūrat al-Aḥzāb, verse 22.

[^s6-6]: The blessed Sūrat al-Baqara, verse 214.

[^s6-7]: Duʿāʾ Kumayl, ḥadīth no. 12. The Arabic phrases cited: *"Yā Rabbi, yā Rabbi, yā Rabbi… Qawwi ʿalā khidmatika jawāriḥī, wa-shdud ʿalā al-ʿazīmati jawāniḥī."*

[^s6-8]: *mustamsak* (root م‌س‌ك) and *mustaʿṣim* (root ع‌ص‌م): a handhold that one grasps; a refuge that gives shelter.

[^s6-9]: *musayṭir* (root ص‌ط‌ر): dominant and overpowering.

[^s6-10]: *rujḥān* (root ر‌ج‌ح): superiority, preponderance.

[^s6-11]: The blessed Sūrat Yūnus, verse 9.

[^s6-12]: From a verse of ʿAṭṭār: "If you are a man of the road, you must go through blood; / and with foot fallen, headlong you must go. / Set foot upon the road and ask nothing at all; / the road itself will tell you how one must go." (ʿAṭṭār)

[^s6-13]: The blessed Sūrat al-Tawba, verse 124.

[^s6-14]: The blessed Sūrat al-Nisāʾ, verses 174 and 175.

[^s6-15]: *maʿmūr shudan* (root ع‌م‌ر): to become flourishing, built up, prosperous.

[^s6-16]: *dad-khūyī* and *dad-manishī*: ferocity, savagery, wildness.

[^s6-17]: The blessed Sūrat al-Baqara, verse 257.

[^s6-18]: A Qur'anic expression: *"And God is the walī of the believers."*

[^s6-19]: The blessed Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, verse 68.

[^s6-20]: *aṣḥāb* (root ص‌ح‌ب): companions, associates, fellows.

[^s6-21]: The blessed Sūrat al-Aḥzāb, verses 41 to 43.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 7: Glad Tidings (2)

*نویدها ۲ — delivered Wednesday, 3 Mehr 1353 / 8 Ramaḍān al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Raʿd (13:28–29):** "…those who believe and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of God. Verily, in the remembrance of God do hearts find rest. Those who believe and do righteous deeds — blessedness is theirs, and a goodly return."

We have stated that God the Exalted, in the Qur'an, gives the believers the glad tidings and the promise of every element of felicity. Those things which we, at the level of thought and conception, identify as the elements of felicity — ten or twelve of which we set out in the previous written note and the previous recitation — all of these have been given to the believers in the Qur'an in the form of decisive promises. Two of those promises — guidance and light — we observed yesterday in the course of the noble verses of the Qur'an; they were recited, and we benefited from them. Two more we shall raise today, reading their verses briefly, and the rest we leave to you, my friends, so that, following the very order which I arranged in the previous note, you take up the subjects one by one. When you study the Qur'an and recite it — those of you who know Arabic, from the text of the Qur'an itself; those who do not know Arabic, with attention to a translation of the Qur'an — try to find those subjects.

For example, one of those subjects is the reward of the Hereafter — one of those promises. Keep your eye on the Qur'an yourselves; see in how many places, and in which places, the Qur'an gives the believers — those who possess faith and righteous deeds — the promise of the reward of the Hereafter. Or, for example, one of them is superiority over and victory over the enemy. See whether there is a place in the Qur'an where God the Exalted has told the believers that you will gain the upper hand over your enemies. Right now, do you know of a place where the Qur'an has promised the believers that you are higher and superior to your enemies, to the ranks of your opponents — provided that you have faith and act upon the commitments of faith? Or that has said that you will ultimately triumph over your enemies, provided that you strive in the path of your thought? Is there such a verse in the Qur'an or not? Can the gentlemen answer whether there is such a verse in the Qur'an or not? And if anyone remembers the verse, he can recite it now.

(One of those present: *wa antum al-aʿlawna in kuntum muʾminīn*.[^s7-1]) Very well, that was one verse — *wa lā tahinū wa lā taḥzanū wa antum al-aʿlawna in kuntum muʾminīn* —

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:139):** "Do not lose heart and do not grieve, for you are the higher ones, if you are believers."

Yes, this is a verse: that if indeed you are believers, you are superior; the upper hand, the stronger hand, is at your disposal in relation to your enemies. There are numerous other verses that give the promise and the glad tidings to the believers that they gain a decisive victory over the enemies and the opposing ranks:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mujādilah (58:21):** "God has decreed: 'I shall surely prevail, I and My messengers.'"[^s7-2]

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ṣāffāt (37:173):** "And verily Our host — they shall be the victors."[^s7-3]

Verses of this kind are abundant. Keep these in mind. When you look at the Qur'an, when you study it, instead of making your aim to finish a *juzʾ* or to finish a *sūrah*, let your aim be to understand the Qur'an. As it is reported from the Imam, peace be upon him, or from the Prophet of God, may God's blessings be upon him and his family, that during the recitation of the Qur'an your concern should not be to finish the *sūrah* or to finish the *juzʾ*.[^s7-4] Whether it is finished or not, recite a single verse — with reflection, with care. See whether, in this verse, there is something of these promises which we have mentioned — the pillars and elements that are necessary and effective for attaining felicity — whether you find something of these elements in the Qur'an that has been promised to them. Has the glad tidings and the promise been given, or not? By this means I want you yourselves to refer to the Qur'an.

Until the end of the month of Ramadan, if I succeed, I will translate and explain very many of the Qur'an's verses for you. Say, for example, I explain fifty verses, or I explain another fifty verses, or another sixty verses. The Qur'an is roughly six thousand — more than six thousand — verses; you yourselves must develop an intimacy with the Qur'an. Those who know Arabic, let them try to make firm this intimacy of theirs and this companionship of theirs with the Qur'an. Those who do not know Arabic, while they try to learn Arabic and to become familiar with the language of the Qur'an, let them not put off the reading and understanding of the Qur'an until after they have learned Arabic; let them read the Qur'an now too — only, find good translations of the Qur'an. Some of the translations, in my view, are good and to my liking. When you read the Qur'an, pay attention to those translations and exercise care and reflection upon the meanings of the verses; see which of these promises that we mentioned here you can find in the Qur'an. Therefore, today I will mention two further subjects that are of interest among these promises which have come in the Qur'an. The rest of it I leave to you yourselves, and I hope that you, too, will not put it off until after the month of Ramadan, or until a time of idleness and leisure. This very night, this very day, at the first opportunity, in the first moment of leisure, when you open the Qur'an, read the Qur'an with this very intention, and teach yourselves familiarity with, intimacy with, and benefiting from the Qur'an. The Qur'an is, after all, the inexhaustible[^s7-5] source of our knowledge. Whatever we have, we draw from the Qur'an, and whatever can be the means of our felicity and good fortune must, too, come out of the Qur'an. This clear, definite text is at our disposal; it is incumbent upon us to make use of it.

Of course, when I say "the Qur'an," I do not wish to confine it to the Qur'an — not that *Nahj al-Balāghah*, no; or the authentic traditions from the rightly-guiding Imams, peace be upon them, no — why? We should make use of *Nahj al-Balāghah* too, alongside the Qur'an; we should make use of the *ḥadīth* too, alongside the Qur'an — the authentic and sound *ḥadīth*. Of course, the Qur'an is a text that is at everyone's disposal, and everyone can have its correct, sound, and complete copy even in his pocket; access to *ḥadīth* is not this easy, nor is access to *Nahj al-Balāghah*.

In any case, above this we wrote "assurance, repose, and security," which we considered close in horizon[^s7-6] — we considered them as one — and we now state what we mean by these three words, and then return to the verses of the Qur'an to see, in this regard — in the regard of assurance, repose, and security — what the verses of the Qur'an teach us.

Assurance means the state of tranquility of the heart. The state of calm of the spirit and the heart. What does "calm" mean? In what sense is "calm" intended by us here? Is it in the sense that our spirit should have no movement and no striving? Calm in the sense that it is in a state of half-sleep and half-unconsciousness in our heart? No — rather, calm as opposed to anxiety and agitation; calm and assurance as opposed to disquiet.

Picture two persons; both are due to be present in the examination session. One has studied these lessons well, has reviewed them ten times, has discussed them with his friends; all the problems of this book on which he is to be examined are present and clear in his mind. The other has not studied this book, or has studied a portion of it, or does not place much trust in his own memory. Both are due to be present in the examination session. Do these two have the same spiritual state? Look: when that first person enters this session, his spirit has calm; it has no turbulence, no agitation, no anxiety, no disquiet. He says, "From wherever they ask, I know it." But that other one is constantly in turbulence, like a skiff, like a small boat that has fallen onto a turbulent ocean, into a tumultuous sea — sometimes to this side, sometimes to that side; sometimes by this route, sometimes by that route; the winds carry him this way and that. The spirit of that unprepared person has just such a state.

In facing a judge, you can reckon these same two states of spirit. In involvement in social activities and in the great struggles of history, you can consider these very same two states and two dispositions in an individual or in a society.

Two soldiers enter the field of battle, possessing two kinds of disposition. One has confidence in the quality of his military equipment,[^s7-7] in the leadership over him, in the planning and competence of his chiefs and leaders, in the weakness of the enemy's force, in the efficacy of himself and his friends and comrades-in-arms. In addition, he knows that the reinforcement forces are over there, behind the front, waiting to come onto the field at the necessary moment, at a single signal, and to help. This one enters the field of battle in one manner. That man who has no confidence whatsoever in his own military equipment, in the effectiveness of his friends, in the use of his weapon, sees himself as small and the enemy as great; sees himself as naked and the enemy as encased in armor and weapons up to below the eye and to the teeth — this one enters the field of battle in another manner.

These are some examples of assurance. I want it to become clear and correct for you what it means — what assurance of the self means, what it means for the self to be assured, what it means for the heart to possess calm, in what sense. That first soldier possesses assurance; his heart is at peace. To have a peaceful heart does not mean that, on the field of battle, he puts his shoes under his head and stretches out and sleeps and his snoring rises to the sky — no, assurance does not mean that. Assurance does not mean that in the middle of the field of battle he says, "Let us now have a cigarette, let us look at the scenery for a little while" — no, certainly not. It does not mean that the slightest movement of the enemy is hidden from his sight. Assurance means that he is not agitated, he is not disquieted; the future is clear to him, he knows that he will prevail, and so he does not fear. This heart and this spirit is at peace — like a ship that, with great weight, with much equipment, is moving on a calm sea: this is what they call assurance. And the unassured one is like that small boat, like that broken plank that is moving on a turbulent ocean, on a turbulent river; constantly agitated, constantly going back and forth by this route, by that route. These are two kinds of disposition.

Let me give another example of assurance. We must gradually draw near to that disposition of assurance that is intended in the Qur'an. We begin from the examination of a student, then a defendant before a court, then a soldier on the field of battle. Another instance: a person who has set out upon a road and is moving, going toward a purpose and a destination. Dozens of motives may hold him back from traversing this road and pursuing this goal. Fear is one of these motives — dread, alarm, terror. Out of fear he may fail to continue this road. Fear of what? Fear of hunger along the way, fear of the thief on the road, fear of the ravening wolf that lies in ambush on the way, fear of the toils and the sleepless nights of this road, and finally fear of not arriving. This is one category of the things that prevent this traveler from pursuing his road and going forward.

Greed is another. Greed for what? Greed for a comfortable life — that if I do not traverse this road, do not set out and go after this purpose, I will sleep in the warm, soft bed of my own home, I will pass my time beside my children and my beloved wife. This is something that, for an ordinary person, for a small person, for a weak spirit, is an ideal, is beloved, is desirable; it draws him to itself, so it is clear that he is not willing to give it up easily. Greed for a comfortable life; greed for the one who says, "If you do not go on this road, this purse of money is yours"; greed for attaining the positions which, if you do not accept this wandering exile, those positions await you. Greed! These are the motives that hold a person back from traversing this road — the fears and the greeds. If we dissect the fears, dozens of categories emerge; if we dissect the greeds, dozens of other categories emerge: love of ease, love of well-being, opportunism, self-interest, and the like.

Well, now picture a traveler who has finally taken the risk, as people today put it, has set foot on this road, is moving and going — but have these motives come to an end? This person who is moving on this road — those obstacles that would not let him enter this road, have they vanished? No, they have not vanished. Pay close attention! These obstacles, at first too, would not let him enter this long and far road; now, too, that he has entered this road, they will not let him traverse it in tranquility and peace. With every step he takes, like a thorn, like a hook, like a chain — they catch his foot, the hem of his garment, his hand, and they pull and drag and hold him back from traversing the road. He keeps falling to this side, keeps falling to that side; this thorn keeps catching his hem, that chain keeps dragging his foot, the love of his child keeps drawing him toward itself, the recollection of a comfortable life keeps pulling him toward itself. From this side and from that side, the various motives carry this person off in different directions. This person becomes unstable, like that very skiff, like that very boat — sometimes to this side, sometimes to that side. This is the unassured person.

There is also a person who, when he has entered, when he has reached this road, creates for himself a motive, a factor of attention, that makes him heedless of all these small motives. An attraction and a pull draws his heart, such that these small attractions — the attraction of the child, the attraction of the wife, the attraction of life, the attraction of money, the attraction of position, the attraction of one's very life — in the face of that greater attraction are empty, are nothing, are annihilated, have no effect. Dozens of magnets attract a small object this way and that, but when a stronger magnet appears, its attraction draws this small piece of iron, this filing, toward itself in such a way that the other magnets have absolutely no effect — their effect is neutralized.

When this person, with this strong attraction, with this strong disposition, has entered a road and become occupied with traversing that road, then the attraction of wife and child and the other things — the beauties and the comforts and the pleasures and the delights and the merrymaking — no longer have any effect on him. Who is this person? This is the assured person:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Fajr (89:27–28):** "O soul at peace! Return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing."[^s7-8]

The one who can traverse the road of God to the end, and attain the resting-place and the intended purpose and destination, is the one who is assured — in whom there is the state of assurance and repose. Assurance means this: that an attraction draws him; the attraction of faith, the attraction of love for God, the attraction of love for the goal, captivates him so and draws him toward itself so that all the other attractions appear to him empty and absurd — empty and absurd. The meaning is not that the earth, with this immensity, whose gravity draws every body toward itself — that this should not exist. But suppose then that some body, some mountain — even Mount Himalaya, even the greatest mountains of the world — should also display itself; a stone that you fling, should Mount Himalaya draw it toward itself? No. Mount Himalaya has gravity, but in the face of the earth's gravity, its gravity is zero, it is neutralized, it does not act. Mount Himalaya, and any body of that magnitude, exerts its effect and draws smaller objects toward itself only where there is no greater body, like the earth, beside it. When the earth's gravity is present, the gravity of Mount Himalaya is nothing.

When faith in God acts in the spirit of a person like a strong attraction, it draws him toward the goals of faith in such a way that the small attractions — these attractions that loom large for the faithless, but in the face of faith are small — these attractions no longer have any effect on him. It keeps tempting the heart of a person to bring up here the abundant historical evidences that exist in the early period of Islam — but I see that time is passing; you yourselves should search out the evidences and find them. Those astonishing attractions that drew certain individuals…

I only want to explain these two or three words. Assurance — so what does assurance mean? The gist of the matter came to this: that assurance means the tranquility of a person's spirit, the calm of a person's heart — that the heart be at peace. What does it mean to be at peace? Not that it be motionless, not that it not go forward and not have progress — rather, that the various attractions not draw him to this side and that side, not become the cause of his trouble. Loves and affections and passions and caprices — none of them should rule over him; rather, under the weight of the burden of faith, at peace and assured, yet with the utmost possible speed, he moves toward the purpose of humanity, the purpose of creation. This is the meaning of assurance.

Next, the second word is *sukūn* (repose). *Sukūn*, too, has this very meaning.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Tawbah (9:26):** "Then God sent down His *sakīnah* (tranquility) upon His Messenger…"[^s7-9]

God sent down *sakīnah* upon the Prophet. In five or six places of the Qur'an this expression occurs, one of which is among these very verses that I think we shall now read. Of those verses that pertain to the *sakīnah* of the self — it seems that, unfortunately, I have brought nothing here. (Because, since time is so short and space is so short and our resources, too, are truly meager, I keep thinking that if I bring two more verses, these two pages will become three pages — how are we to mimeograph this? You understand! Our resources in these matters are this scant and weak. I say this so that you know that if the print of this has come out faint, you should know that, had it not come out faint, it would not have come out at all! Suppose, for example, that our situation is now like this: we cannot easily and conveniently provide even two ordinary sheets of paper. Otherwise, I gathered all these verses pertaining to security and assurance and *sakīnah*; I took pains to gather them, it is much trouble too — searching and finding them is very difficult; at the least a person must search and work for one or two hours just to find the verses, let alone drawing the conclusions. Even though I gathered the verses, I was always troubled by this: that if we were to bring two more verses, this one and a half pages would become two and a half or three pages, and mimeographing that would be a difficult task. See our resources — I want you to bear this in mind, that our resources are this degree of weak.)

In any case: there are five or six verses in the Qur'an that concern *sakīnah* at critical moments. For example, one is in the Battle of Ḥunayn.[^s7-10] In the Battle of Ḥunayn, after the Prophet's troops, on account of an unwarranted pride, on account of becoming conceited about their own force and thinking that today no one would gain victory over them — and according to the way of God, that a conceited person must fall flat on his face, and that every conceited group must become heedless and take a blow; constant alertness and sensitivity are necessary for every person and every community and every nation, such that the Commander of the Faithful, may God's blessings be upon him, said, "I am not like that dazed, dumb animal that is put to sleep with a lullaby, in *Nahj al-Balāghah*: *wa-Allāhi lā akūnu kal-ḍibʿi tanāmu ʿalā ṭūl al-laʾam* —[^s7-11] with lullabies, like the hyena: they go to its den and sing it lullabies; this makes it fall asleep, then they catch it. He says: I am not the sort to be put to sleep with a lullaby and become heedless — I am constantly alert, and it is the way of God that a person who is not constantly alert is bound to see and take the blow." These people became heedless on the field of battle, and they had reason to:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Tawbah (9:25–26):** "…when your great numbers pleased you, but they availed you nothing."[^s7-12]

They were many, and this being-many of the host brought them to wonder, brought them to amazement. Wonder! Are we all this multitude? "But it availed you nothing" — this very pride of yours caused you to be heedless in the war and to be defeated. After they were defeated by the enemy — yet not a decisive and final defeat, a partial breaking — they immediately came to themselves. A number of good soldiers, a number of steadfast, seriously believing commanders — the Commander of the Faithful and a few others — with every effort and exertion, finally turned back the troops, who were being routed. Then, there, the Lord says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Tawbah (9:26):** "Then God sent down His *sakīnah* upon His Messenger."

God returned to you the peace and the *sakīnah* of the spirit — peace, *sakīnah*.

One instance is at the time when, beneath that tree, the Muslims renewed their allegiance to the Prophet.[^s7-13] One instance is for the time when the Prophet had fled from Mecca toward Medina, with the aim of forming the Islamic society, with the plan of dangerous decisions for the unbelievers and the enemies, with a thousand resolves and powerful determinations, with a thousand thoughts. The Prophet has come out, and he must convey himself to Medina; the condition for the realization of all those aims and those thoughts, those plans of the Prophet, is that the Prophet arrive safely in Medina. Otherwise, if the unbelievers were able to kill him on the road, to destroy him, none of those visions would take the form of occurrence. The Prophet has taken refuge in a cave, and there too God says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Tawbah (9:40):** "…so God sent down His *sakīnah* upon him."[^s7-14]

God sent down His *sakīnah* and peace upon the heart of the Prophet in that critical situation. And there are several other instances. The believer possesses *sakīnah*; *sakīnah* means this. So *sakīnah*, peace, repose of the self — again, it does not mean the absence of movement and falling asleep and becoming heedless.

And the last phrase and the last word — security. Of course, it is evident that here the meaning is spiritual security, not social security. Social security, of course, means that all individuals enjoy a certain peace in society, so that each one can take his own right. And silence — enforced silences are something other than security. Security consists in all the people being able, with complete safety, to attain their rights and their legitimate wants. This security that we are speaking of here is other than that security which is raised in the context of social matters and social security. This security means spiritual security — the absence of instability, the absence of agitation, the absence of fear, not being afraid and fearful — these three phrases. Now, in the verses of the Qur'an, see what is stated regarding the believers in the context of these three attributes. (While I read the verses, I request that you look at these sheets, that you pay attention to the translations and also correct the verses themselves. Today it came out very poorly.)

The verse of Sūrat al-Raʿd, verses 28 and 29 — two short verses:[^s7-15]

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Raʿd (13:28):** "…those who believe, and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of God."

Of course, the verse is perhaps a continuation of the previous verses, but mentioning the previous verses would take a little long. "Those who believe, and whose hearts find rest" — those who brought faith and whose hearts found peace — "in the remembrance of God" — in the remembrance of God. See, this remembrance of God is that very strong attraction. That same strong attraction that I said erases the small attractions, neutralizes their effect — this is precisely *dhikr Allāh* (the remembrance of God). Why did they place such great emphasis on the prayer (*ṣalāt*)? Why did they say that if the prayer is not accepted, the other deeds are not accepted? Why have they said that Mecca [the pilgrimage] is once in a lifetime, fasting once a year, *zakāt* for a specific case, *khums* likewise, and the rest of the acts of worship likewise — but the prayer they have said is every day, and that five times a day, and if you do more, it is better? Why have they said this? Because the prayer — I have spoken about the prayer in detail over several sessions; some of the honored attendees may remember — the prayer is a capsule of the remembrance of God. The prayer, from head to foot, is *dhikr Allāh*. That is why the Qur'an itself, after it says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAnkabūt (29:45):** "Surely the prayer restrains from indecency and wrongdoing…"[^s7-16]

— that the prayer forbids and restrains from indecency and wrongdoings — follows it with: "and the remembrance of God is greater" (*wa la-dhikru Allāhi akbar*). The remembrance of God is higher. This aspect and this dimension of the prayer is more important — that it is the remembrance of God; this is higher; the greatest property of the prayer is this. And the property of *dhikr Allāh* is this: the remembrance of God, attention to God, ever seeing God, knowing Him, considering Him present with oneself — one of its important properties is this: that it keeps this heart away from agitations, from temptations, from anxieties, from the various attractions, in every undertaking, on every road. This heart finds peace. Like a weight placed upon a boat of that sort, which makes it a little heavier, reduces a little of its movement and agitation. This is *dhikr Allāh*.

"Those who believe" — those who… (allow me to read the translation from this very note, so that there is again opportunity for you to correct it) — those who brought faith, and whose hearts attained steadiness and assurance through the remembrance of God — I deliberately brought in here the word "steadiness," that is, that state of calm — are guided toward God. This "are guided toward God" I wrote in parentheses; why? Because, as I stated, the verse is connected to what precedes, and in the previous verse it was said that they are guided toward God: "and He guides to Himself whoever turns" — those who believe — to the end of the verse. Therefore the "are guided" that we wrote in parentheses — "are guided toward God" — this translation is with attention and regard to the previous verse, where the guidance of this group is derived from the previous verse.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Raʿd (13:28):** "Surely, by the remembrance of God do hearts find rest."

— indeed, by means of the remembrance of God, hearts take on tranquility and steadiness. The remembrance of God has this astonishing property, and you see how effective tranquility and repose and steadiness are for the success of a person. The believer has this distinction; he enjoys this astonishing spiritual capacity. Its continuation:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Raʿd (13:29):** "Those who believe and do righteous deeds — blessedness is theirs, and a goodly return."

— those who brought faith and did good works, "blessedness is theirs, and a goodly return." (Of course, here again is a translation that I myself brought in this sheet; allow me to read it.) Those who brought faith and did righteous deeds — righteous deed meaning that they carried out the necessary commitments commensurate with faith — blessed be their today, and goodly be their morrow. This is a conclusion[^s7-17] that I drew from "blessedness is theirs, and a goodly return"; its translation differs a little from this sentence that I have written, but in summary and as a drawn conclusion it is this: their today good, their tomorrow too good; their world goodly, their Hereafter too goodly. And the reality, too, is just this. The believing society — and that, a believer who acts upon the commitments of faith — its world too is flourishing, its Hereafter too is flourishing; its world too is paradise, its Hereafter too is paradise. This is one verse.

Then we wrote a short commentary here, all of which I no longer wish to read now. The discussion is the disputation of Abraham with his people. Abraham, the Friend of the Merciful and the herald of monotheism in olden and ancient times, used to dispute and argue[^s7-18] with his own people and his own folk. They would argue with him, and he would argue with them and give answers to their words. The Qur'an narrates and recalls this disputation. It says, "And his people disputed with him"[^s7-19] — and his people, the people of Abraham, took to disputing with him, took to arguing with him. The Qur'an does not say what they said, how they argued with him, what their words were; but from the answer that Abraham gave them, which the Qur'an narrates, one guesses what their words were. I will first read the answer; then you yourselves guess what they were saying to Abraham.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:80):** "He said: Do you dispute with me concerning God…"[^s7-20]

When they took to disputing and arguing with Abraham, "He said: Do you dispute with me concerning God?" — do you argue with me about God? — "while He has guided me" — whereas God has guided me; that is, I am clear, I have no doubt in my own road, that you might wish to exploit that doubt of mine and, with argument and dispute, turn me back from my road. "And He has guided me" — I have been guided; I have found the way, I am clear, I know what I am doing — "and I do not fear what you associate with Him, except that my Lord should will something" — and from what you have set up as partners to God, I have no fear at all, save that God should will something concerning me; that is, in truth, I fear God. From the events that God may place on my road, and from the future that God may intend for me, I am apprehensive, and that is all; but of those things which you have set up as partners to God, I have no apprehension. From here a little becomes evident, and later more becomes evident; from this answer it is derived that they were saying to Abraham: Abraham, fear these partners that we have set up for God, fear them. These will wither you, these will torment you, will blacken your world, will embitter your days — no doubt that is so! Abraham, in answer, says: I do not fear them, I have no apprehension of them at all. From the continuation of the verse and Abraham's speech, this point becomes clearer and more manifest.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:80):** "My Lord encompasses all things in knowledge."

— my Lord's knowledge is extended over everything. *wasiʿa* — is extended; *rabbī* — my Lord; *kulla shayʾin* — over everything; *ʿilman* — in respect of knowledge. The fluent and simple Persian rendering of which becomes just what we wrote here: my Lord's knowledge is extended over everything. "Will you not then take heed?" *a-fa-lā tatadhakkarūn* — with all this, do you not come to yourselves? *tatadhakkarūn* — that is, do you not become aware? Do you not become attentive? Do you not find the state of coming to yourselves? Do you not call to mind? These all mean one thing; so that in the Persian translation that I wrote here, it comes to just what I stated: *a-fa-lā tatadhakkarūn* — that is, with all this, do you not come to yourselves? Well, Abraham's speech with his people still continues. This was one portion of it. (Today our work has become a little difficult on account of the situation of the mimeographing of this paper. Nonetheless, we have a responsibility to read this, and to read it correctly; this too — let us read it, and you correct it, and let us also at times compare with one another, at any rate.)

Following this matter, Abraham says: (here it has come out very poorly in my copy; gentlemen, correct it.)

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:81):** "And how should I fear what you associate, while you do not fear that you have associated with God that for which He has sent down upon you no authority?"

Here it becomes well clarified what argument and disputation they had with Abraham. Abraham says: How is it that I should fear?! It is you who should fear. Should I fear those whom you, without reason, have set up as partners to God and rivals to God in dominion and rule and command and creation and direction — whereas God has guided me, the matter is clear to me — and you do not fear that you have set up, without reason, without logic, without cause, with no rational warrant whatsoever, partners and rivals and peers to the Lord of the worlds? You should fear, not I. From here it becomes evident that they, in their argument with Abraham, were saying: Fear! Fear! Now, fear whom? Fear what? Whether the inanimate partners of God, or the living partners of God, or both — this is no longer evident. Which partners they said one must fear is not clear.

In summary, they set up all sorts and kinds of partners for God the Exalted. The calf-worshipers set up the calf, the stone- and wood-worshipers the stone and the wood, the Pharaoh- and Nimrod-worshipers Pharaoh and Nimrod. And from what we discussed concerning *tawhīd*, it becomes evident that that calf and that stone and that Pharaoh and that Nimrod are all kindling for one fire, are all filth of one valley, and have no difference among them. At any rate, these people were saying that one must fear the partners of God. Now the partners of God — whether the human partners, the partners among the jinn, the living partners, the inanimate partners, and the like — this, again, is not evident.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:81):** "And how should I fear what you have associated…"

— how should I fear what you have set up as partners to God — "while you do not fear that you have associated with God…" — and you do not fear that you have set up partners to God? "…that for which He has sent down upon you no authority" — something for which you have no proof or argument at all. The translation we wrote here is this: How should I fear what you have set up as partners to God, while you do not fear that you have associated partners with Him — even though you have no proof or demonstration whatsoever in this?

Then its continuation:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:81):** "…So which of the two parties has more right to security, if you but knew?"

— we or you? — I am more deserving and more fitting to be spiritually secure, to have spiritual security, to have no disquiet and anxiety; or you? I, whose heart is attached to God — Abraham says — I, who am the object of God's guidance, am I more free of disquiet, or you, wretched one, who in the road and the work you have taken up have no proof or argument, while to me the matter is clear? "So which of the two parties has more right to security, if you but knew?" — which of the two of us, I, Abraham, or you idol-worshipers, is more deserving of security? Abraham was saying. Then in parentheses I have explained: I, who have known God and have found the way to Him with insight and awareness, or you, who have no proof at all for your vain conjecture. "If you but knew!" — that is, it is evident what the result is, it is evident what the answer is, it is evident who is condemned to disquiet and fear and dread, and who is not condemned to these miseries and is assured.

And at the end He says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:82):** "Those who believe and do not clothe their faith with wrongdoing — for them is security, and they are the rightly guided."

Those who have brought faith and have not stained their faith with injustice and oppression — to them alone belongs security, and they are the rightly guided ones. This is concerning security.

Then we wrote: "4 — Being fruitful." What does this mean? It means that the fourth promise is this: being fruitful. One of the things that, if this traveler on the road of his goal and purpose has it, he can move better and there is greater likelihood of his arriving; and if he does not have it, he moves more slowly and the likelihood of his not arriving is also great — this is that he should consider his work fruitful or not. If he considered this road, his movement and his work and his step, fruitful — if he felt that this does not go to waste, his deed is not ruined, that upon this movement, upon this step, some effect follows, that each movement of his creates a wave that brings him nearer to the resting-place — if he believed this, he goes forward faster, works better, his fatigue is less, he moves more easily. If he did not believe this — alas! The believer is of this sort: the believer considers his own work fruitful, and the Qur'an has taught him this. The Qur'an, of course, has abundant instances:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Hūd (11:115):** "Indeed God does not let the reward of those who do good go to waste."[^s7-21]

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Kahf (18:30):** "…the reward of one who does good work [We do not let go to waste]."[^s7-22]

— from the beginning to the end of the Qur'an, beyond count. I have brought one instance of it from the beginning of the Qur'an, which was ready to hand, from Sūrat al-Baqarah. Ten instances, fifteen more instances — you yourselves find them too. (It is from Sūrat al-Baqarah; of course, we did not write its address here, but its address is Sūrat al-Baqarah, verse 147 or 8 — pardon me, 143 or 4 — because these verse numbers in this Qur'an are written very small and poorly.)

The verse is about the *qibla*. Let me first state the history of the *qibla* in a few words. When the Muslims were in Mecca, they used to pray facing the House of the Kaʿba. Before the migration, for prayer, for worship, facing the House of the Kaʿba. When they came to Medina — at the first entry into Medina — they used to pray facing Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis); by the command of the Lord, of course. The very thing the Jews too were doing. At that same time, the Jews of Medina too used to worship facing Jerusalem, and the Muslims likewise. Some time passed, and a verse was revealed:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:144):** "…so turn your face toward the Sacred Mosque."[^s7-23]

— turn toward the House of God, toward the Kaʿba, the Sacred Mosque. And again the Muslims took to praying toward that direction. The episode of the *qibla* has come in detail at the beginning of Sūrat al-Baqarah — numerous verses; perhaps seven, eight, ten verses about the *qibla* are at the beginning of Sūrat al-Baqarah, which explain the various dimensions of this episode. It is itself lengthy, and for now there is no need that we expound it.

One of these verses is this; which says to the believers, says to the Prophet — that the reason we, when you had come from Mecca, at the first entry into Medina, set your *qibla* toward Jerusalem, was this: We wanted a test to be carried out upon you. You, before you became Muslims, when you were in Mecca, even before becoming Muslims, held respect for the House of the Kaʿba, held a sanctity for it; and then, when you became Muslims, your prayer in Mecca was toward the House of the Kaʿba. Now that you have come to Medina, We wished to take from you, all at once and temporarily, an ancestral, forefathers' custom — to see how ready you are, for the sake of God, to trample customs underfoot. This is very important; this itself is a point. It is not connected to our discussion, but it is a very important point. You who are believers — are you ready, for the sake of God, to suddenly trample underfoot an ancestral, forefathers' custom, something to which you became attached, in which you took interest, for which you hold respect, or not? Not that you should imagine that the prayers which, during the time you were in Medina, at first you prayed facing Jerusalem — that We count these prayers as nothing, do not accept them, that the *qibla* of Jerusalem was a false *qibla* — no, certainly not. The things you did, all of them, in all their degrees, are accepted. Your efforts and your endeavors, O believers, both in the matter of the *qibla* and in general, are the object of God's confirmation and affirmation and the object of His reward and thanks. But this interval that took place was for this: that We might test. The verse is on this category. Now see from which part of the verse we wish to draw our point.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:143):** "And We did not appoint the *qibla* which you were upon…"[^s7-24]

— We did not appoint, O Prophet, that *qibla* which you were upon, that is, Jerusalem. So when was the verse revealed? At the time when they had turned back from Jerusalem to the Kaʿba; they were praying toward the Kaʿba. God says: that *qibla* which you formerly used to face — that is, Jerusalem — for what did We appoint it? "And We did not appoint the *qibla* which you were upon" — We did not appoint, did not ordain, the *qibla* which you were formerly upon — "except" — "that We might know" — except that it might become known — "who follows the Messenger from him who turns back on his heels" — those who follow the Prophet, distinct and separate from those who incline and lean toward their predecessors and turn back to the past, so that they become evident. So this is a test that He carried out upon you. The translation we made of the verse in this sheet is like this: We had not appointed your former *qibla*, Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis or Bayt al-Maqdas), in parentheses, except that the true followers of the Prophet might become known and distinguished from the followers of pagan customs. "And it was indeed grievous, except for those whom God has guided" — though it appeared exceedingly great and important, except for those whom God has guided. Those whose hearts had been guided — this matter was for them ordinary, was usual; they could digest it. The unguided, no.

Then the next phrase: "And God was never to let your faith go to waste" — this phrase is the object of our argument and citation. And God would never let your faith go to waste. It was not so that your faith and your deed and your work would remain wasted and void and without effect, that for a span of time you would mark time or not go forward. No! Each step of yours, every movement of yours, every footstep of yours has brought you one step, one footstep nearer to the goal. And to that same degree we were seen on the road of perfection and drew near to the goal. So your faith is not wasted.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:143):** "Indeed God is, toward the people, most kind, merciful."

— indeed God is, toward the people, compassionate and possessed of mercy. Well, take note: both this verse, and several or many other verses in the Qur'an, give the believers the promise of this matter: that your works, your faith, your belief, your deed do not go to waste, are not without effect, are not neutralized; that is to say what? They are fruitful. And if indeed this state comes to exist in the believers, it is very clear and inevitable that the traversing of the road of perfection becomes easier and simpler for him.

Tomorrow our discussion is about *tawhīd*.

---

**Chapter Two:**

**Tawhid**

- Tawhid in the worldview of Islam
- Tawhid in the ideology of Islam
- The exclusive worship and obedience of God
- The spirit of tawhid: the negation of servitude to other than God
- Tawhid and the negation of social classes
- The psychological effects of tawhid

[^s7-1]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (the blessed sūrah), verse 139.

[^s7-2]: Sūrat al-Mujādilah (the blessed sūrah), verse 21.

[^s7-3]: Sūrat al-Ṣāffāt (the blessed sūrah), verse 173.

[^s7-4]: Ḥadīth no. 36.

[^s7-5]: *lā yazāl* (root: z-w-l): inexhaustible, never coming to an end.

[^s7-6]: *qarīb al-ufuq*: close to one another (lit. "near in horizon").

[^s7-7]: *sāzobarg*: the gear and equipment given to a soldier.

[^s7-8]: Sūrat al-Fajr (the blessed sūrah), verses 27 and 28: "O soul at peace! ✷ Return to your Lord, while you are well-pleased with Him and He is well-pleased with you."

[^s7-9]: Sūrat al-Tawbah (the blessed sūrah), verse 26.

[^s7-10]: One of the battles of the Noble Prophet, which occurred after the conquest of Mecca.

[^s7-11]: Ḥadīth no. 13. (From *Nahj al-Balāghah*: "By God, I am not like the hyena that sleeps to the long [lulling of those who hunt it].")

[^s7-12]: Sūrat al-Tawbah (the blessed sūrah), verses 25 and 26.

[^s7-13]: This renewal of allegiance, which took place during the episode of the Truce of Ḥudaybiyya, became known as the Bayʿat al-Riḍwān (the Pledge of Riḍwān). Verse 18 of Sūrat al-Fatḥ (the blessed sūrah) refers to this pledge: "Indeed God was well-pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you beneath the tree; He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down tranquility (*sakīnah*) upon them and rewarded them with a near victory." — truly, God was well-pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you beneath that tree, and He recognized what was in their hearts and sent down upon them tranquility and rewarded them with a near victory.

[^s7-14]: Sūrat al-Tawbah (the blessed sūrah), verse 40.

[^s7-15]: Please refer to the mimeographed sheet at the end of the session.

[^s7-16]: Sūrat al-ʿAnkabūt (the blessed sūrah), verse 45.

[^s7-17]: *muḥaṣṣal* (root: ḥ-ṣ-l): that which has been concluded; a drawn-out result.

[^s7-18]: *mujādalah* (root: j-d-l): to dispute, to wrangle; *muḥājjah* (root: ḥ-j-j): to bring forth a proof/argument.

[^s7-19]: Sūrat al-Anʿām (the blessed sūrah), verses 80 to 82.

[^s7-20]: Sūrat al-Anʿām (the blessed sūrah), verses 80 to 82.

[^s7-21]: Sūrat Hūd (the blessed sūrah), verse 115.

[^s7-22]: Sūrat al-Kahf (the blessed sūrah), verse 30.

[^s7-23]: Sūrat al-Baqarah (the blessed sūrah), verse 144.

[^s7-24]: Sūrat al-Baqarah (the blessed sūrah), verse 143.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

# Part Two: Tawhid

### توحید


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 8: Tawhid in the Islamic Worldview

*توحید در جهان‌بینی اسلام — delivered Thursday, 4 Mehr 1353 / 9 Ramadan al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:255):** "God — there is no deity but He, the Living, the Self-Subsisting. Neither drowsiness nor sleep overtakes Him. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is there that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what lies before them and what lies behind them, and they encompass nothing of His knowledge except what He wills.
