His Throne extends over the heavens and the earth, and the guarding of them does not weary Him. He is the Most High, the Most Great." (255)

In brief, what we are setting out to do is this: to single out the verses about tawhid in the Qur'an, to translate them here for you, and to let you yourselves deduce, first, what tawhid means from the Qur'an's standpoint, and second, what commitment and what practical conduct in life it implies.

And of course you have not forgotten — and must not forget — our earlier discussions, where we said that faith in any idea, in any principle among religious ideas and principles, must, first, be conscious; it must rest upon understanding, awareness, and insight, not be blind. And second, faith must be accompanied by commitment. That which we are to believe in is, inevitably, something that lays a commitment upon our shoulders in our lives, in our conduct — whether individual conduct or social conduct, whether it concerns our own person, or our own community, or humanity at large, or the future of history. Inevitably, it places a commitment upon our shoulders.

With this introduction in mind, when we enter the discussion of tawhid, this discussion will naturally be, for us, a discussion that imposes commitment. We will examine tawhid in such a way that, first, we may arrive at tawhid consciously; and second, that we may see what commitments tawhid places upon our shoulders. Is tawhid merely a kind of irresponsible, commitment-free understanding — a knowing of some truth without that knowing imposing any obligation upon us? Or is it otherwise? Is tawhid a form of awareness and knowledge such that, following upon this knowing, duties, obligations, and responsibilities devolve upon the human being? When we turn to the Qur'an, we will find the answer to this question.

Therefore, the verses we have in view today concern tawhid from one particular standpoint. Tomorrow, the verses will again concern tawhid, but from a different standpoint; and on the coming days, again there will be verses about tawhid, each from a standpoint of its own. And I do not claim that, with a discussion lasting a few days about tawhid, drawing upon the verses of the Qur'an, I will be able to set forth all the dimensions of Quranic and Islamic tawhid. Certainly, if some people, with a more precise eye, with greater clarity of vision, and with a more leisurely study, were to turn to the Qur'an, they would find a boundless ocean in the field of tawhid. To the measure that our gathering's time allows — for even all that we ourselves know about tawhid cannot be set forth in summary form in this gathering — to the extent that the capacity of our time and the scope of our assembly permit, I will speak.

(There is one point I should mention beforehand: the discussion of tawhid, within the Quranic verses and in the style in which we will present it, may perhaps seem a bit heavy. It may perhaps seem like a lesson rather than a lecture. But I have always thought, and still think, to myself: what necessity is there that our public gatherings should not have the character of a lesson, and that a lecture should be something other than a lesson? What objection is there to it, that in a public gathering, with a large crowd, the minds present in the assembly should be ready to listen — ready to hear difficult things that require reflection and thought, just as in a classroom? What necessity is there that — especially during the days of the month of Ramadan, since it is the month of Ramadan and mouths are fasting — the speaker should see himself obliged and compelled to present the material in a more entertaining, more pleasant manner, mixed with more diversionary matters? Why? Until when must we, in our public gatherings and assemblies, give up the hope of thinking better, higher, and deeper? Why should we give it up?

Therefore, you brothers and sisters who are present in this gathering, and who will be present on the coming days — I do not know whether the material will seem heavy to you or not; it may seem heavy, it may seem quite ordinary — but if the material seems weighty and heavy to you, treat it as something that, in the end, must be understood: think about it, study it. Mobilize all the power of your mind in order to understand the material. Keep the words in your mind, hold onto them; if it is difficult, then later raise it with your friends and companions. If the discussion is one that seems unfamiliar to your mind, do not try to push it away from your mind immediately on the grounds that this discussion is heavy and weighty — no indeed; try, rather, to make your mind familiar with it.

And of course, as we have said in many of our discussions, lectures, and lessons: we never expect, even from a single person, that he should accept what we say one hundred percent and as a certainty — no. On the contrary, we expect of our friends, brothers, and sisters, at whatever level you are: that which is given to you as nourishment and intellectual substance — even if you are not like this elsewhere, here be like this — that which is given to you, place under the laboratory[^s8-1] of your own perception, understanding, discernment, and independent judgment, and make yourselves an instance of this noble verse of the Qur'an, which says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Zumar (39:17–18):** "So give good news to My servants — those who listen to the word and follow the best of it."[^s8-2] Give good news to My servants — to those who hear the word and choose its finest part and follow it.)

In any case, we have considered tawhid from two standpoints — though, in the course of arranging the material, other branches may perhaps come up that need to be mentioned. First, we have considered tawhid within the worldview of Islam. There is, most certainly, tawhid within the worldview of Islam. What is tawhid in the worldview of Islam, and what does it mean? And the verse of the Qur'an, the eloquent and articulate language of the Qur'an — how does it expound the tawhid that figures within the worldview of Islam? The second discussion we put thus: tawhid in the ideology of Islam. Of course, I will give a brief explanation of the term "worldview" for those who do not know it, and of the term "ideology" for those who do not know it; for tawhid is part both of the worldview of Islam and of the life-constructing ideology of Islam.

Beyond these, tawhid manifests and shows itself in every one of the subsidiary ordinances of Islam. And wherever you observe a ruling, a law, or a command put forward in the name of religion, in which tawhid limps — in which there is something anti-tawhid, and tawhid is absent — know that this is not from Islam. For tawhid is like a spirit within the body of all the ordinances of Islam. It is like a fine, subtle air present in all the parts of this edifice and frame whose name is Islam. It is like pure, clean, clear, fresh blood throughout this body and frame whose name is Islam and religion — it is even present in all the capillaries. You cannot find a single ruling in Islam that does not bear some color and trace of tawhid. Therefore today's discussion is about tawhid in the worldview of Islam.

What does the worldview of Islam mean? When you, as a human being, turn to your own thought, to your own perception, concerning the universe, the human being, and the world, you arrive at certain conceptions. It may be that you have not given thought to this; but the one who does give thought to it — when he thinks about the world, thinks about the human being, thinks about the relationship between the world and the human being, thinks about something beyond nature and beyond the human being and the world — arrives at a chain of conceptions and ideas. This is what is called a worldview. Every school of thought has a particular vision, a particular standpoint, a particular conception, a particular understanding of the world; this particular conception of the world, this special understanding of the universe, this manner of seeing the universe — its name is worldview. It is among the terms that have only in the last several years come to be seen and noticed in Persian expression. "Worldview" does not mean breadth of outlook, in the sense commonly used in colloquial expression — "so-and-so is a person of worldview," meaning he is broad-minded — no; worldview does not mean this.

In brief, worldview can be defined thus: worldview means a human being's conception of the world, a human being's apprehension of the world, a human being's understanding of the world or of the human being. We said "a human being's conception"; but you may, in place of "a human being," put "a school," "a creed," "a religion," "a social system" — it has a conception of the world, and this conception is what is called its worldview. Islam has a worldview; Islam has a conception of the world. Islam's conception of the world, to the extent that it bears upon our discussion of tawhid, I will set forth briefly.

Islam holds that the whole of this aggregate whose name is "the world" — from top to bottom, from the most insignificant and lowly beings to the great and conspicuous beings; from the basest living or lifeless thing to the noblest and most powerful of living beings possessed of intellect, that is, the human being — all of it, every part of it, every corner of this universe, is a servant, a thrall, a creature of, and dependent upon, an exceedingly mighty power. Beyond this surface that you and I see, beyond what the keen-sighted spectacles of empirical science can reach, beyond all the phenomena that can be sensed and touched, there is a truth higher than all truths, more exalted, nobler, dearer; and all these phenomena of the universe are made, fashioned, and brought into being by the hand of His power. To that higher power we give a name; its name is "God," its name is "Allah."

So the world is a truth that has no independence in itself; it did not bring itself into existence; it did not well up from within and from inside itself. Rather, a powerful hand created and brought into being these various phenomena — which, with every passing day that knowledge advances, show themselves more and more. It is a powerful hand that created that tumult in the heart of the atom, and that, in the highest unknown worlds — the galaxies, and what lies beyond the galaxies, where it may be that there are things to be discovered billions of times greater than all we have discovered to this day — brought all of this into being. This factory has a maker; this apparatus has an originator; it is not a matter of chance, it is not self-existent. Islam regards the world as just such a thing.

These are the materials of the Islamic worldview in the field of tawhid, which I am stating in just this way, little by little, briefly. (Commit them to your minds, so that, God willing, if tomorrow's written text is ready, in the writing too we shall set down something in brief.) That God who is higher than the world and the worldly, that hand of power that turns all the beings of the universe — He made them and He, moment by moment, has kept them enduring; this powerful hand — whose name is Allah, whose name is God — is characterized,[^s8-3] in an original and essential manner, with all the good and goodness-creating attributes: that is, He possesses knowledge, He possesses power, He possesses life, He possesses will and resolve, and all that springs and derives from these attributes. His life was not taken from anyone; His knowledge was not acquired or obtained from anywhere; and so on.

He is the Sovereign of the universe. Who are the inhabitants of the universe? What are the particles of the universe in relation to Him? Once the particles of the universe have been made and fashioned by Him, did they then become separate — like a child that separates from its mother and is thereafter independent? No; the matter is not at all like that. At every moment, for their remaining and their being, they are in need of Him — in need of His power and His will; and all are His servants. All beings are His servants, His thralls, His artifacts, His creatures. He can dispose over all of them; He created all of them with a particular order, brought all of them into being with regular and precise norms and laws. Today, science is discovering these laws — though, of course, my discussion is not in this field. That is, the argument for the existence of God and the proof of the Maker is not part of my discussion; there are abundant books in this field — go and read them.

Nevertheless, it would not be amiss to recall this one statement, on the authority of several scholars of the empirical sciences — not philosophers, but scholars whose work has to do precisely with the laboratory, with industry, with construction, and with such things. In this book — which two or three accomplished Iranian translators have translated, and which is the work of several non-Iranian scholars and authors, titled *The Proof of the Existence of God*[^s8-4] (it is a good book, it would not be amiss to consult it) — several scholars of today's empirical sciences say that, with the advance of knowledge, with penetration into the inmost nature of creatures and beings and the discovery of regular laws, we come to understand that the world has an originator. From the sheer extent to which we see that order exists, the sheer extent to which orderliness and discipline are at work in the affairs of the universe, we understand that the world has a Creator and a Maker. Well, then: all the beings of the universe are His servants, His originated ones, all under His authority and within the grip of His power — and the human being too, like the rest of the other beings.

In the worldview of Islam, tawhid is just such a thing; tawhid means that the world has an Originator and a Maker, and, in a manner of speaking, has a pure and subtle spirit. The world has an originator, and all the parts of this universe are reckoned as servants, thralls, and beings under the authority of that God and that Originator. Tawhid in the worldview of Islam is this; that is, when a Muslim looks upon this universe from the standpoint of Islam, he does not see this universe as an independent being, but rather sees a being dependent upon a higher power. What effect does this have? What benefit does it have? It has a remarkable effect; in the subsequent discussions that we hold about tawhid, it will then become clear what effect this vision, this particular worldview, this perception and conception of the universe, this apprehension of the world and the worldly, has upon the building of life.

We turn to the verses of the Qur'an, and we see that the Lord of the universe has mentioned precisely this same matter in the Quranic verses. From two places in the Qur'an we have selected verses to be recited and translated today. The first is from Sūrat al-Baqarah, the verse famous as Āyat al-Kursī. The first part of Āyat al-Kursī is in this field, which we will now recite, translate, and reflect upon. It is verse 255.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:255):** "In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. God — there is no deity but He."[^s8-5]

"Allah" — this is a name for which an equivalent, by way of exposition and elucidation, cannot be found. What is "Allah"? It is God. And who is God? He is that being of whom it is said: *Lā ilāha illā Huwa* — there is no deity but He. Understand *ilāh* as "object of worship." *Ilāh* means any being before whom the human being submits in the manner of sanctification,[^s8-6] of veneration and reverence — hands over his own free will to him, entrusts the reins of his life to him, sets him up as one given a free hand and unrestrained[^s8-7] over his own life. This, in Quranic terminology, is called *ilāh*. Those who make the desire of the self the holder of the reins of their lives — their *ilāh* is the desire of their self. Those who give a free hand to a rebellious, transgressing human being in the affairs of their lives — their *ilāh* is that very Satan. Those who submit, unconditionally and unreservedly, to hollow customs and beliefs — their *ilāh* is that very hollow custom and belief. Whatever, in the human being's existence and in the human being's life, has a free hand unconditionally, and rules and dominates — that is an *ilāh*.

Here it says: *Lā ilāha illā Huwa* — there is no *ilāh*, no object of worship, but He. What does "there is not" mean? Does it mean there is none in the world? There were thousands of objects of worship in the world; three hundred and sixty of them were hung and set up within the House of the Kaʿba itself — three hundred and sixty idols. And just as many living idols, too, were ruling in the world. How then is there no *ilāh*! So this that says *Lā ilāha illā Huwa* — "there is no *ilāh* but He" — means there is no real *ilāh*, no rightful *ilāh*, no true *ilāh* but Him; that is, anyone other than Allah whom you have accepted in divinity — in that sense of worship-worthiness which I described — you have committed a sin, you have performed an act contrary to right. For other than Allah, no one is worthy of being an object of worship and of divinity.

*Allāhu lā ilāha illā Huwa* — what is the distinctive quality of this Allah, this unique and single *ilāh*? He enumerates some of His qualities: *al-Ḥayy* — He is the Living. All the others are dead; the others are dead beings and corpses; He is the Living. The lifeless ones — that is plain; but the living ones too are dead. A living thing that on one day did not exist and on another day will not exist; a living creature whose very life is constantly under threat; a living being whose life, with the movement of a single hair, with the stirring of the brain, is sometimes threatened with annihilation and destruction — what kind of life is this? The everlasting Living, the original Living, the true Living, is the one to whom life truly belongs, and which is His gift, His bestowal, His grant to all living things — and He is God. *Al-Ḥayy* — the Living. *Al-Qayyūm* — the Enduring and Sustaining; the one whose life is permanent, eternal, and perpetual; the one by whose life the living are alive, and if He were not, if He did not will, if He did not so resolve, not a single living thing and not a single manifestation of life would remain in the world. *Al-Qayyūm*.

*Lā taʾkhudhuhū sinatun wa-lā nawm* — neither drowsiness nor sleep takes hold of Him; light sleep — let alone heavy sleep. It does not seize Him away from Himself. What does this mean? It means that a moment of heedlessness, a moment of inattention, has no way into His being and into His life. The other beings, the false lords, are heedless — of themselves, of the people under their authority, of the world that is in their grip; they are heedless from head to foot, constantly in heedlessness; and even where they claim awareness and knowledge, their awareness and knowledge is false. What prevails over them is heedlessness and ignorance.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Ibrāhīm (14:28–29):** "Have you not seen those who exchanged the blessing of God for ingratitude, and brought their people down into the abode of ruin — Hell, which they will enter; and an evil place of settlement it is."[^s8-8]

It was this verse of the Qur'an that Mūsā ibn Jaʿfar (peace be upon him) recited to Hārūn al-Rashīd. "Have you not seen those who showed ingratitude for the blessings of God, who were unthankful, and who led their own people and their own followers into the valley of destruction, nonexistence, perdition, and wretchedness? Where is that valley of wretchedness and nonexistence? Hell! They themselves too entered Hell, and they dragged their wretched followers after them into Hell as well — and what an evil dwelling-place Hell is." Mūsā ibn Jaʿfar (peace be upon him) was saying to Hārūn, "You are among them."[^s8-9] Well, if Hārūn is not heedless, why would he drag himself and his people into the Fire? So the other gods are heedless from head to foot.

The One whom heedlessness does not seize is the real ruler of this world; that is, Allah. *Lā taʾkhudhuhū sinatun wa-lā nawm.* Do not imagine that *sina*, meaning drowsiness — light sleep — and *nawm*, meaning ordinary or heavy sleep, being removed from God and negated of Him, served no purpose here. They did. In the subsequent discussions we will see why. For every allusion in tawhid, every fine point in tawhid, is an allusion to the negation of the divinity of other-than-God, and an allusion to the deficiencies of other-than-God. Everything that is affirmed of God is precisely those things that are negated of the claimants to divinity. What is said in tawhid is precisely those things that must be reflected in the practical life of the monotheists and the worshippers of God. All the qualities and fine details of tawhid must have their instances in the lives of the monotheist people — which comes up in the subsequent discussions and recitations. Therefore, *lā taʾkhudhuhū sinatun wa-lā nawm* is an allusion to the heedlessness, the slumbers, the lapses of the false lords and lordlings.

*Lā taʾkhudhuhū sinatun wa-lā nawm; lahu mā fī al-samāwāti wa-mā fī al-arḍ* — His is whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth; it belongs to Him, it is His servant. *Man dhā alladhī yashfaʿu ʿindahū illā bi-idhnih* — who is there that can intercede before Him, mediate, except by His permission? We know of no other power that, even to the measure of the power of a single intercessor, could stand up before God. If anyone intercedes for others, he still does so by the permission of God. The prophets who intercede; the saints, the Imams, the righteous, the believers, and the martyrs who intercede before God — they do not do so except by the permission of God. Therefore they too are not powers set up against the power of God, not shops set up against God. It is not that they too are a separate establishment and apparatus set up against the establishment and apparatus of God — no; they are servants of God, only servants who are objects of God's grace and love.

*Man dhā alladhī yashfaʿu ʿindahū illā bi-idhnih* — who is it that can intercede before Him except by His permission and leave? *Yaʿlamu mā bayna aydīhim wa-mā khalfahum* — whatever is before them and behind them, He knows; that is, He encompasses the whole life of human beings and of all beings. *Wa-lā yuḥīṭūna bi-shayʾin min ʿilmihī illā bi-mā shāʾ* — they do not encompass, their knowledge does not extend to anything of the knowledge of the Lord, except what He Himself wills.

See how He divides the whole of the universe into two ranks. One rank: the rank of God; the other rank: the rank of the other beings. In this other rank, all the particles of the universe are servants of God in a single degree. I mean to say that, with respect to servanthood to God, there is no distinction between any two beings in the universe. In this respect — that they are servants of God, that they are under the power and within the grip of God — they are all alike, all of them! Even the greatest being of the universe, the dearest and most precious human being of history — that is, the sacred person of the Noble Prophet (God's blessings be upon him and his household) — with respect to being a servant of God, being under the authority of God, being a slave of God, is like all the other particles of existence. To the same degree that they are under the authority of God, to that same degree the Prophet too is under the authority of God. It is not that a certain prophet, a certain station, a certain great human being, because he is great, because he is select, because he is beloved of God, because he is dear to God, should have some store of power set up against God — no; they are bound-handed servants, submitted to the Lord, and their very greatness lies in just this.

In the *tashahhud* of the prayer you recite: *Ashhadu anna Muḥammadan ʿabduhū wa-rasūluh* — you bring being a servant of God forward first; I bear witness that Muḥammad is His servant — His slave — and His Messenger. You first mention being a servant.

*Wasiʿa kursiyyuhu al-samāwāti wa-l-arḍ* — His throne of power has spread wide over the heavens and the earth. *Wa-lā yaʾūduhū ḥifẓuhumā* — the safeguarding of the heavens and the earth is not heavy or burdensome upon Him. *Wa-Huwa al-ʿAliyyu al-ʿAẓīm* — He is the Most High and Most Honored in rank; He is the Great and Majestic.

From these verses, what do you understand from the whole? Apart from the fine details that are in the verse, apart from the subtleties and intricacies that are in every single sentence — of which some part you may understand, or I may understand, and there are tens or hundreds of other subtleties and fine points that no one understands except the chosen servants of God. From the phrase *Allāhu lā ilāha illā Huwa*, Imam al-Sajjād understands subtleties and fine points that you and I do not understand. Apart from these fine points, apart from these subtleties, apart from the social or doctrinal matters that are present here — from the whole of it, what do you understand?

From the whole, you grasp well the vision of Islam concerning God; and what is it? It is this: that throughout the entire domain of existence there is one power, named God; one center of power, one focus of knowledge, life, and force, named God. And on the opposite side, all the phenomena, facing that mighty and majestic power in a state of destitution,[^s8-10] in a state of servitude, in a state of servanthood — all of them, all of them. And there is no difference, either, among the phenomena of the universe with respect to servitude before that center of power. From a tiny particle to a mighty galaxy, from a believing human being to a disbelieving one, from a worthless being to a worthy human being — all, before that being, are bound-handed, submitted, slaves, servants, thralls; whatever expression you wish to use.

Of course, understanding this subject has effects in the recognition of the ideology of Islam, in the recognition of the practical programs of Islam for society. By way of example — I can now point this out so that a flash of lightning may strike in your minds, that you may know we do not wish to engage in philosophical hairsplitting — once we have understood that all human beings are, in sum, equal[^s8-11] before this power and this center of force, it then no longer makes sense for the emperor of Rome — Valerian,[^s8-12] for instance — in a state of pride and arrogance, to have his statue built there with a slave, a bondsman, fallen at his feet. We ask: why? On what grounds? Has this exalted-stationed emperor found, apart from the rank of the servants of God, some other rank? And has this human being who has now fallen at the emperor's feet, who is prostrating himself before him, who is bowing and abasing himself before him — has he found, apart from the rank of servants, some lower, baser rank? Are not the two of them both attached to one and the same rank? Then why this situation?

Look: if this worldview, this vision, this conception of the world did not exist, the emperors of the universe, the bullies of history, the great landowners and the wealthy and the great aristocrats who existed throughout history — who had thousands and millions of servants, thralls, and captives of power, obedient to their command and order — these could say, "Sir, I am a thing apart, woven from a different cloth; I am other than that one; he must fall at my feet, I must set my foot upon his head; I was created to give commands, he was created to obey; I was created to live as a lord, he was made to be wretched; I am the servant of a god whose power is greater, he is the servant of a god whose power is less." It is like the thing the Children of Israel used to say. The Children of Israel used to say that our God, the God of the universe, is Yahweh,[^s8-13] and Yahweh's favor toward the Children of Israel is greater. The idol-worshipping polytheists of India used to say that society is divided into four classes, each class having a particular god of its own, and that each was created from a particular place. But the pure tawhidi Islamic vision says that all possible existents and beings were created, made, and fashioned from one place, from one origin, from one hand of power; all are servants and thralls before Him; all are captives of His power before Him; all must obey His command. No one has the right to lay his head at the feet of another, just as no one has the right to set his foot upon the head of another. Just as you have no right to set your foot upon someone's head, you have no right to lay your head at someone's feet. For what reason? For the reason that, in either case, you have acted contrary to right and truth. The one who is mounted upon a horse, with servants and thralls writhing beneath the hooves of his horse, is no different from those servants and thralls.

*Man dhā alladhī yashfaʿu ʿindahū illā bi-idhnih* — who is closer to Him? Who has, before Him, any power? Who can have, before Him, any sense of self-importance? Yes — *illā bi-idhnih*: some can intercede, but by His own permission. He never grants the permission of intercession to tyrants; He grants the permission of intercession to the Imams. He never grants to the corrupt the permission of nearness and the leave of intercession. To the prophets, to the saints, to the righteous, to the martyrs, to the high-stationed humble ones — those servants for whom worldly life passed bitterly and poisonously, yet whose spirits grew strong; who moved along the path of duty, who were hard and severe upon themselves so that they might draw one step nearer toward duty — they can; they can intercede before God. And even they — the reason they are able to intercede is this: that they performed the servanthood of God more fully; for they placed themselves more fully under the power of God. No one performed the servanthood of God in the time of the Prophet as the Prophet of God did; no one performed the servanthood of God in the time of the Commander of the Faithful as the Commander of the Faithful did; no one performed the servanthood of God in the time of Imam al-Sajjād as Imam al-Sajjād did. This that the Prophet is the highest of people, that ʿAlī is the highest of people, that Imam al-Sajjād is the highest of people — it is not on account of, say, supposing a man of Shushtar against a man of Dezful;[^s8-14] it is because they performed the servanthood of God more fully that their station is higher, that their station is more honored.

From the overall situation of this verse — which is Āyat al-Kursī — this meaning emerges: that the Lord of the universe is that absolute power, and that before Him all beings, all created things, the whole universe, are servants, obedient, bound-handed, and submitted; and whoever wishes to draw nearer to Him must be more fully His servant. This is one verse.

The other verse is in Sūrat Maryam; we have selected from Sūrat Maryam, verse 88 onward:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Maryam (19:88):** "And they said, 'The Most Merciful has taken a son.'"

They said that the Merciful God has chosen a son for Himself. The disbelievers have voiced this claim in various forms: the Christians in one way, the Jews in another, the polytheists of Quraysh and the polytheists of Arabia in another, the polytheists of other places in yet another. Some said God has a daughter; some said He has a son; some said He has a daughter and a son; some said He has one; some said He has an infinite number — He has a household! In any case, they said: *Ittakhadha al-Raḥmānu waladā* — God has a child. From what angle did they say "He has a child"? Note this point carefully: that God having a child — which was claimed by a group of polytheists, or Christians, or Jews — meant that among the creatures of the universe, among the beings, there is one individual whose relation to God is not the relation of being a servant — note this carefully — but the relation of being a child. He is a young lord's son, not a bondsman; though he is not the lord himself. The Jews, who used to say *ʿUzayr ibn Allāh* — that is, the prophet ʿUzayr is the son of God — wanted to say that, even if all the beings of the universe are servants and thralls of God, ʿUzayr is outside this category; he is no longer a servant of God, he is the young lord's son of God, the light of the eye of God. The Christians, too, who voiced this claim concerning the Messiah; and the disbelievers, too, who said concerning al-Lāt, Manāt, and al-ʿUzzā[^s8-15] that these are the daughters of God; and the polytheists of Greece and Rome, who likewise held that God had numerous god-sons and lordly sons — all of them held this same view. That is, in reality, these two ranks that we have considered — one the rank of God, and one the rank of all the servants and beings, all of whom are humbled before God — these two ranks they were turning into three ranks. They said: God; the servants; and the elite [a class in between] — a child, a young lord's son, a god-son, something. The verses of Sūrat Maryam negate this matter. Pay close attention to the verses through to the end.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Maryam (19:88–91):** "And they said, 'The Most Merciful has taken a son.' Surely you have brought forth a monstrous, grievous thing. The heavens are nearly torn apart by it, and the earth split asunder, and the mountains brought down in collapse — that they should ascribe a son to the Most Merciful."

*Wa-qālū ittakhadha al-Raḥmānu waladā* — they said that the Merciful God has chosen and taken a son; *laqad jiʾtum shayʾan iddā* — you have indeed brought forth an awful and weighty saying. See what God's expression is: a weighty saying, an awful saying, a very dangerous belief you have advanced. *Laqad jiʾtum shayʾan iddā* — truly a heavy and awful saying you have brought forth. *Takādu al-samāwātu yatafaṭṭarna minhu wa-tanshaqqu al-arḍu wa-takhirru al-jibālu haddā* — the heavens were near to bursting apart from one another, to splitting open, and the earth to rending asunder, and the mountains to crumbling down in ruin. *An daʿaw li-l-Raḥmāni waladā* — that they called upon the Merciful a son and held a son for Him.

It is plain that the matter is very grave. God is not such that some obscenity, some bad word, would offend Him; the Lord of the universe is not subject to feelings. That which He gives to the people as a belief is that which has a part in securing the divine aims. And that which He negates as a corrupt belief is that which, believing in it, has a part in securing the corruption of society. The negation of a corrupt belief means the negation of a vein of corruption in human society. And the belief that God has offspring — has a god-son and a lordly son, has a child — the belief in an intermediary between God and humankind, a separating link, this belief has corrupting effects in society, the corruptions of which, of course, will become clear gradually in the course of the tawhidi discussions. This is, in reality, a pretext for the servants, besides becoming the servant of God, to become the servant of yet another as well.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Maryam (19:92–94):** "And it does not befit the Most Merciful that He should take a son. There is none in the heavens and the earth but that he comes to the Most Merciful as a servant. He has indeed counted them and numbered them, numbering."

*Wa-mā yanbaghī li-l-Raḥmāni an yattakhidha waladā* — it does not befit the Merciful that He should take a son. *In kullu man fī al-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi illā ātī al-Raḥmāni ʿabdā* — all who are in the heavens and the earth are nothing but ones who come before God in servitude and servanthood; all are servants of God, all are God's servants. *Laqad aḥṣāhum wa-ʿaddahum ʿaddā* — He has enumerated them, He has taken them in His grip and counted them, a thorough counting. This too is the other verse that was in Sūrat Maryam.

Well, then: in brief, the discussion we had today amounted to this — what does tawhid in the worldview of Islam mean, and what is it? The examination of tawhid as one of the materials of the principles of the worldview. Tomorrow, God willing, we will come to the examination of tawhid as one of the materials of the principles of the ideology of Islam. See, these two differ from each other; this is the introduction to the other, this is the ground for the other. The Islamic vision is this; it sees the world, the universe, the realm of existence, in this way. Well, now — what does this vision inspire in us? What course, what plan, what design for life does it set forth? What is that? What role does tawhid play there? "Tawhid in the ideology of Islam."

---

[^s8-1]: A scientific and technical laboratory.

[^s8-2]: Sūrat al-Zumar, verses 17 and 18.

[^s8-3]: From the root *(w-ṣ-f)*: possessor of an attribute.

[^s8-4]: *The Proof of the Existence of God*, compiled by John Clover Monsma, is a book comprising forty essays by scholars of the modern sciences on the proof of the existence of God. This book was translated by Messrs. Aḥmad Ārām, ʿAlī-Akbar Mujtahidī, and Sayyid Mahdī Amīn.

[^s8-5]: In the sessions held during the year 1352, at the Mosque of Karāmat in Mashhad, when the author was devoting himself to the translation of the invocations of the prayer, he presented a translation comprehensible to the general public of these verses. The sixth discourse of the collected discourses titled *Ṣahbā*, under the title "The Detailed Translation of Āyat al-Kursī," is devoted to that lecture.

[^s8-6]: From the root *(q-d-s)*: to deem pure / hallow.

[^s8-7]: Without restraint or bond.

[^s8-8]: Sūrat Ibrāhīm, verses 28 and 29.

[^s8-9]: *Tafsīr Nūr al-Thaqalayn*, under verse 28 of Sūrat Ibrāhīm.

[^s8-10]: From the root *(s-k-n)*: poverty, indigence.

[^s8-11]: Equal, on a par.

[^s8-12]: He was among the great emperors of Rome, who subjected the Christians to much persecution and torment. This king, toward the end of his life, was taken captive in a war with the Sasanians. The Christians called this captivity a divine retribution upon Valerian, who had persecuted the Christians.

[^s8-13]: The name of God Most High among the Jews. It is a name applied to God in the Torah, signifying the everlastingness of the God of the Jews.

[^s8-14]: This is an example illustrating the meaning of mutual boastings and tribal prejudices over one another. [Shushtar and Dezful are neighboring towns in Khuzestan whose inhabitants were noted for their local rivalry.]

[^s8-15]: In verses 19 and 20 of Sūrat al-Najm, the names of these three idols of the polytheists are mentioned.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 9: Tawhid in Islamic Ideology

*توحید در ایدئولوژی اسلام — delivered Friday, 5 Mehr 1353 / 10 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:165):** "And among mankind are some who take, besides God, rivals [whom] they love as they should love God. But those who believe are stronger in love for God. If only those who do wrong could see — when they behold the punishment — that all power belongs to God, and that God is severe in punishment."[^s9-1]

The theist says that beyond what we see, there is a reality higher and more magnificent than what we observe, and that were it not for that reality, these phenomena would never have taken form. The materialist says: no; we cannot believe in, or commit ourselves to, anything other than what we see. In our laboratories and experiments, however much we have searched, we have found no sign and no trace of this being you speak of. Let the dispute between the materialist and the theist remain for the books and discussions devoted specifically to that subject.

Our belief is that the materialists of our age — and we are not concerned here with Democritus of history, that other materialist atheist of ten centuries ago, Democritus[^s9-2] or some such scholar who lived twenty centuries, thirty centuries ago; we have no quarrel with him, he died and even his bones have turned to dust — the materialist of *our* time, we hold, if he says there is no God, if he believes that beyond this world no other reality exists, in truth says this because he has suffered an intellectual and spiritual disillusionment with the school of theism. Because he believes that the building of today's world, the administration of human beings, the establishment of justice, and the removal of discrimination are impossible except in the shadow of a materialist, materialistic[^s9-3] mode of thought — for this reason he turns his back on the school of the theists. If we look carefully at the intellectual condition of those who, over the last fifty or sixty years, have embraced some of the "isms" of our time, if we study it properly, we will find that the matter is exactly as we have said. It is not that they have some quarrel with God, nor that they reject or refuse to accept God because they lack a convincing rational argument for His existence. Most often there is no rational argument on the other side of the question either; there is no argument for negating God — none at all, neither now nor in the past.

You will not find a single person who says, "I say there is no God for *this* reason" — not one! Among all the materialists of the world, from the first day until now, there is not one who says, "I prove there is no God by this argument." Anyone who has something to say on this matter says, "It has not been proven to me that He exists; I did not understand, I did not accept the argument for His existence." And the Qur'an points to precisely this fact:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Jāthiyah (45:24):** "They are following nothing but conjecture."[^s9-4] — These people merely chase after fancy and guesswork; otherwise they cannot negate God with any proof.

So it is not on account of *not* having a rationally satisfying philosophy, nor is there a rationally satisfying philosophy on the materialist side either. What there is, in the case of the materialist of our age — and this is a subtle point — the cause of his inclination toward what he calls the materialist school is that he imagines that today this materialist school can better administer the world. He says it can better eliminate oppression, better destroy discrimination and inequality, better uproot, dissolve, and annihilate tyranny and despotism. He says religion cannot do these things. Why does he say religion cannot do these things? Because of religion — of the common, prevalent notion of religion — he knows nothing except what he has observed in the hands of the people of the street and bazaar, in its traditional and imitative form. In short, he has no knowledge of religion, no information about it. If you ask him what religion is, he names a series of outward manifestations and says, "This is religion"; and because these are an opiate, because these are the brother and equal of oppression and the oppressor, because these cannot loosen a single knot in the people's tangled affairs,[^s9-5] therefore — abandon it.

It is clear that when a person is faced with such a logic, the best and most correct answer is this: yes; if you have found a religion that colludes with the oppressor, that cooperates with the despot, that does not stand for one moment alongside the oppressed, that does not untie one knot in the people's tangled affairs, that has not the worth of a poppy seed, of a mustard's atom,[^s9-6] for the people's today and tomorrow — then on our behalf you are authorized: if you have found such a religion, wherever you found it, reject it, do not accept this religion for a single moment. Because if a religion is from God, it is not like this.

A religion that God sends is not just anything; it has a sign, it has a distinguishing mark, it bears a standard seal. If this standard seal matches a given religion, we accept it; if it does not match, we do not accept it. (When I speak about prophethood and the Prophet — God willing, in the series of Quranic discussions — perhaps I will convey this meaning there in greater detail.)

The Qur'an says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḥadīd (57:25):** "We have certainly sent Our messengers with clear proofs, and We sent down with them the Book and the Balance, so that the people may uphold justice."[^s9-7]

"We sent Our messengers with clear proofs" — We sent forth Our prophets with luminous proofs, "and We sent down with them" a body of thought and the practical instruments — instruments by which they could judge and adjudicate among the people in their disputes — toward the people. Why did We do these things? "*We* sent" — not just one prophet, not just Moses, not just the Seal of the Prophets, not just Jesus; "We sent Our messengers" — all the prophets were sent with this purpose and with this idea and with this aim. What is that aim? "So that the people may uphold justice" — so that human beings may live on the basis of equity, justice, and fairness. *This* is religion. If you have seen a religion moving in the opposite direction to the philosophy of the religions, know that it is either not divine or it has been corrupted. If you have seen it moving contrary to the philosophy and the line of march of the divine prophets and the divine messengers, know with certainty that it is not the offspring of the Lord's revelation and of the prophets. This is quite clear.

So, O materialist! — the address is to them — O materialist! O you who say, "I have seen religion to be incapable of administering human societies" — we ask: which religion? The religion of Islam? True Islam? The Muhammadan revelation? The sovereign Alawid way of life? Have you seen *these* to be incompatible with the administration of human affairs? Come, prove it; where is the incompatibility? An Islam that comes and removes discrimination, that negates class division in the very heat of the world's class division, that pours together the wealth that has been unjustly distributed and redistributes it per capita, that gives human beings equal opportunities and means, that takes governance out of the hands of human tyrants and entrusts it to the just law of God; that honors the base human being, the human being who is slapped about, the enslaver and the enslaved, the human being who — for the sake of a single word, for the sake of a single bravo, for the sake of a single worthless coin[^s9-8] — would commit the basest of crimes; that elevates this base, abject, despised human being, raises him up, makes him noble, adorns him with moral and human virtues — and secures all of this in the shadow of a just and firmly grounded order.

Nor is the prophet's training a matter of individual cultivation — taking each person one by one by the hand and seating him in a corner of a private chamber, whispering incantations into his ear so that he may become upright, so that he may become a true human being; it is not like that either. Nor is it preaching, with the prophet sitting and preaching to the people — "people, this is bad, do it that way." It is a *social order*. The prophet, in the ignorant atmosphere of that day, cast the foundation of Islamic society as firm as steel, in a determinate shape and mold. Then he brought human beings into this mold, brought human beings into this path and this channel. And bringing them into this path *was itself* their becoming true human beings; the two were one. So if you say that the Islamic religion — which is the truly Islamic religion, with these characteristics — if you say it this way, that it is not on a par with human progress, with justice, with the establishment of security, and with the meeting of human needs — no; we do not accept this, this is unfairness, this is unfairness.

If a scholar who grew up in the hollow milieu of Christianity, who has seen of religious manifestations nothing but the false intercessions of Christ, the forgiveness of sins, and the selling of deeds of paradise for money — if *he* gave himself the right to say this about religion, you, who live a hundred years, eighty years, fifty years after him, and who live in an era in which Islam is more or less showing itself, and has displayed and is displaying the sweetest and most precious human manifestations on the horizon of the world — *you* have no right to say this. If this is your meaning — no, this is not correct. But if you say: the hollow religions, the false religions, the fair-faced and foul-hearted religions, the religions

that everywhere drag the people into lethargies and submissions to oppression[^s9-9] and pessimisms and divisions and fratricides; the religion that says to the poor, "If you have no wealth, if you have no money, you need not strive in the way of money," and says to the rich, "In return for what you have, give a portion to the church or to some other religious institution, and it will be the atonement for all the oppressions you committed in the course of acquiring this money" — if you mean *this* religion, in this we too are of one voice with you, and both of us are followers of the Qur'an:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Tawbah (9:34):** "O you who believe, indeed many of the rabbis and monks devour the wealth of people unjustly and avert [them] from the way of God."[^s9-10] — O you who have believed, many of the Jewish and Christian scholars and ascetics devour the wealth of people without entitlement — "they devour" — and they do not even content themselves with that; "and they avert [them] from the way of God" — they also bar the people's path toward perfection and elevation. Would that they took their money and devoured it but left their path open! He devours their money *and* he severs their path. This the Qur'an says. We too are of one voice with you: a religion that is like that is not religion. A religion that is like that — any irreligion is better than it; because the irreligions may at least sometimes not become accomplices to oppressions and injustices, whereas such a religion may possibly become a means, a staff, a weapon in their hands.

Well, this was, in summary, a set of generalities concerning the belief in Tawhid, in the form of a vision, in the sense of an answer to a single question: is there a God or is there not? Is there anything beyond this material world? Is there a hand of power or not? In answering this question I said that the theist speaks this way, the materialist speaks that way — and set aside the war of theist and materialist[^s9-11] and leave it for the books; what little of it pertained to us, we have said in a word or two.

Now, a very important point. (If today I am unable to say everything I have written here, that is no obstacle, because this discussion will be continued — God willing, we will be with you tomorrow as well, if God wills. These matters which, as a preliminary and as the generalities of the subject, everyone must know — those I must state. Because it is Friday, some of you gentlemen may have been able to come today and may not be able to come tomorrow.) There are certain matters that are necessary for everyone in the domain of Islamic thought; everyone must know these, everyone must understand them. One of them is this: I say, gentlemen, when you raise the question of Tawhid, do not raise it as a dry, simple answer to a scientific and cerebral question; rather, raise it as a matter whose knowing or not-knowing is vital, decisive — raise it that way. Now I will explain.

Suppose at one time you are going along some road; with a friend, with a fellow-traveler and companion, you are moving and going along a road, and a discussion arises between you. You say, "Sir, the lands on both sides of this road are, in my view, salt-marsh; no crop at all can be obtained here." He says, "No, sir, the lands on both sides of this road are, in fact, perfectly suited for the cultivation of such-and-such." You say something, he says something; you offer a proof, he offers a proof. Well, how important is this discussion? You are going on, after all. The gentlemen's automobile is passing along this road at a hundred and twenty. You are not now going to sit down and test its soil, nor are you going to buy a piece of these lands so as to come back later and grow beets, nor are you going to answer some testimony or inquiry made about these lands; this question and answer has no effect for you whatsoever. Just this much: it is an answer to a question, a reply in the face of some matter that has been raised, and nothing more — nothing else at all. Whether you said and proved that this soil is barren[^s9-12] and yields nothing, or he proved that no, on the contrary, excellent and remarkable beets come up and grow green here — whichever of you proves his claim, your road, your work, your present motion undergoes no change. It is not that, if it is proven to be barren, you say, "Well, now that it is barren, let us turn back"; no — you had no business with this place. Or

if it is proven that this place is fertile, you say, "Very well, now that it is fertile, come, let us pray two units of prayer at the foot of this fertile land"; no — it has no effect at all. Whether you prevail and prove your claim, or he prevails and proves his claim, it has no kind of effect on your motion, on this journey you are making, on your future, on your companionship — on anything. This is one kind of discussion, one kind of question, one kind of answer to a question.

At another time it happens that the two of you are seated in the car, this same car at this same speed is moving along this same road, and suddenly your friend says, "In my view, this road we are traveling reaches the north — whereas your destination is the south." You say, "No, sir, this road we are traveling reaches the south." He says no, you say no; this discussion flares up between the two of you. If he proves his claim, the head of the car must be turned around, you must go from the other direction — you had no business this way. If you prove your claim, with the very same course, the very same method, only a bit faster, you must take your road and go forward. Right now this hesitation that has arisen between you, and the two opinions that have appeared — its first effect is that the driver's foot goes to the brake, easing off a little: "Let us see now where we are going; in the end will we reach the destination or not?" This is called a question-and-answer and a discussion whose answer and whose result are *decisive*. The discussion of Tawhid is like this.

The way ordinary, common people, or the socially idle and the irresponsible and uncommitted, raise the question of Tawhid is different from the way a committed person ought to have the question of Tawhid raised for him. An uncommitted, irresponsible person raises Tawhid this way: is there a God or is there not? Well, now if there is, what shall we do? If there is not, what shall we do? What effect does it have on the condition of life, what change or alteration does it create in the social order? If there were a God, what would happen to the capitalist system of such-and-such a great power or such-and-such a superpower? A president who comes to power in a certain country — if he believed there is a God, how would he act? If he believed there is no God, how would he act? Does it make any difference at all? A knowledge of God and a worship of God in which the acceptance of one side leaves no difference and no effect upon the fate of cartels[^s9-13] and trusts[^s9-14] and capitalisms and discriminations — that worship of God, that belief in Tawhid, is like the belief in the fertility of this land we are passing by; it has no benefit, no effect, for us. What benefit is it that some political leader of a certain country believes in God, while for him the worship of God is merely an answer to a

dry, cerebral question and nothing higher? Being a worshiper of God, being a monotheist — *then* is it important, effective, beneficial, necessary, vital for a political leader, for an ordinary person, for a society, for a nation, for a community and a group — when Tawhid is raised, understood, and grasped for the sake of its effects, for the sake of what follows from it, for the sake of the order it proposes, for the sake of the form of life it presents — for the sake of *these* things of it. In our view this is a very important matter.

It comes into our imagination that Tawhid is something we need only clarify in our heads, establish as settled; and that when we come to life, this Tawhid no longer has any effect in life; and if it does have an effect, it is in personal life, not in social life. "Even if I am a monotheist, I will have the same capital and the same automobile and the same company and the same factory and the same condition of relationship with the worker and the same condition of relationship with the land as I would have had if I were not a monotheist." Look at the capitalist countries of the world, at these enormous powers whose name and fame have filled East and West: take two capitalists, two great merchants, two great factory-owners, two of these "sultans of industry," as they call themselves; suppose one believes in God and one is a materialist — what difference is there in their conduct? Now, if the one who believes in God went to church on Sunday

and gave a few coppers[^s9-15] to the monk there to forgive him some of his sins, to smooth and level out a few kilometers of the road to paradise for him — but what is its effect in his life? In the condition of his factory? In his relations with the worker? In his relations with the people? In the manner of his wealth-accumulation, in his spending of wealth, in his amassing of wealth, what effect does it have? This Tawhid is not much different from polytheism.

The Tawhid to which Islam summons is a Tawhid higher than the level of an answer to a question, to an inquiry. So what is it? Islamic Tawhid is an inspiration in the domain of governance, in the domain of social relations, in the domain of the course of society, in the domain of the aims of society, in the domain of the people's duties, in the domain of the responsibilities that human beings bear before God, before one another, before society, and before the other phenomena of the world. *This* is Tawhid. Islamic Tawhid is that same "alif" after which come "be" and "pe" and "che," all the way to "ye." It is not that you say, "God is one and not two," and the matter is finished. "God is one and not two" means that, in the entire region of your own existence — you individually and your society as a whole — no one but God has the right to rule. "God is one and not two" means that all the wealth you possess — you and all other human beings — belongs to God; you are no more than borrowers

and trustees. Who now is ready to become a monotheist? You hold the money in trust and that is all; you are a borrower. If, sir, some money of your friend's were deposited in your hand as a trust, what would you do? You wait for him to give the order. "Sir, give ten tomans of this money to that child, to that old man, to that stranger, to that relative; throw ten tomans of this money into such-and-such a fund; burn ten tomans of it altogether" — you wait for the handwriting of the owner of the money, do you not? Is it other than this? Do you regard yourself as having any right, any ownership, in this money held in trust and deposit? "Wealth is the wealth of God; He has placed it as deposits among the people."[^s9-16] — God has entrusted wealth as a deposit and trust into the hands of human beings. This is the corollary of Tawhid.

If you hold to Tawhid, then class division and discrimination in society have no meaning — no meaning at all. The society that has a top and a bottom, that has an upper and a lower, that society is not a Tawhidi society. Tawhid says, "All of you are from Adam, and Adam is from dust"[^s9-17] — all are the children of Adam, and Adam is from dust. Your nearness to God and your superiority lie in piety, and nothing else; whoever is more mindful of God's command, he is the higher. Otherwise, in a society in which there exist a thousand grounds for discrimination — you say, "Sir, so-and-so is like this," and the reply comes, "Well, so-and-so is among the nobility, he has a different reckoning" — in a society that has nobles and non-nobles, in a society in which

the people's enjoyments differ severely from one another and the privileged one regards it as his right; in a society in which the servants of God are not all on one level and some are still servants of others — in this society there is no Tawhid. When Tawhid comes to a society, all the servants are placed on one level. Meaning what? Meaning that all become servants of God — as I said yesterday. (On this sheet which relates to yesterday, we wrote this sentence.) The beings of the world, human and otherwise, are His subjugated servants,[^s9-18] and in servitude to Him all are partners and equals. This I explained in detail yesterday — that no one and no thing, under the heading of sonship, spousehood, or peerage, is outside the circle of servitude to God. Within the domain of servitude, it no longer has any meaning that one group of servants should again bind a chain around the necks of another group; that has no meaning at all. Servitude to God means freedom from servitude to other-than-God; the two simply do not go together. It has no meaning that someone should be a servant of God and also a servant of other-than-God. Servitude to God means freedom from servitude to, and from the worship of, whatever is other-than-God and whoever is other-than-God.

The envoy of the armies of Islam[^s9-19] came and entered the powerful palace of the Sasanians. A ragged-clothed Arab — an Arab whose whole attire, if you reckon it up, was perhaps not worth one of our tomans — entered the palace of the man who, on the day he was fleeing while the valiant Muslim warriors pursued him, had brought along with him a thousand singing slave-girls

only, apart from other things. His minstrels and singers numbered a thousand. If this is how it is at home,[^s9-20] what must it be on a journey — and a journey of *flight* at that, a journey on which he is fleeing in fear of his life? Wretch! Where are you taking singers? Take a sword with you. One fears they too were under the colonialism of the Jews! (Laughter from the audience.) — It is among the characteristics of the sinister, vile Zionist policies that they keep the people occupied with these very dilly-dallyings and song-singings and song-listenings, under various names — this ragged-clothed man entered the court of such an enormous political power. Do you think he was afraid? Do you think he was overawed? Do you think he came with thoughts of disputation? Never. After all, when, before a powerful and exalted personage, a paltry, small person presents himself, his whole effort is to be able to attach himself even a particle to that great power, to draw himself a little closer to him — even by flattery, by fawning, by displaying fear, by displaying servitude and submission. Do you think it was like that? Never. When he went forward and reached before the throne, it seems he even set his foot upon Yazdgird's throne, because he saw that Yazdgird would not come to take the paper from him. He had brought a paper, brought a message; others came to take it, and he said, "I will not give it to you; I must give it to him himself." And since the latter would not rise from his place to come and take the paper from this Arab, this one was forced to go forward. He went, and when he reached upon his throne

he gave him the paper, so to speak. He said, "For what have you come?" He spoke three sentences — three sentences that ought to be written in radiant letters and pounded onto a tablet over the gateway of the great palace of humanity, so that all may know what the slogan of Islam and the idea of Islam is. He said, "Why have you come?" He said, "We have come" — within the course of his speech, of course; these three sentences of his are what concern us. "We have come to bring the people out" —

> "*Li-nukhrija al-nāsa min ʿibādat al-ʿibādi ilā ʿibādat Allāh*" — "We have come to bring human beings out from the servitude of [created] servants to the servitude of God Most High."

What is the servitude of [created] servants? The servitude of servants is this: that when, in one of the campaigns between Iran and Rome, one of the past kings of Iran — before the Sasanians, from the Achaemenids — when he gives the order for all to come, that old man comes and says, "Sir, my three sons are coming, they are with you in the field; let this one son of mine stay beside me, for I am old, I am decrepit; allow this one to remain and not come with you." In the assembly they say nothing to him; they drive him out of the assembly. Then the next day, when the troops are moving in columns of four, the horses and soldiers behind one another, as they reach the gateway, those three brothers suddenly see their fourth brother cut into two pieces, one piece installed on this side of the gate and one piece on that side of the gate — so that old men will no longer say, "Let our young men not come, and let them not fight for the power of Darius," and will not throw themselves into water and fire. This is the servitude of man. When human beings in a society did not even have the right to *want* what they want — I do not say to *say* it; when human beings in a community had no right

to be partisans and seekers of justice, to be enemies of discrimination; when human beings had no right to love and want freedom for themselves, but accepted suffocation and strangulation as the natural condition and set their seal of approval upon it — when human beings lived in such a society, this is the highest and ugliest and bitterest of the kinds of servitude. This is the worst of the kinds of servitude. Why? Because it is fraudulent.[^s9-21] Those who used to go and seize a handful of innocent human beings in some place, shave their heads, and sell them in other lands — they performed an open and overt act; but to play with human beings in this way, to disregard a human being's thought and want and will and decision in this way, to trample him — this was it.

He said, "We have come to free you from the worship of [created] servants" — that is, to free the people from the worship of you, O Yazdgird! to free others from the worship of your governors, your village-heads, your commanders, here and there — "*min ʿibādat al-ʿibād*." Then where shall we take them? They were not your servants, so what shall they be? Loose and lawless? No — they shall be servants of God. To be a servant of God means to be free, to be a master, to move toward perfection, to use and benefit from the means of perfection to whatever extent you wish — just as it was in the Islamic society. In the Islamic society, the people were servants of God, not servants of powers. Even

at the time when the course of the Islamic society had deviated and was not a hundred percent Islam, it was so. Even in those very years when the conquest of Iran was taking place, the effect of the prophetic and Quranic upbringing was present in the people. A political ruler says from the pulpit, "If I become crooked, set me straight," and a desert Arab rises from the foot of the pulpit and says, "If you do not become straight and you become crooked, with this sword we will set you straight." Did the soldiers and police fall upon his head? Did they throw him into prison as one creating disorder in the situation, did they do away with him, destroy him? Never. He had said something true, he had said something logical. Freedom not in the sense of unbridled license, but freedom in the sense of obedience to a sound human law — so that, in the spirit of man, in that order and in that society, a human being does not carry anyone's burden on his shoulders, not even the ruler's burden. If the ruler spoke from God, spoke by the inspiration of God, he is an Islamic ruler, and his word is accepted. If he did not speak by divine inspiration, his word too is rejected.

> "*Li-nukhrija al-nāsa min ʿibādat al-ʿibād ilā ʿibādat Allāh, wa min ḍīq al-dunyā ilā saʿat al-dunyā wa al-ākhirah*" — The second sentence. He said, "We have come to bring the people, the servants of God, out from the limited and narrow enclosure of the world into the breadth of the world and the hereafter."

The limited and narrow enclosure of the world! In a society in which the people do not live with a sound vision, from whichever side they look, before their gaze there is nothing but the world and its worldly gains; from whichever side the people look, nothing is at issue for them except worldly pleasures and the self-conceits of the world; whatever he looks at, he sees his own small and worthless

animal endeavors and his small, base, paltry, fleeting, and momentary interests. In a society over which Yazdgird ruled, it was not that all the people were content with Yazdgird; there were also many discontented. But because the eyes of these discontented were near-sighted, because the horizon of their vision was narrow and small, they saw that if they showed the slightest discontent and displeasure with Yazdgird, this very meager, base life would also be taken from them — these two extra mouthfuls of food, these two extra days of repose,[^s9-22] these two extra kicks in the alley and the street, this would be taken from him; and because he set great store by this, because he loved this very meagerness so much, he was not ready to undertake any action for freedom, for human honor and authenticity and virtue. What was the cause? His horizon of vision was small, narrow, and limited — the narrowness of the world.

But when a human being became a Muslim, everything is for him a preliminary, a means. For what? A means to what? A means of reaching a vast world — I do not mean the world after death — the world of man's own thought, vision, and sight, which is as wide and expansive as God. Everything is a means for man, so that man may be able to gain the pleasure of God. The life of the world, the money of the world, the comfort of the world, the affections of the world have no value and authenticity for him. It acquires value for him only

> "*fī sabīl Allāh*" — when it is in the way of God. But if this affection, this wealth, this rank, this life, this child, this honor, this dignity were not, and were not placed, in the way of God and in the way of duty, then it has no price and no value for him. The world and the hereafter are sewn together in the Islamic mode of thought; and for a Muslim, the world has no end. In the view of that person who is the servant of servants and the slave of deficient beings, the world is limited; but for this one, the world is vast. Death is a small window: when you look from this window, on the other side are gardens and orchards and worlds and universes and realms; therefore he sees that the most that can happen is that he reaches this window, passes through this window — it does not matter; death is no problem for him.

These are some glimmers and corners of Tawhid. Of course, about Tawhid one must speak much more systematically and under a more definite heading, and we will speak — God willing — in the days ahead. Today I only wanted to have stated some generalities in this regard. (What has been written on the first page — which, of course, I will not repeat — is in fine handwriting and the gentlemen can read it.) This in truth presents a new vision in the domain of Tawhid. It states a correct vision in the domain of Tawhid. Of course there are further aspects and angles that have not come into this, and which — God willing — I hope we can arrange in due order in the writings of the coming days. But in any case, what has been written here is one dimension of Tawhid that reflects and states precisely what is intended in the domain of Tawhid, what is intended by the religions and the Qur'an in particular.

And now, the verses we have selected. (Of course, today — well, it is Friday and you have not much to do; after I have read these verses here, our dear reciter, apparently Mr. Rezaei or Mr. Fatemi — the gentlemen themselves know better, in any case — are responsible for today's recitation; they will come and read the verses, both these verses and yesterday's verses, that is, especially Āyat al-Kursī.) The repetition of Āyat al-Kursī as a slogan of Tawhid is very interesting. I conjecture that the reason it has been so strongly emphasized that Āyat al-Kursī be recited repeatedly in various instances is that this slogan of Tawhid should always be living and enduring in a person's mind, ever-alive and self-subsisting — this memory in a person's mind. These verses written here today are from Sūrat al-Baqarah. It is the description of a scene at the Resurrection, which, of course, has a complete and total connection to the matter of Tawhid; and in the course of the recitation of the verse it becomes entirely clear. Sūrat al-Baqarah, verses 165 and 166.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:165):** "And among mankind are some who take, besides God, rivals…" — Among the people are some who choose, besides God, rivals and competitors; that is, they choose for God a partner of the human kind or of other than the human kind. "*Yuḥibbūnahum ka-ḥubb Allāh*" — they love them like the love and affection one ought to have for God.

Right here the verse opens a parenthesis; for it was not speaking about love, but since the love of these toward God was mentioned, it opens a parenthesis, as if[^s9-23] —

> "*Wa alladhīna āmanū ashaddu ḥubban li-Llāh*" — But those who have believed, the true and real believers — their love and affection toward God is far more intense than all these outward manifestations, than all the poles that draw the human heart toward themselves like amber, than all the false gods, than the god of the self and lust; and so on, take it all the way up to those gods who have seated themselves, and are seated, and are given a seat, at the head and summit of societies — than all of these, God is more beloved to the believer.

> "*Wa alladhīna āmanū ashaddu ḥubban li-Llāh, wa law yarā alladhīna ẓalamū idh yarawna al-ʿadhāb…*"

Suddenly it transfers the discourse to a scene of the Resurrection. It describes that moment when the creatures have been gathered, mustered together at the Resurrection — the disbelievers and the evildoers and the servants of other-than-God and the servants of God and all the existing phenomena of the Resurrection, those that have been described to us and those that have not — all are gathered. The instruments of God's punishment and the instruments of God's mercy and grace are there too; though as to the quality of these instruments, you and I cannot yet rightly grasp or imagine — in this world it cannot rightly be understood what news there is there. In general we know only this much: that the means that are to be foreseen there for torment, for punishment, for wretchedness are all present and prepared. The good servants of God and the bad servants of God are there; then the tyrants and the oppressors suddenly see that all power at the Resurrection belongs to God. It is a very strange thing. Now, in this world, look:

everyone has some power, everyone does something. Those who sit on high have more power, but those who sit below also have power, after all. Everyone prides himself on his own power; everyone has some measure and substance of capacity and force within him; something does come from him, after all — especially that tyrant, that oppressor, whose deed is greater, whose power too is greater. And even that oppressor who performed the worship of an oppressor — for he too is an oppressor, he too is a tyrant — he too, in his own imagination, enjoys some power; because he has connected himself with a higher power, like a fox that has tied its tail to a camel's tail.[^s9-24] In the world it is like this. But at the Resurrection, when they gather, whatever they look at, whoever refers back to himself, he sees that there is no power, no capacity, no competence in him; power is entirely in the hand of God.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mu'min/Ghāfir (40:16):** "*Li-man al-mulku al-yawm? Li-Llāh al-wāḥid al-qahhār*" — "Whose is the sovereignty this day? It belongs to God, the One, the Subduer."[^s9-25]

He takes this scene into view: that tyrant, that oppressor, says — whatever oppressor who oppressed others, whatever oppressor

who oppressed himself and had made himself the servant of that first oppressor — when they look, when they see, "Strange! Here all those words and claims and airs and pomps[^s9-26] and palaces and lives are all nothing and void; nothing at all comes from anyone's hand."

Then the scene there is a strange scene. Take two groups into view: one group

worshiped the other group — that is, obeyed them, with unconditional obedience. These two groups stand face to face on the Day of Resurrection; they have war and dispute and quarrel with one another.

> "*Wa law yarā alladhīna ẓalamū idh yarawna al-ʿadhāb*" — If only those who have done wrong could see — in the parenthesis we explained, we specified this wrongdoing as surrendering oneself to other-than-God. In this explanation we followed some of the old Shiʿi commentators. — If only those who have done wrong by surrendering themselves to other-than-God could see, when they behold the punishment — what would they see?

> "*Anna al-quwwata li-Llāhi jamīʿan*" — that all force and power are entirely from God; "*wa anna Allāha shadīd al-ʿadhāb*" — and that God's chastisement is severe and terrible. If they could see, what would happen? The answer is decreed. In the parenthesis we mentioned its answer in Persian: they would, without doubt, regret their own conduct. They would regret that in the world they had clutched at such a thing[^s9-27] — they went and became the servants and slaves of tyrants who are now, at the Resurrection, so incapable, from whose hands so little comes. If they had looked with the eye of admonition, they would have seen that in the world too they were just as incapable.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:166):** "*Idh tabarra'a alladhīna uttubiʿū min alladhīna ittabaʿū wa ra'awu al-ʿadhāb*" — At that time when the leaders and chiefs disavow the followers and adherents, "*wa ra'awu al-ʿadhāb*" — and they behold the punishment of God. Then Yazdgird, for example, says, "O Lord! O my Sustainer! These ones whom you saw worshiping me in my time — I disavow these ones, do not imagine that I have any attachment to them for setting me up as your partner; they did wrong to set me as your partner, I disavow them." Now see how much the subjects of Yazdgird's day burn within: "We gave him our world and our hereafter, this scoundrel, and now at the Resurrection he disavows us." Now what does this verse of the Qur'an say?

> "*Idh tabarra'a alladhīna uttubiʿū min alladhīna ittabaʿū wa ra'awu al-ʿadhāb*" — At that time when the leaders and chiefs disavow the followers and adherents and behold the punishment of God; "*wa taqaṭṭaʿat bihim al-asbāb*" — and the ties and bonds between them are severed; "*wa qāla alladhīna ittabaʿū*" — and the followers and adherents say, "*law anna lanā karratan fa-natabarra'a minhum kamā tabarra'ū minnā*" — "Would that we had a return to the world, so that we might disavow them just as they disavowed us" — that is, today, the Day of Resurrection.
