"*Kadhālika yurīhimu Allāhu aʿmālahum ḥasarātin ʿalayhim*" — Thus does God show them their deeds as a cause of regret upon them; "*wa mā hum bi-khārijīna min al-nār*" — and they will never be those who come out of the Fire.

Here the matter that was intended in this verse was this: that those who there are receiving blows — they are receiving blows, they receive the blows of servitude to other-than-God; they receive the blow of this, that they became the servants and slaves of other-than-God; that is, something other than Tawhid and against Tawhid — whereas the Qur'an's expression for them is "*ittabaʿū*," the adherents, the followers.

## Footnotes

[^s9-1]: Sūrat al-Baqarah, verse 165. (This is the verse set at the head of the session; its full commentary comes at the end.)
[^s9-2]: Democritus (Persian: *Dimokrāt* / *Zimoqrāṭis*), 460–370 BCE, the famous Greek philosopher. He held that things are composed of small, indivisible parts called atoms. He did not believe in the existence of a soul and considered it bound up with the human brain; he thought that the only thing that exists is the atom and matter, and so he is called a materialist.
[^s9-3]: Materialism, or *mādī-garī*, is a worldview in which everything beyond matter and the sensible is denied and existence is taken to be equivalent to matter.
[^s9-4]: Sūrat al-Jāthiyah, verse 24.
[^s9-5]: An allusion to Ḥāfiẓ: "Will it be, perhaps, that in the taverns they will untie / the knot of our tangled affairs?"
[^s9-6]: I.e., something utterly trifling.
[^s9-7]: Sūrat al-Ḥadīd, verse 25.
[^s9-8]: *Fils* — black, worthless coin.
[^s9-9]: From the roots (r-kh-w) lethargy/slackness and (ẓ-l-m) to suffer oppression, to accept oppression.
[^s9-10]: Sūrat al-Tawbah, verse 34.
[^s9-11]: "Set aside" — meaning to let it go; from this verse of Ḥāfiẓ: "Excuse the war of the seventy-two sects, all of them; / since they did not see the truth, they took the road of fable."
[^s9-12]: *Lam-yazraʿ* — unfit for cultivation.
[^s9-13]: A cartel refers to companies active in the same field that, by agreeing among themselves on the division of the market, the volume of production, and the price of the goods, monopolize the market for that commodity for themselves.
[^s9-14]: A trust arises from the union of several companies producing a similar commodity. In a trust, the shares of the member companies are separate and distinct, but the resources of all are placed at the disposal of the trust.
[^s9-15]: *Shāhī* — a small unit of currency, equal to one-twentieth of a rial, current in the Qajar period and the early Pahlavi era.
[^s9-16]: Hadith no. 37: "*al-Māl mālu Allāh jaʿalahā wadāʾiʿa ʿinda al-nās*" — "Wealth is the wealth of God; He placed it as deposits among the people."
[^s9-17]: Hadith no. 2: "*Kullukum min Ādam wa Ādam min turāb*" — "All of you are from Adam, and Adam is from dust."
[^s9-18]: From the root (q-h-r): vanquished, defeated.
[^s9-19]: Rabʿi ibn ʿĀmir.
[^s9-20]: From the root (ḥ-ḍ-r): place of residence, the city, the home.
[^s9-21]: From the root (z-w-r): hypocritical, deceitful.
[^s9-22]: I.e., sleeping, resting.
[^s9-23]: I.e., "as though."
[^s9-24]: An idiom for dependence on worthless grandees.
[^s9-25]: Sūrat al-Mu'min (Ghāfir), verse 16.
[^s9-26]: Self-conceit, arrogance, and vanity.
[^s9-27]: I.e., what a foolish clutching/grasping they engaged in.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 10: Exclusive Worship and Obedience of God

*عبادت و اطاعت انحصاری خدا — delivered Saturday, 6 Mehr 1353 / 11 Ramaḍān al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Yūnus (10:30–31):** "There every soul will be tried for what it sent ahead, and they will be returned to God, their true Master, and there will vanish from them all that they used to fabricate. Say: Who provides for you from the heaven and the earth? Or who possesses hearing and sight? And who brings forth the living from the dead and brings forth the dead from the living? And who governs the affair? They will say: 'God.' So say: Will you not then be wary of Him?"
>
> *Sūrat Yūnus*

As we have already said, the discussion of Tawhid in the Qur'an is a very long and detailed discussion. It may be said that the longest and most detailed discussion carried out across the whole of the Qur'an is the discussion of Tawhid. That is to say, even the discussion of Prophethood — with all its length, with all the stories and accounts of the prophets that are recounted in certain places as object lessons — still rests upon Tawhid and upon the question of the existence of God, and especially upon the question of negating shirk (associating partners with God), which is carried out in various forms. In this the Qur'an is utterly without parallel and without peer — both in the tenor of its language and in the number of its verses. Of course, as the discussion expands, the issues within the field of Tawhid become more numerous and more varied. Here we can raise only a few of these issues, supporting them with verses — not all the issues that can be raised and discussed concerning Tawhid or in the vicinity of Tawhid.

It appears to us that if we accept and acknowledge that Tawhid — in addition to being a vision, and a reading of reality — is, beyond this, a knowledge that produces action and shapes life (these are formulations that were to some extent clarified and proven in the past days); if we accept that Tawhid is a belief that entails a commitment and a responsibility — then we must search out this commitment, these responsibilities that are folded[^s10-1] and embedded within the heart of Tawhid. We must find these, and item by item, sentence by sentence, chapter by chapter, set each one down as a heading, and then trace[^s10-2] and pursue it in the Qur'an or in the totality of the Islamic sources — that is, in the Qur'an and the Hadith. I ask the brothers and sisters who already have some mental background regarding this discussion — and I think perhaps all the brothers and sisters, or the majority, are like this — to pay attention to the words and formulations that I use. I try to make my formulations deliberate and considered; try to fix them correctly in your minds, and let your reflection, contemplation, and pondering rest precisely upon what is intended.

The point is this: if it is established that Tawhid is a belief that brings in its wake a commitment, a responsibility, and an obligation for the one who holds this belief, then it must be understood and known, after all, what this responsibility, this commitment, and these obligations are. Is this commitment summed up merely in our accepting this belief with our tongue, or in our heart and our thought? That is to say, is belief itself the responsibility? Or does the scope and the domain of this responsibility extend beyond the region of thought and heart — but still within personal acts? For example, a monotheist (muwaḥḥid) has upon his shoulders a series of obligations entailed by Tawhid, among them that he pray; among them that he invoke the name of God at the beginning and end of every undertaking; among them, suppose, that he slaughter the sheep, the sacrificial animal, only in the name of God, and so forth. Is it summed up merely at this level? Or rather, is the commitment that Tawhid gives to the monotheist individual, or to the monotheist society, higher than the level of personal commands and individual obligations? The commitment that Tawhid gives to a monotheist society encompasses the most important, the most general, the greatest, the most primary, and the most fundamental issues of a society. Such as what? Such as government, such as the economy, such as international relations, such as the relations of individuals with one another — for these are the most important fundamental rights for the administration and the life of a society. We believe that the commitment of Tawhid, and the responsibility that comes to rest upon the shoulder of the monotheist, is a responsibility at the level of the fundamental obligations and the fundamental rights of a society.

Let me state the matter in a single phrase, so that afterwards we may turn to its finer and more subordinate points, or its more specific and detailed ones. In a word, the countenance and the body of a monotheist society differ from the countenance and body of a non-monotheist society. It is not the case that if a certain law is enforced in a monotheist society, and that law — or ten laws like it — is also enforced in a non-monotheist society, then that society too becomes monotheist. No. The frame of a monotheist society, the way in which the parts of this society are arranged, the general social body that is built upon Tawhid, upon the worship of the One, and upon orientation toward the One, is utterly different from a society that is not of this kind. In a word, it is what they call today the social system. The social order, the social system, and the social form of a monotheist society are something entirely contrary and divergent[^s10-3] — and at times opposed and contradictory — to a non-monotheist society. In a single sentence, the matter is this. If you dissect this phrase, within the heart of the social order, the social frame, and the social organs there are discussions and debates that can be understood and grasped by drawing on the new and modern cultures current in the world — and still more by drawing on the Qur'an and the sources of Hadith. This is the general gist of the matter.

But let me state it in a more particular, more specific, and more concrete way. We present Tawhid in the form of a resolution that has articles, and we shall set out the articles of this resolution one by one. What articles does the Tawhidic resolution have? Just as, after various negotiations between two groups, two fronts, two persons, the binding agreements are issued in the form of a resolution, so too the monotheists of the world, on the part of their Lord, on the part of the God of Tawhid, are bound to put this resolution into practice and to carry it out. The first article of this resolution must become clear in today's recitation. On the principle of Tawhid, human beings have no right to render servitude and obedience to anyone or anything except God. This is the first article of the Tawhidic resolution. Of course, when we said "anyone and anything," its scope is very wide. See where servitude and obedience apply.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Yā-Sīn (36:60):** "Did I not take a covenant from you, O children of Adam, that you should not worship Satan?"

"Did I not take a covenant from you, and make a pact with you, O children of Adam, that you should not render servitude to Satan?" Concerning Satan, we have at times given explanation in the course of various discussions. Satan does not consist of his lordship Iblīs alone, nor of some hidden, concealed thing that does not come into view and cannot be touched by hand, that may be found everywhere in a person's life — it is not only this. Satan is something vast; it is a general concept. Satan means the evil-stirring forces, the evil-producing power external to the human being. The evil-producing forces, but outside the bounds of a person's own existence — this becomes Satan. Just as the soul (nafs) — which is Satan's companion, Satan's servant, Satan's instrument and tool of action — if we wish to define it, we can define it thus: the evil-stirring and evil-producing forces within the human being. The soul and Satan: the commanding soul (al-nafs al-ammārah) and Satan; the one from within, the other from without. These two are forces that produce corruption, forces that produce evil, forces that produce deviation and degradation. Satan means anything whatsoever that is outside your existence and obstructs you on your path, produces evil, ignites flames, becomes a thorn in the road, becomes the wolf and the robber of the road — or brings the wolf and the robber of the road into being. This is Satan. In the discussion concerning prophethood and the prophetic mission, in this very series of recitation and pondering over the verses of the noble Qur'an, we will arrive at this verse, and I shall, God willing, translate it for you there: every one of the prophets who was sent by the Lord had enemies from among the satans of jinn and men.[^s10-4] Of course, we will later specify who these enemies were, what kind of people they were, from which groups and classes they came, and why they showed enmity. Satan is this general concept.

"That you should not worship Satan" — what does it mean? It means: do not render servitude, do not become surrendered and obedient to those evil-producing forces. And Tawhid means this. When I say "Tawhid means this," I do not mean that the whole of Tawhid is this — no. The subtleties and fine points[^s10-5] of Tawhid have their own place; the other aspects and bodies of Tawhid — some of which we ourselves may perhaps discuss in the future — also have their own place. But this too is one body, one aspect, one face of the pillar of Tawhid: not obeying, not rendering servitude, not submitting to impositions.

There is a hadith that I have perhaps read repeatedly on various occasions; now too is the place to read this very hadith, which is narrated on the authority of the Imam (peace be upon him) in our authoritative books, among them the noble al-Kāfī, Uṣūl al-Kāfī — that the Imam (peace be upon him) narrates it as a sacred tradition (ḥadīth qudsī), in a few sentences, in a few phrases. What has remained with me, and is the closest and most detailed of the wordings, is this: «By My might, I shall surely torment every subject community in Islam that gave its allegiance to the wilāyah of a tyrannical leader (imām jāʾir) who is not from God — even if that community is righteous and pious in its own deeds; and I shall surely pardon every subject community in Islam that gave its allegiance to the wilāyah of a just leader (imām ʿādil) from God — even if that community is wronging and ill-doing in its own deeds.»[^s10-6] The translation of the hadith — or, better, let me state the overall meaning of the hadith: obedience to that power which is not from God and from the representative of God, obedience to that center which has not taken its inspiration from the center of the Lord's power — this is at the level of shirk, or is shirk itself. For even though the people who do this and who are afflicted with this pain, this calamity, this great social disorder, may be orderly and well-arranged people in their own personal affairs — «righteous and pious» (barratan taqiyyatan), wary, God-fearing, watchful — this great calamity causes God Almighty to withdraw His regard of grace and mercy from this ummah, and to render them tormented, punished,[^s10-7] and afflicted with His chastisement.[^s10-8] This is the hadith.

Why? Because obeying other than God, rendering servitude to other than God, is contrary to the purpose for which God created the human being; it is contrary to the perfection and the elevation of the human being; it is contrary to the freedom and emancipation of the human being. That freedom and emancipation which is the prelude to the human being's ascent — for if those freedoms are absent, and bondages instead of freedoms take hold of and cling to the human being, then the human being cannot attain that intended flight, cannot reach that summit which God has appointed for him, cannot grow, cannot reach perfection. Like a plant over which a lid has been placed. Like a plant to the base of whose stem a strong wire has been tied. Like a plant around which dozens of means of preventing growth have been created. This plant cannot grow; and when it does not grow, it bears no fruit; and when it bears no fruit, what benefit is there in its existence, what effect is there in its coming? So why did it come? Why did it appear? Was it not only because it was meant to bear fruit? Obeying other than God and rendering servitude to other than God is just such a blight for the human being. In any case, in this field, throughout the Qur'an, there are abundant verses.

Now, without having investigated[^s10-9] very much — that is, with only a brief search — these verses occurred to me as ones to mention here. Now allow us to begin the main programme: to translate the verses; this is the basic aim. We must become somewhat acquainted with the melody of Tawhid in the Qur'an. We have fallen badly away from the Qur'an in the field of knowing the Islamic teachings, or we have busied ourselves with a series of vulgar and feeble fancies that, in their emptiness, baselessness, and groundlessness, are emptier than any emptiness — coupled with superstitions, coupled with false fancies. And it was precisely this — this alluring exterior and empty interior — that could not withstand the waves of materialism, and we saw how it perished. Either we busied ourselves with these fancies, or, at the opposite pole, we busied ourselves with dry, lifeless, ineffective argumentations in the field of Tawhid. What philosophical discussions — ineffective and dry! See how much the theologians (mutakallimūn) discussed Tawhid, and how ineffective these discussions were in forming and bringing into being a monotheist society. If a matter that is connected to life is discussed for a hundred years, is it possible that after a hundred years it would have no effect in life? For hundreds of years they discussed it in a dry form, in a form alluring in appearance and brainless in substance, in an abstract form, detached and free from connections with the world of reality and the external world. But now that we wish to draw aid from Tawhid for the building of a new life, when we look at those discussions, we see that they have no connection at all — as we ourselves would say, "like a stone beside the human being."[^s10-10] All those discussions, that circularity and infinite regress, those words — well, now we wish to make use of this Tawhid for the external world: what do we have? Whereas if we had returned to the Qur'an, if we had sought Tawhid from the Qur'an — the Qur'an has expressed the dimensions, aspects, and various organs of this body, this frame, this edifice of Tawhid, within the course of hundreds of verses, with the finest expression, with the most eloquent methods. Then it becomes clear what the monotheist life is, and who the monotheist human being is.

Now, in any case: this is one portion of these verses, which one must look at more closely, with pondering, the verses of Tawhid. Now I shall only explain these few verses for you.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Yūnus (10:28):** "And on the Day We shall gather them all together…"

"And on the Day We shall gather them all together" — the talk is of the Resurrection; that Day when We gather all creatures, the whole of them. "Then We shall say to those who associated partners with God" — then We shall say to those who committed shirk, who set up partners for God — "'Stay in your place, you and your partners'" — you and your partners, in a tone full of mastery, in a voice full of rebuke: "Stay in your place, you and your imagined partners — those whom you reckoned to be the rivals, the contenders, and the consorts of God." "We shall separate them" — between them and the imagined partners whom they had supposed for God, We shall cast separation. Here, with even a very ordinary and superficial glance, you understand that on the Resurrection these partners — those who had been chosen as partners of God — are other than that lifeless Hubal of carnelian, which has no resurrection. It is not, as the common and customary saying goes, brought along and told, "Stand in your place — stand up!" — nor is Manāt, nor Lāt (for Manāt is a particular idol, the statue, let us say, of a girl, or of an angel, say; and Lāt is another angel; Hubal is the same; and ʿUzzā is the same). It is not these that are spoken of. The talk is not of such-and-such a statue in such-and-such an idol-temple of Rome or Greece; it is not of the calf in the land of Hinduism;[^s10-11] it is of that human being who was chosen as a partner and rival of God. To them it is said that they should halt in their place: Stop!

The first rebuking address that, on the Resurrection, makes clear the negation of the power of the gods other than God is just this: "In your place!" See — what effect will this have on the Resurrection? It is saying this to me and to you; it is saying that the rival whom one had imagined for God — that polytheist Arab, or that polytheist non-Arab, whether his Persian one, his Roman one, his Abyssinian one, his Indian one, his Egyptian one — that rival whom one had imagined for God and had chosen for rivalry and partnership with God, on the Resurrection his situation is this: he and his followers, with the divine, forcible Halt, stand off to one side, with God's rebuking address and God's compelling, wrathful Halt.

"We shall separate them" — between them We cast separation — "and their partners will say, 'It was not us that you used to worship.'" And the partners, the imagined rivals, with the utmost ingratitude turn to their followers and say: "You did not worship us in the world." Like an accused person who, out of helplessness, in order to lift the guilt from his own neck, clutches[^s10-12] at any means, at any word. Just as in yesterday's discussion, in that dialogue whose verses I think we read yesterday, and just as in other dialogues that are in the Qur'an — "Those who were oppressed will say to those who were arrogant, 'Rather, it was the plotting of night and day…'"[^s10-13] to the end. Those groups whose misguidances were mutual fall upon one another on the Resurrection; they line up against each other. The one who committed shirk for God and took a partner wants to seize that partner by the collar and dash him to the ground, to say: "I had accepted you in place of God, and now I have been afflicted with this calamity." And the one who, in the world, was the object of servitude and worship — he too, to acquit himself, is ready, with the utmost ingratitude, to reject the lovers, followers, and adherents of his own world, to seek disavowal and disownment of them. "It was not us that you used to worship" — you were not worshipping us. "God suffices as a witness between us and you" — this is from the tongue of the partners: between us and you, God suffices as witness. "Indeed, of your worship we were unaware" — we were heedless of your worship; we were not at all aware that you held a view of servitude and bondage toward us, for your own selves.

"There every soul will be tried for what it sent ahead" — this is where every soul, every human being, is tested as to what it has accomplished beforehand. The deeds that were performed in the world — there, the human being himself becomes the object of testing and trial. "There every soul will be tried for what it sent ahead" — there it tries every soul as to what it has done beforehand. "And they will be returned to God, their true Master" — they will all be brought back to God, who is their Master, their Guardian, and their true Lord. "And there will vanish from them all that they used to fabricate" — what they used to say and attach by way of slander and calumny will be lost and vanish from them. All those things that were a motive in the heart of the human being for obeying other than God will be forgotten. All those things that were in the heart of the human being as a pretext and excuse for rendering servitude to other than God, and which a person thought would be a weapon in his hand on the Resurrection, will be taken from the human being's hand. The human being sometimes, in order to fashion a pretext and contrive an excuse for his shirk, makes up thoughts, imaginings, and fancies, and finds pretexts for himself. He fabricates justifiable, ostensibly legitimate (sharʿī) excuses. On the Day of Resurrection, which is the Day of the tribunal, as soon as the human being comes and has arrayed these excuses — first, second, third, fourth, fifth — and starts to count them, he sees that none of it remains; all of it is void, all of it ruined, all of it false. "And there will vanish from them all that they used to fabricate." And there is another possibility in the meaning of the verse: in the world the human being supposes for himself supports, props[^s10-14] — those very ones whom he worships, those very ones whom he obeys and renders servitude to, those upon whom his heart is warm and reliant. But on the Day of Resurrection all this warmth of heart and reliance, all these protectors and supporters, cannot lift a burden from his shoulder. Wretched one! "And there will vanish from them all that they used to fabricate."

Take note: the Qur'an's argumentations are like this. From one side, from one corner of the matter, it establishes the point. Sometimes it does not argue very head-on; rather, the Qur'an sometimes prepares for the human being the ground for an argument from within the very brain of the human being himself. Here God Almighty, in these verses, wishes to prove that one must render obedience and servitude only before God; He enters by this route. "Say: Who provides for you from the heaven and the earth?" — Say: Who gives you sustenance from the heaven and the earth? From the heaven, the life-bestowing and life-giving rain; from the earth, the vital materials. "Or who possesses hearing and sight?" — Or who is it that owns hearing and the faculties of sight? Do not confuse samʿ (hearing) with the ear. The name of this particular organ that in Persian we call gūsh (ear) is, in Arabic, udhun. Samʿ is the state of hearing, the power of hearing; and if it is also applied to the ear, that is by way of that relation. Of someone whose ear, say, has been cut off, they do not say "his samʿ was cut off"; they say "his udhun was cut off." The name of this particular organ is not samʿ. Likewise baṣar. Baṣar does not mean this specific, particular member that we call the eye; it means seeing, sight — that is spirit, and this member is body. If they do also call this member baṣar, it is by way of that very possession of the eye. For example, they do not call a blind eye baṣar. "Or who possesses hearing and sight?" — Or who is it in whose ownership and in the grasp of whose sovereign authority lie the faculty of hearing and the faculties of sight? Who is it that has given you this perception, this understanding, and this power? Who is it that can take it away from you? In reality this verse is pointing to the possession of insight, to the possession of the power of understanding and reason — that, O human being who is now meant to think and give us the answer to this question, you are a possessor of hearing (samʿ) and a possessor of sight (baṣar).

"And who brings forth the living from the dead?" — And who is it that brings out the living from the dead. The apparently living from the apparently dead: for example, a woman dies while she is pregnant, and afterwards her child is alive; they bring it out from a dead mother. Another possibility: from the dead sperm-drop, from the dead body, from a thing in which there is no life, We bring out the living. From this dead earth, from a soil that, although it is the treasury of thousands of life-bestowing and life-increasing materials, is itself dead — from its very heart We brought you out, and even now We are bringing human beings out. As for human beings, what is their original substance and primary root, other than these very vital and nutritive materials that come from the ground and from the earth?! So "He brings forth the living from the dead," with several possibilities, all of which possibilities are acceptable. "And He brings forth the dead from the living" — and who is it that brings out, draws forth, the dead from the living? That is, the dead child from the living mother's womb; that is, the evil human being with a dead spirit from the good human being possessing a living spirit; and so forth — such expressions and possibilities as come to the human being's mind. In any case, it is a sign of the perfection of the Lord's power: to bring a living thing out of a dead thing, to draw a dead thing out of a living thing — this is the sign of the perfection of God's might and the powerful grasp of God's power.

"And who governs the affair?" — Who is it that directs the creational affair of the universe? Who is it that places this force of gravity in the earth so that the human being can live upon it? Who is it that deposits the capacities of life in the earth, the sea, and the mountain as a trust? Who is it that gives the human being the power of extraction? Who is it that, with astonishing order and arrangement, brings into being this sun, moon, and stars, and the far, far, and yet more distant worlds? Who is it that sets that moon at a defined distance from the earth — for if He had set it at more than that, human life on the earth would not have been possible, because the seawater would have overspread the whole surface of the earth; and if it had been farther, again life would not have been possible, because the water would not have remained on the surface of the earth but would have sunk into the earth's depths? Who is it that makes the sun, sets it at a defined distance from the earth — such that if it were nearer to the earth than that limit, the earth from heat, and if it were less near to the earth, the earth from cold, would be uninhabitable — and so forth?

Now, what shall we say about the artistry and power of the Lord! These books issued and written by the hands of the scholars of the empirical sciences of the world in this century of ours, in the twentieth century, are so abundant in content that for a speaker like me to come and stand here would require several days only to recount them — and I, moreover, am not very much in favour of such things. "Who governs the affair?" — this question, while it was addressed to the polytheists of the time of the revelation of the revelation, is also addressed to me and to you in the twentieth century. "Who governs the affair?" — Who is it that is directing the whole universe? From the heart of the atoms to the depths of the most distant worlds, He is turning it with the hand of power. "Who governs the affair?" — Who is it? Without bias, without prejudice, think knowingly, so that you may find the real answer. "They will say: 'God.'" They will say "God." Some, without thinking, say "God" — for their belief, even at that time, was this. I think a little, I reflect — "They will say: 'God'" — I say: it is God. This wondrous orderliness of the world of creation is from God, and from Him alone. It is the hand of His power that is turning this wheel. What we see and observe with the natural eye and the non-natural eye, and what we do not see today but will see dozens of years hence through the advance of knowledge, are nothing but the offspring and products of God's power. "They will say: 'God.'"

Well, now that it is God — "So" — seize them right here, O our Prophet — "Say" — to the human being, the addressee of our call, O human being, the one responsible for the perfection of the human being — "Say: Will you not then be wary of Him?" Do you not have piety (taqwā) and wariness? What does it mean? It means: do you not fear this great God, this God who produces greatness, that you should set up another beside Him as a partner in obedience and in servitude? See — if the creational governance[^s10-15] of the universe is in His hand, why should the legislative governance[^s10-16] of the universe not be in His hand? I once, in that mosque,[^s10-17] was explaining the Sūrah of Tabārak — do you remember? "Blessed is He in whose hand is the dominion (al-mulk), and He has power over all things; who created death and life that He might try you as to which of you is best in deed." Then I entered into a statement of the manner of the creation of the universe, of the heavens and the earths, and such things. But what is its beginning? "Blessed is He in whose hand is the dominion (al-mulk)." Mulk means government, means that power is in His control. Which power? The power of creation is His; the power of legislation is also His. He who adorns the creation of the universe — why should He give the field to another in the legislation of the universe? And that other, moreover, is one who is himself made and fashioned by Him. He who has brought into being and created the natural laws and norms (sunan) in the world and in the human being — why should He give the making and the legislating of civil and penal laws and so on and so forth into the hands of weak individuals, deficient intellects, limited knowledge, and feeble wills, the likes of the human being and the ordinary human being? Why? Why should He not Himself administer society? Why should He not Himself lay down the law? Why should He not Himself appoint the power that guards and keeps watch over the law, and support it? Imamate, wilāyah — why should He not? Why should He not appoint an imam? Why should He not appoint a guardian (walī) from God's side[^s10-18]? Should He leave it to the deficient intellects of the people — why? "That is God, your Lord — the Truth (al-Ḥaqq); and what is there after the Truth except misguidance? Then how are you turned away?" I said that we did not include two or three verses here, which were not what we had in view.

After this it reaches the fourth verse. Of course, these verses that we have not translated will be recited in the recitation by our dear reciter today — so listen, and you will make use of these translations that are also in these Qur'ans. "Say: Are there any of your partners who guide to the Truth?"[^s10-19] O our Prophet! Again, addressing them by way of guidance, by way of teaching and instruction, speak; say: "Are there any of your partners who guide to the Truth?" Among these imagined partners whom you appointed and imagined for God, do you know of anyone who guides human beings toward the Truth? Do you have anyone? It is certain — and when I say "certain," I mean with assurance, with strong probability — that the intent here, again, is not the idols, and that stone and wood, and such things. No one would deem it probable concerning them that they would guide people — that these would guide people. Well, how do they guide? It is evident that the intent here is the living idols: those in whose grasp there has been some authority among the authorities, some power among the powers, whether religious or worldly — which we have written on the following page.[^s10-20] That is, like Pharaoh; like, suppose, Shurayḥ the Judge in his time, or anyone else in that field, in this vein.

"Say: Are there any of your partners who guide to the Truth?" Among these whom you have set up as rival, partner, and contender of God, is there anyone who would guide people toward the Truth? Here, what is the answer of these polytheists? It is possible that these would say in answer: "Yes; these whom we have chosen — not only do they guide; they are the very embodiment of the Truth." Guidance? That is nothing — they do even more than guide. That is why their answer is not recounted. They are wrong; they are wrong to deem the partners of God to be guides. You yourself, in answer to them, say: "Say: God guides to the Truth." Say: it is God who guides and leads human beings and intellects toward the Truth. Why? Because God created the Truth. God knows the fine points of the Truth, and God calls people to the Truth. One who was opposed to God necessarily calls to something opposed to the Truth; so it is God alone who summons to the Truth.

Well, now that it has become clear that God calls toward the Truth and that the imagined rivals do not do so — "Is He then who guides to the Truth…" — this is an inference that one must and can make and use with the human being's God-given intellect and wit and discernment: "Is He, then, who guides to the Truth more worthy to be followed?" — is the one who leads toward the Truth more deserving to be followed? — "or he who does not find the way unless he is guided?" — or the one who himself does not find the way unless someone takes his hand and leads him? After all, whom must one follow? Whom must one go after? After God, who is Himself the Creator of the Truth, the Guide toward the Truth, and the Director to the Truth? Or after the one who, if he wishes to reach the Truth, must himself have his hand taken and be led? "A blind man leading another blind man."[^s10-21] Does he wish to guide us? He himself is compelled to be guided; someone must guide him. Now here, in your view, of whom is the talk? This partner who can or cannot guide people — who, if he wishes to be guided, must have his hand taken and be led — what sort of person can this partner be? What sort of being can he be? Is the intended one the cow of the Hindus and the cow-worshippers? Or is the intended one the statue that the Qurashī[^s10-22] polytheist and the non-Qurashī worshipped? Or is the intended one the sacred fire of the Mazdeans[^s10-23]? Of the Zoroastrians? Or is the intended one the statues inside the synagogues[^s10-24] of the Jews, or in the idol-temples of Rome and Greece? Certainly none of these is meant. The one of whom it can be said that he guides or does not guide — who, if he wishes to find the way, must have his hand taken and be led — he is a human being who claims leadership, who claims that he brings society to felicity. The Qur'an wishes to say: God brings the human being to felicity, God brings the human being to the wellspring of reality, God brings the human being to the Truth. Those who themselves possess nothing of themselves cannot. "Or he who does not find the way unless he is guided? What is the matter with you?" — what is the matter with you? That is, O people who do not reflect and do not understand — "How do you judge?" How do you judge? How do you judge? How do you grant a field and an opening to other than God? This is one word concerning Tawhid.

(Of course, the discussion that we shall have tomorrow, God willing, is again in this same field, with some recitation. There was one more thing down at the bottom; let me set this passage out and read it, for now I no longer have the time to speak much about it.) The negation of the servitude of the god-pretenders (khudā-namā): the god-pretender, those who pose as God, who give themselves a godlike air — beings that have been the living idols of mankind throughout history. The negation of the servitude of the god-pretenders, whether in the garb of religious powers — in parentheses: the Aḥbār and the Ruhbān (the rabbis and the monks) — or in the garb of worldly powers — in parentheses: the Ṭāghūt, the Malaʾ, and the Mutrafīn.[^s10-25]

In the proposal of Islam to the People of the Book — for Islam once made a certain proposal to the People of the Book, and that proposal is in the Qur'an — the negation of obedience to non-divine powers has come in that verse in this form:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:64):** "Say: O People of the Book! Come to a word common between us and you: that we worship none but God, and that we associate nothing with Him, and that none of us take others as lords besides God. But if they turn away, then say: Bear witness that we are submitted [to God]."

"Say: O People of the Book! Come to a word that is equal between us and you" — which you yourselves will look at.

## Footnotes

[^s10-1]: From the root ṭ-w-y, meaning "folded up, wrapped."

[^s10-2]: From the root t-b-ʿ, meaning "to follow after something" (tatabbuʿ: to trace and pursue).

[^s10-3]: From the root b-y-n, meaning "opposed, separate" (mubāyin: divergent).

[^s10-4]: Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:112): "And thus We made for every prophet an enemy — satans (shayāṭīn) of men and jinn." See the session "The Opposing Groups."

[^s10-5]: From the roots r-q-q and d-q-q. Riqqah, in terms of meaning, is like diqqah (precision), but diqqah is used where all aspects of a matter are observed, while riqqah is by virtue of depth in a matter.

[^s10-6]: Imam al-Bāqir (peace be upon him), hadith no. 32. The Arabic: «la-uʿadhdhibanna kulla raʿiyyatin fī'l-islām dānat bi-walāyati kulli imāmin jāʾirin laysa min Allāh, wa in kānat al-raʿiyyatu fī aʿmālihā barratan taqiyyatan; wa la-aʿfuwanna ʿan kulli raʿiyyatin fī'l-islām dānat bi-walāyati kulli imāmin ʿādilin min Allāh, wa in kānat al-raʿiyyatu fī anfusihā ẓālimatan musīʾatan.»

[^s10-7]: From the root ʿ-q-b: tormented, chastised.

[^s10-8]: From the root n-q-m: retribution, chastisement (niqmah).

[^s10-9]: From the root f-ḥ-ṣ: to search out, to investigate.

[^s10-10]: "Like a stone beside the human being" (kāl-ḥajari fī janbi'l-insān).

[^s10-11]: Hinduism is the ancient religion of the people of India, from which other schools later arose over time. Belief in transmigration (reincarnation), observance of the regulations of social classes (castes) in association and marriage, and likewise reverence for living beings — especially the cow — are among the principles of this religion.

[^s10-12]: From the root sh-b-th: one who clutches, one who clings.

[^s10-13]: Sūrat Sabaʾ (34:33): "And those who were oppressed (kept down) will say to those who were arrogant, 'Rather, it was the plotting of night and day.'"

[^s10-14]: From the root w-k-ʾ: a support, a thing to lean upon.

[^s10-15]: From the root k-w-n: to make, to bring into being (tadbīr takwīnī: creational governance).

[^s10-16]: From the root sh-r-ʿ: legislating (tadbīr tashrīʿī: legislative governance).

[^s10-17]: The Mosque of Karāmat, in holy Mashhad.

[^s10-18]: A walī (guardian) from the side of God.

[^s10-19]: Sūrat Yūnus (10:35).

[^s10-20]: Please refer to the mimeographed handout (poly-copy) at the end of the session.

[^s10-21]: From the verse: "O ignorant one, go — from a guide without gnosis, what grace? / Where does the blind man become the staff-bearer of another blind man?" (Kalīm Kāshānī).

[^s10-22]: Belonging to the tribe of Quraysh.

[^s10-23]: A religion that was current in ancient Iran and among the Indo-Aryan peoples. Later this religion was modified in Iran by Zoroaster. Fire, in this religion, is a symbol of purity.

[^s10-24]: Kanāʾis: the plural of kanīsah, the place of worship of the Jews.

[^s10-25]: From the root m-l-ʾ: the great ones and notables of a people (al-Malaʾ). From the root t-r-f: the wealth-hoarders and aristocrats of a people (al-Mutrafīn).


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 11: The Spirit of Tawhid: Negating Servitude to Other Than God

*روح توحید: نفی عبودیت غیر خدا — Sunday, 7 Mehr 1353 / 12 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:114–116):** "…Shall I then seek a judge other than God, when it is He who has sent down to you the Book, set out in detail? And those to whom We gave the Book know that it is sent down from your Lord in truth — so do not be among the doubters. And the word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and justice; there is none who can change His words, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing."[^s11-1]

[^s11-2] However much we reflect, it turns out that Tawhid is not something one can pass over so easily. First, it is the foundation of belief. Second, it is the principal practical reality of the individual and of society. Third, the believing, monotheist Muslim community knows very little about it — indeed, one could say that they know nothing of it. Even though in the old maktab schools children were taught that God is one and not two, for the most part the monotheists know nothing properly about the various dimensions of Tawhid, right up to the years near their departure from this world. Therefore a subject of such importance, about which our people have so little knowledge, is one a person does well to speak about at greater length.

As it happens, the verses of the Qur'an too, in keeping with this very importance, in many places and in various tones, have spoken about Tawhid. And even if I were to go on speaking about this important principle of belief and practice for several more days, one could still bring from the noble verses of the Qur'an testimonies, verses, and examples to clarify the point we have in view. Now, what we are reading today again draws attention to a fresh aspect of Tawhid, one that was alluded to yesterday as well, and which today will be addressed in fuller detail.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:114–116):** "In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. Shall I then seek a judge other than God, when it is He who has sent down to you the Book, set out in detail? And those to whom We gave the Book know that it is sent down from your Lord in truth — so do not be among the doubters. And the word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and justice; there is none who can change His words, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing."[^s11-1]

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:116):** "…they follow nothing but conjecture, and they do nothing but guess."

These are from several verses, which of course we have translated here[^s11-3] and at the end will explain — briefly. And:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:121):** "And do not eat of that over which the name of God has not been mentioned; that is indeed a transgression. And truly the satans inspire their friends to dispute with you; and if you obey them, you are surely idolaters (mushrikūn)."[^s11-4]

The gist of the matter, with respect to today's point of view, is this: at one time, people worship someone in the sense that they regard him as sacred and as possessing powers above the natural world — as, for instance, certain idols, or certain "saints" (qiddīsīn)[^s11-5] have been worshipped by peoples throughout history. From the word "worship," what initially comes to mind is just this. When we say that worshipping God is obligatory, what comes to our minds is this same sanctification, this same state of spiritual and heartfelt reverence — as, for example, Christians ascribe a kind of sanctity to Jesus (peace be upon him) or to his pure and immaculate mother Mary (the blessings of God upon her): before an imagined statue of Christ or Mary they kneel, they weep, they worship him. This is one common, widespread sense of worship.

Beyond this sense, there is another meaning — or call it another corner of this same concept — which can also be called worship, and in the Qur'an "worship" is used in this sense. If someone is worshipped in this second manner, which we will now explain — and if some people worship a being, a human being, in the very manner we will now describe — this too is a kind of worship of other than God. The upshot is that worship is not only that a person, before some being, bends and straightens, prostrates, bows, prays to him, praises him, stretches out his hands toward him in humility, in the form of sanctification, of heartfelt respect, of regarding him as higher. That is not the whole of worship. There are other acts as well that can be called worship — and when we say "can be called," we are not inventing this from ourselves.

(Sir! Draw those curtains so you are not sitting in the sun. Sir! I am talking to you — draw the curtains; why are you sitting in the sun? It has gotten worse! Fix it. Please pay attention — that was a parenthetical sentence; we did not want it to take so long and require explanation.) There is another act — I am still continuing that same point; it is not a third matter — which we also call worship. It is not we who say so; the Qur'an says it is worship. Therefore, in the culture of the Qur'an there exists a broader sense of "worship," and we must discover that broader sense, so that if we wish to worship God and not worship other than God — that is, if we wish to be monotheists and followers of the principle of Tawhid — we may take care not to unknowingly perform the second type of worship before something other than the Lord of the worlds. This is precisely the thing that most of the world's monotheists — even though, in their own estimation and to all appearances, they were not performing any sanctification before other than God, nor prostrating before persons or objects other than God — nevertheless, in practice, in thought, in heart, in spirit, have been performing the worship of other than God in this second sense.

What is the second meaning of worship? The second meaning of worship is very simple. There is a word for it in Persian; this word is current and on everyone's tongue, and it is "obedience" (iṭāʿat). To obey anyone in an absolute and unconditional manner is to worship him. If a person obeys someone — a human being, or a human society — unconditionally, taking his command as that which is to be followed (muttabaʿ)[^s11-6] in his own life, in his body and soul, in his deeds, he has worshipped him. From where do we say this? We say it on the authority of the verses of the Qur'an. It is the Qur'an that interprets "worship" for us as "obedience."

ʿAdī ibn Ḥātim al-Ṭāʾī[^s11-7] — the son of the famous Ḥātim, though the rank of this ʿAdī himself is far higher and more honored than that of his father Ḥātim — when he entered Medina, the Noble Messenger began reciting this verse to him, on account of seeing a cross-cord (zunnār)[^s11-8] hung around his neck:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Tawbah (9:31):** "They took their rabbis and their monks as lords besides God, and also the Messiah, son of Mary; yet they were commanded to worship none but One God."[^s11-9]

The meaning of the verse is that the Christians and Jews took their rabbis and their monks — their scholars and their ascetics — and the Messiah son of Mary, as their lords and gods, whereas God Most High had commanded them to worship none but the One God. When this verse reached the ears of ʿAdī ibn Ḥātim, he turned and said: O Prophet of God, this is not correct! When did we ever take our rabbis and monks as God and lord for ourselves? When did we ever worship them? He objected to the Prophet and to the verse of the Qur'an. Why? Because in his mind, "worship" had the very meaning that is now in your minds: the gentlemen are performing worship — meaning supplication, meaning a state coupled with sanctification, whether of the heart, or of the heart and tongue, or of the heart and tongue and body, as in performing the ritual prayer. Because he understood worship in this sense, when ʿAdī ibn Ḥātim saw that the verse of the Qur'an says these people worshipped their scholars and ascetics, chose and adopted them as their lord and master, it seemed to him objectionable, and he said: No, this is not correct. When did we Christians ever worship our rabbis and monks?

In answer to this notion of ʿAdī ibn Ḥātim, the Noble Prophet replied: Yes — you did not prostrate before them (I am conveying the substance of the Prophet's words), but whatever they said, you accepted unconditionally.

> **Hadith:** "But they made lawful for you what was forbidden, and forbade you what was lawful."[^s11-10]

They made God's forbidden things appear lawful in your eyes, and made God's lawful things appear forbidden, and you — without any concern to grasp the reality of the matter — obeyed unconditionally whatever they said. This is worship; this is what it means to take a being as one's lord and master. And indeed, on this same theme, there is also a narration from Imam al-Ṣādiq (the blessings and peace of God upon him); those who wish may consult Nūr al-Thaqalayn[^s11-11] under this very verse.

According to the culture of the Qur'an, worship of a being other than God — whether that being is a political power, or a religious power, or an inner factor such as the human soul (nafs) with its carnal and lustful inclinations, or a being outside the human being, but not a power centralized politically or religiously: before a woman, before a single human for whom one holds an unwarranted reverence, before a friend — worship before these is to obey them. Anyone who obeys someone or something has worshipped it.

Let me read here a narration, so that it may become clear how widespread this Qur'anic culture is throughout the Islamic sources, and especially the Shīʿī sources — both Qur'an and Hadith. The narration is from Imam al-Jawād (the blessings and peace of God upon him), who says:

> **Hadith:** "Whoever lends an ear to a speaker has worshipped him."[^s11-12]

The sphere of worship is far broader; not only obedience, but even giving one's full attention — all six senses — to someone is to worship him. Well, you will say: Then are we not to listen even to correct words? So, continuing the hadith, he says: "And if the speaker speaks on behalf of God, Mighty and Majestic, then he has worshipped God." If the one who speaks, and who has drawn your full attention and faculties[^s11-13] toward himself, speaks from the tongue of God, then you — while you have given your heart to him, given your mind to him, entrusted your thought and spirit to him — are worshipping God. "And if the speaker speaks from the tongue of Iblīs, then he has worshipped Iblīs." But if that speaker is speaking from the tongue of Satan, talking from the tongue of Iblīs — arguing and speaking contrary to the logic and the philosophy of divine thought — and you are listening submissively[^s11-14] and assentingly, then in that very state you are engaged in satanic worship and obedience; that is, he himself is Satan, absolutely. The very one who is speaking in that manner is Satan. We do not mean to say he is the representative of Satan or the loudspeaker of Satan — no; he himself is Satan, in the sense in which we defined Satan, which is also the Qur'anic meaning of Satan.

To obey a being who is of this sort — who is not even a political power, not even a religious power — to obey him unconditionally, is to worship him. And if someone wishes to worship only God and not to have worshipped other than God — that is, to be a monotheist, a worshipper of the One, oriented to the One — then he must also reserve his absolute obedience for the Lord of the worlds, the great God. Among the things which, if you follow them, you have worshipped, is the law. Among the things which, if you follow them, you have worshipped, is the social order. Among the things which, if you obey them, you have worshipped, are customs and traditions. So which law are we to act by? Are we not to act by any law? Are we not to act by customs and traditions? Are we not to follow some order and system? Indeed we are — but try to make all this divine, so that you, while obeying, while following, are servants of God and engaged in the worship of God.

See how broad a person's horizon becomes. See how the events of history become interpretable for a person. The prophets, when they came, all came with the idea of Tawhid. In the discussion concerning the prophets and specifically prophethood, we will expound this from the Qur'an. All the great divine prophets came to make people monotheists. To make them monotheists — what does that mean? It means to unfasten the chains of obedience to other than God from their hands and necks. And the Qur'an itself explicitly states this meaning in one place:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:157):** "…that He may lift from them their burden and the shackles that were upon them"[^s11-15] — that He may take from their shoulders the heavy load and unfasten from them the fetters that were placed upon them.

And when Tawhid is viewed from this perspective — when you look at Tawhid from this standpoint — you see that it is a thought, a principle, fundamentally for living: connected to the social order, connected to the orientation of human beings in every circumstance, connected to the quality of life of human societies. See how greatly this Tawhid differs from the dry, brainless, lifeless, ignorant "God is one and not two." Tawhid means this.

Now, I have found many instances of this in the verses of the Qur'an. If I had wanted to mention and bring here all the places where this meaning is clearly to be derived from the Qur'an, I would have needed to prepare for you at least five or six pages of paper. I have brought two of them — two examples of those places where it is clearly obtained that obedience to other than God is the worship of it, and that pure Tawhid — the spirit of religion, the foundation of religion — consists in a person obeying God exclusively, and obeying the divine program, the divine order, the divine organization. Just two examples, from which this point is derived, are mentioned here, and you can, with this idea, refer to the Qur'an to find this subject.

Refer to the Qur'an. Try to become acquainted with the Qur'an; try not to be dependent on my coming and explaining the verses to you. Bring yourselves near to this boundless treasure, this shoreless sea. I have repeatedly given this counsel, and whenever I do not give this counsel I feel that a burden is upon my shoulders. First, I feel it my duty to tell you that you must refer to the Qur'an. This vast sea, this shoreless ocean, is such that "It is the sea — from whatever side you come to it";[^s11-16] this was a line of poetry, one hemistich from a poem. From whichever side you approach, you derive some benefit. Whoever keeps company with the Qur'an derives some benefit. In whatever way, in whatever style — have some foundation, so that you can understand this language at least a little, and you will gain a share; and the more you refer to it, the more illumined and aware you become. The Commander of the Faithful (the blessings and peace of God upon him), with a phrasing similar to this, is reported in the Nahj al-Balāgha to have said: "No one has ever sat in the company of this Qur'an without rising from it with an increase or a decrease — an increase in guidance or a decrease in blindness."[^s11-17] This is the word of the Commander of the Faithful. He says: No one ever sat in the company and companionship of the Qur'an without, when he rose from beside the Qur'an, an increase appearing in him and a decrease — a gain appearing in him, an increase occurring, and a loss, a diminution occurring. "An increase in guidance" — that increase was in guidance; his way-finding and his guidance increased somewhat, certainly. "A decrease in blindness" — that hard-heartedness, that blindness of his spirit and understanding and inner self, decreased a little and was diminished.

See how the sentence of the Commander of the Faithful begins: "No one has ever sat in the company of this Qur'an…" No one — it is general, isn't it? It is absolute. I have said this as a reminder — though it was within parentheses and was a marginal note — in order to draw the attention of you all, all the brothers and sisters, to it.

Why have I given this reminder about the Qur'an? So that you may know, you brothers and sisters, that many traps, endless devices, have been prepared over the course of time to keep people away from the Qur'an. And one of these pretexts and traps and devices, which still exists today — still a group of ignorant or ill-intentioned people repeat this claim — is this: "Sir! No one understands the Qur'an except the Infallible Imams (the blessings of God upon them); it is the Infallible Imams who understand the Qur'an." About this statement I can say only one word — the very thing the Commander of the Faithful (the blessings of God upon him) said about the slogan of the Khārijites of Nahrawān, when he declared: "A word of truth by which falsehood is intended."[^s11-18] This is a correct statement, but the intent of the one who utters it is a base and malicious intent. Yes! The Imams of guidance have mastery over the Qur'an, such that nothing higher than that is conceivable. They are those lofty, great spirits and minds and thoughts who hold the Qur'an in their grasp; they themselves are each a Qur'an, each one of them a Qur'an — there is no dispute in this. But that the Imam (peace be upon him) knows the Qur'an very well does not mean that I and you, sir, do not understand even a single word of it, cannot understand even a husk[^s11-19] of the Qur'an. This statement is correct, that the Imams of guidance (the blessings and peace of God upon them) are the knowers and the learned with respect to the Qur'an, but the intent of the speaker is not to exalt the Imam; the intent of the one who says this is for you to remain far from the Qur'an — like that poor fellow whom they told to pray, and he said: O sir, if prayer is what my master ʿAlī performed, then why should I pray at all? As it is said:

> If prayer were what that wronged one performed,
> then he has deprived others of this act.

This logic is the very same logic. If they understand the Qur'an, then why should we and you any longer open the Qur'an? O wretched one! O deprived one! O you deprived of the teachings of the Qur'an — woe to you! Woe to you! Woe to you who do not understand the Qur'an — but greater woe to you if, not understanding it yourself, you also prevent the people from understanding it; if you do not permit the thirsty people, too, to draw from this overflowing, gushing spring. Woe to you! Woe to those who keep people away from the Qur'an, who frighten people off from the Qur'an, who — on various pretexts and excuses[^s11-20] — do not let people open the Qur'an with the aim of understanding. Woe to them! And you must know, brothers and sisters, that today we are dealing with the Qur'an, just as the Prophet of God has said: "When tribulations beset you like pieces of the dark night, then keep to the Qur'an."[^s11-21] On the day when tribulations cast their shadow over your life like pieces of a black night, hold fast to the Qur'an. When is that day? Do we not see the dark, night-like tribulations? Do we not behold the wrong roads before our blind and short-sighted eyes? Do not the seeing eyes behold the thieves and ogres and highwaymen from every side and in every shape? So when must one turn to the Qur'an? For when is it? For the time when the Imam of the Age (the blessings of God upon him) comes? But he himself is the speaking Qur'an. Today is the day when we must refer to the Qur'an. The first condition of this is that we understand the Qur'an.

I have found two portions of the verses related to today's discussion; you search and find ten more portions. I counsel the brothers — those who are able to understand the Qur'an, that is, the outward sense of the Qur'an's sentences, the Arabic-to-Persian translations — to be sure to pay attention; those who cannot, let them create in themselves this possibility, this ability. Study Arabic, learn the Qur'an, take lessons in the Qur'an, make yourselves intimate with the Qur'an, become friends with the Qur'an. Every day and every hour that passes without intimacy with the Qur'an is a cause for regret.

The first portion is from Sūrat al-Anʿām. Of course, one must attend to the tone of the Qur'an. The Qur'an is not like ordinary books that say, "Chapter so-and-so, on the meaning of obedience and worship" — it is not like that. The level of expression and content of the Qur'an, and the Speaker of the Qur'an, is far above these ordinary levels. For the Lord of the worlds, all created things and all beings are on a single level; that is why, in the manner that the occasion of revelation required, a verse came down regarding a given matter. From the verse's allusions, from the expressions and sentences within the verse, from the manner of its construction (siyāq),[^s11-22] you find the point you have in view.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:114):** "Shall I then seek a judge (ḥakam) other than God?" — Shall I accept and seek anyone other than God as judge? "Ḥakam," the commentators have said, means both arbiter and ruler. The one from whom a person seeks judgment — that is, from whom he seeks command, or from whom he seeks arbitration — both are called "ḥakam." And God Most High is both the best arbiter and the best ruler. The command, too, must be given by God:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:54):** "Surely His are the creation and the command."[^s11-23]

Know that creation and command belong to God. He Himself has created, and He Himself also commands. "Shall I then seek a judge other than God?" — Shall I seek any ruler, commander, or arbiter other than God? "When it is He who has sent down to you the Book, set out in detail" — whereas it is God who has sent down this collection, the Qur'an, to you in detail, with elucidation, without admixture or commingling.[^s11-24]

"Set out in detail" (mufaṣṣalan) — meaning without any confusion in its discussions, without its matters being jumbled, without the speech of other than God being mixed into it. "Set out in detail" — that is, made clear (mubayyanan), with complete and perfect clarification.

"And those to whom We gave the Book" — those to whom We have given the Book — "know that it is sent down from your Lord in truth" — they know that this Qur'an has been sent down from your Lord, in truth; it has truly been sent down from God, has come from God. "So do not be among the doubters." So do not be of the doubters and the wavering ones — the address is to the Prophet — do not be hesitant! Do not be shaken and of two hearts! You who know that this Book has come from your Lord, that God gave the Book to you and you are aware, you are certain — do not be hesitant; be resolute.

"And the word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and justice." It is complete, sealed, irreversible — the command of your Lord, founded upon truth and upon firmness. The command of your Lord was this: that the chain of prophethoods should come, raising the people gradually and step by step to the ultimate degree; then the final prophethood should come, placing human beings before a vast horizon, before a limitless field; bestowing on them the means of journeying, the means of running, the means of ever-greater perfection, so that human beings might travel in this field unto the infinite — "and to Him we shall return"[^s11-25] — unto God. This command of the Lord, this measure of the Lord, this word of the Lord, was complete, sealed, irreversible. "There is none who can change His words" — there is no one who can alter or transform His words and commands. "And He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" — and He is the Hearer and the Knower: hearer of the melody of your inner needs, and knower of the way and the manner necessary for you. He is able to design and lay out a program for you.

"And if you obey most of those on earth…" — see how it prepares the listener's mind. In the first sentence, in the first verse, the issue is the rule and judgment of God, who is most worthy of all to rule and judge. In the second verse, the issue is the irreversibility of God's religion and command: let the enemy and the disbeliever and the obstinate adversary do whatever wrong they will — God's command is ratified[^s11-26] and complete. In the third verse, the point is that one must not obey desires and whims and caprices; one must obey God. "And if you obey most of those on earth, they will lead you astray from the path of God." And if you follow and obey most of the people on earth, the majority, "they will lead you astray from the path of God." "They follow nothing but conjecture" — they follow nothing but supposition, "and they do nothing but guess" — and they are only guessing. With illusion, with estimation, with conjecture, the people act. Those who fabricate and propose ways and methods and systems for people's lives — are they certain of the correctness of these ways? If, out of naïveté, they are certain, then one ought to wish that they remain forty or fifty years in this world, so they may see how their solid blueprints turned out ruined, see how their forecasts turned out wrong. They are not even certain; "they do nothing but guess." With estimation, with conjecture, with hypothesis and theory, they want to govern the people of the world, the human societies — with theory. But God does not govern anyone by theory; with the very text of reality, with real knowledge and science, He guides human beings to the straight path. "Surely your Lord — He knows best who strays from His path"[^s11-27] — surely it is your Lord who knows best the state of those who go astray from His path — "and He knows best the rightly guided" — and He knows best those who are guided and rightly led.

"Eat of that over which the name of God has been mentioned, if you are believers in His signs." Here a person, with utter astonishment, sees — at first — that after these general matters: that one must not obey the majority of people, that one must not follow conjecture and supposition and theory and hypothesis, that the command of the Lord regarding the final prophethood and regarding religion is complete and unalterable — after these general statements, suddenly He says: eat of that over which the name of God has been pronounced; a sheep slaughtered with the name of God — you have the right to eat of this; a secondary matter! It seems very astonishing to a person — what connection do these have with one another? Of course, whatever I say by way of connection are things I say from my own conception; there is no decisive proof of it. The field is open for understandings and thoughts and perceptions; let people study, find its relevance — but to us, too, some things occur.

First: in the sight of the Lord, who is above this world and stands at a level and horizon beyond human conception, as we said, all matters are on a single level. For God, the general matters relating to humankind are no different from the particular matters; it is all one. For God everything is one. For the Lord of the worlds, whatever is a means to a human being's felicity is set forth as a command; it makes no difference whether this command is particular, secondary, relating to one person, or whether it is general, universal, relating to all of humankind — that is the first point.

Second: let us properly dissect the matter of slaughter and ritual purification (tadhkiyah).[^s11-28] What does it mean that the name of God must be pronounced at the time of slaughtering an animal that a person intends to eat? You know that the polytheists, and the tribes and nations and communities who were deprived of Tawhid, used to invoke the name of their object of worship on every occasion, in every situation, for every act. "In the name of the Messiah,"[^s11-29] for example, as it is reported regarding the Christians. And we know that worldly idols, worldly powers, too, always try to set their own name at the opening and the outset and the preface of every act. Any act begun in the name of other than God necessarily has a non-divine orientation. When you begin a task for the sake of money, for the sake of carnal desire, for the sake of such matters, with that name, with that remembrance — then necessarily its direction too is the direction of the very thing whose name and remembrance you brought upon your tongue or into your mind at the start of that task. A task done with the remembrance of money, and for money, is oriented toward making money. To the very end it proceeds in such a way that money comes out of it. The act, in its orientation, moves in such a way that nothing other than money is of any concern to it. But a task begun in the name of God, with the remembrance of God, has a divine orientation as well; its orientation, too, is in accord with the command of God and in keeping with the order of the Lord. They tell us that even the slaughtered animal, when you slaughter it, slaughter it with the name of God. That is, your most essential, most primary need, which is your food, must be with the name of God, must be for God. Even when you fill your stomach, you must fill it for God. The result of this is that filling the stomach is not the principle — God is the principle. If at some point you sense that, in wishing to fill your stomach, you will fall far from God, then abandon this, do not fill it, let it remain hungry, let it die of hunger, and let no movement issue from you contrary to the divine orientation. Why? Because the stomach, though it is a genuine need, is nevertheless not the principle in your life; in your life the principle is God and the orientation toward God. This is what "Bismillāh" — uttered while slaughtering the sheep — teaches us; "Bismillāh," uttered while taking a morsel and eating food, teaches us this.

Begin with "Bismillāh," even eating. Begin with "Bismillāh," even the act of mating. Begin with "Bismillāh" — even entering, going, coming out, going into the house, the door of the shop — every act. What does this mean? It means that all the orientations of your life, for the meeting of any need, even the primary needs, must be in accord with God's command and in the direction of the path of God (sabīl Allāh).

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:162–163):** "Indeed my prayer, my rites of devotion, my living and my dying are for God, the Lord of the worlds"[^s11-30] — my prayer, my worship, my life, my death — yes, even my death — Abraham says: "for God, the Lord of the worlds," for God, that Disposer and Lord of the affairs of all the worlds. "He has no partner" — He has no partner whatsoever. The Lord of the worlds has no partner in any single domain of my life, even in my eating. Not only in my praying — in my movements, in my efforts, in my exertions, I admit no partner alongside God. This realm of my being is wholly at God's disposal and within the domain of His power and command. So when you slaughter the animal, slaughter it in this way.

But the Qurayshī disbeliever in one way, and the non-Qurayshī disbeliever in another way, when he slaughters the animal, when he kills the sheep, when he eats food, when he lives, when he opens his shop — not only is it not in the name of God, not with the remembrance of God, but it is in the name of other than God and with the remembrance of other than God. Take this slaughtered animal as a symbol; take the name of God at the time of slaughtering the sheep as a symbol. Although it is itself a ruling, a juristic ruling — and of course the name of God must definitely be pronounced — but take this as a symbol, a symbol of the essential and genuine needs of a human being: the slaughtering of the sheep. This must be with the name of God; what does this mean? It means that even your most essential, most fundamental, most primary, most genuine needs you must regard as for the sake of God, and seek for the sake of God. Even the morsel of bread you eat to relieve your hunger, eat it for the sake of God; for the sake of God relieve your hunger and breathe strength into your body. Well, it is evident that the strength which has come to a person's body for the sake of God must also be expended for the sake of God. This is the logical conclusion, plain as two and two make four. See how precise it is.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:118–119):** "Eat of that over which the name of God has been mentioned" — eat of that over which the name of God has been pronounced — "if you are believers in His signs," if you are believers in the signs of God. "And why should you not eat of that over which the name of God has been mentioned?"[^s11-31] — what is the matter with you, that is, for what reason, why "should you not eat" — "of that over which the name of God has been mentioned"? "When He has set out in detail for you what He has forbidden you, except that to which you are compelled" — whereas God has, in detail, by way of explanation, set forth for you what is forbidden to you, except in the state of necessity; apart from that, everything is lawful for you. "And surely many lead astray by their desires, without knowledge" — many lead people astray by their own whims, without any knowledge, without any insight. By the sheer caprice of their own whims and desires they draw people into deviation and error. "Surely your Lord — He knows best the transgressors" — your Lord indeed knows best the transgressors and the aggressors. These who draw people into deviation and error without possessing knowledge and learning are, according to the indication of this verse, transgressors (muʿtadīn), aggressors, those committing aggression and enmity.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:120):** "And forsake the outward and inward of sin" — set aside that whose sin is outward and that whose sin is hidden and inward. There are some acts whose flaws, whose evil consequences, are outward: that the killing of a soul is an evil deed is plain; to take the life of a living being, without warrant, is plainly a wrongful act; its being a sin is outward. And there are some acts whose being a sin is not properly evident, manifest, and visible. There are many things a person does not realize how great they are: speaking without knowledge, following other than knowledge, treating the name and remembrance of God lightly and as trivial, obeying other than God, hearing and heeding the command of other than God — these are things a person does not imagine could have such great harms and evil consequences; their being a sin is hidden, concealed. Yet, even so, one must set aside both. Whatever is sin, whether overt or hidden, must be forsaken. "And forsake the outward and inward of sin" — set aside and abandon those things which are outwardly sinful, whose being a sin is outward, and those that are inwardly sinful, whose being a sin is inward and concealed. "Surely those who commit sin will be requited for what they used to perpetrate" — those who make sin their acquisition and earning will be requited for the deeds they used to commit. "And do not eat of that over which the name of God has not been mentioned" — do not eat of that over which the name of God has not been pronounced — "for that is indeed a transgression (fisq)" — this is fisq, a going out from religion. Here He places emphasis on the importance of the remembrance of God and the name of God.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:121):** "And truly the satans inspire…" — most of our emphasis is on this segment. Of course, from the beginning of these verses we have recited up to here, there were step by step indications useful to us for the point we have set forth here. And of course, the more you ponder, the clearer it becomes. But the principal portion of our argument and citation is this final segment of the verse: "And truly the satans inspire their friends to dispute with you" — the satans and the poles of wickedness inspire their friends and confederates and allies to dispute with you. The satans and the poles of corruption and decay fill up their agents, their friends, their confederates and adherents, so that they may come and argue with you, contend with you, and dispute with you. What is your duty before these friends of Satan? "And truly the satans inspire their friends to dispute with you" — but as for you — "and if you obey them, you are surely idolaters (mushrikūn)" — if you obey them, follow them, you are idolaters. See — an idolater, very plainly. So obedience to Satan — that is, the pole of wickedness, set over against the All-Merciful; Satan over against the All-Merciful; Satan over against God — obedience to Satan, that is, the pole of wickedness, or obedience to the friends of Satan, that is, his hirelings, his instruments, his friends, his confederates, his servants: obedience to these causes you to become an idolater. "And if you obey them" — and if you obey them — "you are surely" — you indeed — "idolaters" — you are certainly idolaters.

That is one chapter. The second portion is from Sūrat al-Shuʿarāʾ, which we now do not have much time to translate in full detail; I will give a very brief translation so that we may conclude.
