The most striking and penetrating expressions on many themes, whether in the Qur'an or in Hadith, are set forth for people in the form of images and scene-settings of the Resurrection. When they wish to vividly portray a matter for the listener — and it is the same in the hadiths — to make it clear so that it may penetrate to the depths of his soul, they depict for him a scene of the Resurrection; that affair is reflected there, and the present passage is of this kind.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Shuʿarāʾ (26:90):** "And Paradise will be brought near for the God-fearing."[^s11-32]

Paradise was brought near and within reach for the pious. He is speaking of the Day of Resurrection, but He speaks of it in the past tense. The certain-to-occur future, in the Arabic language, can be expressed with the past tense. "The Hour drew near, and the moon was split"[^s11-33] — meaning, it will be. "And Paradise will be brought near for the God-fearing" — and Paradise was brought near and within reach, accessible, for the pious. "And the Blazing Fire will be made manifest to those who went astray" — and Hellfire was shown and made apparent to the astray and the deceived. "Ghiwāyah" means going astray; "We led them astray"[^s11-34] means We deceived them. "Ghāwīn" means the astray, the deceived, the deluded — these are called "ghāwīn." "And it will be said to them: Where is that which you used to worship besides God?" — it will be said to them, to these deceived and astray ones: "Where is that which you used to worship besides God?" Where are those things and persons whom you used to worship in place of God? Where are they? Those poles to whom you were attached in life and whose worship you performed — where are they? Here, take note of the word "worship": "that which you used to worship" — you used to worship them. But let us see what they were, those whom these people worshipped, so that the meaning of worship may become clear. "Can they help you, or help themselves?" — can those objects of your worship help you, or are they themselves helped from somewhere? "Or help themselves" — meaning, are they themselves helped? It becomes clear that these objects of worship are ones in need of being helped — evidently they are human beings. These objects of worship were of the human kind, not of the kind of stone and wood and lifeless idols. "Then they will be flung headlong into it — they and those who strayed, and the hosts of Iblīs, all together." So they are flung face-down into Hellfire — they, those objects of worship and the deluded and astray and deceived, the followers of corruption — "and the hosts of Iblīs, all together." And the hosts of Iblīs all together: everyone who in some way did a deed for Iblīs, rendered a service to Iblīs, took a step on the path of leading God's creatures astray, in whatever manner — in the Resurrection the abode of them all is Hell; their appointment is there. "They will say, while disputing with one another therein…" — then, in the throes of contention with one another, they fall upon each other: these against those, those against these; these cast the blame upon those leaders, and the leaders cast the blame upon these. These say: It was you scoundrels who put the rope around our necks and dragged us behind you, by force, by compulsion; those say: It was you who followed behind us. Each delivers and brings forth a blow, a weapon, against the other group. "They will say" — they say or said — "while disputing with one another therein" — while in Hellfire engaged in contention and combat; they were in dispute and strife. "By God" — they swear by God — "surely we were in manifest error."[^s11-35] We were in a manifest error, in a vast error, in an obvious error. Despite its obviousness, we did not realize we were astray, even though if we had come to ourselves even a little, we would have understood what a wrong road, what an evil road, what a dangerous path, and what a deadly, destructive end it was. Even though, if we had thought a little, these things would have become clear — yet, even so, we remained in that error.

"By God, surely we were in manifest error" — by God, we were in manifest error. In what way was our deed an error? In this deed of ours: "when we made you equal with the Lord of the worlds" — when we set you on a par with the Lord of the worlds. We ought to have feared God; we feared you. We ought to have been attentive to the command of God; we were attentive to your command. We ought to have striven toward God and for nearness to God; we strove for nearness to you. We ought to have sought our sustenance from God; we sought it from you. "When we made you equal with the Lord of the worlds" — when we set you equal and on a par with the Lord of the worlds. "And none led us astray but the criminals" — none led us astray but the criminals and sinners. "So now we have no intercessors" — today we have no intercessor — "nor any loving friend" — and we have no compassionate friend. "If only we had a return, that we might be among the believers!" — would that there were for us a return to the world, so that we might become believers. "Surely in that there is a sign" — in this affair and scene there is a sign; take heed. "Surely in that there is a sign" — in this saying there is a sign and a token. "But most of them are not believers" — most of them are not believers, not possessed of faith.

See here, in these verses, that the discourse was about persons whom the people had worshipped; then, when we look carefully at the words,[^s11-36] we see that their worship had this meaning: that they had fallen in behind them, had regarded them as equal to God, had sought from them what should be sought from God, and had observed their regard in matters where, for the sake of God, one ought not to act.

[^s11-1]: Sūrat al-Anʿām, verses 114–116.
[^s11-2]: This verse is translated here; please refer to the mimeographed handout at the end of the session.
[^s11-3]: Please refer to the mimeographed handout at the end of the session.
[^s11-4]: Sūrat al-Anʿām, verse 121.
[^s11-5]: (q-d-s) "Qiddīs": the faithful, the pious.
[^s11-6]: (t-b-ʿ) "Muttabaʿ": leader, that which is followed and pursued.
[^s11-7]: ʿAdī ibn Ḥātim, son of Ḥātim al-Ṭāʾī, who after his father took over the leadership of the tribe. In the ninth year of the Hijrah, influenced by the character and conduct of the Prophet, he became a Muslim. He was among the companions and devotees of the Commander of the Faithful, present in the battles of the Jamal, Ṣiffīn, and Nahrawān, and he offered three of his sons to Islam in the Battle of Ṣiffīn. He died in the year sixty-seven of the Hijrah.
[^s11-8]: The cord by which Christians used to hang the cross around their necks.
[^s11-9]: Sūrat al-Tawbah, verse 31.
[^s11-10]: Hadith no. 39.
[^s11-11]: Nūr al-Thaqalayn, a narration-based commentary, written by Shaykh ʿAbd ʿAlī ibn Jumʿah al-ʿArūsī al-Ḥuwayzī, one of the scholars of the eleventh century of the Hijrah (lunar).
[^s11-12]: Hadith no. 45.
[^s11-13]: (sh-ʿ-r) plural of "mashʿar": the five outward senses and the inner faculties.
[^s11-14]: (dh-ʿ-n) "Mudhʿinan": acknowledging, submitting, bowing the neck (in acceptance).
[^s11-15]: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf, verse 157.
[^s11-16]: "It is the sea — from whatever side you come to it; / its depths are renowned good, and its shores are beneficence." (It is a sea: from whichever side you come to it, its waves are goodness and its shores are beneficence.)
[^s11-17]: Hadith no. 14.
[^s11-18]: Hadith no. 15.
[^s11-19]: "Husk": the outer shell.
[^s11-20]: (ʿ-dh-r) plural of "miʿdhār": pretexts, excuses.
[^s11-21]: Hadith no. 3.
[^s11-22]: "Siyāq": style and manner, the manner of constructing sentences.
[^s11-23]: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf, verse 54.
[^s11-24]: (kh-l-ṭ) to become intermingled; (m-z-j) to become mixed.
[^s11-25]: Sūrat al-Baqarah, verse 156.
[^s11-26]: (m-ḍ-y) made current, ratified, made definitive.
[^s11-27]: Sūrat al-Anʿām, verses 117 and 118.
[^s11-28]: (dh-k-w) in the language it means to kindle a fire. As a technical term, it means to expel the instinctive heat of an animal from its body.
[^s11-29]: Hadith no. 38.
[^s11-30]: Sūrat al-Anʿām, verses 162 and 163.
[^s11-31]: Sūrat al-Anʿām, verses 119 and 120.
[^s11-32]: Sūrat al-Shuʿarāʾ, verses 90–103.
[^s11-33]: Sūrat al-Qamar, verse 1: "The Hour drew near and the moon was split."
[^s11-34]: Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ, verse 63.
[^s11-35]: (continuation of Sūrat al-Shuʿarāʾ, verses 90–103).
[^s11-36]: To examine a word or thing closely.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 12: Tawhid and the Negation of Social Classes

*توحید و نفی طبقات اجتماعی — Monday, 8 Mehr 1353 / 13 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:91–92):** "God has not taken to Himself any son, nor is there any god alongside Him — for then each god would have taken off with what he created, and some of them would have lorded it over others. Glory be to God, far above what they describe."[^s12-1]

Dear brothers, with your attention please. We said that if we take Tawhid to be a belief that gives rise to action and that imposes commitment, then inevitably this belief lays upon our shoulders a whole series of obligations and commitments. And we said that we must understand these obligations and commitments. Of course, these commitments are not confined to the individual life of human beings; rather, the greater weight of these commitments falls upon social life, upon the social order and the form of society. That is, when Tawhid enters a society, the first thing it does is to construct that society in a form that befits this belief. Once that work is done, only then does the turn come to ask what duties a person who professes Tawhid has as an individual.

In any case, these commitments must be recognized and known. We have given the name "the resolution of Tawhid" to the whole set of these commitments. We said that Tawhid hands us a kind of resolution — it places into our hands a set of contracts and obligations. In order that we may, God willing, live as believers in divine unity, it is necessary that we treat these commitments as active and binding. The first of these commitments was this: that servitude and obedience belong exclusively to God; this is the first commitment of Tawhid. We explained this point in the course of one or two — or two or three — recitations.

Today we come to the second commitment that Tawhid places upon the shoulders of the believer in unity — upon the monotheistic believer, the monotheistic society, the monotheistic world. This can be designated under the heading that we have written here: Tawhid and the negation of social classes.[^s12-2] (In these written notes you have in hand, it is good that you put the number "2" at the top of the page. Because on that note concerning exclusive servitude and obedience, the number "1" had been written; here too you should put a "2" so that the sequence of the topics is clear. We will, God willing, present the third one later, and the fourth, and if there are more, then more.)

The negation of social classes — and in a brief phrase, whose details we shall present later, the monotheistic society, according to the terminology now familiar to your ears, is a classless society. It is a society in which the various groups of human beings are not separated from one another with respect to rights and privileges. All human beings live under a single roof of rights. Everyone lives and moves along a single course, with one kind of opportunity and one kind of rights. This is the society that Tawhid sets before the eye of our mind and our imagination, with regard to social stratification.

When we turn back to history, we see that class division is among the chronic afflictions of history in all societies. Not only in the backward tribal societies, not only in the societies of lands remote from civilization, but in those very countries and lands that are the mother of human civilization and the cradle of human civilization — there, in those very places, class division shows itself to us from between the pages of history in its ugliest face and most repugnant form. It is truly the great injustice of history and the great stain of shame upon human history — among them, this very thing: class division.

What does "class division" mean? It means that the human beings who live in this society are not all alike. One group is condemned to suffer deprivation, to bear hardship, to serve the other groups — and must not even voice any complaint about this deprivation and hardship. Another group, by contrast, is to be well-provided, to be favored; the pleasure and enjoyment of life is to be theirs, they are able to make use of all the privileges, and there is to be no problem in this.

For example, in India — and as an example, the best place one can cite is India. You know that India is the cradle of Aryan civilization, and the Aryan race, from the very beginning of urban settlement, from the very beginning when it formed a nation and an organized community, was in this very India. They say the Aryan race came from the northern lands and from Siberia, and when they entered, they split into two branches: one group went toward India, the other toward Iran. And the people who were in India reached civilization sooner than those who came into Iran and settled into their lives.

India is the cradle of human civilization, or one of the cradles of human civilization, and there class division is in full uproar. They held that there were four principal classes — one class beneath the others, and hundreds of sub-classes in between these four principal ones. The details of this you can search for in the books concerning the history of religions — those who have a taste for it and an interest, let them search, and let them reflect a little, with deliberation and thought, on Tawhid and the Quran, and make the comparison.

They divided the people into four classes. The first class was the Brahmins, the religious and clerical men, who were the highest of the social classes. The second class was the warriors and the princes; they were in the second class. Of course, between these two classes there were abundant disputes and conflicts. Formerly the princes were the upper class and the clerics were the second class; later, owing to certain factors that arose and the struggles that took place, the clerics and Brahmins managed to appropriate to themselves the scholarly and upper class, and to place the warriors and soldiers and princes and chieftains in the second class.

Passing beyond the second class, there was the class of farmers and laborers, the craftsmen. These two upper classes did no work for society. What did the Brahmin do, except to recite a few incantations and litanies and wave his hands about? What did that chieftain and prince do, except to arrange for himself a little more land, a little more wealth, and a somewhat larger and broader domain of lordship? But the craftsmen and the farmers — they performed all the work of this vast land; they were the third class. The fourth class was the common people. The commonality, that is, ordinary folk — no doubt tradesmen, those engaged in society in small and manual tasks. Beneath these four classes there was yet another class, which they said was not of the Aryan race at all. These four classes were all of the Aryan race; the others they said were outside the Aryan race, and therefore were impure — the class of untouchables.

Of course, what I am now describing pertains to history — but a history that extended onward and onward and onward, right up to the twentieth century. In India, what I have just described to you remained in full force and full authority until a few years ago. Perhaps the gentlemen who have studied the affairs of India know that the class of untouchables and the defiled was abolished and eliminated by Gandhi, the late leader of India, who said: "What is this 'untouchable'?!" The former prime minister of India himself — that is, Nehru[^s12-3] — and now his daughter[^s12-4] is the head of state — they themselves are among the Brahmins. Nehru himself was among the Brahmins, in the upper class. His father, Pandit Nehru, was among the Brahmins. Of course, since he was anti-religious and irreligious, he disliked being called "Brahmin" and "Pandit" — the special title of the Brahmins.

In any case, I mean to say that these are not really things belonging only to ancient history. Even after some fourteen centuries — or thirteen-and-some centuries — had passed since the rising of the sun of Quranic Tawhid, still in India, in that place which is the birthplace of human civilization, this class division existed.

Well, now my aim is to expound class division. They had four classes, and as we said, in between the classes — according to the account of John Noss,[^s12-5] who wrote a history of religions — there were tens, indeed hundreds, of subsidiary classes. And then, two persons from two classes had no right to marry one another, to speak with one another, to shake hands with one another, to sit side by side, to walk together, to be friends with one another — *wa qis ʿalayhi, faʿaltu wa tafaʿʿaltu* (and so on and so forth).[^s12-6] They were utterly separated from one another by a wall of steel.

Why should they be separated? What does the Brahmin have that I, the farmer, do not have? Or that I, the ordinary tradesman, do not have? Or what natural privilege does some great warrior enjoy that I, the ordinary commoner, do not enjoy? If this question were asked, do you know what they would say in answer? They would say: "Mind your own business!" Because in the very root of creation, in the very root of your coming-into-being, you were created as two groups, of two kinds. It has nothing to do with law and contract and the convention of Zayd and ʿAmr — you were created of two kinds. Because when Brahma, the great god, wished to bring forth creatures, when he wished to fashion the human being, he created the Brahmins from his own head, the chieftains and soldiers and princes from his own hands, the farmers from his own arms, and the people of the laboring class from his own feet; and the impure classes are not of Brahma at all, are not of that pure root, are not of this race.

When the fourth class was created from Brahma's foot, what right does it have to consider itself the equal and peer of the Brahmin, who was created from Brahma's head? You are not at all equal in creation; one group was created from a nobler origin, they are worthy of greater respect, their endowment is natural, their being-favored is innate; and the deprivation of the lower classes is likewise innate. This was the logic.

Do you imagine that in such a society it is at all possible for class division ever to be overthrown? Is such a thing possible? Why would it be possible? Who do you imagine could possibly abolish class division in a class-divided society? Is it the favored class? Does the well-provided class come forward and say, "For God's sake, I have relinquished my rights"? That is a vain fancy. Surely, if class division is to be overthrown in a society, it is the deprived class that must demand its own right, must claim the prosperity that belongs to it, and must protest against the excessive privileges of the upper classes — the lower class must protest. But the lower class, in such a society as I have depicted, is impossible to make protest. Why? Because it believes that they were created this way by their very essence, that the natural state is just so; it is not at all subject to change or alteration, nothing can be done about it — they were made this way, they were created this way.

When a person has believed that nature and innate disposition and temperament and the divine creation within him have created him in a particular form, it is impossible for him to expect that any form other than that should be hoped for him, or that he should enjoy privileges and rights that are not consistent with the structure of his constitution and his nature. He says: "This is my lot. The threadbare carpet of one whose fortune was woven black cannot be set right. From the first we were woven in black cloth, from the first we were spun of an inferior sort; nothing more can be done."[^s12-7] Take some black object to the edge of a stream, to the shore of an ocean, and begin to wash it and wash it — when it is black by its very essence, when from the beginning its natural color has been black, is it possible for this color of it to disappear? Rather, its dust and dirt will be cleaned off, its blackness will become clearer, it will turn even blacker. Therefore, throughout history — as you have observed — in these societies, this great injustice existed, and there was no means of remedying it.

Of course, reformers would appear and change people's thinking. Pay attention to this point I am about to make — I will not state this meaning explicitly, but this is a sign of something peculiar to the religions. Reformers would come; first they changed the thought, first they transformed the culture, first they said that this philosophy is wrong, and on the basis of the wrongness of that philosophy they convinced the people that this state of affairs is tyrannical. The correct understanding of historical transformations is this. It is not correct for us to say that first the situation changed, that first the social form was transformed and then the philosophies and ideas were transformed — no. Reformers would come. This is our history, the history of the Orient. That is to say, those who thought that way and assumed that way had, in our view, not read the history of the Orient — they had seen the history of Europe. This is India, this is China, this is Iran, this is Egypt. The reformers who arose, the religious reformers, first changed the ideas, transformed the philosophies; and then, once the ideas had changed and the philosophies had changed, only then did the ground become ready for the social condition and the corrupt social order to be transformed as well. At any rate, throughout history it was this way.

You observed: here it was India; Iran too was like this, Egypt too was like this, China too was like this. Wherever you have any trace of a civilization, the situation was on this same pattern. What does that mean? It means it was said: since human beings have been created of two kinds, since they have been created from two natures and two dispositions and two temperaments — or more — therefore their social rights too must not be equal with one another. It is necessary that they not be alike; and if anyone said they ought to be alike, he has spoken wrongly. This is the way they used to put it.

Islam came and proclaimed that there are no "gods" — many gods are false; there is [one] God. Those who imagined that two gods had created two groups of human beings, with two kinds of distinctions and characteristics, have erred. There is one God, and He created the whole of the cosmos as well; the governance of the world and of its inhabitants is entirely in the grip of His power, and He created all alike. He fashioned all from one root, from one disposition, from one nature, from one material root and source. The verses of the Quran on this theme are abundant:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:21):** "O mankind, worship your Lord, who created you and those who were before you."[^s12-8]

Now, the verses that we mentioned today in this note and in today's recitation, we shall read. He said that all human beings are of one root, one source, one stem. All human beings came into the world with one kind of equipment. All human beings are fit for the soaring and the ascent toward infinite perfection. All human beings can become great, and great, and ever greater. This capacity exists in everyone. Of course, take note: the beings and the human beings who are perceptibly above the level of the ordinary human being — that is, the prophets and the imams — they are not included in this general statement of mine.

This thing we said, that every human being can soar and reach to infinity — its meaning is not that a human being can also become a prophet or an imam through his striving and his struggle; that is another discussion. How it is that a prophet becomes a prophet, or an imam becomes an imam — that is a subsequent matter. In another talk, in another discussion, if we are able, we may make a brief reference to it. In summary: the prophets and the imams — God's blessings be upon them — they enjoy greater capacities, they have certain peculiarities that do not exist in the ordinary human being. Of course, you know that their number is small, they are exceptional, and the discussion is about the individuals of human societies in general. Yet even those same prophets and imams, in their root and in their material origin, are no different at all from ordinary human beings — "their father is Adam and the mother is Eve."[^s12-9]

This is an Islamic teaching; in the shade of Tawhid it was taught to human beings, was established, was set down as a matter of record, that in the very fabric of society there is no class division. The society that Islam brings into being is not group-by-group, is not class-by-class; it is impossible for some people to enjoy rights that others do not enjoy. In that society it is not said that people were created from two roots, two sources, two stems, or more. In that society it is not said that one person was created from clay and that other one — let us suppose — was created from light. From the material standpoint, the material origin of all is one and the same thing.

Therefore Tawhid — in the sense of holding that there is one God, of regarding the governance and the creation and the bringing-into-being and the administration of the world as proceeding from one God — is the guarantor of the negation of social classes.

Of course, there is a point here, and it is this: that social classes have not always been in the form I described, that I expounded, regarding India. It is not the case that everywhere there is a philosophy of that sort. It may well be that in certain societies, among certain communities and nations, from the standpoint of the philosophy and the claims and the outlooks that are put forward among the people, everyone stands on a single level — yet in practice class division exists, just as it exists in our world today.

Today, look at the world: especially in the capitalist world, especially in those countries that have risen to a greater height in terms of capitalism, class division is conspicuously visible. Of course, there they have never said that the laborers and the employers were created from two origins and two roots of creation and formation — no. They do not say that the gentleman who owns some great company — for example, a certain cartel — and the laborer of some mine are of two stems, of two roots — no. But the practice that they carry out together, the regulations they lay down for these two groups of people, the mode of conduct they establish in society between these two kinds of human being, differs little from those people who believed that these were created from two roots and two origins and two stems. For one group, infinite opportunities; for the larger group, zero opportunities. One group exploits the wealth of the whole world to its own benefit; another group is not even permitted to eat alone the fruit of its own toil.

Class division in this sense too holds true, and this exists in the world today. Indeed, I want to say even more: this class division is more base and more insidious than the class division of that earlier day. Those people said outright: "Yes, we are different from one another." These people say: "We are brothers with one another, we are all on a single scale, we are indeed the protectors of your rights" — yet in practice we see that it is not so; in practice those same distinctions exist. Sometimes, in the matter of administering legal regulations, certain equalities and parities are observed — that So-and-so likewise committed such-and-such a sin and was subjected to such-and-such a penalty, even though he is of the upper class. But again, when we look, we see — no, the root of the matter is something else. We see that again those same class divisions, again those same social distinctions, remain in full force. In reality, the favors are confined to one particular group, and a majority are deprived; and the first thing of which they are deprived is a sound understanding and perception and full and correct development.

Islam has negated all of this. In summary, what I have drawn from the verses of the Quran, in the whole collection of this precious Book, on the theme of the negation of social classes — when I gave thought to these, I saw that there is a great deal of it, and I have written down a few here. A corner of these teachings, the mark of which is manifest in the verses below, is as follows:

The Creator and the Worshipped One and the Director of the affairs of all is God. This is an Islamic statement. The Creator of all is one — He is God. You may say, "What difference does it make?" It makes a great deal of difference. If we hold that the Creator is two — not the scholastic debate, which I do not wish to enter into, a hypothetical one — then that society and that philosophy of two-gods, its being of two gods, the first effect it produces is this: that two groups come into being in society. When we are believers in one God, its meaning is that the people of society are all of one rank, of one group, of one class, brothers and side by side with one another.

I am concerned with the implication of this statement; I am not concerned with the implications of that obsolete, ancient philosophy. I want you to take note of what it means when we believe that God is one. God is one and is not two — that is, the servants of God are on a single scale, they are not on two scales. Among the meanings of "God is one and is not two" is this one — not that this is its entire meaning. That is, the servants of God are not in two classes; they are in one class. Why? Because their God is one, their Creator is one.

Did God have greater love for one group of them when He created them?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:18):** "And the Jews and the Christians said: 'We are the sons of God and His beloved ones.'"[^s12-10]

The Jews said: "We are the children of God and His friends." God the Exalted, in another place, throws one of their crimes in their faces. He both answers them and reproaches them:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:91):** "Then why did you kill the prophets?"[^s12-11]

If you are so very friendly and close and kindred with God, then why did you kill His prophets? Why did you kill His chosen servants? The Jews used to say: "We are the children of God and the intimates of God; we have a special privilege." No indeed — Islam says this is a false claim. In another place He says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Jumuʿah (62:6):** "Say: 'O you who are Jews, if you claim that you are the friends of God to the exclusion of [other] people, then wish for death, if you are truthful.'"[^s12-12]

If you say that you are so very friendly and close with God — if you are speaking the truth, then wish for death! Well, escape from this hollow life, attain to the beloved God who is on the same front as your kinsfolk — since for you that place is your own home — so then, wish for death. And He says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Jumuʿah (62:7):** "But they will never wish for it."

It is impossible for these people to wish for death. A Jew, and to wish for death?!

So the Creator and the Worshipped One and the Director of the affairs of all is God. This is a very important matter — that if the Creator and the Worshipped One become one, then inevitably human beings are on one class and one scale. All were created from a single material root and stem. We dwell upon this word "material," we lay a special emphasis upon it: from the material aspect, from the aspect of the bodily structure, all human beings were created from one root and one stem.

No one, in creation, is endowed with a privilege that would be the source of legal rights and prerogatives. Take note of this too — it is not that one is endowed with no privilege at all; on the contrary, it is possible that some human beings, owing to certain conditions, acquire a particular aptitude; well, that is a privilege. One person may, from the standpoint of the conditions of creation, have come into being in such a way that he is a genius. Someone may have been produced from a father and a mother who is gifted. One is less gifted, one is more powerful, one is weaker and thinner, one is more beautiful, one is uglier — so these differences exist; but these differences are not the source of a difference in rights. It is not the case that the one who is endowed with greater bodily strength is thereby endowed with greater social rights — never! It is not the case that the one who has come into the world in an aristocratic and influential household is thereby endowed with greater opportunities. Never!

If Islam opens a school, it opens it for all children; if it provides education and upbringing, it provides it for all children; if it gives human beings the opportunity for trade and work and effort and striving in life, it gives it to all; if it gives work, it gives it to all. In an Islamic society, under the shadow[^s12-13] of Islamic regulations, with an Islamic government, it is not the case that anyone needs connections in order to study, to work, to understand things, to earn money, to obtain employment, to be employed — even up to the highest positions. In an Islamic society it is like this: a vast field, possessing millions of roads before millions of human beings — let them go, let all make use of it, let all run in this field toward material and spiritual goals and aims; there is no problem whatever, the road lies open before everyone — contrary to the non-monotheistic systems, contrary to the systems of *jāhiliyyah* (ignorance), which pave the road for some and fill the roads for others with thorns and stones and obstacles. As Saʿdi put it: "They tie up the stone and let the dog loose."[^s12-14] In an Islamic society it is not like this.

In an Islamic society everyone can reach the highest positions. Well, you see — Bilal the Abyssinian reached the rank of muezzin (don't look at the muezzin of today, where the muezzin of the Imam Hasan Mosque, Mash Ishaq, feels a sense of inferiority, and at the proper hour often does not come — as if he is not here to hear this — some days he comes, some days he does not come). The rank of muezzin is a very great and splendid rank in Islam, a most honorable rank; not everyone became a muezzin. Bilal, the black Abyssinian, of a low social origin by the standards of the *jāhiliyyah* of that time — he reaches the rank of muezzin; this is the highest, one of the highest of ranks. Salman the Persian, by the standards of that day, was of a lower origin, because the Arabs considered themselves superior and all other human beings inferior to themselves. Well, Salman was a Persian, after all — he was from Isfahan. Even though he is a Persian, is a foreigner, has come from a distant land, has perhaps learned the Arabic language poorly — that is, was a sound Muslim, but perhaps did not speak very well — he reaches the governorship and rule of a vast region. And many, many more of this same kind.

Therefore no one, in creation, is endowed with a privilege that would be the source of legal rights — even though he is endowed with certain privileges. All the privileges are at the disposal of all, and are bound up with one's own continuous efforts and striving and struggle. Everything, all the positions — I said, except for those special positions that have been divinely appointed and prepared for the guidance and leadership of mankind, which I am not now speaking of — the whole world is His; that is, it belongs to God, and human beings are all paupers at His threshold. All must ask from Him, all must take from Him, all must stretch out the hand of need toward Him; and here all are equal.

Even Imam Sajjad — God's blessings be upon him — who is the son of the Prophet and the son of the Commander of the Faithful and the son of Fatimah al-Zahra and the son of Husayn ibn ʿAli, peace be upon them all — he too must lament, must wail, must shed tears; and he does this very thing. A pure man not belonging to this household must, in the face of God, likewise wail. For the work and the affairs of this world, Imam Baqir — God's blessings be upon him — and Imam Sadiq too go and wield the spade. An ordinary man too wields the spade. Let me not cite Imam Sadiq as the example — the Commander of the Faithful, peace and blessings be upon him, too works; works in the era of his power, in the era of his lordship. In the time of the Prophet, one of the greatest commanders of the Prophet's army, in most of the battles and in all the battles, is the Commander of the Faithful. He goes and wields the spade, he goes and tends gardens, he goes and does the irrigation, and so on. You see — if you want to obtain money, you must work; if you want knowledge, you must study; if you want a political position, you must make the necessary effort. And the road of effort lies open before all; everyone, whoever has made the effort, attains.

This is the logic of Islam, and of course this logic can be clearly found throughout the Quran, in various verses, in abundance. Here too, again, I refer you to the Quran and to the text of the Quran. Go, open the Quran, look at the Quran with deliberation, so that we may not be included in this verse:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Muḥammad (47:24):** "Do they not then ponder the Quran, or are there locks upon [their] hearts?"[^s12-15]

Let there be no locks upon our hearts; let us ponder the Quran. Now attend to these verses of the first portion, relating to Sūrat al-Muʾminūn, verses 84 to 91:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:84):** "Say: 'To whom belongs the earth and whoever is in it, if you know?'"

O Prophet, say: the earth and whatever is in it, whose is it? — if you know. Say to the polytheists, to those polytheists who used to divide up and demarcate the spheres of influence of their own gods — say: the earth and whatever is in it, to whom does it belong?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:85):** "They will say: 'To God.'"

They will say: it is God's. Well, the polytheists of Mecca believed in Allah, and they regarded the idols as intercessors who would intercede for them before Allah.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:85, cont.):** "Say: 'Will you not then take heed?'"

Say: then why do you not come to your senses?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:86):** "Say: 'Who is the Lord of the seven heavens and the Lord of the Great Throne?'"

Say: who is the Director of the seven heavens and the Director of the great Throne? What is the meaning of "Throne"? Which are the seven heavens? Some of it we gave a brief explanation of in certain discussions in the past, and some of it we did not explain — now is not the place for it; there are things to be said, but at any rate, when there is some occasion, some opportunity, I shall present them. In summary, it is not very important either —

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:87):** "They will say: 'To God.'"

They will say: those too are God's. The power and dominion of the Lord is spread over the heavens and the earth.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:87, cont.):** "Say: 'Will you not then be God-fearing?'"

Say: then why do you not have fear of this God? Why do you not order your deeds and your thoughts according to His word and His command?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:88):** "Say: 'In whose hand is the dominion of all things, and He grants protection while none can be protected against Him?'"

Say: who is it in whose hand is the absolute dominion and rule and sovereignty of all things? Those who have power at their disposal — the utmost of it is that they may have mastery over the outer surface of some physical thing. You, who are the owner of your own house — you move a brick from one place to another; your mastery is only to this extent. You pick up a piece of iron from here and place it there, you weld two pieces together or separate them from each other; your mastery over a piece of iron is only to this extent. But the One who has mastery over all the atoms and parts of this being, the motion of its atoms is at His disposal and at His command; in plants the growth and development, in animals and human beings all the inner motions of their being and all of their affairs — in short, are in the grip of His power; He is God. "Say: 'In whose hand is the dominion of all things'" — say: in whose hand is the dominion and sovereignty and power of all things — "and He grants protection" — and He grants protection, "while none can be protected against Him" — against Him, no one is granted protection; that is, were you to flee from God and take refuge with someone else against God, "none can be protected against Him" — this is its meaning. Suppose the Christians disobey God, flee into the embrace of Jesus, and Jesus grants them protection and defends them against God — such a thing is not possible. "And He grants protection" — He grants protection to every being, to every human being — "while none can be protected against Him" — against Him, in opposition to Him, no one is granted protection — "if you know."

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:89):** "They will say: 'To God.'"

— if you know. Who is it that is so? "They will say: 'To God.'" They will say: the dominion of all things is in God's hand, and it is God who grants protection, and against Him no one is granted protection.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:89, cont.):** "Say: 'Then how are you bewitched [deluded]?'"

Say: then how are you deceived?

And it is subtle that the Quran lays emphasis on being deceived — being deceived, remaining heedless. Constantly it wants the people not to remain heedless, not to be deceived, to open their eyes. The Quran is confident that if the people open their eyes, its purpose is secured — and that is indeed correct. We too today say this very thing. We too say: if the world today would open its eyes, this Quran of ours would establish a world government, if it would open its eyes; but ignorance and arrogance do not allow it, and on the other hand treacherous hands do not allow eyes to be opened.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:90):** "Rather, We have brought them the truth, and indeed they are liars."

Rather, We have placed the truth at their disposal, and truly they are liars. We have made the matter clear to them, We have placed the truth at their disposal; with this attitude they have adopted, intellectually and practically, they fabricate excuses, they fabricate lying excuses.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:91):** "God has not taken to Himself any son, nor is there any god alongside Him."

God has not taken to Himself any son — "nor is there any god alongside Him" — no worshipped being has existed beside Him and with Him.

Note also that the point of reliance and citation lies more in this part — although the earlier verses too had some bearing on what was intended.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:91, cont.):** "…for then each god would have taken off with what he created."

Were there to be gods, each god would have carried off its own creature and creation in some direction — that is, division, that is class division among human beings, that is the removal of cohesion and unity in creation, in the creation of the world and of the human being. The one who holds that there is a god of light and a god of darkness and a human god and a god of the upper class and the lower class regards this creation as a patchwork of separate scraps. But according to the view of Tawhid, creation is a single, cohesive piece. The human being and the animal and the mountain and the sphere and the earth are connected and joined; and the beings, all of them together, have connection and bond with one another, all have unity with one another. "For then each god would have taken off with what he created" — were there several gods and worshipped beings, each god, each worshipped being, would have carried its own creature and creation in some direction —

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:91, cont.):** "…and some of them would have lorded it over others."

And some of them would have gained supremacy over others.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:91, cont.):** "Glory be to God, far above what they describe."

Pure and exalted and free is God from what they describe.

(This was one portion of today's recitation. The other portion — allow me to read it from this very paper, for if I wish to read from the text of the Quran, I would have to keep searching to find it. Does any of the gentlemen have a Quran in hand?) The next portion is from Sūrat al-Baqarah, verses 21 and 22:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:21):** "O mankind!"

Behold, O people, O human beings — addressed to whom? It is not addressed to the upper and lower classes. It is not addressed to black or white human beings. It is not addressed to one group of humanity; it is addressed to all of humanity. Our emphasis is on this word: "O mankind," behold, O human beings — "worship your Lord," render worship and servitude to your Lord, who is one — "who created you," the One who created you — "and those who were before you," and all those who came before you — "that you may be God-fearing," so that you may take up *taqwā* (God-consciousness). That is, this worship, this servitude, is a cause of *taqwā*. One of the things that brings into being *taqwā* — that fine, attractive state, I have expounded for you what *taqwā* means — that state of immunity from sin, within the soul, within the human being — is the servitude of God. Hence, in a society where all the people are servants of God and the society has the form of servitude to God, in that society *taqwā* is as abundant as you wish. As much *taqwā* as you wish is abundant; there is no famine of *taqwā* as in our age!

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:22):** "[He] who made the earth a couch [a resting-place] for you."

See, everything is for all. The One who appointed for you — and "you" means whom? Human beings, all of them. "The earth a couch" — He spread out the earth, "and the sky a structure [an edifice]," and the heaven a firm-built edifice, "and sent down from the sky water," and from the sky He sent down rain water, "and brought forth thereby fruits as a provision for you" — He brought forth, by means of this rain, fruits as a provision for you; not a provision for a particular class, of whose charity you might eat — no; for all. Now that it is so:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:22, cont.):** "…so do not set up rivals to God while you know."

So do not become believers in two gods. By positing two gods, by supposing a rival and a competitor for God, do not divide human beings into two groups and three groups and ten groups — all are one group. "So do not set up rivals to God while you know" — you who know, do not suppose for God partners and competitors.

Next, Sūrat al-Ḥujurāt, this famous verse that is very current and in circulation on tongues, verse 13:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḥujurāt (49:13):** "O mankind, We created you from a male and a female."

O human beings — again the address is to all — We created you from a male and a female. All of you, from a single man and a single woman — the very thing that, in the *Dīwān* attributed to the Commander of the Faithful, peace and blessings be upon him, is expressed thus, and its content is this same verse:

> "People, with respect to their composition, are equals; their father is Adam and the mother is Eve."[^s12-16]

All are alike and equal with respect to the origin and root of creation. "And We made you into peoples and tribes that you may know one another" — the being-made into branch upon branch and tribe upon tribe is for mutual acquaintance and recognition. (This too, of course, needs a brief explanation, but since the time has passed, I leave it for another time, if it should be necessary.) "Indeed, the most honored of you in the sight of God is the most God-fearing among you" — this is the decisive Islamic ruling on the theme of the negation of social classes: the most honored of you in the sight of God is the one with the most *taqwā*. That is, being of a certain class, being attached to a certain family, being attached to a certain lineage and clan, is not a cause of being more honored.

And here is a yet more interesting and subtle point: those who possess *taqwā*, who are higher than others, still are not endowed with greater privileges of rights. It is not the case that people of *taqwā* receive a little more money, have a few more rights, or enjoy more and better social rights — no, no indeed; it is not so. Rather, "Indeed, the most honored of you in the sight of God" — they are dearer in the sight of God. Of course, *taqwā* is the source of certain social effects, up to a certain limit, but not very much; there are certain occupations and posts whose condition is the possession of *taqwā* — only to this extent. "Indeed, God is All-Knowing, All-Aware" — indeed, God is Knowing and Informed.

Again, another portion: there is a verse relating to Sūrat al-Isrāʾ, verse 70:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Isrāʾ (17:70):** "And We have certainly honored the children of Adam, and carried them on land and sea, and provided them with good things, and favored them above many of those We created with a marked favor."

Certainly We honored the children of Adam — "and carried them on land and sea," We bore them, We carried them, on the dry land and on the sea, We provided the means of their travel — "and provided them" — that this "and carried them on land and sea" may also, incidentally, be an allusion to the existence of relations among human beings; for if human beings could not go from one point to another, if this capacity, this characteristic, did not exist in them, then within a human society it may well be that, on a certain supposition, fragmentation would arise — "and provided them with good things," and We endowed them with good things, "and favored them above many of those We created with a marked favor," and We gave them superiority over many of Our creatures. Whom? Of whom is the talk? Whom did We honor? To whom did We give provision of good things? And whom did We favor above many of Our creatures? — Human beings, not a particular class or clan.

(My presentation has come to its end; and it seems the time too has somewhat passed. Today's recitation of the Quran will be performed by one of our dear reciters. I request that he come forward.)

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, make the Quran our support in this world and the hereafter. By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, O Lord! Do not withhold from us Your good through the teaching of the Quran.

---

[^s12-1]: Sūrat al-Muʾminūn (23:91–92).

[^s12-2]: Refer to the polycopy (handout) at the end of the session.

[^s12-3]: Jawaharlal Nehru (1889–1964) was among the leaders of the struggle for India's independence movement. A graduate in law from the University of Cambridge, upon seeing the suffering of the people of India and the oppression of the British during the course of the First World War, he joined the Indian independence movement and the Congress Party. After India's independence he was elected prime minister. His daughter, Indira Gandhi, was elected prime minister of India some time after her father.

[^s12-4]: "Daughter."

[^s12-5]: *Man's Religions* (*A History of the World's Religions*), written by John Noss, in which he examines the rites and religions of the ancient world up to the present age in four parts: the primitive and past religions, the religions of India, the religions of the Far East, and the religions of the Near East.

[^s12-6]: And such-and-such activities of this kind — *qis ʿalā hādhā* ("measure [the rest] by this").

[^s12-7]: "To the ear of the soul, a wayfarer brought a cry / from the Presence of the One: 'There is no god but God' — / that, O dear one, whoever is allotted abasement, / in truth attains not rank and station by force. / The threadbare carpet of one whose fortune was woven black / cannot be made white by the water of Zamzam and Kawthar." (Hafez)

[^s12-8]: Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:21).

[^s12-9]: A hemistich from the *Dīwān* of poetry attributed to the Commander of the Faithful [Imam ʿAli].

[^s12-10]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:18).

[^s12-11]: Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:91).

[^s12-12]: Sūrat al-Jumuʿah (62:6–7).

[^s12-13]: (ẓ-l-l) shade.

[^s12-14]: *Golestān* of Saʿdi.

[^s12-15]: Sūrat Muḥammad (47:24).

[^s12-16]: A hemistich from the *Dīwān* of poetry attributed to the Commander of the Faithful: "*al-nāsu min jihati al-timthāli akfāʾu / abūhum Ādamu wal-ummu Ḥawwāʾu*."


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 13: The Psychological Effects of Tawhid

*تأثیرات روانی توحید — delivered Tuesday, 9 Mehr 1353 / 14 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:172–173):** "Those who responded to God and the Messenger after the wound had befallen them — for those of them who did good and were mindful of God there is a great reward. Those to whom people said, 'The people have gathered against you, so fear them,' yet it only increased them in faith, and they said, 'God is sufficient for us, and how excellent a Guardian He is.'"[^s13-1][^s13-2]

In the field of the effects that tawhid produces within the fabric of society, and the shape it gives to human community and to a monotheistic community, a number of discussions were undertaken. There are, of course, still other discussions on this subject, but for various reasons we set aside continuing with them. The most important of these reasons is the following. For instance, the effect of tawhid and of the monotheistic outlook upon the financial affairs of a tawhid-based society — which is one of the important planks of the tawhid "resolution" — is a topic that, of course, requires a great deal of discussion. But to derive[^s13-3] this matter from the verses of the Qur'an is a somewhat exacting and delicate task, and it is not a subject suited to public sessions and general gatherings. Although the Qur'an, with the expression *"and give them of the wealth of God which He has given you"*[^s13-4] — that is, give of the wealth of God, the wealth that God has given you and which is God's wealth; meaning give it to the needy and to cases of need and want — does show the monotheistic outlook in the matter of worldly wealth, these points do exist. But to take the totality of the verses dealing with wealth and, in light of the monotheistic outlook, draw inferences and make use of them, is a task that belongs to more scholarly settings. It is delicate, it is exacting, and it is not really suitable for us to write out on these mimeographed pages, hand them to people, and then sit here for an hour and debate it. So we set those discussions aside.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nūr (24:33):** "…and give them of the wealth of God which He has given you…"[^s13-4]

The other reason was this: we have more pressing discussions to undertake regarding the doctrinal and ideological foundations of Islam. We still have material to cover, and it is necessary that — if life and opportunity permit — we bring these discussions to a conclusion by the end of the month of Ramadan, or at least bring them to some point of completion. And it is obvious that if we wished to proceed in this manner and discuss every chapter and every issue, we would not reach the destination that is intended. So that is the second reason. All in all, these were the reasons that we set aside continuing with the discussion of the social effects of tawhid.

Today, although our discussion is about tawhid, it is a discussion that bears a relationship to our earliest topics — the ones we discussed concerning faith, the effects of faith, and the glad tidings given to the believers. Faith, as we said back then, means precisely that state of action-producing, commitment-creating belief. Action-producing belief — and of course we said that it must arise out of awareness, that it must not be blind, bare and stripped, and divorced from righteous action. These were the discussions we conducted earlier. Belief in tawhid is a kind of faith. It is a conscious faith for the aware monotheist; it is an action-producing, commitment-engendering faith. The commitment that tawhid places upon the shoulders of the monotheist is the greatest of commitments, the heaviest of commitments, the most consequential of commitments among all Islamic and religious beliefs. The commitment of tawhid, on the part of a monotheist, is in truth summed up in the commitment to build a monotheistic world. The commitment of tawhid, from the standpoint of the monotheist and for the monotheist, consists in the commitment to wipe away all the traces of polytheism. These are the commitments of tawhid.

Let me here point out something to the friends and brothers who have a little familiarity with the Arabic language. The word *tawhid* belongs to the *tafʿīl* form (the causative/intensive verbal pattern). Ask any seminary student what tawhid means, what its literal meaning is, and he will say: "to make one." Tawhid comes from *waḥdat* (oneness), from the root of oneness, from the stem of unity — that is, to be one. Tawhid, in its *tafʿīl* form — as we seminary students put it — means "to make one." Tawhid means to make one, to render unified, to make into a single thing. What does that mean? It means turning a multiplicity of gods into one single God; turning a non-monotheistic society into a monotheistic one; making the polytheist's mind and the polytheist's heart into a monotheist's mind and a monotheist's heart. Tawhid — making-one — is entirely commitment. There is doing in it, there is work in it; the very word *tawhid* demands and entails this. And this is a point such that attention to it causes a special clear-sightedness regarding tawhid to come into being within a person.

Well then: belief in tawhid is a faith of this magnitude and grandeur, and a commitment of this weight, this decisiveness, and this determinativeness — such that, whichever of the other Islamic beliefs, or religious beliefs, or any of the non-Islamic social beliefs you may consider, its commitment is not of this weight and this grandeur. Removing poverty from society, for instance, is a kind of commitment; or moderating wealth in any society is a kind of commitment that a given school of thought may have undertaken. Abolishing war is a commitment that a given school may have placed upon its own shoulders, and a people, by inclining toward that school, by adhering to that school, may have transferred that commitment to themselves. All this is correct. But tawhid in its proper meaning, in its Islamic meaning — not in its superstitious, lazy-minded sense, but in its true and correct sense — if we conceive of tawhid as making God one, bringing into being divine governance, a divine society, divine law, a divine order, it encompasses all of those commitments that I mentioned, which a given school and a given school may possess, and beyond them other commitments as well. See how heavy the commitment of tawhid is.

Well then: so this is a faith; it is conscious; it is accompanied by commitment; its commitment is heavier than the commitment of all foundations of belief, religious and non-religious, and it is more decisive, more comprehensive, and more all-embracing. This is tawhid. If tawhid carries this meaning and is a sound, real faith, then it is fitting that we examine and see what the effect of this faith, the effect of this action-producing belief, will be upon the psyche of the believer, and what it ought to be. This is an independent question. Let us see: one who has come to believe in tawhid — the one who has come to believe that God is one in every corner and nook and in the various domains of human life, just as God is one in all the corners of the world of creation — when a person attains this belief, what sorts of effects come into being in his spirit.

The benefit of this discussion is twofold. One is that we will become a little more acquainted with tawhid. We will understand what kind of spiritual and psychological instruction tawhid means. If at some point someone draws a numbing, sedative[^s13-5] inference from tawhid, we can say: Sir, this is wrong; the effect of tawhid is not what you say; what I say — that is what it is. So we come to know tawhid: this is the first benefit. The second benefit is that we come to know ourselves — whether we are monotheists or not monotheists. It is a touchstone for recognizing the substance of tawhid within one's own heart. How am I to know that I am a monotheist? How am I to know that this faith has, within my spirit, in the depths of my being, taken effect and penetrated? Once I have learned what the psychological effect of tawhid is like — once I have understood what definite effects this medicine produces in one's being — then I will be able to understand: when I look and see whether these effects are present or absent, I will know whether I took the medicine correctly or took the wrong one; whether they gave me the genuine one or the counterfeit. If I see that those intended effects do not exist in me, I will understand that the bottle of medicine they had given us was a bottle of counterfeit medicine, that it was not the genuine one — for if it had been genuine, it would have produced other effects. As for me, if I wished to speak about the psychological effects of tawhid as well, the discussion would drag on and grow long. So I want to finish this today too, so that tomorrow, God willing, we may turn to the discussion of prophethood.

The psychological effect of tawhid can be summed up in a few sentences. Among the effects that the monotheist's spirit derives from and through tawhid, one is this: he comes to possess a breadth of horizon in his vision. The monotheist is free and at ease from narrow-mindedness, from shortsightedness, from near-sightedness. The monotheist does not say, "I was defeated in this arena," or "our front retreated in this matter and the affair ended to our loss." He is not so shortsighted. He knows that monotheistic thought has a domain as long as the lifespan of mankind. Measured against the lifespan of humanity, ten years, twenty years, fifty years, a hundred years are no more than a single instant, a single minute. To put it another way, and from another vantage point: the monotheist's horizon of vision is not confined to, does not come to a halt at, material concerns and base, paltry needs. When the monotheist looks before himself, alongside his material needs he sees dozens of needs, hundreds of needs, among the most immense and most precious of human needs. His entire mind and thought and attention are not restricted and arrested at base, paltry, petty needs — in the way that human beings who are inwardly material, even if outwardly divine, outwardly spiritual, have imprisoned and incarcerated themselves within them. When the monotheist looks, he sees the future before himself as infinitely vast. As I mentioned on one of the past days, the monotheist does not regard the world as having an end — because he sees the end of this world as connected to the Hereafter. He regards the Hereafter and this world as the two ends of a single scroll. He does not regard death as the wall of life; he does not suppose it to be the end of this road; rather he regards it as an opening and a passage[^s13-6] and a thoroughfare toward a vaster world. These are the properties of tawhid.

A non-monotheist, however self-sacrificing, however captivated by noble and humane ideals, finds that everything for him comes to an end at the moment of death. Whereas for the monotheist, death is the beginning of a vaster life and a more interesting, more pleasing environment. A materialist, even if he were very self-sacrificing, is prepared to cast himself into that place, into that zone, which in his view is nothingness and non-existence. But a monotheist, even if he is very self-sacrificing — like a moth, like a candle — nothing is expected of him except self-immolation, except not giving a thought to his own personal interests. And even if he were not so self-sacrificing, even if he did not rise to it, even if he did not want to sacrifice himself so much, still, for him, falling into that zone which the materialist saw as non-existence is easier — for the reason that he does not see that place as non-existence. He knows and recognizes that place too as another environment, another station, another zone of this vast zone of human life.

Among the effects of tawhid upon the spirit of a monotheist is this: it dries up the root of fear within him — and this is very important. In the Qur'an, in several places — and in today's recitations one or two instances will reach the hearing of the gentlemen and will be reflected upon — the believers are addressed with: *"…so do not fear them, but fear Me…"*[^s13-7] — dry up the root of fearing others within your heart, and fear Me. And whoever fears God fears no one else. Whoever was a monotheist, whoever had belief in the power of the Lord, fear vanishes within him. And I, when I look, when I reflect, when I calculate, I see that it is fear — dread and terror — that strips this world and the Hereafter from those who possess them. Fear of poverty causes a person not to give in charity. Fear of being afflicted and beset by hardships causes a person to submit to crimes and catastrophes and humiliations and degradations. Fear of not being able to live in this world for even two more days — and what a life! what a kind of life! — fear of losing this very base life, which is not even certain to last two days or three days (no official deed was issued guaranteeing that you will be alive until such-and-such a year, until such-and-such a date), fear of losing such a worthless life causes a person to put an end to other lives, to embitter or destroy social life. Greed traces back to fear, and fears are the root of the miseries in human lives. Look across the whole of history and you will see what things caused the supporters of truth to find themselves in the minority — there, where they found themselves in the minority. What things caused a people who had recognized the truth not to follow the truth — there, where truth-recognizing people did not follow the truth. What things caused a people to stain themselves with great crimes — there, where you see a people staining themselves with crimes. Most often, when a person pursues, investigates, and studies, he finds that the source of all of this is fear. Fear!

After the course of the Islamic society — as all of you know — fell into deviation and decline, what was it that caused the Muslims to fail to keep for themselves true Islam, the gift and present that God had given and delivered to them free of charge (the second generation had received it free of charge; the sacrifices had been made for the first generation) — this gift that had been placed at their disposal free of charge, what was it that caused them to be unable to preserve it for themselves? What but fear? Who did not know Muʿāwiya among the people who were around the Commander of the Faithful and within range of that great man's teachings and instructions and discourses — who? In the Hijaz, who did not know Muʿāwiya ibn Abī Sufyān? Who did not know Yazīd ibn Muʿāwiya? Who did not know ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwān? The dynasty of the Umayyads — who were the ones who did not know them, did not recognize them, had not tested them, had no historical experience of them, had not understood — had not felt — the word of the Qur'an and the word of the Prophet and the condemnatory historical reality concerning them? Everyone knew. What but fear? The reason they went and submitted, the reason they collaborated, the reason they became hirelings, the reason that in their station of hireling-service they also engaged in flattery and danced attendance — this was nothing but fear. And this fear, from the lowest classes of the people up to the elite classes, up to the famous figures, up to those in whom the people had placed their hopes — it had taken hold of them all.

ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar[^s13-8] was a symbol of incapacity, of weakness of soul, and of refusal to accept the truth. His refusal to accept truth was paired with incapacity — as I have said, otherwise ʿAbdullāh ibn al-Zubayr too was a refuser of truth. When a person looks, he finds, on the whole, the figure of ʿAbdullāh ibn al-Zubayr — that executioner-henchman opposed to ʿAlī — more acceptable than the figure of a man like ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar. ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar is a man who, after ʿUthmān was killed, did not pledge allegiance to the Commander of the Faithful. Why did he not pledge allegiance? He said: "The matter is not clear to me." He exercised caution; he did not pledge allegiance to ʿAlī, peace be upon him. Out of utmost caution for the sake of religion, he did not pledge allegiance to the Commander of the Faithful. For he saw that — by his own reckoning and in his own imagination — not all the Muslims were in consensus on this question. Well, if you had no personal interest, if you were not gripped by ulterior motives and diseases of the heart, this sentence — *"Do not feel desolate on the path of guidance because of the fewness of those upon it"*[^s13-9] — which you had heard from the Commander of the Faithful — but even the import of this sentence — had you not, from the Prophet, from the Noble Qur'an, eaten its flesh (heard it well)? Had you not known that on the path of God, on the right path, on the path of guidance, whether the travelers be few or many, a person ought not to change his path and alter his resolve, but must see whether the path of guidance exists or not? Because this important Islamic principle effective in life — not paying attention to what others say, not heeding how the ignorance-enslaved or motive-driven majorities act — because this principle was not at his disposal, was not in his hand, was not operative, he withdrew from the Commander of the Faithful and did not pledge allegiance, out of caution.

Years after this episode, when the Commander of the Faithful — God's blessings be upon him — had been martyred, when Muʿāwiya had ruled for years, this same ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar had come to know Muʿāwiya thoroughly and from close up; and even if it had not been clear to him that day, it had become clear to him afterward. Then too the era of Yazīd — those three disgraceful years in the history of Islam — had passed; Marwān ibn al-Ḥakam was Yazīd's successor and the father of ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwān, and he too spent his brief period of rule; then ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwān came to power. And throughout all this period ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar had been in Medina, in the heart of affairs and events, in the very midst of historical incidents and occurrences. He had not been uninformed; he had not been unaware of what had passed and had come upon the Islamic society; individuals had not remained unknown to him; he had come to know the Umayyads well. Even so, Ḥajjāj ibn Yūsuf al-Thaqafī, the notorious executioner-henchman of the Umayyads, came on behalf of ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwān to conquer Mecca, which was in the possession of ʿAbdullāh ibn al-Zubayr. ʿAbdullāh ibn al-Zubayr was resisting there and fighting fiercely against the forces of ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwān that had come from Syria. They saw that they must bring this disturbance to an end and finish it. Ḥajjāj ibn Yūsuf was the most ruthless executioner of them all; they sent him. And he came, set up catapults atop the mountains surrounding Mecca, and rained stones upon the House of God; he killed a great number of the people; then they killed ʿAbdullāh ibn al-Zubayr too, and crucified the corpse of his slain body.

Ḥajjāj ibn Yūsuf was sitting in his tent, occupied with writing a letter and reporting the conquest to Syria, the center of the caliphate. ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar too was among the people of Mecca. The people began coming group after group and pledging allegiance to Ḥajjāj ibn Yūsuf. It was not that these people did not know Ḥajjāj — they knew him. It was not that they were well aware of his Islam, his piety, his godliness, and his competence — everyone knew what a vile, base creature Ḥajjāj was, and on behalf of what even baser and viler creature he was an agent here. They knew these things too. But nevertheless, because he was the conqueror, because the power was in his hands, because if the people did not pledge allegiance to him he would set the sword loose upon them — out of fear, group after group they came and pledged allegiance to Ḥajjāj ibn Yūsuf. ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar, in the midst of thousands of other people, came near the tents of Ḥajjāj, which had been pitched outside the city. He said: "Tell him ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar has come" — imagining that they would honor him, would receive him with open arms, would warmly welcome him.[^s13-10] He did not know that power, by its very nature, is heedlessness and shamelessness. He imagined that because he had once helped Muʿāwiya and had given no help to the household of the Prophet, they would now appreciate his worth. He did not know that these people eat the salt and then break the saltcellar. He came; he said, "I am ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar." They told Ḥajjāj, "It is ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar." He said, "Tell him to come in." He came in. Ḥajjāj did not stir for him, did not pay him respect, did not even lift his head up from the paper to look at him and offer him a welcome. He said, "Commander, give me your hand so that I may pledge allegiance to you." Who is saying this? ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar. To whom? To Ḥajjāj. The same ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar who did not say to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, "Give me your hand so that I may pledge allegiance," who exercised caution there — caution in religion — says to Ḥajjāj, "Commander, give me your hand so that I may pledge allegiance to you." What did Ḥajjāj say in reply? Ḥajjāj said, "My hand is occupied with writing the letter; pledge allegiance to my foot." He stretched out his foot and said, "Pledge allegiance to my foot." And the man pledged allegiance to the foot of Ḥajjāj ibn Yūsuf. A base and abject person who, for the sake of two more days of living — and a living so wretched at that, a living in which there is neither the sweetness of this world nor the sweetness of the Hereafter, a living that holds within it nothing but the making-forbidden of a few kilos of food and nothing more, a living that does not bring a person closer to God; and brings him closer to Satan — for the sake of two more days of living of this sort, a man should come and pledge allegiance to Ḥajjāj, and to the foot of Ḥajjāj? What is its source? Its source is nothing but fear.

Therefore, in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt — peace and blessings be upon them — and in these supplications that have come down to us, especially in the month of Ramadan (and I have a most emphatic recommendation to everyone: the reciting of supplications and attention to the supplications transmitted from the Imams) — in these supplications these matters, psychological matters, what pertains to the human spirit, have been given much attention, care, and special emphasis. In one of the supplications of Imam al-Sajjād — God's blessings be upon him — see how Imam al-Sajjād's teachings are — he says: *"O God, grant me long life"* (*Allāhumma ʿammirnī*).[^s13-11] This is one sentence of supplication that I am explaining for you — first, so that those who recite supplications and those who love supplications may understand why supplication has been so emphasized, so that they may not take supplication lightly, may not recite it thoughtlessly and ignorantly, but recite it with a little attention; and second, so that those who are against supplications may know what things they are setting themselves against. Of course, we do not have a "supplication" in the sense of an anti-supplication; let those who categorically reject supplication know and understand what things lie within the teachings hidden in supplications.

*"O God, grant me life — long life — so long as my life is expended in obedience to You"* (*Allāhumma ʿammirnī mā kāna ʿumrī bidhlatan fī ṭāʿatik*): O God, grant me life, grant me living — but I do not want it unconditionally. I do not want a long life and a hundred years and a hundred-and-twenty years in vain. *"…so long as my life is freely poured out in obedience to You"* — so long as my life is poured out without stint upon the path of Your obedience and on the path of servitude to You, grant me life. The long life that makes my servitude to You longer — that is what I want. The long life that is expended in work, expended in action — that is what I want. I do not want a long life that is purposeless and pointless and idle. *"O God, grant me life so long as my life is expended in obedience to You; and when…"* — *fa-idhā kāna ʿumrī martaʿan li-l-shayṭān fa-qbiḍnī ilayk* — "O God, whenever You see that my life has become a pasture-ground for Satan…" Satan — pay close attention; bring well into your mind the meaning of Satan, the meaning of Satan that we gave and that the speaking Qur'an gives: the forces that engender evil, anyone who drags the human being and humanity toward the way of error and the way of sin and the way of corruption — Satan means that — "…whenever You see that my life is the pasture-ground of satans, You see that it is a means by which the satans make use of me, You see that my life is the substance and the ground by which the fronts opposed to God profit from it, make use of me as propaganda, make use of my ignorance, make use of me as a weapon and an instrument, make use of my deception, make use of my pride, make use of my arrogance; whenever You see that I have become a means for the success of the wicked and for the failure of the good and virtuous; whenever You see that I have, in practice — even without my own knowing it — become a slave of Satan, become a pasture-ground of Satan — *fa-qbiḍnī ilayk* — then take me unto Yourself. I do not want this life." This is the supplication.

I implore you by God: a person who says these words with his spirit, who says these words truthfully, who says these words out of attention to their meaning — what state does he come to find in his life? This is one supplication. This is how the Imams of guidance — peace and blessings be upon them — instruct us: those lives that are a means by which the enemies of God attain their desires — death is far better than those lives.

Well, then why fear death, you wretch! ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar! Why? Well, let yourself not stay alive. What would Ḥajjāj have done to you? As for your life — about which you did not even know whether you would be alive ten minutes after that shameful pledge of allegiance, or whether you would be alive ten years, and if alive, what kind of alive — let Ḥajjāj put an end to that life; let him put an end to it. Did you not see with what wretchedness ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās departed from this world?
