The ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās who left ʿAlī alone in one place, who left al-Ḥasan alone in one place, who left al-Ḥusayn alone in one place — from his fate, O ʿAbdullāh — ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar — you should have taken a lesson.

ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās did not live very long. His heart was full of blood. He had seated his son — ʿAlī ibn ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās — beside himself and was pouring out his sorrows to him. They had slaughtered a cow, or apparently a calf, whose liver was diseased — what condition it was in, all torn to pieces — and he showed it to his son and said: "Look, your father's liver is more torn to pieces than the liver of this sheep or cow or calf that we slaughtered. My father — my own self — has been worn down in this life." And what had happened, after all? That very city of Mecca, which had been ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās's refuge from the wrath of the Commander of the Faithful and his place of comfort in the face of the responsibility that the Commander of the Faithful would assign to him — that same city of Mecca had become the hell of ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās. I say that ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās does not have a sound tawhid; ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar does not have a sound tawhid. Had they been monotheists —

> A monotheist — whether Indian sword be set upon his head,
> or showers of gold be poured before his feet —
> has neither hope nor dread of anyone:
> this and this alone is the meaning of tawhid.[^s13-12]

He speaks truly — he speaks truly. The most important psychological effect of tawhid upon the spirit of a human being is this: that on the path of God, on the path of duty, on the path of what he discerns to be the purpose of his own existence, he does not dread the enemies of this path. I do not say that he should have no nervous weakness, that his heart should never sometimes feel a tremor of anxiety — no; he should have no active fear, no active dread. The fear and dread that would hold him back from treading the path of God — that should not exist within his being. These fears, these dreads, these terrors that obstruct virtues, that are a very good ground for the growth of unmanliness, the growth of crimes and catastrophes — these fears and dreads he should cast aside.

Now, in any case, this passage that we have selected today from the Noble Qur'an to recite for you consists of two parts: one is from Sūrat Āl ʿImrān — as we said, we will first recite that — and then there are a few verses from Sūrat al-Raʿd. Those of you who have a Qur'an with you, open it and find this portion that I mentioned. And the gentlemen who do not have a Qur'an, pay attention and concentrate.

(I am afraid I will forget this matter. Gentlemen, tonight — on the occasion that it is the night of the middle of the month of Ramadan and the birth of Imam al-Mujtabā, God's blessings be upon him — we are holding a ceremony in this mosque, in this very half-built mosque; that is, we have in fact arranged a celebration, an ordinary celebration, you understand. Last year too there was, of course, a celebration gathering here, and this year there is one as well. We have tried to make it from seven until nine — of course seven is the start of the session, but it may run from nine to nine-thirty, for instance; as for the end of it, we have nothing definite. In any case, we have arranged a youthful program, a fresh and delicate new program, to be carried out and performed here. A passage of the Qur'an will be recited, a poem will be recited, a short address will be given — in brief, it may be a very friendly gathering. Whichever of you gentlemen has the time may come. I forgot to mention it yesterday and today too I was afraid it would slip my mind, so I have mentioned it now.)

Which verse did we say? 172. Yes! Verse 172. Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, verse 172:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:172):** "Those who responded to God and the Messenger after the wound had befallen them — for those of them who did good and were mindful of God there is a great reward."

Of course, the preamble to the verse is lengthy; even this portion that I have selected — it is two or three verses of it that I have in view. So that you may have hold of the thread of the discussion, I will read two or three verses before it. *"Those who responded to God and the Messenger"* — those who gave answer to God and the Prophet. What does "gave answer" mean? It means they accepted the call of the Prophet and the divine call; they accepted it actively. Not just to say in their hearts, "Well, sir, we accept what you say" — no; they set out after the Prophet of God. And when? In the most severe and most difficult of circumstances they accepted the call of God and the Prophet. And when was that? It was at the time when they had been wounded on the battlefield. *"After the wound had befallen them"* — *min baʿdi mā aṣābahum al-qarḥ* — after the *qarḥ*, that is, the wound, had reached them. *"For those of them who did good and were mindful of God there is a great reward"* — for those who did good and acted with virtue and adopted godfearing piety, those who gave answer to the call of God, a great reward, an immense recompense, is in store for them.

This verse relates to the Battle of Uḥud. You know that at the Battle of Uḥud a group had fled, and the Prophet of God was calling out to them; some were afraid and did not come, and some, even though they were wounded, came. The Commander of the Faithful on that day sustained seventy-one wounds — seventy-one, perhaps fewer, perhaps more; I do not exactly recall right now — he sustained many wounds, and a number of the other companions of the Prophet likewise, a little fewer. Some responded and accepted the call of God and the Messenger, and some did not accept and fled. This verse declares the recompense and reward of that group who did not flee but accepted and responded.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:173):** "Those to whom people said…"

*Alladhīna qāla lahum al-nāsu* — pay attention to the general aspects of these verses; let us not imprison ourselves in the Battle of Uḥud, let us not confine and restrict and limit the Qur'anic ideas to the events of early Islam and to what occasioned the revelation of these verses. What matters to us is the idea of the verse. What does the verse mean to say?

And this is a point in the Qur'an that I have perhaps noted on suitable occasions. Sometimes, in the course of a story, by the utterance of a single sentence connected to that story, a general Islamic principle is stated. Pay attention to this. For example, in the story of the Prophet Noah — at the point where Noah the Prophet has boarded the ark, and has also brought his family and household aboard, and has brought aboard the believers and adherents and those on his front as well, while the rest of the creatures were placed under the threat of the divine fury of the Flood, and a flood is coming, the water is welling up, the death-dealing waves are drowning the people on all sides — there is a son of Noah who is not among the believers in him, who has not come to board. This is a story, an ordinary story being told, but full of subtleties and allusions.

Noah the Prophet — an old man, white-bearded, very advanced in years — and this, his young son, his beloved son, he says to him: "Come, board; come, board; you will drown." He says: "No indeed, I will not board, nor will I drown; I will go up the mountain, and I have no need of you either." In the midst of these very exchanges and conversations, a wave comes between them as a barrier, and the father no longer sees the son, and the son drowns. The matter comes to its end. Noah's heart, from its human side, is somewhat troubled — well, his son perished in this event. He lays his trouble before the Lord of the worlds, saying: "My Lord, You had promised that You would save my family, that You would save my household; well, my son too, who was of my household — would that he had been saved." Then, from the Lord of the worlds, an address comes:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Hūd (11:46):** "Indeed he is not of your family; indeed it is an unrighteous deed."[^s13-13]

*"Innahu laysa min ahlika"* — he is not of your family; *"innahu ʿamalun ghayru ṣāliḥ"* — he is an unrighteous deed. *"…an unrighteous deed"* — that act is unrighteous. We do not have to dwell on the whole story — though of course I am now leaning on a single word of it, otherwise there are abundant points to lean upon in these stories and in the story of Noah — *"innahu laysa min ahlika"*: it presents to us and teaches us a general Islamic principle. It says: two brothers who do not think alike are strangers to one another, and two strangers who think alike are brothers to one another. If father and son did not think alike — this one a believer in God, that one a denier of God; this one on the front of God, that one on the front of Satan — then father and son have no kinship or relation with one another; they are not of one family. Kinship by lineage and by marriage, kinship related to blood, is, from the standpoint of Islam, of the second degree; kinship of thought is of the first degree. See — this is an Islamic principle.

Again, when a person looks into the narrations as well, he finds abundant indications and many pieces of evidence for this principle. I have seen a narration from the Imam, peace be upon him, who says: *"The believer is the brother of the believer through his father and his mother"* (*al-muʾminu akhū al-muʾmini li-abīhi wa-ummih*)[^s13-14] — the believer is a brother through father and mother; that is, you may be as distant from your full brother — your brother through father and mother — as the East is from the West, but with a believer on the other side of the world you may be a brother through father and mother; with one who thinks like you, with one on your front, with one whose faith matches your faith. This is an Islamic principle. In these Qur'anic accounts, pay attention to these points; see what it means to say, what principle, what idea, what historical reality — which may perhaps be repeated in a hundred other places — it is stating. Now pay attention to this verse.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:173):** "Those to whom people said…"

*"Alladhīna qāla lahum al-nāsu"*[^s13-15] — these recipients of our reward and recompense are the ones to whom they said, "the people"; a group said to them. What did they say? They said: *"Inna al-nāsa qad jamaʿū lakum fa-khshawhum"* — they said to them, "The creatures have gathered against you, they have plotted, they have joined hands, they want to wipe you out; so fear them." This is what the well-wishers and the prudence-minded said to this group of believers. The believer whose reward and recompense we mentioned in the previous verse is the kind of believer who, when the well-wishers and prudence-minded said such a thing to them, in answer — what did they say? *"Fa-zādahum īmānan"* — first, their faith was increased by this matter. That the enemy's plot should increase the believer's faith is most remarkable. First, their faith was increased; then what did they say? *"Wa-qālū ḥasbunā Allāhu wa-niʿma al-wakīl"* — they said: "God is enough for us, and how excellent a Guardian is God." A good one is He to whom a person can entrust his affair.

*"Ḥasbunā Allāhu wa-niʿma al-wakīl"* — God is enough for us. What does "God is enough for us" mean? "God is enough for us" has several meanings, all of them correct. God is enough for us, and He aids us with His own help. God is enough for us — He has set, and sets, the forces of nature in the direction of our course, which is the direction of the truth. God is enough for us — meaning, even if we do not attain the sweetness of this world, may God be pleased with us; God is enough for us. And each of these meanings, and the other meanings that you may take from *"ḥasbunā Allāhu wa-niʿma al-wakīl,"* is correct. *"Fa-zādahum īmānan"* — when it was said to them, *"fa-khshawhum,"* "fear the people, fear the enemies," their faith was increased, *"wa-qālū"* — and they said: *"ḥasbunā Allāhu wa-niʿma al-wakīl"* — God is enough for us, and He is the best guardian and support.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:174):** "So they returned with a blessing from God and bounty; no evil touched them, and they followed the good pleasure of God; and God is the possessor of great bounty."

*"Fa-nqalabū bi-niʿmatin mina Allāhi wa-faḍlin lam yamsashum sūʾ"* — *"lam yamsashum sūʾ,"* no evil touched them; they returned with a blessing from God and bounty and increase. First, these very people — their abode became the blessing of God, and no evil, distress, or harm touched them, and they became happy and glad. How did they become happy and glad? The Qur'an does not say what became of them; it makes no difference; however it may have come about, whether they were martyred on that battlefield or returned safe and free to their homes, made their way back to the city of Medina — it is all one (*ʿalā al-sawāʾ*).[^s13-16] If they returned to their home and life, they came as victors and triumphant ones, and no distress touched them — because the wound of the battlefield, in the warm environment of family and with contentment from triumph and victory, heals quickly. *"Fa-nqalabū bi-niʿmatin mina Allāhi wa-faḍl"* — and if they fell upon the dust of the battlefield and were martyred, then here, all the more — *"fa-nqalabū bi-niʿmatin mina Allāhi wa-faḍl"* — there they returned toward the blessing of God: blessing without decline, bounty without end, a bounty in which there is no evil whatsoever, an ease in which there is not the least trace of distress or harm. *"Lam yamsashum sūʾ"* — no evil touched them.

*"Innamā dhālikum al-shayṭānu yukhawwifu awliyāʾah"* — remember this verse, keep it in mind, recite it often and learn it. *"Innamā dhālikum al-shayṭānu yukhawwifu awliyāʾah"* — it is nothing but that these speakers are Satan. It is Satan who frightens his own friends. He says: "Sir, they have laid a plot for you, they have hatched a conspiracy against you, the enemies have gathered an army, the hypocrites have joined hands with the Quraysh unbelievers, the hypocrite has fastened a sword beneath his cloak, he is about to strike you, he is about to kill you, he is about to do such-and-such." The one who frightens you — that is Satan.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:175):** "It is only Satan who frightens his friends; so do not fear them, but fear Me, if you are believers."

*"Innamā dhālikum al-shayṭān"* — the one who frightens you of the enemy of God is Satan, and Satan frightens. But — you? Do you take fright at Satan's word? Are you made afraid? It depends on who you are. He succeeds in frightening his own friends. If you were his friend, you would be afraid; if you were not his friend, you would not be afraid. See how short the verse is and at the same time how pithy and full of meaning. *"Innamā dhālikum al-shayṭān"* — indeed this is Satan; *"yukhawwifu awliyāʾah"* — who frightens his friends — the time is short; I want to translate quickly — *"fa-lā takhāfūhum"* — so do not fear them; *"wa-khāfūn"* — but fear Me, observe My command, fear My chastisement and My wrath; *"in kuntum muʾminīn"* — if you are believers.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:176):** "And let not those who race toward unbelief grieve you; they will not harm God in the least. God wants to assign them no portion in the Hereafter, and for them is a great chastisement."

*"Wa-lā yaḥzunka alladhīna yusāriʿūna fī al-kufri"* — let them not grieve you and sadden you, O Prophet — those who hasten and rush ahead in the valley of unbelief — *"innahum lan yaḍurrū Allāha shayʾā"* — they will not harm God in the least. *"Yurīdu Allāhu allā yajʿala lahum ḥaẓẓan fī al-ākhirati wa-lahum ʿadhābun ʿaẓīm"* — for them is a great chastisement; God wills that they have no share or portion on the Day of Resurrection. Of course, I think the subsequent verses are no longer the ones we have in view; it was just these two or three verses, in which the question of the fear of God and of Satan was contained.

Peace be upon you, and the mercy of God and His blessings.

---

- The Philosophy of Prophethood
- The Bi'thah in Prophethood
- The Social Resurrection of Prophethood
- The Aims of Prophethood
- The First Melodies of the Call
- The Opposing Groups
- The Outcome of Prophethood (1)
- The Outcome of Prophethood (2)
- The Commitment of Faith in Prophethood

[^s13-1]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (The Family of ʿImrān), verses 172–173.

[^s13-2]: This is the opening epigraph of the session, comprising Āl ʿImrān 3:172–173.

[^s13-3]: (Translator's/editor's note) *istintāj*: drawing a conclusion, inference.

[^s13-4]: Sūrat al-Nūr (Light), verse 33.

[^s13-5]: A weakening, enervating [inference]; i.e., a numbing, sedative one.

[^s13-6]: (Root: *m-r-r*) a place of passing, a thoroughfare (*mamarr*).

[^s13-7]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, verse 175.

[^s13-8]: ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar, son of the second caliph, who because of his young age was not present at the battles (*ghazawāt*) of the Prophet. He affected asceticism more than he practiced it. ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar was among the few who did not pledge allegiance to the Commander of the Faithful. When Ḥajjāj came to Mecca on behalf of ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwān to overthrow the house of al-Zubayr and seized Mecca, he pledged allegiance to him in a humiliating fashion. Some time later, in the year 73 AH, he was put to death by the order of Ḥajjāj.

[^s13-9]: Hadith no. 16. (From the Commander of the Faithful: *"Lā tastawḥishū fī ṭarīqi al-hudā li-qillati ahlih"* — "Do not feel desolate on the path of guidance because of the fewness of those upon it.")

[^s13-10]: To welcome, to receive.

[^s13-11]: Supplication of Makārim al-Akhlāq (the Noble Traits of Character), hadith no. 31. (From the Ṣaḥīfa al-Sajjādiyya of Imam ʿAlī ibn al-Ḥusayn, Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn.)

[^s13-12]: Saʿdī.

[^s13-13]: Sūrat Hūd, verse 46.

[^s13-14]: Hadith no. 34. (*"Al-muʾminu akhū al-muʾmini li-abīhi wa-ummih"* — "The believer is the brother of the believer through his father and his mother.")

[^s13-15]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (The Family of ʿImrān), verses 173 to 176.

[^s13-16]: Equal; the same; on a par with one another.

# Part Three: Prophethood

### نبوت


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 14: The Philosophy of Prophethood

*فلسفۀ نبوت — delivered Wednesday, 10 Mehr 1353 / 15 Ramaḍān al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Jumuʿah (62:2):** "He it is who raised up among the unlettered a Messenger from among themselves, who recites to them His verses, purifies them, and teaches them the Book and wisdom — though before that they had been in manifest error — and [He raised him] also for others among them who have not yet joined them. He is the Mighty, the Wise."[^jumuah-blessed]

The discussion that we intend to pursue today from the Qur'an, God willing, is a discussion about prophethood. As you can see in these notes that are in your hands, and as you will see — or have already seen — the text, we have set before ourselves several topics in the field of prophethood, so that, relying upon and seeking help from the blessed verses of the Qur'an, we may, God willing, come to understand these several discussions. The brothers of course know that prophethood is one of the principles of all religions. If it can be called a principle among the principles of religion — indeed, one must say something higher than that. When I said "if it can be called a principle among the principles of religion," I do not mean by this that anyone would deny its being a principle. No; rather, it is something higher than a principle: religion has no meaning at all without belief in prophethood. Religion means that program, that path, that school, that creed which has come by way of a messenger from God the Exalted. So the coming of a messenger, and a coming from the side of God — this is among the essential elements of religion. The very subsistence of religion rests upon this. Therefore, in the field of prophethood, it is fitting that we discuss and speak about it as one of the important and fundamental matters of religion.

Of course, in the field of prophethood there is a series of discussions that are common and customary. Pick up any book that has been written about prophethood and look at it: you will see these discussions in it. Of all those discussions that are usually raised in the books about prophethood, we have taken up just one — only one of them — and that too with a particular taste, in this series of our discussions about prophethood. And that is precisely our discussion today, which is the subject of today's recitation. Otherwise, the rest of the discussions that are conducted in the field of prophethood, about prophethood, around prophethood, in the various books of theology (kalām), are in our view matters which — although in their own place they are correct points, indeed necessary points — being correct does not necessarily mean that I too must be compelled, at this time, under these circumstances, with these needs, to raise that correct point here. Many of the points scholars make are correct — and from here on, for a few words, what I am saying is a general teaching regarding all our intellectual and research work — we must see, among the correct things, which is necessary? Among the necessary things, which is more necessary? Among the more necessary, which is urgent? Among the urgent, which is pressing and vital? We should take up that first; and once we are free of it, the later discussions, and so on in this order, we proceed this far, until at the end of the work we come to discussions that, although correct, their necessity and urgency are not felt to that degree.

Concerning the discussion of prophethood: it is true that the question of how much divine knowledge or human knowledge a prophet possesses is itself a discussion; or the question of knowing whether our Prophet — who did not write and did not read, as the eloquent Qur'an states:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAnkabūt (29:48):** "And you did not write any book with your right hand, otherwise the falsifiers would surely have fallen into doubt."[^s14-ankabut]

The Prophet neither wrote nor read. Was it that he did not write and did not read because he was unable to? That is, in the ordinary expression of today, was he entirely illiterate? Or rather, was he able, and yet did not write and did not read? Well, it is a discussion, after all. Or the question of which religion, which creed, among the religions and creeds of the world, the Prophet of Islam believed in and practiced before his prophethood and his Biʿthah — of course it is a discussion, but how necessary is this discussion for us? How necessary for us is the discussion before it? How necessary for us is the discussion before that one? It is not necessary at all.

Of course, when a person has learned everything related to prophethood and related to religion, there is no harm in his coming, at the end of the work, to understand which religion the Prophet adhered to before the Biʿthah. But we are still in the bend of a single alley.[^s14-attar] Our society still does not know the meaning of prophethood, the meaning of the Biʿthah, the aim of the Biʿthah, the outcome and conclusion[^s14-conclusion] of the Biʿthah, the path of the Biʿthah, and the watchword of prophethood; it does not even know that it is afflicted with this condition. The Muslim does not know the aim of the Muḥammadan Biʿthah — that is why his affairs are the way they are. If he knew the aim of his Prophet's Biʿthah, he would go after that aim. We are still in the first matters, the preliminary matters, the fundamental matters in the field of prophethood — and are we to grasp at and occupy ourselves with the secondary matters, those of the fourth and fifth degree? That is why, in our discussion of prophethood, we do not raise any of those discussions which the theologians have conducted and continue to conduct in their books. Here are you, and here are those books that the theologians wrote — go and read them. And we have no doubt about the necessity of those discussions, nor about their goodness; but again we have no doubt that their necessity is by far less than the necessity of many other discussions that ought to be raised today. That is why we do not discuss them.

The first matter that we discuss concerning prophethood is the philosophy of prophethood. Why must there be a prophet? Why must someone, from the side of the Lord, gird himself to guide humankind? Can man himself not do it? Are human knowledge and human thought not sufficient? Why a prophet? Why a messenger between the unseen (ghayb) and the witnessed (shuhūd)? This is a question that we must know. If we do not know the philosophy of prophethood, the rest of the discussions about prophethood will be a series of discussions that are almost suspended in mid-air. So first we must know: prophethood for what? This is our first discussion today, in these notes that I wrote, which are in the gentlemen's hands; we have set this question down briefly, in a few sentences, very concisely, and the verses we recite today bear upon this matter.

Concerning the philosophy of prophethood we will not speak much; it is one word, and that one word is this: that the senses of man, the instincts of man, and the intellect of man fall short of guiding and taking the hand of man. One word. A series of creatures can be managed by their senses. Some animals we perhaps know to be of this sort, that they draw only upon their own senses — the outward senses. Most kinds of animals draw on their instincts in order to be guided; that is, the honeybee's instinct tells it: go and alight upon the flower, upon the fragrant flower, and suck from its perfume and its essence; then go to the house, the hive; build the houses in that six-cornered, hexagonal[^s14-hexagonal] form; and there, with certain arrangements, enter the houses and exit; and have a queen and helpers[^s14-helpers] and guards — in short, a bee civilization. If you imagine that one day the bees sat down together and a great conference of the chiefs of the bees of the hives or regions was convened, and they said, "How is it better for us to build the house?" — one said octagonal, one said square, one said hexagonal; then they studied it and saw, no, the hexagonal is more suitable — if you imagine it happened this way, I must say you have made a mistake. No such situation occurred.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:68–69):** "And your Lord revealed to the honeybee: Take houses for yourselves in the mountains, and in the trees, and in what they build as trellises. Then eat of all the fruits, and tread the paths of your Lord, in humble obedience."[^s14-nahl]

This is divine revelation (waḥy) to the bee. The divine revelation to the bee does not mean that the bee sits down and Gabriel descends upon it; the bee's structure is like this, the bee's instinct is like this — there is an instinctual pull that draws the bee, compels it; it cannot do otherwise. Naturally and instinctually the bee is compelled, it is fashioned[^s14-fashioned] to build the house, to build it in this form, to find flower and plant from such-and-such a place and suck from them. If a bee were to deviate — if instead of flower and plant it were to smell and suck foul-smelling and rotten plants — they would behead it right there at the entrance of the hive. All this is their nature (fiṭrah) and their instinct.

Instinct suffices for the bee. This gentleman Maurice Maeterlinck[^s14-maeterlinck] sat for years and reflected on the life of bees and ants and termites; he sat and studied it. This very termite about which you complain so much — it has a certain arrangement inside those ceilings of yours; in your usurped house it has built an organized structure. You are not content for it to settle on the beam of your house and build a house there — it is usurped — but in this usurped house it does its work. It builds a house, in a particular shape, in a particular order. And the strange thing is this: that if you could — and if you had been able — to find one of the houses of the termites, or a hive of the honeybees, from the time of Noah's flood, for example, or ten centuries before Noah's flood, and to place it beside one of the hives in one of the pleasant, fine-aired highlands of the Mashhad of this present time, you would see that, to the breadth of a needle's point, this hive does not differ from that hive in its arrangements. Evolution, advancement, progress — these do not exist in the work of the honeybee, nor in the work of any other animal. What does exist is the revelation of nature, the revelation of the instincts, and the guidance of the quality of its creation and formation, which draws it, moves it, shows it the way, points out the obstacles to it, until it is able to do its own work. There is nothing other than instinct.

Man too uses instinct, but little. When you first came into the world, your state — with all due distinction — was the state of one of these very animals. It was by instinct, by the pull of nature (fiṭrah) and disposition, that you were able to find the store of your food in your mother's breast, and when they placed it in your mouth, to suck. No one had taught you to suck; you had not learned its practical, auditory, and visual instructions somewhere — this was your instinct. As you rose from the stage of childhood, gradually this tool, this eyeglass, this weapon whose name is instinct grew weak, became ineffective, became of little use; and something more effective, stronger, more powerful, by the name of the reason and intellect (ʿaql wa khirad) of man, took its place, and you became reasoning. You no longer work by instinct now; it is not instinct that tells you, "Sir, go and open your shop, or close it at such-and-such an hour, or answer the customer this way, or study the lesson this way, or teach in that manner" — these are not instinct, these are instructions that you took up from thought and from knowledge, learned, and found your way of life.

But does this intellect, this human thought, suffice to guide you and bring you to the destination of felicity? Is human reason adequate to guide him? Reason itself, if it is open, if it thinks, if it does not become bigoted, if it wishes to judge without ulterior motive, will say no — like a court that gives its own verdict that it has no jurisdiction over itself, a court that rules, "I have no competence to judge in this case." Sound human reason, free of ulterior motive, judges that "I am not competent to guide humanity independently." You want proof? We have two kinds of proof. One proof is that human reason is limited, not infinite, whereas the needs of human beings are infinite. How can a single intellect understand all the needs, so that, in the face of those needs, it might supply what would fill the place of those needs, or might enact a law? Human reason cannot do this work; it is too weak, too incapable, too inadequate to recognize all the pains and to provide the appropriate remedy for all of them.

The other proof is this: look at the historical and scientific realities. See, were the intellects able? Were intellects such as the intellect of Aristotle and Plato and Socrates able to manage humankind? Plato the thinker, after he sits and thinks, consults, studies, researches, sketches an ideal city (a utopia) — this ideal city is good only for the mind and the inner strongbox of the honorable Plato himself, for the reason that for a single moment, in the world, this ideal city was never put into practice. And if you look today at Plato's ideal city, with the arrangements of the world of the present time, it appears to you as something unacceptable and ridiculous.

Look at the rational and philosophical schools, how they array themselves one against the other. See that humanity, unless it is connected to a source and a point higher, loftier, and deeper than human reason, cannot reach the way to guidance and felicity. This is the meaning of prophethood: man needs a force higher, a guidance stronger and deeper than the guidance of the senses, than the guidance of instinct, than the guidance of reason. This guidance comes — and what does it do? Does it come to compete with your senses? Does it come to oppose your instinct? Does it come to dash the head of reason against a stone? Never. It comes to guide reason, to nurture reason, to bring the buried reason out from beneath the tons of dust.

Our Commander of the Faithful, may God's blessings and peace be upon him — I misspoke — the Commander of the Faithful of all humanity (let us not confine the Commander of the Faithful to ourselves) — he is the teacher of humanity, the Commander of the Faithful is the great one of humanity — he says that God sent the prophets, according to what is in the Nahj al-Balāghah, so that they might urge human beings to remain faithful to the covenant and pledge of their nature (fiṭrah), and might bring to their remembrance the forgotten blessings, and then "and they might unearth for them the buried treasures of the intellects" (wa yuthīrū lahum dafāʾin al-ʿuqūl).[^s14-hadith11] The intellects, the reasons, the understandings, the consciousnesses of the human societies which had been buried by the Pharaohs and the Nimrods and the great ones and the powerful — the prophets come to bring out these buried treasures, to stir up these buried intellects. Pharaoh does not like the people to possess intellect; he does not like human beings to understand — because if they understand, his existence will become void and a fable, which we will see explained in the later discussions of prophethood, God willing. Pharaoh does not like the people to employ the force of intellect; because this is so, he buries the force of intellect. How he buries it is a matter for the later discussions, God willing. The prophets come to extract these buried treasures, these hoards, these hidden and concealed treasures; they refine them, and they place them in the people's hands.

So the prophets, with the force of revelation that they have, do not go to war against reason. The one who imagines that religion is at variance with reason either does not know religion or has no reason. Otherwise, one who possesses reason, one who has tested and tried reason and also knows religion, knows well that religion cannot, and does not, have any variance whatsoever with human knowledge and human reason — none at all. What religion says, sound intellects understand and approve. Those ignorant people who, in the name of defending religion, sometimes say, "Sir, you must not seek the rationale of religion; you must not seek argumentation in religion; you must not seek philosophy in the matter of religion" — they imagine that this saying (seeking philosophy, asking for philosophy) diminishes the worth of religion. They must know that it is not so. Such a thing is not the case. Sound religion, when it is presented before the complete intellect, has no variance and no conflict with it whatsoever. Today the great intellects of humanity understand the tawḥīd of religion, the prophethood of religion, the prayer of religion, the fasting of religion, the zakāt of religion, the secondary rulings of religion.

When the human intellect and the experience of human knowledge recognizes alcohol, knows the harms of alcohol, understands its blow and injury to the body, to the nerves, to the spirit, and to the general condition of society — why should I not be able and not have the courage to recite the verse of the Qur'an with power?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:90):** "Indeed, wine, gambling, idols, and divining-arrows are an abomination from the work of Satan, so avoid it."[^s14-maidah]

Why should I not raise the verse of the Qur'an? Why should I not appeal to what human knowledge has understood in this field? Why should I not say that it is from the work of Satan — that is, it is the satans who give this to you, and it is the satans who profit from the drinking of that wretched drinker? Why should we not say this?

That which you have heard that Imam al-Sajjād, may God's blessings and peace be upon him, said — "The religion of God is not attained by the intellects" (dīn Allāh lā yuṣābu bi-l-ʿuqūl)[^s14-hadith30] — its meaning is something else. That is, the divine creed cannot be discovered by reason. What does "cannot be discovered" mean? It means that if you have no narration (riwāyah) telling you that the noon prayer is four cycles, you cannot understand by reason that the noon prayer is four cycles — and this is a very correct point too. If the Qur'an itself does not state the time of prayer:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Isrāʾ (17:78):** "Establish the prayer from the declining of the sun until the darkness of the night, and the Qur'an of dawn (the dawn prayer and its supererogatory prayer)."[^s14-isra]

If the Qur'an does not state this, you cannot understand by way of reason and thought and ordinary intellect when the time of prayer is. The Qur'an must state this; the hadith must state this; revelation must state this to us. This is what Imam al-Sajjād means; the outward of his statement too is just this — "The religion of God is not attained by the intellects." Its meaning is not that we cannot see the rulings of religion and the teachings of religion with the vision of reason and with the eyeglass of reason and human intellect — such that a group of ignorant people, in the name of defending religion, with fervor, with heat, cry out, "Sir, do not set forth a philosophy for religion!" Why should we not? Why should we not set it forth? Of course we always say, we repeat, we confess, that what we understand is not a thousandth of the deep teachings of religion; what we can apply and explain is far less than what may in reality be done and exist — there is no doubt in this. But I say one word, the next person says one word, the person after him says one word. In the longitudinal line of human history, when you reckon, after two hundred years, five hundred years, you see that humanity, humankind, is far more believing, more confessing, more acknowledging of the depth of religion than in the age when these thoughts and these reflections and these applications had not yet come to it.

Therefore religion, when it comes, does not come to crush reason, does not come to abrogate[^s14-abrogate] reason and drive reason out of life. So for what does religion come? It comes to guide reason, to take the hand of reason. Reason exists, but when caprice is beside it, it cannot judge correctly. Reason exists, but when greed is shoulder to shoulder with it, it cannot see correctly. When ulterior motive is beside it, it cannot understand correctly. Religion comes to take away from reason the caprices, the whims, the greeds, the fears, the ulterior motives; it strengthens the sound, complete reason, confirms it, so that it may understand well. And when you turn to Islam, you see that Islam, from beginning to end, is full of the manifestations of reason. In the Qur'an how much we have: "Will you not reason?" (a-fa-lā taʿqilūn); how much we have: "Will you not reflect?" (a-fa-lā tatafakkarūn); how much we have — so that you may understand, so that you may know — how much we have: "Indeed in that are signs for those endowed with understanding" (inna fī dhālika la-āyātin li-ūlī l-albāb) — for the people of reason. In the narrations how much we have: "Indeed God has two proofs over the people" (inna li-Llāhi ʿalā l-nāsi ḥujjatayn)[^s14-hadith42] — God has two proofs over the people: one the prophet and one the reason. And again reason. About reason we will not speak more than this now.

In sum, keep this word in mind: the summary of the discussion comes to this, that man, without the guidance of revelation, without revelation coming to him and coming to his aid, cannot bring himself to the destination of felicity; and when revelation comes, it does not suppress reason, just as it does not suppress instinct, just as it does not destroy the outward senses. No; it comes to strengthen the outward senses, the human and mortal instincts, and the force of the intellect and thought of man; it refines them, purifies them, takes their hand, teaches them. This is the task of revelation. Therefore the philosophy of prophethood is this.

Because it is so — because we are deficient, because human knowledge and insight are not sufficient for our guidance — a hand must come out from the unseen and guide us. The place for a hand to come out from the unseen is here, not there where, when I have gone a little hungry, I do not go after a morsel of bread but wait for a hand to come out from the unseen. No — it is not for the place where, when I am confronted with a sin, I content myself with regret and sorrow and grief and wait for a hand to come out from the unseen for me; no — it is not for the place where I do not perform my divine duty, do not enjoin good, do not forbid evil, do not tread the path of God, and wait for a hand to come out from the unseen and do something[^s14-hafez] — contrary to the verse of the Qur'an:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Māʿūn (107:1–3):** "Have you seen the one who denies the Religion? That is the one who drives away the orphan and does not urge the feeding of the needy."[^s14-maun]

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muddaththir (74:43–44):** "[They will say:] We were not among those who prayed, nor did we feed the needy."[^s14-muddaththir1]

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muddaththir (74:46):** "And we used to deny the Day of Judgment."[^s14-muddaththir2]

The verse of the Qur'an counts among the deniers of religion those who do not incite the feeding of the destitute. They do not stir up and rouse the people to feeding the destitute and — with a broader, deeper view, and an interpretation closer to the very text of Islam — to uprooting poverty and hunger. That person who does not conform himself to this verse takes not a single step on the path of eliminating hungers, advances not a single pace on the path of destroying the root of poverty, and sits waiting for a hand to come out from the unseen and do something. Such people must know that if a hand were to come out from the unseen, it would first strike upon their own brains, to throw these spoiled and void existences away, to bring them down. There is such a thing as a hand coming out from the unseen and doing something — but this is the point I made: it is necessary, for the guidance of man, that a hand should come from the unseen, and that is the hand of the prophet, the hand of prophethood; that is the skill of the messengership which comes and guides human beings, in just the manner we have stated: to rouse the force of reason within them.

Then several other matters arise here, several other matters are present here, of which I have briefly written down a list of some.[^s14-polycopy]

One is this: what concept does prophethood have? What is its relation to the Biʿthah and being raised up (bar-angīkhtagī)? We say the prophet was sent (mabʿūth), was raised up; what does "raised up" mean? Is there a stirring (angīzish) in prophethood? Is there a movement? This too is a discussion. In the discussions related to prophethood we do not encounter this discussion.

What is the starting point of the work of the prophets? From where do they begin reform and aid to human beings? How is the conclusion of their endeavor? Where do their works arrive? Just this — that they laid him against a tree and sawed him, and it was finished and over?[^s14-zakariyya] They cut off the head of Yaḥyā before that tyrant (ṭāghī), and it was finished and over? Is the end of the work of prophethood just this? Or no — another conclusion, another ending for this beginning is conceived and envisaged, and it is alluded to in the Qur'an, and there are matters which we will of course raise gradually, one by one, in the coming days, and set forth. Concerning the philosophy of prophethood, just this much that I have stated is, in my own view, sufficient. Of course, this was the briefest expression that could be set forth in this field. (You dear brothers listened well to this, God willing — for I see that you listen well — God willing the matter has been well grasped too. If anything in this field has not become clear to someone, here or there, he can ask me, or ask his neighbor at his side, so that this matter may become clear: what fundamentally is the philosophy of prophethood?)

The verses that exist in this field are very pithy and very meaningful verses. My first thought was to explain in detail today this very verse that I am now reciting; then I saw that if I wished to explain it, besides the fact that the matter about prophethood that I wished to say would remain unsaid — it might be put off to tomorrow or the day after — I cannot fully explain even the meaning of this verse in this one hour. It is very interesting; of course I refer the good minds and thought of the friends to be sure to consult and look at this verse in the light of the Qur'an,[^s14-taleqani] for there is a relatively good discussion in that field there. I will content myself with only a brief translation.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:213):** "Mankind was one community (ummah)."[^s14-baqarah]

The people were all one ummah and one group. About this the commentators have an extensive discussion among themselves. Were they one ummah — a good ummah? Or were they one ummah — a bad ummah? On either supposition, the parties to the dispute raise an objection. Some say that "Mankind was one community" alludes to the primitive and initial communalism (ishtirākiyyat) of the prehistoric eras. I say that there is no evidence for this meaning, except that the name of the communalist and primitive era is on people's tongues, and we feel it a pity that our Qur'an too should have no share of that cloak[^s14-proverb] — only that. Otherwise, "Mankind was one community" alludes to an era when human beings lived with the primitive and original communalism, as the gentlemen say, like — let me put it this way — animals, naked and bare and in herds, going about in the deserts and performing feats of strength, scraping stone with their claws and catching animals, eating them, and sometimes eating one another too. Should we take him [Adam], or us, to be the referent in this verse "Mankind was one community"? No, no! We never attempt to attach something to our Qur'an from elsewhere. No; what we understand from our Qur'an, that is enough for us. If we understand it, we have done a great feat. Our Qur'an has no need that we should give it some marginal note or appendix from the sayings of others and the cultures of others, and exalt it thereby. No; the Qur'an does not want this kind of exaltation.

"Mankind was one community" can be given several meanings. Two of its meanings are present in *In the Light of the Qur'an*, which you will now consult and observe; and one meaning occurs to me that is other than those two meanings that I alluded to in this translation. "Mankind was one community" means that human beings and people were all in an equal and identical state, in respect of needs and in respect of capacities. All human beings were of one kind of need, and all human beings had one measure of capacity — and the social reality is always like this. All human beings have reason, have thought, have intelligence, have a sixth sense, have outward senses, have inward senses. All human beings have hunger, have thirst, have sexual desire, want a house, want clothing, and the like. The needs are all equal, almost of one kind, and the capacities are similar. If one human being has been raised in a better upbringing, his capacity having blossomed, that is another matter. If some young nobleman has been born in an aristocratic house, with a private tutor at home teaching him at six or seven years of age to speak Indian and Chinese like a nightingale, while the child of some laborer in the kilns does not know even Persian properly at seven or eight years of age — that is another matter. This is no proof that this one's capacity was greater than that one's; no, this one's capacity was extracted, and the capacity of that poor one remained and was not extracted. The oil deposits that exist in regions — let us suppose northern, southern, in such-and-such a country — it is not certain that they are richer and more abundant than the oil deposits of some other point that has not been extracted. The oil of this one has been extracted; it comes into the lamps, its gasoline comes into the automobiles; everyone knows it too, its name is famous too. The oil of that one, poor thing, has not yet been extracted — it may even be richer.

So human beings, naturally and ordinarily, were one community (ummah wāḥidah), with identical needs, with equal and similar capacities, which we have specified in the translation.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:213) [continued]:** "So God sent the prophets" (fa-baʿatha Llāhu l-nabiyyīn).

Among these human beings of equal capacities, God the Exalted raised up the prophets. Among these of the same level, the Lord of the worlds singled out one human being — higher, stronger, deeper, more ardent, and of greater capacity — and raised him up. To do what? "as bearers of glad tidings" (mubashshirīn) — bringers of good news — "and warners" (wa mundhirīn) — and givers of warning. Of what do the prophets give glad tidings? They give the glad tidings of Paradise, they give the glad tidings of the felicity of this world, they give the glad tidings of the ideal city, they give the glad tidings of the establishment of security and peace and welfare, they give the glad tidings of the disappearance of poverty and despair and fear and insecurity and ignorance; they are bringers of glad tidings; and at last they give human beings the glad tidings of the establishment of a virtuous government and a righteous city, and after that the glad tidings of going to Paradise and connection to the good pleasure (riḍwān) of the Lord — they give glad tidings. And they are warners; they frighten — they frighten of the fire of Hell too, they frighten of the narrowness of the bridge of Ṣirāṭ too, they frighten of the wretchedness of this world too, they frighten of the domination of the demon[^s14-demon] of ignorance and poverty too, they frighten of the fall into the valley and the slope of corruption too, they frighten of the destruction of the human capacities too; they are bringers of warning, they are warners.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:213) [continued]:** "So God sent the prophets as bearers of glad tidings and warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth" (wa anzala maʿahum al-kitāba bi-l-ḥaqq).

It is not all just that they should say "People, fear" and "People, glad tidings be to you." What do you have with you? A book from the side of the Lord. He sent down with them a book by the truth, in accordance with the truth. I have explained "the truth" (ḥaqq) several times (now I can no longer explain it; I think that if I wished to explain "the truth" again, I would have to speak about that point alone for some ten or twelve minutes). In sum, that which conforms with the nature (fiṭrah) of the world, that which accompanies the natural course of the world — that is the truth. That which conforms with the nature of man and the disposition of the universe — that they call the truth. The book of the prophets too is the truth; it drives man forward on his natural path, on his innate course, on his ordinary bed and evolutionary course; it advances him, aids him. The book of the prophets carries the truth with it.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:213) [continued]:** "He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it might judge among the people concerning that wherein they differed" (li-yaḥkuma bayna l-nāsi fīmā ikhtalafū fīhi).

Until that book — "that it might judge" (li-yaḥkuma); that is, until the book judges among the people, in those things about which they differ with one another. After all, people are not free of difference; differences among human beings are a custom (sunnah), and their existence is necessary, and their absence is harmful. Know this: the existence of difference is good, for the reason that difference is a cause of evolution. In the matter of differences — of course this thing I said, that its absence is harmful, in this word I must reconsider; I do not know whether it is harmful or not. At any rate, I must elaborate and interpret. Its existence at any rate is beneficial; "its absence is harmful," I do not know — I take that statement back. The book comes to judge among the people, to render judgment, to rule, concerning the things about which they have differed. What do we understand here? We understand that the government which the prophets bring into being is not the government of the individual prophet, the government of a person, the government of despotism; rather, it is the government of law, the government of the Book. When the prophet comes, he makes a society in which the ruler in that society, in meaning and in reality, is that very book — that is, the law.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:213) [continued]:** "that it might judge among the people concerning that wherein they differed. And none differed concerning it except those who were given it" (wa mā ikhtalafa fīhi illā lladhīna ūtūhu).

Concerning this book no difference arose; they did not differ — "except those who were given it," except those people to whom the book had been given, those people for whom this very heavenly book came; they themselves differed concerning the heavenly book. What does this show us? It shows the existence of distortion (taḥrīf) in the heavenly religions, among the sayings of the prophets. When the prophets came, they brought the book, the law, the school; then those very people to whom this book and law and school had been given differed about it. They differed — meaning what? Meaning that one type spoke the truth, and one type spoke contrary to fact. So therefore there are those who are followers of a religion and who speak the word of religion, but speak contrary to fact — an allusion to the existence of abrogation (naskh) and distortion (taḥrīf) in the religions.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:213) [continued]:** "after the clear proofs had come to them, out of mutual envy" (min baʿdi mā jāʾathumu l-bayyinātu baghyan baynahum).

Out of enmity and transgression with one another.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:213) [continued]:** "Then God guided those who believed to the truth concerning that wherein they differed, by His leave" (fa-hadā Llāhu lladhīna āmanū limā ikhtalafū fīhi mina l-ḥaqqi bi-idhnih).

Those people who are believers, who have inclined to the true religion — God guided them to the answer to that wherein they had differed, by His leave and permission.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:213) [continued]:** "And God guides whom He wills to a straight path" (wa Llāhu yahdī man yashāʾu ilā ṣirāṭin mustaqīm).

And God guides whomever He wills to the straight path. (This is one portion of today's recitation, about which it seems it was necessary that I should speak more, in these last words of it — except that I now feel I have grown very tired, such that I will content myself with the verses of Sūrat al-Jumuʿah too in just the translation that is in your hands, and will not explain them further.)

Let those who have a Qur'an with them bring Sūrat al-Jumuʿah, in the twenty-eighth part (juzʾ):

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Jumuʿah (62:1):** "In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth glorifies God, the Sovereign, the Holy, the Mighty, the Wise."

This is Sūrat al-Jumuʿah, up to "and God is the possessor of mighty bounty" (wa Llāhu dhū l-faḍli l-ʿaẓīm). (Of course, this sūrah I have explained several times up to now, both here and in that mosque,[^s14-mosque] on various occasions; because on Thursday nights we usually recite this sūrah in the maghrib prayer, so that the believers and friends who participate in that prayer may know its meaning — on this account, I have explained Sūrat al-Jumuʿah several times up to now and have given a brief commentary too; and it may be that many of you gentlemen who are present here have heard from me the commentary on Sūrat al-Jumuʿah. Besides that, I am now tired. And as for the translation, which I have done, in these very papers that are in your hands the translation has been written — this much, in our view, is sufficient for now.)

So what have we understood up to now? We have understood the philosophy of prophethood, and that this is a principle in all religions — indeed, the mother principle, which, if it is not present, religion cannot have a correct meaning and concept; because religion is that thing which comes from the side of the Lord, by way of a messenger, a bearer of revelation.

[^jumuah-blessed]: Sūrat al-Jumuʿah, the Blessed [Sūrah], verse 2.

[^s14-ankabut]: Sūrat al-ʿAnkabūt, the Blessed Sūrah / verse 48: "And you would not write any book with your right hand, otherwise the falsifiers would certainly have fallen into doubt."

[^s14-attar]: "ʿAṭṭār traversed the seven cities of love / We are still in the bend of a single alley" (Rūmī).

[^s14-conclusion]: Conclusion, outcome.

[^s14-hexagonal]: (s-d-s) six-sided, hexagonal.

[^s14-helpers]: (ʿ-w-n) helpers, companions.

[^s14-nahl]: Sūrat al-Naḥl, the Blessed Sūrah / verses 68 and 69: "And your Lord revealed to the honeybee: make houses for yourself from some of the mountains, and from some of the trees, and from what they build as trellises. Then eat of all the fruits, and tread the paths of your Lord in humble obedience."

[^s14-fashioned]: (f-ṭ-r) created, made.

[^s14-maeterlinck]: Maurice Maeterlinck (1862–1949 CE) was a Belgian poet, writer, and philosopher who won the Nobel Prize with his play *The Blue Bird*. He wrote essays entitled *The Life of the Bee*, *The Life of the Termite*, and *The Life of the Ant*.

[^s14-hadith11]: Hadith no. 11.

[^s14-maidah]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah, the Blessed Sūrah / verse 90.

[^s14-hadith30]: Hadith no. 30.

[^s14-isra]: Sūrat al-Isrāʾ, the Blessed Sūrah / verse 78: "From the first declining of the sun until the utmost darkness of the night (until its midpoint) establish the prayer, and also the Qur'an of dawn (the dawn prayer and the supererogatory prayer of dawn)."

[^s14-abrogate]: (n-s-kh) to render void, to abrogate.

[^s14-hadith42]: Hadith no. 42.

[^s14-hafez]: "The city is empty of lovers; perhaps from some quarter / A hand may come out from the unseen and do something" (Ḥāfiẓ).

[^s14-maun]: Sūrat al-Māʿūn, the Blessed Sūrah / verses 1 to 3.

[^s14-muddaththir1]: Sūrat al-Muddaththir, the Blessed Sūrah / verses 43 and 44.

[^s14-muddaththir2]: Sūrat al-Muddaththir, the Blessed Sūrah / verse 46.

[^s14-polycopy]: Please refer to the mimeographed handout at the end of the session.

[^s14-zakariyya]: An allusion to the tradition of the prophet Zakariyyā, who, according to narrations, was sawed in half inside a tree where he had taken refuge.

[^s14-taleqani]: A reference to *In the Light of the Qur'an* (*Partuvī az Qurʾān*), a commentary (tafsīr) written by Ayatollah Ṭāleqānī.

[^s14-baqarah]: Sūrat al-Baqarah, the Blessed Sūrah / verse 213.

[^s14-proverb]: It is a proverb, alluding to having a benefit and share of something.

[^s14-demon]: An ugly and frightful being; in common parlance applied to satans and jinn.

[^s14-mosque]: The Mosque of Karāmat, in holy Mashhad.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 15: The Mission (Bi'that) in Prophethood

*بعثت در نبوت — Thursday, 11 Mehr 1353 / 16 Ramaḍān al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:1–8):** "Read in the name of your Lord who created — created man from a clinging clot. Read, and your Lord is the most generous — who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know. No indeed! Truly man transgresses when he sees himself self-sufficient. Truly, to your Lord is the return."[^s15-1]

The subject of our talk today is this: the prophet and messenger — at the moment the burden of messengership (risālat) and prophethood (nubuwwat) is placed upon his shoulders, after he receives the message of God — what state, what condition, comes into being within him and in the world around him? It is, of course, clear that we are not greatly concerned with the inner qualities and conditions of the prophet himself; if we do raise the matter here, it is in order to draw a subtle, precise benefit from an ordinary, common word that is present in the Qur'an and in the usage of the religiously learned[^s15-2] — a word that, incidentally, illuminates for us one dimension of the question of prophethood.

You know that in the Qur'an, whenever there is talk of a prophet's coming, generally the talk is of his bi'that — his being commissioned and sent forth. As the verse says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:36):** "We raised up a messenger in every nation."[^s15-3]

We commissioned Moses, Abraham, and the other prophets. Bi'that — what does this bi'that mean? And what is the relationship between bi'that and prophethood? You will notice that the title of today's discussion, written on those papers in the possession of you brothers and sisters, reads: "The Bi'that in Prophethood." Within prophethood there is a bi'that. What does bi'that mean? What is this bi'that? If within prophethood there is a bi'that, to whom does this bi'that belong, what is it, what is its benefit, and so on?

Bi'that means a being-roused, a being-stirred-up. What does being-roused mean? It means motion, movement after lethargy, slackness, and stagnation. A dead person who has lain for years in some graveyard, whose bodily parts have become dust, when by the power of the Lord he rises up on the Day of Resurrection — they say he has been raised up. As the verse says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Rūm (30:56):** "This is the Day of the Raising-up."[^s15-4]

It is the day of being-roused. A human being who had been asleep in his house, or who in the ordinary course of social life merely moved along — in whom there was no fervor, no striving, no activity, nothing contrary to the customary state of society; who was in reality like a plank of wood, like a lifeless plant, like a wisp of straw cast upon the ordinary flood of society, which carried him this way and that — such a person, when he comes to himself, when he emerges from this state of indifference, when he senses that this path, this natural current, this ordinary stream, need not necessarily be one hundred percent reassuring and convincing to him; that perhaps another current can be sought out which will bring that human being to the destination of felicity — when, as a result of these thoughts, the human being awakens, becomes alert, finds striving, finds movement — this human being, they say, has been raised up. In smaller, more particular examples: when you awaken from sleep and rise, you say "he was roused." When you come out of slackness and laziness and listlessness and lethargy and busy yourself with movement, they say "he was roused." This is the meaning of being-roused; ba'th means this.

The Day of Resurrection, as you know, is called "Yawm al-Ba'th," the day of being-roused, or in another expression, the Day of Rising. The Rising — that day of standing up, that day of coming out of slackness and listlessness, that day of movement, the day on which human beings, from the moment they emerge from the grave until the moment their final destiny is determined, are continually in striving, in agitation, in movement; this is what they call Yawm al-Ba'th, the Rising. In prophethood there exists exactly such a state.

I want your vision regarding prophethood to become a fundamentally new vision. Some imagine prophethood in this way: as though a preacher enters a city to expound for the people of that town a few matters of religion or of something other than religion; or, let us suppose, a teacher of religious rulings rises up among a people and stands to tell them a few subsidiary legal questions; or some orator, some speaker — let us suppose a rabble-rouser — enters a gathering to stir up a bit of clamor and hold a rally.[^s15-5] They usually imagine the prophet to be a person of this sort — like a learned, clerical, well-bred man among a group of people. Except that these people, too, sometimes recognize his worth — these are good folk, and they are called believers; and sometimes they do not recognize his worth — these become unbelievers, and they are called polytheists. We have imagined that the prophet is something of this kind.

In prophethood there is a transformation and an upheaval — I should say two transformations and upheavals. The first is in the very being of the prophet himself. The bi'that, the rising, the revolution, the transformation, first comes into being within the inner depths and the essence of the prophet himself, within the prophet's own inner self. First he is changed; first he emerges from the state of stagnation and lethargy. After a resurrection has been set up within his spirit, within his inner self — after a rising has come into being within the essence and the soul of the prophet — after all the abundant, brimming reserves deposited by God within the prophet have been awakened — like a spring from which, at every instant, billions of streams of water are drawn forth and pour out (whereas before this instant, all these waters were hidden, concealed within it) — after, in short, the prophet himself becomes a Muslim, when he himself comes under the influence of the transformation of divine revelation — then this very spring, this very transformation, this very fervor, this very revolution, this very rising, pours out from this overflowing, surging fountain that is the spirit and inner self of the prophet into society; it is transmitted into the very text of human society. After a transformation has come into being within him, a transformation comes into being in society. After a mighty rising has been set up within his inner self, a still mightier rising is set up within the text of society. After a revolution has come into being within his heart, by his hand a revolution comes into being in society, and the bi'that in its true sense is realized. So you see that in prophethood, whatever there is, is fervor and transformation and upheaval and rousing and being-roused.[^s15-6]

What is the prophet's condition before prophethood? Two points concerning the life of every prophet, before prophethood, exist in a manner that seems contradictory. Of course, when I say "contradictory," I do not mean that there is a real contradiction; it may merely appear contradictory. The first is that the prophet, even though he has not yet been commissioned, possesses very strong and deep human reserves — more than the rest of the people. His capacity for understanding, his capacity for movement, his capacity for outburst, is not comparable to that of other people, other human beings. His capacity for the worship and servitude of God, his readiness to be a servant of God, is to a degree that is inconceivable for other human beings. All the reserves that draw a human being out of the abasement[^s15-7] of being mere dust and raise him to the summit of:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqara (2:156):** "Truly we belong to God, and truly to Him we return,"[^s15-8]

— that is, of being a true servant of God and being adorned with the character-traits of God — all these reserves are present in the prophet more than in others.

Now, why are they more than in others? Did God commit some injustice here, some discrimination here? That is another matter, and in answer to it we can offer a brief, summary reply, in this way: namely, that in the end, in order to bear the burden of the responsibility of prophethood, a heavier, stouter frame and a more powerful reserve was required. The burden of messengership is no penny-trifle of a task.[^s15-9] The burden of prophethood, conveying God's message to the people and transforming a society from one of ignorance (jāhilī) into one of monotheism (tawḥīdī) — this is a task of enormous magnitude and a very heavy burden, and in the end it is necessary that some group bear this burden. Who will bear it? Ordinary human beings? Or a human being who, by virtue of the conditions and characteristics of his environment, family, climate, and kin, possesses greater capacities and greater possibility — and because God sees the greater possibilities in this being, He helps him, grants him grace, bestows favor upon him, places at his disposal whatever is a means of nurturing, so that these two hundred maunds of burden may rest upon a hundred maunds of the prophet's bone[^s15-10] — He makes of him a person ready for this work. In the end, if the Messenger of God had not taken up this heavy burden, this heavy burden would have remained on the ground; it was not the work of you and me to lift it. It was not the work of Gandhi[^s15-11] and Lumumba[^s15-12] to lift it; it was not the work of Socrates and Plato and Aristotle. A frame more powerful than all these frames is necessary to be able to lift this heavy burden — which is named messengership and prophethood and bi'that — and the detail of this is, of course, very extensive. The prophet is among the people with such reserves as these. This is one point, and this was the summary of the first point: that the reserves which exist in the prophet, in the messenger, are more brimming, richer, deeper, and more usable than the reserves of ordinary people.

The second point is this: that the prophet, before the bi'that and before prophethood, is, in the ordinary course of life, a companion and partner of the people; they are moving along in one and the same current. From the beginning he is not preoccupied with thinking, "What shall I do to overturn this society?" It is possible that he is discontented — and indeed he is discontented. An intelligent human being is, of course, discontented with the state of class division of his time, with the state of ignorance of his time, with the poverty of his time, with the oppression of his time, with the foolishness of his time, and — to put it in Islamic terms — with the jāhiliyya (state of ignorance) of his time; he is of course discontented. But this discontent is only at the level of discontent; it is not at the level of bringing about a transformation, a revolution, of creating a path counter to the ordinary social path.

You see Moses son of ʿImrān — peace and blessings upon him — living in Pharaoh's house. As one of the elders of a few years back put it, he was eating Pharaoh's pilaf and roast, even playing the young nobleman; in the marketplace, out of zeal for being of the lineage of the Children of Israel and for those who believed in his idea, he even kills a man. Later, when he is commissioned to prophethood and messengership — while returning from the presence of Shuʿayb and from Midian (after he returns from Shuʿayb and Midian, he is commissioned to messengership there) — concerning that killing of his on that day he says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Shuʿarāʾ (26:20):** "...and I was among those who go astray."[^s15-13]

I was astray. What does "I was astray" mean? It means: I had not yet found this path that runs precisely counter to the ordinary path of the Pharaonic society of that age. Not that killing one of the Pharaonic individuals is a sin — no, not at all; rather, he means to say: on that day when I killed him, I was not a person who, with a definite idea, was pursuing a correct revolutionary orientation. I was a man among other men; I was a walker among other walkers; I was striving, mixed in with the strivings of others; I was moving, alongside the movements of others. Today my path has changed; today I condemn that general movement and that collective course of society. That is, today I have become a person who sets up a rising in society. When? After the bi'that.

Or the Noble Prophet himself — God's blessings be upon him and his Family — the verse of the Sūra "By the Forenoon" makes the matter quite clear. (It is in these writings;[^s15-14] and I will now explain it.) The commentators have said various things here, and there are also narrations beneath the Sūra, which I examined: the narrations are interpretation (taʾwīl), not exegesis (tafsīr). What the narration beneath the noble Sūra "By the Forenoon" says — like ninety percent of the narrations beneath the verses of the Qur'an — is not exegesis of the apparent wording; the apparent wording gives its own meaning, which we will now explain and which we wrote there. That is taʾwīl, the addition of a point to the points of this verse; otherwise the verse itself is clear. The Imam — peace and blessings upon him — does not wish to construe the apparent wording of the verse contrary to its apparent sense; rather, the apparent sense of the verse remains preserved in its place, and they wish to add something. What you and I do not understand in an ordinary glance at the verse, the Imam — peace be upon him — teaches us as well; this is what is called taʾwīl. Incidentally, a portion of this very discussion of today's is precisely that our recitation today is "By the Forenoon."

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:1):** "By the forenoon."[^s15-15]

By the brightness of the mid-morning. Al-ḍuḥā means before noon. Notice, this oath itself is meaningful; this very swearing by that time is meaningful. Perhaps there is an allusion in it, and it is clear that, since the discourse is about the bi'that and messengership of the Prophet, therefore this "forenoon" is an allusion to that light which, through the bi'that of the Prophet of Islam and the prophethood of Islam, encompassed all the horizons of the world.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:1–2):** "By the forenoon, and by the night when it grows still."

And by the night, when its darkness covers all the horizons.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:3):** "Your Lord has not forsaken you, nor does He despise you."

It seems that after revelation had begun, when a period had passed, the revelation was cut off. The Prophet — after that being-roused had come into being within him, after that fervor and excitement had arisen in him, after he had grown intimate with the bearer of God's revelation, Gabriel — suddenly sees that the revelation has been cut off; he was intensely grieved. How long did the period of fatra (interval) last?[^s15-16] They call it the period of fatra. Some have said forty days, others have said more — up to two or three years. Then the Sūra "By the Forenoon" is the first glad-tidings Sūra that addresses the Noble Prophet, saying:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:3):** "Your Lord has not forsaken you, nor does He despise you."

Your Lord did not abandon you, did not cast you off, and did not grow angry with you. "Nor does He despise you" — He did not grow angry with you.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:4):** "And surely the Hereafter is better for you than the former [life]."

The Hereafter, the future, is better for you than the beginning, than the past; the end of your affair is better than the beginning of your affair.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:5):** "And surely your Lord will give to you, and you will be satisfied."

And your Lord will give to you, as much as will make you well-pleased. Of course, there is a narration beneath it that what is meant is intercession (shafāʿat) — and that is correct, too. Intercession is one of the things given to the Prophet of God, and it is given to such a degree that he becomes well-pleased. But in this very world, too, so much was given to the Prophet of Islam that he became well-pleased: the guidance of human beings, the establishment of the virtuous city (madīna fāḍila), conquest and victory over bloodthirsty, obstinate enemies, the conquest of lands, the setting of the evolving course of Islamic society onto its track — these were all blessings of God to the Prophet of Islam.

In sum:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:6):** "Did He not find you an orphan and give shelter?"

Did God not find you an orphan and give you refuge? God Most High found you an orphan, without kin, without refuge, and gave you shelter. You had no father — before your birth your father had departed this world; your mother too departed this world a short while later; your grandfather too, after a few brief years, departed this world; you remained, alone, you and you alone, under the guardianship of your uncle Abū Ṭālib. And we sheltered you, the orphan — who were an orphan from the very beginning — at every moment; we kept you in the embrace of our affection and love, O our Prophet. This is giving hope; it means to say: God's shelter is always with you; it was there from childhood, and now too, when a burden of messengership this heavy is upon your shoulder and in your hands. Do not fear, do not be apprehensive, never imagine that God has abandoned you — never; God will not abandon you, when on that day He gave you shelter.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:7):** "And He found you lost (astray), and guided you."

And He found you lost and guided you. Here there are several narrations — though some of these narrations are also weak — and several are sayings of the commentators that have become mixed in with the narrations. It says: "And He found you lost, and guided you." What is meant is not intellectual or spiritual lostness; rather, what is meant is that once the Prophet, in his childhood, had become lost in the mountains of Mecca, and then God guided his grandfather, who was able to find him; or in another episode he had become lost, and a certain woman came and found him; or "you were unknown among the people of Mecca, no one recognized you, and God guided the people of Mecca to you." They construed it in these senses. We do not deny these senses — pay close attention, grasp the point of the words. These senses may indeed be the case; if a sound narration in accordance with any one of these senses should come, then with all respect, very well. It is possible that, from the root, the narrations that exist are weak; it is possible that they are sound. And if sound narrations in this regard should come for any one of these meanings, of course it is acceptable, and one can accept it. But despite our accepting any one of these meanings in accordance with a sound narration, the apparent meaning of the verse is other than this. And accepting a narration that gives us this meaning does not conflict with accepting the apparent meaning of the verse.

The apparent meaning of the verse is something else; the apparent meaning of the verse, plain and explicit, in plain Persian, is saying: "You were astray, you were lost; we guided you." What does "you were astray" mean? Does it mean you were an idolater? Never! Does it mean you were a deviant person? Never! Does it mean you were a sinner? Never! Then what? It means: this straight path that was presented to you through the bi'that and prophethood was not at your disposal. Is it not so? Those bodies of knowledge, those laws, those thoughts, those ideas, that became clear and manifest to his holy heart with the coming of revelation upon the Prophet — did they exist for that great one before prophethood and before the bi'that? Certainly not. "You were lost" means this, and this is the apparent meaning of the verse.
