And there is no problem at all in our construing the noble verse — whose apparent sense is this — from the standpoint of the taʾwīl meaning, as referring to "having been lost among the people of Mecca," "getting lost in the mountains around Mecca," "getting lost while herding certain sheep along a path," for example — just as is found in some of the narrations and reports. Did you grasp it?

Well:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93:7–8):** "And He found you lost, and guided you. And He found you in want, with dependents, and made you free of need."

He found you needy, with a family to support, and made you free of need. What, then, was the purpose of this verse and of laying out this Sūra in this writing? It was this: that the Noble Prophet — just as is the apparent purport of the verse — was lost; he was moving among ordinary people, walking and journeying among society. Although he was distressed by the conditions, although he suffered from the fact that the young nobles of Quraysh would seize the slave-girl of some destitute man and forcibly take possession of her — and he founded the Ḥilf al-Fuḍūl,[^s15-17] the pact of the chivalrous; although he did not for an instant associate any partner with God, nor pay homage and humble himself before these idols; although he did not for an instant make peace with the bullies and the men of brute force, and lived in that society like a chivalrous human being — yet, with all these conditions, what the Prophet did was within the ordinary course of the life of that society; it was within the ordinary course of the life of that society.

(To the one at the door — gentlemen, I request that you come a little further this way, so that the ladies who are coming and going may be able to come and go. If there is room in front, please leave the area by the door open enough for one person to pass. If there is room in front, let them come forward; otherwise let them sit outside, but be sure the doorway is kept open enough for one person — both now and always.)

The Prophet lives along the ordinary course of the collective life of that society — and suddenly the divine revelation arrives. A deep transformation comes into being within his being and within his inner self. So strange is this transformation, so intense is it, that it even affects the body of the Prophet, even affects the nerves of the Prophet. When the Noble Prophet, on the Mountain of Light (Jabal al-Nūr), was struck by the first flame of revelation upon his soul, he caught fire; he saw that the bearer of God's message was saying:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:1):** "Read!"

The Prophet, since he did not know how to read, said: "But I cannot read"[^s15-18] — what shall I read? Or, I do not read, I cannot read — that is, "mā" being either negating "mā" or interrogative "mā"[^s15-19] — "and what shall I read?":

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:1–5):** "Read in the name of your Lord who created — created man from a clinging clot. Read, and your Lord is the most generous — who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know."[^s15-20]

Fire is set to the Prophet's soul; it brings about a transformation. Suddenly, within this thinking, upright, ready human being, it produces a revolution, a rising. He is no longer the same person at all; he is not the person of a moment before. Muhammad was not the Muhammad of an instant before; he was not the human being of an hour before; he was something else entirely, another element, another essence. First the bi'that came into being within his being, the revolution and transformation within his inner self, and then this very revolution became the source by which he was able to draw a whole world into transformation. If he himself had not changed, he could not have changed the world — and this is a lesson for the followers of the Prophet, that they may know: until they themselves change, they cannot change the world. Let them know that:

> An essence that has not received from the Bestower of being —
> how can it become a bestower of being?[^s15-21]

You, who have not carried away a portion and a share of being for yourself, who have not taken any benefit for yourself from this divine fervor and from this glow of the Lord's grace and the blessing of His light, who have carried away no reserve — what do you wish to give to the people? First become fire yourself, become molten, become aflame, so that you may be able to kindle and set alight the coals, the cold ones, the black ones.

The Prophet — first fire was set to his own soul, first his own heart was transformed and overturned, first the fervor of resurrection came into being within his own inner self — by which he was able to draw the world into the fervor of resurrection. He was able to fashion a human being who would give up his life but would not give up his thought. Is it a trifle? Is it a trifle that they would come and throw down dark-skinned Bilāl the Abyssinian and beat him — not the kind of beating that is a mere trifle, an iota or two; but those beatings — and how they beat him! Then, right there beneath the lash, right there beneath the torture — not one minute later, not one hour later — he would cry out, "Aḥad! Aḥad! One! One!" If we wished to render this into Persian expression, to find a sentence equivalent to it, it would mean: "Death to you, death to you, death to you" — this is the meaning of Bilāl's "Aḥad! Aḥad!"[^s15-22] The Prophet cannot, just like that, fashion an Abū Dharr;[^s15-23] he cannot, for nothing, fashion a Bilāl, or fashion a Miqdād,[^s15-24] or fashion an ʿAbdullāh ibn Masʿūd, in the early days of Islam and the bi'that — first he himself was transformed. Now let us see what this melody of the first revelation has done with the Prophet, and what matters it contains.

For another of the portions of today's recitation is the Sūra "Read."

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:1):** "In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Ever-Merciful. Read in the name of your Lord who created."

Read in the name of your Lord, the One who created. Notice, it begins an orderly sequence. The first thing for a God-worshipping human being like our Prophet — who even before the bi'that was a God-worshipper, was not a polytheist — the first thing that for a God-worshipping human being can become a cause of attention to God, that draws his heart to God, is the simplest subject: it is the subject of creation. "Read in the name of your Lord who created." Creation belongs to Him; all these vast manifestations of creation are from Him.

When this matter settles in the mind, it goes up one step, it establishes something higher than creation:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:2):** "Created man from a clinging clot."

He created man — and man, moreover, from a clinging clot. How great is the difference between man and the other creatures? A person does not at first notice this. As you walk down the street, a car passes by, a human being passes by; when you are riding in a car, ten people pass by your side, ten trees pass by the side of your car — by a kind of error in the perception that man has. A person pays no attention; when one looks closely at the difference between man and non-man, one does not give it much thought. Well, man too is a being — of course a more precious being, a better, higher being; but those too are beings of a sort. The tyrannical, self-willed people of the world do not, even to the very end, understand the difference between man and non-man — for the reason that thousands of human beings are not worth, in their eyes, as much as thousands of ants. A person destroys thousands of ants with two pennies' worth of kerosene; these people destroy thousands of human beings with a single decree, a single order, with a single bomb. Let us leave that aside.

Man, in the first instance, does not understand the importance of man and the astounding difference that exists between man and the other creatures; but when he looks closely, then what? He sees: "Oh, it is astonishing!" The chasm that exists between man and the other creatures is a very deep chasm. A chasm of what? What distinguishing mark, what differentiating feature, does man possess that sets him so far apart from the other creatures? The power of intellect, reason, the cognition of universals, the deriving of conclusions from particulars. A work that neither the tree does, nor the stone does, nor the animal does: innovation, novelty. For if there were no innovation and novelty in man, man would always remain at one level, like the other creatures — as I gave the example of the honeybee yesterday. The power of will and decision, free choice — he does whatever he wishes, contrary to the other creatures, which are compelled to follow their own instinct. Whatever its instinct dictates, it does; there is no question of willing. Man is able to act precisely against his own instinct. Man's instinct is to eat food; man's instinct is to be able to satisfy his sexual appetite — these are instinct, are they not? Yet you see a human being who, throughout his entire lifetime, does not satisfy his sexual instinct even once; this is against instinct, this is ascetic discipline. A human being lives for days on end on a single almond; only man is capable of this — man can act against his own instinct.

In short, the power of thought and the power of free choice and the power of will and the power of innovation in man — these are things that have set man apart from the rest of the creatures. Man is altogether something else compared with them. Of course, the materialist scholars too, more or less, in recent times have arrived at this point, saying that man is an astounding species. Although they make no distinction, still — an astounding species. And all these distinctions that exist in man are by virtue of the outpouring[^s15-25] of God's spirit in him; the manifestation of God's spirit in man, as in the verse:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḥijr (15:29) / Sūrat Ṣād (38:72):** "And I breathed into him of My spirit."[^s15-26]

In sum, suddenly God draws the Prophet's attention to something higher than dry, bare creation: the creation of man, the creation of reason, the creation of the power of understanding and perception — and that, from what?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:2):** "From a clinging clot."

From clotted, congealed blood — from where to where? "What is dust and what is the Lord of lords!"[^s15-27] How is it that something which has no soul, no power of movement, no power of understanding, is transformed into an Einstein! Transformed into a great human being, transformed into a Socrates, into a thinker, into a philosopher, into a prophet — how does this happen? Is it anything other than the mighty handiwork of the Lord in the world?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:2):** "Created man from a clinging clot."

In the beginning of revelation, they drew our Prophet's attention to this very important point. Notice, it wishes a transformation to come into being within him; it wishes to put a shoe of steel upon his foot, to place a staff of steel in his hand, so that he may go — to such a degree that fatigue no longer has any meaning for him. From the very start they fashion him with these words.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:2):** "Created man from a clinging clot."

He created man from a clinging clot — that is, from clotted, congealed blood.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:1, 3):** "Read... and your Lord is the most generous."

Read; God is higher than even this. "And your Lord is the most generous" — He is greater and more noble and more bounteous than even this. "Read, and your Lord is the most generous" — read, and your Lord is the most generous of all.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:4):** "Who taught by the pen."

The One who taught by means of the pen. Once again He raises for the Prophet the matter of teaching in man. And this you know: that if there were no pen, if there were no writing, mankind would not have progressed, even so. That which lays the advancement of one generation before the next generation — so that the next generation may use it like a staircase, set its foot upon the experience of the previous generation, and itself construct yet another experience — that is nothing other than the pen. If the scientific discoveries, the scholarly research of the previous generation, were not placed at the disposal of the next generation, the next generation could not add anything to it — know this. If the book of Aristotle had not been at the disposal of Avicenna (Bū ʿAlī Sīnā),[^s15-28] Avicenna would not have become Avicenna — who is higher than Aristotle, for example. If the jurisprudential book of Shaykh Ṭūsī,[^s15-29] for example, or ʿAllāma Ḥillī,[^s15-30] had not been at the disposal of Shaykh Anṣārī[^s15-31] and Mīrzā Shīrāzī[^s15-32] — who are more accomplished and more learned than those forebears, more learned than the scholars of a thousand years before themselves — if those scholars' books had not been at their disposal, if the product of their research had not been at their disposal, then certainly these latter ones would not have reached that level; it is quite clear. The first person has five tomans; he gives it to the second person; with his own five tomans, the second makes it ten tomans; he gives it to the third person, who also has five tomans, and it becomes fifteen tomans. The third person is now wealthier than the first person — he has fifteen tomans. But where does this fifteen tomans come from? If the first person had not existed, would he have this fifteen tomans? If the second person too had not existed, would he still have fifteen tomans? Never — he would have had even that five tomans only by force. It is the product of the past experiences and discoveries of man that reaches later generations — and by what means? By means of the pen, by means of writing and inscription.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:4–5):** "Who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know."

So, higher than the very principle of creation and bringing-into-being, there is something in God.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:3):** "Read, and your Lord is the most generous."

Read, and your Lord is the most generous.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:4–5):** "Who taught by the pen — taught man what he did not know."

Who gave instruction by means of the pen. "Taught man what he did not know" — He taught man the things he did not know. These are blessings of God concerning man. Well, then man ought to give thanks for these blessings. When God has taught man, when God has shown man the way, has given man the pen, has made man rational and given him instruction — then man ought to go toward the loftiest summit; then man ought not to have a single instant of decline. Man ought no longer to have any backsliding and misery — is it so? The next verse answers; it is as though it confronts an imagining of this kind: that if God creates and nurtures humanity in this way, and gives it the pen, and shows it grace, and treats it with such generosity — if it is so, then mankind ought to have no misery and waywardness and corruption and error and the like — has it turned out so?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:6):** "No indeed!"

No, it is not so as you suppose and as you speak. The state of mankind — how is it? No; rather:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:6–7):** "No indeed! Truly man transgresses when he sees himself self-sufficient."

The transgression of mankind, the rebellions and the arrogance of feeble human beings and of the tyrants (ṭāghūts) against the merciful ones, their drawing up of battle-lines — these made mankind wretched. These transgressions did not let mankind reach guidance. These transgressions and arrogances and rebellions did not let mankind make the fullest use of the Lordly reserves, and be nurtured and reared as God had wished, and become what God had willed for him. The transgressors did not allow it. When they saw themselves free of need, they transgressed, they rebelled, they departed from the path of God. Notice — once again the Prophet is being fashioned. Attention to the grace of God, attention to the nobility of God, attention to the teaching of God, attention to the fact that God is the Creator, attention to the fact that God is the Teacher, attention to the fact that God is the Most Generous, and attention to the fact that humanity has not reached where it ought to reach — it has not reached, as conveyed by "No indeed!" — and attention to the fact that the fault is the transgressors'.

And transgression arises from the sense of being free of need. Self-sufficiency, wealth, the amassing of riches, the heaping up of treasures and fortunes, raises up necks; and when the necks are raised, when un-divine powers come into being and take shape, then it is that mankind no longer reaches that destination. Notice — these are the inspirations of God at the outset of the rousing; these are that very flame which set the Prophet of God ablaze, set him afire.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:6):** "No indeed!"

No, it is not as you suppose. You imagine and expect that mankind has reached a state of being set in order? No!

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:6):** "Truly man transgresses."

Man assuredly transgresses and is arrogant.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:7):** "When he sees himself self-sufficient."

— that he sees himself as free of need.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:8):** "Truly, to your Lord is the return."

Will these transgressors ultimately be successful? No! Again, the return is to your Lord; the end belongs to God; the conclusion of the affair is in favor of God and the divine front. That very path which the Lord of the world has appointed — in the end, mankind will, upon that very path, reach the destination, there is no doubt:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96:8):** "Truly, to your Lord is the return."

And then there are other matters in this noble Sūra. In sum, notice with what truths these matters bring about, in our Prophet, the revolution and transformation — and it is this very transformation that is reflected in the very text of society.

Again, in the Sūra "By the Star," of which we cited a portion of its verses here, there is an allusion to this very inner transformation of the Prophet — and there are, of course, many other verses, which I did not see the need to cite all of here. But since the discussion is a very precise, subtle, and sweet one, and since I myself, in the discussions I had studied, had not come across this point being attended to with precision, I wished to explain this point a little more, since knowing it is useful for acting as well.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:1):** "In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Ever-Merciful. By the star when it sets."[^s15-33]

By the star, at the time it sets.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:2):** "Your companion has not gone astray nor erred."

Your companion and comrade has not gone astray, nor inclined toward falsehood. This pertains to the matter of the Miʿrāj (the Ascension), as it is found in many of the commentaries. Although it alludes to the inner transformation of the Prophet and to the state of receiving revelation, yet the occasion of the Sūra "By the Star" is this: that the Prophet, when he would recount the episodes of the night journey, the journey of the Ascension, these people would not listen; the verse is speaking in this context.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:3):** "Nor does he speak from caprice."

He does not speak out of whim.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:4):** "It is naught but a revelation revealed."

It is nothing but a revelation that has reached him from the side of the Lord.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:5):** "Taught to him by one mighty in power."

He has been taught by that one of surpassing power, that one possessing great strength. (I do not know what I chose in this translation that I made here, for "one mighty in power" (shadīd al-quwā). "Shadīd al-quwā" means the one who is very powerful. Hand me one of these papers, please. Yes — "that one of great might has taught him.")

"One mighty in power" means surpassingly powerful.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:6):** "Possessor of soundness/sagacity (dhū mirra)."

That sagacious, wise one — yes; "mirra" meaning sagacity and wisdom and judgment — which the commentators have said is an allusion to Gabriel: that what he (the Prophet) reports, he reports on the authority of Gabriel, and God taught him these things by means of Gabriel.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:6):** "...so he stood upright."

This divine revelation came to him, and he stood erect upon his own feet — "so he stood upright."

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:7):** "And he was on the highest horizon."

And he was on the loftier and higher horizon. "Highest" means the most lofty; the horizon, too, is clear. The Prophet stands upon a loftier horizon.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:8):** "Then he drew near and came down."

Perhaps it can be construed in this way: "And he was on the highest horizon" means the Prophet stood upon a higher level, by which he was able to become a prophet. As we said, the Prophet, before prophethood, lived with reserves more brimming than the ordinary, among ordinary people. "Then he drew near" — then he approached; "and came down" — then he drew nearer. On the supposition that the one meant is the Prophet: that is, the Prophet drew near to God, and nearer — through acts of worship, through ascetic disciplines, through reflections and ponderings, and through the special graces that God had bestowed upon him, his spirit drew near and nearer to God and became ready to receive revelation. Some have said that what is meant by "he drew near" is that Gabriel drew near to the Prophet, and "came down" — and hung over him; that is, he brought himself to the Prophet in order to convey the revelation to him. In any case it makes no difference; but the first meaning seems to us nearer and more apparent. That second meaning too I wrote, "or he drew near and hung down," in parentheses, so that it may allude to the second meaning. The first meaning I myself prefer more.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:9):** "Until he was at the distance of two bows' lengths, or nearer."

The Prophet drew near to God, and so he reached the distance of two bows' lengths, or less. Do not say, "What does 'two bows' lengths' mean, what special significance does it have?" — at the measure of two bows, taking each bow to be one meter, at a distance of two meters from God. Where was God, that the Noble Prophet reached within two meters of Him? No — these are figurative, metaphorical expressions. He drew near to Him, not in respect of place; "two bows' lengths" means he drew very near, very near; the holy spirit of the Prophet drew so near to God, drew so near, that nearer than that is inconceivable for any human being — the very nearest of the near.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:8–10):** "Then he drew near and came down, until he was at the distance of two bows' lengths or nearer, and He revealed to His servant what He revealed."

So God revealed to His servant what He revealed.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:11):** "The heart did not lie about what it saw."

The heart did not lie about what it saw. What are the observations of the Prophet's heart — it did not lie to the Prophet, it saw correctly, it did not see in error.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Najm (53:12):** "Will you then dispute with him about what he saw?"

Will you then dispute with him over what he saw?

These, in any case, are verses from the noble Sūra "By the Star." At the bottom here, too, we have written a sentence that is the subject of tomorrow's discussion. In sum, then, it is after this inner and innermost rousing that the path of the prophet changes and his striving takes on another color, and with continual effort and struggle he strives. He strives that what may come about? That in society and in the very text of human beings' lives, a rising and a transformation may, from the foundation up, be brought into being. After a transformation has come into being within himself, he endeavors to create a transformation in society — and this is precisely the responsibility of messengership, and the Prophet exists for this work.

---

[^s15-1]: Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96), verses 1–8.
[^s15-2]: Aware of and committed to religious and legal (sharʿī) matters.
[^s15-3]: Sūrat al-Naḥl (16), verse 36.
[^s15-4]: Sūrat al-Rūm (30), verse 56.
[^s15-5]: A "rally" (mītīng): a gathering of groups for discussion or for expressing views on social and political matters.
[^s15-6]: "Being-roused" (inbiʿāth), from the root b-ʿ-th, "to be raised up / stirred up."
[^s15-7]: From the root ḥ-ḍ-ḍ, meaning "lowness, debasement" (ḥaḍīḍ).
[^s15-8]: Sūrat al-Baqara (2), verse 156.
[^s15-9]: An idiom (lit. "the work of a one-shāhī, half-penny") signifying something of trifling worth.
[^s15-10]: Drawn from this Persian proverb: "Two hundred maunds of bone are needed to bear a hundred maunds of burden."
[^s15-11]: Mahatma Gandhi (1869–1948 CE), spiritual and political leader of the Indian nation in the cause of freedom from the rule of the British Empire. Influenced by the Brahmanical creed, he lived very simply and emphasized non-violent struggle. In one initiative, Gandhi boycotted the consumption of British goods. After the independence of India, he was assassinated by an extremist Hindu who opposed broad relations with the Muslim country of Pakistan.
[^s15-12]: Patrice Lumumba (1925–1961 CE), leader of the anti-colonial movement of the Congo against Belgium. After reaching the office of prime minister, through a plot of the American and Belgian intelligence services he was dismissed by the president, and after a period in hiding he was killed by Colonel Mobutu Sese Seko.
[^s15-13]: Sūrat al-Shuʿarāʾ (26), verse 20.
[^s15-14]: Please refer to the mimeographed handout at the end of the session.
[^s15-15]: Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā (93), verses 1 to 8.
[^s15-16]: The fatra: the period between two intervals (of revelation).
[^s15-17]: The Ḥilf al-Fuḍūl, or "Pact of the Chivalrous," is a pact that the Prophet, at the age of twenty, concluded with a number of the young men of Mecca, in order to support the oppressed and the strangers who entered the city and fell victim to the oppression of the powerful.
[^s15-18]: Al-Burhān fī Tafsīr al-Qurʾān (Sayyid Hāshim ibn Sulaymān al-Baḥrānī, d. 1107 AH), under the exegesis of Sūrat al-ʿAlaq.
[^s15-19]: Negating "mā": placed before a verb, it makes its meaning negative. Interrogative "mā": placed before a verb, it makes its meaning a question.
[^s15-20]: Sūrat al-ʿAlaq (96), verses 1 to 5.
[^s15-21]: ʿAbd al-Raḥmān Jāmī.
[^s15-22]: Sharḥ Nahj al-Balāgha of Ibn Abī al-Ḥadīd, Letter Nine, the story of the Battle of Badr.
[^s15-23]: Jundab ibn Junāda, known by the kunya Abū Dharr, of the tribe of Ghifār, was the fourth or fifth person to become a Muslim. When he heard the news of the appearance of the new Prophet, he hastened to Mecca and embraced Islam, then returned to his own people and, after the Battle of the Trench, emigrated to Medina. After the passing of the Messenger of God, Abū Dharr was among the supporters of the Commander of the Faithful and refused to pledge allegiance to Abū Bakr. During the caliphate of ʿUthmān, with the lavish gifts of the government to the Banū Umayya, Abū Dharr turned to criticism against the government; for this reason he was exiled to Syria (Shām) and then to Rabadha. Abū Dharr al-Ghifārī died there in the year 31 or 32 AH.
[^s15-24]: He was among the first to embrace Islam, and Islamic historians have counted him the seventh Muslim. Miqdād is among the eminent companions; on account of the oppression of the Meccan polytheists he emigrated to Abyssinia. He then returned to Mecca and set out for Medina, and took part in all the battles of the Prophet of Islam. He was among the famous archers. After the passing of the Messenger of God, Miqdād was among the few companions of the Commander of the Faithful. At the time of the people's allegiance to ʿUthmān, Miqdād rose in opposition. He finally died in the year 33 AH in a place about one farsang from Medina, was transferred to Medina, and was buried in the Baqīʿ cemetery.
[^s15-25]: From the root f-y-ḍ, "outpouring, overflowing" (fayaḍān).
[^s15-26]: Sūrat al-Ḥijr (15), verse 29; Sūrat Ṣād (38), verse 72.
[^s15-27]: "Where is dust and where is the Lord of lords!" — an idiom for the immense distance between man and the Lord.
[^s15-28]: Abū ʿAlī Sīnā (Avicenna) (370–428 AH) was born in a village near Bukhara. With extraordinary talent he gained mastery in various sciences, including the rational sciences and medicine. He authored works in various fields, among them al-Qānūn in Islamic medicine and al-Shifāʾ in philosophy, theology, the natural sciences, and logic.
[^s15-29]: Shaykh Abū Jaʿfar Muḥammad ibn Ḥasan ibn ʿAlī al-Ṭūsī (385–460 AH), known as Shaykh al-Ṭāʾifa, was born in Ṭūs. To complete his studies he went to Iraq and studied under masters such as Shaykh al-Mufīd and Sayyid Murtaḍā ʿAlam al-Hudā. Many works remain from Shaykh al-Ṭūsī, among them the two books Tahdhīb al-Aḥkām and al-Istibṣār, which are among the four principal ḥadīth books of the Shīʿa.
[^s15-30]: Ḥasan ibn Yūsuf al-Ḥillī (648–726 AH) was born into a family of learning. He studied under masters such as Muḥaqqiq al-Ḥillī and Khwāja Naṣīr al-Dīn al-Ṭūsī, and through the mastery he attained in various sciences such as jurisprudence and ḥadīth, theology and philosophy, mathematics and geometry, he was given the title "ʿAllāma" (the most learned). ʿAllāma Ḥillī, after the death of Muḥaqqiq Ḥillī, attained the leadership and religious authority (marjaʿiyya) of the Shīʿa at the age of twenty-eight. Through the ʿAllāma's debates with the scholars of the Sunnis, Öljaitü, the Mongol ruler of Iran, adopted the Shīʿa school and chose the name Muḥammad Khudābanda for himself.
[^s15-31]: Shaykh Murtaḍā (1214–1281 AH), a descendant of Jābir ibn ʿAbdullāh al-Anṣārī, was born in the city of Dezful. Before his birth, his mother had seen in a dream Imam al-Ṣādiq giving her a gilded Qur'an. After beginning his studies in Dezful, he set out for Karbala and benefited from the presence of scholars such as Shaykh Mūsā Kāshif al-Ghiṭāʾ, Sayyid Muḥammad Mujāhid, and Ḥājj Mullā Aḥmad Narāqī. After the passing of the late Ṣāḥib al-Jawāhir, he assumed the religious authority and leadership of the Shīʿa. His jurisprudential works, such as the book al-Makāsib, are still a reference for the learning of students and scholars.
[^s15-32]: The late Ayatollah Muḥammad Ḥasan Ḥusaynī Shīrāzī (1230–1312 AH) was among the distinguished students of the late Shaykh Anṣārī. After the passing of Shaykh Anṣārī in the year 1281 AH, he attained the universal religious authority. With the migration of Mīrzā Shīrāzī from Najaf to Samarra, the religious seminary of Samarra was founded. Among the influential measures of Mīrzā Shīrāzī is the proclamation of the fatwa prohibiting tobacco. This fatwa, and the people's compliance with it, brought about the annulment of the Régie concession between the Iranian government and England.
[^s15-33]: Sūrat al-Najm (53), verses 1 to 12.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 16: The Social Resurgence of Prophethood

*رستاخیز اجتماعی نبوت — delivered Friday, 12 Mehr 1353 / 17 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394 (1974)*

Today's recitation is drawn from two places: one from the blessed Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ, and the other from the blessed Sūrat al-Ṣaff. So that some of the brothers who have not yet found a place in the prayer hall may be able to find somewhere, look at these verses on the printed sheets while I read the Arabic text once.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ (28:1–6):**
> "In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. Ṭā Sīn Mīm. These are the verses of the clear Book. We recite to you something of the account of Moses and Pharaoh, in truth, for a people who believe. Indeed Pharaoh exalted himself in the land and divided its people into factions, oppressing a group among them: slaughtering their sons and sparing their women. Indeed he was of the corrupters. And We desired to show favor to those who had been oppressed in the land, and to make them leaders, and to make them the heirs, and to establish them firmly in the land, and to show Pharaoh and Hāmān and their hosts, through them, the very thing they were dreading."[^s16-1]

The subject that we have in mind to discuss, drawing upon these verses and the verses of Sūrat al-Ṣaff, is the matter for which the best title and heading is exactly the one we wrote on these sheets: the social resurgence of prophethood.

Our task, of course, is to explain what the social resurgence of prophethood is. You heard some of the preliminaries to this discussion on previous days, when we said that the Bi'thah — the commissioning, the arising, the coming into being of a fervor, an effort, and a stirring within the inner being of the Prophet — begins, with the onset of revelation, in the heart of the Prophet. This servant who is God's chosen one, whom God — in view of the deep and abundant capacities he possesses — has appointed for a mission, a responsibility, a commitment of such magnitude and weight. After divine revelation took place upon such a servant, a surging and a stirring comes into being in his spirit and in his inner being; a revolution is created within the very spirit of the Prophet. In his ordinary life, in his comings and goings, in his social orientation — in short, throughout his entire being — a resurgence rises up, a revolution comes into being. The man after revelation is not that man of yesterday or of two hours before.

Some writers who do not believe in the Prophet of Islam — among these orientalists, some of whom may even be impartial, though some are certainly biased — write, regarding the life of the Prophet, that throughout his life before his Bi'thah the Prophet was studying, thinking, reflecting,[^s16-2] and that it was these reflections which brought him to the revolution he created. This claim, with its apparent shape, is false and a lie — unless they mean something else.

The Prophet did not arrive at the Islamic revolution and call through reflections and meditations at all. After this call, this revelation, this arising came into being in him, he became another being, he became another human, he took on another state. And not only that Prophet — all the prophets. Moses, while he had taken his wife and child and was heading out on an ordinary journey, in the middle of the desert, at that critical moment, when divine revelation descended upon him — Moses forgot everything else. Moses, at the moment he senses the mission, is a human being other than the human of a moment before; within him another fervor, another surging, another stirring comes into being. The one who makes this claim is someone who has no belief in prophethood, the Bi'thah, revelation, and connection to the unseen world in the case of the prophets. It is a claim that the unbelieving or biased orientalist writes and makes, and the ill-informed and non-biased researcher repeats it too in his book, in his writing, in his speech.

In any case, the Bi'thah thus means a stirring, a revolution, a transformation, a change, an upheaval, a resurgence — whatever expression you wish to line up — in the being of the chosen Prophet. After this revolution came into being in him, in the Prophet, then it is time for this very revolution to be carried out in the external environment.

That same transformation which was created in the spirit of the Prophet must, in some form, in some particular way, be carried out in the very fabric of the reality of society — and this is exactly the matter we have named the social resurgence of prophethood. Of course, after we explain this transformation today, God willing, then it will be time for other discussions concerning prophethood. Again, one of those discussions — which happens to pertain to tomorrow, and perhaps the day after — is a very important discussion: it concerns the goal of the mission and prophethood — for what aim, with what purpose, for what work this social resurgence is carried out. That will be undertaken over the course of one or two discussions. And this, of course, in turn entails other matters, which, if life and opportunity are granted, I will present to the brothers and sisters in the days to come.

Now, regarding this resurgence that we conceive and imagine for the Prophet, what can be said? Today in the world the word "revolution," with the meaning it carries in the new and modern cultures, is a meaningful word, a clear and significant word. Of course, it is quite clear that what is meant by the word "revolution" is a transformation and a deep, fundamental change from the root in a society. The word "revolution" does not, by definite necessity, contain conflict; the word "revolution" does not, by definite necessity, contain bloodshed and killing; it does not, by definite necessity, contain dispute and clash. A dispute may arise, a clash may come into being — but the word "revolution" does not carry the meaning of dispute and clash.

The meaning of revolution is this — if we want to explain it to you brothers through a very small, simple, and tangible example: suppose that this mosque, in its condition, its building, its walls, its structure, is of a particular form. They made the foundations and footings and bases here according to, suppose, a rectangular building bounded on the east at such-and-such a limit, on the west at such-and-such a limit — the hall of the mosque's prayer chamber, without internal partitions. That laying of the foundations and footings beneath these walls and bases was made for the sake of these things. If, instead of a mosque hall and prayer chamber, this were meant to become, suppose, a residential building, then certainly the foundation of this building would have been poured differently, the footings of the building would have been laid differently. If partitions were to be made here, inside this prayer hall, with five, ten, twenty rooms appearing in it — as an inn, for example — then surely the foundation and footings here would have had to be laid differently. Now, because it has been readied for this work, because it has been built entirely for the purpose of a gathering, the footings and foundation and the rubble-grouting[^s16-3] and the wall and base are of a particular form.

If it were decided to demolish a building that has been built and finished and completed — suppose, for instance, a hotel — or to convert it into a mosque; suppose they wanted to convert a bank building into a lecture hall; or wanted to convert a residential house into a center that is not for family residence — what would they have to do? They would have to change all the foundations and substructures and bases and underpinnings. They cannot say: we will give it a coat of paint, take it out of the form of being a house, and make it a mosque. No! Such a thing cannot be done. They cannot say: we will gather up these middle walls, suppose, so that the National Bank becomes such-and-such mosque, for the gathering of Muslims and prayer and worship. Such a thing cannot be done. Why? Because the manner of laying the building's foundation was not made for a mosque. For this building to be made into a mosque requires that a special grouting, a special foundation-laying, a special walling, a special partitioning, a special roofing be carried out. If they want to convert a building that was built for one purpose into a building built for another purpose entirely contrary to that first purpose — to convert this first building into the second building — they inevitably have to carry out a revolution. What does revolution mean? It means that they change the bases, substructures, foundations, main sections, frames, and essential underpinnings of this structure into other frames, other underpinnings, other walls, other bodies, other limbs. This is what is called revolution.

For example, suppose a society — that is, fifty thousand people, five hundred thousand people, fifty million population, it makes no difference — a society means that number of people who live together with one course, with one general program; although it has a top and a bottom, the path is altogether one path and is bound together in one social direction. This is called a society, a unit. This society, which consists of fifty thousand, five hundred thousand, five million, fifty million people, can be built in two ways — pay close attention. When I say two ways, in general there are two ways it can be built, though there are also various kinds and sorts within these two.

In general there are two ways a population can be built, a social form can come into being. One way is that among the fifty thousand, or the fifty million population who live in a certain place, a class of people, a minority of people, are the rulers and commanders and dominators and controllers and masters of all the affairs of the rest of the people. They determine the people's path, they lay down the people's laws; if the people act contrary to their wishes, they take severe revenge upon them; if the people say to them, "You have an eyebrow above your eye" [i.e., dare to object], they discipline them severely; if some event arises within this society, they turn that event to their own benefit, even if it be to the detriment of the rest of the people; if it is decreed that this community, this population, should be struck by some calamity, they make the people their shield against the calamity and sit themselves off to one side. This is one kind of society. The basis in the foundation and structure of a society is precisely this: whether or not there is class difference in this society.

If we suppose a society that has class difference — class difference, although the term sounds pompous to some people's ears, is exactly what I said; it was very simple, you saw how easy its meaning is — if in a society there was class difference, meaning that the people of the society were not all equal in terms of rights, were not all on one level in terms of the possibilities and advantages of life; a group were able to take more, take better, speak better and bully and do exactly what they want; and a larger group were forced to keep their eyes on the hands of those people, to be at their command, to stand before them with hands folded at the waist, to prostrate in the dust before them — if a society came into existence in this form, this is called a class society. In this society, the economy too is a class economy; in this society, the government too is to the benefit of the upper classes, is class-based; in this society, basic rights too are to the benefit of the upper classes — that is, they are class-based. This is one kind of society.

The society of a number of people, from five thousand to fifty million, is sometimes in this form, and sometimes it is not in this form. So how is it? Among these fifty million people who are living in this society, no one has the right to bully anyone else; not only is there no class higher than the rest of the people — no, no one at all! You cannot find even a single person in this entire society of whom, if you said, "Sir, why did you do such-and-such a thing?" he would say, "I felt like it." "I felt like it" does not exist in this society. No one has domination, mastery, bullying, brutishness, usurpation of rights, theft, and slapping-on-the-head over anyone else; and in turn, no one experiences, before anyone else, abasement, smallness, weakness, submission to force, being wronged — these have no meaning for him. All the people in that society, from five thousand to fifty and five hundred million, are obedient to the command of one power, and that power is higher than the power of humans and of man. Who is it? It is God. This too is one kind.

We have two kinds of social structure. There is one kind of social structure in which the people, all of them or the majority of them, are slaves and captives before other humans; and we have one kind of social structure and edifice in which the people, all of them, are free from captivity to other powers. Just as in the first kind, when you reckon it up, the economy is to the benefit of that same upper class, the government is in the hands of that same upper class, the basic rights and advantages of society are exclusive to that upper class or to whoever is close to them — likewise, in this second society, the classless society, the society in which no one bullies, imposes upon, or dominates anyone, in this society the economy is communal, the government in the sense of governance is communal and is in the hands of all. Basic rights are communal, are for all; in short, whatever is good is for all, whatever is bad concerns all. If discomfort arises, all share in it; if a good thing comes about, all share in it — and the world is a paradise.

Two kinds of society can be conceived. These two kinds of society that we have described and explained for you, we have had both kinds throughout history. (I must keep the points short and brief, because this discussion is one of those richly clamorous and content-laden social and religious discussions, and if a speaker were to set it forth in detail and explanation — especially in a gathering and assembly like this one, where the level of thinking varies greatly, and one wished everyone or the majority to understand — it would require several hours of discussion. I am constantly trying to lower my hand, to bring down the level of the matter, so that the majority might at least grasp something of it.)

These two kinds of society: one kind, the class society in which there is oppression, there is injustice, there is class difference, there is exploitation,[^s16-4] there is domineering, there is brutishness, there is discrimination; and one kind, the classless society in which there is justice, there is humanity, there is freedom — I lean especially on freedom for a reason: some of the classless societies named in the world have, by their own claim, everything minus freedom. A classless society in which there is welfare, and especially freedom; in which no one is anyone's servant, in which no one is anyone's slave, in which no one is subject to anyone else's word. Both these kinds of society have existed in history, both.

The societies of the first type, in which there is discrimination, are the societies that the Qayāṣirah and the Akāsirah[^s16-5] of the world and the tyrants of history brought into being. And the societies of the second type — the prosperous, free, non-discriminatory, humane societies — are the very societies that the great divine prophets brought into being throughout history. You say: did the prophets really form societies? In reply we say: yes, the prophets formed societies. In the Qur'an the signs of the societies that the prophets formed are abundant: the affair of Solomon, the affair of Saul (Ṭālūt), the affair of Moses and his coming to the Holy Land, the affair of Moses and his demand to bring out the Children of Israel — where did he want to take them? He wanted to take them so as to form a society and a virtuous city. These are the subject of discussions that I may touch on tomorrow and the day after; for now I wanted only this much to be a brief reference.

We have two kinds of society, and both kinds have existed in history. The bad type — the one that reason deems bad and that humanity condemns — the anti-religious political powers of the world and history have always had; the good type, the divine and spiritual powers of history have always had — that is, the prophets. When the prophets come into a society, for what do they come? They come to convert that first type into the second type. This is the spirit of the substance of our discussion today.

Most often the conception of the prophets is a different conception. People imagine that the prophets, when they appear in a society, are like a wise, learned, eminent sage — a mountain of knowledge — who comes into society, takes a house, sits in a corner so that the people may come in groups to benefit from the harvest of his grace and his knowledge. They imagine the Prophet to be such a person, such a being. The Prophet, for example — suppose he enters a society — Abraham the Friend of God (Khalīl Allāh), say, or Moses the Interlocutor of God (Kalīm Allāh), enters a society and takes a house, now whether dignified or small or large, and sits there, and has set aside an hour for meeting believers and non-believers, and whoever comes to him he enjoins to good and forbids from evil: "People, fear God; people, God exists." He argues with them, debates with them; he reforms a number of people, and then departs from the world. They imagine the Prophet to be such a thing.

The Prophet is not of this sort. When the Prophet is commissioned in a society — that is, is made to arise — and, as I explained, a resurgence comes into being in his inner being and in his spirit, then, when he comes into society, he is a man who knows not his head from his foot, a man who has no rest and no repose, a human being who has been turned into a blazing, searing whirling torch[^s16-6] — when he comes into society, he casts a glance at the condition of society, he sees, and — ah! — this structure is a wrong structure, this edifice was badly built, was wrongly founded; its walls and its footings and its grouting were laid incorrectly and contrary to the architectural method of human nature (fiṭrah). He understands that this must be changed; he understands that this edifice must be converted into a good edifice. Meaning what? Meaning he understands that this society, this class society, this society in which there is discrimination, there is oppression, there is disorder, there is inhumanity, must be changed and converted into a tawḥīdī (monotheistic) society.

What is a tawḥīdī society, sir? We spoke of it in the discussion of Tawhid. We pointed out that Tawhid is the negation of classes. Divine Tawhid means the power and government of God; divine Tawhid means that everything — law, custom, regulations, manners, culture — must be inspired from the side of God. Divine Tawhid means that all people are servants of God and that alone, and are not the servant of anyone else; that the servants are free from the servitude of other servants. When the Prophet enters society, he enters with this idea, with this aim, with this thought: to transform that class society, to demolish it, to break it apart, and to bring into being a tawḥīdī, classless, non-discriminatory, non-oppressive society under the government of the Lord of the worlds. The Prophet comes for this work.

If I wanted to give examples of the famous figures and personalities other than the Prophet, I would surely have to cite these same famous figures whom those who study know more or less; only I do not like to insert non-religious names into religious discussions, and to want to define their limits, some time might pass. He comes into society to transform it — I do not say he comes for bloodshed, never; I do not say he comes for slaughter, never; I do not say he comes to set people at one another's throats, never. It is not so. As I said, the word "revolution," the word "transformation," the word "change," does not even contain the flowing of blood from one person's nose. In all the ordinary affairs of life, bloodshed may possibly occur, and it is also possible that it may not. For what did the First World War come about? A small assassination against one gentleman, one master, in one corner of the world, dragged the whole world into the maw of death and war and fire — was it not so? The crown prince of Austria and I-don't-know-what. A small assassination may set a whole world on fire. Of course, in a revolution too there may be bloodshed, and it is also possible that there may not be. The word "revolution" does not contain bloodshed, killing, division, or setting people at one another's throats.

If the Prophet, when he entered society, when he uttered his revolutionary speech — that is, when he said to Pharaoh that you must not sit there and must not put the Children of Israel under so much pressure and must not build different social classes — when he said this to Pharaoh, if Pharaoh had said, "Certainly, I am ready, I hear your command, I will come down from there," then the Prophet would begin, with his own powerful, skilled hand,[^s16-7] to build society, and not a ripple of water would be stirred. The reason conflict and clash arise in the revolutions of the prophets — as God says in the Qur'an —

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:146):** "And how many a prophet there was alongside whom many devoted servants of God fought."[^s16-8]

— or jihad is legislated in Islam, the reason is that that affluent class, that class toward whom the sharp edge of the revolution is directed and from whom privileges are being taken away, is not willing to accept this revolution. Otherwise — if he became reformed, if he truly understood the truth, if, like many of the great political, economic, and financial figures of history who suddenly, under a beautiful and good transformation in their spirits, turned into ordinary human beings — all those who stood against the prophets and against the call of the prophets, if they had submitted, had come down from that summit, the Prophet would in no way be compelled to resort to slaughter and conflict and such things.

So the Prophet, when he comes into a society, comes for this transformation. The Prophet comes to create change. Meaning, to do what? Meaning, for example, in the Arabia of the Prophet's time, to say: why must the sources of wealth in this society, the springs of surging riches, be in the hands of a minority of aristocrats? Why? Why must slaves and bondsmen be those who toil under the whims of the masters? Why must the weak be the burden-bearers and beasts of burden of the inclinations of the Walīd ibn Mughīrahs and the Abū Jahls and the Abū Lahabs? Why? Why should these not all be on one level?

«al-nāsu sawāsiyatun ka-asnāni l-mushṭ»[^s16-9] — take a comb in your hand: from the first tooth of the comb to the last tooth of the comb, do you see one of these teeth of the comb enjoying any greater privilege? All at one limit, all at one level, all of one size, all of one shape, equal — and how beautiful! Humanity is precisely this. «al-nāsu sawāsiyatun» — humans are equal — «ka-asnāni l-mushṭ» — like the teeth of a comb. This is the call of the Prophet: «kullukum min Ādam, wa Ādamu min turāb»[^s16-10] — humans are all the children of Adam. That gentleman who has raised a palace in some corner of Mecca must eat well, drink well, have trade reserved for him, have others be his slaves and camel-drivers, take the profit himself, and chart the destiny of Mecca himself. That gentleman, with these traits, is no different at all from that lowly servant in his house; they have no difference at all, in terms of human element and essence they are equal, they have no difference whatsoever. Why must the field for that man's benefits be so wide, while this wretch cannot even flap a single wing in this cage? Why? The field must be open before all equally.

You see, these are the claims of the Prophet. In all the societies in which a prophet appeared, he appeared with this claim; that is, he came to convert society from a wrong form, an unbalanced form, a form coupled with oppression and injustice and tyranny, into a balanced form, a beautiful form, a just form. This is one matter. The resurgence of prophethood is this. Every prophet who was commissioned and appeared in any society appeared with this aim — know this. In general, there has never been a prophet who came merely to expound for the people a number of secondary matters and minor details of life.

Of course, the great prophets, whom we call the prophets of firm resolve (ulū al-ʿazm) and by which title we know them, these were the poles of the divine revolutions; and the other prophets — some came to take up the continuation of their revolution, some came to complete their work, some came to bring their work to fruition, some came to turn back the regressions that came about after the fruition of their revolutions, and to bring about a new revolution once again. This is the same work that the executors (awṣiyāʾ) of our Prophet performed after the passing of our Prophet: the work of the Commander of the Faithful (Amīr al-Muʾminīn), the work of Imam Husayn, the work of the other Imams, the work of the scholars of the Ummah of Islam, and finally the work of the Lord of the Age (Ṣāḥib al-Zamān), the Master of the Era (Walī al-ʿAṣr) — God's blessings and peace be upon him.

(Ṣalawāt in unison. We are not usually in the habit of having the gentlemen send aloud ṣalawāt in the middle of our speech without our requesting it ourselves. Now that a murmur has arisen — at last, without our permission, the gentlemen have sent ṣalawāt — let them send one ṣalawāt at my request too. Ṣalawāt in unison.)

Another matter regarding this resurgence that they should pay attention to (which we wrote on this paper, on these sheets and printed copies): We ask, sir, what is the problem with the order of ignorance (jāhilī) remaining established? With this just order not being established? This Prophet who comes, who suffers so much, who throws himself into water and fire to convert this wrong condition into a beautiful and good condition — what would be the harm if he did not do this work? What would be the harm if he let it remain in that same form? Why is that condition bad? Why is that condition wrong? What right is there here that you take as your measure? "al-ḥaqqu li-man ghalab"[^s16-11] — right belongs to whoever prevails; whoever became dominant, whoever obtained power, may it be sweet and wholesome to him; the one who gets slapped on the head, to hell with him, he had only to refuse, he had only not to be there; now that he is there, let them carry out whatever plundering raids[^s16-12] they wish — why does the Prophet throw himself into water and fire? Why?

It is another question. You may say, sir, no one asks this question. Yes — by tongue no one asks this question, but in the inner being and the spirit of most people who, faced with disorders, do not have the prophetic stance, this question exists, and an answer has not been given to this question. The reason is this: the Prophet does not move without cause, the Prophet does not throw himself into water and fire without reason. What he sees of the existing condition of his time is a false condition, contrary to human and universal nature (fiṭrah); what he wants to carry out is the condition of right (ḥaqq) — that is, in accordance with universal and human nature.

Right (ḥaqq) and falsehood (bāṭil): these two words that you encounter very often throughout the Qur'an, in the instances that are named — the drawing up of battle lines between right and falsehood — are embodied and specified in dozens of verses of the Qur'an. What does right and falsehood mean? We mentioned a point here that is the explanation of right and falsehood. We said, sir: the human being — this being who, with this form, with this stature, is walking about in the world — was made and built with certain characteristics. The human being has certain characteristics, has certain possibilities, has certain needs; in short, he is a being who came into existence with a certain measure of his own special characteristics and possibilities. Keep this in mind here. This world too, in which the human being lives, was again structured with certain conditions, with certain characteristics, possessing a certain movement and toward a certain direction. This world that you look at and see with your eyes — each of its things seems to have fallen into some separate corner: its sun is millions of leagues away from such-and-such a planet; its human, its plant, its animal apparently have no connection with one another. In the view of a God-worshipping, God-knowing person, this is an indivisible unity.[^s16-13] This entire world is one thing; the parts of this world are the parts of one body. Just as in one body there is a similar movement among all its parts, so too in this world it is the same.

In a human being, the stomach does one task, the eye does one task, the liver does one task, the brain and nerves do one task — but the sum total of all these tasks is one single, shared thing, and what is that? It is the staying alive of this human being, the moving of this human being, the striving of this human being, the continuing of the life of this human being. The sum total of all the movements that you observe in this world too — from above and below and this sphere and the other spheres — their sum total comes out as one single thing, comes out as one single movement. (A little attention is needed; I said that day too: do not always expect, in these discussions, to drink something down like running water and benefit from it; consider these matters somewhat in need of digestion, in need of activity; reflect and think on them a little.) All the other non-human beings, because they have no consciousness, because they have no will and choice, are willy-nilly advancing along this path, along this general movement; it is the human being who possesses choice, it is the human being who can be obstinate and withdraw himself from this caravan of the world's things and move in the opposite direction. It is only the human being who can fail to follow this caravan, this caravan of the world which we said has one single direction — fail to set out and fail to advance; only the human being. The rest of the beings, even if at some time they go out of their natural course, it is the human who takes them out of it. If they split the uranium and, instead of using it to cure thousands of ailments, make an atom bomb and drop it and kill millions of humans — the human does this too; the uranium itself never transgresses or oversteps this natural course of the world; it moves along this ordinary path. If this substance LSD,[^s16-14] which ought to be used for the healing of ailments, is turned into a narcotic substance and given into the mouth of some youth, making him dizzy and oblivious for hours to what is going on around him — this is the fault of the human, not the fault of that plant itself, not the fault of that substance itself, not the fault of that thing itself.

In this caravan that we have depicted for you — this caravan in which the sun is one of the parts of this caravan, the earth is one of the parts of this caravan, the galaxies and the small and great beings of this vast world are each elements and parts of this vast caravan — they are all going their way with heads bowed, all are going; it is only this being, the human, who is a mobile and active being, this being who possesses choice, who sometimes bends his own path crooked, turns back in the opposite direction, goes left and right. Sometimes too he drags along the hand of other speechless beings and takes them with him: he takes uranium with him, he takes LSD with him, he takes morphine with him. Morphine, which ought to come out to the benefit of man, comes out to the detriment of man; that so-called plant which ought to give life to man gives death to man. It is not the thing itself that does this — this too is done by the same human who has turned back from the path and gone out of the course; the human possesses this characteristic.

Because the human possesses this characteristic — he has choice, he has will, he can change his own path — because it is so, therefore it is necessary that a law be set for him, that a line of travel be specified for him, that he be told, "Sir, you must move along this line of travel so that you have set out behind this caravan. If you transgress this line of travel, if you go out of this map that we draw for you, know that you have gone out of the course of this caravan." Meaning what? Meaning a law is necessary for this human being. This law that is in accordance with the line of travel of the collective movement of the beings of the world — what is the name of this law? If you guessed it? Right (ḥaqq). Right means this. Right means that law which is conformable with the nature of the world; and because it is conformable with the nature of the world and the universe, it is also conformable with the nature of the human; because the human too is one of the parts of this world, because the human too is a part of the parts of this great body; because it is so, because this law is conformable with the nature of the human and the universe, therefore it also comes out to his good and welfare.

What is falsehood (bāṭil)? Falsehood is that course, that law, that way and custom which is laid down, fabricated, and carried out contrary to the nature of the universe and the nature of the human. Falsehood is what the brutes and the satans and those who want to deviate from this course make. The prophets always bring right so that falsehood may be removed. That society which Pharaoh makes, dividing people into several classes, putting pressure on one class of them, or keeping one class affluent and oppressing the others — that condition and that order and those regulations and that social form is the form of falsehood. The prophets come to overturn that form of falsehood. They come to dismantle it, to do away with it, to give its place to right. So when the Prophet throws himself into water and fire, it is for the sake of right; because right is the thing that he says and that he wants. It is for the sake of right that he places himself in such discomfort and exposes himself to hardship. It is for the sake of substituting right in place of falsehood that the prophets do not for a moment set down their striving, do not forget it, are not for one moment idle and do not take their ease. The matter is the substitution of right in place of falsehood; this is the interpretation and analysis of the work of the prophets.

We sum up the matter: the prophets — in order to specify the being-class-based, the being-oppressive, the being-exploitative, the being-inhumane, and negative points of this sort, the best word is the word "jāhilī"; jāhiliyyah means the unjust, un-natural (ghayr-fiṭrī), inhumane order — they want to convert this jāhilī order into a divine order, into a tawḥīdī social mold; they want to convert it such that society becomes the domain of the government of God, not the domain of the government of caprices and lusts. This is the outcome of the work of the prophets. The prophets come to convert a wrong social order into a correct social order; their slogan of Tawhid too is for this very reason, their struggles against the false deities (ṭawāghīt) too are for this same purpose. The struggles of the false deities against them too are for this same purpose. This too is a chapter of this continuous discourse; one day, God willing, it may be that we will set forth who the classes opposed to the prophets were, and what were the motives of their opposition and resistance.

The verses that I chose from Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ to be recited describe this very condition — the jāhilī condition, the condition of the Pharaonic government and the Pharaonic society — and the condition which Moses wanted to establish in place of the Pharaonic condition. Along with these two conditions being specified in contrast to one another, a glad tiding is also given to those who move in the direction of the Mosaic condition. The glad tiding is given that ultimately the will of the Lord has settled upon this: that the Mosaic ones of the world — that is, the tawḥīdī ones of the world and the divine ones of the world — shall be successful and victorious. Its reason too is clear. This too is again the subject of another discussion among those titles that we have in mind to set forth in turn: the outcome of prophethood — what is the final end of the work of prophethood? Conquest and victory. And why? Because it is in accordance with the nature and disposition of the universe and the human.

In summary, in these few verses that we recite here it is mentioned, and these are what is intended, so now we present their translation to the gentlemen. (I beg you to look at these sheets, and whoever has a Qur'an in his hand, let him look at the Qur'an.)

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ (28:1):** "In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. Ṭā Sīn Mīm."[^s16-15]

This cipher at the opening of the surah is, of course, among the very minor discussions that may be raised concerning some of the verses of the Qur'an — namely, what these ciphers are, as the commentators have said. We have no business with that.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ (28:2):** "These are the verses of the clear Book."

These are the verses of the clear and clarifying Book.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ (28:3):** "We recite to you something of the account of Moses and Pharaoh, in truth."

We recite to you a portion of the important story of Moses and Pharaoh. A portion of that important affair is to be set forth. The method of the Qur'an is that, in each section where it narrates the affair of a prophet, from whatever dimension it looks at the story, it has a particular purpose, and it is in keeping with that same purpose that it selects a special portion of this story to narrate. Here it mentions a very short portion; this is because it has a particular purpose, and that is the matter of the victory of right over falsehood. Of course, we will not bring those portions of its story into this discussion today, and we did not bring them.

We recite to you a portion of the important story of Moses and Pharaoh — "in truth," in accordance with right, in accordance with reality, far from fable and legend — "for a people who believe." That which we set forth and recite to you is not useless and of little effect; rather, for the believers who have inclined toward you, it is extremely effective, beneficial, and profitable. When they hear this story with this insight and understand the divine custom (sunnah) in this regard, they specify and determine their path.

The gist of the matter is this: "Indeed Pharaoh exalted himself in the land." Pharaoh sought superiority in the land, sought greatness for himself, took on loftiness (ʿuluww). What does it mean that he took loftiness? At this level — equal with other humans — he suddenly rose up, sat upon that ledge, took himself to be higher than others. All were equal, all were on the same level; he took superiority over the other servants. "Indeed Pharaoh exalted himself in the land" — on the face of the earth he attached superiority and greatness to himself and chose it for himself, "and divided its people into factions." He placed himself in the upper degree and also partitioned the land, classified it, made it into groups: one group nearer to himself — the class of Hāmān and classes resembling his; one class lower, one class lower; he made classes in his Pharaonic society and on the face of God's earth. "Oppressing a group among them" — in addition to making society class-based and possessing various groups, he especially increased the pressure on one class. "Oppressing" — he made a group of them oppressed (mustaḍʿaf). Here, for "oppressing," a Persian equivalent occurred to me which I wrote: he took one class under tyranny and in the grip of weakness and incapacity — istiḍʿāf. Istiḍʿāf cannot be rendered as "making wronged" — though I did write "subjected to tyranny"; because the mustaḍʿafīn are usually interpreted to mean those counted as wronged. He kept these in the grip of weakness; within society he took away the possibilities from them, took the powers from them, made them weak and kept them in weakness. "Oppressing a group among them" — a group of the people of society, and one class of them, he kept severely in the grip of weakness and incapacity, casting them into the maw of weakness, into the maw of incapacity. "Oppressing" — he counted them as weak, took them as contemptible, in various expressions; the upshot is that within society he deprived them of all possibilities of growth and progress, of all possibilities. And you, if you are familiar with social issues and social books, see and grasp this matter fully in the world — especially in these Third-World countries. All the possibilities of growth and progress fall into the monopoly of a special few; others cannot grow, cannot breathe, and even if they do grow, their growth is in the service and exploitation of that chosen class. "Oppressing a group among them" — he took a group of them severely under pressure, in weakness and incapacity.

And his pressure was this, that "slaughtering their sons" — he killed their young men. He did not let the young generation of theirs come up; he sensed that among them there was a movement and a ferment, and he knew that the one who could bring this movement and ferment to its conclusion was the young generation. And because in that era and in the social conditions of that age the women perhaps had no involvement in these and social matters, and the burden was on the shoulders of the sons, for this reason the pressure was greater on the sons. Of course, you know this from outside the verse; in the accounts and narrations that have come down on this subject, we have heard and have word that he knew that among these very youths a Moses was to come forth, and it is that Moses who in the end would transform and overturn the world upon him. Either he knew it specifically — as is apparent from the narrations — that a soothsayer had said: "A son with these traits will be born whose name is Moses." Or he did not know it specifically, but he was a clear-sighted man; he understood, he understood that ultimately, among this young generation that is in the Pharaonic society among the Children of Israel, with much fervor and idea and uproar, ultimately a Moses, a great human being, a self-sacrificing man would appear among them — and he feared this; therefore he killed the young men.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ (28:4):** "and sparing their women."

And he kept their women alive — either in order to corrupt them, or in order to drag their lineage into vice, or in order to destroy the soundness of their stock (nasab); that is, the daughters of the Children of Israel apparently did not marry the sons of the Israelites. He wanted there to be no young men, only daughters, so that the lineage would become mixed and the Children of Israel would be absorbed into the Pharaonic society, would be absorbed into Egyptian society, would dissolve, would disappear, would be wiped out. Because — as I once said, on an appropriate occasion, in the opening verses of Sūrat al-Baqarah — for four hundred years the Children of Israel had held firm within that disordered, unbalanced Pharaonic society and had preserved their own noble ideals.

Well, this drawing-up of lines between these two groups: on one side Pharaoh has such a condition, which at the end of the verse says: "Indeed he was of the corrupters." Truly and certainly he was of the corrupters and the fomenters of corruption. He created corruption in nature (fiṭrah), created corruption in society, created corruption in the world — as in that other verse, in Sūrat al-Baqarah:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:205):** "And when he turns away (or gains authority), he strives to spread corruption in the land and to destroy crops and offspring; and God does not love corruption."[^s16-16]

The characteristic of the likes of Pharaoh is that they create corruption in the land, they bring about ruin; they prevent — in all kinds and sorts of ways — the maturing and bringing to fruition of the spiritual reserves of this world, or if they do bring them to fruition, they bring them to fruition in a wrong and incorrect form. And Pharaoh too was of this sort.

Now, in contrast, what is right? Upon what has the will of God settled, and in which direction has the divine custom (sunnah) been set? "And We desired" — and We desire — that is, it is Our creative custom and will. "And We desired to show favor to those who had been oppressed in the land." He does not say "We had desired" (wa aradnā), so that this will was made concerning the Children of Israel only — no, it is for always, for all of history. We have desired and We desire to bestow favor upon those same oppressed, upon that same class which has been in the grip of subjugation and captive to the claw of weakness and incapacity; upon them We want to bestow favor and to deliver them from that lowliness, to bring them out of oppression. "And We desired to show favor to those who had been oppressed in the land" — that We bestow favor upon the oppressed of the earth. "And to make them leaders" — that We bring them up from the rear of society, from being followers, to leadership and governance and being followed; that We bring up the oppressed of the earth, that We give them mastery over the Akāsirah and Qayāṣirah[^s16-17] of the world, over the bullying powerful ones. "And to make them the heirs" — and that We make them the heirs and inheritors of the good things of the earth; this too is Our will. "And to establish them firmly in the land" — that We make them settled and established in the land. "And to show Pharaoh and Hāmān and their hosts" — and that We show to Pharaoh, to Hāmān. These are the representatives of two classes: Pharaoh, although he belongs to the high class, because he is the eminent person of the high class, is the exemplar of a privileged class under whose authority and exploitation even Hāmān is. And Hāmān is the symbol and representative of another class, a class that puts all its possibilities and forces into the service of Pharaoh — those who in Quranic expressions are apparently referred to as the malaʾ, of whom we will speak in the future, God willing. "And their hosts" — and their armies, those who strive on their behalf without seeing any good from them, but who are nonetheless in their service. That We show to them, "through them" — through these oppressed — "the very thing they were dreading." That same thing which Pharaoh feared, We bring down upon his head; that same thing from which Hāmān fled and shrank, We bring down upon his head. That is, We take these powers away from them and place them in the hands of the oppressed, place them in the hands of the dust-dwellers, place them in the hands of the destitute — who, of course, always constitute the majority of any society. This too is the will of the Lord.

Then, of course, it goes on to other matters: "And We revealed to the mother of Moses: 'Suckle him,'"[^s16-18] which shows the power of the Lord. In that other verse too which we chose for you gentlemen from Sūrat al-Ṣaff, there the matter is that reference is again made to the victory of the divine order over the jāhilī order — though with the aspect of its victory we have no concern in today's discussion. We are concerned with this aspect: that in contrast to the existing jāhilī order, when the Prophet comes, he presents a divine and tawḥīdī order of right and strives in the establishment of that order and in the settling of that order of right. (We have written the translation of these verses here too. As for me, my time is over, and my energy too is almost spent.
