But we also have this right, and we grant it to ourselves, to say this matter to those who today stand in this path, in this garb:[^s18-16] while we acknowledge that a number [of them] act exactly as we would wish — a number of the clerics and propagandists truly act as they should and perhaps as they do — there are also some who simply do not in any way attend to the fact that today their point of beginning is not the point of beginning of the prophets' work. Matters of the tenth rank, of the eighth rank, of the fifth rank, have, for them, more value and worth than the question of Tawhid — than the exposition of the Quran, than the principles of religion, than the lofty teachings of Islam.

He is ready to discuss for hours the question of how Nakīr and Munkar [the two angels of the grave] come when they come into the grave: do they stand on a person's right side, or on his left side, or do they stand facing him, or what are these [things]? — a question whose knowing or not knowing has not a particle of effect on a person's being a Muslim, has not the slightest effect on action and on the fulfillment of our Islamic commitments. And of this sort of question there are very many. These they bring into the reckoning[^s18-17] of the necessities of religion as well; like the first-rank matters of religion, they put these forward, but they are never ready to give thought to this question: that Tawhid — Tawhid's proposal for the form of society, for the social order, what is it? Does it have a proposal or not? Our message is that these tasks must be placed in the first rank.

The lesson we take from the work of the prophets and from the point of beginning of the prophets' call is, among others, this: that we too must make our own point of beginning the very point of beginning of the prophets. If we cannot accomplish the prophets' resurrection, at least we can say that the prophets' resurrection is this, that the aim of the prophets is this, that their road is this; we can explain it — this work we can indeed do. If there is a discussion about the Prophet of the End of Time, preference is given to matters of the fourth and fifth rank in the life of that great one — for example, the question of the Messenger of God's casting no shadow [is taken up]. There is a narration in the *Khiṣāl* of Ṣadūq that the Messenger of God (may God's blessings be upon him and his family), when he walked, used to see from behind him; but, of course, the late Ṣadūq himself says in the footnote that what is meant is that the Messenger of God was so alert, so intelligent and watchful, like a person who continually sees what is around him. There are some who walk along the street dazed and stupefied; someone behind them makes faces and mimicry, and you could walk behind him for an hour and he would not notice. With some people, these things are difficult [to do] behind their backs. He is an intelligent person, he is attentive, he is watchful of everything around him; the slightest gesture, the slightest movement, around him, near him, behind him, draws him to it. He [Ṣadūq] says: the Prophet was an intelligent person, a discerning[^s18-18] man.

This is Ṣadūq's statement, mind you — the statement of Muḥammad ibn ʿAlī ibn Bābawayh al-Qummī, a traditionist [muḥaddith] of eleven hundred years ago, and one of the greatest scholars of the Shīʿa, a man whose books, from a thousand years ago until now, have always been at the height of fame — this very *ʿUyūn Akhbār al-Riḍā*, *Ikmāl al-Dīn*, *Man Lā Yaḥḍuruhu [al-Faqīh]*, and so on and so on, *Khiṣāl*, *Amālī*; dozens of books by this great one are now printed, are extant, which are among the high source-texts of the Shīʿa. This man — his opinion is this. Now, I am not concerned with whether this opinion is correct or not correct; but they are ready to put forward this matter, with its opinion, with its criticism, with so-and-so traditionist's view about it, with another's view about it — to discuss it; whereas, as to what the noble Prophet had come for at all, what his proposal was concerning the form of society, what the Prophet said concerning government, what the Prophet's view was concerning the manner of human upbringing — did he consider individual upbringing sufficient, or did he hold social upbringing to be necessary? Concerning these things — that which is not raised at all — this is exactly it.

Today is not a day on which the world of Islam can bear delay in these matters. Today we do not have that abundance of time; today we do not have that opportunity; today our patient is in his death-throes — in his death-throes, his hour is late. Today we must put forward, ahead of all else, whatever is most necessary; that whose necessity is a hair's breadth less than this, a hair's breadth behind. The breath and the hour of today are, for us, weighty and abundant and important.

Yes — those detailed, extensive theological discussions concerning the particulars of the second- and third- and fourth-rank teachings of Islam, leave for that day when we have no other work to do, when we have at least solved these first tasks. The final word is this: that if you do not accept this advice from us, at least do not take offense at our having advised you; this is yet another matter. The best of people is the one who does not take offense at receiving advice.

Therefore, the point of beginning of the prophets' call is Tawhid. The witness that we cited for you from the Quran for this meaning — since I do not wish to go into detail — one [of them] is Sūrat al-Naḥl, and its verse is this:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:36):** "And We did indeed raise up in every nation a messenger" — and indeed We raised up in every nation a prophet.

What was the message of this prophet and messenger? His message, his proclamation, was this:

> "Worship God and shun the Tāghūt" — that you should worship God and shun the Tāghūt. This is the first word of the prophets.

As soon as they come, before they have even rested from the dust of the road, their message is this: "Worship God and shun the Tāghūt."

The Tāghūt is the rival of God. The Tāghūt is that which stands horn to horn, opposite God and the command of God — whoever it is. Sometimes this Tāghūt is your own self;

> **Hadith:** «Your most hostile enemy is your own self, which is between your two sides.»[^s18-19]

Sometimes the Tāghūt is that very babbling[^s18-20] heart of yours. Sometimes the Tāghūt is your own caprice of day and night. Sometimes a person's love of mastery is his Tāghūt; the arrogance of a human being is his Tāghūt. And sometimes [the Tāghūt] is the powers outside the human being's existence — powers that thus expand in widening circles, expand and rise up. In any case, when the prophets came, they said God, not Tāghūt. The first sentence they uttered was this: that you should worship and serve God, and avoid, keep away from, and disregard the Tāghūt.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:36):** "Then among them were some whom God guided" — some of them God guided; "and among them were some upon whom error became deserved" — some of them were those upon whom error and misguidance were laid.

> "So journey through the earth" — travel about in the earth — "and behold how was the end of those who cried lies."

See what became of those upon whom error was laid, who cried the Prophet a liar and did not accept his guidance. Behold the fallen civilizations; behold the ruined countries and cities; behold Babylon and Assyria and Chaldea,[^s18-21] destroyed, of which nothing remained but a name in the column of history. Behold the power of the Pharaohs of Egypt, [how] Egyptian society was rolled up like a scroll. These are what the Quran speaks of; behold the nations and countries that did not lend ear to the word of the prophet — what their end became: they are doomed to extinction. The talk is not even of a miracle; of course, in the earliest age, miracles used to come — because they had to disappear quickly, it could not be allowed that, with the passage of ages[^s18-22] and days, the people of ʿĀd, for instance, should be brought to perdition [over time]. There they would send down chastisement upon them — the blowing of a wind, an earthquake, a flood, something — and remove them from existence.

But, generally speaking, until the end of the world this is so: any society, any nation that does not move along the course of religion and does not move in accordance with religion will be destroyed. Not that its people will all die — no; the destruction of a nation means the destruction of its national organization. They are absorbed, they are digested, they become part of other nations, their nationhood vanishes entirely. Today, you determine where the nationhood of Chaldea is; determine where the nationhood of Assyria is; determine where the nationhood of Babylon is. These great civilizations of history, the first human civilizations, are these — where are they? Where are they? Is there any trace of them at all?

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:59):** "Behold how was the end of those who cried lies."

This was in Sūrat al-Naḥl; now Sūrat al-Aʿrāf.

In Sūrat al-Naḥl it says, generally: "And We did indeed raise up in every nation a messenger" — in all the nations We sent a prophet. But in Sūrat al-Aʿrāf, it mentions the prophets one by one. First it begins with Noah, saying:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:59):** "Indeed We sent Noah to his people" — We sent Noah to his people; "He said: O my people, worship God."

See — his first word is this; he said, O my people! "Worship God" — serve God. "You have no deity other than Him" — there is no object of worship for you besides Him; that is, the true object of worship. "Truly I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day" — I fear for you the chastisement of a great day. Then his people answered him, saying that we do not accept, and so on and so on, and the affair of the Flood; but those parts were not the place of our discussion, until again the turn reaches ʿĀd.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:65):** "And to ʿĀd [We sent] their brother Hūd" — We sent to the people of ʿĀd their brother Hūd.

Now the people of ʿĀd are among those ancient and antique peoples — perhaps prehistoric — for it has not been correctly discovered or made clear who they were and in what era; [they are] after the Flood of Noah, [belonging] to the very, very ancient. 

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Shuʿarāʾ (26:149):** "And you skillfully hew out houses from the mountains."[^s18-23]

This is about this people of ʿĀd, apparently — that they built houses within the mountains; one does not deem it far-fetched that this might be for that late Stone Age, which some materialist scholars have mentioned and depicted. At any rate, they belong to the very ancient. They had a prophet named Hūd, who likewise said to them this same thing:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:65):** "He said: O my people, worship God; you have no deity other than Him" — he said: O people, worship and serve God, for you have no god besides Him.

Incidentally, see how, by this statement, the theory of those who say that Tawhid and religion in general arose from the natural condition of human beings and out of the ignorance and foolishness of human beings — and that religion gradually arrived at Tawhid — becomes utterly wrong and false. They say that at first the peoples were polytheists — some of these sociologists who speak by rote. It is very bad for a person to speak by rote[^s18-24] — like someone who wishes, say, to describe the Mosque of Imām Ḥasan al-Mujtabā, to describe this building, without ever having even come near this building. He sets about describing: yes, I see the Mosque of Imām Ḥasan thus — these marble stones have covered all its wainscot,[^s18-25] the chandeliers are attached in such a manner, these walls they have decorated with such beauty. He has not seen [it]; he has heard that the Mosque of Imām Ḥasan draws a large crowd, for example — suppose he has imagined it must be like this, on conjecture — whereas when he entered, you would see: no indeed, it has neither roof nor wall! (Laughter of the audience.)

In matters of sociology, if someone speaks without study and by rote, it comes out just like this: a cause for laughter and ridicule. They speak about the interpretation of history without having studied an important part of history; they speak about the questions of the phenomena of religion and creed and these things without paying attention to the religions of history. Well, this is a religion that existed perhaps tens of thousands of years ago — or, if we take that famous date found among the narrations as correct and authoritative and reliable, that seven or eight thousand years have passed since the descent of Adam, then suppose this was perhaps six or seven thousand years ago — and these people carry the chronicle along with ceremony [saying]: that at first there was polytheism, then Tawhid appeared — whereas no; we see that from ancient times, from antiquity, there was Tawhid.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:65):** "Will you not then guard yourselves [against evil]?"

— O my people, will you not show piety? He wished to make Tawhid acceptable to them and to make them understand it. Now here I have cited the conversation and discourse of his people with the prophet Hūd. I read it from the Quran and give a brief translation:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:66):** "The chiefs of his people who disbelieved said:" — those notables and dignitaries of his people who were disbelievers said to him thus: "Indeed we see you in foolishness" — we see you in foolishness and folly; "and indeed we reckon you among the liars" — and we deem you to be among the liars.

This is among the accusations and false notions that, throughout all time, have been directed against those who call to the truth. One of my discussions concerning prophethood is precisely this — perhaps it will be the last chapter — [namely] of what sort the slanders that were cast at the prophets were, and what those things were; it is, of course, a side and marginal discussion. They say: you are a foolish man; they accuse him of ignorance.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:67):** "He said: O my people, there is no foolishness in me" — he said: O my people, there is no folly in me; "rather, I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds" — rather, I am a prophet from the Lord of the worlds.

In the face of their accusation, their improper speech, he repeated his own true statement.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:68):** "I convey to you the messages of my Lord" — for I deliver to you the messages of my Lord; "and I am to you a faithful, trustworthy counselor" — I am to you a trustworthy well-wisher.

I desire your good, I desire your growth, I desire your progress — for I call you to God and Tawhid.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:69):** "Do you marvel that a reminder from your Lord has come to you by means of a man from among yourselves, that he may warn you?"

Do you marvel that a reminder from your Lord came to you, by means of a man from your very selves, to warn you? That is, do you find it astonishing that an ordinary human being, in this same garb of yours, in this same dress of yours, should attain the station of prophethood and messengership?

> "And remember" — and then he directs them to a historical matter — "and remember when He made you successors after the people of Noah" — remember that God made you successors after the people of Noah, that sinful people; "and increased you abundantly in stature" — and in creation, in physique and frame, gave you greater strength and power. "So remember the bounties of God" — remember the bounties of God; "that perhaps you may prosper" — that perhaps you may reach success and victory and felicity.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:70):** "They said: Have you come to us" — see, the enemy too at once understands what the exclusive worship of God means. "They said: Have you come to us that we should worship God alone?" — have you come to us that we should worship and serve God alone? "And forsake what our fathers used to worship?" — and that we should set aside what our fathers used to worship — of inanimate idols and animate idols, both? "Then bring upon us that with which you threaten us" — bring us that with which you threaten and warn us; "if you are among the truthful" — if you are speaking the truth.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:71):** "He said: There has [already] befallen you defilement and wrath from your Lord."

He said: assuredly there has befallen you defilement and wrath — defilement and wrath: a defilement within your own beings, and a wrath from the side of your Lord has overtaken you; you are wretched.

> "Do you dispute with me concerning names that you have named, you and your fathers?"

"Do you dispute with me?" — do you argue with me concerning hollow, marrowless names that you yourselves and your fathers placed upon them, that you fabricated and coined these names — for the sake of this being that you yourselves made and to which you granted power; for the sake of it do you dispute with me? 

> "for which God has sent down no authority [sulṭān]" — concerning which God has sent down no *sulṭān*, that is, no power — or no proof.

We have written both [meanings]: *ḥujja* means a proof and argument; and *sulṭān* comes both in the sense of power and in the sense of *ḥujja*, that is, proof and argument; here both meanings can hold. One is this sense — that we say God Most High has sent you no proof or argument for the correctness, truth, and soundness of these gods of yours and these lords of yours. The other interpretation is that we say: no, God has given them no power at all; and these abject, helpless, powerless beings, to which God has given no power whatsoever, you place at God's side.

> "Then wait; indeed I am with you among those who wait."

— Wait, I too am with you among those who wait. He said these things; then the chastisement of the Lord came down.

---

[^s18-1]: From the root (k-f-l): guarantor, surety, one who pledges.

[^s18-2]: Refer to the mimeographed handout [polycopy] at the end of the session.

[^s18-3]: From the root (j-m-l): to flatter, to use smooth and oily speech.

[^s18-4]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:116). The rendering of the closing phrase: "If I had said it, You would certainly have known it; You know what is in my soul."

[^s18-5]: Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:79). The phrase «Be servants to me» (*kūnū ʿibādan lī*) appears in this verse.

[^s18-6]: "The thumbs are alerted" — a proverbial expression meaning to become informed and aware.

[^s18-7]: Abū al-Ḥakam ʿAmr ibn Hishām, whom the Prophet named Abū Jahl ["Father of Ignorance"]. He undertook many measures to prevent the spread of Islam, including the design of an unsuccessful plot to kill the Noble Prophet with the participation of various tribes. He was finally killed, together with a number of the chiefs of polytheism, at the Battle of Badr.

[^s18-8]: Umayya ibn Khalaf was the head of the Banū Jumaḥ clan of the tribe of Quraysh, who, with the beginning of the Prophet's call, rose in opposition to him and in persecution of the Muslims. Bilāl the Abyssinian was his slave, who, after embracing Islam, was severely tortured. He [Umayya], together with one of his sons, was killed at the hands of the Muslims at the Battle of Badr.

[^s18-9]: ʿĀṣ ibn Wāʾil ibn Hāshim al-Sahmī, the chief of the Banū Sahm clan of Quraysh and one of the bitter enemies of the Prophet of Islam. When Qāsim, the first son of the Prophet, died in childhood, he called the Prophet *abtar* ["cut off, without posterity"]. In response to him, God revealed the Sūra of al-Kawthar. He has been mentioned as the father of ʿAmr al-ʿĀṣ, the minister of Muʿāwiya.

[^s18-10]: This proverb is used on occasions when a person is harmed in a dispute that does not concern him, or loses property in a contrived quarrel.

[^s18-11]: *Dawal dādan*: delay in carrying out a task.

[^s18-12]: From the root (ṣ-d-m): colliding with one another.

[^s18-13]: An Egyptian writer, man of letters, and intellectual who, despite his blindness from childhood, succeeded in receiving a doctorate from the two national universities of Cairo and the Sorbonne in France. He wrote many books, of which the one he wrote concerning his own life-story, titled *al-Ayyām*, gained very great fame.

[^s18-14]: Master Aḥmad Ārām (1904–1998) is one of the pioneers and masters of the art of translation, who has a great claim [to credit] in providing sound and faithful translations. After graduating from the Dār al-Funūn, he entered the field of law but, after a time, abandoned it and busied himself with the study of medicine. In his final year, he abandoned medicine too and entered cultural activities. The master was one of the first authors of textbooks and educational books. He translated more than 140 titles from the English, French, and Arabic languages into Persian, including: the *al-Ḥayāt* collection of narrations, *The History of Science*, the commentary *Fī Ẓilāl al-Qurʾān*, and others.

[^s18-15]: Non-professional, beginner.

[^s18-16]: From the root (k-s-w): garment, covering.

[^s18-17]: From the root (ʿ-d-d): number, count.

[^s18-18]: From the root (k-y-s): wise, alert.

[^s18-19]: Hadith no. 4: «Your most hostile enemy is your own self, which is between your two sides» (*aʿdā ʿaduwwika nafsuka allatī bayna janbayka*).

[^s18-20]: Garrulous, talkative.

[^s18-21]: Babylon and Assyria were among the ancient civilizations of the Mesopotamian region before the Common Era. With the decline of the civilization of Babylon, the Chaldean civilization replaced it.

[^s18-22]: From the root (d-h-r): age, era, time.

[^s18-23]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Shuʿarāʾ (26:149): "And you skillfully hew out houses for yourselves from the mountains."

[^s18-24]: To speak with eyes closed and from memory.

[^s18-25]: The portion of the wall of a room or porch [īwān] that extends from the ledge down to the floor.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 19: The Opposing Groups

*گروه‌های معارض — delivered Monday, 15 Mehr 1353 / 20 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:112–113):** "And thus We have appointed for every prophet an enemy — devils from among mankind and the jinn, inspiring one another with embellished speech in delusion. Had your Lord willed, they would not have done it; so leave them and what they fabricate. And [it is] so that the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter may incline toward it, and be pleased with it, and so that they may commit what they are committing."[^s19-1]

(We have started a little late today — by some ten minutes — and on the other hand we intend, God willing, to keep our time disciplined; that is, to finish in an hour and a half. So we will not recite the rest of the verses.) We said that prophethood is a social resurrection, and we said that one of the principles of this resurrection consists of the negation of social classes — meaning that in the environment which is the making and the handiwork of the Prophet of God, there does not exist, on one side, a class of the weak and the enslaved and the destitute and the deprived, and on the other side, a class of the powerful and the bullies and the privileged.

As we know from our own Islam — and indeed all the heavenly religions are essentially of the same character — in Islam it has not been posited, it has not been imagined, that there should be a situation in which one person, because of being weak, because of being powerless, would be unable to obtain his own rightful due. Such a supposition is not among the suppositions of the existence of an Islamic government and of a monotheistic, divine order. Therefore He says: «**لن تقدس امة لا یؤخذ فیها للضعیف حقه غیر متعتع**» ("No community will be sanctified in which the weak one cannot take his right without stammering")[^s19-2] — meaning that if in some society you see that a human being is weak — that is, he is not at the head of some power, he does not hold one of the political and social posts — and such a person, an ordinary human being, cannot take his right without a stammer of the tongue, then know that such a society is not a saved, successful, victorious society. That is, if the weak one took his right but took it with a stammer of the tongue — went and stood there, and his tongue stuck a little, and his face flushed a little — this too is of no use. Does a person who is a child of his own house, a child of this household, when he goes toward the kitchen and toward the food and the storehouse and the source of food to take his own food — which is a right he has assumed for himself — feel any shame, any sense of humiliation, any sense of being a burden? Certainly not.

In a society, in the way that Islam teaches and instructs us, and the way it wishes us actually to act, the situation is like this: all are children of one environment and members of one body, without any difference or distinction whatsoever — meaning that the Islamic ruler, like the Commander of the Faithful (may God's blessings be upon him), in the Islamic society, has exactly as much right and exactly the same rights as any one of the individuals of the common people has, and only that much right.

In the Islamic society, the very highest authorities cannot use force, bullying, or even harsh speech against an ordinary human being. So much so that even in that period when the course of the Islamic society had changed and, according to our belief, had drifted away from the axis of divine and authentic vicegerency — that is, from the axis of the imamate of the Commander of the Faithful (may God's blessings and peace be upon him) — even in that very period, when a governor, a powerful man, in some region far from the Islamic capital — when his son, not he himself, struck a single lash against an Arab — a high-spirited Arab gets up and traverses this long road, crosses this distance, comes to Medina to seek justice and to file his complaint. The caliph writes to the governor that both the governor and his son must come; he does not say "Send your son alone" so that the governor might excuse himself in reply and say "I am unwell at the moment" — no, the governor himself must come too. The governor and his son both come. When the caliph asks them why he harmed this Arab and struck him with a whip, the son of the governor says: "O Commander of the Faithful, O caliph! Tell him to bring a witness." He turned and said: "His witness is precisely this — that with such hardship he rose up from Egypt and came all the way to Medina. In the desert, where would he bring a witness from? Should he find four upright witnesses to attest that you struck him in the desert?! If you struck him in the lonely desert, what is he to do? Had you not struck him, this motive would not have arisen in him to rise up all that distance from Egypt and come on foot to Medina to complain to me. Lay him down." They stretched the governor's son out in the mosque and began to flog him. When the son rose up, [the governor] said, "Lay the father down too." The clamor and outcry of ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ went up — the governor was ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ — "Me, why? You say my son struck him; why are you striking me?" He said: "For the reason that your son struck him relying on you, struck him relying on your power. Had you not been his father, had you not held power, had you not been his protector and supporter, he would have struck his own head against a stone[^s19-3] before striking this Arab in the desert. Lay him down." For whose time was this? This was for the time when Islam had separated from the God-willed course. It was for the time when our Commander of the Faithful was not in office; those who were in office — we do not grant them the right of vicegerency — and yet, even so, it was like this.

Well, this is the Islamic society. The things I have said, I said them with a purpose: I wished that, from the discussion we are having today, I should not draw the conclusion myself — rather that you yourselves should draw it. What I am about to say and will say in this discussion, you yourselves, before I say it, should arrive at the very same conclusion.

This is the society that Islam wants, and like Islam are all the monotheistic religions of the world — a society in which there is no force, no bullying, no accumulation of wealth alongside the deprivations and destitution of the destitute classes. This very school and this very religion say: «**ما رأیت ثروة مجموعة الا و فی جانبها حق مضیع**» ("I never saw a heaped-up fortune except that beside it I saw a right that had been wasted").[^s19-4] If wealth were to be distributed per capita, this much would not reach Mr. Rockefeller.[^s19-5] The reason you see this much has reached him is that he has piled his own share together with the share of ten million other human beings, twenty million other human beings, and it has become this much. A ten-millionth is his, a hundred-thousandth is his. «**ما رأیت ثروة مجموعة الا و فی جانبها حق مضیع**».

The Islamic society, the ideal society from the standpoint of the logic of the monotheistic religions, is such a society: in it there is no force, in it there is no bully, no one uses force; and if anyone should wish to use force, they will not let him. If anyone should wish to spread class difference in a society built upon the Islamic order, they will not let him do so. Why? Because the order was an order that the Prophet had built. You have seen these skilled tailors — when they fashion a garment, you see that this garment has become torn and tattered, all the parts of the garment have become full of holes; yet its seams have not come undone, the cut of the garment has not been lost. The Islamic society they had punctured with holes again and again, yet the cut of the society had not been lost. Its tailor is the Prophet; its architect and its craftsman is the capable hand of the Messenger of God. The God-willed, God-made, Prophet-made order is an order of this kind. In it there is no force, no bullying, no exploitation, no despotism, no absolute rule of one human being or a group of human beings over other human beings. In such a society — which God wants and says human beings must be gathered together in this form — there is no ignorance, no unawareness, no superstition-worship. In this community the people are obliged, are bound, all to think. It is necessary for all to find their own path; and when they have found it, it is necessary that they tread that path.

In that society all are duty-bound to defend the rights of the weak and the deprived and the oppressed. In that society no one has the right to say, "My own interests are enough for me, my own troubles are enough for me, my own affairs are enough for me; I have no time to attend to others' affairs" — for the reason that all are together, all are parts and members of one body and the limbs of one body. Can they speak in this way? In that society there is no laziness; in that society there is no division and discord; in that society there is no blind submission and moving sightlessly after Zayd and ʿAmr and Bakr. Such a society the Prophet wishes to build. Pay careful attention, keep in mind the characteristics of that society which the Prophet wishes to build with his powerful hand and with the teaching and inspiration of God's revelation. This society — its great characteristics, its main outlines — are: the existence of knowledge and awareness, the existence of justice and equity, a single class, the negation of social classes, the negation of exploitation, the negation of the accumulation of wealth, the negation of despotism and absolute rule, the negation of taking the side of falsehood, and the obligation and compulsion of taking the side of right and truth. These are the features of this society.

The main point of my words is here, and it connects to today's discussion.

If a prophet should come into an ignorant society and also state his own message, also declare his own aim, and say what kind of society and world he wants to build, say how he wants to arrange the social order — if a prophet should speak these words, who will struggle against him in this ignorant community?

I said what kind of society the Prophet wants to build. It is very clear who will oppose and struggle against the Prophet. The first people to struggle against the Prophet are those who live off class difference. They are alive for the reason that they are able to milk a group of people, able to wrongfully use the energies of a group of innocent human beings. If it should come to pass that they were placed in the same rank, in the same class, as those human beings, then off whom would they live? After this, whom would they milk? These become opponents.

Another of those who will oppose the call of this prophet and the establishment of such a society and order are the wealth-hoarders, the wealth-amassers. Those who are eager to pull money out of this one's pocket, that one's pocket, out of the knotted handkerchief of some deprived old woman, from the bottom of the cash-box of some grocer, from the meager till of some street vendor, and to pour it into their own enormous, infinite purses. Those who are eager — by founding usurious institutions, by establishing a system of usury and taking interest[^s19-6] on money — to turn every business that exists, and all the trades, and all economic activities, in the end to their own profit. Is it not so? Sir, what is your occupation? You are a tradesman in this bazaar; whatever business you do — if you tell me, so many percent of it is for you, so many percent of it is for such-and-such bank and so-and-so and so-and-so. See how much credit you have used, see how much interest you are paying, and then see for whom you are running.

When the system was a usurious system, when it came to pass that in a society everyone pays interest and a group take interest and devour it, then consequently all the businesses are to the profit of the usurious institutions. If, in such a society — where wealth and wealth-hoarding are the noble, legitimate occupation of a group of human beings who consume and lord it over others — a prophet should come, a reformer should come, and say, "Sir, wealth-hoarding is forbidden!" — well, it is obvious that this one will oppose that prophet; it is quite natural. So this too is one group.

One group are the wealth-hoarders who will struggle against this prophet. One group are the despotic rulers who will struggle against this prophetic call and divine mission — for the reason that as soon as «**لا اله الا الله**» enters a society in its true form, its meaning is that Pharaoh has risen up out of that society, has departed; either he has gone, or he has become one of the ordinary individuals of the people. This is the meaning of «لا اله الا الله», after all. If «لا اله الا الله» should come to be, in its true form, the source of the building of a society, then in that society which is formed on the basis of «لا اله الا الله», at the apex of its pyramid stands God, not Pharaoh. God is placed there, not Pharaoh and Hāmān, not Nimrod, not Shaddād,[^s19-7] not Muʿāwiyah. So it is very clear that Pharaoh and Nimrod and the other despotic powers of history will vehemently struggle against the call of the prophets to establish such a society. This too is one class of the strugglers and opponents of the prophethoods.

Yet another class are the rabbis and the monks — those who deal with people's minds. Those who, in order to preserve the social standing they derive from the teaching they give the people — and if this teaching were a correct teaching, a life-building teaching, an awakening teaching, an enlightening teaching, then the survival of that lordship, that spiritual leadership, those material and prestige privileges would not be possible for this class either — therefore the rabbis and monks of history try to keep the people in unawareness. Jesus son of Mary — his struggle was with the rabbis and monks before it ever reached the Roman emperor; for in his own time it had not reached [the emperor]. Those who did not want the Jesusine, the Messianic call to take root in the corrupt Jewish society of that time — who were they? They were the rabbis of the Jews, the scholars of the Jews — even though they knew Jesus well.

In the era of the rise of Islam, those who did not want the prophetic movement, the Muhammadan movement and the Islamic mission, to take root and strike down roots — who were they? Those for whom, if the Prophet came, if those correct teachings came, that pure, delicious, thirst-quenching wellspring of Islam came and filled the minds to overflowing, opened the eyes, lifted away the ambiguities and the ignorances — for whom there would no longer be any scope for lordship and [comfortable] life. It is clear: where Islam is, the fabrications of Kaʿb al-Aḥbār[^s19-8] and ʿAbd Allāh ibn Salām[^s19-9] have no color and no luster. It is obvious that when the sun of truth shines upon the precinct of human minds and thoughts, their superstitions and their darknesses dissolve and vanish of themselves. Therefore, as soon as the prophet comes — the prophetic society which is based on awareness, based on information and knowledge and clear-sightedness and free thought and enlightenment, the divine, monotheistic society — as soon as the tune of such a society begins to be played, among the first groups who sense danger are the rabbis and the monks. The people's awareness is to their detriment; the people's awareness is to the detriment of themselves and to the detriment of the powers allied with them. Both they themselves suffer harm, and the powers that are allied with them — even if they are not religious powers, but rather political powers — they too suffer harm; these too sense danger.

As I read in that letter of the Fourth Imam (may God's blessings be upon him) to Muḥammad ibn Shihāb al-Zuhrī — in which the Imam (peace be upon him) draws for Muḥammad ibn Shihāb al-Zuhrī a picture such that when we, today, in the twentieth century, turn back to history and observe the coalition of religious and political forces for crushing nations and destroying growths and talents and trampling the rights of the masses of the people — only today do we newly understand the very thing that Imam al-Sajjād explained that day in that letter. «**و اعلم ان ادنیٰ ما کتمت و اخف ما احتملت ان آنست وحشة الظالم و سهلت له طریق الغی بدنوک منه حین دنوت و اجابتک له حین دعیت**» ("And know that the least thing you have concealed and the lightest burden you have borne is that you befriended the desolation of the oppressor [grew familiar with him and kept him company] and smoothed for him the path of error by your drawing near to him when you drew near, and by your answering him when you were called").[^s19-10] This is a letter which is in *Tuḥaf al-ʿUqūl*; the exposition and interpretation of this letter would take long. (Moreover, some months ago I read out and explained this letter in detail. Those gentlemen who are interested — in the noble book *Tuḥaf al-ʿUqūl*, among the words of Imam al-Sajjād (may God's blessings be upon him), "his letter, peace be upon him, to Muḥammad ibn Muslim al-Zuhrī" — and Muḥammad ibn Muslim and Muḥammad ibn Shihāb are one and the same person; he is named after his father, Muslim, and after his father's grandfather, Shihāb; Muḥammad ibn Muslim and Muḥammad ibn Shihāb — find this. As it happens, this book has also been translated; *Tuḥaf al-ʿUqūl* has been translated two or three times by now; consult it, and find this, God willing, and those who are interested may read it.)[^s19-11]

In short, then, one of the groups whom the coming of the prophet alarms and terrifies is this very group of the masters of religion — but of the non-real religion, the superstitious religion. These too were among those who, in the face of the prophets' missions, in the face of the liberating calls, opposed and struggled vehemently. We have an example of this in the case of the call of Islam itself; we have it also in the affair of Abraham, the Friend of the Merciful — that all the attendants of the idol-temples, when they saw that the idols had been broken, said, "Who has done this to them?" and set up an uproar and clamor and outcry; and it was those very people who incited Nimrod to cast Abraham into the fire. The ordinary people had no knowledge of the idol-temple; the idol-temple has a priest, it has attendants. It was those very people who, in the affair of the appearance of Moses son of ʿImrān in the Pharaonic society, reassured Pharaoh and emboldened him, saying, "We, by our own magic, by our own soothsaying,[^s19-12] will nullify his magic." It was those very people who, in the call of Jesus (peace be upon him), formed the greatest opponents and the front against Jesus and prevented the call of Jesus. Look — this ordinary Gospel, these very Gospels that are in hand — look, with all the distortions, these things are there; these historical portions of it can be a teller, for us, of the historical realities of that time. And again, it was these very people who, in the call of Islam — these many stories you have heard — kept coming to pose questions, to test, to set people upon the Prophet; to set up the affair of the mubāhalah (the mutual imprecation) with the Christians of Najrān, and the like, and the like; to reassure the people and bind them to the rotten and ancient stuff they possessed.

When a new idea is raised in a society, and the course of human thought is bound to move toward greater comprehension and consciousness and awareness, the nature of the matter — the nature of the human being — is this: that human beings, old and young, must set out after this new current of thought, and they do set out. Human beings want the new word more; that which is more acceptable to their minds, they accept more. Moreover, "the lamp of falsehood has no radiance."[^s19-13] Superstitions are falsehood; religious distortions and substitutions are falsehood. If some clear statement, some rational mind, should prove the emptiness of these superstitions, their nonsensical and baseless nature, the people would readily accept it.

But it is this very class of rabbis and monks who, throughout history, despite the clarity of the prophets' speech, did not let the people believe in the prophets. Despite the fact that the prophets came with manifest authority, with a clear proof, everywhere carrying light with them, everywhere enlightening human beings — they never spoke obscurely,[^s19-14] they never delivered pseudo-philosophical jargon to the people, they did not philosophize for the people, they spoke clean and straight and frankly with the people; despite this, the people ought to have accepted very quickly, the people ought very quickly to have grasped the soundness and firmness[^s19-15] of the speech of the prophets of God. What is the cause that so much obstinacy and bigotry and stubbornness was expended, and the call of the prophets was not accepted quickly and easily? Why? It is these very soothsayers and rabbis and monks, and that very class which the Qur'an names "the rabbis and the monks" — these were the obstacle. They summoned the people to greater and greater adherence to the wrong intellectual traditions and the inherited superstitious notions. These too were alarmed at the coming of the prophet, because they knew that if the prophet came, if the prophet was commissioned, if that society came into being — the society in which there is awareness, in which there is light, in which there is intellectual growth, in which all the people are either learned or learners — there would no longer be any arena for the illiterate and the self-satisfied and the falsely revered and the people who wish human beings to remain in unawareness and superstition; there would no longer be any place for these in that society. This is why these too struggled, with all possible vehemence, against the prophets, against the divine calls, against the historical missions.

That makes four classes. Up to this point in my talk, I have not cited anything from the Qur'an; I have only explained this much: what kind of world the prophets come to build. I described that world for you — what kind of world it is, what kind of society it is. Then I said: who fears such a society? It is obvious — of a society in which the accumulation of wealth is forbidden, the wealth-hoarders are afraid. Of a community and order in which class difference is a sin, is forbidden, is abolished, the upper classes are afraid. The one who has grown accustomed to living among the upper classes — if he sees that an order is going to come into power which will bring him down, which will level him with the dust-dwelling people, or level the dust-dwelling people up to him — it makes no difference, for him it is the same — he can remain a lord only if there are slaves; otherwise, if all are also lords, then he again is no lord. The lordship of the lords lies in this: that they alone are lords and the others are slaves; otherwise, if the slaves became lords, then lords become many, lords become cheap. The costliness of the lords is on account of their fewness. This society that comes into being either brings him down and sets him at the level of the slaves, or raises the slaves up and places them at his level; lords become many — so he falls from being falsely revered. So he too opposes.

That absolute despotic power, the one whose heart desires to be — in the expression of the late Mīrzā-yi Nāʾīnī (may God's mercy and exaltation be upon him) in the introduction to *Tanbīh al-Ummah*[^s19-16] — «فعّال ما یشاء و حاکم ما یرید» ("the doer of whatever he wills and the ruler by whatever he wishes");[^s19-17] the one who wishes to be, in the Qur'an's expression, «**لا یسأل عما یفعل**» ("He is not questioned about what He does");[^s19-18] who does whatever he does, says whatever he says, goes wherever he goes, and no one may ask "why" — this one too will not be on very happy terms with the call of the prophets and the rule of the prophet, for the reason that he knows that if the prophet comes, the first one to be struck is he. This too is one class — and the class of rabbis and monks we have also explained.

These four classes, which we have determined and specified for you by mental analysis, are — as it happens — named in the Qur'an. Except that the people who are in the class of the chiefs and heads and khans and ringleaders of the powers and orders, those who hold these posts, are referred to in the Qur'an as the *malaʾ*, and are alluded to. «**قال الملأ الذین کفروا من قومه**» ("The chiefs of his people who disbelieved said")[^s19-19] — the *malaʾ*, those people who fill the eyes; *yamlaʾūna al-ʿayn*, those persons of pomp and majesty, those who, when they walk, their glitter and pomp, their "clear the way, get back" outriders,[^s19-20] make every human being submissive and small before them. The *malaʾ*, those eye-fillers — one class of the opponents of the prophets are these. Like whom? In the ignorant, Pharaonic order, like Hāmān. «**و قال فرعون یا هامان ابن لی صرحا**» ("And Pharaoh said: O Hāmān, build me a lofty tower");[^s19-21] the fixer and the lackey of Mr. Pharaoh, and at the price of this fixing, at the price of this servitude and lackeyship for Pharaoh, he is the lord of all the non-Pharaohs, of all but Pharaoh. As it was said: "A fly that you set flying is a falcon."[^s19-22] Hāmān is that very fly that Pharaoh has set flying, and it does the work of a falcon. He is weak, he is wretched, he is a lone man — to deal with[^s19-23] him is within the power of any single man; but, relying on Pharaoh, he is all-in-all. Thus, when he walks in the street, if you look, you see that, as it were, the light around him casts such a curtain[^s19-24] that a human being is utterly unable to bear to look at him. "Clear the way, get back, go, come — who is it? Hāmān is coming."

Or, in the ignorant order of Muʿāwiyah — for example, it is al-Mughīrah ibn Shuʿbah,[^s19-25] it is Ziyād ibn Abīhi[^s19-26] — these are the same *malaʾ*. They keep the surroundings of Muʿāwiyah's throne so firm and steady that whatever Muʿāwiyah looks at, he sees a friend, he sees a companion-counselor, he sees a well-wisher. In the Qur'an this class is expressed as the *malaʾ*; the aristocratic class is expressed as the *mutrafīn*, those who are afflicted with *taraf* — aristocratic indulgence, the great fortunes that are the source of miseries and crimes and trampling of rights; *taraf*. The verse that we have now written here for you and will recite says this very thing; it says: in every community, when We sent a prophet, its *mutrafīn*, its aristocrats, were the first ones to oppose; they were the first ones to strike up the tune of opposition. This too is yet another class.

That class of intellectual ringleaders the Qur'an names with this very name, "rabbis and monks": «**ان کثیرا من الاحبار و الرهبان**» ("Indeed, many of the rabbis and the monks…"),[^s19-27] whose verse we will now explain for you. And that class of despotic powers — and here I am speaking by way of conjecture, this part I say as a probability — I think the Qur'an refers to them by the expression *ṭāghūt*. Although *ṭāghūt* is a general word; as I said the day before yesterday, *ṭāghūt* means that rebellious power in opposition to God. It may be that your own carnal soul is the *ṭāghūt* — for I read this hadith too: «**اعدی عدوک نفسک التی بین جنبیک**» ("Your most hostile enemy is your own soul that lies between your two sides")[^s19-28] — your own soul, your own self, your own caprices and lusts, your own child, your own wife, your own beloved friend, may be your *ṭāghūt*; and it is also possible that the great powers are the *ṭāghūt*. *Ṭāghūt*, then, has a general meaning. But since we see that in the Qur'an the *ṭāghūt* is everywhere set in opposition to Allah, and is portrayed as possessing very important roles and stations, we understand that the *ṭāghūt* is the highest stations of an ignorant order.

In one place He says: «**الذین آمنوا یقاتلون فی سبیل الله و الذین کفروا یقاتلون فی سبیل الطاغوت**» ("Those who believe fight in the way of God, and those who disbelieve fight in the way of the ṭāghūt")[^s19-29] — the believer fights in the way of God, the unbeliever in the way of the ṭāghūt; the ṭāghūt is in opposition to God. In another place He says: «**الله ولی الذین آمنوا یخرجهم من الظلمات الی النور و الذین کفروا اولیاؤهم الطاغوت یخرجونهم من النور الی الظلمات**» ("God is the Guardian of those who believe; He brings them out of the darknesses into the light. And those who disbelieve, their guardians are the ṭāghūt; they bring them out of the light into the darknesses").[^s19-30] From the beginning of the Qur'an to its end, in about eight places I think the word *ṭāghūt* is used; in seven places of the Qur'an the word *ṭāghūt* is used, and the style of the expressions is such that when a human being looks, it appears that what is meant by *ṭāghūt* is those same upper despotic powers at the apex.

In short, these four classes are the opponents of the prophets — not only in the time of Moses, not only in the time of the Prophet, not only in the time of Abraham, but in every age of history. Wherever a word of truth appeared, wherever a claim and a tune calling for following the prophets of God and the heavenly books came into being, these four classes lined up — either simultaneously, or one after another. This is the general rule. And here lies the instructive point of these noble verses. Allow me to read the verses; our time is running out.

First, in the first verse, in the first portion, He says: «**و کذلک جعلنا**» ("And thus We appointed"),[^s19-31] «**کذلک**» — meaning "thus." Thus, meaning what? Meaning "like you." Just as in your own case, O Prophet, you see, likewise «جعلنا لکل نبی» ("We appointed for every prophet") «عدوا» — an enemy, an adversary — «شیاطین الانس و الجن» ("devils from among mankind and the jinn"). These enemies are the devils of mankind and the jinn. I have explained the meaning of "devil"; this too confirms it. "Devil" means the evil-creating powers that are outside the human being. One kind is this very Iblīs who did not prostrate to Adam (peace be upon him), Adam the father of mankind. That devil is the most ill-famed of the devils of the world; the ill-fame of the other devils too is laid to his account, poor wretch. Every corruption, every wrong that any devil — whether of mankind or of the jinn — works in the world, the people write its ill-fame, its curse, to the account of that Iblīs and reckon it from him, whereas some of these devils are the masters of that devil.

«**عدوا شیاطین الانس و الجن یوحی بعضهم الی بعض**» — these devils who are the enemies of the prophet, some of them inspire others, teach others. Sometimes the class of rabbis and monks teaches the class of the *malaʾ*; sometimes the class of the *malaʾ* teaches the rabbis and monks; sometimes the *mutrafīn* instruct both classes; and most often all three classes take their inspiration from the *ṭāghūt*. «**یوحی بعضهم الی بعض**» — some of these enemies give inspiration and instruction to others. «**زخرف القول**» — with beautiful, adorned, fair-seeming words, with fair-seeming speech. I will, in a future chapter among the chapters relating to prophethood, expound on this fair-seeming speech. So fair-seeming — Pharaoh says: «**ذرونی اقتل موسی**» ("Let me kill this Moses").[^s19-32] Well, why? Why kill him? «**انی اخاف ان یبدل دینکم**» ("Indeed, I fear that he may change your religion") — he says, "I fear that Moses will ruin your religion." This is the word of Pharaoh. Pharaoh fears that Moses will come and ruin the people's religion. Such fair-seeming words — «زخرف القول», fair-seeming speech — «**غرورا**», out of creating delusion and ignorance; they delude the people and one another.

«**و لو شاء ربک ما فعلوه**» ("And had your Lord willed, they would not have done it") — if God had willed and decreed, these people would not have done this deed, would not have found the means. If God had willed to turn all these opposing classes to ashes in a single instant and scatter them to the wind, He could; but, well, the divine custom (sunnah) is not so, God's law is not so. The divine law is this: that they should carry out their enmities so that the believer may be distinguished from the non-believer. The road should be a little uneven so that the people whose thighs, whose shanks, are strong — who can run, who can go — may be recognized, may be made evident. On an asphalted road, where everyone can walk these few steps, [there is no test]. «**و لو شاء ربک ما فعلوه**» ("And had your Lord willed, they would not have done it") — had your God willed, had your Lord decreed, these people would not have been able to carry out these enmities; but God Most High never decrees a decree contrary to the custom He Himself has placed in the world.

«**فذرهم و ما یفترون**» ("So leave them and what they fabricate") — leave them with whatever they slander and lie about. What does "leave them" mean? It means: do not grieve and be distressed at their words, do not weaken, do not lose your path.

«**و لتصغی الیه افئدة الذین لا یؤمنون بالآخرة**»[^s19-33] — the result of these words is this: this fair-seeming, deceiving, delusory speech has as its result that the hearts which do not believe in the Hereafter fall under the influence of this false propaganda, this fair-seeming propaganda. That propaganda which is made against the call of the prophets, the propaganda which is made against the word of truth and the true tune of monotheism — this false propaganda, this deceiving and fair-seeming propaganda, deceives certain hearts, draws them to itself. But the hearts of whom? «**و لتصغی الیه افئدة الذین لا یؤمنون بالآخرة**» — the hearts of those who have no faith and no belief in the Hereafter. Therefore the one who believes in the Hereafter does not so quickly become the captive and the dupe of false propaganda. «**و لتصغی الیه افئدة الذین لا یؤمنون بالآخرة**» — so that the hearts of those who do not bring faith in the Hereafter may incline toward it; «**و لیرضوه**» ("and that they may be pleased with it") — so that these hearts may be pleased with these words; «**و لیقترفوا ما هم مقترفون**» ("and that they may commit what they are committing") — so that they may perform, may commit, what they are committing. See: in this verse He has alluded, in summary, that all the prophets have enemies — enemies of jinn and mankind, open and hidden — and these enemies give inspiration to one another, teach one another, instruct one another. In these verses there is only this much. After these verses, [there are] verses from Sūrat al-Muʾmin which I will not now find from the Qur'an itself — if I were to look for them, it would take long, the time is ending — so I read them from this very written page.

«**و لقد ارسلنا موسی بآیاتنا**»[^s19-34] ("And indeed We sent Moses with Our signs") — truly We sent Moses together with Our signs, Our marks; «**و سلطان مبین**» ("and with a manifest authority") — with a manifest proof or power. What was that manifest power, that manifest proof, that manifest argument? His strong logic, his word of truth, the staff in his hand, his white hand. With these matters, by which every human being, ordinary and extraordinary, must accept his word, We sent Moses. Toward whom did We send him? To war against whom did We send him? Who was on the other side of Moses in this society? If we read the verses with a little reflection, see how clearly the matter comes out; social matters of this importance, in the firm verses, in the outward sense of the verses, with this clarity. To war against whom? Toward whom? «**الی فرعون**» ("To Pharaoh") — toward Pharaoh. Who else? «**و هامان**» ("and Hāmān") — toward Hāmān, the minister of Pharaoh, of the heads of Pharaoh's kingdom, of the eye-filling aristocrats, the same *malaʾ*. Who else? «**و قارون**» ("and Qārūn") — who is Qārūn? Qārūn is a wealthy man, Qārūn is a moneyed man; Qārūn is not the chief, Qārūn was not the sovereign of the society, was not Pharaoh — indeed Qārūn even came for a later period — yet nonetheless He says that We sent [Moses] toward him too, sent him to war against him too. That is, for Moses, Pharaoh and Qārūn are one and the same: just as he fights Pharaoh, just as he fights Hāmān, he fights Qārūn too.

Even though Qārūn's crime is this: that he has treasured up wealth, gathered the people's possessions for himself. At a tablecloth upon which they have set food enough for fifty persons, fifty persons eat of it, become full, and much is even left over; this gentleman has sat cross-legged, or spread out his legs, and put the food of forty-nine persons in front of himself. Well, it is obvious that one man does not eat the food of forty-nine persons — but he does not let the others eat either. Forty-nine other persons' food — one person eats it; this one person has imprisoned the food of forty-nine other persons — would that he had eaten poison! — he has imprisoned it; Qārūn is of this trade. Qārūn has imprisoned the public wealth; that is why Moses comes to war against him too.

The strange thing is that, although Pharaoh is at the head of the government, he is one class; Hāmān is beside him, he is another class; Qārūn has no connection with them at all, he is wealthy, he is a treasure-hoarder, he is another class; and despite there being three classes, their answer is one answer. All three take one stance in the face of Moses (peace be upon him), say one word. What do they say? «**فقالوا ساحر کذاب**» ("So they said: a sorcerer, a liar") — they said, "He is a lying, falsehood-fabricating sorcerer." «**فلما جاءهم بالحق من عندنا**» ("And when he brought them the truth from Us") — when Moses stated and brought the truth, from Us, to these opponents, what did they say? Did they sit silent? Did they sit waiting for Moses to come and cut down the root and stem of their existence and the rotten tree of their life? Never. Just as Moses, with the order he proposed, was clawing at their life, they too clawed at the head and face of Moses. What did they say? «**قالوا اقتلوا ابناء الذین آمنوا معه**» ("They said: Kill the sons of those who have believed with him") — kill the young sons of those who believed and gave their faith to Moses. Those who believed in the thought of this newly arrived prophet, this new and luminous and life-building thought — kill their sons. Kill them so that they may not be there, so that they may not threaten us tomorrow; kill them so that they may not be there, so that no spark may strike from their being; kill their young men.

«**و استحیوا نساءهم**» ("and keep their women alive") — and keep their women alive. Now why keep the women alive? There is an explanation for that. So that their lineage may be mixed; so that they may be drawn into shamelessness; so that they may be made to gorge upon the instincts; so that they may become cowed. There are several aspects to this.

But then He says: «**و ما کید الکافرین الا فی ضلال**» ("And the plot of the disbelievers is only in error") — they drew up these schemes and plots, but the plots of the disbelievers are in error; that is, they are astray, they do not reach a result. It is like an arrow that you shoot toward some enemy, some person, some target — and a wind comes and makes the arrow lose its way from this course. They shoot the arrow, but the winds of God's custom come and carry these arrows away from this aim and target. Let them lay schemes and plots for Moses. Here too you noticed: in this verse, three classes are named — the class of Pharaoh, the class of Hāmān, the class of Qārūn; here these three classes have been named together.

In another verse that exists, again the *mutrafīn* are named, especially the class of Qārūn. He says: «**و ما ارسلنا فی قریة من نذیر**»[^s19-35] ("And We did not send a warner into any town") — We did not send into any town a warner, a forewarner — meaning a prophet — «**الا قال مترفوها**» ("but that its affluent ones said") — except that the *mutrafīn* of it, the aristocrats and the wealthy and the treasure-hoarders of that town said: «**انا بما ارسلتم به کافرون**» ("Indeed, in that with which you have been sent, we are disbelievers") — "We disbelieve in what you have brought, we do not accept it." And what was their reason for not accepting? «**و قالوا نحن اکثر اموالا و اولادا**» ("And they said: We are greater in wealth and children") — "We have more money than you, we have more children than you," they said to the prophet. On what grounds? See how low the level of his thinking is. «**و ما نحن بمعذبین**» ("And we will not be punished") — "We will not be of those who are tormented."

And as for the last verse, which is from Sūrat al-Tawbah, it concerns the class of the rabbis and the monks. «**یا ایها الذین آمنوا**»[^s19-36] ("O you who have believed") — O you who have believed; «**ان کثیرا من الاحبار و الرهبان**» ("Indeed, many of the rabbis and the monks") — truly many of the scholars and ascetics; «**لیأکلون اموال الناس بالباطل**» ("surely consume the wealth of the people wrongfully") — without doubt they devour the people's wealth without entitlement; «**و یصدون عن سبیل الله**» ("and bar [them] from the way of God") — they devour their money, and they also turn them away from the path of God. And again He speaks of the class of the *mutrafīn*, the money-gatherers, the treasure-hoarders: «**و الذین**» ("And those who…") «**یکنزون الذهب و الفضة**» ("hoard up gold and silver") — those who treasure up gold and silver; «**و لا ینفقونها فی سبیل الله**» ("and do not spend it in the way of God") — they do not spend it in the way of God; «**فبشرهم بعذاب الیم**» ("then give them tidings of a painful punishment") — give them the tidings of a grievous, painful torment.

We, therefore, in these few verses and in dozens of other verses of the Qur'an, see the mark of these four classes and recognize their enmities.

[^s19-1]: Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:112–113).
[^s19-2]: Hadith no. 5. «لن تقدس امة لا یؤخذ فیها للضعیف حقه غیر متعتع» — "No community will be sanctified in which the weak one cannot take his right without stammering."
[^s19-3]: A figure of speech for being utterly restless and agitated.
[^s19-4]: Close to Hadith no. 18. «ما رأیت ثروة مجموعة الا و فی جانبها حق مضیع».
[^s19-5]: John Rockefeller was one of the greatest capitalists of America. The center he established under his own name later, with the influence of Zionist currents within it, played an effective role in the political events of the world. As an example, one may point to the close relationship of Muhammad Reza Shah with this center.
[^s19-6]: (r-b-ḥ) profit, gain.
[^s19-7]: He was the son of ʿĀd who, after his father's death, became the ruler of the people of ʿĀd. In response to the descriptions that the Prophet Hūd would give him of Paradise, he resolved to build a paradise better than the Paradise of Hūd's God. He built a great palace — one brick of gold and one brick of silver — and a garden of various trees with abundant jewels, with streams of honey and milk and pearls and coral. When the work of building his palace and garden was finished, as he wished to dismount from his horse to view and behold them, before his foot reached the ground, the angel ʿIzrāʾīl took his soul.
[^s19-8]: Abū Isḥāq Kaʿb ibn Mātiʿ al-Ḥimyarī was one of the Jewish scholars of Yemen who, after the Prophet, in the early caliphate of the second caliph, converted to Islam and came to Medina. He learned the Qur'an from the Companions. Because of his familiarity with the books of the Jewish scholars, he became known as Kaʿb al-Aḥbār. He related many false narrations, with the claim that they were transmitted from the Torah, which are known as the *Isrāʾīliyyāt*.
[^s19-9]: ʿAbd Allāh ibn Salām ibn al-Ḥārith al-Isrāʾīlī was one of the rabbis and notables of the Jews of Banū Qaynuqāʿ who, according to one account, embraced Islam in the first year of the Hijra. He and Kaʿb al-Aḥbār were, in the time of the third caliph, among the close associates and counselors of the caliph — which very thing caused the creation of many deviations in the government of the Islamic society. ʿAbd Allāh was, in the time of Muʿāwiyah too, among the intimates of his court, and he refrained from giving allegiance to the Commander of the Faithful.
[^s19-10]: Hadith no. 29. "And know that the least thing you have concealed and the lightest burden you have borne is that you grew familiar with the desolation of the oppressor (and kept him company), and smoothed the path of error the moment you drew near to him and the instant you accepted his call."
[^s19-11]: The exposition of this letter by the Supreme Leader is given under Chapter 59, the third circle, "The 250-Year-Old Human."
[^s19-12]: (k-h-n) the claim of being informed of the unseen secrets; fortune-telling.
[^s19-13]: Khwājah Naṣīr al-Dīn al-Ṭūsī composed these two couplets in reply to the accusation of unbelief leveled against him: "Let the disordered Niẓām call me an unbeliever / the lamp of falsehood has no radiance. / I call him a Muslim, for he is not / worthy of any lie but a lie."
[^s19-14]: (gh-l-q) difficult, hard, closed.
[^s19-15]: (t-q-n) firm, sturdy.
[^s19-16]: Mīrzā Muḥammad Ḥusayn Nāʾīnī (1277–1355 AH) was born in the city of Nāʾīn. After benefiting from the presence of the scholars of Isfahan, he set out for the holy Najaf and the lessons of Mīrzā-yi Shīrāzī. With the formation of the Constitutional movement in Iran, he declared his support for it and composed the book *Tanbīh al-Ummah wa Tanzīh al-Millah*. In this book he brings proofs for establishing the absolute guardianship of the jurist (wilāyat-i muṭlaqah-yi faqīh) in the age of occultation. The late Nāʾīnī, in addition to playing an effective role in the Constitutional movement of Iran, was an influential figure against the colonial domination of England over the country of Iraq.
[^s19-17]: "He does whatever he wills, and rules in whatever manner he wishes."
[^s19-18]: Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ (21:23): "In what He does there is no questioning."
[^s19-19]: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:66): "The chiefs of his people who disbelieved said…"
[^s19-20]: A group used to walk ahead of the king's harem on foot, calling out "clear the way, get back" — that is, "close your eyes and move aside from the road."
[^s19-21]: Sūrat al-Muʾmin / Ghāfir (40:36): "And Pharaoh said: O Hāmān, build me a lofty tower."
[^s19-22]: "Call me your own servant, that I may attain to kingship / a fly that you set flying is a falcon." (Saʿdī)
[^s19-23]: Literally, to strike someone with a sword.
[^s19-24]: A large tent or curtain (tutuq).
[^s19-25]: Al-Mughīrah ibn Shuʿbah was from Ṭāʾif; he came to Medina in the fifth year of the Hijra and embraced Islam. After the passing of the noble Prophet, he was among the key counselors and operators of the people of the Saqīfah of Banū Sāʿidah. After the martyrdom of the Commander of the Faithful, he made his way to the court of Muʿāwiyah. The selection of Yazīd as crown prince was his proposal.
[^s19-26]: Ziyād ibn Abīhi — his mother's name was Sumayyah, but his father's name was not clear, so he was known as "Ziyād, son of his father." In the time of the second caliph, Abū Sufyān attributed him to himself, but met with the immediate rebuke of Imam ʿAlī. After the Battle of the Camel, Ziyād was for a time, on behalf of the Commander of the Faithful, the governor of Basra. In the affair of the peace of Imam Ḥasan, he at first stood against Muʿāwiyah, but finally was deceived by Muʿāwiyah. Muʿāwiyah, contrary to the explicit text of the ruling of Islam, called him his own brother. He later became ruler of Basra and Kufa and severely harassed and tormented the Shīʿah of the Commander of the Faithful. ʿUbayd Allāh ibn Ziyād, one of the killers of Karbala, was his son.
[^s19-27]: Sūrat al-Tawbah (9:34).
[^s19-28]: Hadith no. 4. «اعدی عدوک نفسک التی بین جنبیک» — "Your most hostile enemy is your own soul that lies between your two sides."
[^s19-29]: Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:76).
[^s19-30]: Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:257).
[^s19-31]: Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:112).
[^s19-32]: Sūrat al-Muʾmin / Ghāfir (40:26).
[^s19-33]: Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:113).
[^s19-34]: Sūrat al-Muʾmin / Ghāfir (40:23–25).
[^s19-35]: Sūrat Sabaʾ (34:34–35).
[^s19-36]: Sūrat al-Tawbah (9:34).


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 20: The Outcome of Prophethood (1)

*فرجام نبوت (۱) — delivered Tuesday, 16 Mehr 1353 / 21 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Raʿd (13:16–18):** "Say: God is the Creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-Subduing. He sent down water from the sky, and the riverbeds flowed each according to its measure, and the flood carried a swelling froth. And from that which they heat in the fire, seeking to make ornaments or utensils, there rises a froth like it. Thus does God strike [the parable of] the true and the false. As for the froth, it goes away as scum; but as for that which benefits people, it remains in the earth. Thus does God set forth the parables. For those who respond to their Lord is the most beautiful reward. But as for those who do not respond to Him — even if they possessed all that is on earth and the like of it besides, they would offer it as ransom. Theirs is a terrible reckoning; their abode is Hell, and what an evil resting place!"[^s20-1]

*(To the gentlemen who have a Qur'an with them, and to those who have these mimeographed sheets — please look at these sheets. I will recite the verses. Some of the vowel-marks on the verses are incorrect; correct them with your pens.*

*I also have a request to make of the friends: if you sense that there is no room inside the prayer hall, do not come in from outside into the hall, because then space becomes cramped and continuing the talk becomes a little difficult. If the friends and brothers who are inside the hall, and those who are outside, are ready for us to begin our talk, let them all send a collective ṣalawāt [blessing on the Prophet and his family].)*

*Now, gentlemen, I ask you to look at those sheets of yours — those who have them. Some of the vowel-marks on the verses have minor errors. I will recite, and if you see any mistake, correct it.*

The next verses pertain to Sūrat al-Ṣaffāt. Those who have a Qur'an, find Sūrat al-Ṣaffāt.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ṣaffāt (37:171–180):** "Our word has already gone forth to Our servants, the messengers: that they will surely be the ones helped, and that Our hosts will surely be the victors. So turn away from them for a while, and watch them — for soon they too shall see. Do they then seek to hasten Our punishment? When it descends upon their courtyard, evil will be the morning of those who were warned. So turn away from them for a while, and watch — for soon they too shall see. Glory be to your Lord, the Lord of Might, above what they ascribe."[^s20-2]

The prophets, with that very lofty and progressive aim that we explained on the previous days — an aim that, even by the cultural standards of the age of light and the age of human awareness, that is, the twentieth century, is the most progressive of all aims — namely the aim of bringing humankind into one class, of leveling and equalizing human beings, and of casting out ignorance and poverty and oppression and exploitation and class division — with this most precious and progressive and lofty aim, the prophets were commissioned and set foot in their societies.

A continuous chain of activity and striving and struggle is the harvest of the lives of God's prophets. From the very beginning of the mission, never again to sleep in comfort, never again to slumber on the bed of ease,[^s20-3] never again to taste pleasure and rest, but to struggle unceasingly — this is the summary of the story of the divine prophets. At the end of their lives, too — as we have learned from the religious sources — some had their heads severed from their bodies by the tyrants and despots of the age and carried off as a trophy; some were placed within a tree and sawn from head to foot, cut in two; some passed from this world in exile and in distress. None of them amassed any wealth, however little; none of them, at the end of their lives, left behind a palace, an apparatus, and vast riches, as the rulers of this world and the worldly leaders do. This is the summary of the lives of the prophets and the envoys and the deputed ones and the messengers of God; these things we have come to know.

A question now arises. As it stands in our minds, and as we read in the history of the prophetic missions, the prophets all passed their lives in struggle and striving, and many of them drank the draught of martyrdom. So was their work, then, a fruitless and useless work? Did the prophets meet defeat in history? Is it as the simple-minded of the world still imagine it to be — and as the anti-prophetic powers, too, are not displeased to have lodged in the minds of the common people — that the prophets gained no fruitful result from their striving, and that across the whole of history, oppression and tyranny and inhumanity and rebellion and unbelief have held sway? Is it so?

We believe that it is not so. We believe that these dear deputies of God — whose chain began with Adam and Noah and Abraham, and in whose chain the choicest of creation came one after another — these men not only were not defeated, not only did not fail, not only did not see what they said and what they wanted in the world, in history, in society meet with defeat; rather, among all of humanity, and among all those who have ever run after some aim and goal and striven for it, no one has had the chance of success that the prophets had. This is our belief. We believe that the outcome of prophethood and the end of the prophets' work, throughout all history, in the time that is past, has ended according to what was desired — and so it will be in the future too. And we shall prove this.

There are two matters and two topics here. (I ask the brothers who have heard this series of discussions from the previous days to pay careful attention, so that the important part of this chapter may be connected to the earlier discussions; and the brothers who, it seems, were not here on the other days and have come today — let them attend well to this very topic again, for in itself it is a topic that must be grasped and known. But you must pay attention.)

We have two matters here. The first is this: this chain that is known by the name of prophethood and messengership — that is, the caravan of the prophets from Adam to the Seal [of the Prophets] — what did they do? What did the totality of them accomplish? Did they push things forward, or did they go bankrupt? This is one matter. The other matter is this: each of the great divine prophets, in his own time — did he succeed, or did he meet defeat? These are two questions, and I would like to finish both of them today, God willing. By your own measure, if you reflect, you will find that you would like to understand this matter. This is something the knowing of which is beneficial to us — by the very criterion I have always placed in your hands.

It is beneficial in the sense that it not only increases our information — for there are many words that increase a person's information but are of no benefit to him. There is no harm at all in all of you, this whole gathering, now being aware of how the tiny particles of the rocks in the volcanic mountains of the moon are composed. To know it — is that bad? But is it beneficial? No. Much of the information that we run after, or that they make us run after, is of this sort. After we have sat and listened and spoken and studied and researched and put pen to paper and brought our life to its end, then when we look, we see a heap of empty information, useless information, that did not bring us a single step closer to Paradise and to the good pleasure of God, nor a single step closer to the sound, authentic Islamic society in this world; it did nothing at all. Yes, it had only this benefit: that we could pride ourselves, could believe that we are very learned, very knowledgeable, and could close upon ourselves the window of receptivity and learn nothing more from anyone; this was its only benefit.

This matter that I am presenting is beneficial in the true sense; it brings not only awareness but awareness and commitment. It is of that kind of faith that I was explaining and speaking of to you here on the third and fourth and fifth of the month. The first question is this: this chain whose members are called the prophets — from first to last, what flower did they pin upon the head of humanity? The answer is this: the prophets came and took a being who could not even, at the level of an animal, distinguish the road from the pit — a being in whom even instinct was not so capable and path-opening — and they brought this being up to the level of a being from whom even the angels of heaven must come and learn something. They brought humankind from the lowest depth[^s20-4] of savagery and ignorance up to the level of a civilized human being, such that if he wishes to act according to their teachings, the loftiest, the most beautiful, the most eloquent manifestations of creation will appear in his life.

The prophets treated human beings like a schoolchild — I am trying to bring the matter down to a very low level — who knows nothing, not even the alphabet.
