For one year they labored over him, and he came to the second grade; but the teacher of the first grade, in the very midst of this,[^s20-5] when the child had reached the second grade, had laid down his own life over this work. They labored for another period, and he came to the third grade; but the teacher who had brought him from the second to the third grade lost his own life on this path. They labored for another period, and he came to the fourth grade; and again that compassionate teacher, that kind father, that aware guide — over the course of this time during which he was raising the pupil to a higher grade, to a higher rank — lost his own life, departed from this world, and passed his life in hardship. Grade by grade, step by step, stage by stage, they kept bringing this little child up, and up, and up. Now, when you look, you see that the level of his culture, his knowledge, his insight, his sensitivity is far higher than in that time which was two periods before the present, and he is at the peak of progress and elevation and comprehension and understanding and thought.

You look, and the teachers are not there. Where are the teachers? One, in the midst of the first grade, while he was bringing this slow-witted pupil up — let us say — gave up his life. Another, while these very pupils were learning something from him, at the end of the year, over some disagreement, over some matter, gathered together, rose up against him, and killed him. The teacher of the third grade in another way, the teacher of the fourth grade in another way. The teachers are not there; each teacher carried out his mission and, to outward appearance, died in failure and perished. But did he really fail? Think well; see whether that teacher really failed. What, after all, was the teacher's aim? Did not this compassionate teacher want exactly this? He wanted this pupil to rise from the black dust to the summit of the peak of culture and knowledge and understanding — and has this not now come to pass?

So the teachers did not die as failures. It is true that they died, it is true that they did not attain the affluent, comfortable life of this world, it is true that they did not see with their own eyes this sapling come to fruit — but did they fail? No, they did not fail. Their aim was precisely this: that this human being should traverse the first and second grade; that this ignorant, unaware student should go up this road with difficulty, with hardship, with toil, until he reached that summit of the peak — and now he has reached it.

The prophets were just like this: Adam, Noah, Lot, Hud, Salih, Shuʿayb, Abraham, Ishmael, Moses, Jesus, and thousands of other prophets throughout history — their work was this: to bring this human being up, to teach him knowledge and understanding, to prepare him for ordering the life to come, to give him the necessary substance. And this work was accomplished — although, along the way, a number of these prophets were killed in a horrible manner, in a pitiable form, and departed from this world without seeing this end. And still humanity is moving forward.

Today the world, more than it was a thousand years ago, is prepared to hear the true word of Islam. Today humanity is more ready to accept the divine government than it was a thousand years ago, than it was twelve or thirteen hundred years ago — and a thousand years from now it will be more ready than today. The day that our Imam of the Age — may God hasten his noble relief — went into occultation from sight, and as an Imam who must spread out the carpet [of justice] among the people does not spread it out, on that day humankind was not ready to accept a revolutionary reforming Imam with sword in hand. And if that noble one had wanted to rise up and make a revolution and overturn society, he certainly could not, given the unfavorable conditions of that time, have built a society such as he desired.

The experience of the noble Imams of the Prophet's household had reached the point that this society had become so corrupted, the oppressive and tyrannical hands of the despotic powers, of the Umayyads and the Abbasids, had so worked this field for the worse and poisoned it and corrupted it, that no sound plant could any longer be grown in it; and so the Imam, peace be upon him, was hidden from sight. The day he comes — how will it be? The day he will appear — and we do not know whether it is ten years away or ten thousand years away, nothing is known at all — on that day, when the Imam appears, humanity will have reached readiness; it will be ready to hear and to accept the true word of Islam. It will be ready to carry the elevated and lofty Islamic society upon its own shoulders. On that day humanity will be ready for the Qur'an to be put into practice. But in the time of the Imam of the Age it was not ready — and who has done this work? The teachings of the prophets have done it, and the Imams are the followers of the prophets.

In the discussion of the Imamate, when I expound the philosophy of the Imamate, I will explain what the philosophy of the Imam's existence is; I will make this clear. So the prophets were not unsuccessful in history, and we see that every day humanity has moved forward and added to its own age, it has come one step closer to the peak of progress and perfection and elevation. What more do we want than this? What do the prophets want? The Lord of the worlds wants to bring this unripe, raw substance, by a natural movement, toward its natural and innate destination — that is, perfection and elevation. This the Lord of the worlds has willed, and humanity will certainly reach that point of ultimate perfection. And this is the determinism of history; the determinism of history means just this, and in this form he [who used the phrase] spoke correctly.

These are precise discussions; I ask you to pay attention, to the very words and expressions. The course of humankind is toward elevation and perfection, and toward the promised paradise of this world; that is, humanity will see a day for itself in which, on that day, there will be no oppression, no evil, no ugliness — everything will be according to the desire of humanity. The creation of the human being is like this, and the creation of the world; that this being, in its own course, will at last arrive at such a destination — it must arrive. In that era when humanity will have ready all that is necessary for its perfection, then it will enter the channel of progress and perfection and elevation, and with the utmost speed and pace it will move toward God — that is, toward absolute perfection. And the more we have come forward from the beginning of human history up to this time of ours, the closer we have drawn toward that destination; this is the determinism of history. The creation of the human being is like this, and the creation of the world; God has so created that human beings move toward progress and elevation, willy-nilly. As for the "willy-nilly," you must of course derive its meaning in the course of these discussions — what "willy-nilly" means — for later, when we tie it to the subsequent discussion, it will become clear what it means. Of course the will of human beings is intensely involved, and it is the wanting of human beings that brings human beings to progress, and this wanting will be there.

This is one of the principles among the Islamic principles; in the Islamic worldview, this is among the certainties: that the end of humankind is in accordance with goodness. Why? Because God Most High created the earth and the heavens in accordance with truth; and He created the human being too with a truth-seeking innate nature. Since the human being possesses will, he must move along the path that accords with his innate nature, in order to reach that destination. And who can present this path to him and tell him: if you do such-and-such, you have acted in accordance with your innate nature? The prophets — the prophets come for this. They come to show humankind the path of innate nature; that is, to hasten and ease his course toward a good end. Therefore human beings are moving forward; humanity is drawing day by day closer to felicity and to the destination of goodness, and this is because of the movement that the prophets gave it. The prophets set it in motion; if some delay has occurred in the middle of history, and in each segment and stage of the stages of history, it is because of a relative distancing from the teachings of the prophets — but in the end they had this course. This is one matter in general.

In the first question, we draw this conclusion: we say that the great divine prophets, although each one of them individually met with deprivations and failures, yet in the aggregate, the course of humanity has been toward progress and elevation, and its agent was the prophets. It was the prophets who pushed the human being toward that intended destination and toward the kneaded-in human end, set him in motion, helped him in traversing this road; this is the first question.

But now the second question — the one that is of greater interest to many — this is the second question that I shall make clear for you. The second question is this: when a prophet brings about a movement in the world, raises up a revolution and an uprising, can it be said that this revolution has a good outcome, or not? Can one hope that the end of this work is good? Or can one not hope? What is the general rule here?

Some say: however much we look, we see that wherever a true word issued from a tongue, wherever the melody of truth reached the ear from some quarter, in the end it did not reach result and fruit, and it was choked off. From this they have constructed an "experience"; they say: the experience of human history has always shown us that the prophets failed. Now, even granting that you say overall they succeeded — fine — but in the end the revolution they brought about, they were unable to bring it to fruit. Not in a single place were they able to bring truth to power and destroy falsehood. So then, what should we do? Should we too, after this, not raise a hand, not draw it from our sleeve, not follow the path of the prophets? Since the great divine prophets themselves, in their own time, were unable to accomplish anything, and in the end falsehood has always — even if seasonally and by stages — gained the upper hand over truth, then let the truth-seekers and the truth-speakers of the world sheathe their swords, go and lie down to sleep, and know that it is of no use — unless some hand should come out of some sleeve, a hand of power, an unseen hand should come forth and do something.

This is the logic of many people, and this is the very logic that I mentioned: the tyrants of history take great pleasure in this logic. This is the very logic that the bullies of the age have always loved the people to hold as their belief — that is, that the people should believe that, sir, no striving, no activity, no struggle in the path of truth will ever reach a result. This the tyrants of the world have always wanted.

The political bluffs of the heads of the states of the world, which you read so much of in the newspapers that carry world news, are for precisely this purpose. That certain man, who has only a few hours or a few days left before the throne of his rule collapses and he comes down from the peak of the presidency to become an ordinary man — that very man, even a few days before, is bluffing: "We will crush our enemies, we will wipe out our opponents, we will continue to rule" — just as, quite recently in the world and on a very vast global scale, you yourselves saw with your own eyes, read, were informed: a power of such magnitude suddenly comes down, turns into an ordinary man, but a little before this action and reaction, this transfer and transformation, he says, "They cannot, they cannot, they cannot touch us, our position will not be lost" — and then we see that it was lost. These bluffs are for precisely this purpose. They always wish to make the people understand in this way: that nothing can come from your hands, because our power is a power that you cannot even imagine. All means and resources are at our disposal, and you have none.

Do you imagine that these things have arisen in the world only today? No; they have existed at all times in history. This is a very interesting and amusing affair in history that, whenever I recall it, I am amazed at the cunning of al-Mansur the Abbasid. Al-Mansur the Abbasid was, as you know, the greatest and most powerful caliph of the Abbasids, and the second Abbasid caliph. As it happened, in al-Mansur's time, opposition had begun from the margins of the religious community of that day. Among those who had placed al-Mansur under severe pressure were Muhammad ibn ʿAbdullah and Ibrahim ibn ʿAbdullah, the two sons of ʿAbdullah al-Mahd.[^s20-6] These two men struggled fiercely against al-Mansur. Al-Mansur had been rendered helpless. Although everything was at al-Mansur's disposal and these two self-sacrificing brothers had nothing at their disposal but faith and a handful of believers, nevertheless, in the face of their relentless striving and activity and struggle, al-Mansur had lost his footing. Many rumors, concerning the power and might of al-Mansur's apparatus, were being spread among the people, with the sole aim of preventing the true believers and the genuine Shiʿa from joining these two brothers. Among them was this: al-Mansur had spread the rumor that he had a mirror, and that when he looked into this mirror he could tell where Ibrahim ibn ʿAbdullah was — and woe to that man into whose house al-Mansur, looking into the mirror, should see that Ibrahim was, or with whom he was fleeing. The story of the mirror — the very same one that, a thousand, two thousand years before al-Mansur, those tyrant kings of history, Jamshid and this one and that one, fashioned in this form as the world-revealing cup. No doubt this too was for the same purpose: that the enemies should know and imagine that they could never carry out any deed outside the sight of these powers. They had even resorted to such things.

Therefore the tyrants of the world ask of the God of the two worlds — a God in whom they do not believe — that the people should imagine that the prophetic missions and the great divine prophets and the envoys of truth and reality have always been doomed to defeat. They ask of God that the people should imagine and suppose that no truth-worshipping melody will ever receive a favorable and positive response from the nature of this world. They ask of God that the people should despair of any movement ever growing and reaching fruit against falsehood without being defeated. But is the reality of the matter really like this? Because a number of minds under the sway [of these powers] and uninformed have imagined it so, because the people have supposed that the prophets and the followers of the prophets and the Imams have always been defeated and that every movement in the world will be defeated — are we too obliged to accept it? Should we set aside the Qur'an? Should we not hear correctly the word of the Qur'an, which recounts to us the opposite of this logic, and instead hear the word of false fancies? No; we will not do this. We turn to the Qur'an; we take a lesson from the Qur'an as to what the outcome of the prophetic missions is, and what effect the revolutionary movement of the prophets and their followers can have. Let us learn this from the Qur'an, then we return to the text[^s20-7] of history, and we see that history too bears witness to this matter.

The Qur'an strikes a parable in these very verses that I recited, and which I now recite again and translate; it tells us: No! In the world there is a truth and there is a falsehood; the truth remains, the falsehood is annihilated. From the mountains, in the rivers, water flows and streams; froth appears on the surface of the water; the froth dies, the waters remain. Falsehood is that very froth that will die; the water is that very truth that will remain.

Up to here, as the Qur'an teaches us and historical reality also confirms it, each of the prophetic missions that has appeared in this world, from first to last — besides having, in the chain of the prophetic missions of history, performed a deed, having moved humankind one step forward — each of them in itself also possessed the possibility of success. It is not as al-Mansur the Abbasid kept pouring into the people's ears, saying, "It is not possible, people; it is not possible for you to carry anything forward against my power." That is not how the matter is. It is not as the powers of history, over the course of time, made the people understand and instilled in them — that against the worldly powers who are upon falsehood, truth will have no room even to draw breath. No, it is not so; rather it is the opposite of this. The prophets have everywhere had the possibility of success, and the followers of the prophets too everywhere have the possibility of success, and they will surely succeed — but on two conditions, there are two conditions (here is where I said that the human will is involved in the building of the future of history). If these two conditions exist, then all the movements that, on the basis of religion, on the basis of Qur'anic and Islamic thought, have been carried out and will be carried out throughout history, will succeed forever and will be victorious; there is no need for a miracle either. It is not necessary that a miracle must certainly take place for the adherents of religion to succeed and be victorious — no; without any hand coming forth from the unseen, if these two conditions exist, Islam and the Qur'an and religion and Qur'anic thought will have the essential possibility of success. What are those two conditions?

One of those two conditions is faith — a belief grounded in awareness, a conviction accompanied by the acceptance of commitment, an assent accompanied by striving and movement; this is the first condition. The second condition is patience [ṣabr]. What does patience mean? It means resistance, not fleeing the field, not abandoning the work at the sensitive and dangerous moment.

Wherever you see, throughout the history of the prophetic missions, a prophet defeated by the tyrant of his time, it has been because his followers, those around him, did not have sufficient faith or sufficient patience. Every time you see that truth has advanced, it is because they possessed brimming faith and the necessary patience. Is there any witness from the Qur'an to this matter? Yes; there are several witnesses in the Qur'an. The verses I recite today are the intellectual foundation, and there are many other verses too that state the matter. I will read two of its verses for you.

One verse is this:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:139):** "Do not be faint, and do not grieve; for you are the higher, if you are believers."[^s20-8]

Here it is a verse of the Qur'an — this is not a narration that someone might cast doubt on, saying perhaps its chain of transmission is weak; nor is it an ambiguous [mutashābih] verse[^s20-9] either, that some might cast doubt on, saying we do not understand its meaning — no; clear, plain. *"Do not be faint"* means: O Muslims, do not become weak. *"And do not grieve"*: and do not be sorrowful. *"For you are the higher"*: you are the higher, your hand is the upper one, you will be the victorious, you will overcome the enemy, your thought will at last be seated upon the throne[^s20-10] of the world's governance. What is its condition? *"If you are believers"* — if you are believers, if you have faith, if faith exists, this is so.

You say: "Sir, I have seen Imam al-Sadiq — the blessings of God be upon him — struggle so much against the tyrant of his time and yet was unable to seize the government." Yes; but that was because those around him, many of his friends, many of the claimants to his Shiʿism, were like you and me, were like us — impatient, weak in faith. Did you want Imam al-Sadiq to be able to reach power against the established custom of the Lord in history? This very thing is itself proof that no helplessness [requiring a miracle] and no unseen hand is meant to appear in the field of conquests and victories. If a miracle were meant to occur, then a miracle would have occurred so that Imam al-Sadiq might reach power. Imam al-Sadiq was, in the end, killed at the hand of the tyrant of his own time; and Musa ibn Jaʿfar likewise. This was due to the fact that his friends and followers did not have, to the necessary degree, as much patience as is needed; faith and conviction together with commitment had not been stored up in their souls to the degree they should have been. If today, too, it is like that, the result is defeat — the defeat of Islam, the defeat of the Qur'an, the condemnation of the Muslims. But if today we became like the time of the Prophet! The Muslim of the Prophet's time — his faith was a certain and indubitable faith. His patience was that very patience before which the angels of heaven were small; a patience that, on a day of the month of Ramadan, in the heat of the Hijaz, with a fasting mouth, fights the enemy and digs a trench — this is the patience of that Muslim.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:153):** "Seek help through patience and prayer."[^s20-11] By means of the weapon of patience, conquer your enemies; by means of patience, carry your aims forward. This is the miracle that belongs to patience. That hand which must come out of the sleeve and do something — it is the hand of human patience; patience.

We even misconstrue patience. We have imagined that patience means that we should now sit and watch how things will turn out. I tell you how things will turn out: it does not mean that we should sit and watch. If you sit without activity, without striving, without throbbing effort, the end is wretchedness, it is abasement, it is disgrace, it is irreligion and worldly ruin.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḥajj (22:11):** "the loss of this world and the hereafter — that is the manifest loss."[^s20-12] But if you go forward with patience, the end is both religion and this world.

This is the end that the Qur'an tells us of. These verses are from Sūrat al-Aʿrāf — which, if I wanted to write out, would become lengthy; you must go and look, reflect over it. What I am saying — my heart wishes that you would act on it as a religious mode of thought. Truly pay attention; if you see that it accords with the Islamic standards, accept it as a mode of thought. This is the Qur'an, in Sūrat al-Aʿrāf, from verse 120 onward — indeed even before these — the verse I have in mind is verses 126 and 127. The matter is raised through the affair of Pharaoh and Moses: Moses comes and states the divine call, Pharaoh does not accept it, does not assent to it, and not only does he not accept it, but he even threatens Moses. After all these words, the sorcerers of Pharaoh come; their sorcery accomplishes nothing against the Mosaic miracle, and in the end they themselves come to faith. Pharaoh threatens them, saying, "I will kill you. Why did you believe in Moses?" All this passes, and after the affair of Pharaoh's magicians and their defeat by Moses, then it is like this:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:127):** "And the chiefs of Pharaoh's people said: …" The chiefs — the leaders and commanders and notables of Pharaoh's people said to Pharaoh: *"Will you leave Moses and his people to spread corruption in the land…?"* — "Are you releasing, letting go of Moses and his people, the believers in him, his party, his throng, that they should make corruption in the land? That they should cause disorder? You are letting them go? *…and abandon you and your gods?*" — and that they should set aside you and your gods? Is it like this?[^s20-13]

*"He said…"* — Pharaoh, in the face of this speech and this casting of doubt, said: No; I will destroy Moses. He prepared a harsh assault against Moses, devised a new scheme for crushing the party of the Moseans in Egypt. What was it?

> *"He said: We will slay their sons and let their women live."* Pharaoh said: I will not leave Moses be; I will not permit what he wants to do; rather I will kill their young men and keep their women alive. This is a new plan. Pharaoh, after he had struggled against Moses in every way and had thought that Moses would be destroyed, has now reached the conclusion that — no, even his sorcerers and magicians are coming to faith in him, and the sorcery will not stand up against the miracle — he falls to thinking of carrying out a severe measure. The plan becomes a plan of severity; he says we must resort to severity. What shall we do? All the young men who have inclined toward Moses — these we will kill. All their women we will keep alive. *"and let their women live"* — taking the women alive, keeping the women alive — no doubt for debauchery, or to ruin their lineage, or for something of this sort.

Well, the matter became hard now; the party of Moses found themselves facing the severe-measures plan of the Pharaonic apparatus. This is the place where the bones of the elephant tremble; this is the place where firm and sturdy hearts are shaken — is it a joke? Someone like Pharaoh saying, "I will burn the fathers of them; I have a plan for them, to leave them not a single young man." *"We will slay their sons"* — I will kill their young men. This is the place where the Moseans must be charged up; in this very sensitive and dangerous moment they must not feel defeat.

A sentence came to my mind from one of the great men of our own history — from about a hundred years ago, fifty or sixty years ago. They used to quote a sentence from him in these constitutional struggles and the like; his name is much on people's tongues. They say he used to tell his companions, his friends: "Fight, struggle. At the very moment when you see that the work is hard, struggle still. Until you see that you will certainly be defeated, keep on; reach the point where you see that now you will certainly be defeated; and when you reach the point where you see that you will surely be defeated, struggle still — then you will be victorious." And this is a correct saying. A nation, a community, an individual who wants to strive for his own welfare and salvation, at one moment is hopeful that this striving will bear fruit; when the severity increases, his hope too diminishes. If he lets go of the striving, then assuredly his striving will no longer reach a result; but if he carries on his striving in that very place, continues it so far, goes forward so far, until he sees that it is certainly futile — if at the very moment he sees that this striving of his is futile, he strives still, that striving is successful.

Moses is using this scheme to mobilize the Children of Israel. When the Children of Israel see that they will certainly be defeated — because Pharaoh is resolved to kill all their young men — against the new Pharaonic scheme, Moses too presents his own new scheme. What does he say? When they find themselves facing this situation, Pharaoh speaks thus; Moses, in turn, in the face of this Pharaonic proclamation:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (7:128):** *"Moses said to his people: …"* Moses says: *"Seek help from God and be patient."* Ask aid of God and be patient. Continue, resist, do not leave your striving half-finished — hope breathes within this — why? *"Indeed, the earth belongs to God"* — the earth is God's; *"He gives it as inheritance to whom He wills among His servants"* — He gives it and grants it to whomever of His servants He wills. That is, we are the servants of God; Moses tells the Children of Israel: you are the servants of God; the servants of Pharaoh — their work will reach no result. The earth is for the servants of God. *"And the [good] end belongs to the God-fearing"* — the outcome of the work, the end of the affair, belongs to the people of piety.[^s20-14]

This is the Qur'an, and historical reality too conveys and shows us the very same thing. The very Abraham whom one day they cast into the fire — one day we see that in Mecca and at the House of God he forms a monotheistic society, and for centuries after himself that society endures. Moses, who one day has such a conflict with Pharaoh and the Children of Israel are under pressure — another day we see that the command to enter the holy land is given to him: *"O my people, enter the holy land…"*[^s20-15] — go there, and there form the divine, monotheistic society. Jesus son of Mary, whose striving and activity during the short period of his sojourn upon the earth and among human beings did not reach any outward fruit — after he ascends from the surface of the earth and departs from among the people, after one or two centuries the greatest powers of the world of that day fall under the sway of the thought of the Messiah; that is, the Roman Empire, the vast Roman Empire, falls under the sway of Christian thought, and the emperor himself becomes a Christian and becomes a believer in the religion of the Messiah.

Our Prophet, who in Mecca was so much under pressure — for thirteen years they arranged for him the most severe tortures and conditions — then comes to Medina, forms a government there, brings a society into being, establishes an order, drives human beings toward perfection, and crushes his foundational enemies, casts them to the ground. But under the shadow of what? Under the shadow of faith and patience. Whenever there was faith and there was patience,

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:125):** *"Yes, if you are patient and God-fearing…"*[^s20-16] — if faith together with deeds and patience existed, if piety together with faith was present, then it is that victory comes. And this is one of the customs of the world, the custom of the Lord in history. Yesterday it was like this, today it is like this, tomorrow too it will be like this. All the religious powers, if they have been able to become a power or to remain in being, it has been under the shadow of faith and patience.

Today too, those who love the Qur'an, Islam, Tawhid, prophethood, the sacred Islamic principles — those who wish that these could take into hand the reins and governance of people's lives in the world, those who wish to see God sovereign in the world — let them strengthen within themselves two substances: the substance of faith and the substance of patience. If the substance of faith and the substance of patience grow strong within us,

> *"Indeed, the earth belongs to God; He gives it as inheritance to whom He wills among His servants, and the [good] end belongs to the God-fearing"* — but without faith and without patience it cannot be.

This is the answer that we give to these two questions. The first question was: did the divine prophets, in the aggregate, succeed or fail? We say: in the aggregate they succeeded, all the prophets — for the reason that they wanted to bring humankind up, and they brought it up. Of course, in the various gatherings and assemblies in which — in lessons, in Qur'anic exegesis, in the discussions after the prayer — I have raised this matter, each time I would give an example; if I wanted to give all those examples it would take long. I gave you one of its examples: the schoolchild.

The prophets in the aggregate succeeded and were triumphant; they did not fail. But now, each single one of the prophets — that is, each single one of the divine and monotheistic revolutionary movements — did they succeed or not? Here we say there is a general rule. The general rule is this: each one that possessed sufficient faith and patience succeeded; each one that did not possess sufficient faith and patience did not succeed.

Now pay attention while I read these verses of the Qur'an. Today's verses are from two places: one from Sūrat al-Raʿd, and the other from Sūrat al-Ṣaffāt.

*(Allow me to mention this matter first, because I am afraid I will forget it, and I also become tired and may not be able to say it. Gentlemen, this recommendation has been made to me on behalf of the friends attached to this mosque — and I myself, of course, was not unwilling to say it — concerning help to this mosque, which in the previous sessions, at the beginning of the month, we once mentioned. With the situation that has arisen regarding that back courtyard and a place for the ladies and a passageway for the ladies' coming and going, and likewise concerning the electrical arrangements here — you have observed how disordered our electricity situation is, suddenly leaving us in the middle of our work — it needs a great deal of help. Of course, of the tasks that are very hard for me and that I do not really know how to do well, this is one: to take money from people for the mosque and the like. This is one of the tasks that is very difficult for me and that, truth be told, I really do not know how to do — you see that my present conduct shows that I do not know how. But I wanted to say it in summary: let the gentlemen who have financial means make an effort, take pains, and finish this work as soon as possible. Here, these friends who have shops beside the mosque are ready to accept the gentlemen's cash contributions. Please refer your cash help or your pledges to them, and refer to me as well, and let the friends apply themselves. This is money that, for such a work, is very little — though in itself, of course, it is a large sum. Let this work be carried out. This is something I wanted to say at the end of the talk, and in the middle of the talk it was perhaps a little out of place; but since I saw that there is a great likelihood that I would forget, and I would certainly have forgotten, and just now it suddenly came to my mind, I said: now that it has come to mind, let me say it. Well, now send a ṣalawāt so that I may read the verses. [The audience sends ṣalawāt.])*

> *"Say: God is the Creator of all things."*[^s20-17] Say: God is the Creator of everything. Since He created, He can say what its outcome will be. Because afterward He wants to speak about the outcome of truth and falsehood, He first says: We created this world, We know the customs and laws of history, hear them from Us. *"Say: God is the Creator of all things"* — say: God is the Creator of everything. *"And He is the One"* — He is the sole and unique; *"the All-Subduing"* — the powerful and mighty.

*"He sent down water from the sky"* — He is striking a parable for you. This verse is so beautiful in respect of its Arabic phrasing — unfortunately, those who are not familiar with the Arabic language cannot properly grasp this beauty either. He does not say beforehand, "I am striking a parable"; you who are listening do not realize that this is a likeness and to what it applies, you do not catch it; at the end you understand what the matter is — He is striking a parable. *"He sent down water from the sky"* — from the sky He sent down water, a rain.

*"and the riverbeds flowed each according to its measure"* — rivers and streams of various sizes flowed; small rivers, large rivers flowed from the rainwater. *"and the flood carried a swelling froth"* — this flood that flowed in the rivers carried upon its shoulders a swollen, prominent froth. When you stand beside a river that the flood-water is bringing along, what you see is not the water; it is froth. The water is beneath the froth. These froths, prominent and surging upon this water that flows in the river, the flood-water, are showing themselves off. You see froth; beneath the froth is water.

Let us go to another place — the other example is this: *"And from that which they heat in the fire"* — those things that they heat in the fire, like mineral substances and like iron, which they place in the fire; *"seeking [to make] ornament or utensil"* — in order to make some adornment, they place gold in the fire so as to make jewelry; they place iron in the fire so as to make objects; they place copper in the fire so as to make things. These things that they place in the fire so as to make from them an ornament and adornment[^s20-18] and finery and goods and wares — in these too there is *"a froth like it"* — a froth there is, like the froth of the river-water. There too, when they melt this iron, they see that upon it there is froth; it too has a froth. When they melt gold, it too has a particular froth. There the froth was upon the water — what was the essence? What was the life-giving substance? The water or the froth? The water. But what met the eye outwardly? What was making a display? What showed itself more? The froth.

Here, is the desired means the iron or the froth, in the melting of iron? The gold or the froth, in the melting of gold? Of course the gold, of course the iron. What is the froth? A parasitic thing, a superfluous thing. But which comes more to the eye? That froth — not that gold and not that iron. Once these are set up, immediately He says: *"Thus does God strike [the parable of] the true and the false"* — God shows you truth and falsehood in this way; the parable of truth and falsehood God strikes for you in this way. *"As for the froth"* — that is, the thing that you saw to be visible, that very froth, that is the false. The thing that is hidden beneath the froth — that is, the water, that is, the gold, that is, the iron — that is the truth.

Now that you have learned this much of the parable, hear from God the continuation of the parable — what its outcome will be. *"As for the froth, it goes away as scum"* — but those froths will go to vanity and waste and emptiness, will be annihilated. The froth on the river is not permanent; it is there one instant, gone the next. When you open the water onto your field, what remains for you from the river is the water, not the froth; its froth perishes. *"As for the froth, it goes away as scum"* — but that froth goes to annihilation and emptiness; *"and as for that which benefits people"* — but that which benefits people, that is, that water, that iron, that gold, it tarries *"and remains in the earth"*, it is not annihilated. *"Thus does God set forth the parables"* — God strikes the parable for you in this way. He means to say: the truth remains. The call of the prophets is truth, the movement of the prophets is truth, and it remains. The falsehoods that puff out their chests against the prophets, show themselves off, rebel, run riot — they are froth, they are bubbles upon the water, they are doomed to perish. This is the parable that God strikes.

The continuation of the verse applies this same parable to the field of the social struggles of the Muslims. He says: *"For those who respond to their Lord is the most beautiful"* — for those who answer their Lord are the most beautiful of outcomes and rewards; the good end is for them. The end of the work is for those who respond to the movement of the prophets. *"But those who do not respond to Him"* — but those who do not respond and step onto the path of falsehood, they — *"even if they had all that is in the earth"* — if all the surface of the earth were theirs, *"and the like of it with it"* — and they had as much again in addition and overflowing, *"they would offer it as ransom"* — they are ready to give it as ransom and rescue themselves from the predicament. Did you not see this in history? Did you not see the conflicts of truth and falsehood — how the leaders and chiefs of falsehood were rolled up like a scroll; and then, if they could, they were ready to give the whole world, and themselves, their lives, their position, to hold on? *"For them is the evil of the reckoning"* — for them is the worst of reckonings; *"and their refuge is Hell"* — their abode is Hell; *"and what an evil resting place!"* — and what an evil place it is. These are the verses of Sūrat al-Raʿd.

The verses of Sūrat al-Ṣaffāt too we have rendered in these writings; they too say this very same thing. *"And Our word has already gone forth to Our servants, the messengers"*[^s20-19] — already Our command for Our deputed servants, that is, the prophets, has passed and been concluded; beforehand We took the requisite and necessary decision concerning them. What is that decision? *"that they will surely be the ones helped"* — they are the ones helped; they are helped by Us alone. *"And that Our hosts will surely be the victors"* — it is Our soldiers who become victorious.

I told you its condition is that very faith and patience. In the battlefields of the Prophet, the Messenger of God called the people to patience. In the battlefields of the Commander of the Faithful, the Commander of the Faithful called the people to patience. They always used to say: be patient. What does patience on the field of battle mean? What does patience in the state of struggle mean? It means not growing weak in the struggle, it means not finishing the striving, not leaving it half-done; this is the meaning of patience. If the Muslims of the world, on the path of cultural advances and economic advances and political advances, take up these two factors today too — the factor of faith and the factor of patience — the Islamic community, culturally and politically and economically, will gain the upper hand over the unbelievers and the enemies of religion.

Let the Muslims not imagine that a stamp of nullity — of ill-repute, of backwardness, of poverty, of abasement — has been struck upon their foreheads; no. Let them not imagine that the enemies who are against religion and against Islam in the various parts of the world, who are these very hostile global powers, must always ride upon the backs of the Muslims and lord it over them and exploit them; no, never is it so. If the Muslims of the world, if the Islamic countries, if the Muslim masses and nations — in short, if the great Islamic ummah, who today number six hundred, seven hundred million in population — if they acquire faith, if they also store up patience for themselves, they will be victorious over the whole world. And this is the Qur'an's counsel to all Muslims in all times. This is the harvest of our discussion today. I wanted you to know that the outcome of the prophetic missions is good. I wanted you to know, from the Qur'an, this matter: that the prophetic missions and their uprisings will in the end be victorious, and that we today are the followers of the prophets. Why should the Islamic nations feel weak? Why? Why should you feel inferior? Why do you imagine that the enemies of religion can always be victorious over the Islamic nations and countries? Why? No; it is not so; rather it is exactly the opposite — provided that we have faith and patience. So from today's session let us at least take this benefit in practice: let us know the meaning of patience, and let us also strive to perfect our faith within ourselves.

Our discussion today has ended. I ask you to pay attention: the major and important part of our program is from now on. Let me give one reminder here. Before the recitation of the Qur'an by our dear reciters begins, I translate these verses for you, I set the ground, so that when they recite the Qur'an you may understand its meaning. Let our dear reciter, our dear Qari, Mr. Fatemi, come today, please come forward; if they have come, let them come forward.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, make us among the followers of the prophets.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, bring into being within us a strong and rich substance of faith and patience.

O Lord! Make us partake of the fruits of faith and patience.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, make the Muslim nations and the Muslims of the whole world victorious over their enemies.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, those who, in any spot of the world, in any form, carry on striving and activity to weaken the Muslims, to humiliate the Muslims, to hold the Muslims back — annihilate and destroy them.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, in any spot of the world, each of those people and human beings who pursue some striving and activity for the benefit of the Muslims and for the benefit of Islam and for the benefit of the lofty Islamic ideas — make them successful and aided.

O Lord! Cut short and sever the hand of the enemies of religion from the Islamic countries and from the Islamic nations.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, cut short and sever from the head of humanity the hand of the stubborn, obstinate enemies of religion and of the Muslims and of all humankind — namely the Jews [of the Zionist enmity].

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, make us the true followers and adherents of Muhammad and the family of Muhammad.

O Lord! Make every day of our life more aware, more learned, more earnest, more believing, and more patient than the day before.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, take under Your protection all the oppressed and the wronged of the world.

Annihilate and crush and humble all the oppressors and tyrants of the world;

Guide our hearts toward Yourself;

Make our souls flourishing and inhabited with Your remembrance and recollection;

Make our intention and purpose in this speaking and listening and sitting and rising sincere for Yourself alone;

Keep polytheism and hypocrisy and ostentation far from us.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, grant deliverance, very soon, to the innocent prisoners in every spot of the world that they may be.

O Lord! By Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, fulfill all the sound and reasonable and logical desires and wishes of the community of Muslims.

And remove all the personal and individual afflictions from among the Muslims.

*(One of the matters I wanted to mention is this very subject of the mosque, which I wanted to repeat and to say: that we have not wished to make use of the ordinary kinds of help that are sometimes used in mosques.)*

---

[^s20-1]: Sūrat al-Raʿd (the Blessed Sūra of al-Raʿd), verses 16 to 18.

[^s20-2]: Sūrat al-Ṣaffāt (the Blessed Sūra of al-Ṣaffāt), verses 171 to 180.

[^s20-3]: Literally, "never to let the eye grow warm with sleep" — that is, never to close the eyes in restful sleep.

[^s20-4]: ḥaḍīḍ (root ḥ-ḍ-ḍ): a lowering, a lowliness, the nadir.

[^s20-5]: athnāʾ (root th-n-y): in the midst, in the interval, during.

[^s20-6]: ʿAbdullah ibn al-Hasan al-Muthanna, known as ʿAbdullah al-Maḥḍ ("the pure"); so called because his father, al-Hasan al-Muthanna, was a son of Imam al-Hasan, and his mother, Fatima, was a daughter of Imam al-Husayn — for which reason he was called purely (purely) ʿAlawid. Two of his sons, named Muhammad — known as al-Nafs al-Zakiyya ("the Pure Soul") — and Ibrahim, rose in opposition against al-Mansur al-Dawaniqi and went into hiding in a secret place. To learn their whereabouts, al-Mansur treated ʿAbdullah al-Maḥḍ very harshly and imprisoned him several times in Medina and Kufa. After the uprising of his sons, al-Mansur severed both their heads from their bodies and sent them to him. ʿAbdullah was killed at the age of 75, by order of al-Mansur, in the year 145 AH.

[^s20-7]: naṣṣ (root n-ṣ-ṣ): text.

[^s20-8]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (the Blessed Sūra of Āl ʿImrān), verse 139.

[^s20-9]: This term is applied to those verses whose words and expressions bear multiple meanings, such that the intended sense is not arrived at from their translation and outward form. These verses are interpreted by referring to the "firm" (muḥkam) verses — those that have a clear and plain meaning.

[^s20-10]: awrang: a royal throne.

[^s20-11]: Sūrat al-Baqarah (the Blessed Sūra of al-Baqarah), verse 153.

[^s20-12]: Sūrat al-Ḥajj (the Blessed Sūra of al-Ḥajj), verse 11.

[^s20-13]: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (the Blessed Sūra of al-Aʿrāf), verse 127.

[^s20-14]: Sūrat al-Aʿrāf (the Blessed Sūra of al-Aʿrāf), verse 128.

[^s20-15]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah (the Blessed Sūra of al-Māʾidah), verse 21.

[^s20-16]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (the Blessed Sūra of Āl ʿImrān), verse 125: "Yes, if you are patient and God-fearing, and they come upon you in a rush, your Lord will reinforce you with five thousand marked angels."

[^s20-17]: Sūrat al-Raʿd (the Blessed Sūra of al-Raʿd), verses 16 to 18.

[^s20-18]: ḥilya (plural of ḥilyy): an ornament made of mineral stones. pīrāya: that which goldsmiths make for the adornment of women.

[^s20-19]: Sūrat al-Ṣaffāt (the Blessed Sūra of al-Ṣaffāt), verses 171 to 173.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 21: The Outcome of Prophethood (2)

*فرجام نبوت (۲) — delivered Wednesday, 17 Mehr 1353 / 22 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾmin / Ghāfir (40:51–54):** "Indeed, We will surely help Our messengers and those who believe, in the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses arise — the Day when their excuses will be of no benefit to the wrongdoers; and theirs is the curse, and theirs is the evil abode. And We had certainly given Moses guidance, and We bequeathed to the Children of Israel the Book — a guidance and a remembrance for those endowed with understanding."[^s21-1]

Yesterday we said that God's promise to the prophets and messengers — His promise concerning their mission among the people — is a promise full of hope and glad tidings. That is to say, God the Exalted has promised to grant His prophets, and those who bear the trust of the prophetic mission, and likewise everyone who calls toward religion, truth, and reality — victory over their enemies both in this world, and in the next world a recompense, a reward, and a return. And the whole of what can be said about the victory of the prophets, peace be upon them, I summarized into two points.

The first point is this: when we examine the chain of the prophethoods of history from the first to the last, we see that, taken as a whole, these teachers of mankind succeeded and triumphed. It is true that some of them, in the course of their call — or even up to the very end of their call — met with certain failures and ingratitude on the part of the people. But when we reckon the matter as a whole, we see that the dear prophets, from the first of them to the last, accomplished the very work they had set out to accomplish.

I compared the prophets to teachers who wish to educate a student from beginning to end, one after another. Six teachers, ten teachers, fifteen teachers — from the lower grades up to the higher grades — they want to keep passing this child along, hand to hand, so that he may rise from the depths of ignorance to the summit of knowledge. Even though the first-grade teacher places before the feet of this child only a small portion of this road, of this path, and helps him in traversing it; even though the first-grade teacher does not live to see this student reach the peak of his perfection, and passes away before that — what is our judgment regarding the success or failure of that first-grade teacher? Because he was not able to bring this child all the way to the final stage, or because he was not able to see this student reach the final rank, do we say he failed? No. He did his own work; he handed the trust to the next person only after he had fulfilled his own share of the responsibility.

Another example we have always given in this regard is this: We say that a very heavy, exhausting load is to be carried from here to the far end of the mosque. This load is such that a single person cannot lift it onto his shoulders and carry it by himself, without anyone else's help, from here to the far end of the mosque. And it is also not possible for all of the people together to help and lift this load all at once and carry it to the end. One way remains, and it is this: that one of these several strong men who are gathered here, ready to lift this load, picks it up from here and carries it forward one meter; then a second person comes, takes the load from the one-meter mark and carries it to the two-meter mark; the third person does the same; the fourth the same; the twenty-seventh, the twenty-eighth, the thirtieth, the thirty-somethingth — and this load will reach the far end of this hall. For instance, from where I am now standing to over there is a distance of about thirty-three or thirty-four meters, say. This much distance, this road, was traversed; this load was carried. But each of these men who lifted this load was able to advance it only one meter — and with that single meter of advance, it may even be that he gave up his very life. So heavy is this load that if a person wishes to push it forward even a single meter, he is compelled to sacrifice his own life on this path — and indeed he did sacrifice it. Yet the sum total that we have here is this: one precious life given as a ransom for one stage of this road being traversed, and for one stage of the load being brought nearer to its destination.

The prophets are those very robust, powerful men, each of whom carried this load forward by one meter. Noah, peace be upon him, came and pushed this load — the load of guiding mankind and bringing the human race to the summit of culture and virtue — forward by one meter. Even though in that single meter he endured torments, even though he preached for nine hundred and fifty years and had only just advanced a little, even though in the end he gave his life over this work — did he, in the end, fulfill his duty or not? Did he bring this load one meter nearer to its destination, or not? You see that he did. After Noah, the prophet who comes after him takes this load from him and likewise advances it one meter. A third comes, and he too advances it one meter. The Seal of the Prophets, may God's blessings be upon him and his household, graces us with his presence and is commissioned to his mission; he takes this load — which had reached a certain appointed limit — and sets it upon a road where it rolls along on its rails and will move forward: humanity has reached maturity. Therefore the prophets, from the first to the last, succeeded.

And then, too, at the conclusion of the work — the last divine emissary, of whom we also wrote in these papers[^s21-2] — the matter of the appearance of the Awaited One (the Wali of the Age), may God's blessings be upon him, is itself a glad tiding to all the godly and to all the "national-minded" of the world in this regard. The last appointee of God, whom we consider to be our own Imam of the Age, and whom all the "national-minded"[^s21-3] of the world await — he will come and bring this load all the way to its final point. Whose work is the Imam of the Age continuing? He is continuing the work of Noah, the work of Abraham, the work of Moses, the work of Jesus, the work of our own Prophet, and the work of the pure Imams from the household of the Prophet. Had this load not reached its end, Noah would have failed. But because this load will reach its end — because he too had a share in bringing this load to its end — Noah is not a failure; Abraham is not a failure; Zachariah, whose body was sawn in two, is not a failure; that prophet whom they threw into the well[^s21-4] is not a failure. It is true that he fell into the well; it is true that in this world he did not see what he longed to see; it is true that Yaḥyā (John), peace be upon him, had his head severed for the tyrant of his age and was sent as a gift. All of this is true — but none of these men were defeated. Why? Because they bore these calamities and afflictions at the price of pushing forward the load of the trust and bringing that load nearer to its destination. This was one point that I made yesterday.

The other point I also touched upon yesterday is this: that in the final outcome, in the overall end of the work, we saw that defeat never awaits the prophets; in the end, all of them triumphed and succeeded, in the sense I have explained. But some of the prophets, besides that final triumph — bringing the load one meter nearer to its destination — beyond this also attained certain successes in this world. Those successes consisted in their being able to bring into existence a society based upon monotheistic thought and upon the school they proposed. The clearest instance is our own Prophet, who — upon the foundation of Islamic thought, the Qur'anic mode of thought, and the inspiration of God — brought into being a society, a polity (madīnah), a community, and an order. And of the same type as the Prophet were many of the past prophets, as I also mentioned yesterday. The conversion of the Roman Empire came after the departure of Jesus; the formation of the virtuous society of the Children of Israel came after the passing of Moses. Abraham, during his own lifetime, established the divine society — of this the Qur'an speaks. Solomon spread divine rule throughout the entire world. The Israelite prophet named Solomon, son of David, gathered the whole world into the form of a single monotheistic, divine society, around one another and around a single axis. So some of the prophets did attain certain successes and triumphs even during their own lifetimes, and these triumphs were summed up in what? In the establishment of a divine, monotheistic order and society.

Some of the prophets did not have this opportunity. Like whom? Like Zachariah, whom I gave as an example; like Yaḥyā, whom I gave as an example — they failed in their lifetimes; in the end they were killed; and like certain others among the prophets before Zachariah and before Yaḥyā. What is this? How is this matter to be analyzed and explained? Why do some of the prophets succeed and others fail? Why can they not all establish the divine and monotheistic society? And why do only some attain this possibility and this success? The answer lies in a single short sentence: everywhere that the divine men, leaders, and great ones won their successes, those successes were contingent upon faith and patience; and everywhere that the great divine leaders and the callers to truth and reality were defeated, it was the result of not having a sufficient measure of faith or of patience — without exception. Everywhere it has been just so. Wherever the companions of the prophet and the believers in him exercised patience in the struggle against the enemies, the opponents, and the adversaries of the prophet's call, they pushed forward.

The requisite for victory and progress is present in the call of the prophets — because the prophets speak in accordance with truth, they act in accordance with truth, and truth is victorious. Truth is in accordance with the nature and disposition of the world, and therefore it is successful and victorious. The prophets too are speaking in accordance with the disposition of the world; therefore the substance and the requisite of success in the movement of the prophets, in the revolution of the prophets, is completely present. If you see that some prophet in history was defeated, do not take this as proof that the word of truth must be defeated — no, the word of truth must be victorious, the order of truth must prevail, the hand of truth must strike falsehood upon the head and shatter it to pieces. So why, then, does a prophet sometimes meet defeat? Because the followers of the prophet either do not have sufficient faith or sufficient patience; they do not exert patience. Otherwise, the verse of the Qur'an — that very verse we recited yesterday — says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ṣāffāt (37:171–173):** "And Our word has already gone forth to Our servants, the messengers: that it is they who will be helped, and that it is Our host that will surely be the victors."[^s21-5]

Our host, Our army, Our people are the ones who prevail — this word of God is not subject to default. If somewhere you saw that those who utter the word of truth were defeated, know that they were not the host of God; the conditions for being the host of God were not present in them. Those others who arranged for that victory, who arranged for that defeat and prepared its ground — they were not the host of God. The Islamic society: on the day that it is the host of God, it pushes forward; on the day that it falls behind, it is not the host of God, it is not the army of God, it is not the troops of God.

The upshot is summed up in two words: the cause of defeats on the one hand, and of victories and triumphs on the other. Wherever the followers of the prophet brought about within themselves complete faith and complete certainty, and stood firm upon the word of truth, they assuredly succeeded and pushed forward; defeat had no place in their affair. Yesterday I related something, on the authority of one of the great ones of our own near-contemporary history,[^s21-6] who used to say to his friends: "When you are able, struggle. When you see that you are being defeated, struggle still — until you reach that moment when you become certain that, yes, you will surely be defeated — even then, struggle on. When, at the very moment that you are certain you will be defeated, you still continue your effort, your striving, and your struggle, then victory and triumph will be granted to you." A verse of the Qur'an says almost this same thing — or, indeed, says it definitively. It says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Yūsuf (12:110):** "…until, when the messengers despaired and thought that they had been denied, Our help came to them…"[^s21-7]

It reached a point where the pressures, the blows, and the strikes of the opposing front against the prophets were so severe that the prophets themselves grew hopeless. It reached a point where the prophets and the people who were with the prophets had their hearts shaken — not as regards faith; their faith did not vanish, they did not lose their belief in God — but because they had believed that they would be victorious, their faith and certainty in victory fell into decline, and they imagined that they had misunderstood. They had been certain that God had told them, "You will surely be victorious." The matter would reach a point where they began to think that perhaps they had misunderstood, that God had not in fact made them such a promise. *Wa ẓannū annahum qad kudhibū* — "and they thought that they had been denied" — *jāʾahum naṣrunā* — "Our help came to them": in that very moment when, from the intensity of the pressure and the severity of the enemy's blows and strikes, the front of truth and the front of religion felt that, yes, it was now surely being destroyed, it was perishing, the enemy was gaining the upper hand, the roads were closed upon it — in that very moment, because it still persevered, because it still strove, the help and succor of God arrived. This is the verse of the Qur'an.

We imagine that if, throughout history, some of the movements calling toward truth were defeated — if Zayd ibn ʿAlī[^s21-8] was struck in the forehead by an arrow in the mosque of Kufa and fell; if Muḥammad ibn ʿAbdullāh, the one called "the Pure Soul" (al-Nafs al-Zakiyyah),[^s21-9] was defeated in his struggle against Manṣūr; if Ḥusayn ibn ʿAlī al-Ḥasanī, the martyr of Fakhkh,[^s21-10] was killed near Medina together with all his companions; if Ibrāhīm ibn ʿAbdullāh[^s21-11] was slain in Kufa or Basra — then this ought to produce in us a general lesson, that we say: wherever a movement of truth rose up against falsehood, it is condemned to defeat. A group of ignorant people, unaware of the logic of the Qur'an, hold this very notion. I said that this notion settles, like sweet water, into the hearts of the powerful and the tyrants of history. They ask of the God of both worlds that the people should assume and imagine things in this way. And of course it is evident that they themselves also promote this mode of thought. But this mode of thought is contrary to reality.

If Zayd ibn ʿAlī is martyred there, in that defeated state, this is no proof that truth is condemned to extinction and defeat; rather, it is proof that truth, even though it is truth, has need of work, effort, and striving. Let us not suppose that, because our word is truth, it is therefore unnecessary to strive on its behalf. Let us not think that, because our cause is the Qur'an, God Himself will therefore push the Qur'an forward — no! The word of truth, true, is truth; true, the world is destined and certain[^s21-12] to accept it in the future — but it requires effort, it requires activity, a group must exercise patience for its sake, a group must eat the blood of their own hearts to enthrone it. The episode of Zayd ibn ʿAlī gives us this lesson; it does not give us the lesson that truth is condemned to extinction. Why do they err? Zayd ibn ʿAlī's word was true; Imam al-Ṣādiq, peace be upon him, had also endorsed Zayd ibn ʿAlī's struggle against the apparatus of Hishām ibn ʿAbd al-Malik — they had permitted him that wondrous struggle, and history speaks to this meaning. This noble man set out; he arranged matters well, too — only, a group of people: ignorant and unaware, or fault-seeking, or under the influence of the evil propaganda of a vigilant enemy — at the necessary moment forsook[^s21-13] him and left him alone. This is the lesson we learn from that episode: that even if a word is true, even with the rightfulness of Zayd ibn ʿAlī's word, still, if his followers and supporters left him alone, if they did not exert effort and struggle on his path and at his side, they are defeated; and if they did struggle, they push forward. All the affairs of the world are just so.

How many people in the world do you know of — how many ideas, how many schools of thought — whose followers and friends, through their effort and struggle, enthroned that school of thought? How is it that false words, untrue words, words contrary to the way of the world and the nature of man, are enthroned through effort and struggle, yet the word of truth is not enthroned through struggle? What kind of talk is this? Yet some people do indeed say this very absurd, false thing.

The Commander of the Faithful (Amīr al-Muʾminīn), may God's blessings be upon him, makes this matter entirely clear in one of the sermons of the *Nahj al-Balāghah* — in a short sermon. I did not, of course, intend today to go into great detail, and I will not go into great detail; I would rather explain these verses. But for now I will recount this sermon for you as well. It is a sermon I have read a number of times until now, in various gatherings, for the brothers who were present. The Commander of the Faithful describes the advances of the soldiers of Islam in the time of the Prophet. He says:

> *Wa laqad kunnā maʿa rasūli'llāhi naqtulu ābāʾanā wa abnāʾanā wa ikhwānanā wa aʿmāmanā* — "We were with the Messenger of God, slaying our fathers, our sons, our brothers, and our uncles…"[^s21-14]

He says: when we were beside the Prophet and in the Prophet's ranks, this is how we would act. When we went onto the battlefield, we would slay our own fathers, our own sons, our own brothers, our own uncles who were in the front of unbelief and polytheism. If our uncle, our father, our son, our brother came under the banner of unbelief to war against the Prophet, we — who had gone forth with the Prophet — would not say, "This is my brother, let me not kill him; this is my son, let me not kill him." Without hesitation we mowed them all down in the way of God. And afterward, when we had killed them and were returning, our hearts were not shaken within us so as to cry out, "Alas, woe! Look what we have done! For the sake of this new religion, this new thought, we have killed our own beloved child!" — no. Our faith, as a result of this sharp and decisive deed, did not lessen; it increased. *Mā yazīdunā dhālika illā īmānan wa taslīman* — "that only increased us in faith and submission" — this struggle of ours only added to our faith and our submission. *Wa muḍiyyan ʿalā al-ḥaqqi wa taslīman li-l-amri* — this struggle of ours caused us to act more decisively in difficult matters, and to exercise more patience over the pains and torments of the struggle.

Well, then the Commander of the Faithful proceeds to describe the battlefields there in brief. He said: *Wa jiddan fī jihādi al-ʿaduwwi. Wa laqad kāna al-rajulu minnā wa al-ākharu min ʿaduwwinā yataṣāwalāni taṣāwula al-faḥlayni yatakhālasāni anfusahumā: ayyuhumā yasqī ṣāḥibahu kaʾsa al-manūn* — which I do not wish to expound — namely, how our soldier in battle would leap upon the enemy, how he would grapple and lock with him, how our soldiers and the brave warriors and champions of Islam would race one another toward death — these I will leave aside. Then, at the very end of this same sermon — which is also short — he says:

> *Fa-lammā raʾā'llāhu ṣidqanā anzala bi-ʿaduwwinā al-kabta wa anzala ʿalaynā al-naṣr* — "So when God saw our truthfulness, He sent down humiliation upon our enemy and sent down victory upon us."

We struggled to such an extent that God saw that we were speaking truthfully, saw that we were truly Muslims, saw that we were truly believers — that we demonstrated our deep faith by our deeds; and when we acted in this way, then the crushing of our enemies, and victory and triumph for us, descended upon us from God. He utters one, two, three further sentences, and then he says:

> *Wa la-ʿamrī law kunnā naʾtī mā ataytum mā qāma li-l-dīni ʿamūdun wa lā-khḍarra li-l-īmāni ʿūdun* — "And by my life, had we done what you are doing, not a single pillar of the religion would have stood, and not a single branch of faith would have grown green."

The Commander of the Faithful says this during the time of his caliphate, when he was confronting a handful of lazy, comfort-seeking men who, when he said, "Come, let us go to the war against Muʿāwiyah," would make excuses; who, when it was time to go to the war against Ṭalḥah and Zubayr, would fabricate a thousand kinds of religious justification for their own staying behind. In short, comfort-seeking, life-seeking, ease-seeking people, far from divine knowledge — it was with such men that he was dealing. Weak, base, slack, abject, surrendered to humiliation, accustomed to comfort — with such men the Commander of the Faithful was contending. He says to them, *Wa la-ʿamrī* — "by my own life I swear" — *law kunnā naʾtī mā ataytum* — "had we, in that day with the Prophet, done the very thing you are doing today," O Muslims, *mā qāma li-l-dīni ʿamūdun wa lā-khḍarra li-l-īmāni ʿūdun* — not one pillar among the pillars of the religion would have stood erect, not one branch among the branches of faith would have grown green.

What do you understand from this sermon? The word of the Commander of the Faithful — which is nothing other than the word of the Prophet, for the path that ʿAlī trod is the very path that his brother the Prophet trod — why, in that day, did the Prophet of God advance so, and why, in the time of the Commander of the Faithful, did affairs come to such a standstill? Why? The Commander of the Faithful is stating its secret. He says: because in that day we exercised patience on the battlefields; in that day we exercised patience over the burning of pain; in that day we were ready to come out of our beds onto the battlefield; in that day we were ready to forgo our material interests and our livelihoods for the sake of God — but today you are not ready. In that day we advanced; today we fall behind. A very simple, clear matter; two times two is four. This is the social analysis of the Commander of the Faithful.

At any rate, the summary of the matter comes to this: that the divine prophets, besides the fact that in the chain of prophethoods their work is accompanied by victory, and that at the conclusion of the work the eternal and final triumph and outcome belongs to them — even in this very world the prophets are certain to attain victory and triumph and to achieve their own aims and the aims of their school — but on condition that their followers, their companions, bring faith, exert real faith, render aid, and exercise patience on the fields of struggle and jihad. On condition of faith and patience — this is the upshot of the discourse.

Now let us return to the verses of the Qur'an. Today I have chosen verses from several places in the Noble Qur'an — the written text of which will, of course, reach your hands tomorrow — and I saw that there are even more verses of glad tiding in the Qur'an than these. One is in Sūrat al-Muʾmin (Ghāfir), where He says — and there is no need for me to read those verses here now: 

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾmin / Ghāfir (40:51):** "Indeed, We will surely help Our messengers and those who believe, in the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses arise."

Allow me to read them, there is no harm in it; I would regret not explaining these verses today, since tomorrow too I will not have time to explain them. Sūrat al-Muʾmin, verse 51, according to this Qur'an. *Innā la-nanṣuru rusulanā wa alladhīna āmanū* — "Indeed, We will surely help Our messengers and those who believe."[^s21-15] The *lām* in *la-nanṣuru* is for certainty, verification, and emphasis; and *innā* too is in this same sense of verification and emphasis. That is to say: We, definitively and without any doubt, help — *rusulanā*, "Our messengers." Only the messengers? No — *wa alladhīna āmanū*, "and those who believe," the believers as well. The believer who moves along the path of the prophet — this promise is for him too. The pure-natured human beings who set out following the call of the prophets — this promise is for them too: *wa alladhīna āmanū*. Where do We help them? *Fī al-ḥayāti al-dunyā* — "in the life of this world": right here, in this very worldly life, We help them; We do not leave it for later. *Wa yawma yaqūmu al-ashhād* — "and on the Day when the witnesses arise." This — that the witnesses arise — is in some commentaries interpreted as the Day of Resurrection.

There is a tradition concerning this noble verse in which the Imam, peace be upon him, said to Jamīl ibn Darrāj — according to that tradition — that what is meant by God's help to the prophets is their being helped in the world of the Return (rajʿah).[^s21-16] That is to say, after the Wali of the Age, may God's blessings be upon him, comes, and that all-encompassing divine government comes into being in this world, and the banner of the Qur'an and Islam is planted across the entire world, and all the people move toward religion, God, and monotheism, and the single divine government comes into being — after that, God the Exalted brings back to life the prophets, the messengers, the executors of the prophets (awṣiyāʾ), the martyrs, and the righteous among the believers — in accordance with verses of the Qur'an interpreted to this meaning, and traditions that are explicit on this meaning. I will not now go into the discussion of the Return. In this narration the Imam says that this is for the Return — this verse is connected to the Return, and the help is for the Return. I myself imagine that the Imam, peace be upon him, does not wish to interpret this phrase — *fī al-ḥayāti al-dunyā* — as referring to the Return. The Imam does not wish to say that what God has stated — that We help them in the life of this world — means "in the Return within the life of this world." I think that *yawma yaqūmu al-ashhād* is being interpreted as the Return, and not as the Resurrection — for what helping is there in the Resurrection? In the era of the Return, God the Exalted helps the prophets. This I offer as a probability, and I draw this inference from this narration only as a probability. At any rate,[^s21-17] even if we set aside this benefit altogether, the phrase *fī al-ḥayāti al-dunyā* itself, on its apparent meaning, and by the indication of the verses that come afterward as well, shows that God the Exalted gives an explicit promise to the prophets and to the believers that, in this very worldly life, We will help you.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾmin / Ghāfir (40:52):** "The Day when their excuse will not benefit the wrongdoers; and theirs is the curse, and theirs is the evil abode."

*Yawma yaqūmu al-ashhād* — who is it that arises? When do the witnesses arise? At that time when the apology of the wrongdoers will be of no benefit to them. There is another verse in the Qur'an: 

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām (6:158):** "…the Day when a sign of your Lord comes, no soul will benefit from its faith if it did not believe before…"[^s21-18]

This too is interpreted as the era of the coming of the Wali of the Age — it is possible this is the same. *Wa lahumu al-laʿnah* — the curse is upon the wrongdoers. *Wa lahum sūʾu al-dār* — theirs is a most ill-omened, evil abode.

Then, to bring a witness to the fact that We help the prophets, He cites the episode of Moses; for since, before this verse, from the early part of Sūrat al-Muʾmin, the discourse is mostly about Moses and his struggles against Pharaoh, here too — where He gives the promise of help to all the prophets — He says, as one type, one instance, one example:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾmin / Ghāfir (40:53–54):** "And We had certainly given Moses guidance, and We bequeathed to the Children of Israel the Book — a guidance and a remembrance for those endowed with understanding."

*Wa laqad ātaynā Mūsā al-hudā* — "We gave Moses guidance and right-guidedness." *Wa awrathnā Banī Isrāʾīla al-kitāb* — "and We bequeathed the Book to the Children of Israel as an inheritance." The Children of Israel remained possessors of the Book, possessors of the collection of divine knowledge and ordinances — and this shows that they succeeded; otherwise, had the unbelievers and tyrants of the age prevailed over the Children of Israel, they would not have allowed them to act according to their own heavenly Book; they would have destroyed the Book from among them. *Hudan wa dhikrā li-ulī al-albāb* — that this Book is guidance, is right-guidance, and is awareness for those endowed with intellect and reason.

*Fa-ṣbir* — then, when this matter is concluded, He addresses the Seal of the Prophets, saying: *Fa-ṣbir* — "so be patient, persevere, resist the impulses that bring about decline." *Inna waʿda'llāhi ḥaqq* — without doubt, God's promise is true. This promise that God has given — that He has said you will be victorious; that promise which was in Sūrat al-Ṣāffāt: *Wa laqad sabaqat kalimatunā li-ʿibādinā al-mursalīn, innahum lahumu al-manṣūrūn* — and likewise the other promises that are in the Qur'an — these are true, these are correct. You will surely be victorious, O Prophet, in accordance with the divine promise; but the condition for it is patience. *Fa-ṣbir* — you must be patient, you must resist and persevere, you must not turn back from the sacred contest you have undertaken.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾmin / Ghāfir (40:55):** "So be patient — indeed, the promise of God is true — and seek forgiveness for your sin, and glorify your Lord with praise in the evening and the morning."

*Fa-ṣbir inna waʿda'llāhi ḥaqq, wa-staghfir li-dhanbik* — "and seek forgiveness for your sin." Of course, the Prophet's sin, the sin in the Prophet's category, is not of the type of our sins. The Prophet is infallible — this is certain. By the explicit text of the Qur'anic verses and by the judgment of reason, the Prophet does not sin. This "sin" is of the kind of sins and lapses that, for a human being of that station, count as a lapse — even though, if you and I were to do the very same thing, it would be no lapse at all.
