It is those sins He speaks of, those lapses He speaks of. *Wa-staghfir li-dhanbik* — ask pardon, seek forgiveness, and seek to make amends for your small lapses of the past. *Wa sabbiḥ bi-ḥamdi rabbika bi-l-ʿashiyyi wa-l-ibkār* — "and glorify, and praise your Lord, in every evening and every morning." This verse teaches us very clearly that the outcome of the prophets' work is the help of God.

And now as for the verses of Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ — that too, again, in the same way, I will read briefly. Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ is in the seventeenth juzʾ, after Sūrat Ṭā Hā. The verses of Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ are excellent. I recommend to those friends who have intimacy with the Qur'an to read Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ carefully. From the beginning of the sura, God the Exalted is constantly saying that the prophets will be victorious, the enemies of the prophets will be crushed, and in this world too — before the next world — they will be punished; this He keeps saying, constantly, from the start of the sura. Then, after repeating these matters somewhat, He turns to history. He recounts the story of Moses — the victory of Moses and the defeat of the power opposing Moses; He recounts the story of Abraham — the victory and success of Abraham and the defeat of the powers opposing Abraham; the story of Noah, the story of Solomon, and other stories. All the episodes recounted in this sura are in this form: the prophet advances, succeeds, is victorious; and the anti-prophet, the counter-revolution against the prophet, the opposition to the prophet's new word — that is, the reaction that opposes prophethood — is overcome, is subdued, is defeated. And this is the way (sunnah) of history.

He says: 

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ (21:7):** "And We did not send before you any but men to whom We revealed…"[^s21-19]

— these are the verses at the beginning of the sura — He says: before you, We sent prophets and gave them revelation. *Wa mā jaʿalnāhum jasadan lā yaʾkulūna al-ṭaʿām* (21:8) — "We did not make them bodies that did not eat food": We did not make them into angels having no body, who require no food. *Wa mā kānū khālidīn* — *khālidīn*: nor were they immortal, eternal, everlasting. They too died at some point. But — *thumma ṣadaqnāhumu al-waʿd* (21:9) — "then We fulfilled for them the promise": We made Our promise to them come true, the promise of victory. *Fa-anjaynāhum wa man nashāʾ* — "We saved them and whomever We willed": We saved them, and whomever We willed We saved along with them. *Wa ahlaknā al-musrifīn* — "and We destroyed the transgressors": We destroyed the transgressors. *Laqad anzalnā ilaykum kitāban fīhi dhikrukum, a-fa-lā taʿqilūn* (21:10) — "We have sent down to you a Book in which is your remembrance": now to you too, O believers, We have sent down a Book, a Qur'an, in which is the remembrance, the awareness, and the reminder of you. *A-fa-lā taʿqilūn* — "do you not, then, use reason? Do you not reflect?"

Then He arrives at verse eleven, and He says: 

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ (21:11):** "And how many a town that was wrongdoing have We shattered…"

*Wa kam qaṣamnā min qaryatin* — this is truly a hymn of the victory of the prophets, a stirring anthem that shows how the prophets, with the divine, unseen aids that lie within the womb of this world — and not unseen aids of the sort that the common people wish for and love, that a hand should come from the unseen and strike the chest of the enemy; no — unseen aids that are concealed within the very womb of this world, in keeping with the disposition of the world and the nature of man and the cosmos: with these unseen aids, how greatly did the Lord of the world grant the prophets success. *Wa kam qaṣamnā min qaryatin* — how many, and how often, did We shatter — did We shatter the settlement and society that *kānat ẓālimah*, that was wrongdoing. The wrongdoing society, the wrongdoing order, the wrongdoing civilization — that society in whose very construction injustice has been employed, in which class divisions have arisen, in which exploitation has been established, in which the exploitation of man by man has been set up; this is "the wrongdoing town," this is "the wrongdoing society," in whose foundation tyranny has been employed — how many of these did We shatter! *Wa kam qaṣamnā* — that is, "how many did We shatter, We crushed." *Wa qaṣamnā* means "We shattered, We pulverized." *Min qaryatin kānat ẓālimatan wa ansh'anā baʿdahā qawman ākharīn* — "and after them We raised up another people": and after them We brought into being and made manifest another people, another group, another class, and set them to the work.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ (21:12–15):** "Then, when they perceived Our might, at once they fled from it. [It was said:] Do not flee, but return to the luxury you were given and to your dwellings, that you might be questioned. They said: Woe to us, indeed we were wrongdoers! And that did not cease to be their cry until We made them stubble, extinguished."

*Fa-lammā aḥassū baʾsanā* — a description of the misery of the wrongdoers at the moment they behold the punishment of God. *Fa-lammā aḥassū baʾsanā* — the very moment those wrongdoers sensed Our wrath and anger — whether, for instance, suppose a heavenly punishment descended, or whether they saw that the believers, alongside their prophet, had charged upon them and were about to bring down the sword of God's wrath upon their heads — the moment they sensed Our wrath, *idhā hum minhā yarkuḍūn* — suddenly, from those settlements, from that civilization, from that society, they took to flight, they set to fleeing and running away. *Lā tarkuḍū* — "do not flee"; *wa-rjiʿū ilā mā utriftum fīhi* — "and return to the luxury you were given": return to your places of luxurious ease, return to those palaces in which you lived in luxury, return into the midst of the society and city in which you lorded over others and boasted — where are you fleeing to? *Wa-rjiʿū ilā mā utriftum fīhi wa masākinikum* — "and to your dwellings." Return to your dwellings; *laʿallakum tusʾalūn* — "that you might be questioned": perhaps they wish to interrogate you, perhaps they wish to question you — where are you going? *Qālū yā waylanā innā kunnā ẓālimīn* — they said, "Woe to us! Woe to us, for we were oppressors and wrongdoers." *Fa-mā zālat tilka daʿwāhum* — this remained their cry, their speech; *ḥattā jaʿalnāhum ḥaṣīdan khāmidīn* — "and they kept on speaking in this way until We made them all prey to death and annihilation."

Pay attention. There are two or three more verses; let me read these. These following verses, in truth, state the intellectual foundation of this historical reality. Why did things become this way? Why must the wrongdoers be destroyed and the oppressed take their place? Why must the call of the prophet surely advance and his opponents be crushed — why? The cause is this:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ (21:16–17):** "And We did not create the heaven and the earth and what is between them in play. Had We wished to take a diversion, We would have taken it from within Ourselves — if We were to do so."

*Wa mā khalaqnā al-samāʾa wa-l-arḍa wa mā baynahumā lāʿibīn* — We did not create the heaven and the earth and what is between them in vain. *Law aradnā an nattakhidha lahwan la-attakhadhnāhu min ladunnā in kunnā fāʿilīn* — had We wished to do a frivolous thing, We would certainly have done it; We would have done it of Our own self — if We were the doers of such a thing — but We never do. The Lord of the world does not do a vain thing, does not act in accordance with falsehood, does not act in futility. What does this mean? It means that this heaven and earth, and what is between them — if We created them, We created them according to a purpose; We created them for the attainment of an aim and a goal. Truth (ḥaqq) means the line of progression of this heaven and earth, and of whatever is in this heaven and earth, toward that goal — that is, truth. That path which brings the heaven and the earth and their creatures to that destination, that aim and goal for which they were created — that line of progression is truth. Any means that brings human beings to that destination — that means is truth. These things are not made explicit in the Qur'anic verse; this is the import of the verse. That is, by reflection upon the verse, it is quite clear; and the foundation of the Qur'an is this: that mostly, many of the things which are understood and clear and which the human intellect arrives at, it does not bother to make explicit.

Then He continues, saying: 

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ (21:18):** "Rather, We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes it, and at once it vanishes. And woe to you for what you describe."

*Bal naqdhifu bi-l-ḥaqqi ʿalā al-bāṭili* — rather, We strike falsehood with the means of truth. This path of truth, this correct way, this innate and natural way of man and the world, will in the end be victorious over falsehood; rather, We make the truth prevail over falsehood. *Fa-yadmaghuhu* — so this truth utterly effaces and annihilates falsehood. *Fa-idhā huwa zāhiq* — then you see falsehood as it perishes, heading toward annihilation. *Wa lakumu al-waylu mimmā taṣifūn* — then for you, O wrongdoers, O oppressors, O followers of falsehood and deniers of truth, is woe; for you is wretchedness and ruin. *Wa lakumu al-waylu mimmā taṣifūn* — woe to you for what you have described and asserted.

Then, of course, He continues with a most interesting and beautiful Qur'anic style — which requires that a person become intimate with the Qur'an in order to grasp these interesting and beautiful subtleties of the Qur'an correctly. Mostly, those who do not perceive these delicacies, these fine points and refinements of the Qur'an, have no intimacy with the Qur'an. If a person becomes intimate with the Qur'an, the tone of the Qur'an's speech becomes familiar to his ear, and he understands how the Qur'an is speaking — He discusses and speaks about this matter: the coming of truth, the going of falsehood, the victory of truth, the defeat of falsehood. Then, regarding the creation of the heaven and the earth, He once again takes hold of the point that the Lord of the world is the owner of the heaven, the owner of the earth, the ruler over all the regions of the world of existence. Therefore, in the life of human beings too, He must hold sway; in the system of human life too, He must give the law, He must administer, He must govern. Therefore, those who, in opposition to the prophets, laid claim to government, power, and dominion, were upon falsehood and are condemned to annihilation. I ask you, gentlemen, please remain seated.

O Lord! By Muḥammad and the household of Muḥammad, make us true followers of Muḥammad and the household of Muḥammad.

O Lord! By Muḥammad and the household of Muḥammad, do not separate us from them in this world, in life, and in the Hereafter.

By Muḥammad and the household of Muḥammad, draw their kindness and their love toward us.

O Lord! Make us followers and adherents of the Qur'an, and those who act upon the Qur'an.

---

[^s21-1]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾmin (Ghāfir), verses 51–54.

[^s21-2]: Refer to the mimeographed handout at the end of the session.

[^s21-3]: *Milliyyūn* — "the national-minded," partisans of the nation (literally, supporters of the *millah*/community).

[^s21-4]: A reference to the prophet Joseph (Yūsuf), who was cast into the well by his brothers.

[^s21-5]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ṣāffāt, verses 171–173.

[^s21-6]: A reference to one of the great figures of our own near-contemporary history, who counseled continued struggle even in the face of certain defeat.

[^s21-7]: Qur'an, Sūrat Yūsuf, verse 110.

[^s21-8]: Zayd ibn ʿAlī rose up during the imamate of Imam al-Ṣādiq and the caliphate of Hishām ibn ʿAbd al-Malik, as the first ʿAlid to take up armed struggle. His aim was to restore the lost rights of the House of Muḥammad to the chosen Imam of the House of Muḥammad. Zayd gathered companions for himself in Kufa and then raised the banner of revolt. Yūsuf ibn ʿUmar al-Thaqafī was commissioned to battle him. Before this confrontation, many of Zayd's companions abandoned the field, and in the end Zayd went into the arena of struggle with only a few companions and was martyred. Imam al-Riḍā said concerning Zayd: "He rose for the truth and was killed in the path of truth, and had he been victorious he would have restored the caliphate to its rightful holders."

[^s21-9]: Muḥammad ibn ʿAbdullāh al-Ḥasan, known as "the Pure Soul" (al-Nafs al-Zakiyyah), was among the most excellent of the Banū Hāshim. In the closing days of the Umayyad state, the Banū Hāshim agreed among themselves to give allegiance to "the Pure Soul" as the Mahdi of this Ummah, but Imam al-Ṣādiq did not consent. After Manṣūr came to the caliphate, all the descendants of Imam al-Ḥasan and Imam al-Ḥusayn were taken captive and sent to Iraq and imprisoned in Kufa, where they died one by one in prison. On hearing this news, he rose up in Medina and expelled the governor of Medina from it. Near Medina he fought against an army sent by the caliph and was killed.

[^s21-10]: Ḥusayn ibn ʿAlī ibn Ḥasan, one of the descendants of Imam al-Ḥasan, lived in Medina during the imamate of Imam al-Kāẓim. With the harshness of the Abbasid caliph and the governor of Medina, a group proposed that he rise up and pledged their support. He first rose up in Medina, and after taking the city set out for Mecca with three hundred men. Near Mecca, at a place called Fakhkh, he met the army of the Abbasid caliph and, after great effort, was martyred. His head and those of a hundred others were severed from their bodies and placed before the pilgrims in Mecca. Their headless bodies lay upon the ground for three days. Imam al-Jawād said concerning him: "After the event of Karbala, there was for us no slaughter-ground greater than the calamity of Fakhkh."

[^s21-11]: Ibrāhīm ibn ʿAbdullāh, brother of the Pure Soul, at first took allegiance from the people in his brother's name; and when his brother was martyred, he called the people to himself. He rose up in Basra and quickly seized the city. A group of the people of Kufa invited him to that city. On the road to Kufa, Ibrāhīm encountered the army of the Abbasid caliph and was martyred.

[^s21-12]: (s-l-m): *musallam* — surrendered to, accepted, established as certain.

[^s21-13]: (kh-dh-l): *makhdhūl* — rendered abject and humiliated, forsaken.

[^s21-14]: Hadith number 17 (a sermon of the Commander of the Faithful, from the *Nahj al-Balāghah*).

[^s21-15]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Muʾmin (Ghāfir), verses 51–55.

[^s21-16]: Imam al-Ṣādiq, in *Tafsīr Nūr al-Thaqalayn*, on verse 51 of Sūrat al-Muʾmin.

[^s21-17]: *ʿAlā ayyi ḥāl* — "in any case, at all events."

[^s21-18]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anʿām, verse 158.

[^s21-19]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anbiyāʾ, verses 7–18.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 22: The Commitment of Faith in Prophethood

*تعهد ایمان به نبوت — Thursday, 18 Mehr 1353 / 23 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anfāl (8:72):** "Indeed, those who believed and emigrated and struggled with their wealth and their lives in the way of God, and those who gave shelter and aid — those are allies of one another. But those who believed yet did not emigrate — you owe them no guardianship at all until they emigrate. And if they seek your help in religion, then upon you is to help, except against a people between whom and you there is a treaty. And God is Seeing of what you do."[^s22-1]

This is an issue that must absolutely be raised within the discussions of Prophethood, and if we fail to understand it and do not grasp it, then much of what has been discussed on the subject of Prophethood will have almost no practical effect for us. This last discussion is a guarantee for the previous discussions to be put into practice and to enter into our lives.

What is this final discussion? It is this: we who say *"Ashhadu anna Muḥammadan rasūl Allāh"* — "I bear witness that Muhammad is the Messenger of God" — I announce, in my call to prayer, in my ritual prayer, in the act of pronouncing the two testimonies (the *shahādatayn*), everywhere, that I am of the Prophet's Ummah, and I accept, I am convinced, I have faith in his prophethood — this testimony that I give, what commitment does it place upon my shoulders? Does this belief, this testimony, this profession, create any commitment for me at all, or not?

There is one kind of testimony: you say, "I bear witness and accept that — suppose — the narcissus flower is more fragrant than the Muhammadan rose." That is one matter. Some people do not even know this; some believe the opposite. You, sir, whether rightly or wrongly, have become convinced that this flower's scent is more pleasing to the nose and better than that flower. Well, now that you have given this testimony — *thumma mādhā*, as we seminary students say, "and then what?" Nothing. You see that if a person testifies and accepts that this flower is better than that flower, or, conversely, accepts that that flower is better than this flower, it has no effect on our life, it creates no commitment in our life.

Let me give another example. In the Christian world, one person stands at the head of the religious hierarchy, called the Pope. As you know, this Pope is in reality merely a kind of statue that people must respect. He does not put forth any new theory in Christian beliefs, nor does he issue any new ruling among Christian laws that differs from previous rulings; his being or not being is just like the presence or absence of a beautiful statue in your reception room — if it is there, the room's décor is complete; if it is not there, then in the eyes of luxury-loving people the room is deficient. The existence of the Pope and his absence have no greater effect than this on the Christian world from the standpoint of Christian thought. Now, in this very year in which we are living, if an informed Christian were to be aware that the present Pope has passed away and so-and-so has taken his place, or were not to believe it, it would have no effect whatsoever on his life. If a Christian were to say, "I bear witness that the existing Pope of my time is Mr. So-and-so," this testimony brings him no commitment. That is to say, the state of this Christian and the state of that broken-down old man sitting in a corner of some village — who has no news that this former Pope has died and that someone has come in his place — the state of this one is the same as the state of that uninformed man cast off in his corner. Just as the latter's life situation underwent no change after the death of the former Pope, so too in the life situation of this gentleman who knows the Pope died and another has taken his seat, no change of any kind comes about. "I bear witness that today the Pope is so-and-so" — this testimony creates no commitment.

Now: when I say in the *tashahhud* of my prayer, *"Ashhadu anna Muḥammadan rasūl Allāh,"* when it is proclaimed from the minaret of my city and province as a general slogan, as a portrayer of the overall countenance of this society, *"Ashhadu anna Muḥammadan rasūl Allāh"* — this testimony to prophethood, this faith in prophethood and the proclaiming of this faith, does it or does it not place a commitment upon my shoulders as the one professing it, upon the shoulders of that society for which this profession is uttered as its slogan? This is the question.

The answer is this: yes, it does. What commitment does it place upon the shoulder of the follower of the Prophet and the acceptor of his call? In a single phrase, I summarize this commitment: the commitment, the responsibility, that a person who believes in the prophethood of the Prophet takes upon his shoulder consists of following the path of the Prophet and accepting the responsibility of carrying the Prophet's burden home. These words are uttered very easily, but their responsibility is very heavy; and fundamentally, to be of the Prophet's Ummah and to be a witness to his prophethood is precisely this.

A group of people imagine that if they have said "We accept that so-and-so is a prophet" — that as soon as they have proclaimed with the tongue the acceptance of his prophethood that is in their heart — their work is done, and they have brought themselves across the border of Hell into the border of Heaven. Listen carefully; see whether this belief I am describing exists in your mind too, or not. I have no concern with who holds this belief and who does not. Some imagine that human beings were burning in the fire of God's wrath and fury, and then the matter of the prophethood of the Seal of the Prophets was raised; a number of these people who were in the region of torment and the precinct of God's wrath said *"Ashhadu anna Muḥammadan rasūl Allāh"* — "I bear witness that Muhammad is the Prophet of God" — and by uttering this phrase they came out of the region of God's torment, they were pulled out, and they fell into the region of mercy. Now, if they prayed, they come a little closer, within the region of mercy, to the wellspring of mercy; if they fasted, they come a little closer; if they paid *khums* and *zakāt*, they come a little closer; if they also enjoined good and forbade evil, they come a little bit closer; if they performed other deeds too, then they reach the wellspring of mercy; and even if they did not perform them, in the end they are still within the region of mercy. Did you grasp it? Some imagine it this way.

The result of this way of thinking is that if today a person were to write in his identity document "Muslim, follower of the Prophet of Islam" — back when they used to write it in identity documents, though now they do not — or, in a question and answer put to him, when they ask, "Sir, what is your creed and religion?" and he said "Islam"; merely because he has specified Islam and not specified Christianity, not specified materialism, not specified Judaism, not specified some other religion — merely because, among these various things and items presented to him under the name of religion, he has put his finger on Islam, they say: "Very well; since you have specified Islam, go to Heaven. Now, if you also prayed, all the better; if you also fasted, all the better; if you also did other deeds, all the better; and even if you did not do them, your place is in God's Heaven — except that before you reach the Resurrection, they will squeeze you hard." This is a saying and a thought that is current in people's minds.

We say this saying is not correct. Faith in the Prophet is necessary, but faith in the Prophet brings commitments along with it; if those commitments are fulfilled, then to whatever extent those commitments are fulfilled, to that same extent a person's faith is sound. If a person held a faith on his tongue, or even in his heart — a faith — but did not commit himself to any of the commitments that this faith specifies for a person, did not accept any of the responsibilities that acceptance of the Prophet's prophethood places upon a person's shoulder, then this person, although outwardly a believer, is not a true believer. What will God do with him at the Resurrection? I do not know, nor do I have any concern to know; but by the measures of this world, as a matter of judgment, as a basis on which we can pass a verdict on the faith or lack of faith of a human being, we do not regard this man as a person of faith.

Of course, let me also add this: the utterance of this phrase and the outward declaration of belief — although it preserves a person's life and property, as the saying goes (though of course there is a bit of loose usage in this expression), that is, it brings a person inside the Islamic sphere — but the discussion is not about whether we should see whether a person's life and property are protected or not. We want to see whether he is a believer or not a believer. On the basis of the Quranic criteria, whose verses I will now explain for you, we say: that human being who is not bound to the commitments of faith is not a believer. A believer is one who is bound to the commitments and responsibilities that faith in prophethood brings for a person.

What is this commitment? This commitment is that I should see what the Prophet wanted to do in this world. The Prophet wanted to lift a tremendous burden, the Prophet wanted to uproot a tremendous boulder, the Prophet wanted to erect a tremendous edifice. And I too, in my own time, should look and see: has that burden which the Prophet wanted to lift been completely lifted? Has that stone which the Prophet wanted to uproot[^s22-2] from the ground and completely tear out been completely torn out? Has that edifice which the Prophet wanted to demolish and to build been built? If I saw that the answer is negative — that is, that burden is still on the ground, that stone has not been torn out, that edifice has not been built — let me strive to do that work which he wanted to do. Let me strive to lift this burden myself. If my bones were weak, if my strength did not suffice, then to the extent that I can, let me strain at it, and let me also find ten other people so that we lift this burden together; let me find yet another group so that we build this edifice together. If I could not finish the building, can I not at least bring ten bricks and stack them there? Can I not dig out even a portion of its foundation and footing? Can I not at least prepare the preliminaries of the work? If I say I cannot, it is a lie.

He must be bound to this commitment; otherwise, if he were not bound to this commitment, it is a lie for him to say, "I bear witness that he is the Prophet of God." This is a superficial testimony. "I bear witness that he is a prophet, but I cannot bear witness that I believe in his prophethood" — like the hypocrites:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Munāfiqūn (63:1):** "When the hypocrites come to you, they say, 'We bear witness that you are indeed the Messenger of God.' And God knows that you are indeed His Messenger, and God bears witness that the hypocrites are surely liars."[^s22-3]

They would come and say, "O Messenger of God, we bear witness that you are the Prophet of God." God says: yes, We Ourselves know that you are Our prophet; this matter is clear to Us, in Our view certain — the statement is, in itself, true. But We bear witness that they, in this testimony of theirs, were liars; in their hearts they do not accept this matter, while with their tongues they are saying it.

The commitment of the messengership is the building of a world in the form that Islam has stated; this is the commitment of the messengership. The Messenger, when he comes, comes in order to build a world to the dimensions that Islam proposes. The Prophet is commissioned for this — so that he may set right the form of life and the order of life of human beings in the manner that God states. If you, in your own time, saw that humanity is not living by the God-ordained form, saw that mankind is deprived of having a divine society, saw that various schools are pulling humanity this way and that and that Islam has remained confined to a corner of people's minds and a corner of their hearts and no more — then your duty and your commitment, on the basis of the testimony you give to the messengership of the Prophet, is to strive to bring the world into the form ordained by Islam. This is the responsibility and the commitment of prophethood.

Just as has been written in these papers, which you have studied or are studying: Islam brings a new thought; on the basis of this new thought, a new front-formation comes into being, a new lining-up comes into being. And we have repeatedly arrived at this conclusion, through the study of the verses of the Quran, that religion, in essence, means the creation of a new front-formation and lining-up.

When human beings are living in an ignorant (*jāhilī*) society, the Prophet, when he comes into this society, divides into two groups the human beings who are all obedient and tamed, the human beings who are all facing one direction and one way; one group he rescues from this misguidance, this straying, this bewilderment, he changes their path, and they become two groups. In the sense I have described, the prophets are an agent of division; in this sense, the prophets are an agent of duality. Remember the sense in which I said this, so that if you wish to recount it somewhere, you do not say "So-and-so says that the Prophet is an agent of division." In this sense the Prophet is an agent of division; in which sense? In this way: everyone is going, like the cars of a single train, toward the downward slope of ruin; the Prophet comes and takes hold of these cars from behind. Some of the cars detach themselves from the Prophet's hand and go toward the downward slope of ruin; a number welcome this holding-back of the Prophet's; division falls among the cars.

This caravan is going toward the bandits, is going toward the place of death, is going toward the earthquake-zone; the Prophet comes and says, "Do not go." A number listen to his word and do not go; a number do not listen and go. So they become two fronts — division. In this sense, the prophets, when they come, create division and duality among societies — except that it is a duality within a society that was uniformly misguided. They come and say, "Turn back toward God"; among those uniform masses division falls; some turn back, some do not turn back.

So a new front, a new lining-up, a new mutual positioning comes into being in society with the coming of the Prophet. The Prophet on one side, in one rank; the enemies and opponents and the obstinate adversaries of the Prophet on the other side, in another rank. Pay close attention to these two ranks that I am sketching. The Prophet was at first all alone; everyone was in that opposing rank. The Prophet has exerted effort, has striven, one by one, two by two, until at last he has been able to form a rank facing that misguided and hellbound rank; to bring into being a rank facing the rank of error. They are two ranks facing each other: one rank is the rank of the Prophet; one rank is the rank of the Prophet's enemies.

What did the Prophet want to do? He wanted to take the people to Heaven — the Heaven of this world and the Heaven after death, both. Because he wants to take the people to Heaven, the people must come with him; if they did not come with him, they do not reach Heaven. Is this matter correct? The Prophet wants to take human beings to the destination of felicity; if they do not come with him, if they do not become his companions and fellow travelers, they do not reach the destination of felicity. Remember this well. Now, between these two ranks there is a person; he looks at the Prophet, he sees that he speaks true words; however much he listens, he sees that the Prophet's word is a good word. On the other hand, he sees that if he comes into the Prophet's rank, he will be compelled to contend, together with that opposing rank, against one another. He has no heart to go into the opposing rank, because he sees that they are going toward Hell; he has no heart to come into the Prophet's rank, because he sees that the Prophet's rank has trouble. What does he do? He comes between these two ranks and chooses a safe, secure, peaceful spot; he pitches a tent there and sits down. What sort of person is this? You tell me.

This man who has sat down in the middle, slumbering between the two ranks on a comfortable bed — will this man reach Heaven or not? It is obvious that he will not; because the Prophet wants to go to Heaven, and those go who set out together with him, while this one has not set out with the Prophet. Whoever is between the two ranks is not with the Prophet; whoever has not joined the Prophet is against the Prophet; whoever is not with Ali is against Ali; whoever is not with the truth is against the truth. The Quran tells us this too, but the eloquent, clear tongue of the Imam (peace be upon him) — which is much closer to comprehension — states it for us. He says, *"Al-sākitu akhū al-rāḍī"*[^s22-4] — learn this hadith; the reference for the hadith is *Biḥār al-Anwār*, the volume of admonitions (*mawāʿiẓ*) — *"Al-sākitu akhū al-rāḍī, wa man lam yakun maʿanā kāna ʿalaynā."* The one who is silent in the face of a false situation is the brother of the one who is content with that situation. They do not even go on to say what the one who is content is; that is obvious — *man raḍiya bi-ʿamali qawmin fa-huwa minhum*: whoever is content with the deed of a community, content with the life of a people, is one of them; they too will take him and tie him to the same manger to which they tie those people. This one who is silent — even though in his heart he is discontented, yet he does not proclaim this discontent — he too is the brother of the one who is content. Then it continues: *"wa man lam yakun maʿanā kāna ʿalaynā"* — whoever is not with us is against us. Between these two ranks, Islam has not supposed any place.

Those who, like the companions of ʿAbdullāh ibn Masʿūd — among them this very gentleman Rabīʿ ibn Khuthaym[^s22-5] (if this grave in Khurasan is indeed his) — said at the Battle of Ṣiffīn, "We are not prepared to accompany the Commander of the Faithful, because he is going to shed the blood of Muslims," and came in a comfort-seeking manner and requested of him that he send them to the frontiers to do frontier-guarding — these people read it blind. They imagined that the war between Yazdgerd the Third and — I do not know — Heraclius the umpteenth of Rome was such that, if either of them prevailed, it would be of no benefit at all to a person's religion and worldly life! So let a person, seeking ease, conclude a pact of non-aggression, conclude an agreement of non-intervention in the war, and go and sit in some corner. They imagined it was an international war in which several nations are fighting one another over power and government; whichever country can keep itself aside has won. They did not understand that the war between truth and falsehood is an unavoidable war. They did not know that in the war between truth and falsehood, if you were not with the truth, you are with falsehood. To be with falsehood does not necessarily mean that you definitely fight against the truth; rather, even that case in which you do not fight for the truth is also included in the concept of being with falsehood. This they did not understand. Prophethood comes and makes the ranks distinct; it says, "Let those who are with us come."

> He said: O company, whoever has not love for us,
> let him take to the road and depart from our Karbala.[^s22-6]

Let him go; he is no longer with us. That one whom Ḥusayn ibn ʿAlī, on the road, summons — "Come, help us" — and who says, "O son of the Messenger of God, shall I offer this horse of mine to your service, or this sword of mine?" — it is clear that he is no longer with Ḥusayn; he is against Ḥusayn, not with Ḥusayn. And so you see that our traditionists write and say: that unfortunate one — truly unfortunate. And they speak the truth too. And woe to those wretched people who, like him, are unfortunate throughout time; O unfortunate one! The prophets come and make the road clear; they say, "This is it. If you are a man, if you are a seeker of truth, if you are for God's sake, if you bear witness to our prophethood — then come, by God, our road is this. But if you have sat down there, and you saw our road, you saw the wayfarers of our road, and because it was difficult you did not come, because it was difficult you did not help, because it was difficult you even turned your face the other way — that is, 'I did not see' — but at that very moment you took up a rosary in your hand and said, 'I bear witness that you are prophets, I bear witness that you are prophets, I bear witness that you are prophets,' and you also told off one round of the rosary — this is of no use. Say nothing, and come; do not bring this remembrance to your tongue, do not act:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ṣaff (61:2–3):** "O you who have believed, why do you say that which you do not do? Great is the hatred in the sight of God that you say what you do not do."[^s22-7]

Why do you not act upon that which you say with your tongue, "I believe in it"? Woe unto me — I am describing my own condition. It is a great sin for a person to say something with the tongue, to declare a belief, but not act upon it. Why should I say I am a follower of the Prophet of God, while I am a follower of Abū Jahl? Why should I say I am after Islam, while I am after polytheism? Why should I say I am an ʿAlawī (a follower of Ali), while I am of Muʿāwiya and of Abū Jahl?

What was the difference between Ali and Muʿāwiya? By God, today you sketch out Ali and Muʿāwiya in your society. Picture one person of whom everything he says is against ease and indulgence and personal comfort, everything he says lays a burden of responsibility on the shoulder, everything he wants is effort. He is repelled by lying; he is made uneasy by a bribe; he is made uneasy by a gift that gives off the smell of a bribe. On the path of God and for God's sake he is fierce.[^s22-8] He has a strange leopard-temper on the path of God. On the path of God and the ruling of God he shows mercy to no one; even his own brother, when he comes and asks him for money from the public treasury (*bayt al-māl*), he places red-hot iron toward his fist. One such person in society — strict, precise, cautious in the execution of the divine and Islamic rulings and prescribed limits — this is one man. And one man is his exact opposite; he is willing for a person to make merry, willing for a person to live in comfort, willing — whatever you want from him — to give it to you, and he sets only one condition before a person in return, and that condition is that he says, "Do not help Ali, help me; and the help I want from you is not much help either — sometimes praise me, and that is enough." Well, by God, of these two persons, after which one do you go? Which one do you accept in this time and in this segment of history? Are you willing to go with him, to be with him, to act upon his command — the one who has trouble, responsibility, movement, effort — to be with him? Are you willing to pass over the one who has money for a person, has rank, has comfort, has title, has influence, has a powerful hand — to pass over him for the sake of this? If you are willing, blessed are you; if you had been alive in Ali's time too, you would have been a Shia of Ali. But if you see that your heart takes flight toward comforts, indulgences, pleasures, monies, ranks, prestige, preening and boasting before this person and that — even if it be outside the path of God — then know that if you had been there, you would have been one of those who, if you were very calculating, would by night, secretly, without having bid farewell to the neighbors, mount your horse, even telling your wife and children, "I await you in Sham (Syria); yā ʿAlī madad!" — you would go off toward Sham, you would leave Ali alone, just as many of the respected figures of that time left him.

ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās, the cousin of the Commander of the Faithful and cousin of the Prophet, the narrator of so much hadith, the commentator of the Quran, a figure respected among Shia and Sunni — did this very thing to Ali, so that the Commander of the Faithful, in *Nahj al-Balāgha*, has two letters and two addresses to ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās. And who was ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās, after all? Merely because they narrated some four hadiths from him, on the authority of the Prophet, both Shia and Sunni accept him, all accept him. He was not even a Companion of the Prophet, yet all accept him — and this is a point worth noting. If he had been a Companion of the Prophet and Shia and Sunni accepted him, it would not matter — like Salmān, like Abū Dharr, like ʿAmmār, whom all accept. But he is one of the Successors (*tābiʿīn*); he did not properly perceive the time of the Prophet; he was a child when the Prophet passed away. I was studying a bit of history, and I saw that this gentleman ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās was among the companions and entourage of the second Caliph; he was very fond of him too — this one also was mostly in the train of Mr. ʿUmar. For a man to be one of the Successors, not to have perceived the time of the Prophet, and yet for both Shia and Sunni to accept him — *yā lal-ʿajab!* (how astonishing!) — this must be a very strange man. What is the name for this in Islamic convention — that two hostile groups should both accept one person? This man, while he was the governor and ruler (*ustāndār*) of Basra, took the monies of the public treasury and made his getaway! Where to? Mecca — into the safe and secure sanctuary of the Lord. No doubt he took the monies there and gave them in charity! He gave them to the poor — yes, he gave them to the poor slave-girl dealers; he bought a few slave-girls, beautiful slave-girls, with whom he might be at ease and busy himself in pleasure-seeking.

Now, if ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās were here today, what do you suppose he would say about the Commander of the Faithful? He would narrate all the first-hand hadiths on the merit of Ali; he would shed tears at the remembrance of Ali; from having been with Ali he would recount memories. But would you and I — clever and shrewd as we are — accept that he is a Shia? We would say: "Sir, go set this snare for some other bird,[^s22-9] go; if you were a Shia, you ought to have shown yourself at the time of the test." *"ʿInda al-imtiḥān yukramu al-rajulu aw yuhān"*[^s22-10] — at the time of the test a man is either honored or disgraced. *"Fī taqallub al-aḥwāl ʿulima jawāhir al-rijāl"*[^s22-11] — in the vicissitudes of circumstances the substances of men become known. If you had been a Shia of Ali, you would not have given Ali so much heart's blood; by your fleeing you would not have wrung out the moaning of Ali so much, such that Ali moaned that way over the departure of ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās: "You were closer to me than all my kinsmen; I had hope in you; I relied upon you. You left your cousin alone in these conditions and went away?!" It is a letter that Ali has in *Nahj al-Balāgha*, addressed to ʿAbdullāh — except that the late Raḍī (may God's pleasure be upon him),[^s22-12] in *Nahj al-Balāgha*, because he was living in the time of the Banū al-ʿAbbās, either out of fear or out of embarrassment to write "From a letter of his to ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās," wrote, "From a letter of his to one of his administrators"[^s22-13] — he wrote that the Imam (peace be upon him) wrote to one of his agents, his governors, but did not write who this governor was. When you read this letter, well, it is obvious that it was ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās; and moreover, apart from *Nahj al-Balāgha*, in other places they have narrated that it pertains to ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās — that "you left your cousin alone, you did such and such and such and such."

In any case, the commitment of accepting and embracing prophethood is this: walking behind the Prophet, accepting his assignment, acting in the very manner that he wants. My time has now grown short for me to explain all the verses; let me explain just this brief portion of the verses for you, so that you may see what the commitment of Islam is.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Anfāl (8:72):** *"Inna alladhīna āmanū wa hājarū…"* — "Indeed, those who believed and emigrated"[^s22-14]

Those who believed and emigrated and struggled with their wealth and their lives. This is in regard to the time of the Prophet — not that I mean to say it is not for other times; no, this is a general ruling. It is being stated in regard to the time of the Prophet, when the matter of emigration (*hijra*) was also at issue there — emigration to the Islamic society. In those days a number of people would become Muslim, would accept the thought of the Prophet, but were not prepared to come out of Mecca. They would say, "Well, why should I go out? In Mecca I have a shop with two or three doorways,[^s22-15] I have an excellent round telephone number, I have known and recognized customers, I have kinsmen and relatives and friends and drinking companions — should I leave all these and go off to the side of the Prophet? Well, why? Is faith required? I have faith — I will say it a hundred times, in my heart, on my tongue, softly of course so that no one hears: God is One, and the Prophet too is in the right. The Prophet wants prayer from me — I pray; he wants fasting — instead of thirty days I will fast sixty days; why should I go to Medina?" Some thought this way. Emigration was required there. The Islamic society was newly founded; they had to go, they had to strengthen it, and they had to make that society invulnerable against its enemies; therefore emigration was a decisive condition of the acceptance of faith.

> *"Inna alladhīna āmanū"* — those who believed and adhered; *"wa hājarū"* — and emigrated to Medina; *"wa jāhadū bi-amwālihim wa anfusihim"* — and with their wealth and their lives, both, exerted struggle in the way of God; and likewise, from the other side; *"wa alladhīna āwaw"* — those who gave shelter to these people, who were without protector and without home, in Medina; *"wa naṣarū"* — and aided them; *"ulāʾika baʿḍuhum awliyāʾu baʿḍ"* — these are allies of one another.

These are some who are joined to and on the same front as the others. These are those same believers who are like bricks interlocked into one another. Look at a building, look at this edifice: the bricks have gone into one another, the timbers have gone into one another, the parts of a building are interlaced and knotted together with the other parts; the believers in the Islamic society are just like this — all joined and welded and knotted to one another. *Awliyāʾ* means this, *wilāyat* means this: complete joining, complete cleaving and adhesion[^s22-16] — this is *wilāyat*.

> *"Wa alladhīna āmanū…"* — *"ulāʾika baʿḍuhum awliyāʾu baʿḍ"* — they are some the allies of the others.

But now — listen here — those who believed, who in their hearts believed that you are a prophet, but *"wa lam yuhājirū"* — but did not emigrate, did not take the commitment of faith upon their shoulder — *"mā lakum min walāyatihim min shayʾin ḥattā yuhājirū"* — you have no joining or connection with these people at all, until they emigrate. As long as they have not emigrated and they remain there, they are strangers to you. There is no Islamic bond or brotherhood-tie between you.

Except that there is another ruling alongside it: those very ones who are there — if they happen to fall into war with another group and seek your help, you, of course, must go and aid them, because they are of one mind with you and because they are in a state of war. If a Muslim were engaged in war with a group of unbelievers, it is obligatory upon you — even though that Muslim is not beside you, is not in your homeland, has not emigrated to you — it is obligatory upon you to go and help him. Yes? Except in one case, and that is this: that the one with whom that Muslim is fighting has concluded a peace treaty with you; in that case, helping that Muslim is no longer obligatory. What does this verse make us understand here? First, it makes us understand that helping a Muslim, wherever in the world they may be, is obligatory, even if they have not emigrated. Second, it says that the Muslim who has not emigrated, who has not migrated to the Islamic society — although of course today, in the world, we do not have an Islamic society in this sense — who has not come to the Islamic society, who has remained in the abode of unbelief (*dār al-kufr*), this man, if he fell into war with one or a group of unbelievers, and you have a peace treaty and non-aggression pact with that unbeliever, you have no right to go to the aid of your Muslim brother. Why? Because he has not emigrated, because he is not your brother, because he has not migrated toward you.

> *"Wa in istanṣarūkum fī al-dīn fa-ʿalaykumu al-naṣr illā ʿalā qawmin"* — except against a people; against the harm of a people — *"baynakum wa baynahum mīthāq"* — between whom and you there is a commitment and a treaty. *"Wa Allāhu bimā taʿmalūna baṣīr"* — and God is Seeing of what you do.

> *"Wa alladhīna kafarū baʿḍuhum awliyāʾu baʿḍ"* — those who are unbelievers, some are on the same front as and joined to the others.

Do not look at the fact that they are in two camps; in enmity toward you, they are of one camp and one front.

> *"Illā tafʿalūhu takun fitnatun fī al-arḍi wa fasādun kabīr"* — if you do not do this, there will be a discord (*fitna*) in the earth and a great corruption.

Perhaps the meaning is that if you do not observe this front-formation and this lining-up — if you do not know, and have not known, that your rank, facing the rank of the enemies of God, is a distinct and separate[^s22-17] rank, and if you do not know that whoever is between the two ranks is of the rank of the enemies and opponents, not of this rank — if you do not know these things and do not act in accordance with their requirements, there will be discord in the earth, there will be corruption. The *fitna* is the discord of distance from religion; the *fasād*, probably, is the corruption of the non-existence of God's ruling in the midst of society.

> *"Wa alladhīna āmanū"* — those who have believed; *"wa hājarū"* — and migrated

— pay attention to this verse too, for the verse is an interesting witness to these matters we have set forth —

> *"Wa alladhīna āmanū"* — those who have believed; *"wa hājarū"* — they emigrated; *"wa jāhadū fī sabīl Allāh"* — they struggled in the way of God; *"wa alladhīna āwaw wa naṣarū"* — those who gave shelter and aid; *"ulāʾika humu al-muʾminūna ḥaqqā"* — these are the true believers.

Did you understand correctly? The true believer is this. But the one who has believed and from whom emigration, struggle, sheltering, and aiding have not issued — what is he? *Hum al-muʾminūna ghayra ḥaqq*: false believers, lying believers. This is the import of the verse — to the end of the verses.

Let me also set forth one word from those verses of Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, because it is necessary that I expound it briefly, since otherwise our meaning is not understood:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:81):** *"Wa idh akhadha Allāhu mīthāqa al-nabiyyīn…"*[^s22-18]

In this verse, whose translation I rendered there and will not translate again, this is the matter it wishes to express: that We took a commitment even from the past prophets; We said to them, to the past prophet — for example to Moses — We said: that which We have given you, if after you a prophet came who confirmed and ratified what We have now given you, it is required of you to have faith in that prophet and to aid him. That is to say, Moses regarding the prophets after himself, Jesus regarding the prophets after himself — every prophet who comes after another prophet and ratifies the word of the previous prophet — it is required of the previous prophet, who acknowledges him, to believe in that prophet who comes after him; and in addition to this, to aid him too. *"La-tuʾminunna bihi wa la-tanṣurunnah"* — he must aid him.

Then it says: now, what is the aid of the prophet? The aid of the prophet is this — Moses, for example, how does he aid our Prophet? In that he enjoins upon his Ummah, his friends, his companions: beware, beware, beware — if this prophet came with these signs, do not oppose him. Well, that is aid. *"Qāla a-aqrartum…"* — God said to these prophets: Did you acknowledge? Did you accept this commitment of Mine? This covenant that I am taking from you, that you must believe in the prophets after yourselves, on this condition: *"a-aqrartum wa akhadhtum ʿalā dhālikum iṣrī"* — did you acknowledge, and did you take My burden (covenant) upon yourselves? Likewise, did you take this very covenant from your Ummah too, until the Day of Resurrection? That is to say, the Jews of the world are, right now, on the part of Moses son of ʿImrān, being held to account for this covenant; right now Moses son of ʿImrān, by the tongue of his state and his word, is as though saying to them: "O you ignoble ones! Did I not take a covenant from you that, forever, whoever has faith in Moses — Moses himself has faith in the Seal of the Prophets — must believe in the Seal of the Prophets and aid him, help him?"

---

**Chapter Four:**

**Wilāyat (ولایت)**

- The Bonds of the Islamic Ummah
- The Heaven of Wilāyat
- Concerning Wilāyat (1)
- Concerning Wilāyat (2)
- Concerning Wilāyat (3) — (Emigration)

---

[^s22-1]: Sūrat al-Anfāl (the Blessed Sūra of Spoils) / verses 72 to 74.

[^s22-2]: (q-l-ʿ) to uproot, to tear out from the root.

[^s22-3]: Sūrat al-Munāfiqūn (the Blessed Sūra of the Hypocrites) / verse 1.

[^s22-4]: *"Al-sākitu akhū al-rāḍī"* ("The silent one is the brother of the one content") — Hadith no. 23; reference: *Biḥār al-Anwār*, the volume of admonitions.

[^s22-5]: Rabīʿ ibn Khuthaym was an ascetic and devout man, counted among the Successors (*tābiʿīn*). According to what is reported in the book *Waqʿat Ṣiffīn*, he came, together with a number of others, to the Commander of the Faithful and said that they had doubt about the rightfulness of this war, and requested of the Imam that he send them to one of the frontiers for frontier-guarding and to fight against the unbelievers.

[^s22-6]: Nayyir Tabrīzī. (The two lines of poetry: "He said: O company, whoever has not love for us, / let him take to the road and depart from our Karbala.")

[^s22-7]: Sūrat al-Ṣaff (the Blessed Sūra of the Ranks) / verses 2 and 3.

[^s22-8]: (n-m-r) angry, leopard-tempered (*mutanammir*: fierce, enraged).

[^s22-9]: "Go set this snare for some other bird, / for the ʿAnqā's nest is lofty" (Ḥāfiẓ).

[^s22-10]: *"ʿInda al-imtiḥān yukramu al-rajulu aw yuhān"* ("At the time of the test a man is either honored or disgraced") — Hadith no. 19.

[^s22-11]: *"Fī taqallub al-aḥwāl ʿulima jawāhir al-rijāl"* ("In the vicissitudes of circumstances the substances of men become known") — Hadith no. 20.

[^s22-12]: Abū al-Ḥasan, Muḥammad ibn al-Ḥusayn (359–406 AH), known as Sayyid Raḍī, was born in Baghdad. Together with his elder brother, Sayyid Murtaḍā ʿAlam al-Hudā, he was raised under Shaykh al-Mufīd. This devout scholar became famous chiefly through his compilation of the book *Nahj al-Balāgha*.

[^s22-13]: *"Min kitābin lahu ilā ʿAbdillāh ibn ʿAbbās"* ("From a letter of his to ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbbās") — instead, he wrote *"Min kitābin lahu ilā baʿḍi ʿummālih"* ("From a letter of his to one of his administrators"). Letter forty-one of *Nahj al-Balāgha*.

[^s22-14]: Sūrat al-Anfāl (the Blessed Sūra of Spoils) / verses 72 to 74.

[^s22-15]: "Two or three openings" (i.e., a wide shopfront).

[^s22-16]: (l-ṣ-q) to stick to and adhere to something.

[^s22-17]: (ḥ-w-z) in terminology, meaning "independent / separate" (*munḥāz*).

[^s22-18]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (the Blessed Sūra) / verse 81.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

# Part Four: Wilayah

### ولایت


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 23: Wilayat

*ولایت — delivered Friday, 19 Mehr 1353 / 24 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah (60:1):** "O you who have believed, do not take My enemy and your enemy as allies, extending to them affection while they have disbelieved in what has come to you of the truth, having driven out the Messenger and yourselves because you believe in God, your Lord. If you have gone out for striving in My cause and seeking My pleasure [do not take them as allies], confiding to them affection — while I am most knowing of what you have concealed and what you have declared. And whoever of you does it has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way."[^s23-1]

The matter we are raising here today is a matter called Wilayat (ولایت).

The matter of Wilayat, in the form in which we raise it and derive it from the Qur'an, is most often given little attention. Of course, the name "Wilayat" and the word "Wilayat" are familiar to a Shia ear. In our supplications, in our requests from God, in our narrations, in our common and widespread thoughts, there is a matter called Wilayat — bound up with complete sanctity and reverence, and with the longing to secure something called Wilayat. As Shia, we always consider ourselves to possess Wilayat; and if a Shia is somewhat scrupulous, precise, and careful, he always asks God, he yearns, that God keep him alive with Wilayat and cause him to die with Wilayat, and make Wilayat understood to him. So a word called "Wilayat" is familiar to the ear.

Ultimately,[^s23-2] I want to speak about this word and the concept of Wilayat very much from its roots. We will arrive at the Wilayat of Ali ibn Abi Talib (upon him be blessings and peace); but for now I want to speak about the stages that come before that. We will draw Wilayat out of the Qur'an and from the noble verses of the Qur'an — we will derive and extract it — and you will see what a modern, advanced, fascinating principle the principle of Wilayat is, and how a nation, a community, the followers of one thought and one creed, if they do not possess Wilayat, are aimlessly abandoned and left idle. You will grasp this and you will feel it.

In the light of this discussion it can be well understood why someone who does not have Wilayat — his prayer is not prayer, his fasting is not fasting, and his acts of worship are not worship. With this discussion it can be well understood why a society and an Ummah that does not have Wilayat — even if it spends its whole life in prayer and fasting and in giving away all its wealth in charity — is still not worthy of God's forgiveness and grace. And in short, in the light of this discussion, the meaning of the narrations concerning Wilayat can be understood — among them this well-known hadith that has come from several of the Imams (peace be upon them), some of whose sentences and words I will repeat here: «If a man were to stand in prayer through his nights, and fast through his days, and give all his wealth in charity, and perform Hajj every year of his life, yet did not recognize the Wilayat of the friend (walī) of God so as to take him as his guardian and have all his deeds be by that one's guidance...»[^s23-3] — if someone fasts his whole life, not only the month of Ramadan; if he stays awake all his nights until morning; if he gives away all his wealth in the way of God; yet does not have Wilayat with the friend (walī) of God — pay attention to this expression — does not have Wilayat with the friend of God, or does not possess the Wilayat of the friend of God — such a person, all that he has done is vain, fruitless, and null.

The matter of Wilayat is a continuation of the discussion of Prophethood; it is not something separate from the discussion of Prophethood. The matter of Wilayat is, in truth, the completion, the appendix, and the conclusion of the discussion of Prophethood. Now we will see that if there is no Wilayat, Prophethood too remains incomplete. Therefore we are obliged to briefly repeat the past discussions regarding Prophethood — let me state its broad outlines for you so that we may arrive, little by little, entering from the margins into the body of the matter of Wilayat. Of course, let me also say this: raising this matter is very difficult, and expounding this matter is even more difficult. The reason is that so many weak, flimsy, and illogical ideas about Wilayat have become entrenched in the minds of ordinary people that when you want to say the correct thing — the thing that conforms to the text of the Qur'an and the text of the hadith — about Wilayat, one of two problems arises: either it is confused with those very things that are already in people's minds, or a feeling of estrangement arises toward what is being said as "Wilayat." This happens in two groups; therefore this discussion is very difficult and troublesome. But I seek aid from the grace of the Most Exalted Lord, and I will try to complete this discussion within two or three days, God willing. And you brothers too, pay attention, take heed, do not grow weary from the monotony of the discussion, so that I can follow the discussion through completely.

For what does the Prophet of God come? We said that the Prophet comes to bring human beings to perfection; he comes to imbue human beings with the character of God (akhlāq Allāh); he comes to complete and bring to fullness the noble traits of character. What I said is the substance of the narrations: «I was sent to bring the noble traits of character to completion.»[^s23-4] The Prophet comes to build human beings; he comes to bring to firmness this substance whose name is "the human being."

By what path and by what means does the Prophet make use, to build the human being? How does the Prophet build human beings? Does he set up a school? Does he set up a philosophical doctrine? Does the Prophet set up a monastery and a place of worship? We said: no. The Prophet, in order to build the human being, sets up a human-building factory. The Prophet prefers to succeed ten years later, twenty years later; but what he builds should not be one human being and two human beings and twenty human beings — rather, he should set up a human-building factory that automatically delivers complete human beings, the kind of human beings the Prophet desires. So the Prophet, in order to build human beings and bring the human substance to firmness, makes use of a human-building factory.

What is the human-building factory, sir? The human-building factory is the Islamic society and order. Here is the crux, the point on which the essential attention and emphasis of the discussion rests. Everyone says the Prophet wants to build human beings; everyone says the Prophet comes for teaching and training, and everyone understands this. What must be understood — what must be understood with precision — is that the Prophet does not take human beings one by one to some secluded corner to whisper the love of God into their ears. The Prophets do not form a scientific and philosophical school in order to produce a handful of students whom they then send out to guide people across the regions of the world. The Prophet's work is firmer and more steadfast than that, sturdier and more deeply rooted. What does he do? He builds a factory from which nothing comes out but human beings — and that factory is the Islamic society.[^s23-5]

What is the Islamic society? What sort of element and nature is it? These are other discussions. Now, just so that this corner of the matter is briefly clarified, I will say — though, in the course of our discussion, it is not very relevant. The Islamic society means that society, that civilization, at the head of which God governs; the laws of that society are God's laws; the divine bounds are carried out in that society; the appointing and dismissing is done by God. In the social cone — if we suppose and draw it in the shape of a cone, as is customary and common among a number of sociologists — at the apex of the cone is God, and beneath God, all of humanity and all human beings. The structure is brought into being by the religion of God; the law of peace and war is established by divine ordinances; social relations, the economy, government, rights — all and all and all of them are determined by the religion of God, and the religion of God carries them out, and the religion of God stands behind this law. This is the Islamic society.

Like where, sir? Like Medina. Just as the Prophet came to Medina and formed a society. Well, they were in Mecca too; they could have scattered to the regions of the lands and worshipped God there as well — no, that was not enough. They came to Medina and formed a society, at the head of which God governed. The vicegerent of God who governed there — who, in practice, was he? The Prophet. Who established the ordinances? Who legislated them? Who enacted them? The Prophet. Who carried out the laws? Who compelled the people to carry them out? The Prophet.

In such a society — where everything is from God — the congregational prayer, and the sermon after the congregational prayer, are one and the same as the war-song of the battlefield. In that very mosque where the Prophet of God would stand and lead the congregational prayer, and ascend the pulpit for the people and speak and teach and purify and instruct — in that very mosque the banner of jihad would be brought, and the Prophet would bind it and place it in the hand of Usama ibn Zayd,[^s23-6] place it in the hand of so-and-so, [saying] "Go forth!" «Set out in the name of God!»[^s23-7] — Move in the name of God, corner the enemy at such-and-such place, do such-and-such. In that very mosque, the Prophet of God would carry out the prescribed punishments (ḥadd). In that very mosque, the Prophet's judiciary was convened. In that very mosque, the Prophet's administration of labor and economy was convened. In that very mosque, the zakat was collected and distributed. There was teaching, there was prayer and supplication, there was the war-song of the battlefield, there was wealth and economy — and in short, everything, both this world and the hereafter, was unified and gathered into one, under the leadership of the Prophet, in the house of God. This is the Islamic society. The Prophets come to set up such a society. In this society, whoever comes becomes a human being. Even if he does not become a complete human being, he is compelled to move according to the conduct of human beings. Whoever wishes to be good can be good in the Prophet's society; in non-divine societies it is not like this.

In non-Islamic and non-divine societies, people want to be good but cannot. You want to be religious, but you cannot. You want not to give or take usury, but you find that it cannot be done. A woman wants not to step outside Islamic chastity, but the environment pressures her. All the factors and motives drive a person away from the remembrance of God. Pictures and shows and excessive entertainments and comings and goings and dealings and conversations — all of them drive a person away from God and estrange the remembrance of God from a person's heart. In the Islamic society, the matter is the reverse. In the Islamic society, its marketplace, its mosque, its seat of government, its friend, its kinsman, the father of the household, the youth of the household — all and all of them turn a person toward the remembrance of God, draw him toward God, reconcile him with God, establish a relationship for him with God, make of him a servant of God, and keep him away from servitude to other than God.

If the Islamic society of the Prophet's time had continued for fifty years; if the same leadership had remained at work; or if, after the Prophet, Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) had sat in the Prophet's place with the very leadership that the Prophet had appointed — rest assured of this: after fifty years, all those hypocrites would have been transformed into true believers. If they had allowed the Prophetic government and the Alid government to continue, one after the other, that human-building society would, of necessity, have made all the adulterated[^s23-8] people pure of adulteration, made all the hypocritical hearts believing, and made all those whose spirits were not acquainted with faith likewise acquainted with God and with faith. This is the natural character of the matter pertaining to the Islamic society. The Prophets come to set up such a thing. When this is set up, like a human-building factory, people become Muslims — not one by one, not ten by ten, not a hundred by a hundred, but group by group, host by host: both outwardly Muslim, in the outward conduct of Islamic matters, and inwardly Muslim and believing, truly and inwardly. Is this clear? So the Prophet comes for such a work.

I said we want to discuss Wilayat from its roots. At the very beginning, when the Prophet brings the Islamic thought; at the very beginning, when the Prophet's call commences — can the Prophet, single-handedly, administer a society? Does a society not need an organization? Does it not need a group of people to administer this organization? Does it not need a soldiery to defend this society and put the enemies of this society in their place? Are people who would assist the Prophet and spread the call of the Prophet not necessary? We see that, yes [they are]. Every task must be done through ordinary means. The Prophets too, in most of their activities, made use of ordinary means. When the Prophet comes, in order to be able to form the intended society — which is that very human-building factory — for this work he needs a bonded, unified group, possessing firm faith, from the depths of the hearts of those who believe in and are committed to this doctrine, with steadfast steps, dynamic and seeking that goal. The Prophet needs such a group at the beginning of the work. Therefore the Prophet begins to gather and form this group. With the verses of the Qur'an, with goodly exhortations — «Call to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good admonition»[^s23-9] — with goodly exhortations, with the verses of the Qur'an, with the penetrating power of the Prophetic word, he brings into being, around himself at the beginning of the work, the hearts of certain Muslims. These constitute a community. So the Prophet, at the beginning of the work, when he raises his call, brings into being a community, brings into being a rank, creates a front against the front of unbelief. This front is composed of whom? Of steadfast Muslims, of believers committed and penetrating of heart — of those of whom [it is said] «no blame of any blamer turns them back»[^s23-10]: no censure of any blamer turns them away from the path of God. These are the Muslims of the first step.

(I am speaking very briefly and in compressed form, for minds that are willing to grasp. I feel it does not need much explanation and elaboration; moreover, if I were to explain a great deal, we would stray entirely from the thread of the discourse, and the main point would be lost. If on other days I see that there is a need, it is possible I will explain more. For now, let me state the main matter.)

A group and a front are brought into being by the Prophet. In the midst of what society are these living? In the midst of the society of ignorance (jāhiliyya). For example, the Muslims of early Islam are living there in the midst of the ignorant society of Mecca. If this thin current — which has come into being under the name of Islam and the Muslims — is to remain in the midst of that conflict-ridden and troublesome ignorant society; if this very community, this very rank, this very front is not to be annihilated, not to perish, not to be digested, not to be dissolved — then this group of Muslims must be welded to one another like tempered steel. These Muslims must be so connected and bound to one another that no factor can separate them from each other. In the words of today's people and today's cultures, a very intense and hard party discipline must be established among these Muslim individuals. They must be stuck together more and more, welded and knotted together more and more, and kept further and further from other fronts, from other currents, from opposing motives; because these are in the minority. A community that is in the minority — it is possible that its thought may be overshadowed by the thought of the majority. Its deeds, its dignity, its character may be lost amid the dignities and characters and deeds of the rest of the people, who at times are opposed to them; [it may] be annihilated, be digested, perish. So that these are not digested, so that these are not annihilated, so that they can remain as a single group — so that in the future the Islamic society may be built and administered and continued by the hands of these steadfast ones, and so that they may be helpers of the Prophet, and so that they can endure — they are connected to one another more and more, and separated from the other fronts more and more.

Like what? Like a group of mountaineers passing along a difficult, treacherous mountain path. Ten people in the midst of the snows, staves in hand, must traverse this narrow, danger-filled path — these twists and turns, this mountain pass, this defile[^s23-11] — to reach the mountain's summit. They are told: stick together, tie your belts to one another, do not move separately and one by one — for if you are left alone, there is the danger of slipping. They are firmly welded to one another. Besides welding them to one another, it is said: do not take much baggage with you, do not look this way and that, keep your attention only on your own path and your mind on your own work. They also bind them firmly to one another — their waists and hands — so that if one of them falls, if two fall, the rest can hold him up. The intense interconnection of the mountaineers is the indicator and exhibitor of the state of interconnection and welded-togetherness of the Muslims at the beginning of the work. Does this intense interconnection have a name or not? This bondedness of the Muslims of the front at the beginning of the religion — who have been welded to one another, knotted to one another, who are inseparable from one another, who are completely cut off from the other fronts, who are ever more hand-in-hand and clasping one another — does it have a name in the Qur'an and hadith or not? Yes; this name is Wilayat — Wilayat.

There is still a long way to go before we reach the Wilayat of Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), and the [hadith] «And the Wilayat of Ali ibn Abi Talib is my fortress.»[^s23-12] We will reach that after an hour or two of speaking. We have not yet reached those points; you must listen.

So, what does Wilayat mean in its first Qur'anic sense? In its first Qur'anic sense, Wilayat means the interconnection, the shared-frontedness, and the intense bonding of a group of human beings who possess a single thought and seek a single goal, who are stepping forward on one path, who are striving and moving toward one objective, who have accepted one thought and one creed. The individuals of this front must be connected to one another more and more, and must separate and hold themselves apart from the other fronts, the other poles, the other sections. Why? So that they do not perish, do not get digested. This is what is called Wilayat in the Qur'an.

The Prophet brings into being the Muslim community of the beginning of the work with this bondedness and welded-togetherness; he connects them to one another, makes them brothers of one another, turns them into a single body, and by means of them forms the Islamic Ummah and brings the Islamic society into being. From the bonding of these with the enemies, with the opponents, with the hostile, with the other fronts — which, God willing, you will see in the verses tomorrow — he prevents them. Between them and the other fronts he creates separation; from joining the front of the Jews, from joining the front of the Christians, from joining the front of the polytheists, he restrains them, and more and more he strives to make their ranks compact and welded together. For what? Because if they are not in this state, if they do not have Wilayat, if they are not one hundred percent interconnected, discord will arise among them, and they will be incapable of bearing the burden of the trust that is upon their shoulders. They will not be able to carry this burden to its destination.

Of course, later, when the Islamic society is transformed into a great Ummah, Wilayat is still needed. How Wilayat is needed within an Ummah, and why it is needed — this I will expand upon. At this very point in the matter, let us come and pay attention to the Wilayat of which the Shia speak. We said: a small group, in the midst of a dark world, in the midst of an ignorant world, must be connected and bound to one another in order to be able to remain. If they are not stuck and welded together, their remaining and the continuation of their life is not possible. We gave the example of the Muslim community at the beginning of Islam, in the midst of the ignorant society of Mecca, or in the early period when they had come to Medina.

Is there another example as well, or not? Yes. When? The small group of Shi'ism, during the anti-Shia and anti-Islamic caliphates in history. Do you imagine that the Shia could easily remain? Do you imagine that the propaganda weapons on one side, suffocation and strangulation on another, the prisons and tortures and killings on another — could possibly have allowed a group to remain? An intellectual group like the Shia, which was so contending with the powers of its own time and such a cause of trouble for them — how did it come to remain? It remained on account of Wilayat. A strange interconnection and shared-frontedness had been created among the Shia of the time of the nominal caliphates by the Imams (peace be upon them), so that in the light of this Wilayat this Shi'i current could remain protected amid the various other currents.

Imagine a vast river, into which water enters from several directions. The waters are moving with swift currents. The surface of the river is uneven; whirlpools form; the waters tumble over one another; various kinds of opposing currents are sinking into one another in this river, in this bed; they neutralize one another, strike against one another's heads — this water is moving forward. Amid these currents, in the midst of these turbid, dusty, muddy waters, a current of sweet, clean, pure, clear water is continuing along its way; and in this riverbed of flood and torrent, this remarkable channel moves forward. This thin current — it happens, in some seas and rivers, perhaps some have seen it: two colors of water, two kinds of water moving side by side, owing to natural factors that exist, which I do not wish to explain. In the midst of this remarkable channel where swift currents are moving, one thin current of water is going forward amid these swift currents, and it remains sound and goes on. The astonishing thing is this: it never mixes in, its color is never ruined, it never takes on the taste of the other salty and bitter waters, but continues and preserves the same sweet taste, the same clear color, the same purity and limpidity and undisturbedness, and moves forward.

Liken the world of Islam in the time of the Umayyad and Abbasid caliphs to this very river, in which various kinds of intellectual, political, and practical currents were running and moving against one another. When you look from beginning to end, you see the current of Shi'ism. You see that it is a thin stream of water. Amid this remarkable tempest it appears insignificant and small and comes to the eye [as such], but it has held itself together, has never become turbid, its taste has never been ruined, it has never lost its purity, has never taken on the color and taste and smell of the other waters; it has remained and gone on. What has held this together? What has been able to become the factor of the survival of this Shi'i current? The existence of that walī who counsels Wilayat among the people and among his own followers, who binds them to one another, makes them affectionate toward one another, promotes Wilayat — and this too is in the very same sense as the Wilayat of the Prophet's time.
