The Shi'i Wilayat, that Wilayat upon which so much emphasis has been placed — this is one of its dimensions; it has other dimensions too, which I will also state. What I have said is not yet the whole matter; this is one dimension and one side of the matter. Wilayat means interconnection.

The Qur'an considers the believers to be the allies (awliyāʾ) of one another. Those who possess true faith are the shared-fronted and bonded ones of one another — and they call this "Shia." In the narrations, the Shia is referred to as "believer" (muʾmin). If you look in the words of the earliest jurists,[^s23-13] and in many of the narrations, in some Islamic offices they mention certain conditions — among them they say "Islam," and after Islam, "faith (īmān)." One says: Sir, what does faith mean? What is meant by "faith" is possessing the particular mode of thought of the Shia — that is, seeing Islam from the perspective from which the Shia sees it, and establishing it by the logic with which the Shia establishes it: this is the meaning of "faith." We see that in the time of the Imams (peace be upon them) they made the Shia cohesive, interconnected, brotherly, and bonded with one another in this way, so that they could preserve the Shi'i current in history; otherwise the Shia would have perished a thousand times over; a thousand times over its ideas would have been digested — just as some other sects became this way, lost their color, perished, and were annihilated.

In any case, this is one of the dimensions of Wilayat, and tomorrow, God willing, I will present another of its dimensions, which is perhaps from one aspect of even greater importance — and because, after this dimension, it must certainly be expounded, I will mention it, and that is the Wilayat of the friend of God (walī Allāh). The Wilayat of the Shia with one another has become clear; what does the Wilayat of the friend of God mean? What does the Wilayat of Ali ibn Abi Talib mean? What does the Wilayat of Imam al-Sadiq mean? This [matter] that you and I must today have the Wilayat of the Imams — what does it mean? For a number of people imagine that the Wilayat of the Imams means only that we love the Imams; and how greatly they err; how greatly they err! It is not merely loving; otherwise — is there anyone to be found in the world of Islam who does not love the Imams, the infallible ones of the Prophet's household? So does everyone have Wilayat? Is there anyone who is an enemy of these [Imams]? Were even those who fought against them in early Islam all their enemies? Many of them loved these [Imams]; but for the sake of the world they were prepared to fight against them. Many of them knew what stations and ranks these [Imams] possessed. When they brought the news of the passing of Imam al-Sadiq to Mansur,[^s23-14] Mansur began to weep. Was he pretending? In front of whom? Did he want to pretend in front of his own servants? In front of Rabi the chamberlain,[^s23-15] did he want to pretend? It was not pretense; his heart truly ached, he truly grieved that Imam al-Sadiq should die. But who killed him? He himself! He himself had given the order that Imam al-Sadiq be poisoned; yet when the news arrived that the deed was done, Mansur's heart was shaken. So did Mansur too have Wilayat?!

Of this same kind is the error of those who say that Ma'mun the Abbasid[^s23-16] was a Shia. What does "Shia" mean? Does "Shia" mean someone who knows that the truth is with Imam al-Rida? Only this? If this is so, then Ma'mun the Abbasid, and likewise Harun al-Rashid, Mansur, Mu'awiya, Yazid, were more Shi'i than everyone. Did the rivals of the Commander of the Faithful at the Saqifa not know that the truth was with Ali? They all knew — so were they Shia? Did those who clashed with the Commander of the Faithful not have love for him? Yes, often they had love — so were they Shia? So did they have Wilayat? No, sir, Wilayat is other than these things. Wilayat is higher than these. If we have understood what the Wilayat of Ali ibn Abi Talib and the Wilayat of the Imams is, then we have the right to turn back upon ourselves and see whether we have Wilayat or not. Then, if we see that we do not, we should ask God for it and strive to attain the Wilayat of the Imams.

A number of people are pleased to fool themselves. Merely because they have love for the Imams, they imagine that they truly possess the Wilayat of the Imams; merely because they have belief in the Imams, they imagine that Wilayat means just this. This is not Wilayat; Wilayat is higher than this — which, of course, I will explain tomorrow: what Wilayat with the rightly-guiding Imams (peace be upon them) means; how it is that we can consider the Imams (peace be upon them) our walī and possess their Wilayat. Then we will understand how many of the claims to Wilayat are made unknowingly and contrary to reality.

Always, in the days of the Eid of Ghadir, it is customary; people recite this: «Praise be to God, who has made us among those who cling to the Wilayat of Ali ibn Abi Talib (upon him be peace).» I most often say it this way; I say: do not say "Praise be to God, who has made us..." — I fear it may be a lie — say: "O God, make us among those who cling to the Wilayat of Ali ibn Abi Talib (upon him be peace)." O God, place us among those who hold fast to Wilayat. Let us see whether we are or not. Then we will arrive at this matter. That too is another of the dimensions of Wilayat.

What I have said today is summarized in these words: the Wilayat of the Muslim Ummah, and the Wilayat of that front which strives in the way of God and for God, means this — and it consists in this: that among the individuals of this front there should come into being more and more connection and interconnection. That their hearts should be knotted together and drawn close more and more, and that they should separate, more and more, from the opposing poles, from those who think against them and act against them. This is the meaning of Wilayat.

I think the Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah could, in this sense, be named the Sūra of "Wilayat." The verses of the Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah make this meaning well clear. Now pay attention so that I may interpret for you these very verses that I recited.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah (60:1):** "In the name of God, the Most Merciful — O you who have believed" — O you who have brought faith — "do not take My enemy and your enemy as allies."

Do not take My enemy and your own enemy as allies (awliyāʾ), as guardians. Here, of course, in the [usual Persian] translation it is not rendered badly: "You should not take the unbelievers, who are the enemy of Me and of you, as your supporters." It is not bad; this expression accords with that meaning which is in our minds. Some translate it as: "Do not take My enemy and your enemy as your friend." This is not a complete meaning. It is not merely a matter of friendship and affection; it is higher than these. "Do not take them as your walī" means: do not consider them your shared-front; do not place yourselves in their ranks; do not, in your heart, suppose yourselves and them to be in a single rank; the one who is the enemy of God and your enemy — do not count him as beside you; rather, see him as facing you, as an enemy and opponent against you.

> "extending to them affection."

Do not consider them your shared-front and shared-rank, such that you would send them a message of friendship.

> "while they have disbelieved in what has come to you of the truth."

While you know that these have disbelieved in what of truth and reality the Lord has sent down to you.

> "having driven out the Messenger and yourselves."

They drive the Messenger and you out of your city and homeland.

> "because you believe in God, your Lord."

Because you bring faith in your Lord, in God, who is your Lord. Do not take My enemy and your own enemy as your shared-front, helper, and supporter.

> "If you have gone out for striving in My cause and seeking My pleasure."

If you have gone forth in the path of jihad and striving and effort for Me, and to attain My pleasure — if you truly speak the truth, if you truly strive and struggle in My path, you have no right to make the one who is My enemy and your enemy your shared-front, helper, and bonded ally.

Of course, this Sūra is a very fascinating and important Sūra. On another page of these very verses that we are reciting, God makes clear which unbelievers He means. He does not say: do not lay down a plan of friendship with all unbelievers; He does not say: cut off, absolutely, your relations with the unbelievers — no; afterward He says with which group of the unbelievers. He divides the groups of unbelievers — and if we get to it, it is possible I will also interpret that verse for you tomorrow.

> "confiding to them affection."

In secret and concealment, you deliver your love and affection to them.

> "while I am most knowing of what you have concealed and what you have declared."

I am more knowing of what you conceal, what you have hidden, and what you have made manifest.

> "And whoever of you does it" — whoever of you does this thing, lays down a plan of friendship and support with the enemies of God, and considers himself and shows himself to be their shared-front — whoever does this thing — "has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way."

He has lost the middle path. He has strayed from the middle path.

After that, in order that the Muslims may also be convinced intellectually as to why they must sever relations with the unbelievers, He identifies these [unbelievers]. Of course, it is not amiss for me to give this reminder: the occasion of revelation of these verses is concerning Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a. Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a was a Muslim of slightly weak faith. When the noble Prophet wanted to go to war with the unbelievers of Quraysh, this man thought that it was possible the Prophet might be defeated in this war and his relatives, who were among the unbelievers, might come to harm — and he was one of the Prophet's soldiers. Here he wanted to make use of cleverness; he resorted to a sly, cunning trick.[^s23-18] [He thought:] "Now that we are beside the Prophet, in the company of the Prophet — and we are even performing jihad, and even gaining the reward of those who strive in the way of God — let us, as a precaution, also write a letter to the unbelievers; let us here also declare our friendship and loyalty toward them. What harm is there? If we face them on the battlefield, of course we will not help them. But for now, what objection is there to my writing a letter and — as he imagined — duping the unbelievers and laying down a plan of friendship with them as well? What harm is there? Let us have both God — in the common saying of children — and the dates." A master and a village headman were quarreling; one said, "Which of them speaks the truth?" He replied, "Both the master speaks the truth and the village headman!" Let us have both sides.

He took up a letter and wrote to the chiefs of Quraysh, and signed his name at the bottom of it. He also introduced himself well, so that they would know that this man was good and friendly and kind toward them. He gave the letter to a woman, and this woman placed it among her hair, or amid her clothing, and set off toward Mecca.

The dear and noble Prophet, by the revelation of God, became aware of the affair. He sent the Commander of the Faithful, and one or two others; along the way they found that woman, threatened her, and took the letter from her. Afterward, when [Hatib] came, the Prophet said: "Well, why did you do this thing, man? Why do you reveal military and war secrets to the enemy?" He said: "O Messenger of God, I have friends there, I have relatives; I fear they may come to trouble. I wanted to write this letter so that perhaps their hearts might soften a little toward me." The verse, in reply, says: Do not be mistaken; their hearts will not soften toward you. Those who are against you intellectually, those to whom your religion, your faith, is a detriment, and who have set their resolve upon the destruction of your religion and faith — these will never be kind and friendly toward you. This next verse states this matter, saying:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah (60:2):** "If they gain dominance over you" — if they get the upper hand over you — "they will be enemies to you."

They will be your enemies. O foolish Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a! Do not imagine that if you now render them some help here, tomorrow they will keep [it] in return for your help — no, by God. If you help them, they will gain greater mastery over you. If you help them, they will open further the hand of oppression and tyranny upon you.

> "they will be enemies to you, and will extend against you their hands and their tongues with evil."

They open their hands and their tongues against you with evil. They open their hands; they place you under greater pressure. They open their tongues against you with evil; they degrade you, they strip you of dignity and honor, they do not recognize you as a worthy human being. Now you are helping them — do not imagine that these helps will be of any use to you.

> "and they wish that you would disbelieve."

Tomorrow, if they gain mastery over you, they will not even let you keep this little bit of heartfelt belief; they would love for you to turn unbeliever. Do not imagine that they would leave you free and at ease to remain Muslim and act upon your Islamic duties.

Then a verse — a decisive sentence — concerning the kinfolk and relatives of Mr. Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a, and the kinfolk and relatives of all the Hatib ibn Abi Balta'as of history. For the sake of your children, for the sake of your kin and relatives, for the sake of your dear sons, for the sake of the comfort of your loved ones — are you prepared to make peace with the enemy of God? To draw the friendship of God's weak servants, and to draw the profit of yourselves and your loved ones, are you prepared to disregard the command of God, to befriend the enemy of God? Just how useful are these kin and offspring to a person? This youth — for the sake of whose job, for the sake of whose scholarship, for the sake of whose livelihood you are prepared to make peace with the unbelievers of Quraysh in the midst of the unbelievers of Quraysh — how useful will he be to you, you poor wretch? How much will he save you from the chastisement of God? O you foolish one!

Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a goes and conspires with the unbelievers, with the enemies of the Prophet, for the sake that his relatives, his kin, his offspring there should not come to harm! Just how useful are these kin and offspring to a person, that a person should, for their sake, draw upon himself the chastisement of the Lord and the divine wrath?[^s23-19]

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah (60:3):** "Never will your relatives or your children benefit you" — your kin and relatives and your children will not profit you — "on the Day of Resurrection He will judge between you."

On the Day of Resurrection He casts separation between you and them. Or let us read and interpret it this way: "Never will your relatives or your children benefit you" — on the Day of Resurrection your kin and offspring will not profit you — "He will judge between you" — God separates between you,

> **Qur'an, Sūrat ʿAbasa (80:34-37):** "On the Day a man will flee from his brother, and his mother and his father, and his wife and his children."[^s23-20]

On that Day of Resurrection. The day a person flees from his own brother, flees from his own father and mother, from his kind wife and his children and the light of his own dear eyes. This very child, whose grief you so consume yourself with today — know that on the Day of Resurrection you will flee from him and he from you, and both of you from all the other creatures; everyone flees from one another, they have no time, no opportunity to reach one another, to inquire after one another's state.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat ʿAbasa (80:37):** "For every man, that Day, will be a matter occupying him."

Everyone, on that Day, has some affair, some preoccupation, some trouble — a trouble that is enough to occupy him entirely. On the Day of Resurrection a person is so caught up in himself that he does not get to the trouble of others, even his own child. A thorn pricks the hand of your child — are you prepared to sacrifice your this-world and your hereafter for him? Poor wretch! On the Day of Resurrection you will flee from him. If we understand the logic of the Qur'an in this regard — if those who, for the sake of the comfort and ease of their children, are prepared to turn their backs on the felicity of this world and the hereafter and are prepared to turn toward misfortunes and wretchedness and dark days — if they understood this logic of the Qur'an, perhaps they would be shaken. This child of a person, this daughter of a person, this son of a person, this dear one who draws you toward Hell — and you who are not prepared to let the slightest harm come to yourself yet for his sake endure the greatest harms — how much is he with you? How much is he for you? How much benefit does he give you? Where, on the Day of Resurrection, is he ready to place upon his own shoulders any of your heavy burdens — where?

> "Never will your relatives or your children benefit you. On the Day of Resurrection He will judge between you. And God, of what you do, is Seeing."

God is knowing of what you do.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah (60:4):** "There has already been for you an excellent pattern in Abraham and those with him."

These verses are the climactic part of these verses that I recited. He says to the believers: Believers, in the conduct of Abraham and the followers of Abraham there is a goodly example for you. See what Abraham and his followers, his companions, did; you do that very thing too. What did they do? They turned, plainly and frankly, to the misguided people of their own time, to the servants and worshippers of the Taghut and the false deities of the age, and said: We are quit of you and your lords; we have disbelieved in you; we have turned away from you; between us and you, for all of time, are hatred and enmity, hostility and wrath and rancor. There is only one way to reconciliation, and that is — "until you believe in God alone" — come, enter the logic of our thought, become believers. He says frankly: Believers, you too act as Abraham did.

> "There has already been for you an excellent pattern" — there is for you a goodly example — "in Abraham and those who were with him."

And those who were with him, his companions.

> "when they said to their people."

That time when they said to their own people.

> "Indeed, we are disassociated from you" — we are those quit of and free from you — "and from whatever you worship other than God."

And we are also quit of all that you worship and serve besides God.

> "We have disbelieved in you" — we disbelieve in you — "and there has appeared between us and you enmity and hatred forever."

Between us and you, enmity and hatred and rancor have become manifest and apparent forever.

> "until you believe in God alone."

Until you bring faith in the One God.

The Qur'an says: "There has already been for you" believers "in Abraham and those with him an excellent pattern" — for you believers, in Abraham and his companions there is a goodly example —

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah (60:6):** "for anyone who hopes in God and the Last Day."

For those who have hope in God and the Day of Resurrection.

> "And whoever turns away — then indeed, God is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy."

Whoever turns his back and turns away from this command and shuns it — God the Exalted is Free of need, praised and well-pleasing, and upon the skirt of His majesty no dust may settle.[^s23-21] If you make peace with His enemies, the skirt of your humanity and honor will be stained, and God suffers no loss. Keep this saying of Abraham in mind: Abraham and his companions said to the unbelievers and deviants of their own time:

> "Indeed, we are disassociated from you."

We are quit of you.

The deviant people of their own time spoke in this same way. Imam al-Sajjad and his companions spoke this way with the deviant people of their time. In the Bihar al-Anwar there is a hadith which says: Yahya ibn Umm Tawil,[^s23-22] the disciple of the Fourth Imam, used to come to the mosque of Medina and stand facing the people. The very people among whom Imam al-Husayn and Imam al-Hasan had lived for twenty years; the very people who were neither Umayyad nor affiliated with the Banu Umayya — so what were they? They were cowards who, for the sake of Ashura and Karbala, out of fear of the suffocation that the Banu Umayya had brought about, had left the side of the family of Muhammad empty; yet they were people of [some] belief.

Yahya ibn Umm Tawil would stand before these very people and repeat this very Qur'anic word: «He would say: We have disbelieved in you, and there has appeared between us and you enmity and hatred.»[^s23-23] We have disbelieved in you, and between us and you wrath and rancor have become manifest — that is, the very saying that Abraham would say to the unbelievers of his own time and to the polytheists and deviants and misguided ones of his own age. See that Wilayat is the very same Wilayat. Abraham too possesses Wilayat; the Shia of Imam al-Sajjad too, in their own time, possess Wilayat. They must have one another and be separate from the enemies. If a Shia of the Shia of Imam al-Sajjad, in the time of Imam al-Sajjad, out of fear or greed, went after the enemy's front, he is outside the Wilayat of Imam al-Sajjad and is not connected to the front of Imam al-Sajjad. Therefore the close student of Imam al-Sajjad says to them: «We have disbelieved in you, and there has appeared between us and you enmity and hatred»; and Yahya ibn Umm Tawil was among the very good and choice companions of Imam al-Sajjad.

The end of the affair of this choice Muslim was this: Hajjaj ibn Yusuf seized him, cut off his right hand, cut off his left hand, cut off his right foot, cut off his left foot; yet he still began to speak with his tongue, so they cut out his tongue too, until he departed this world. And this was while he had set the Shia in order and had provided and made firm the essential pillars of the edifice of Shi'ism after Imam al-Sajjad.

## Footnotes

[^s23-1]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mumtaḥanah (60:1).

[^s23-2]: Finally; in the end.

[^s23-3]: Hadith no. 33. The Arabic, in part: «If a man were to stand in prayer through his nights, and fast through his days, and give all his wealth in charity, and perform Hajj every year of his life, yet did not recognize the Wilayat of the friend of God so as to take him as guardian and have all his deeds be by his guidance...»

[^s23-4]: Hadith no. 6: «I was sent to bring the noble traits of character to completion» (buʿithtu li-utammima makārim al-akhlāq).

[^s23-5]: For more information about the discussions related to Islamic government, the conditions and characteristics of Islamic rulers, and government during the era of Occultation, you may refer to the book *Wilayat and Government* (Vilāyat va Hukūmat).

[^s23-6]: Usama ibn Zayd, son of Zayd ibn Haritha, the freed slave of the Prophet, and of Umm Ayman, the maidservant of Lady Amina, the mother of the Messenger of God. After returning from the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Prophet, while Usama was still young, made him commander of the army of the Muslims for the battle against the Romans. In the time of the Commander of the Faithful, he first pledged allegiance to the Imam, but he was not present in the wars of the Commander of the Faithful. On account of his remorse for this failing in the latter part of his life, Imam al-Baqir, in a hadith, recommended that nothing but good be said about him.

[^s23-7]: Hadith no. 7: «Set out in the name of God» (Inṭaliqū ʿalā ismi-llāh).

[^s23-8]: Flawed; tainted (lit. having "adulteration" / impurity).

[^s23-9]: Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:125): "Call to the way of your Lord with wisdom and goodly admonition, and dispute with them in the manner that is best."

[^s23-10]: Hadith no. 22: «no blame of any blamer affects them» (lā taʾkhudhuhum fī-llāhi lawmatu lāʾim).

[^s23-11]: A passage and crossing-place (the mountain defile / ford).

[^s23-12]: Hadith no. 43: «And the Wilayat of Ali ibn Abi Talib is my fortress» (wa wilāyatu ʿAliyyi-bni Abī Ṭālib ḥiṣnī).

[^s23-13]: (q-d-m) earlier, former; the predecessors — i.e., the earliest/foremost jurists.

[^s23-14]: 'Abdullah ibn Ahmad, surnamed al-Mansur; because he was very miserly and calculating, he became known as Mansur al-Dawaniqi. After the death of his brother al-Saffah in 136 AH he came to the caliphate and ruled until the year 158. In addition to martyring Imam al-Sadiq, he suppressed the uprisings of Muhammad al-Nafs al-Zakiyya and his brother Ibrahim. He had Abu Muslim al-Khurasani killed and consolidated the rule of the Banu Abbas over all the Islamic regions.

[^s23-15]: Rabi, the special servant of Mansur al-Dawaniqi and, in the expression of that day, his chamberlain (ḥājib). From the year 153 AH until the end of Mansur's life he served him in this post.

[^s23-16]: 'Abdullah ibn Harun, surnamed al-Ma'mun; by the killing of his brother al-Amin, he came to rule in the year 198 AH. He is reckoned among the most learned of the Abbasid caliphs. In order to control the Alids, he designated Imam al-Rida as his heir apparent and summoned the Imam from Medina to Khurasan. He sought, by defeating the Imam in scholarly debates, to lower his standing among the people; yet each time the rightfulness and superior knowledge of the Imam became ever more evident. Ma'mun ruled until the year 218 AH.

[^s23-18]: Slyness, cunning, cleverness (ʿrindī"/cunning trickery).

[^s23-19]: (s-kh-ṭ) a severe wrath that brings punishment and chastisement in its train.

[^s23-20]: Qur'an, Sūrat ʿAbasa (80:34-37).

[^s23-21]: "Though all created beings should turn unbeliever, / upon the skirt of Your majesty no dust would settle." (Baba Afdal Kashani.)

[^s23-22]: Yahya was the son of the foster-nurse of Lady Sajjad [Imam al-Sajjad]; through the blessing of his close connection with the Imam and his benefiting from the Imam's teachings, he was steadfast in defending the rightfulness of the Imams and in struggling against the dominance of the culture of the Taghut.

[^s23-23]: Hadith no. 40: «He would say: We have disbelieved in you, and there has appeared between us and you enmity and hatred» (yaqūlu: kafarnā bikum wa badā baynanā wa baynakumu l-ʿadāwatu wa l-baghḍāʾ).


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 24: The Bonds of the Islamic Ummah

*پیوندهای امت اسلامی — delivered Saturday, 20 Mehr 1353 / 25 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mā'idah (5:51):** "O you who have believed, do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies (awliyāʾ). Some of them are allies of one another. And whoever is an ally to them among you — then indeed, he is one of them. Indeed, God does not guide the wrongdoing people."

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mā'idah (5:52):** "So you see those in whose hearts is disease hastening into [allegiance with] them, saying, 'We fear lest a turn of fortune strike us.' But perhaps God will bring [you] victory, or a decision from Him, and they will become, over what they had concealed within themselves, regretful."

*(First, before we begin our discussion, I want to mention to our friends and brothers a tradition from the Messenger of God, may God bless him and his family and grant them peace. In that tradition the Messenger of God is reported to have said: «raḥima-llāhu imra'an ʿamila ʿamalan fa-atqanahu»[^s24-1] — that is, may God have mercy on a person who, whatever he does, does it firmly and soundly. Let him do his work solidly; meaning, let him also take account of the possibilities in his work. The possibility that the electricity may go out, the possibility that the microphone may fail, the possibility of dozens of other such incidents. Today's incident became a lesson for us. How good it is for us to think in advance about the things that might happen.*

*Our master, the Commander of the Faithful — God's blessings and peace be upon him — expresses this very point in a sermon of the Nahj al-Balāgha. He says: I am not like that stunned, dazed animal that sleeps in its lair until the hunter comes and seizes it; rather, from the events — from what has befallen me and occurred to me — I make use of them for understanding subsequent matters; I gather experience. And this is a lesson for us, that in all the affairs of life we should gather experiences from what has passed for what remains, and take account of the possibilities and the events that may occur.)*

Permit me to speak in summary, although the discussion requires elaboration — and of course I too will elaborate the discussion to the extent that I deem necessary for it to continue; I will continue it for another day or two days, until the matter becomes clear. In brief, what needs to be presented in the continuation of the discussion of Wilayat is this: that an Islamic society and a community formed as the Islamic Ummah — meaning a community administered by divine regulations, by a divine mode of thought, and by legislation and the execution of law through divine power — this community, which we name the Islamic Ummah; if this Islamic Ummah wishes to possess Wilayat in that Qur'anic sense which I spoke of yesterday — little by little we arrive at that meaning of Wilayat held in the minds of the elite. That Wilayat which is in the minds of the common people, we are not concerned with now; that Wilayat which is in the minds of the elite — that is, the scholars and the learned and the men of knowledge who are versed in Islamic and Shiʿi sciences — we will come to that now.

But for now the discussion is about Qur'anic Wilayat. If they wish to attain that Qur'anic Wilayat — that Wilayat which is raised in the Qur'an — if the Islamic Ummah wishes to secure it for itself, it must observe two directions. One direction is the direction of internal relations, within the Islamic society; one direction is the direction of external relations — that is, the relation of the Islamic world and the Islamic Ummah and the Islamic society with other communities.

In the sphere of internal relations, the Islamic Ummah possesses Wilayat in the Qur'anic sense only when it secures within itself the utmost solidarity, connection, relationship, and unity of ranks, and the greatest possible cohesion of its various individuals and factions. Let there be no kind of division or disagreement throughout the great Islamic Ummah. Let no separate ranks be formed within this Ummah.

If two units within the Islamic Ummah rise up against one another in war — for instance, suppose a group in the east of the Islamic lands and a group in the northeast of the Islamic lands set out to fight and do battle with one another — the rule is, the Qur'anic command is, that the rest of the Muslims should strive as much as they can to reconcile those two hostile and mutually hostile parties.[^s24-2] If they see that of these two adversaries one is willing to reconcile, but the other is not willing to reconcile, or the word of that other is a word of coercion while the word of this one is a word of truth — and that man who supports coercion is not willing to submit to the word of truth and to incline toward the just word — in such a situation, the whole Islamic world must become as one, must join hands, must go after that aggressor and silence him. They must contend with him until they set him back in his place.

It is a verse of the Qur'an:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḥujurāt (49:9):** "And if two factions among the believers fight, then make reconciliation between the two. But if one of them oppresses the other, then fight against the one that oppresses until it returns to the command of God."

"And if two factions among the believers fight" — if two parties, two groups among the Muslim groups, fight one another; "then make reconciliation between the two" — reconcile between these two Muslim groups. "But if one of them oppresses the other" — if among these two groups one wrongs the other, commits transgression and aggression and excess, speaks the language of coercion and seeks to play the bully; "then fight against the one that oppresses" — against the one that speaks coercion, fight; "until it returns to the command of God" — until it is forced to return to God's command, until it is compelled to accept God's command. This is the command of God in the sphere of preserving unity within the Islamic society.

And as for the sphere of external relations: in the sphere of external relations, the Islamic world must strive — the Islamic Ummah must strive — to so order its relations with the non-Muslim world and with those outside this Ummah that it does not fall in the slightest under their command, does not come in the slightest under the influence of their thinking. That its independent politics, even slightly, do not lose their independence under the influence of their politics. Sharing a front and joining together with them is altogether forbidden — that is, when this joining together causes the Muslim Ummah to come under their influence.

There is a famous account here, whose details I will not now relate. It is a narration mentioned in most of the reliable books of the Shiʿa. It pertains to the time of Imam al-Bāqir — God's blessings be upon him — when the coinage of the Islamic world was being brought from the land of Rome (Byzantium), and the Byzantine ruler made a threat, and they were left helpless and at a loss. Then the Imam, peace be upon him, guided the apparatus of the caliphate. It is very strange! This is the only point — one or two exceptional cases — in which I see that the Imams of guidance, peace be upon them, showed a slight favorable face to the apparatus of the caliphate. One of them is here. The Imam guided the apparatus of the caliphate and instructed them that you should mint the coin in this way. They did not know how to mint coinage; they did not know the measure of the silver, where to obtain it and how to provide it — the measure of a dirham and a half-dirham and those things upon which the coin had to be struck. The Imam guided them.

Therefore, from the standpoint of external relations, the slightest receptivity to influence from anti-Islamic or non-Islamic factions — and especially the anti-Islamic — is forbidden. The Islamic society and the Islamic Ummah have no right to establish their bond with the world outside Islam except in the form of being the upper hand, being superior. That is, if a relationship is to be established between the Islamic Ummah and a non-Muslim Ummah, an exploitative relationship — suppose, for example, of the kind of the Tobacco affair and the Régie company,[^s24-3] which you gentlemen know and have heard about — the Islamic world has no right to enter into this relationship. Or suppose, like those Mughal sultans and rulers of India who permitted foreign governments to come and establish companies there — they performed an act contrary to the Wilayat of the Islamic world. It was not permissible, this act of letting a company come; because they knew, or if they did not know, they should have known — they must know — that the company — for example, the East India Company[^s24-4] — when it entered that region, when it entered that land, what calamity it would bring upon the lives of those people, and how it would spread the cancer of colonialism into the very veins and sinews of that vast subcontinent. They should have understood these things; they did not understand. And the Islamic world and the Islamic Ummah never permit relations of this kind.

Take note. When we say that relations are cut off with non-Muslim governments and with non-Muslim nations, this does not mean that the Islamic world and the Islamic Ummah will live in political isolation. No; the issue is not the issue of political isolation, such that you might imagine that the Islamic world has no commercial relation with anyone, no political relation, no diplomatic relations, neither sends an ambassador nor receives an ambassador — no, it is not like that; it has ordinary relations. It has no Wilayat with them, no attachment with them. It has no essential and substantial bond[^s24-5] with them. It will not be such that, if they wish, they can place the Islamic world under their own influence.

So Wilayat has two facets — Qur'anic Wilayat. One facet is that within the Islamic society all elements must step toward one goal, toward one direction, by one path, with one footstep. Outside the Islamic society, the Islamic Ummah must sever its bonds with all the blocs and the anti-Islamic factions.

Here there exists a precise point which shows that Wilayat in this sense, which has come in the Qur'an, is in the very same sense of the Wilayat that we, the Shiʿa, hold. Pay attention so that I may express this point, so that you all may understand how Wilayat in Shiʿism connects with Qur'anic Wilayat. It is possible that four persons may be present who, out of devotional acceptance, or [as] the past generation may have been present and, out of devotional acceptance, heard certain things about Wilayat and accepted them — but this young person who wishes to understand and is connected with the Qur'an must pay attention to where Shiʿi Wilayat comes from.

This [fact] that we have considered the connection with the Imam to be so important, this [fact] that we have considered the command of the Imam to be in force in all the affairs of the life of the society — what is this for? From where does it come? Here the Qur'an speaks to us — that point is the very thing I am saying — if a society and an Ummah wishes to possess Qur'anic Wilayat in this sense — that is, wishes for all its internal forces to set out in one direction, toward one goal, along one line, and wishes all its internal forces to be mobilized against the anti-Islamic powers abroad — it needs a point of concentrated power at the heart of the Islamic society. It needs a point to which all the internal forces are joined, from which all draw inspiration, and to whom all listen and obey, and who knows all aspects of the interests and harms, so that — like a strong, powerful-handed, keen-eyed watchman — he may assign each person on the battlefront to his own particular task. There must exist a leadership, a command, a concentrated power in the Islamic society, which knows what can be expected of you, what can be expected of me, what can be expected of other human beings, so that to each it may assign the task that is required of him, saying: act.

For example, by way of analogy — if I want to make an analogy: have you seen these carpet-weaving workshops? A number of people are seated weaving a carpet; each is working — every child or grown person seated there is weaving thread so that by dusk he may receive two or three tomans. Each is expending some enthusiasm and labor here. If these works are not coordinated, if there is no higher thought and eye and power that continually reads out that pattern of the carpet according to those particular instructions, so that they may know which thread to use, and how to strike it, and how to cut it — if such a concentrated power does not exist, how will this carpet come out?! You will see that its right side tells of the East, and its left side tells of the West! One part speaks of a Kurdish carpet, another part speaks of a Turkmen carpet! The patterns disordered, it comes out something altogether absurd.

The orderliness of the carpet — this [feature] that you see, that a flower has appeared on this side, [and] like that flower, without any addition or subtraction, on this side too one has grown, has appeared, and both have reached that medallion (toranj) in the center, and just like these two, on that side of the carpet also there are two more, and all things are orderly and everything is in its place — what is this for? It is because, first, the instruction is determined, and one person too is seated, calling out in a loud voice — I don't know — "Bring two of those down from above, strike that thread from that side, cut that one from that side." Which I have never heard and do not know how to do; the carpet-sellers know it. The carpet-sellers too do not know it; the carpet-weavers know it. Did you notice?

In a society, if all the forces are to be put to work, and all to work in one direction, and none of the forces to go to waste, and all the forces of the society to be brought to bear, in the form of one concentrated power, upon the collective interests of humanity, and the society to be able to be like a single fist in the face of the opposing factions and ranks and powers — if it wishes to have these, it needs a concentrated power. It needs a heart, this society and this general body of the Islamic Ummah needs a heart. Of course, it also has conditions. He must be very aware, must know very much, must be very resolute, his eye must possess a different kind of vision, he must fear nothing on the path of God, he must, when necessary, sacrifice even himself. What do we name such a being? The Imam.

The Imam means that ruler and leader who is appointed in the society on the part of the Lord. This that I say, "on the part of God," what does it mean? It means either that God appoints [him] by name and designation — as He appointed the Commander of the Faithful, Imam Ḥasan, Imam Ḥusayn, and the rest of the Imams; the Prophet himself too is an Imam; the Prophet himself too is an Imam:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Baqarah (2:124):** "Indeed, I will make you an Imam for the people."

God the Exalted says to Abraham: I have made you an Imam. The Imam means that leader and ruler and guide in a society.

At one time God appoints this Imam by name and designation, saying: after the Prophet it must be ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, peace be upon him, for example. At another time God the Exalted does not appoint the Imam by name; He appoints [him] by a mark (sign). Like what? Like the statement of the Imam, peace be upon him:[^s24-6] «fa-ammā man kāna mina al-fuqahāʾi ṣāʾinan li-nafsihi ḥāfiẓan li-dīnihi mukhālifan ʿalā hawāhu muṭīʿan li-amri mawlāhu fa-li-l-ʿawāmmi an yuqallidūhu» — the Imam has appointed [him], you see; this too is a designated Imam (imām manṣūṣ),[^s24-7] he is himself an Imam, he is an Imam. That jurist (faqīh) who is the successor [of the Imam] — but he is an Imam who has not been appointed by name; he has been appointed by a mark. Whoever this mark applies to, he becomes the Imam. I wished to explain for you the word "Imam." The Imam means leader, means ruler, means the one who holds the reins, means the one whom — wherever he goes — human beings follow; who must be on the part of God, must be just, must be fair, must be religious, must be resolute, and the like of these statements which exist concerning the Imamate, which we are not now in a position [to discuss].

So the principle of Qur'anic Wilayat necessitated — what thing? The existence of the Imam. The discussion is not yet finished; pay attention. If this great body, whose name is the Islamic Ummah, wishes to remain alive, to remain successful, to be ever enduring, what must it do? Its connection with this center, with this moving and impassioned heart, must always — all of it, ever — be firm, powerful, established. So what does Wilayat mean? Let all pay close attention: the other dimension of Wilayat — what does it mean? It means the firm and powerful connection of each of the individuals of the Islamic Ummah, in every state, with that heart of the Ummah. Connection of what? Intellectual connection and practical connection. That is, taking one's pattern truly from him, truly following him in thoughts and visions, and truly following him in deeds and conduct and activities and movements.

So what does the Wilayat of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib mean? It means that in your thoughts you be a follower of ʿAlī; in your deeds you be a follower of ʿAlī; that a powerful, firm, unbreakable bond has tied you to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, peace be upon him, so that you do not become separated from ʿAlī. This is the meaning of Wilayat. Have you understood well what Wilayat is? It is here that we understand the meaning of this tradition: «wilāyatu ʿalī ibn abī ṭālibin ḥiṣnī»[^s24-8] — the Wilayat of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib is my fortress and stronghold — [said] on the part of God — «fa-man dakhala ḥiṣnī amina min ʿadhābī» — whoever enters this stronghold will remain safe and secure from the punishment of God. It is a very interesting statement. What does it mean? It means that the Muslims, the followers of the Qur'an — if both from the standpoint of thought and reflection and from the standpoint of deed and effort and activity they are connected to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib — they are safe and protected from God's punishment. Is it other than this? Is it anything but this? If ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib is recognized today, [and] after he is recognized, you and I act like him, then we attain Wilayat; Wilayat means this.

A servant [of God] who deems the Qur'an incapable of being understood — of course I [do not say this of myself]; God forbid that I should deem the Qur'an incapable of being understood; [I mean] the one who does not [understand it] — that servant of God who deems the Qur'an incapable of being understood, how can he say "I have the Wilayat of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib and I am connected with ʿAlī from the standpoint of thought," while ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib says in a sermon of the Nahj al-Balāgha: «wa-aʿlamū anna hādhā al-qurʾāna huwa al-nāṣiḥu alladhī lā yaghushshu wa-l-hādī alladhī lā yuḍillu wa-l-muḥaddithu alladhī lā yakdhibu wa-mā jālasa hādhā al-qurʾāna aḥadun illā qāma ʿanhu bi-ziyādatin aw nuqṣānin: ziyādatin fī hudan aw nuqṣānin min ʿaman»[^s24-9] — the Commander of the Faithful is in this manner referring people to the Qur'an, in this manner driving people toward the Qur'an; that servant of God who says "the Qur'an cannot be understood," does he have the Wilayat of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib? Never. ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib is willing, for the sake of God, to give up his entire being; the conduct of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib is this. This fellow is not willing to give up a single mithqāl of his money, of his life, of his social standing, of his comfort, of his lordliness, for the sake of God — does he have the Wilayat of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib? The Wilayat of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib is possessed by the one who is joined to ʿAlī with an unbreakable bond — both from the standpoint of thought and reflection, and from the standpoint of deed.

If you reflect well, brothers, this meaning which I have given of Wilayat is the most precise and most subtle meaning that can be expounded, that can be expressed, concerning Wilayat; and this too emerges from the Qur'an. Now listen so that I may read the verses of the Qur'an for you, for in these verses of the Qur'an from Sūrat al-Mā'idah, eloquent allusions have been made to all these things: to the positive aspect of Wilayat — that is, the internal bond; to the negative aspect of Wilayat — that is, the severing of external bonds; and to that other dimension of Wilayat — that is, the connection and link with the walī (the walī meaning that pole, meaning that heart, meaning that ruler and Imam). Now pay attention so that the matter may be grasped.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mā'idah (5:51–56)**

"O you who have believed" — O you who have believed; "do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies (awliyāʾ)" — do not take the Jews and the Christians, the Jewish and Christian peoples, as your awliyāʾ. Awliyāʾ is the plural of walī; walī is from wilayat; wilayat means attachment, walī means one who is attached and bonded. Do not take the Jews and the Christians as those bonded and attached to yourselves; do not choose them. "Some of them are allies of one another (baʿḍuhum awliyāʾu baʿḍ)" — they are awliyāʾ of one another, sharers of a front and attached to one another. Do not look at the fact that their blocs are separate from each other; in meaning, for the sake of enmity against your authenticities, they are all one front. "Baʿḍuhum awliyāʾu baʿḍ" — some are of the same front as others. "And whoever is an ally to them among you (wa-man yatawallahum minkum)" — whoever takes them as his walī — tawallī means accepting Wilayat, of the form tafaʿʿul — whoever sets foot in the valley of their Wilayat and bonds himself with them, connects himself with them, establishes a relationship; "then indeed, he is one of them (fa-innahu minhum)" — without doubt he himself is one of them. "Indeed, God does not guide the wrongdoing people (inna-llāha lā yahdī al-qawma al-ẓālimīn)" — and God will not guide the wrongdoing people.

"So you see those in whose hearts is disease (fa-tarā alladhīna fī qulūbihim maraḍ)" — those in whose hearts there is disease, which we have translated "the sick of heart"; "fa-tarā alladhīna fī qulūbihim maraḍ" — those who in their heart have disease and sickness, do you see them? "Hastening into [allegiance with] them (yusāriʿūna fīhim)" — they hasten amid the front of the enemies of religion; they are not content merely to go toward them, but they hasten; they are not content merely to go up beside them — they go all the way into the depths of their front. If you ask, "Sir, why do you make so [much] friendship with the enemy of religion? With someone whom you know to be against religion, why do you not only not oppose him at all, but even expend friendship?" If you ask this of him, he answers you thus: "Saying, 'We fear lest a turn of fortune strike us (yaqūlūna nakhshā an tuṣībanā dāʾirah)'" — they say: we fear that some harm may reach us; if I do not befriend [them], I fear they will inflict some harm on me, or [someone] will inflict it. How familiar these words are to one's ear! "Nakhshā an tuṣībanā dāʾirah" — we fear that some trouble will be made for us, we fear that some occasion of distress will be made for us. See what familiar words these are — it is the very same: "nakhshā an tuṣībanā dāʾirah."

What does God say in answer to these [people]? He says: "But perhaps God will bring victory, or a decision from Him (fa-ʿasā-llāhu an yaʾtiya bi-l-fatḥi aw amrin min ʿindihi)" — it is hoped that God will grant victory to the front of the believers, or will bring about, on His own [initiative], some event in their favor. After this happens, then: "and they will become, over what they had concealed within themselves, regretful (fa-yuṣbiḥū ʿalā mā asarrū fī anfusihim nādimīn)" — so that these wretches who had aligned with them may become regretful, may be put to shame, may say: "Did you see what a blunder we made; if we had known that the front of the believers would be so victorious and powerful, we would not have aligned with the enemy of religion, with the enemy of God; we would not have disgraced ourselves."

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mā'idah (5:53):** "And those who believed will say, 'Are these the ones who swore by God their strongest oaths that indeed they were with you?' Their deeds have become worthless, and they have become losers."

"And those who believed will say (wa-yaqūlu alladhīna āmanū)" — after they [the hypocrites] have disgraced themselves and aligned with the enemies, what do the believers say? Those who believed say: "Are these (a-hāʾulāʾi alladhīna aqsamū bi-llāhi jahda aymānihim innahum la-maʿakum)" — the believers, the poor ones, sink into astonishment and bewilderment: "Eh! These were the very ones! These fair-faced, respectable countenances, who used to swear, with stern and weighty oaths,[^s24-10] that they were with us." Whenever we spoke with them, whenever we said anything to them, they would say: yes, we too are of the same belief as you, we too have no disagreement with you, we too say the very thing you say. In their manner of expression, they used to speak with one in this way; later it became clear that the hearts of these [people] had been diseased, and despite their good outward appearance, they had filthy, black, hypocritical hearts. The believers, on that day, say: "Amazing! Look what an oath these [people] used to swear. Are these really them?" "A-hāʾulāʾi alladhīna aqsamū bi-llāhi jahda aymānihim" — are these the very ones, those who used to swear by God with hard and stern oaths? "Innahum la-maʿakum" — that they were with you, of the same belief and same mind as you. "Their deeds have become worthless (ḥabiṭat aʿmāluhum)" — the works they had done became void and useless and destroyed; "and they have become losers (fa-aṣbaḥū khāsirīn)" — they became losers — the true losers.

Well, this was concerning external relations. Now, concerning internal relations, pay attention:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mā'idah (5:54):** "O you who have believed, whoever of you reverts from his religion — God will bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, humble toward the believers, mighty against the disbelievers; they strive in the way of God and do not fear the blame of any blamer. That is the favor of God; He gives it to whom He wills. And God is all-Encompassing, all-Knowing."

"O you who have believed (yā ayyuhā alladhīna āmanū)" — O you who have believed; "whoever of you reverts from his religion (man yartadda minkum ʿan dīnih)" — if you turn back from your religion, if you set down from your shoulders onto the ground this burden of the mission and responsibility which you had accepted with faith in God, and do not deliver it to its destination — do not imagine that this burden will fail to reach its destination; this imagining would be a false notion. No, this burden of God will reach its destination; only, someone else will carry its felicity, another community will be granted this honor — to carry this burden to its destination. "Man yartadda minkum ʿan dīnih" — whoever of you turns back from his religion and apostatizes; "God will bring a people (fa-sawfa yaʾtī-llāhu bi-qawm)" — God will bring a people. This people is that very ideal Islamic people. That ideal Islamic society, in terms of internal and external bonds and relations, is as has come in this verse. "God will bring a people whom He loves (fa-sawfa yaʾtī-llāhu bi-qawmin yuḥibbuhum)" — God the Exalted will bring forth a community whom God Himself loves; "and who love Him (wa-yuḥibbūnahu)" — and they too love God. Do we too love God? No! This little bit of love that you sometimes [express], saying "O God, may I be sacrificed for You" — though God has no need at all of such "sacrifice"-saying or sacrificing — and on top of that we are lying, for we are by no means willing to go and be sacrificed for God — this is not love. The love of God: "Say, if you love God, then follow me, [and] God will love you (qul in kuntum tuḥibbūna-llāha fa-ttabiʿūnī yuḥbibkumu-llāh)"[^s24-11] is [the standard]. This is the Qur'an. If you love God, follow me, who am the Prophet, so that God too may love you. So "yuḥibbuhum wa-yuḥibbūnahu" — meaning these [people] were one hundred percent submitted to the command of God, [and] thus were the object of God's love.

Well, this is one property and attribute: that affection and love between them and God is mutual. "Humble toward the believers (adhillatin ʿalā al-muʾminīn)" — they are humble before the believers; this is the sign of the perfection of intimate relation and bond. Before the believers, before this mass of Muslims who have filled the body of the Islamic society, there exists in them no kind of arrogance,[^s24-12] no kind of pride, no kind of excessive expectation, no kind of vain and empty pretension. That is, when they stand before the people, they are part of the people, they are with the people, they are on the path of the people, they are for the people; they do not pull themselves out from among the people, they do not entrench themselves in an ivory tower so as to view the people from afar and occasionally show some pity for the people. They are among the people. "Humble toward the believers (adhillatin ʿalā al-muʾminīn)" — they make themselves humble and small before those who believe in God. "Mighty against the disbelievers (aʿizzatin ʿalā al-kāfirīn)" — the opposite point — before the disbelievers and the enemies of religion and the opponents of the Qur'an, they are "aʿizzah" (mighty); that is, immune to influence, that is, lofty-headed, that is, having wrapped and drawn around themselves a fortress of Islamic thought, so that they accept no infiltration from them.

"They strive in the way of God (yujāhidūna fī sabīli-llāh)" — another property of theirs is that they wage jihad and struggle in the way of God, relentlessly, unconditionally, just as in the verse it occurs: "And they do not fear the blame of any blamer (wa-lā yakhāfūna lawmata lāʾim)" — they do not fear or dread the blame of any blamer. As to what sort of blamer now — think for yourselves; I can no longer explain, I am tired. "That is the favor of God; He gives it to whom He wills (dhālika faḍlu-llāhi yuʾtīhi man yashāʾ)" — this is the bounty and grace and favor of God, which He gives to whomever He wills. "And God is all-Encompassing, all-Knowing (wa-llāhu wāsiʿun ʿalīm)" — God is open-handed, generous-handed, and Knowing.

Then, concerning the connection and bond of the parts of the Islamic society with that heart, with that power, with that Imam, with that leader, the next verse comes following the previous verses. See how fitting and beautiful it is. See how much pondering (tadabbur) in the Qur'an enlightens a person regarding these matters which he had thought were not Qur'anic — how eloquently the Qur'an speaks. It spoke of external relations, it spoke of internal bonds; now it states the heart of the internal bonds — that is, the leader, the guide, the Imam.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mā'idah (5:55):** "Your walī is only God and His Messenger and those who have believed — those who establish prayer and give zakat while they are bowing (in rukūʿ)."

It says: "Innamā waliyyukumu-llāh" — your walī, and the one who upholds the command, the one to whom all the energies and activities of the society and the Islamic Ummah must return and from whom they must draw inspiration, is God. Well, but God does not become embodied to come and sit among the people and command and forbid — so who else? "And His Messenger (wa-rasūluh)" — it is evident that between the Messenger and God there is never any rivalry or contention or mutual aversion;[^s24-13] the Messenger is His Messenger after all. [It is] God and His Messenger. Well, but the Messenger does not always remain:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Zumar (39:30):** "Indeed, you will die, and indeed, they will die."

After the Messenger, what? After the Messenger too we have [a walī]: "And those who have believed (wa-alladhīna āmanū)" — those believers. Which believers? Is it enough that anyone simply believes? No, there is a sign. "Those who establish prayer (alladhīna yuqīmūna al-ṣalāh)" — they establish the prayer; "and give zakat (wa-yuʾtūna al-zakāh)" — they give the zakat; "while they are bowing (wa-hum rākiʿūn)" — the exegetes took the wāw as the wāw of state (ḥāliyya): while they are in rukūʿ, in the state of bowing they give zakat. Meaning whom? Meaning the Commander of the Faithful, ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, who is appointed as walī.

Now, you may at one time derive this from the verse, taking the wāw as the wāw of state — very good indeed. If, supposing, you raise a doubt even about this, saying "no, it means the absolute [body of] believers who possess this characteristic" — I ask: who can be the symbol and emblem of such a totality in the Islamic society? We know of no one other than ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib. In that Islamic society, the one who could be the symbol of such a firm and solid faithful faction is ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib — even if we suppose that the verse does not have his Eminence in view (although, by reason of the historical reality that has been narrated, it does have [him] in view).

Of course, let the attention of the gentlemen be [noted]; I found it necessary to give this reminder regarding yesterday's discussion or today's discussion: if our discussion is in the matter of the Imamate or Wilayat or the emphasis upon Shiʿism, the positive aspect of the matter is what we have in view, not the negative aspect of the matter. As we have repeatedly said, we deem it necessary that the Shiʿa should know himself, should know his own thought, should make his own faith as firm and effective as possible — this we believe.

We who are conducting this discussion wish to establish Shiʿism; we do not wish to negate others. We do not wish to needlessly create intellectual and tastes-based disagreement again — no. But it is necessary for you to understand how you comprehend Shiʿism. When I say "Shiʿism," do not say to yourself, "Sir, first establish Islam, [and] let Shiʿism be afterward" — no; that Shiʿism which I speak of is nothing other than Islam, [and] Islam is nothing other than Shiʿism. The viewers see Islam in ten ways; one way of seeing — that is the seeing which Shiʿism sees — true Islam is that very [thing]. The understanding which the Shiʿa has of Islam and the Qur'an, that understanding is the correct and logical and just and rational understanding. Do you note? Therefore, we are stating the Islamic principles; in our own opinion and to our own view, these things we are discussing and expounding are the ideological principles of Islam. And I do not think that you too, if you reflect, would have a view contrary to this. Therefore, the positive matters and the positive aspects of the matter are what we have in view.

We are expounding Islam just as we understand and know it in the school of Shiʿism. We are not concerned now with other factions who may know and understand it in another way; we do not even debate with them; for now, we have neither quarrel nor conflict nor opposition with them — we are brothers, we even extend the hand of friendship. Why? Because we have an enemy, because outside the house an enemy stands. We two brothers have no right right now to beat each other about the head and face. This too is our way, which I wanted to mention so that you gentlemen may know: the discussion concerning Shiʿism and the Shiʿa is for the sake of the authenticity of Shiʿism, for the sake of the fact that we believe in Shiʿism, for the sake of the fact that we see Islam from the viewpoint of Shiʿism — not for the sake of wishing to create disagreement between Shiʿa and Sunni; never. We deem this disagreement forbidden too.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mā'idah (5:55):** "Your walī is only God and His Messenger and those who have believed — those who establish prayer and give zakat while they are bowing."

Well, now, if we observe Wilayat, what comes of it? Does it have an effect for us too? If Wilayat, which up to now has had three dimensions — one, the preservation of internal bonds; one, the severing of bond and dependence upon the opposing external poles — these two — and one, the preservation of a permanent and deep connection with the heart of the body of Islam and the heart of the Islamic Ummah, that is, the Imam and leader — the three dimensions of Wilayat which we have expounded — now, if we observe these three dimensions, how will it be? The verse of the Qur'an answers us:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Mā'idah (5:56):** "And whoever takes God and His Messenger and those who have believed as allies — then indeed, the party of God — they are the victorious."[^s24-14]

"And whoever takes God and His Messenger as his walī (wa-man yatawalla-llāha wa-rasūlah)" — the one who accepts Wilayat with God and with His Messenger and with those who have believed, [and] observes and preserves this bond with the believers and does not sever it — these are the dominant ones, the victorious ones. Of all, these very ones are the most victorious, and it is they who will have the upper hand over all the other factions.

## Footnotes

[^s24-1]: Hadith no. 8. "May God have mercy on a person who performs a deed and does it with perfection."

[^s24-2]: (kh-ṣ-m) one who shows enmity, [the two parties] showing enmity against one another.

[^s24-3]: On Nāṣir al-Dīn Shāh's third journey to Europe, a contract was concluded between the government of Iran and Gerald Talbot, founder of the Régie company, by which the concession for the trade in tobacco, pipe-tobacco, and cigarettes was given over to the monopoly of this company for a period of fifty years in exchange for the payment of a certain sum annually. Under the pretext of commercial activity, this company brought many foreign nationals into Iran, a considerable number of whom were Christian missionaries. This concession was annulled by the issuance of the prohibition decree (fatwa) of Mīrzā[-yi] Shīrāzī.

[^s24-4]: The East India Company was an English joint-stock company which, by receiving a charter from the Queen of England and exclusive concessions from the ruler of India, was able to attain enormous profit. Gradually, under the pretext of defending its property and so forth, this company stationed military forces in India and in practice made this great country part of the colonies of Britain.

[^s24-5]: A matter relating to the foundation, the root, and the essence of each thing and each affair.

[^s24-6]: Imam Ḥasan al-ʿAskarī, hadith no. 46. "As for whoever among the jurists is one who guards his soul, preserves his religion, opposes his [base] desire, and obeys the command of his Master, then it is for the common people to follow him (do taqlīd of him)."

[^s24-7]: (n-ṣ-ṣ) designated by way of [Qur'anic] verses and [prophetic] narrations.

[^s24-8]: Hadith no. 43. "The Wilayat of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib is my fortress; whoever enters my fortress is safe from my punishment."

[^s24-9]: Hadith no. 24. "And know that this Qur'an is the counselor who does not deceive, and the guide who does not lead astray, and the teller who does not lie. No one sits with this Qur'an but that he rises from it with an increase or a decrease: an increase in guidance, or a decrease in [his] blindness."

[^s24-10]: (gh-l-ẓ) plural of ghalīẓ — coarse, heavy, weighty; (sh-d-d) plural of shadīd — severe.

[^s24-11]: Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:31).

[^s24-12]: (n-kh-w) arrogance and self-conceit.

[^s24-13]: (n-f-r) to feel aversion to and distance from one another.

[^s24-14]: Continuation of verse 56: "And whoever takes God and His Messenger and those who have believed as allies — then indeed, the party of God — they are the victorious (wa-man yatawalla-llāha wa-rasūlahu wa-alladhīna āmanū fa-inna ḥizba-llāhi humu al-ghālibūn)."


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 25: The Paradise of Wilayat

*بهشت ولایت — delivered Sunday, 21 Mehr 1353 / 26 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Ḥajj (22:41):** "[They are] those who, if We give them authority in the land, establish the prayer and pay the alms-due and enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong; and to God belongs the final outcome of all affairs."[^s25-1]

The subject which, following the previous discussions, is worth noting in the matter of wilayah, is: first, a brief introduction to the society possessing wilayah and to the individual possessing wilayah; and second, the role and the panorama of a society in which wilayah exists. In the course of the past day or two, when we discussed this theme, what came into our hands from reflection upon the verses of the Qur'an — this reflection, of course, being aided by, and reaching its conclusions on the basis of, our connection with the teachings of the Ahl al-Bayt in the matter of wilayah which already existed in our minds; so that what we have stated is a distillation, both from the Qur'an and from the hadith — the distillation that emerged from these discussions was this: that wilayah has several dimensions, several manifestations.

One of them is this: that the Muslim society should not be dependent upon and attached to elements outside its own being; it should not have attachment to the non-Muslim. And of course we explained that this not being attached and not being dependent is one thing, while having no relations at all is quite another thing. We never say that the world of Islam should live in political and economic isolation and should have no relationship whatsoever with any of the non-Muslim nations and countries and powers; no, that is not the point. The issue is the issue of not being dependent, not being attached and a mere follower, not being digested and dissolved into other powers, of independence and standing on one's own feet — in short.

Another manifestation, another body and surface of wilayah, consists of the very intense internal cohesion and connection and bond among the Muslim elements; that is, when the Islamic society is under discussion for us, in our minds, what they call wilayah consists of the Islamic society's being unified and moving in one direction. As there is in the Prophetic and non-Prophetic narrations: «**مثل المؤمنین فی توادهم و تراحمهم کمثل الجسد**» ("The likeness of the believers in their mutual affection and their mutual mercy is like the likeness of a single body")[^s25-2] — like one single frame, like one single building, like the interconnected parts of a single structure; the Muslims must be joined to one another, fused to one another, knotted to one another, and in short, must be a single hand against the other hands and against the conflicts and enmities that will arise. You saw this point fully in yesterday's verse.

Again, among the verses of Sūrat al-Māʾidah is the verse «**اذلة علی المؤمنین اعزة علی الکافرین**» ("humble toward the believers, mighty against the disbelievers").[^s25-3] In another verse of the Qur'an, this very same point is again expressed even more clearly: «**محمد رسول الله و الذین معه اشداء علی الکفار رحماء بینهم**» ("Muhammad is the Messenger of God, and those who are with him are severe against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves").[^s25-4] When they stand facing the external front, you see nothing firmer and more impervious to breach than they are; you see nothing more impervious to influence and more impenetrable; independent. But in the internal fronts — and to use the term "front" here is essentially incorrect, since there is no front-formation inside; inside, among the bodies and the wings of this immense Islamic frame, there is no impenetrability at all, no breach and no imperviousness to influence; on the contrary, all of them act upon one another, all of them draw one another toward goodness and virtue, all of them counsel one another to follow the truth ever more, all of them counsel one another to persevere ever more firmly in the path of truth and to resist ever more strongly against the motives of evil and corruption and decline, all of them hold one another up. And as I gave the example the other day: like ten seasoned mountaineers and climbers, who are making their way through these twists and turns of the mountain, along these narrow trails where, if a single clod, a single stone, slips out from beneath the foot of one climber, that is enough to send him plunging headfirst into the depths of a ravine — a hundred meters, two hundred meters, five hundred meters down. Here, the way for all the individuals to remain safe is this: that they bind their waists to one another with a rope as strong as possible, that they have mutual attraction,[^s25-5] that they take one another's hand, that they call out to one another: "So-and-so, you have not lost the way, have you? You have not fallen behind, have you? You have not gone hungry, have you?" They watch to see whether there is among them anyone who, intellectually, materially, or in terms of knowing right and truth, is weaker than the others. If there is, then all strive to guide him, all try to draw him onto the straight path — in short, a family with members and individuals who are one hundred percent sincere with one another. This too is another of the manifestations of wilayah, that the Islamic society is of this kind.

Another of the manifestations of wilayah, which again we mentioned in the previous days as being very important and the most important of all, and which is also the guarantor of the survival of wilayah in the first and second senses, is this: that the Islamic society needs an administering center with particular conditions. Since the Islamic society must have the status of a single frame, being both mutually attracted and cohesive[^s25-6] with one another within, and outwardly manifesting like a single fist, like a single body, like a single person in the face of the external fronts — because this unity is necessary in the Islamic society; and unity, without a concentration of administering power, is not possible. If over every corner of the corners of the world of Islam there should rule some agent, some power, some pole [of authority], the parts of this frame will disperse from one another, will separate from one another, will not move along a single path. It becomes exactly like this: that the organism of the administering nerves of the human being should be transformed, from its present condition, into the state in which there were two nervous command-systems in the human being — one for the right side, one for the left side. Now that both of them are one, if I wish to move these two hands of mine in a coordinated fashion, I can; because both hands take their inspiration from a single center. The gentleman who wishes to lift a column and set it on that side, to lift a weight and place it on that side — both his hands, since they obey a single command-center, work together, work in proportion with one another. This hand comes and takes hold from this side, that hand also takes hold from that side, both press toward one side, then together they lift the weight. But if the command-center of the body's nerves became two in the human being — one for the right side, one for the left side — then here the right hand and the left will no longer have coordination with one another. The right hand, to take hold of this weight and heavy load, has opened its fingers; now the left hand, since it is taking its command and inspiration from another center, at that very moment is pressing these fingers together all the more tightly. The right hand says to the left hand, "Cooperate, you too take this side of the weight." No indeed, it does not lend an ear at all. At the very moment when this hand is ready to lift and grasp this load, the other hand has turned completely into a fist drawn in upon itself, clenched, frozen — under no circumstances is it willing to take this load at all. They reach another place, [where] they come up against an enemy; this hand that had been a fist now wishes to strike that enemy on the head; it says to the other hand, "Well, you help too, so we may land two double-blows[^s25-7] on his head, with two fists smash his brain to pieces, so we may sooner be rid of him." But this hand now, to make up for the fact that the other had been obstinate there, in obedience to the command-center of the right-side nerves, becomes obstinate. It says, "No indeed, I now wish to be open, wide open." Well, this one comes down as a fist; that one is not at all ready to strike upon the head of that enemy. You see, sir, that if the body of the human being were to take its command and its inspiration, in its nerves, from two command-centers, what an absurd condition this body would acquire. Where it is supposed to lift a load, it cannot. Where it is supposed to transfer something from one place to another, again it cannot. Where it is supposed to repel an enemy, again it cannot.

If the Islamic society wishes to repel its enemies in the right place, at the right time, and well — if one corner of the Islamic society today takes a thought to repelling the enemy, and another corner takes it up the day after tomorrow, again there is no benefit. If a society and a single frame wishes to do battle with its enemy, all the wings of this Islamic society must, at one and the same time, confront and face that enemy. If this one takes the thought today, that one tomorrow, the third day that other wing and that other corner awakens from sleep, then that enemy, with an easy mind — [creates and brings about] the affair of those same three persons who had gone into the vineyard[^s25-8] which Rumi has related — brings about and produces this affair, and will out-trick them all; just as this experience has occurred not once, not twice, but at least ten times in the history of Islam.

So if the Islamic society wishes to attract benefits at the right time, to repel harms and losses at the right time, to be on the inside parts that are congenial[^s25-9] and connected with one another, and on the outside, in the face of the enemy, to hold the status of a single fist and a single hand — in short, if it wishes to secure within itself those two surfaces and two dimensions and two aspects of wilayah, it needs to have within itself a powerful command-center, so that all the active and energetic elements of this society may take their inspiration from that center: their intellectual energy, their practical energy, the activities of their life, their crushing of enemies, their cherishing of friends.

That center which, in the heart of the society and within the body of the Islamic society, administers all the wings, sets each person to the work worthy of his rank, prevents conflicts, guides the forces in one direction — he must be from God, must be learned, must be aware, must be trustworthy and safeguarded [from error], must be a crystallized being of all the constituent elements of Islam, must be a manifestation of the Qur'an. What name do we give him? We give him the name of *walī* (guardian). So wilayah in the Islamic society, in those two respects which I have stated, requires that there be a *walī* present in the society. This too is one dimension and one body and one surface from among the dimensions of the matter of wilayah. This was the explanation of the previous discussions.

The question that arises after this is: do I and you, sir, possess wilayah or not? It is possible that I and you possess wilayah; but does our society, taken as a whole, possess wilayah or not? Does it make a difference, sir? Yes — it is possible that one member [of the body] should in itself be sound, but the soundness of one member, first, does not mean the soundness of the whole body — this is one point; and second, a sound member, if it is placed in an unsound body, cannot possess all the merits of a sound member — these are two points. First let us see what kind of person a human being with wilayah is, so that we may understand whether I personally and you personally possess wilayah or not. And if it should be proven and made clear — God willing — that I and you possess wilayah, then we must proceed to see how a society must be in order to possess wilayah.

There is no obstacle to it whatsoever; "there is no obstacle" — I mean it is possible; that is my meaning; not that there is no problem with it — there is a great deal of problem with it — but there is no obstacle at all to our supposing a human being possessing wilayah within a society devoid of wilayah. Has this human being's responsibility come to an end? Merely because he himself possesses wilayah, even though he lives in a society deprived of and bare of wilayah — is this life a desirable life?
