And if a person himself possessed wilayah but lived in a society that is without wilayah, and he felt no responsibility in the face of the society's lack of wilayah — does this very absence of a sense of responsibility not itself injure and ruin his own wilayah? These are headings upon which you, the Muslim man, the Muslim woman, and especially the Muslim youth, must reflect. It is possible that I may not have enough time and opportunity to expound each one of these for you; and if I wished to expound each one of these for you, so that all the individuals here present might properly grasp the matter, this would require that we discuss each one of these headings for a day, say — and I do not have that much time.

The question is this: first, let us see what kind of human being a human being possessing wilayah is. How must I be, [so that] I possess wilayah? How must I be, [so that] I do not possess wilayah? That is one. Second, let us see how we — the society, the social body of human beings who have gathered together in one place — must be, in order to possess wilayah, and how we will not possess wilayah. In what condition is a society a society possessing a guardian (*walī*) and a custodian and friends-and-helpers (*mawālī*),[^s25-10] in the form that Islam has commanded; and in what condition, and under what circumstances, is it deprived of wilayah — that wilayah which Islam has spoken of.

The third question — these are two questions (I am repeating, so that each of you gentlemen may fix it in your minds; those of you who are people of precision and reflection and note-taking, take notes. Go and think on it yourselves. Use your own stored-up Islamic mental resources and the existing Islamic texts. Arrive at the meaning of the verdict on these questions yourselves). The third question: a human being who possesses wilayah — has his duty come to an end merely with the possessing of wilayah for his own person? Does he have no duty to build a society possessing wilayah? This too is a question.

The fourth question: if a man himself possessed wilayah, lived in a society deprived of wilayah, and felt no sense of duty to make the society possess wilayah — will this not feeling of duty not blemish his own wilayah? This very thing, that he is not thinking of making others too possess wilayah — does not this very not-thinking itself weaken his wilayah, blemish it, ruin it? This too is another question.

These questions we must discuss. Now I will raise one or two of them, discuss them, talk them over. Of course, when this has been discussed, then compare in your own minds this progressive, exalted, fascinating, sensible meaning of wilayah — pronounced by the Qur'an, spoken by the hadith — with that meaning which the lazy, ease-loving, comfort-seeking, hardship-fleeing, facility-seeking man imagines wilayah to be for himself.

A group imagine that possessing wilayah is a matter of a man's merely weeping in the gatherings of the Ahl al-Bayt. They imagine that possessing wilayah is a matter of necessarily saying «علیهم‌السلام» ("peace be upon them") after the name of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) is mentioned; only this and nothing more. They imagine that possessing wilayah is a matter of having the love of the Ahl al-Bayt in one's heart, this and nothing more. Yes, to have the love of the Ahl al-Bayt is obligatory and incumbent. To utter the name of these great ones with reverence is most fascinating and necessary. To hold a gathering in their name and to draw lessons from their mourning and their joy, to recount their mourning, to recount their joy, to weep over their nobilities, over their gallantries, over the wrongs done to them — all of these are necessary; but all of these are not wilayah; wilayah is higher than this. The one who sits in the gathering of the Master of Martyrs (the blessings and peace of God be upon him) and sheds tears does a good thing in that he sheds tears; but he does a bad thing if he reckons the shedding of tears to be sufficient for the possessing of wilayah.

Understand well what I am saying. Those whose minds are under the influence of the malicious or ignorant suggestions and inducements of certain mercenary and malicious hands — let them pay close attention. Let it not be said that someone is opposed to weeping for the Master of Martyrs — no. Even if anyone is opposed, we are not opposed; we are in favor. We say that weeping over Imam Ḥusayn (the blessings and peace of God be upon him) can at times save a whole nation. The Tawwābūn (the Penitents)[^s25-11] went and sat at the grave of Ḥusayn ibn ʿAlī, and for twenty-four hours, or forty-eight hours, or three days, did nothing but weep. What was the result of that weeping? The result of those weepings was that they made a pact of death and blood with one another. They said, "We will go into this battlefield, we will not turn back; we make a covenant that we will not return alive." This is the weeping for Imam Ḥusayn. No one is opposed to this.

To utter with reverence the name of Ḥusayn ibn ʿAlī (the blessings of God be upon him) and of the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) — no sane person is opposed to it, no Shīʿah; the Sunnī too is not opposed, the unbeliever too is not opposed. Anyone who comes to know them confirms that the name of these ones must be uttered with reverence. On the twenty-third night I said here, for those of you who were present: a family whose inheritance is martyrdom, a family whose most precious keepsake is sacrifice and giving one's life in the way of God, a family whose every resource, whose whole being, is dedicated and devoted purely to God — should a human being not commemorate this family with reverence? He must do so.

I give you my word: go to Europe, to America, to any city of any unbeliever-land, to any land of foreigners-to-Islam, in any place where they have not even heard the name of Islam; say, "There was a man, his name was ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, his life-story is this." Begin to recount the life-story of the Commander of the Faithful from beginning to end in summary, and you will see that, in honor of this figure, in honor of this human being who has so many glories in his life, they will applaud, they will show respect, they will glorify, they will bow in reverence, and they will keep his name in their minds as a precious memorial from you. This is not peculiar to the Shīʿah. Have you now performed some great feat that, when you say "ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib," you say "peace be upon him" after it? Have you done some momentous thing? And do you imagine that wilayah means just this? What a great mistake. That wilayah which carries a human being to Paradise — this is not it. Of course, weeping over Imam Ḥusayn can be counted as one of the corners, the branches, the very far-removed sections of the great principle of wilayah. A group of people, truly out of ignorance, and — God willing — out of ignorance and not out of malice, raise these issues. They raise the issue of weeping; the issue of, may I say, verbal enmities with the enemies of the Ahl al-Bayt — not intellectual enmities; they raise issues of this sort; they raise the superficial issues of the chapter of wilayah and Shīʿism, and they confine wilayah to these; and how astonishing it is that they beat down the comprehenders of wilayah, the knowers of wilayah, and the possessors of wilayah with this very weapon. This is most amusing. They confine wilayah within a domain[^s25-12] and a narrow, small precinct that is not worthy of this great Islamic principle.

Wilayah in a human being means an ever-greater and ever-increasing intellectual and practical attachment to the *walī*. Find the *walī*; come to know the *walī* of God, the one who is the rightful[^s25-13] *walī* of the Islamic society; identify him; and after you have identified him, connect yourself to him personally, intellectually, in deed, in temperament, in path and method and manner; link yourself to him, bind yourself to him; set out after him, move. If your effort is his effort, your *jihād* his *jihād*, your friendship his friendship, your enmity his enmity, your front-formations his front-formations — then you possess wilayah. Two words, and easy. Did you grasp what I said? The person possessing wilayah is this: that he know the *walī*, know the thought of the *walī*, become of one mind with the *walī*, know the conduct of the *walī*, become of one deed with the *walī*, set out after him, make himself attached to the *walī* both in thought and in deed — this is [a person] with wilayah.

Which of you is ready to raise your hand and say, like a man, "I possess wilayah"? And which of us is ready to confess, like a man, that we did not possess complete wilayah? We confined wilayah to nothing but this — that in our hearts there is love of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib and that we shed a single tear too for the Commander of the Faithful — even though our deeds are contrary to the deeds of ʿAlī, our thought and our notions contrary to the thought and notions of ʿAlī. Have we named this wilayah? We made up for ourselves a legend, a myth, a superstition, and gladdened our hearts with it; we reckoned ourselves to be among the friends and helpers (*mawālī*) of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib (peace be upon him) and to be possessors of wilayah, and were happy that all that belongs to the friends of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, we reckon to be certainly and definitely ours. This is a great wrong; this is a great injustice against the right of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib. This is a great injustice against the right of Islam, since wilayah is for Islam; and this is a great injustice against the right of this great principle, the principle of wilayah. And we are not even willing to understand wilayah.

This — that Imam al-Ṣādiq (the blessings and peace of God be upon him) recognizes the worth of his friends, those who possess wilayah, and holds them in honor, and reckons wilayah to lie in deed, and says, "The one who possesses deed, he is our *walī* [friend]; the one who does not possess deed, he is our enemy" — this is based on this very thing. Since Imam al-Ṣādiq interprets wilayah in that way, since wilayah in the culture of Imam al-Ṣādiq is different from wilayah in the culture of that ignorant or malicious person who is living off the name of Imam al-Ṣādiq — these two are different from one another. Why should I and you not understand the meaning of wilayah? Why should we not deeply comprehend this Islamic principle, so that we may not remain, ever more, in stagnation and rigidity and torpor and backwardness and remoteness from Islam and remoteness from this world and the next — why? I fear that we may keep ourselves, in hope of Paradise, a lifetime in the hellfire of this world, and that at the moment when we give up our soul — which is the time for the embodiment of wishes and the realization of long and far-reaching hopes — there too there may be no trace of Paradise. How great a cause for regret that is. The wilayah of a human being is his absolute attachment and dependence upon the *walī*; he must be attached and joined — this is one.

And now, the wilayah of a society — in what does it consist? The wilayah of a society consists in this: that the *walī* in that society, first, be identified; that they know that this is the *walī*; this is one. Second, that he be the source and the inspirer of all the forces, energies, and activities of that society. That he be a pole from which all the streams and wellsprings flow down. That he be a center from which he issues all commands and by which he executes all laws. That he be a point to which all the threads and strings return, to which all look, after which all go. That he start up the engine of life in this society; that he be the driver and the vanguard of the caravan of life in the society. This society is a society possessing wilayah.

The Commander of the Faithful (the blessings of God be upon him), after the Prophet, did not hold the reins of the community in his hand for twenty-five years. The Islamic society, after the Prophet, for twenty-five years did not possess wilayah; it was finished and gone. In that society, Salmān personally possessed wilayah, Abū Dharr personally possessed wilayah, Miqdād personally possessed wilayah, that other one personally possessed wilayah — but what of the Islamic society? The Islamic society, after the Prophet, for twenty-five years did not possess wilayah. Then the Commander of the Faithful reached the rule, and the Islamic society came to possess wilayah. How much of it? Exactly that much by which ʿAlī, in that measure, in that portion, in that part, was the source and the inspirer and the bedrock of the commands and ordinances and gnoses and recognitions. The wilayah of a community is in this. Whenever the Imam rules in a society, when the Imam becomes the source of command and prohibition in the society, when all the threads issue from the Imam, when the Imam is actually administering the society, when it is the Imam who fastens the banner of war, when it is the Imam who gives the order of attack, when it is the Imam who writes the peace treaty — then the society is a society possessing wilayah; and otherwise, the society does not possess wilayah. This too is the society possessing wilayah.

Now, if you possess wilayah, give thanks to God; if you do not possess it, set out after it. If such a blessing rests upon your head, give thanks to God. There is no blessing greater than the blessing of wilayah. I will now explain for you how there is no blessing greater than wilayah. If you do not possess it, set out after it, secure wilayah within your own person and within human society. Try to live in the manner of ʿAlī; let us try to set out after ʿAlī; let us try to establish a bond between ourselves and ʿAlī, who is the *walī* of God. These require striving, require effort, require struggle, require the consuming of heart's blood. And let me tell you that the rightly-guiding Imams (peace be upon them), after the martyrdom of the Commander of the Faithful, strove ceaselessly in the path of wilayah, ceaselessly. The whole effort of the Imams was for this: that they should bring wilayah to life, revive the Islamic society, and make this sapling — which has been planted in the name of "the human being" in this field, in this orchard, in this nursery — alive and verdant and flourishing with the wholesome[^s25-14] water of the life-giving, life-creating wilayah. This was the effort of the Imams, by its own path, in the requisite form.

To strive for the wilayah of a society does not mean that we should sit and constantly wag our chins and constantly babble idly and curse Zayd and ʿAmr, this one and that one. By these deeds, wilayah is not built; by these deeds, we fall farther from wilayah. The way is for us to strive to give power to the *walī* of Islam, to the *walī* whom God has named. Yesterday I specified what kind of being the *walī* whom God has named is. Yesterday I said: one *walī* God names by name, one *walī* God names by description. There is a case in which ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib (peace be upon him), Ḥasan ibn ʿAlī (peace be upon him), Ḥusayn ibn ʿAlī (peace be upon him), ʿAlī ibn al-Ḥusayn (peace be upon him), down to the last of the Imams, are specified by name and by particular characteristics; and there is a case in which, no, they are not specified by name; that very *walī* who has been specified by name says: «**فاما من کان من الفقهاء صائنا لنفسه حافظا لدینه مخالفا علی هواه مطیعا لامر مولاه**» ("As for the one among the jurists who is a guardian of his own soul, a protector of his religion, an opposer of his own caprice, obedient to the command of his Master…");[^s25-15] in this way he specifies him. The *walī* is the *walī*; this one too is from God, that one too is from God. Only, that one they have specified by name, this one they have not specified by name; this one's characteristics they have stated. You yourself reckoned, measured, took the dimensions, set the pattern, and saw that — for example — His Eminence Grand Ayatollah Āqā Burūjirdī fitted it. Did you take note? When a human being is resolved to bring to life and revive the Islamic laws and the divine ordinances in the society, in the form that wilayah dictates, then he goes searching and finds the way to it. He searches, and learns the methods and modes that the world lays upon the shoulder and around the neck of the human being. Now we are not discussing methods and modes; we are discussing the principle of it.

Then, if a society comes to possess wilayah, what happens? It is well that I say it in a single word: it is a corpse that will come to possess a soul. This single word is enough. Consider a corpse: it lies there lifeless. It has a brain — it does not work. It has eyes — it does not see. It has a mouth — it does not digest and swallow food. It has a stomach and a liver and a digestive apparatus,[^s25-16] but it does not absorb food. It has veins, but the blood does not circulate in them; it has blood, and it does not circulate in it — why? Because it has no soul. It has hands, but it does not even repel this tiny ant from itself. It has feet, but it is not willing to move from the sun into the shade — it cannot. It is just a corpse, just a corpse. Once the soul is breathed in, the brain works, the nerves work, the hand grasps, the mouth eats, the stomach digests, the digestive system absorbs, the blood turns and circulates, conveys energy to the whole body, warms the body, sets it to striving — it walks, it crushes the enemy, it attracts friendships, it makes itself ever more perfect and more flourishing.

Place this parable before your eyes for the purpose of grasping the importance of wilayah in a society. Take away the dead corpse of the human being, set a human society in its place; take away the spirit, set wilayah in its place. A society that does not possess wilayah — the talents and capacities exist in this society, but they are either neutralized, wasted, destroyed, squandered, or, worse, set to work to the detriment of the human being. It has a brain and it thinks, but it thinks for the creating of corruption, thinks for the killing of human beings, thinks for the setting of the world ablaze, thinks toward making human beings wretched, thinks for the strengthening of the foundations of exploitation and oppression and despotism. The human being has eyes, but what it ought to see, it does not see, and what it ought not to see, it sees. It has ears, but it does not hear the word of truth; the nerves do not convey the word of truth to the brain. The brain issues no command in accordance with truth to the limbs and organs. The limbs and organs perform no deed in accordance with truth in the world. The conditions of the world give the human being no leave to act in accordance with truth. This is the society without wilayah.

In the society without wilayah, the lamps not only do not flare up and grow brighter, but even that little bit of oil they have is constantly being used up, being used up, until it is altogether dried out. The oils that the Prophet had poured at the foot of the lamps and torches of guidance — did you see how they dried up? Did you see how, in the days after the passing of the Prophet, [the lamp] would flare up, would illuminate, would give light? Its oil the Prophet had poured; but since the hand of wilayah was not above the head of these torches and lamps, did you see how its oil ran low, ran low, dried up, went bad, became foul-smelling, became full of smoke, became dim — reached the time of ʿUthmān and was handed over to Muʿāwiyah? Did you see what happened? The very things that Fāṭimah al-Zahrāʾ (peace of God be upon her) said to the women of the Emigrants and the Helpers, and they did not listen in the early days; the very predictions that Fāṭimah al-Zahrāʾ made, [which] the heedless Muslim people of that day could not understand and did not listen to — those very things were all carried out. That very «**سیف صارم**» (the cutting sword),[^s25-17] that very blood-shedding sword, that very blade that slew authenticities and virtues, that very hand that strangled human beings and human virtues — all of these Fāṭimah al-Zahrāʾ had foretold, and before Fāṭimah al-Zahrāʾ the Prophet too had foretold. These ones saw, these ones understood, they spoke — the Islamic society did not understand. Ears grew heavy, grew deaf. Today too the voice of Fāṭimah al-Zahrāʾ still reaches our ears. O sensitive and alert ears, do you hear?

The society possessing wilayah becomes a society that develops all human talents. All the things that God has given to the human being for his perfection and elevation, it brings to fruition. It makes the human sapling flourish. It brings human beings to perfection. It strengthens human virtues. In this society, the *walī* — that is, the ruler, the very one to whom all the reins return — makes the society, in terms of its general course, set upon the path of God and possessed of the remembrance of God. In terms of wealth, he brings about a just distribution of wealth. He tries to spread good things; he tries to wipe out evils and uproot them.

«**الذین ان مکناهم فی الارض**» ("Those who, if We give them authority in the land…")[^s25-18] — this is a verse of the Qur'an; those who, if We firmly establish them in the land, if We make them firmly settled in the land — «**اقاموا الصلاة**» ("they establish the prayer") — the remembrance of God; the prayer is a symbol of the godly orientation of a society — «**اقاموا الصلاة**» — for the remembrance of God and for the attention toward God and the orienting toward God, they establish the prayer, they go toward God, they set their orientation in accordance with the orientation that God has pronounced and God has commanded. «**و آتوا الزکاة**» ("and they pay the alms-due") — the just distribution of wealth; they give the *zakāt* (alms-due). According to a likelihood and a conjecture that is in my mind, the *zakāt* in the Qur'an has a far wider scope than what exists in people's minds. I deem it likely — of course this is only a likelihood, mind you, I myself do not yet have proper certainty about it, and you too should not take this as a certain matter — I deem it likely that the term *zakāt* in the Qur'an encompasses all financial spendings and charitable givings. I have, of course, found some indications too, though not to the degree that I would be certain of this matter and could state it decisively. In any case, such a likelihood is in my mind.

«**آتوا الزکاة**» — and, generally and certainly, it means that they bring about a moderation in wealth, in view of the narrations that exist in the chapter of *zakāt* which say that *zakāt* is a cause of the moderating of wealth. «**و امروا بالمعروف**» ("and they enjoin what is right") — the spreading of good things, the extending of good deeds. «**و نهوا عن المنکر**» ("and they forbid what is wrong") — the uprooting of evil things. We imagine that enjoining the right and forbidding the wrong means that I should command you, sir, not to do such-and-such bad deed, to do such-and-such good deed — only this commanding and telling. Whereas commanding and telling is [only] one of the manifestations of enjoining the right and forbidding the wrong. They said to the Commander of the Faithful, "Why do you fight Muʿāwiyah?" — its narration is lengthy, I will not read it. He said: "For the reason that enjoining the right and forbidding the wrong is obligatory." Listen carefully, draw the conclusion. Sir, what business do you have, after all, with Muʿāwiyah in the Battle of Ṣiffīn? Go off toward Kufa, and let him too go off toward Syria. He said that God has made enjoining the right and forbidding the wrong obligatory. Imam Ḥusayn rises up from Medina; "Sir, where are you going?" — «**ارید ان آمر بالمعروف و انهی عن المنکر**» ("I wish to enjoin the right and forbid the wrong")[^s25-19] — I wish to enjoin the right and forbid the wrong. See how wide and far-reaching is the circle and the scope [of it], and how small and constricted and narrow it is in our view. In any case, when wilayah was present in a society, these things too are carried out: the establishing of the prayer, the giving of the alms-due, the enjoining of the right, the forbidding of the wrong — in short, a lifeless frame finds soul and spirit and life.

[^s25-1]: Sūrat al-Ḥajj (22:41). «الذین ان مکناهم فی الارض اقاموا الصلاة و آتوا الزکاة و امروا بالمعروف و نهوا عن المنکر و لله عاقبة الامور».
[^s25-2]: Hadith no. 9. «مثل المؤمنین فی توادهم و تراحمهم کمثل الجسد» — "The likeness of the believers in their mutual affection and their mutual mercy is like the likeness of a single body."
[^s25-3]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:54). «اذلة علی المؤمنین اعزة علی الکافرین».
[^s25-4]: Sūrat al-Fatḥ (48:29). «محمد رسول الله و الذین معه اشداء علی الکفار رحماء بینهم».
[^s25-5]: (j-dh-b) mutual attraction, the drawing of one another toward each other.
[^s25-6]: (m-s-k) holding oneself fast, gripping firmly.
[^s25-7]: A blow struck upon the crown of the head with the two open palms pressed together.
[^s25-8]: It is a tale in the second book of Rumi's *Mathnawī-yi Maʿnawī*, under the title "The gardener separating the Sufi and the jurist and the ʿAlawī (descendant of ʿAlī) from one another." In this tale, a Sufi, a jurist, and an ʿAlawī (one of the sayyids) enter a garden. The gardener, in order to drive these three out of his garden, separates the three from one another by a stratagem and has each punished at the hands of the others.
[^s25-9]: (m-l-ʾ) agreeable and suited to one's nature.
[^s25-10]: *Mutawallī*: custodian, overseer; *mawālī*: friends and helpers.
[^s25-11]: They were a group made up of the Shīʿah of Kufa, under the leadership of Sulaymān ibn Ṣurad and al-Musayyab ibn Najaba, who, regretting their failure to aid Imam Ḥusayn at Karbala, rose up to avenge the blood of that Imam against the Banū Umayyah. Because of their repentance for their past negligence, they became known as the Tawwābūn (the Penitents). In the end, they and a group of the Shīʿah of Basra who had gone to their aid clashed with the army of Syria and fought valiantly. Although at first they met with some success, in the end many of them were martyred. The uprising of the Tawwābūn (65 AH) was the first uprising against the Umayyad state after the event of Karbala.
[^s25-12]: (n-ṭ-q) a zone, region.
[^s25-13]: Ascribed to truth/right (*ḥaqq*).
[^s25-14]: Wholesome, agreeable to drink.
[^s25-15]: Hadith no. 46. «فاما من کان من الفقهاء صائنا لنفسه حافظا لدینه مخالفا علی هواه مطیعا لامر مولاه» — "As for the one among the jurists who is a guardian of his own soul, a protector of his religion, an opposer of his own caprice, obedient to the command of his Master…"
[^s25-16]: The digestive system.
[^s25-17]: Hadith no. 25. «سیف صارم» — "a cutting (sharp) sword."
[^s25-18]: Sūrat al-Ḥajj (22:41).
[^s25-19]: Hadith no. 26. «ارید ان آمر بالمعروف و انهی عن المنکر» — "I wish to enjoin the right and forbid the wrong."


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 26: Concerning Wilayat (1)

*در پیرامون ولایت (۱) — delivered Monday, 22 Mehr 1353 / 27 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:58–59):** "Indeed, Allah commands you to render the trusts to those to whom they are due, and when you judge between people to judge with justice. Excellent is that with which Allah counsels you. Indeed, Allah is ever Hearing and Seeing. O you who have believed, obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those in authority among you. And if you should dispute over anything, refer it to Allah and the Messenger, if you should believe in Allah and the Last Day. That is best and best in outcome."

The subject that, today, in continuation of our previous discussions, we decided to raise is the statement of several issues from among the matters that we encounter around the question of Wilayat. These are not part of that original framework and the statement of the position of this question itself. The discussion about Wilayat — about what Wilayat is, where in the Qur'an it is derived from, and how many dimensions, aspects, and sides it has — was the discussion already presented earlier. However, there is a series of issues which, for the question of Wilayat, are subsidiary matters; even though each of these matters, in its own place, is itself a fundamental and decisive issue, which, like a principle, must be used for the orientation of Islamic directions and for determining the path of the Muslim society.

These several matters we will mention in order. Two of them we have noted on today's sheet,[^s26-1] and one or two more, God willing, we will present tomorrow.

One of them is this: well, you established from the Qur'an that the preservation of internal bonds and the negation of external dependencies — I repeat: the preservation of internal bonds and the negation of external dependencies — for a Muslim society, is contingent upon there existing a center, a concentrated power, in the Islamic society, so that it may lead and administer all the activities and efforts, and all the orientations and stances of the various poles and factions of this society. And the name of this is the *wali*. The *wali* — with the doubled *yāʾ* — means the ruler, the sovereign; that is, the one from whom all forces must take their inspiration, to whom all affairs must return, and, in short, the one with whom the management of the Islamic society lies, both on the intellectual side and on the practical side — this one is called the *wali*. These things we established from the Qur'an. Now tell me, let us see: who is the *wali* of the Islamic society? Do we have a concise word in this regard, so that if they say to us, "Very well, now we wish to know the *wali*; who can be the *wali*? Which power is it to which all the forces of the Islamic society must return, which must exercise rule?" — if this question is put to us, do we have an answer or not?

Yes, we have. Of course, I have presented the answer to this matter, the response, from time to time within the previous discussions, and you yourselves know it too; it is not as though the matter were unknown. We wish, from the standpoint of logical method and the natural sequence of the subject, to have examined this too.

The Qur'an's answer here is a single word. It says: the one who is the true *wali* of the Islamic society is God. The ruler in the Islamic society, other than God Most High, is no one else. This is a matter that Tawhid too was telling us, that Prophethood too was establishing as a certainty for us; and now you see that Wilayat too tells us this same thing. Let me state this sentence here by way of indication and pass on: the principles of a school and a doctrine must always be like this — that each of the principles yields a conclusion which the other principles also yield. It must not be that from one of the principles of the school we draw an inference whose opposite we infer from another principle. And unfortunately, in the Islam that exists today in the mind and heart of some simple-hearted Muslims, this is exactly what happens: from some of its principles, things are inferred and deduced that are exactly the opposite of what is deduced from some of its other principles.

Therefore, the one who, in the Islamic society, has the right of command and prohibition and decree, and the right to execute commands, and the right to give orders and to determine the path of the society, and, in short, the right of rule over all the particulars of human beings' lives, is God:

> **Qur'an, Āl ʿImrān (3:68):** "And Allah is the wali of the believers."[^s26-2]

I have traced[^s26-3] the verses in the Qur'an that contain the expression *wali* and *awliyāʾ*. I made an approximately summary study of them all, and I saw that this expression — that God is the *wali* of the Islamic society, that the believers have no *wali* and helper other than God, that God must be the ruler of all the affairs of mankind — this is a matter that, in the Qur'an, is among the established certainties. Of course, the brothers are aware of this; but nonetheless it is good for me to bring it to the attention of some in whose minds the matter might become confused. The discussion is not about the creational (*takwini*) sovereignty of the Lord; that is preserved in its own place — it is clear that God Most High arranges the revolution of the earth and the sky by His overpowering will.[^s26-4] The discussion is about this: that the laws of human life and the individual and social relations of human society too must take inspiration from God; that is, the legal ruler and sovereign, in the divine, Islamic, Qur'anic society, and in the shade[^s26-5] of the Islamic order — which we always refer to as the Alawi order, and this is an expression that I would like to remain in minds: the Alawi order, the Alawi government, this order whose name we set down as the Alawi order and the Islamic order, and "the Qur'anic society" can be a name for it for us — in such a society and such an order, the ruler is God alone.

Well, here we arrive at another matter: "Sir, what does it mean that the ruler is God? God Most High does not come face to face with the people so as to command and prohibit. Human beings need a human being to govern over them; a human being is necessary to take the reins of human affairs into his hand." When I say "a human being," I do not insist on the "one"; I do not wish to negate collective leadership — no. A human being is necessary to take the reins of human affairs into his hand. Otherwise, if, among the human and social society, there were only law — even if that law were from God's side — and there were no commander, in the expression of the Commander of the Faithful, peace be upon him, which I shall now present; if there were no sovereign, or there were no body to exercise sovereignty; in short, if there were no overseer over the execution of the law in human society, then the order of human society would again be disrupted. Who is this human being to be?

That human being, or those human beings, who are to be — over mankind and human society — to practically exercise sovereignty, to practically be the *wali* of the society, to practically take charge of the Wilayat of the society — who are these to be? The answer of the various schools to this question has been varied. The answer of historical realities to this question too has been varied. One group has said: *al-mulk li-man ghalab* — "Sovereignty belongs to whoever conquers"; that is, the law of the jungle. One group has said: whoever has the greater management. One group has said: whoever is accepted by the people. One group has said: whoever is from such-and-such a dynasty. One group has said other things; they have expressed other logics and approaches[^s26-6] and tastes.

The answer of religion and the school of religion to this question is this:

> **Qur'an, al-Māʾidah (5:55):** "Your wali is only Allah and His Messenger…"[^s26-7]

The one who practically takes the rein of command and of command-and-prohibition into his hand in the society, on behalf of the Lord of the world, is His Messenger. Therefore, in a society, when a Prophet has come, it makes no sense that, with the Prophet present, a ruler other than the Prophet should govern over the people. "Prophet" means precisely the one who must take the rein of power in the society into his hand, who must be the ruler. When the Prophet departed from this world, what is the duty? When the Prophet of God, like all other human beings, departed from this world and surrendered his soul to the Soul-Creator, what do we do there? Again a verse of the Qur'an answers this matter:

> "…and those who have believed" — the believers are your *wali*.

Which believers? Whoever believes in the school of religion is the *wali* and ruler of the Islamic society? Well, this would require that we have, to the number of all believing souls, a ruler! What do we do here? Believers who have a special sign. It is evident that the verse of the Qur'an, while it wishes to designate a particular person, to put its finger upon a specific, known human being and say "your ruler is this one," also wishes, implicitly, to provide the criterion. Pay attention! It wishes that human being who, in the view of the Lawgiver and the legislator of Islam, is specified — it wishes to give him rule over the people; but at the same time it also wishes to state the criterion: the reason we said your ruler should be this one is this criterion. This is the cause of his being chosen by the Lord, or of his being appointed by the Lord. Therefore it says:

> "And those who have believed" — those who have believed, whose faith is correct, for the requirement of the absoluteness[^s26-8] of "have believed" is that correct faith is intended, not outward faiths. Those who, throughout their lives, showed that they believed; those who, with their own deeds, signed their faith.

So "those who have believed" — the first condition is that they be truly believers. It has other conditions too:

> "Those who establish the prayer" — those believers who establish the prayer, not those who merely perform the prayer. Performing the prayer is one thing; establishing the prayer is another thing.

If they had wished to say "they perform the prayer," they could have said *yuṣallūn*; an expression that is both shorter and more concise.[^s26-9]

The establishment of the prayer in a society means that the spirit of the prayer comes alive in the society, that the society becomes a praying society; and you know that a praying society means that society in which, in all its corners and edges, the remembrance of God and the recollection of God surge completely; and you know that in a society in which the remembrance of God and the recollection of God surge, in this society no catastrophe occurs, no crime, no treachery, no blow against human values occurs in this society. That society in which the remembrance of God surges, in which the people are mindful of God — its orientation is a divine orientation; in this society, all the people's deeds are done for God.

The cause of thefts, the cause of degradations, the cause of injustices, the cause of submitting to injustice — from both sides — the cause of the transgressions[^s26-10] that occur, and likewise the cause of submitting to the transgressions that occur, is entirely remoteness from the recollection of God and the remembrance of God. That society which possesses the remembrance of God — its ruler is like ʿAli ibn Abi Talib, blessings of God upon him, who does not commit injustice and crushes injustice; its subject is like Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, who, even though he is beaten, even though he is exiled, even though he is threatened, even though he remains a stranger and friendless, yet does not submit to the burden of injustice, does not turn back from the path of God. This is the society in which the remembrance of God exists, this is the society in which the establishment of the prayer exists.

The believer who, in the society, establishes the prayer, who sets the orientation of the society toward God, who makes the divine remembrance current and established in the society:

> "Those who establish the prayer."

But it does not even end here, the *wali*:

> "And they give the zakat" — they distribute wealth justly, they give the zakat, they are among those who spend in the way of God.

And following it, it says:

> "And while they are bowing" — they give the zakat while they are in the state of bowing (*rukūʿ*).

This is an indication of a particular case and a particular story. I have heard, and I know, the words that some of the other commentators have said — they have said that "and while they are bowing" means that these people are always in the state of bowing, and that it is not a reference to a particular event. But the spirit of Arabness and of knowing Arabic negates this possibility. "And while they are bowing" means: in the state of bowing they give the zakat. This too confirms that very matter that I presented yesterday — which comes to my mind as a possibility, which I suppose: that "zakat" is the absolute of all acts of charity (*infāqāt*); because the ring that the Commander of the Faithful gave while in the state of bowing was not the technical zakat — it was a charity in the way of God, and the term "zakat" has been applied to it.

> "And they give the zakat while they are bowing"

— meaning a human being so attached to equality,[^s26-11] so devoted to charitable spending, for whom the sight of poverty and the poor is so painful, that he does not wait for his prayer to finish, lest this beggar leave and another poor one come. He gives his only and sole ring — he says, "Let me set it aside, then I will give it." So great is this human being's attraction toward charity, so absorbed is this man in the path of fulfilling this duty, that he has no patience to wait, no room to bear it. He has seen a poor one, has seen a poverty, has seen an appearance that God does not love — he too does not love it — and he has nothing but the ring; so, in the state of the prayer, he takes it off and gives it to the beggar.

This is a reference to a special and specific episode in history, which the Commander of the Faithful, blessings of God upon him, brought about; that is, this noble one was in the state of prayer, a poor one came, and this noble one gave charity, and the verse was revealed. So, as you observe, the verse, by way of indication, designates ʿAli ibn Abi Talib, peace be upon him, as the *wali* of the affair (*waliyy al-amr*); but not in the manner of the designation of the history's bullies — when, say, Muʿawiya wishes to designate a successor for himself, he says, "This son is my successor; he is my child, so he must, after me, reach this station." But God Most High does not designate a successor for the Prophet in this way. He says ʿAli ibn Abi Talib must reach government after the Prophet, because the qualification for government exists in him to an intense degree. What is it? Complete faith in God, the establishment of the prayer in the society, attachment to charity and the giving (*ītāʾ*)[^s26-12] of zakat to the point of being beside oneself. While appointing the successor, while designating the successor — who is ʿAli ibn Abi Talib, peace be upon him — it also clarifies the criterion, the linchpin,[^s26-13] and the philosophy of his caliphate. This is a verse of the Qur'an.

Therefore, in Islam, the *waliyy al-amr* is the one who is the envoy of God, the one whom God Himself designates; because the premise is that no human being, by the very nature of his creation and formation, has the right of rule over other human beings.

The only one who has the right of rule is God; and since God has the right of rule, God can, according to the welfare of human beings, give this right to whomever He wills. And we know that the work of God is not outside of welfare, is not dictatorship, is not bullying and coercion; the work of God is according to the welfare of human beings. Since it is according to the welfare of human beings, therefore He designates and we submit. He designates the Prophet, He designates the Imam; after the Imam, He designates those who conform to particular criteria and qualifications. The one who possesses this quality, possesses this quality, possesses this quality — He designates qualities and says: these, after the rightly-guiding, infallible Imams, are the rulers over the Islamic society. So the *wali* is designated by God: He Himself is the *wali*, His Prophet is the *wali*, the Imams are the *wali*. The Imams of the Prophet's household have been appointed — twelve Imams. In the rank after that, those who conform to and match particular criteria and qualifications — they have been designated for government and caliphate. The question regarding the designation of the *wali* in Islam is this.

Of course, this was one verse that I mentioned for you; there are other verses in the Qur'an too, some of which we have brought on this sheet, and some you must search out and find for yourselves in your own Qur'an. We have many verses here.

That upon which emphasis is placed in Islam is this: that the rein of the people's affairs not fall into the hands of those who drag human beings to hell. Did history not show us this matter? Did we not see what they did to the Islamic society a little after the radiant[^s26-14] dawn of the beginning of Islam, and what happened, and what befell this society? That society in which the people do not recognize the worth of good men, that society in which the people mistake the criteria of goodness, that society in which the people cannot recognize the one who is their reformer and their compassionate counselor — how much must have been worked upon this society for it to have reached this point and to have acquired such a condition? The poisonous propaganda that was carried out, on the part of the apparatuses of unjust and tyrannical power, within the Islamic society, so changed the horizon of the people's knowledge and insight, did such a thing to these people, that the people were as if they saw black as white and white as black; they had come into such a state. It was for this reason that, in the second and third centuries of the Hijra, when one looks at history, one sees the atrocities of the apparatus of power and caliphate, and one sees the heedlessness and indifference of the people, and one is astonished. Are these the same people? Are these the same people who, before ʿUthman, did not show patience, who surrounded him from all sides and then, finally, with that gruesome circumstance, removed and deposed him from the caliphate? Are these the same ones? Are these the same ones who now, on the wedding night of the Abbasid caliph, see what enormous expenditure — which could have administered a vast section of the Islamic society — is being spent, see on what debaucheries it is being spent, for what kinds of things it is being consumed, how the treasury of the Muslims (*bayt al-mal*) is being spent on personal matters? I am not concerned with whether it is right or wrong; it is a personal matter — it is money for a thousand persons, and a single person spends this money only on himself. We do not say he spends it on his own debauchery — no indeed, even if he spends it on his own prayer and fasting, is this act permissible? And they saw that, in the very fabric of the reality of the Islamic society, this act was being done, and yet, at the same time, they were heedless and uninformed.

Perhaps I have once said this: that on the wedding night of Jaʿfar al-Barmaki, the beloved vizier of Harun al-Rashid — he was a young man of twenty-eight or nine, thirty years old, Jaʿfar al-Barmaki. When Jaʿfar al-Barmaki departed from the world, he was thirty-four or five years old, when they killed him. At the peak of his popularity Jaʿfar became a son-in-law — Harun al-Rashid too had abundant affection for Jaʿfar al-Barmaki — on the wedding night, in place of the sweets (*nuql*) that they scatter over the bride and groom, the noble, high-ranking guests of the rank of the Barmakis and Harun al-Rashid saw that some things were scattered over the bride and groom. When they went near and picked them up — well, they had been scattered, they picked them up, they picked up two, three, five — they saw, yes, these are not sweets, nor are they coins. They looked carefully: they are delicate little boxes the size of a fingertip; they are boxes, made of pure gold; then, when you opened the box, from inside it came out a paper, a very thin and delicate paper. When you unfolded this paper, you saw — how astonishing! — it became a large sheet. You read the sheet, and you saw that on this sheet, the decree of ownership of such-and-such a part of the country had been given to you. It is a decree of land-grant (*iqṭāʿ*),[^s26-15] it is a decree of fiefdom (*tiyul*).[^s26-16]

In a single night, God knows, five hundred, eight hundred, a thousand decrees of fiefdom on very thin papers, in golden boxes, were scattered over a bride and groom, and people picked them up whom the caliph did not know who they would be. For example, suppose that the estates of the broad plain of such-and-such a region fell to a child, fell to the hand of a drunkard, fell to the hand of a knife-wielder, fell to the hand of an incompetent man. These the caliph no longer knew; he had scattered them — whoever took whatever, plundered it, picked it up, went off, devoured, carried away. But in that region whose land-grant has now fallen to the hand of this man, how many people will be trampled, how much wealth will be wasted, how many rights will be lost, ruined and trampled — these they did not think about. In those very circumstances, class disparity is to this degree; in those very circumstances in which these gifts and grants are being made, these extravagances and luxuries[^s26-17] are being carried out — Yahya the Alawi[^s26-18] was, in the mountains of Tabaristan, fighting against injustice and oppression, while he himself and his wife had only one single garment, which the husband would take at the time of prayer and wear, then would give to the woman so that she might cover her body and perform the prayer. The household of the Prophet, who were fighting against injustice, were passing their lives in such a condition — and these people saw, and were indifferent.

My complaint is not against Harun, there is no grievance against Harun. If Harun does not do these things, he is not Harun. Harun's class dictates that, as long as this class exists, it does such work; there is no grievance against him. The grievance is against the people who had forgotten the sensitivity of the early days of Islam, who had lost the alertness and understanding that they had in the early days of Islam, who, in the face of this situation, did not feel any duty, did not feel any responsibility, did not feel any pain. What is this for? It is because the harmful, malicious, vile propaganda had so worked upon the people. These propagandistic hands and propaganda apparatuses, in the regions[^s26-19] of the Islamic society and the Islamic country, had for years worked upon the people's brains, upon the people's souls, upon the people's spirits — they had worked and worked until it had come to this point. So you see how important it is who the ruler of the Islamic society is. He must be the one whom God designates.

> **Qur'an, al-Nisāʾ (4:59):** "Obey Allah"[^s26-20] — obey God — "and obey the Messenger" — obey the Messenger — "and those in authority among you" — and the possessors of command from among yourselves.

What does "the possessors of command" (*ulu al-amr*) mean? The ignorant, foolish one who bears the name of Muslim imagines that "possessor of command" means anyone who can give commands, anyone whose force prevails for giving commands; he says this is the *ulu al-amr*. We say: no, that is not the *ulu al-amr*. If it were to be that whoever can give a command is the *ulu al-amr* and is recognized as legitimate by the Qur'an, well, then in such-and-such a mountain, such-and-such a dagger-girt bandit with a mustache stretching to his ears also gives commands — he too is the all-powerful one there; so is he there the *ulu al-amr*? And is obedience to his command obligatory?

The one who commands everyone that, for three days and three nights, only women should come out of the houses and the men have no right to come out of the houses — this was instituted by one of these very grandees of the Safavid era, according to the account of some of the histories, if it is true; in the Safavid era he instituted it, and it became instituted in Isfahan. An eyewitness of the episode wrote a [book of] history — now I do not know whether that history is reliable or not — only women should come out, in the covered markets and caravanserais and shops and bazaars and everywhere and everywhere, women and girls should come and work, the men should not come. Is there to be no man in this city? Well, are the women comfortable without strangers (*na-maḥram*)? No indeed — the master himself and his friends and companions and those around him, they may come. The people whose mother and sister are in the bazaar — they have no right to come; these may come. Well, is the command of this one the command of God? Is he the *ulu al-amr*? Can one obey this? The *ulu al-amr* in which the Shia believes is that *ulu al-amr* whose charter of command God has issued in his name; this is what we want. It is that human being who, even though he is "from among you" (*minkum*), is among the other human beings, yet has received the Wilayat from God — for the possessor of the supreme Wilayat (*wilāyat al-kubrā*) is God — has received it from God.

Now, Harun al-Rashid, with that condition of his, with that "broken neck"[^s26-21] of his, with that lavishness and extravagance, with that man-killing of his — who massacred this very Jaʿfar al-Barmaki and many of his household in a single day, with slaughter and uprooting and extermination, and many of the believers and many of the Muslims; and, to as much as God wills, other deeds — well, this is the one whom Abu Hanifa used to say is the *ulu al-amr*. With Imam Jaʿfar al-Sadiq, too, there was always strife and war and dispute, for precisely this reason: they used to say, "Sir, why do you fall out with the *ulu al-amr* of your time?" The *ulu al-amr* of the time meaning this same notorious Harun — you noticed?

So the logic of the Shia in this question is a very subtle and precise logic. While it senses and infers the appointment by God from the Qur'an, it also gives the criteria and qualifications into the hands of the people, so that the people may not be deceived. They should not say, "Very well, ʿAli ibn Abi Talib — upon our head, upon our eye, we accept him; and now too the successor of ʿAli ibn Abi Talib is Harun al-Rashid." Yes, they should not speak in this way — as in fact this speech used to be spoken. The Banu al-ʿAbbas had a chain of descent which, on occasion, also reached ʿAli ibn Abi Talib or reached the Prophet! They should not say, "We accept ʿAli ibn Abi Talib, and his successorship too finally reached Harun al-Rashid — it reached, by succession, all the way to Harun al-Rashid." Mansur the Abbasid used to say, "We accept Imam Hasan, the caliph; but he took money and sold the caliphate." It is the logic of Mansur the Abbasid, isn't it! Since he sold the caliphate, he therefore has no right to the caliphate. And we too, from those to whom the caliphate had been sold, took the caliphate by force — it belongs to us. This is their argument. So they would even accept ʿAli ibn Abi Talib, would set him upon their head, upon their eye, by outward appearance; yet, at the same time, they saw no contradiction between the government of ʿAli ibn Abi Talib and the government of Harun the Abbasid.

The Shia says: no, this speech is not correct. The Shia says: you who accept the government of ʿAli ibn Abi Talib must also accept the criteria of caliphate and Wilayat. You must accept that ʿAli ibn Abi Talib was chosen as *wali* because of the assembling of these criteria; so if someone — these criteria were not in him, or the opposite of these criteria was in him — this man has no right to introduce himself as the successor of ʿAli ibn Abi Talib. He has no right to claim the Wilayat of the Shia, the being of the *waliyy al-amr*; and no one has the right to accept it from him. This is the first matter that arises in this domain, around the question of Wilayat.

Of course, the second matter too we have indicated here, and we have also written its verse. What is the Wilayat of God for? If someone should ask this — should say, "Sir, on what proof do you say: why is the Wilayat of the affair in God's hands and for God? Why?" — its answer is this: that this arises from a natural philosophy that has been specified and determined in the worldview of Islam. In the worldview of Islam, everything in the world arises from the power of the Lord:

> **Qur'an, al-Anʿām (6:13):** "And to Him belongs whatever rests in the night and the day."[^s26-22] — Whatever, in the day and the night, is at rest and still, belongs to God.

Well, the one to whom all the phenomena of creation belong, and in whose hand the creational rule over everything lies, the legal and legislative (*tashriʿi*) rule too must lie in his hand — there is no alternative. This too is the second matter, until we reach its subsequent matters. Now pay attention; let me, quickly, give the meaning of these verses.

> **Qur'an, al-Nisāʾ (4:58–60):** "Indeed, Allah commands you to render the trusts to those to whom they are due"[^s26-23] — indeed, God commands you to return the trusts to those to whom they belong — "and when you judge between people to judge with justice" — and when you judge and rule and adjudicate among the people, judge and adjudicate according to justice and equity — "Indeed, Allah — excellent is that with which He counsels you" — indeed, the Lord — how excellent a thing it is with which He admonishes and counsels you — "Indeed, Allah is ever Hearing and Seeing" — indeed, the Lord is Hearing and Seeing.

That which He commands you is out of complete hearing and awareness and knowledge. Because He is the Hearer of your inner needs, because He is the Seer of your destiny, therefore that which you need, He gives and bestows upon you.

This — from the first verse, which speaks about the trust and says "render the trusts to those to whom they are due" — is, in reality, a preparation of the ground for the second verse. The trust is not only that I have one toman of yours in my hand, "Return this one toman to me." The most important examples of the trust is this: that which is God's trust among the people, the human being should convey it to its place and to those to whom it is due. The obedience of the human being, which is the covenant and pact of God with man, that he should expend it and spend it in its place, that he should obey God and obey the one whom God has commanded to be obeyed. This is the most important instance of the trust.

> Then He says: "O you who have believed" — O you who have believed — "obey Allah" — obey God — "and obey the Messenger" — and obey the Messenger of God — "and those in authority among you" — obey the possessors of command from among yourselves; those who, among you, are the possessors of command.

Here the Islamic thesis (*tez*), with some of the other theses and ideas, makes its differentiator[^s26-24] and point of divergence apparent. The Islamic thesis does not say that there will be a day when government is not necessary — no; the Islamic idea does not foresee that day on which, in society, there is no state and government — no indeed; whereas some schools foresee that day on which society is the ideal society, and one of its characteristics is that there is no longer state and government in that society. No; Islam does not foresee this.

The Khawarij, under the pretext of divine government, were the ones who used to say that ʿAli ibn Abi Talib must not be. They used to say, "*lā ḥukma illā li-llāh*" — "rule belongs to none but God." The Commander of the Faithful, in answer to this, says: "*kalimatu ḥaqqin yurādu bihā bāṭil*"[^s26-25] — a word, a true word, but falsehood is intended by it. The speech is correct: the ruler is truly God; the one who gives the regulations and takes the rein of life into His hand is truly God. But you say "*lā ḥukma illā li-llāh*" or you say "*lā imrata illā li-llāh*" — law and rule belong to God; who is the executor of the law? Do you also say that other than God, no one should be the executor of the law? Then, therefore, in answer to this speech, He says: "*lā budda li-l-nāsi min amīr*" — in the end, humanity and human society needs a commander (*amīr*), needs a ruler and sovereign. This is human nature: that one's life must be accompanied by an executor of the law; the existence of the law is not sufficient — there must be someone to execute this law and to oversee the precise and correct execution of this law. This is what it says:

> "And those in authority among you"

— however, the *ulu al-amr*: is it the absolute *ulu al-amr*? Whoever commands is correct? Sometimes it may happen that, in a single place, two persons command against each other — are both of them *ulu al-amr*? Sometimes it may happen that a man commands whom the human intellect and reason negates as a commander — is he still *ulu al-amr*? It is here that, between us and the mode of thought of Sunnism, an essential disagreement arises. We say: the *ulu al-amr*, the possessor of command, is that possessor of command who conforms to the divine criteria. Whereas they, in practice, do not impose such a condition. Now, what is in their jurisprudential books, I have not made a proper reference to see. That which is widespread on tongues and in mouths is this: that no — whoever reaches the station of command and sovereignty is respected and authoritative for them.

> "And if you should dispute over anything" — so if, in anything, there was a dispute and disagreement — "refer it to Allah and the Messenger" — refer it to God and the Messenger. "If you should believe in Allah and the Last Day" — if you are believers in God and the Last Day — "That is best" — this is better — "and best in outcome" — it has a better end.

You see, He directs the people to the good consequences of the sovereignty of the good, and the evil consequences of the sovereignty of the bad. This too is one verse.

Following it is the third verse. It is a reproach to those who turn their heads away from this general command:

> "Have you not seen those who claim that they have believed in what was revealed to you" — Do you not look at those who imagine that they have believed in what was revealed to you, in your religion — "and what was revealed before you" — they imagine that they have faith in your religion and the religion of your predecessors, the heavenly religions; they suppose themselves believers. Despite supposing themselves believers, an act issues from them that is contrary to faith in God.

What is that? "They wish to refer judgment to the Tāghūt" — they wish to take their disputes for arbitration (*taḥākum*) to the Tāghūt; that is, for the resolution and settlement of their own affairs they refer to the Tāghūt, they seek the Tāghūt's view, they take command, and according to his view, his command, they conduct their lives. This is contrary to faith:

> "They wish to refer judgment to the Tāghūt" — they strive, they wish, to refer judgment before the Tāghūt — whereas — "while they were commanded to reject it" — whereas these have been commanded to disbelieve in and reject the Tāghūt. "And Satan wishes to lead them far astray" — and Satan wishes to lead them astray, a misguidance and bewilderment exceedingly far.

I consider it probable that this Satan is the very Tāghūt itself, nothing else. These people wish to go after the Tāghūt, heedless that this Tāghūt-Satan — this Satan that is known under the title of Tāghūt in the Qur'an — leads them far from the straight path, until it takes their heads out into the valleys of the wilderness (*tīh*)[^s26-26] of bewilderment, out of misguidances. The Tāghūt, the Satan, throws them so far from the road that their return is no matter of a bit or two; it is with much difficulty that they may, then, return to the straight path of guidance. This is a verse of the Qur'an.

Of course, the next matter that we have written here, I present exactly as I have written it: "The Wilayat of God and its acceptance on the part of the believers arises from a philosophy that has been ordained in the worldview of Islam, and therefore it is a natural matter." That is, this that we say — that one must obey God, and the *waliyy al-amr* is God — this has a clear natural philosophy. Because everything belongs to God. Which the verse:

> **Qur'an, al-Anʿām (6:13):** "And to Him belongs whatever rests in the night and the day"[^s26-27]

— this matter it clarifies properly.

God willing, tomorrow we will again continue the sequel of this discussion.

---

[^s26-1]: Please refer to the mimeographed sheet at the end of the session.

[^s26-2]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, verse 68.

[^s26-3]: (t-b-ʿ) *tatabbuʿ*: to follow after, to investigate.

[^s26-4]: (q-h-r) *qāhira*: overpowering, dominant.

[^s26-5]: (ẓ-l-l) *ẓill*: shade, shadow.

[^s26-6]: (m-sh-y) *mamshā*: manner of conduct, way of proceeding.

[^s26-7]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah, verse 55.

[^s26-8]: (ṭ-l-q) *iṭlāq*: generalization, inclusion (i.e., the term being absolute/unqualified).

[^s26-9]: Short and concise.

[^s26-10]: (ʿ-d-w) *taʿaddī*: to transgress, to exceed bounds.

[^s26-11]: (s-w-y) *musāwāt*: equality, sameness.

[^s26-12]: (ʾ-t-y) *ītāʾ*: giving.

[^s26-13]: (n-w-ṭ) *manāṭ*: the criterion, the basis/aim upon which a thing hinges.

[^s26-14]: (sh-ʿ-sh-ʿ) *mushaʿshaʿ*: radiant, brilliant.

[^s26-15]: (q-ṭ-ʿ) *iqṭāʿ*: a parcel (of land); a land-grant.

[^s26-16]: *Tiyul* (fiefdom): the assignment of land and property to a person by the king, whereby that person provides himself an income through the taxation of that property.

[^s26-17]: *Turaf*: indulgence, luxurious living.

[^s26-18]: Yahya ibn ʿAbd Allah, one of the descendants of Imam Hasan, who in the time of Harun al-Rashid, in various regions of the Islamic government, gathered allegiance (*bayʿa*) for himself and made his call openly in Tabaristan. Harun appointed Faḍl ibn Yahya al-Barmaki to suppress his uprising. After matters became very difficult for Yahya, he requested a guarantee of safety (*amān*) from Harun, and Harun granted him safety. In the end he was martyred in one of Harun's prisons.

[^s26-19]: (q-ṭ-r) *aqṭār*: corners, sides, regions.

[^s26-20]: Sūrat al-Nisāʾ, verse 59.

[^s26-21]: "Broken neck" (*ʿunuq munkasira*) literally means a broken neck, but in idiom it is said of an ill-tempered and sullen person.

[^s26-22]: Sūrat al-Anʿām, verse 13.

[^s26-23]: Sūrat al-Nisāʾ, verses 58 to 60.

[^s26-24]: (m-y-z) *māyiz*: that which separates, the differentiator.

[^s26-25]: Hadith no. 15.

[^s26-26]: (t-y-h) *tīh*: the wilderness in which the Children of Israel were left wandering.

[^s26-27]: Sūrat al-Anʿām, verse 13.


<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 27: Concerning Wilayat (2)

*در پیرامون ولایت (۲) — delivered Tuesday, 23 Mehr 1353 / 28 Ramadan al-Mubarak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:98–100):** "So when you recite the Qur'an, seek refuge in Allah from the accursed Satan. Indeed, he has no authority over those who have believed and rely upon their Lord. His authority is only over those who take him as their guardian and who associate others with Him."

Now, the point that we wish to establish and explain today by means of these verses is this. After we came to understand that every Muslim, and everyone who claims to be a servant of God, must regard the master of his own life — the ruler and the one in charge of all the activity and striving of his entire lifespan — as coming from God, must seek that from God, must surrender his head to the *walī* whom God appoints and designates, and must give the hand of obedience to the *walī* of God. In short: in all the activities of life, one must recognize as governor and ruler only God, and whoever God has chosen to be His own deputy. Of course, we have already discussed this question too — who exactly are those whom God has chosen to be His deputies. We said that they are the prophets, and after the prophets, the *awliyāʾ* — the very ones whom you also know by the name of *awliyāʾ*; that is to say, the divine rulers and governors. However, we said that the *walī* and the divine ruler is designated in one of two ways: either with both name and description, or else not designated by name but designated by a description. These were the matters that became clear in the course of yesterday's discussion, and perhaps somewhat in the day before's as well.

The matter under consideration today is this: if someone does not accept the *wilāyat* of God and goes under the command of other than God, then, first, what is the ruling concerning this person himself? Second, what is the name of this act? Third, what is the outcome of this deed? These are the discussions that we said belong to the topics surrounding *wilāyat*. Although, once we have accepted *wilāyat* in our minds and embraced it, it will then come to be counted among the established principles of practical Islam. Even though, within the framework of the discussion about the *principle* of *wilāyat*, these issues become secondary, peripheral, and subsidiary matters — yet in themselves they are matters of principle.

In the Noble Qur'an, every *wilāyat* other than the *wilāyat* of God has been introduced as the *wilāyat* of the Tāghūt. Whoever is not under the *wilāyat* of God is under the *wilāyat* of the Tāghūt. What does Tāghūt mean? Tāghūt is from the root *ṭughyān*. *Ṭughyān* means to rebel, to transgress — to go beyond that natural and innate boundary and circle of a human being's life. For example, suppose that humanity came into being in order to reach perfection; whoever causes humanity to fall short of reaching perfection is a Tāghūt. Suppose human beings ought to live by the religion of God — this is an innate matter, natural and in accordance with the very disposition of human beings. If someone brings people up in such a way, does something to them, exercises such control over them that they come to live by a religion other than God's, this person is a Tāghūt. A human being must always be in earnest effort and striving to bring his own existence to fruition. Any factor that encourages and incites a human being toward lack of seriousness, toward not striving, toward laziness, toward ease-seeking, toward seeking only comfort — that is a Tāghūt. Human beings must be obedient to the command of God; anything that draws a human being aside from the command of God, takes him out of it, causes a human being to rebel in the face of God — that is a Tāghūt.

So Tāghūt is not a proper name — as some imagine, that Tāghūt is the name of a particular idol. Yes, it is the name of an idol, but that idol is not one specific idol. Sometimes that idol is you yourself; sometimes that idol is your money; sometimes that idol is your ordinary, comfort-seeking life; sometimes that idol is your object of desire; sometimes that idol is the person into whose hand you placed your own hand, closing your eyes and bowing your head so that he may take you wherever he takes you. Sometimes this idol is gold and silver — it is inanimate, it is metal; sometimes it is a human being — it is alive; it is a social order; it is a law. Tāghūt, therefore, is not a proper name. (Of course, in connection with the social classifications I mentioned in the discussion of prophethood — the way one infers it from among the Qur'anic verses — alongside the *malaʾ* and the *mutrafīn* and the *aḥbār* and the *ruhbān*, the Tāghūt is the rank above all these. That is a different interpretation and a different place, which we are not discussing for now.) Therefore, anyone who has left from under the *wilāyat* of God has inevitably entered into the *wilāyat* of the Tāghūt and of Satan.

What is the relationship between Satan and the Tāghūt? Do they have a relationship with each other? Yes — and they have more than a relationship. Satan is the very Tāghūt; the Tāghūt is the very Satan. The verse of the Qur'an says this. I want to recite the verse only for a verbal inference, so that it may become clear that Tāghūt is equivalent to Satan. The verse of the Qur'an says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:76):** "Those who believe fight in the way of Allah" — those who have believed, who have become believers, their fighting and struggle is in the way of God; "and those who disbelieve fight in the way of the Tāghūt" — those who have disbelieved in and rejected the religion, their fighting, their struggle, their war is in the way of the Tāghūt. Then He says: "So fight against the allies of Satan" — fight, oppose, and combat the *awliyāʾ*, the comrades, the adherents, and the dependents of Satan; "indeed, the plotting of Satan is weak" — for the scheme of Satan, the contrivance of Satan, the trickery of Satan, is weak.[^s27-1]

In this verse you see that Tāghūt is named in place of Satan, and Satan in place of Tāghūt. Satan is every element that, from outside the human being's existence, drives him toward wicked deeds, toward corrupting deeds, toward degradation, toward baseness, toward abjectness, toward oppression, toward evil, toward crookedness — this is called Satan. We have satans among humankind, satans among the jinn; we have a satan among kin and women, a satan among the great, a satan among strangers, a satan among the emotions — this is what is called Satan. One of the instances and examples of it is Iblīs, who, in the face of Adam, the Chosen of God, stood up in opposition and independence, made his claim of superior knowledge, and said those words. Whatever curses you and I utter throughout our lifetimes, we curse that poor wretch, the first Satan — whereas Satan was not only him; perhaps it did not begin with him, nor did it end with him. There are satans without number in the world. They can even be touched, they come within reach, they come within sight; a human being is sometimes even in their company. Satan is Satan. Whatever drags a human being from the path of God toward corruption and wickedness and evil and degradation is Satan.

Every *wilāyat* that is not God's is a satanic and *ṭāghūtī* *wilāyat*. Whoever does not live under the command of the true *walī* must know that he is living under the command of the Tāghūt and of Satan. You may ask: Sir, what harm is there in living under the command of Satan and the Tāghūt and submitting to his command? This too is one of the points of today's verses. In this regard the Qur'an gives us several answers.

The first answer is this: if you submit to the *wilāyat* of Satan, Satan will gain mastery over all the creative, generative, fruit-bearing energies of your existence. That is the first point. If you turn your neck toward Satan and the Tāghūt, so that he casts the rope of his *wilāyat* around your neck, you will no longer find any deliverance from his hand. Whatever exists within you — of strength, of initiative, of constructive activity, and of radiant manifestations — will come under the grip of the Tāghūt and of Satan. And once your entire existence has come into his grip, he can then easily drag you and take you along the very road that he himself wants, to the very place that he himself wants, by the very means that he himself wants. And it is plain that Satan and the Tāghūt do not guide a human being toward light, knowledge, ease, welfare, and spirituality. These things are not his aim. For Satan and the Tāghūt, his own personal interests are the primary aim, and he wants to secure them; so he puts you to work in the path of his own personal interests.

If you pay attention, this sequence I have laid out — these words and these sentences, which are carefully calculated, with a meaning beneath every sentence — you will see how greatly it corresponds with the historical realities, from the beginning of recorded history down to today. If you have submitted to the *wilāyat* of the Tāghūt, all your forces, energies, initiatives, and talents fall into the grip of the Tāghūt; and once they have fallen into the grip of the Tāghūt, they are not put to work for your benefit, because your benefit is of no concern to the Tāghūt and Satan. For Satan, he himself is what matters; his interests are what matter; his path is what matters. If, on that path and for those interests, you are sacrificed, then you are sacrificed; if you are led astray, then you are led astray. If, to secure those interests, it becomes necessary for you to be sacrificed, then you must be sacrificed; if it becomes necessary for you to be led astray, then you must go astray. He is Satan, after all; the power is in his hands, and you, who have given yourself into his hands, are dragged wherever it pleases him.[^s27-2]

This is the verse of the Qur'an — this verse that I am reciting for you from Sūrat al-Nisāʾ — a verse very worthy of attention and reflection. Even I myself, although I do not have the habit — that is, I am less in the habit of reading the Qur'an without attention and reflection — at times, when a greater reflection is applied and I find and sense something fresh in the Qur'an, I am all the more grieved that we still reflect so little upon the Qur'an. This verse, which we have written down here, which we have rendered, and which I will also explain now:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:115):** "And whoever opposes the Messenger after guidance has become clear to him" — whoever rises up in dispute and contention with the Prophet after the path of guidance has become clear to him, separating himself from the Messenger, branching his road off from the road of prophethood, the very road that we earlier mapped out for you; "and follows other than the way of the believers" — and follows a way other than the way of the believers and the Islamic community and the goals of faith, withdrawing himself from the company of the upright, purposeful, directed Muslim; "We give him over to what he has turned to" — that very thing which he himself has cast around his own neck, We make fast upon his neck. That very *wilāyat* which he himself accepted with his own hand, We make the fixed coloring of his wretched life. With his own hand he had gone to that region and taken up residence there; We make him stay right there, broken-footed. "Indeed, Allah does not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves."[^s27-3] You yourself went and gave your reins over to Satan; so let these reins remain in the hand of Satan. This is Our way; this is the law of creation.[^s27-4]

This — for this world of yours — and for that world of yours, what is the consequence? "And We will burn him in Hell"; from here, once you have closed your eyes, you will go straight to the Hell of the Lord's wrath and the everlasting torment of God. When a human being looks at history, he sees that it is exactly so. These matters are matters that are very important; these are important social matters. We have worked very little on the Qur'an from the standpoint of these matters, and we have applied them very little to the history of Islam. And how good it would be if people who possess the gift of reflection — those interested in the Qur'an, and especially in the social and historical matters of the Qur'an — would reflect and ponder more; and then, if they would apply these to historical realities, it would become clear which interpretations and historical accounts conform to reality. This historical piece is an interpretation of this verse.

Of course, you know it is not my custom in gatherings of this kind to recount history; today I want to recount a bit of history. The city of Kufa is one of the most remarkable cities in the history of Islam. You hold several kinds of memories of Kufa. What I am about to say is nothing new concerning Kufa. You remember that Kufa is the place that the Commander of the Faithful, among all the Islamic lands and the cities attached to the great Islamic realm, chose for his caliphate. This is one positive point, as the gentlemen say.
