Again you remember that the people of Kufa took part in the wars of the Commander of the Faithful: they brought the Battle of the Camel to its conclusion; they finished the Battle of Nahrawan; in the Battle of Ṣiffīn too it was the tribes around Kufa and certain other tribes — and these very fighting men of that city — who brought that war to its conclusion, to where it reached. Again, in another place, you remember that it was these very people of Kufa about whom the Commander of the Faithful would complain and grumble: why is it that when I say, "Come to battle," you do not come? Again, it was these very people whose notables wrote letters and went into the service of Imam Hasan al-Mujtaba (the blessings of God be upon him): "Sir, come; we will hand this city over to you completely, at your disposal" — yet he did not come. Again, it was these very people whose notables wrote a letter to al-Husayn ibn ʿAli (peace be upon him): "Indeed, we have no Imam"[^s27-5] — we have no Imam, we have no leader, we have no ruler, and God has set this tyrant in authority over us; so you come. And they were telling the truth: Sulayman ibn Ṣurad, Habib ibn Maẓāhir, Muslim ibn ʿAwsaja, and others.[^s27-6] And again, it was these very people who came, in a most unequal and unbalanced battle, and lined up against al-Husayn ibn ʿAli (peace be upon him), and that catastrophe took place. Again, it was these very people who, a little later, brought about an episode in history that is among the very rare and glorious examples in the history of Islam: the episode of the *Tawwābūn* (the Penitents). They went out with the intent of going to sacrifice their lives and to repent for not having reached the episode of ʿĀshūrāʾ and the event of Karbala. And again, it was these very people among whom the seed of most of the revolts of the Umayyad and the Abbasid periods was sown, and sprouted, and came to fruition. How many martyrs they gave, how many slain they gave, how many splendid, brilliant, glory-bringing, glory-displaying deeds they brought forth from themselves! And again, it is these very people in whom — in certain affairs — you find slackness and laziness and weaknesses and the spiritual and intellectual incapacities that meet the eye.

What is this, then? Did these people have two kinds of character, two spirits, two faces? The very ones to whom Zaynab the Great says: "O people of Kufa, O people of deceit and treachery,"[^s27-7] recalling them as a deceitful folk.[^s27-8] This is a problem; the matter of Kufa is a problem. In my view, a study of Kufa and a discussion of the psychology of the city of Kufa in history would be a very interesting discussion — if someone were equal to this task. Let the specialists, the sociologists, the psychologists sit down and talk about Kufa, think about it, discuss it, and see what a strange creature this is: in one place, all that astonishment in displaying the great manifestations of humanity; in one place, all this slackness and laziness and incompetence. What is it, then? Are the Kufans two classes? Does the society of Kufa have two aspects and two faces?

Kufa is a region that was so shaped and so tempered under the command of the dignified, eloquent words of the Commander of the Faithful, that whatever it nurtures, whatever it brings up green, is a sapling fit to be cultivated. This sowing-ground — its every shoot, its every nurtured thing, its every seedling — is altogether of fertile soil and life-giving climate; the very ground and air are human-making. That is why the great epic-creating men of the history of Shiism are, more than anywhere else, even more than Medina, in Kufa. On account of what? On account of the teachings and the instilling of the Commander of the Faithful during those very four years. It is no trifle that someone like ʿAli ibn Abi Ṭālib should govern in a city. It is true that this government, in the span of four years, at the level of the Islamic world was a failure; but at the level of Kufa it was certainly a success. In the society of Kufa it surely had wondrous and remarkable effects. Kufa thus became a cradle of Shiism, the birthplace of Shiite authenticities and virtues. Is that right?

But wherever there is a birthplace of authenticities, it is not necessary that everyone who is there be authentic and virtuous and ideal people. One class of people are always, in a passionate and tumultuous society, the showcase of the passion and tumult of that society. Among millions of people, sometimes several tens of thousands, even several thousand human beings, perform some heroic deed of their own such that the name of those several million becomes fruitful in the world for heroism and passion-creating and epic-creating. There was one class among the people of Kufa who were striking. Not a class in the sociological sense, no — rather a stratum, an assembly, a group, a streak of people who were like this. But the mass of the people, the common people, were like the people everywhere else; they were no worse. Like the people of Mashhad, like the people of Tehran, like the people of Isfahan, like the people of Medina, like the people of everywhere else. But because that small group, in that corner of the Islamic realm — namely Kufa — were a source of dread and terror to the governments of the time, the governments would always appoint the worst elements, the vilest governors, the basest of their own men, their lackeys and their executioners, over that city; and they would do such things to those people — whether by way of severity and suppression, whether by way of poisonous propaganda, whether by way of spreading poverty and destitution among them — that the mass of the people would, unconsciously and unawares, develop a greater pull toward corruption and degradation.

For whose sake did they do this? For the sake of that fighting group, that elite group, that existed in this city and did not exist in other cities. For the sake of crushing the ground that those pure, pure-natured, great human beings might have been able to make use of. So that they could not make use of it, the rulers would go and ruin that ground entirely. They would carry out poisonous propaganda, would put the people under pressure, would put them under suffocation, would weaken the people financially — in short, by all sorts of means they would place the people of the city of Kufa in conditions in which the people of other cities were not. And it was precisely on account of this that the common people, under the influence of the activity of the oppressive, treacherous, tyrannical apparatuses, performed certain deeds that were unbecoming; but their source was not the wickedness of the people of that city. This is a brief account concerning Kufa. In this field, if some people are able to think and study, in history, in my view they will come upon interesting things.

I want to explain the verse for you. Hajjaj ibn Yusuf was sent to Kufa by the Umayyad caliph ʿAbd al-Malik, because he knew that no one but Hajjaj was equal to the task of dealing with the rebellious, epic-creating people of Kufa. He sent the most cruel and the basest of his own lackeys, and that was Hajjaj ibn Yusuf. Hajjaj ibn Yusuf came; he entered the city in the middle of the night. No one realized that Hajjaj had arrived. The previous governor, too, the people had apparently expelled, or something like an expulsion. He had brought with him a hundred swordsmen, or thirty or forty men. (Today we want to recount history. When I recount history I tire less. These are the last days of the month of Ramadan; I am tired, you are tired too; we want to recount history.) He came and stationed his men around the mosque of Kufa, where the murmuring of the worshippers at the prayer-niches, the night-vigil keepers, and the pious could be heard. He himself slipped into a corner, off to one side, among the crowd. He gave the necessary instructions to his slaves and lackeys.

The people in the mosque of Kufa, at the first call to the dawn prayer, came and gathered to pray the dawn prayer with the imam. They came for worship and prayer. But it turns out that, as they should have, they were not worshipping with awareness. The proof of it is the very thing I am about to relate. Hajjaj ibn Yusuf came — without the people realizing that he had entered the mosque, or for what he had come and what his intention was. He came and slipped among the crowd, without attracting anyone's attention, and suddenly threw himself up onto the pulpit; he went up there, to the top. And you have seen how large the mosque of Kufa is. The people did not notice at first. And here he was, seated on the pulpit, without uttering a single word. Suddenly one person raised his head and saw that Hajjaj was on the pulpit — of course, he did not recognize him. He said: Who is this on the pulpit? He had also fashioned a strange appearance: he had wrapped around his head a turban of silk, red in color, and he had also bound a veil[^s27-9] over his face — that is, he had unwound the *taḥt al-ḥanak*[^s27-10] of his turban and bound it up to the bridge of his nose, in such a way that only his eyes were visible. They saw that it was a strange figure: an armed man with a sword and a cloak and a red turban, seated this way on the pulpit, in the mosque of Kufa; and the silence drew the people's attention. This one said to that one, that one said to this one; suddenly all of this crowd that was scattered in the mosque — their eyes fell upon him and their attention was drawn. And little by little they were drawn toward the pulpit, to see who this was.

Pay close attention to what the verse of the Qur'an says: "We give him over to what he has turned to." It says that whoever turns aside from the path of faith and the believers — the bond that he has cast around his own neck, We draw tight around his throat. Well — you were a Muslim; on the pulpit of your own mosque a man you do not know is seated. Why do you go there and sit down? You should have gone up and said, "Sir, who are you? Introduce yourself!" The first person, the second person, the third person should have questioned him. Had they asked this question, the situation would have been different. They displayed slackness. They displayed a state of will-lessness and weakness of soul. They sat down so that he might speak.

Then, when he saw that all the people were looking at him, he said: It seems the people of Kufa do not know me. The people glanced at one another — well, it was obvious they did not know him. He said: I will now introduce myself to you. He removed the turban from his head. This veil too was removed. He cast a glance at the people and recited a line of Arabic verse:

> *Anā ibn jalāʾ wa-ṭallāʿ al-thanāyā / idhā aḍaʿ al-ʿimāmata taʿrifūnī*[^s27-11]
>
> "When I lift off my turban, you will recognize me." Some looked — once before, this man had come to Kufa — one or two said: I think it is Hajjaj. A whisper spread: Hajjaj, Hajjaj, Hajjaj. The people were suddenly seized with dread, seized with fear, that Hajjaj had come and was seated on the pulpit. He said: Yes, Hajjaj — yes, yes, you have understood rightly. The people now sat in a state of terror. Here, not one person says to himself: Well now, friend, Hajjaj is one man, I too am a man; he is seated above, I am below; whatever he has, I have too. The people's weakness of soul took hold of them here.

He said: O people of Kufa, I see heads that, like ripe fruit, hang upon the necks, and the time for their plucking has come. I see that it is necessary for a number of heads to be parted from these bodies. The people were all the more terrified by his pronouncements, which were hollow, which were empty. Well — Hajjaj had not gone to Kufa with an atom bomb; and even if he had had an atom bomb, he would not have detonated it, because if he had detonated it, no one would have remained for him to rule over. It was necessary that a number remain alive; he would not kill them all. If he killed them all, over whom would he rule? Over the walls and doors? What pleasure is there in that? Would he go and rule in the desert? The people did not think of this point.

He said: I see that heads are hanging upon the necks. The time for plucking and severing them has come. Well, now I shall determine whose head must be plucked. Stand up, slave! He called his slave; his slave rose and stood upon the pulpit. He said: Read out the letter of the Commander of the Faithful for these gentlemen. The slave — the letter of the Commander of the Faithful, by which, of course, you know is meant his honor ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwan! — the slave opened the letter of ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwan, the caliph, and began to read. Its opening was this:

> "In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. From the Commander of the Faithful ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwan to the people of Kufa. O people of Kufa, peace be upon you." — "O people of Kufa, peace be upon you."

He had read to this point when suddenly Hajjaj turned to the slave and said: Quiet! Be still. He fell silent. He turned to the people and said: How very ill-mannered you have become! The Commander of the Faithful is greeting you, and you do not return his greeting. Slave, read from the beginning. The slave began again from the start:

> "From the Commander of the Faithful ʿAbd al-Malik ibn Marwan to the people of Kufa. O people of Kufa, peace be upon you." — Suddenly from the whole mosque a voice rose up: "And upon the Commander of the Faithful, peace." They returned his greeting. A satisfied smile took shape upon Hajjaj's lips; in his heart he said: The work is done. And indeed the work was done. The work of the people of Kufa was finished right there.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:115):** "And whoever opposes the Messenger after guidance has become clear to him, and follows other than the way of the believers, We give him over to what he has turned to."

You accepted Hajjaj? You answered the greeting of "the Commander of the Faithful" — he who is the commander of the disbelievers and the commander of the corrupt? Very well; now that you want him — Hajjaj for you! Now that you are of Hajjaj's persuasion,[^s27-12] God will not, by a miracle, remove Hajjaj and put Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn (peace be upon him) in his place. That same Hajjaj for you, until such time as you no longer want Hajjaj. As long as you yourselves find Hajjaj to your liking, all of your life and your thought and your spirit will be at Hajjaj's disposal. This is the way of the world of creation; it is the way of history.

This is history. I would have very much liked to recount ten more historical pieces for you, in exchange for the twenty-seven days past on which I did not recount history for you. History is a lesson.

> *Better that the description of beloveds / be told in the discourse of others.*

History is the interpretation of the Qur'an. Find the Qur'an in history.[^s27-13] And know that:

> *For the wise, world-seeing man, two lives would be needed in this age:*
> *that in the one he might gather experience, and with the other put that experience to work.*[^s27-14]

And the experience of history is our very former life. Pay attention to history; become a little familiar with history. But strive to take from history that which lies in its heart. Do not content yourselves with merely versifying a tale, telling a story out of history. See what history wants to tell us. What does the episode of Hajjaj tell us in history?

It is not amiss for me to add this word too: that this very Hajjaj, at the hands of the very people for whose sake he was committing these atrocities, met his end in the most atrocious manner. It is also not amiss for you to know this: "Whoever aids an oppressor, God sets that very oppressor in authority over him."[^s27-15] This too is another way. See what lesson history holds for us, what word it has for us, what message, what counsel. Let us delve into history with the utmost attentiveness; then we will see that the verse of the Qur'an comes to have meaning for us. I have recounted this history; its connection and link with the verse of the Qur'an, I leave to your own minds.

Let me explain the verses for you:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:98):** "So when you recite the Qur'an" — when you have recited the Qur'an; "seek refuge in Allah from the accursed Satan" — then seek refuge in God from the harm of the outcast Satan. Now that you have recited the Qur'an, now that you have learned the teachings of Islam — O brother, you who until now did not understand, now that you have understood — keep yourself safe from the satanic harm that wants you not to know and not to understand the Qur'an. What does "keep yourself safe" mean? It means: strive that he not take from you the Qur'anic knowledge that has been heaped up in your heart; that he not close upon you the road of action and the road of fuller understanding. "So seek refuge in Allah" — seek refuge in God — "from the accursed Satan" — from the evil of the outcast Satan.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:99):** "Indeed, he has no authority." Can I, then, take refuge in God? Can I, then, flee from the evil of Satan? Yes. "Indeed, he has no authority over those who have believed and rely upon their Lord." Indeed Satan — the evil-creating, corruption-creating, powerful one — has no mastery or might over those who come to believe in God and put their trust and reliance upon God. Those who place themselves under the *wilāyat* of God, and strive and hasten toward the region of the *wilāyat* of God — over them Satan has no mastery.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Naḥl (16:100):** "His authority is only over those who take him as their guardian." Indeed, the mastery of Satan is only over those who have accepted his *wilāyat*. The mastery of Hajjaj is only over those who accepted his harsh speech, who with their own hand gave the rope around their own neck into his hand. "His authority is only" — solely and only the power and mastery of Satan — "over those who take him as their guardian" — over those who have accepted his *wilāyat*; "and over those who associate others with Him" — and those who have taken him as a partner with God.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:115):** "And whoever opposes the Messenger" — whoever separates himself, whoever wars in contention with the Prophet; "after guidance has become clear to him" — after the path of guidance has become clear and manifest to him, and yet he separates himself from the path of the Prophet; he does not submit his body and neck to the commitment of faith, to the profession of prophethood, to faith in prophethood, and to bearing witness to prophethood; "and follows other than the way of the believers" — and follows a way other than the way of the true believers; "We give him over to what he has turned to" — every thing and every one whom he has accepted into *wilāyat*, We make a *walī* and ruler over him. "We give him over" — We make him his ruler, We make him his *walī*; "to what he has turned to" — whatever he himself has accepted into *wilāyat*. "And We will burn him in Hell" — and We hurl him down into Hell — "and evil it is as a destination" — and how evil a fate and end Hell is.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:116):** "Indeed, Allah does not forgive that He should be associated with." God does not pass over the one by whom He is associated with partners. Go back to the discussion of *tawḥīd* and the meaning of *tawḥīd* and *shirk*; see what *shirk* is, what *tawḥīd* is — and what is the sin that God does not pass over, which God does not forgive? "Indeed, Allah does not forgive that He should be associated with." If I want to be more precise and exact, there is still more to say. God does not extend His forgiveness to the one who has accepted *shirk*. The one who has become a polytheist has entered into the *wilāyat* of other than God, has surrendered the domain of God's influence to other than God; those wounds that have settled upon his soul from sin and disobedience, from evil and wretchedness and disorder, will never be healed — that is, he attains no forgiveness. This is the meaning of *maghfirah*, after all; I explained it to you. The forgiveness of sin means the smoothing-over and healing of that wound which arises in the human soul on account of sin and error and slipping and deviation. When it is smoothed over, forgiveness and pardon have included him; when that wound and distress, which had come upon your soul by way of evil conduct, of a wrong course, of not walking the path of God — once it has healed — then you have become forgiven. *Ghufrān* means this. And if you are under the *wilāyat* of other than God, that wound which had arisen from sin will never heal; the blow and kick of sin will never be made good. "And He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills" — but the sins less than and lower than this, He brings under forgiveness for whomever He wills.

Of course, God's willing is not arbitrary either. The one who repents, the one who makes amends, the one who turns toward God — him God wills to place under forgiveness. "And whoever associates others with Allah" — again it comes back to the matter of *shirk*: whoever assumes a partner for God and a peer — "has certainly gone far astray" — has gone astray very, very far from the path of guidance. There is a time when you lose your way from the road in the wilderness, but you are only one kilometer apart from it. There is a time when, in the desert, you lose your way from the road and you have become tens of kilometers apart from the road; returning from that is not easy. It requires greater effort, greater alertness, a stronger guide. Those who have associated partners with God have likewise gone very, very far from the straight and middle path of guidance; "has certainly gone far astray" — they have fallen into bewilderment and a far-reaching misguidance.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:117):** There is a narration in connection with this phrase that those who are people of hadith should consult and examine; I do not relate it.

> "They call upon none besides Him but female deities" — other than God, whatever they call upon are nothing but a few female idols. "And they call upon none but a rebellious Satan" — and they call upon none but God except a Satan who is rebellious and far from virtue and devoid of goodness. We have rendered *marīd* here as "rebellious"; but you may also write, beside "rebellious," "devoid of goodness and virtue" — this too is a meaning of *marīd*.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:118):** "Allah cursed him" — a Satan who is outcast from God. "And he said" — from the very beginning, Satan made his pact with God; the front of Satan and the front of God, by their very nature and disposition, are not capable of reconciliation. "And he said" — He is speaking of the nature of the satan-like ones and the satans; all the satans, and the class of Satan, are essentially this way. "I will surely take from among Your servants an appointed portion." Satan has pledged and said: I will, O God, set apart for myself a fixed portion and an allotted share from among Your servants; that is, I will drag a number of Your servants from the straight path into misguidance; I will seize their reason; I will blind their insight and intellect; and instead of their being under Your *wilāyat*, I will bring them under my own *wilāyat* and rule.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:119):** "And I will surely lead them astray" — I will lead them severely astray. "And I will surely arouse in them desires" — I will bind them severely to far-flung, long desires. Dwell a little upon the phrase "and I will surely arouse in them desires." Far-flung, long desires — that which holds a human being back from every effort in the path of God. The desire to live for another ten years in pleasure and comfort and welfare; the desire to marry off the eldest son and find husbands for the daughters; the desire to enlarge this small two-room house and make it three rooms; to make this one-room shop a two-room one; the desire to become the head and director of such-and-such an office and organization and to draw such-and-such an amount of money; the desire that they call my son "Mr. Engineer," or call me that — far-flung, long desires. Desires that, like a millstone fallen upon a human being's neck, bend the head, double the back, and bring a human being to his knees; desires that, if you draw out the tooth of their greed and cast it away, suddenly you find yourself free, you find yourself light, you find that no shackle or bond has tied your hand and foot in the path of God. "And I will surely arouse in them desires" — I will bind them to far-flung, long desires.

> "And I will command them so they will slit the ears of cattle" — I will command them to slit the ears of the cattle. This is an instance of an ignorant and false custom — though it is possible that a greater secret and mystery lies hidden in this phrase. I have not thought much upon this phrase. I did not have much opportunity to examine all that has been said in this regard; perhaps there is a fresh point in it, though I did not have much hope of that either. But I think it likely that there is a point fresher than what I am stating in this phrase; you too should think about it. The apparent matter is this: the ignorant custom of that time, in the time of the Prophet, dictated that the ear of this animal be cut, snipped, slit, so that by this means — suppose, for example — they might secure sustenance and blessing and well-being for it. It is an ignorant custom. He cites this here as a symbol of the non-divine customs and ideas and methods and programs. See how ridiculous it is; see how hollow this custom is. The satanic customs are all of this sort. In the view of the very followers of this custom, it is not hollow, but in the view of a rational person it is hollow.

> "And I will command them so they will alter the creation of Allah." This phrase is very striking: "And I will command them so they will alter the creation of Allah" — I command them to alter and transform the creation and the *fiṭrah* and the God-given nature. Those human beings who are under my command — O God, those whom I hunt out from the region of Your governance and *wilāyat*, my Lord, and carry off to the saltmarsh of my own *wilāyat* — I command them, I compel them to set aside the divine creation and *fiṭrah*, to depart from that line of conduct which You appointed for them. I set down laws for them, laws contrary to *fiṭrah*; I give them commands, commands contrary to nature; I place before their feet a road, a road that brings them to a destination other than the natural, human destination. "And I will command them so they will alter the creation of Allah" — I will command them, definitely and decisively, certainly and emphatically, to transform Your creation, Your fashioning, the God-given *fiṭrah*. This is Satan's pact with God.

Satan's pact with God is a pact of this sort, a pact of obstinacy and a pact of defiance against God. "This is how we are, O God!" This is the scroll, the placard of the program of all the satans. The work of all the satans of the world is this very work. Be assured that if a people want to live by the God-given *fiṭrah* and nature, Satan will not allow it; the Satan who holds *wilāyat* over them will not allow it — it is impossible that he should allow it. And by whatever means, those who are under his *wilāyat*, under his *wilāyat* and dominion, he draws them away from God-given creation and *fiṭrah*; because without this, his work does not get done — Satan's satanity goes lame. Therefore, after this, God says — and the address is to me and to you:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:119, conclusion):** "And whoever takes Satan as a guardian instead of Allah" — whoever accepts Satan, instead of God, into *wilāyat*; "has certainly suffered a manifest loss" — has incurred a very plain and evident loss, has suffered injury.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:120):** "He promises them" — Satan makes promises to these people: your future, your lifetime, your life — all lies. "He promises them and arouses desire in them" — and he afflicts them with far-flung desires and fancies. "But Satan does not promise them except delusion" — and Satan gives a human being no promise but delusion and deception and falsehood. And Satan gives them nothing but deluding and beguiling promises. This too is one verse.

The next verse as well — "Allah is the ally of those who believe" — its translation I have written here, with a brief explanation. I ask you to consult it.

## Footnotes

[^s27-1]: Sūrat al-Nisāʾ / verse 76.

[^s27-2]: *A rope the Beloved has cast around my neck / He draws me wherever it pleases Him.* (Persian couplet quoted in the source.)

[^s27-3]: Sūrat al-Raʿd / verse 11.

[^s27-4]: Refer to the polycopy (handout) at the end of the session.

[^s27-5]: Hadith no. 27 — «انه لیس علینا إمام» ("Indeed, we have no Imam over us").

[^s27-6]: These three were counted among the notables of the Shiʿa of Kufa. Habib and Muslim reached Karbala and were martyred fighting alongside Imam Husayn; Sulayman later launched the uprising of the *Tawwābūn* (the Penitents).

[^s27-7]: Hadith no. 47 — «یا اهل الکوفة یا اهل الختل و الغدر» ("O people of Kufa, O people of guile and treachery").

[^s27-8]: (gh-d-r) "deceitful, treacherous." (Lexical note.)

[^s27-9]: A *liṯām* — a face-cover or veil.

[^s27-10]: The continuation of the turban which, after being passed under the chin, is thrown over the shoulder.

[^s27-11]: *Sharḥ Nahj al-Balāghah* of Ibn Abi al-Ḥadīd / under aphorism 475. — "I am the son of one who is manifest and a climber of the mountain passes; when I lift off my turban, you will recognize me."

[^s27-12]: "To one's taste, to one's liking." (Lexical note.)

[^s27-13]: The hemistich "Better that the description of beloveds be told in the discourse of others" is attributed in the source to Iqbal Lahori.

<div style="page-break-before: always;"></div>

## Session 28: Concerning Wilayat (3): Hijrah

*در پیرامون ولایت (۳): هجرت — delivered Wednesday, 24 Mehr 1353 / 29 Ramadan al-Mubārak 1394*

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:100):** "And whoever emigrates in the way of God will find in the earth many a place of refuge and abundance; and whoever goes forth from his house as an emigrant to God and His Messenger, and then death overtakes him — his reward has fallen due upon God; and God is ever Forgiving, Merciful."

The question of *hijrah* (emigration) relates to the question of *wilāyah*, with that breadth in which we have framed the question of wilāyah here. We said that wilāyah consists of forging a firm bond and connection among the elements of the believing ranks with one another, and of severing every form of attachment between the believing ranks and the non-believing ranks; and, at a further level, of forging a most powerful and strong bond between every individual of the believing ranks and that central point and concentrated authority upon whom the administration of the Islamic society rests — that is, the *imam*, the *walī*, the ruler, the leader. Alongside this, we then discussed who the *walī* and leader is within the Islamic society. The Qur'an gave us our answer:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Māʾidah (5:55):** "Your guardian (*walī*) is only God, and His Messenger, and those who believe — those who establish the prayer and pay the alms while bowing."[^s28-1]

— to which we pointed, in connection with the affair of the Commander of the Faithful, may God's blessings be upon him. If we have been able to understand wilāyah with this breadth, and if we do not reduce the matter to the level of the subsidiary issues of wilāyah and to the second degree of wilāyah — as some, without due attention, do reduce it — then the question of hijrah turns out to be one of the corollaries of the question of wilāyah. Why do we say it this way? Because if it is necessary that every human being live within the wilāyah of God and of the *walī* of God — which is what the principle of wilāyah taught us — and if we have accepted that a human being must put all of his energies, all of his bodily, intellectual, and psychic vitalities to work under the will of the divine *walī* and the governor who is *min qibal Allāh*[^s28-2] (from God), and, in short, that with every element of his being a human being must be a servant of God and not a servant of the *Tāghūt* — if we accept and embrace these points, then we have no choice but to accept this too: that if somewhere our existence, our being and all our energies and vitalities, were not under the command of the divine wilāyah but rather under the command of the *Tāghūtī* and satanic wilāyah, then our divine commitment is that we free ourselves from the bonds and shackles of the wilāyah of the Tāghūt — that we rescue ourselves, liberate ourselves, and go beneath the most blessed shade of the wilāyah of God. Coming out of that oppressive wilāyah and entering that just wilāyah — its name is *hijrah*. You see, then, that the question of hijrah is one of the matters that arises in the train of wilāyah. This is why we placed the discussion at the very outset: "Concerning Wilāyah" — that is, in connection with wilāyah we mentioned three topics, and this is the fourth.

Why must a human being flee from beneath the wilāyah of the Tāghūt and Satan? This question that I am raising is very much worth attending to. When I raise the question, I would dearly like you, the friends who are listening, to break down and analyze my question at once in the laboratory of your minds, in such a way that you can give it an answer in accordance with your Islamic and religious learning and accumulated knowledge. Then, if your answer turns out not to coincide with my answer — the answer that I will give — and is at variance with it, then there remains room for discussion between you and me. The question is this: Is it not possible to be within the wilāyah of the Tāghūt and yet be a Muslim? Is it not possible for us to suppose a Muslim who is under the wilāyah of Satan, yet who lives as a servant of the All-Merciful? Is such a thing possible or not? Is it possible that, in a setting where over the whole horizon and across all the regions of human life a non-divine factor is busy ruling — where a non-divine factor is administering the human body, where a non-divine factor is administering human thought, where that same factor is dragging the morale, the affections, and the sentiments of the members of society this way and that — while a human being lives gripped in the fist of the power of such Tāghūtī and satanic factors, that nonetheless he be a servant of God, be a Muslim? Is such a thing possible or impossible? I beg you to set about answering this. You need not answer aloud; prepare an answer to this question in your minds; see whether it is possible or not possible. In order for us to know whether it is possible or not, we must analyze and interpret this very question a little, so that the answer may become clear.

We ask: can a person be under the wilāyah of Satan and be a Muslim, or can he not? These two questions are, in reality, a single question composed of two parts; let us properly break down these two parts and see what each means.

The first part is that a person be under the wilāyah of Satan. What does it mean to be under the wilāyah of Satan? What does wilāyah even mean? If we place that meaning which we derived for wilāyah from the verses of the Qur'an — a meaning which was supported by several Qur'anic verses — beside this phrase "the wilāyah of Satan," then it becomes clear what the wilāyah of Satan means. The wilāyah of Satan means this: that Satan — in that comprehensive sense of "Satan" of which I have spoken many times — be dominant over all the powers, capacities, initiatives, and works within a person's being. Whatever the person does is along the course that Satan has determined; whatever he thinks is on the path that Satan inclines toward or sketches out. It is like a man who has fallen into the current of a river. A swift water is rushing down in the bed of a flood from the mountainside; a man falls into this river. Of course he does not wish to strike the huge, towering boulders and split his head open; of course he does not wish to be carried off by this water and dropped into a swamp; of course he does not wish to be choked and drowned amid the waves of this water — but, despite not wishing it, this swift current of water is carrying him off against his will. He flails his arms and legs too, clings to this side and that, grasps at everything — but the current of the water is a swift current; against his will it carries him away.

The wilāyah of the Tāghūt and the wilāyah of Satan is just such a thing. This is why the verse of the Qur'an says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ (28:41):** "And We made them leaders (*aʾimmah*) who call to the Fire."[^s28-3] — There exist leaders and chiefs who drag their followers and those under their command toward the Fire of Hell, drag them toward wretchedness.

Another verse of the Qur'an says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Ibrāhīm (14:28–29):** "Have you not regarded those who exchanged the bounty of God for ingratitude and settled their people in the abode of perdition — Hell, which they shall enter? And what an evil place of settlement!"[^s28-4]

Have you not looked upon those people who were ungrateful for the bounty of their Lord? This bounty for which they were ungrateful — what was it? It could be anything. The bounty of power, which is a manifestation of the power of the Lord; worldly powers; the bounty of stewardship and administering the affairs of thousands of human beings; the bounty of having at one's disposal the capacities, thoughts, energies, and abundant powers of human beings — all of these are bounties; these are capitals that could have been a source of good for humanity. The human beings who came under the control of those people referred to in this verse — these human beings could have been great human beings, could have been the elite servants of God, could have reached the highest degrees of perfection; but these people were ungrateful for these bounties, did not put them to work in the way that they ought. Then the verse goes on to say: "*wa aḥallū qawmahum dār al-bawār*" — and they too cast their own people into the abode of perdition; they drove their people and the people under their command into the realm of nonexistence, annihilation, and destruction. "*Jahannama yaṣlawnahā*" — and they drove them into Hell, into which they will be made to slide and be flung. "*Wa biʾsa al-qarār*" — and what an evil abode and place of settlement it is!

This verse was recited by Mūsā ibn Jaʿfar, may God's blessings and peace be upon him, to Hārūn; he made Hārūn understand that "you are that very one who will cast your people and yourself into the worst dwelling place, into the most calamitous of resting-places." Mūsā ibn Jaʿfar recited this meaning to Hārūn explicitly. He asked: "Are we then unbelievers?" — meaning, "do we then not believe in God, in the Prophet, and in the religion?" The Imam, peace be upon him, in answer recited this verse so as to make him understand that the unbeliever is not only the one who says plainly, directly, and explicitly, "No, there is no God; no, the Qur'an is a lie," or "the Prophet, for instance, is a legend." This is one type of unbeliever, and it is the best kind of unbeliever, the one who states his position explicitly so that a person knows him and properly arranges his own stance toward him. The worse unbeliever is the one who is ungrateful for these immense bounties at his disposal, who casts them into the wrong channel — who drags not only himself, but all the human beings under his command, into Hell. The wilāyah of the Tāghūt is just such a thing.

The one who lives under the wilāyah of the Tāghūt has, in reality, as it were no will of his own. I do not say he is utterly devoid of will — now that we have interpreted the verse of the Qur'an, the explanation of the matter becomes clear in the Qur'an — but he is being carried off in the current of the flood. He wants to flail his arms and legs, he cannot. He wants to turn back from the road of Hell; he sees that everyone all around is moving toward Hell, and they drag him along with them. Those places where there is a large crowd — have you been there? Sometimes you want to go this way; the crowd carries you off against your will like a wisp of straw. He wants to be good, to live a good life, to live as a human being, to remain a Muslim and die a Muslim — he cannot; that is to say, the social current is dragging and carrying him off, carrying him off in such a way that he cannot even flail his arms and legs. And even if he does flail, he has accomplished nothing other than the waste of a certain amount of energy. Not only can he not stir his limbs — what is more painful is that sometimes he cannot even comprehend.

These fish that they catch at sea — I do not know whether you have seen this or not. Sometimes thousands of fish are being dragged within a single net toward the shore; from out in the midst of the sea, from several kilometers out, this net is dragging them all forward, and they are unaware. If you were to say to that fish, "Where are you going?", it would think it was going toward some destination by its own choice; but in reality it has no choice; its destination is the very place that is the destination of that fisherman who owns the net. This invisible net of the ignorant order drags a human being along so, drags him so toward those directions in which the steerers of that net incline, that the person does not comprehend where he is going. Sometimes he even imagines that he is heading toward the abode of felicity and salvation — heedless of the fact that no, he is heading toward "*Jahannama yaṣlawnahā wa biʾsa al-qarār*."

Well, this wilāyah is the wilāyah of the Tāghūt, the wilāyah of Satan. This was the first clause of the two clauses out of which the question was composed. We said: Can one live within the wilāyah of the Tāghūt and live as a Muslim? Can one? Now we have understood, in summary, what living within the wilāyah of the Tāghūt means. And if we wish to understand it in detail too, we can turn back again to history.

Look at how, in the time of the Banū Umayyah and the Banū ʿAbbās, the world of Islam moved with such vitality; look at what an immense wave of knowledge and learning arose within the Islamic society of that day, what great physicians appeared, what great translators appeared in that era of dearth of linguistic knowledge and of general world-knowledge, who translated into Arabic the great works of the ancient cultures and disseminated them among the Muslims. In every field — history, hadith, the natural sciences, medicine, astronomy, and even the beautiful and delicate arts — the Muslims of that day became among the most outstanding exemplars; is that not so? — to such an extent that even now, when a man like "Gustave Le Bon"[^s28-5] the Frenchman, or some other writer and orientalist whose eye is dazzled only by these very outward appearances, looks back, he regards the second, third, and fourth Islamic centuries as the centuries of Islam's radiance. "Adam Mez"[^s28-6] wrote a book called *The Civilization of Islam in the Fourth Islamic Century*; he conducts a remarkable lament over this civilization, which in his view is immense. He describes a great civilization in the fourth Islamic century. A French orientalist, a European orientalist, in general, when he looks back at those third, fourth, and second Islamic centuries, his eyes are still dazzled. Why is this? It is because of this: that strange activities, energies, and vitalities issued in that day from the Islamic society. But I ask you: did all this vitality and activity that was carried out in that day ultimately turn out to the benefit of the Islamic society and of humanity? I ask you.

Ten centuries are passing from that era. We have no fanaticism toward that era. Vis-à-vis the non-Muslim world we can say, "Yes" — it was the world of Islam that brought those universities into being, it was the world of Islam that constructed that philosophy, it was the world of Islam that did such and such in medicine and the natural sciences. But, between ourselves, in all truth and fairness: did all that power and energy turn out to be in the right place, at the right time, and to the benefit of humanity and of the Islamic society? After ten centuries, what does the Islamic society have of that legacy? And why does it not have it? Why does it not have it? Why has that scholarly and cultural wealth not remained for us? Why is it that we, as a society that ten centuries ago possessed all that radiance, today do not shine in the world and make no display? Why? Is it not because all that vitality and activity — though it was a human activity — was beneath the signet-ring of the Tāghūt? As the poet said:

> *That signet of Solomon I would not take at any price,*
> *Upon which from time to time there rests the hand of Ahriman.*[^s28-7]

It was these *Ahrimans* (demons) who toyed with the Islamic society. If they did speak, if they translated, it was for the sake of writing in the translation, "In the reign of His Excellency So-and-so the umpteenth Abbasid caliph, Manṣūr or Hārūn or Maʾmūn" — in order to raise up their own names. If, instead of all this activity in the natural sciences, mathematics, astronomy, literature, jurisprudence, recitation, and the rest, they had permitted the ʿAlawī government to take charge, had permitted Imam Ṣādiq to take charge — had they allowed Imam Ṣādiq to take in hand the vitalities and energies — then even if, scientifically and from the standpoint of literature and of those matters that the world today preens and boasts about, they had fallen back a hundred years, it would still have been to the benefit of humanity. Humanity would have grown, Islam would have blossomed, energies and capacities would have been put to work along the right path. Of what use is it that they translate books, that they bring medicine and scientific progress to the utmost degree, but from the standpoint of morality and of social ethics they are so weak that their class discord is still to be counted among the legends of history?

Exactly like the filthy, disgraceful civilization of our own contemporary world. The great world powers preen over their scientific discoveries, preen over their mind-boggling inventions, raise the cocked corner of their cap of pride[^s28-8] to the very sun — "We discovered such-and-such a drug, we produced such-and-such a formula, we accomplished such-and-such a feat." This is from the scientific standpoint; but from the human and moral standpoint, even now, to this very day, they are living in the eras of thousands of years of pre-history. Even now, fabulous riches stand alongside fabulous hungers and poverties; even now there exists the enjoyment by a very small one percent in the world of privileges that exist at the expense of the millions of starving human beings in the deprived countries. They preen over this — and this is exactly the same condition as that great Islamic civilization in the second, third, and fourth centuries; it is the very same matter; in that day too it was just so. Vast scientific progress, but aristocratic excess and indulgence and heedlessness of virtues, of human qualities, and of authenticities; class discord in its severest form, deadly hungers in the face of deadly satieties — in that day too these were evident and to be seen. On account of what were these miseries? Why was it that the Islamic society of that day, with all its scientific vitality and activity, could not be a flower in the garden of virtues and human qualities? Why?

Even now, when we range over the history of the second and third Islamic centuries, those whose names we can recall together with honor and pride, those whose names we can deliver to the world's high stations on the list of the honor-creating men of the world, are those who fought severely against that very civilized order. There is Muʿallā ibn Khunays,[^s28-9] whom they hang in the marketplace and kill; there is Yaḥyā ibn Umm Ṭawīl, whose hands and feet they cut off and whose tongue they sever; there is Muḥammad ibn Abī ʿUmayr,[^s28-10] to whom they administer a hundred lashes; there is Yaḥyā ibn Zayd,[^s28-11] whom they kill at the age of eighteen in the mountains of Khurāsān; there is Zayd ibn ʿAlī, whose corpse they keep on the gibbet for four years. Those whose names we today can deliver with pride to the list of the honor-creating figures of the world of humanity are these men — and these, on that day, had no connection whatsoever with that splendid civilization that Mr. Gustave Le Bon recalls and names; rather, they were the very opposite of that civilization. So you see that when the wilāyah of the Tāghūt and of Satan holds sway over a society, over human beings, and holds the reins of their affairs in its hand, their energies are set to work, their capacities are set flowing — but in what manner? In the manner in which they have been set to work today in the civilized world, and in the manner in which they were set to work in the world of Islam ten centuries, eleven centuries ago. In the manner that, by the logic and authentic values and the human standards and measures, is not worth a copper penny — this is the wilāyah of the Tāghūt.

So then, within the wilāyah of the Tāghūt, can one live as a Muslim? What does living as a Muslim mean, sir? Living as a Muslim means that all the resources, energies, powers, strengths, and capacities of a human being be wholly and without reserve at the disposal of God. Everything of theirs: their wealth at the disposal of God, their lives at the disposal of God, their daily activity and movement at the disposal of God, their night's sleep at the disposal of God, their thought and reflection at the disposal of God. Can you give an example of this? Yes. We have examples both at the level of societies, in the form of a community and a civic order, and we have examples in groups; among the rebellious groups that came out of the Tāghūtī systems and fled and made *hijrah* toward God — there we have examples.

The first — like the society of Medina, in the lifetime of the Prophet. In the lifetime of the Prophet, the society of Medina was a society of servants of God; it was a Muslim society. There, every step you took was in the way of God. There, even its Jew and its Christian, if he lived under the command of Islam, his life was an Islamic life. The Jew under the *dhimmah*[^s28-12] too, the Christian under the protection of Islam too — in the Islamic society they move along the path of Islam. From the standpoint of personal acts, he is a Jew; but from the standpoint of being a member of society, he is a Muslim. Far more a Muslim than that Muslim who lives within an ignorant order. In the time of the Prophet, money in the way of God, the sword in the way of God, the tongue in the way of God, thought and reflection in the way of God, every task of which a human being is capable was in the way of God; the affections and sentiments in the way of God; this is for a society. In the time of the Commander of the Faithful too, it was more or less the same way, because the Commander of the Faithful, may God's blessings be upon him, was, from the standpoint of being a divine ruler and the *walī* of God, no different from the Prophet; but he was the heir of a bad society. He was the heir of disarrays, the heir of disorders; and even the Prophet himself, had he been in the place of the Commander of the Faithful and come twenty-five years later, would certainly have faced the very same difficulties as the Commander of the Faithful. This is in communities.

And as for groups: the group of the Shīʿah around the Imams, peace be upon them, throughout history. The month of Ramadan has come to an end, and we have not reached a detailed discussion of the Imamate; otherwise, in continuation of the discussion of wilāyah, I would have discussed the Imamate. Then, had I been able to conduct this discussion, I would have told you what kind of a unit the Shīʿah were in the time of the Imams. We would have shown what the Imam's, peace be upon him, relations and dealings with the Shīʿah were, and what the Shīʿah's relations and dealings with the society surrounding them were; this would have become clear. For now I will mention it in summary. The Shīʿah, outwardly, lived within the Tāghūtī system, but inwardly they moved precisely in the direction opposed to the Tāghūtī system. Like that very small group who were with Ḥusayn ibn ʿAlī, may God's blessings and peace be upon him, at Karbalā. These had truly cleft the flood and come against the very direction in which the flood was steering them. So, then, in history we have certain examples — both in society and in groups. But ordinary individuals — in general the individual cannot be a Muslim; cannot have his whole being, his resources, his energies, all his powers and forces and capacities under the command of God, while living within such a society as I described earlier — the Tāghūtī society. Such a thing is impossible. If a person lives within a Tāghūtī environment and a Tāghūtī system, some fraction of his being a Muslim is, after all, in the way of the Tāghūt; some portion of his life is, after all, in servitude to the Tāghūt; he cannot be one hundred percent a servant of God.

A noble hadith has been related in *al-Kāfī* in several wordings. The book *Uṣūl al-Kāfī*, which is among the most reliable and most ancient of Shīʿī books, has related this hadith in several phrasings; the one that I shall recite is one of those several phrasings. The gentlemen can refer to *al-Kāfī*, the *Kitāb al-Ḥujjah*; the title of its chapter, apparently, is this: *Bāb man dāna Allāh ʿazza wa jalla bi-ghayri imāmin min Allāh*[^s28-13] (the chapter on whoever serves God while under a leader who is not from God). It says, on the authority of the Imam, peace be upon him, and the Imam on the authority of God, that God Most High has said:

> «I shall surely punish every flock (*raʿiyyah*) in Islam that gave its allegiance (*dānat bi-wilāyah*) to an unjust leader (*imām jāʾir*) who is not from God, even if the flock was, in its own deeds, godfearing and pious; and I shall surely pardon every flock in Islam that gave its allegiance to a just leader from God, even if the flock was, in itself, wrongdoing and evildoing.»[^s28-14]

A remarkable hadith, this. This hadith says that those people who live under the wilāyah of the *walī* of God are people of salvation, even if in personal and private affairs they sometimes have shortcomings, failings, and sins too; and those people who live under the wilāyah of Satan and the Tāghūt are people of wretchedness and torment, even if in personal affairs, in private deeds, they are people of good works and good deeds too. This is very remarkable. Of course, I have said that there are several phrasings, and all the phrasings yield this very same meaning. The hadith is in *Uṣūl al-Kāfī*. Just now I did not refer to it — it seems to me, since I am usually scrupulous about looking at the chains of transmission of the hadiths, I think its chain was a good chain too. Some time ago I saw it; just now I do not recall.

I always used to liken this hadith — I would say it is as though you boarded a car bound for a certain definite point, say, intending to reach Nishapur. If this car is heading toward Nishapur, you will certainly reach the goal. If this car, instead of going toward Nishapur, is going, say, toward Sarakhs or toward Quchan — well, it is certain that you will not reach the goal. Now, in that car that is going toward Nishapur and brings you to the goal, if in the car the people, with courtesy, kindness, and good conduct, with human manners, associate with one another — so much the better; and if they do not associate with one another with human manners and humaneness and goodness and beneficence — in the end they reach Nishapur, they reach the goal; even though along the way they did a little evil, those evils of course will have certain punishments, certain effects, certain consequences; they are compelled to bear those consequences too; but they reach the goal. Contrary to that: the car that is supposed to bring you to Nishapur is going to the point opposite Nishapur; if in this car all the individuals behave toward one another with courtesy, with refinement, with respect, deal with one another with good cheer, and they see that this car, instead of going to Nishapur, is heading toward Quchan, yet in the face of this event and this occurrence they show no reaction whatsoever — these are very good people, very kind to one another, but will they reach the goal or not? It is obvious — no.

In the first example, the driver was a trustworthy driver, a driver who knew the road — he was "*imāmin min Allāh*" (a leader from God) — he brought them to the goal, even though they were of bad character: "*wa in kānat al-raʿiyyatu fī aʿmālihā ẓālimatan musīʾatan*" (even if the flock was, in its deeds, wrongdoing and evildoing). In the second example, the driver was not a driver who knew the road; he was not a trustworthy driver; he was a passion-worshipping driver, drunk, he had lost the road, he had business in Sarakhs and Quchan, he had given precedence to his own want over the wish of the people, he was heading there; these will certainly not reach the goal — even though inside the car the people were very kind to one another, very warm, very good-natured: "*wa in kānat al-raʿiyyatu fī aʿmālihā barratan taqiyyatan*" (even if the flock was, in its deeds, righteous and godfearing) — but in the end, "*la-uʿadhdhibanna*" (I shall surely punish), they will fall into the torment of God, they will not reach the goal. Therefore, in a society administered by the wilāyah of the Tāghūt — like that very car steered by the driving of an untrustworthy man who does not know the road — human beings will not reach the goal and cannot remain Muslims.

What are they to do? What are they to do? Here the verse of the Qur'an gives the answer. The verse of the Qur'an gives the answer to this "what are they to do"; it says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:97–100):** "Indeed, those whom the angels take in death while they were wronging themselves..."[^s28-15]

Those whose souls the angels seize while these wretches have been wronging themselves; those people who wronged their own souls, their own future, their own everything, and are now dying — the angels of God, the agents of the Lord, are seizing their souls. "*Qālū*" — the angels say to them — "*fīma kuntum*" — "In what condition were you? Where were you?" A person might imagine that the angel of heaven, when it sees that this man's condition is so wretched — like that man who has come, that physician, or that surgeon who has come to operate, say, on a patient, and sees that his condition is very bad, that this patient's condition is very lamentable and despair-inducing — says, "Wherever were you living? Why is your condition like this?" I draw this analogy[^s28-16]: that the angels, out of the wretchedness of this poor man's state, out of the badness of his soul, out of the misery and torment that await him, are astonished, and say to him, "Wherever were you living, you poor wretch? Where were you that you wronged yourselves so, and are now departing this world, departing this world as a wrongdoer against your own soul?" "*Qālū fīma kuntum*" — "Where were you? How were you?"

In answer they say — "*qālū*," they say — "*kunnā mustaḍʿafīna fī l-arḍ*": "We, in the earth, among the people where we were living, were among the oppressed and downtrodden (*mustaḍʿafīn*); we were part of the will-less mass." The *mustaḍʿafīn* are that group within a society in whose hands the control of the society does not lie; these are the downtrodden. In the society's course and in its going, in its movement, in its repose, in its orientation, in its activity, they have no say. As I said yesterday, it goes wherever the pullers of the rope desire. So they go, the poor wretches, having no say of their own as to where to go and what to do.

Imagine a handful of small elementary-school children, first grade — and not even at the age of seven, for seven-year-old children today have their eyes and ears far more open than this — imagine a four- or five-year-old child placed in a school, a *maktab*, like the *maktabs* of old. I recall: when we used to come out of the *maktab* toward home, in a group, we did not even understand at all where we were going. Children do not pay attention to what is where. A monitor, say — some older child with a stick — "Go this way, go that way, don't go under a car," or "Do go under the car!" — sometimes he even wishes that this would happen (laughter from the audience) — they do not at all pay attention to where they are going; suddenly they find they are in their own house, say; they find they are in the house of some other student, say. Now if this monitor takes a fancy to lead them around the street a little, they find they are at such-and-such a place, say.

The *mustaḍʿafīn* of the earth are those who, within a society, have no news of the goings-on of the society. They do not know what is where; they do not understand where they are going; and from here where they are going, where they will end up, and who is carrying them, and how one might avoid going, and, now that we have not gone, what we must do next — they understand none of it at all, they do not pay attention at all; just so, they have hung their heads down, like — with all due distinction, with all due distinction, with all due distinction — the millstone horse.[^s28-17] The horse, with all due distinction, of course (laughter from the audience). They have blindfolded its eyes; it keeps pacing, keeps walking. It just keeps going, going, going; it goes round, goes round, goes round. If this animal were capable of understanding anything, at dusk it would imagine to itself that "by now we must be around Paris and thereabouts, we have come such a long way" (laughter from the audience). Then, when they uncover its eye, it sees it is the very same place where it was at first dawn. It does not at all know where it has gone; it does not understand where it is going while it is moving.

The *mustaḍʿafīn* in a society are these — the majority, the uninformed mass of the people. Of course, in societies other than those administered by a sound system — not those societies that set a value on the jewel of humanity; not those societies that hold a dignity for the human being and for the human will; not that society whose leader is the Prophet, of whom, although he is the Prophet, the Qur'an says:

> **Qur'an, Sūrat Āl ʿImrān (3:159):** "And consult them in the affair."[^s28-18]

— even though he is the Prophet, even though he has no need to consult the people, he is nonetheless commanded to consult the people, to hold them dear, to deem them great, to give them personality. Not these societies — such a society does not have such a mass; but in those societies administered by an individualistic system, by a tyrannical system, by an ignorant system and the like, the majority of the people are *mustaḍʿafīn*.

They say: "*kunnā mustaḍʿafīna fī l-arḍ*" — "We were among the *mustaḍʿafīn*." They dragged us along so, carried us off, flung us down, trampled us, befouled us, and we did not understand; and now we are dying too. This is the excuse and answer of the *mustaḍʿafīn*. See what the angels say to them now. "*Qālū kunnā mustaḍʿafīna fī l-arḍ*" — they say, "We were oppressed in the earth; we were among the weak, the overpowered, the will-less, the powerless." The angels reply. See — it becomes clear that the logic of the angels is exactly equal to the reasonable, intelligible human logic; the human intellect too says this very thing. As that servant of God said seven or eight hundred years ago:

> *Set not your heart upon any one beloved, nor upon any one land,*
> *For land and sea are wide, and human beings are many.*

Saʿdī's view too was one with the view of these angels. The angels of the Lord say — "*qālū*," they say — "*a-lam takun arḍu Allāhi wāsiʿatan fa-tuhājirū fīhā*": "Why, man, you were oppressed there — but was the earth of the Lord confined to that one place? Was the whole world summed up in that one society in which you were living in a downtrodden way? Was the land of God not spread out, that you might go forth from this prison, go to a point of freedom? — to a point where you could worship God, to a point where you could put your energies to work along the right path, to a point where you would not be downtrodden? Was there no such place in the world?" "*Qālū a-lam takun arḍu Allāhi wāsiʿatan*" — "Was the earth of God not broad and wide?" "*fa-tuhājirū fīhā*" — "that you might make *hijrah* in the earth of God and go to another place?"

They have no answer left to give. What can they say, the poor wretches — it is well known that a sound argument has no rejoinder. This is why the Qur'an, regarding the outcome and end of these wretches, speaks thus: "*fa-ulāʾika*" — those *mustaḍʿafīn*, those whose power and will were in the hands of the Tāghūts, who were carrying them off to Hell — "*fa-ulāʾika maʾwāhum jahannamu wa sāʾat maṣīran*": those, then — "their abode" — where is their dwelling-place? — "Hell, and what an evil journey's end" and final destination it is for a human being. This too is the end of the affair.

Of course, there is an exception. Not everyone is able to make *hijrah*; not everyone is able to rescue themselves from those bonds of the ignorant system. A certain number are incapable; a certain number are old; a certain number are children; a certain number are women for whom the possibility of this undertaking does not exist. Hence these are excepted: "*illā l-mustaḍʿafīna*" — except the oppressed and downtrodden, "*mina l-rijāli wa-l-nisāʾi wa-l-wildāni*" — among the men, women, and children — those weak and incapable ones who, "*lā yastaṭīʿūna ḥīlatan*" — who have no recourse, of whom no undertaking is possible, "*wa lā yahtadūna sabīlan*" — they find no way toward the region of light, toward the region of Islam and the worship of God; they find no way, they can do nothing — "*fa-ulāʾika*," then, these who can do nothing, "*ʿasā Allāhu an yaʿfuwa ʿanhum*" — it is to be hoped that God will pardon them. See, these are the very words of the Qur'an: it is to be hoped that God Most High will pardon them; this is how their case stands. "*Wa kāna Allāhu ʿafuwwan ghafūran*" — and God is ever Pardoning, possessed of forgiveness.

Then, so that those to whom the address of *hijrah* is directed should not feel, should not imagine, that *hijrah* will be a source of wretchedness and loss for them — "How will it turn out? Can we? Can we not? Will we reach some place, or not?" — in answer it says: "*wa man yuhājir fī sabīli Allāh*" — that one who makes *hijrah* in the way of God — "*yajid fī l-arḍi murāghaman kathīran wa saʿatan*" — finds in the earth a vast field of movement and a spacious land. He sees, "What a flight one can make in the world! What a heart's-content soaring of wing one can do in the Islamic society! Until now our wing and our flight, when we soared very high, reached only to the ceiling of the cage. What horizons there are, how vast! In Mecca, the poor wretch prayed his prayer by force; if he was very keen, in the Masjid al-Ḥarām he prayed a two-*rakʿah* prayer and then took a thorough beating into the bargain. The utmost of being a Muslim was this, no more than this. Then, when he made *hijrah*, when he came to the free land, to the free air, to the Islamic society, beneath the wilāyah of God and of the Messenger of God, he saw, 'What a place this is! Here they vie with one another toward good works (*yatasābaqūna ila l-khayrāt*); here, by the verse of the Qur'an and by piety and worship, the rank of individuals is distinguished and made known. Whoever moves more for God, worships more, strives more, performs *jihād* more, gives more in charity — he is the more honored in this society.'"

Yesterday, if they had found out that one had given a single dirham in the way of God in Mecca, they would have heated the iron skewer red-hot and burned him with fire under torture. When you come and make *hijrah* in the way of God toward the city of the Messenger (*Madīnat al-Rasūl*), O Muslim of the early era of Islam, then you see what a field of movement it is, what a place for flight it is, how a human being can soar his wing to his heart's content. "*Wa man yuhājir fī sabīli Allāh*" — whoever makes *hijrah* in the way of God — "*yajid fī l-arḍ*" — that is, toward the divine and Islamic society — finds in the earth, in the way of God, "*murāghaman kathīran wa saʿatan*" — a vast field of movement, a place of abundant flight, and a spaciousness, an opening-up, a breadth.

Now, if you moved in the way of God from Mecca, from the abode of unbelief (*dār al-kufr*) toward the abode of emigration (*dār al-hijrah*); and along the way God took your soul, you died — how is it here? They say, "Then your wage and reward is with God, because you did your own part. The movement that it was incumbent upon you to perform issued from you."

> *Going to the desert road is better than sitting idle;*
> *For though I do not attain my desire, I strive to the measure of my capacity.*[^s28-19]

And Islam wants this. Islam wants every person, to the measure of his own ability — the amount that he can, and is able and capable of — to move in the way of God.

> **Qur'an, Sūrat al-Nisāʾ (4:100):** "Whoever goes forth from his house as an emigrant to God and His Messenger..." — that one who goes forth from his house, while he is making *hijrah* toward God and His Messenger — "*thumma yudrikhu l-mawt*" — then death overtakes him along the way — "*faqad waqaʿa ajruhu ʿala Allāh*" — then his reward has assuredly fallen due upon God and is upon the responsibility of God — "*wa kāna Allāhu ghafūran raḥīman*" — and God is Forgiving, possessed of mercy.

Note this point: since today is the last day of the discussion of recitation and our Qur'anic study, this topic remains roughly half-finished. Let me state this point: *hijrah* from the abode of unbelief, from the wilāyah of other-than-God, from the wilāyah of Satan and the Tāghūt — to where? To the abode of emigration (*dār al-hijrah*), the abode of faith (*dār al-īmān*), beneath the wilāyah of God, beneath the wilāyah of the imam; beneath the wilāyah of the Prophet and the divine *walī* one must make *hijrah* — this is *hijrah*. Now, if such a region did not exist in the world, what is to be done? Must one remain in the abode of unbelief? Or must one set about creating an abode of emigration? The Prophet himself, too, was among the emigrants, was he not? The Prophet too made *hijrah*; but before the Prophet made *hijrah*, an abode of emigration practically did not exist; the Prophet, by his own *hijrah*, created the abode of emigration.

Sometimes it becomes necessary that a group of human beings, by their own *hijrah*, inaugurate the starting-point of *hijrah*, found the divine and Islamic society, bring it into being, create the abode of emigration; then the believers make *hijrah* to that place. The upshot of the matter — this is the substance of the discussion concerning *hijrah*.

## Footnotes

[^s28-1]: Sūrat al-Māʾidah, verse 55.

[^s28-2]: "From God."

[^s28-3]: Sūrat al-Qaṣaṣ, verse 41.

[^s28-4]: Sūrat Ibrāhīm, verses 28 and 29.

[^s28-5]: Gustave Le Bon (1841–1931 CE), a French sociologist and orientalist who carried out extensive studies on Muslims and Islamic civilization. His works contain comparatively fewer bigoted opinions. *The Civilization of India*, *The Civilization of Egypt*, *Arab Civilization in Andalusia*, and *The Civilization of the Arabs* are among his books.

[^s28-6]: Adam Mez (1869–1917 CE), a German orientalist resident in Basel, Switzerland. His book was translated into Arabic and published under the title *al-Ḥaḍāra al-Islāmiyya fī l-Qarn al-Rābiʿ al-Hijrī* (Islamic Civilization in the Fourth Hijrī Century).

[^s28-7]: Ḥāfiẓ.

[^s28-8]: *Kulāh-gūsheh* (the cocked corner of the cap): grandeur and pomp; a metonym for greatness and pride. "The sultan's worth and majesty lost nothing / from his attention to the cottage of a peasant; / The peasant's cap-corner reached to the sun / when it cast its shade upon his head, with a guest like you." (Saʿdī)

[^s28-9]: Muʿallā ibn Khunays of Kufa was among the close companions of Imam Ṣādiq and was the Imam's agent in the affairs of his household's maintenance. The governor of Medina, on the pretext of learning about the Shīʿah, arrested Muʿallā and threatened him with death. Muʿallā answered thus: "Do you threaten me with killing? By God, were the Shīʿah of Imam Ṣādiq beneath my feet, I would not lift my foot from them; and if you kill me, you have made me fortunate and yourself wretched." And in this cause he attained martyrdom.

[^s28-10]: Muḥammad ibn Abī ʿUmayr al-Azdī was among the close companions of three illustrious Imams — Imam Kāẓim, Imam Riḍā, and Imam Jawād. There is, however, a difference of opinion as to whether he attained the presence of Imam Ṣādiq. Hārūn summoned and imprisoned him in order to make him disclose the names and whereabouts of the companions of Imam Kāẓim. According to one account, they administered a hundred lashes to him to make him open his lips, but he uttered not a word. Both in the time of Hārūn and in the time of Maʾmūn, on various pretexts rooted in his non-cooperation with the apparatus of the caliphate, he was repeatedly thrown into prison. He passed away in the year 217 AH.

[^s28-11]: Yaḥyā ibn Zayd is the son of Zayd ibn ʿAlī ibn al-Ḥusayn. He was present in his father's uprising against Hishām, and after his father's martyrdom in 121 AH he went from Iraq toward Khurāsān. After gathering some companions around himself, he went to Herat and took it. But in the next battle, against the army of the governor of Khurāsān, he was martyred in the year 125 AH. After they severed his head from his body, they hung his corpse on the gibbet, and it remained on the gibbet until the time of the uprising of Abū Muslim.

[^s28-12]: *Dhimmah* lexically means safe-conduct and covenant. To the People of the Book who commit to the conditions of *dhimmah* and are under protection on the part of the Islamic government, the term *ahl al-dhimmah* (the protected people) is applied.

[^s28-13]: One who obeys God while under the sway of a ruler who is not from God.

[^s28-14]: Hadith no. 32.

[^s28-15]: Sūrat al-Nisāʾ, verses 97 to 100.

[^s28-16]: *Istīnās* (from the root *a-n-s*): familiarization; a metonym for bringing a concept near to the mind.

[^s28-17]: A horse that, in the old mills, by its own movement along a fixed circular path, set the mill turning.

[^s28-18]: Sūrat Āl ʿImrān, verse 159.

[^s28-19]: Saʿdī.
